⚖️ Chapter 1 — Background

⚖️ Chapter 1 — Background

GST Litigation Reference — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
1.1 Introduction
1.2 Procedures Established by Law
1.3 Due Process
1.4 Abuse of Process
1.5 Spot Recovery
1.6 Demand in GST
1.7 Principles of Natural Justice
1.8 Cause of Action
1.9 Allegation and Evidence
1.10 No Presumption from GSTN Data
1.11 Effect of Non-Denial
1.12 Vagueness of Denial
1.13 Rights, Remedies & Safeguards
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
1.1 — Introduction
GST NOTICE — NOT ROUTINE; IT IS A LIS (DISPUTE) INITIATED IN LAW
What Revenue Has Done
POSITION Revenue has 'taken a position' on taxpayer's transaction
Examined info on Common Portal or otherwise
Disagrees with taxpayer's position
What Notice Does
PURPOSE 'Sets the law in motion'
Creates a lis — a dispute and legal action
NOT An invitation to exchange information
Common Taxpayer Mistake
WRONG Assumes reply = Revenue obliged to accept & drop proceedings
This is NOT the logical progress of a lis in law
AA acts as final authority ONLY if taxpayer admits liability and discharges it
GST — Plurality of Interpretation
Revenue is free to disagree with taxpayer's interpretation of law and its application to facts
GST facts often not capable of singularity of understanding
EXAMPLE Mobile phone given to best dealer claimed as 'gift' — ITC reversed. On examination: taxing ingredients exist — (i) lawful & permanent transfer of property (ii) from manufacturer to dealer (iii) in course of business (iv) under enforceable terms for mutual non-monetary consideration → Taxable supply. ITC reversal does NOT excuse liability to output tax.
1.2 — Procedures Established by Law
WHEN LEGISLATURE ENACTS — THINGS MUST BE DONE IN THE MANNER SPECIFIED. NO OTHER WAY.
The Mandate
Certain things must be done in certain ways
CANNOT Be done in any way one likes
If bypassed → rest of law can also be bypassed → law per whim of each Officer
Constitutional Basis
Traceable to Part III of Constitution
Implementing law = carrying out will of Legislature
Executive CANNOT implement independent of Legislature's guidance — even to best of Officer's understanding = failure of Legislature
Role of Procedures
Declare the path to fulfilling legislative will
Bear legislative consent as to steps in implementation
GST Law Structure
(i) Act
(ii) Rules
(iii) Forms
Standardization → clarity for dept. functionaries + transparency for taxpayer
Rules & Forms give irrefutable validity to actions of tax Officers
1.3 — Due Process
DUE PROCESS = COMPLETE METHODOLOGY CHOSEN BY PARLIAMENT TO 'SET LAW IN MOTION' (DEMAND & RECOVER)
What is Due Process
All steps comprising the methodology to demand & recover any sum due
Every proceeding in law has a clearly specified due process
NOTE Not the same as 'Procedure established by law' — often used interchangeably but distinct
Three Facets — WHAT / HOW / WHO
WHAT — the action/demand mandated in law
HOW — the manner; cannot substitute with alternate procedure
WHO — the Proper Officer; empowered by law per provision; circulars u/s 5 specify rank
Notice — Mandated First Step
Procures jurisdiction to Proper Officer
Must contain ALL ingredients to bring home the allegation
WITHOUT NOTICE Due process has not even commenced
Even if liability is staring in the face — due process is the ONLY methodology Parliament has chosen
Officer CANNOT Deviate
CANNOT Substitute procedure — even if a 'better' one exists
Deviation = 'attempting to legislate'; usurpation of role of Parliament
Deviating from mandate = outwilling Legislature
SCOPE 'Due process' makes steps knowable & transparent — whether filing returns, scrutinizing returns, best-judgment determination, initiating demand, or recovery. There must be a due process and that alone must be followed — without detracting, altering or improving upon it.
Proper Officer — WHO Matters
IMPROPER Proceedings improper + demand illegal if exercised by non-Proper Officer
EXAMPLE Clearance u/s 81 (disposal of business assets) → Additional Commissioner
EXAMPLE Registration u/s 25 → Superintendent of Central Tax
DEFENCE Failure in adhering to due process steps = grounds for defence — apart from merits — capable of unseating the demand. Deep appreciation of due process mandated in law is imperative. Juhi Industries (P) Ltd. v. State of JH (CA 1991/2021) — High Court quashed entire proceedings for failure of due process even after SCN, adjudication, Orders & rectification were done.
1.4 — Abuse of Process
ABUSE OF PROCESS = BYPASSING THE 'HOW' LAID DOWN IN LAW — EVERY DISUSE OF PROCESS IS ITS MISUSE
Prescribed Flow for Demand
① Observe demand → Issue DRC-1A (pre-notice consultation)
② Taxpayer accepts → Discharge with concessional penalty → Proceeding ENDS
② Taxpayer disputes → Continue ↓
③ Issue SCN + DRC-1 → Allow 30-day window to discharge
④ Entertain reply by way of objections
⑤ Grant personal hearing; allow adjournment
⑥ Speaking Orders + DRC-7 → Allow 3 months → Recovery
Examples of Abuse
ABUSE Officer visiting premises demanding immediate ITC reversal — passion of administration outdoing due process
ABUSE Demand for output tax via Letter of Endorsement — bypassing due process, legislating own procedure
ABUSE Summons u/s 70 to bring books to tax office for verification — contrary to specificity mandated in s.70
Doctrine of Acquiescence
DOCTRINE Taxpayer treated as having consented to proceed
Section 160(2) — bars objection of failure of due process in later proceedings
Enthusiasm to reply eclipses rights & safeguards in law
Validity supplied to an otherwise invalid proceeding by acquiescence
Governing Principle
Sakal Papers (P) Ltd. v. UOI AIR 1962 SC 305
"Legitimacy of result does not imply every means to achieve it is permissible; means must not transgress constitutional limits"
1.5 — Spot Recovery
SPOT RECOVERY — DEMANDING DUES 'ON THE SPOT' — NOT PERMITTED IN GST
Rule of Law Prevails
In-person engagement permitted by Legislature only in exceptional instances
NOT For purposes of recovery of dues
Natural Justice — Even Without Statute
Even in a law that does NOT provide for NJ, Rule of Law demands:
(i) Person must be 'put at notice'
(ii) Allowed to offer reasonable defence
Before any such orders are passed
NJ Applies to Admin Functions Too
Not only quasi-judicial functions
Also administrative functions — e.g., rejection of application for registration or cancellation
Dr. (Mrs.) Binapani Dey v. State of Orissa 1967 AIR SC 1269 — final authority on this
ABUSE EXAMPLE
Officer issuing verbal instructions to disclose login credentials + generating DRC-3 challan for output tax — another variation of overstepping rules of procedure
RECOVERY RULE When adjudication cannot be without serving a valid notice, recovery cannot be with anything less than complete adherence to Rule of Law.
1.6 — Demand in GST
DEMAND IN GST MUST FOLLOW DUE PROCESS IN CHAPTERS XII & XV OF CGST ACT
Rule 142(1) — Mandatory Documents
Notice to be issued "along with" summary of demand
Both SCN + DRC-1 with respective reference numbers are mandatory
CANNOT Make do with just a notice or just the summary — or any alternate
Invalid Notices — Examples
NO NOTICE Notice entitled "DRC-1" or "DRC-1 — show cause notice" — so incomplete it is 'no notice at all'
NO NOTICE SCN issued without DRC-1 summary; DRC-1 issued later on objection (with later date) — still 'not a notice' in law
Governing Principle
Nazir Ahmad v. King Emperor AIR 1936 PC 253
"Where a power is given to do a certain thing in a certain way, the thing must be done in that way or not at all"
BYPASS = AFFRONT
Bypassing explicit law + detailed rules + prescribed forms would be an affront to the legislative labour of Parliament & Government as its delegate
1.7 — Principles of Natural Justice
TWO CARDINAL PRINCIPLES OF NATURAL JUSTICE — EMBEDDED IN DUE PROCESS
Audi Alteram Partem
(Hear the Other Side)
Person to be heard must first be 'put at notice' with full disclosure of allegations & evidence
Allowed opportunity to answer those allegations
Transparent procedure must be declared
Determination based on objective consideration of facts; speaking order completes the mandate
Nemo Judex in Causa Sua
(Rule Against Bias)
No person shall be judge in own cause
Ridge v. Baldwin 1964 AC 40 — same person cannot be judge, jury and executioner
Absence of fairness → process reduced to empty formality; mind already made up, hearing for show
Effect of Violation
VIOLATION = Failure of due process → process arbitrary
If Executive action is arbitrary → violates Articles 14, 19 & 21
Menaka Gandhi v. UOI AIR 1978 SC 597 — violation occurs at threshold by not following due process, NOT by wrong conclusion on merits
'Useless Formality' Argument — Rejected
REJECTED Cannot bypass due process even if outcome seems obvious — akin to res ipsa loquitor
Board of High School v. Chitra AIR 1970 SC 1039
"Duty to issue SCN before penalty depends on nature of order proposed — NOT on authority's satisfaction that noticee has no defence"
SL Kapoor v. Jagmohan AIR 1981 SC 136 & Swadeshi Cotton Mills v. UOI AIR 1981 SC 818
"Non-observance of NJ is itself prejudice to any man — proof of prejudice independently is unnecessary"
1.8 — Cause of Action
CAUSE OF ACTION ≠ PROVISION VIOLATED — THREE SEPARATE PROVISIONS OPERATE IN SEQUENCE
Three-Layer Structure
Layer 1 — Provision imposing obligation on taxpayer (compliance)
Layer 2 — Provision taking cognizance of alleged violation
Layer 3 — Provision authorizing action against taxpayer
Examples
EG 1 No e-invoice (s.31 violated) → cause of action u/s 9 r/w s.7(1)(a), rules 46 & 48; penalty u/s 122
EG 2 Erroneous exemption (s.11 violated) → cause u/s 9 r/w s.7; demand u/s 73 or 74
EG 3 Erroneous refund of zero-rated supply → violation s.16(1) r/w 2(6) IGST; cause u/s 54 CGST; demand u/s 73
Wrong Provision = Fatal Defect
FATAL Notice under incorrect provision — cannot fill-in-blanks or accommodate error
Incurability is fatal to demand
Oryx Fisheries (P) Ltd. v. UOI 2011 (266) ELT 422 (SC)
CCE v. Brindavan Beverages 2007 (213) ELT 487 (SC)
Doctrine of Election
DOCTRINE Where Revenue has more than one cause of action → must elect one
EXAMPLE Ineligible transition credit may be demanded (i) as ineligible transition credit (no interest — no statutory provision) OR (ii) as unpaid output GST with interest & penalty u/s 73
NOTE Certain non-compliances in GST have NO actionable cause prescribed — enforcement is either automated (portal blocking for GSTR-1/3B late fee) or allowed to escape (Annual Return late fee — no functionality block, no power to demand via SCN u/s 73).
1.9 — Allegation and Evidence
ALLEGATION PROVES NOTHING BY ITSELF — MUST BE SUPPORTED BY IMPEACHING EVIDENCE
What is an Allegation
An opinion or interpretation forming 'another view' of tax position
To merit consideration — must be supported by evidence that impeaches credibility of taxpayer's claim
Presumption — Section 135
Only section in CGST raising a presumption
Relates only to animus (mental state) in prosecution context
Even this = only rebuttable presumption — NOT assumption
CANNOT Assume taxpayer has undischarged liability; presumption ≠ substitute for evidence of the wrong
What Qualifies as Evidence
Arises contemporaneously with the act alleged
Demonstrates occurrence/existence or non-occurrence
May be documentary or from witness
May be circumstantial or from attendant transactions
Introduces probative value to the act/omission; makes allegation reliable and true
Statements u/s 70 — Not Impeccable
NOT HIGH QUALITY Even owner's statement on oath — made under anxious circumstances, hence unreliable
Statements often contain 'opinion' — must be limited to facts only
Taxpayer's opinion on law = only opinion; neither admission of fact nor authoritative declaration
1.10 — No Presumption Flows from GSTN Data
GSTN DATA ALONE PROVES NOTHING — NO PRESUMPTION OF LIABILITY IN LAW
GSTN — Not Uniform Access
Not all States/UTs subscribed to GSTN
Some States use native systems (Model 1 & 2)
EXAMPLE ~9 States via GST Prime (NIC) — do not have access to all reports available on GSTN
Wrong Practice by Taxpayers
WRONG Entertaining notices on presumption based on GSTN data alone
Treating GSTN data as placing 'burden to prove innocence' on taxpayer
The Correct Position in Law
NO PRESUMPTION Whether by Model 1 or Model 2 States — data from portal raises NO presumption in law
Data itself proves nothing in absence of 'taxing ingredients' presented in accordance with due process
1.11 — Effect of Non-Denial
'SILENCE IS ACCEPTANCE' HAS LIMITED RELEVANCE IN GST — BUT PARTIAL RESPONSES CAN CONSTITUTE ADMISSION
Silence Alone — Insufficient
RULE Merely remaining silent is NOT sufficient
Silence + overt explanation may tilt scales in favour of Revenue = possible admission of wrongdoing
Admission by Non-Denial — Example
DANGER SCN demanding credit repayment (2A-3B mismatch); taxpayer's reply asserting bona fides of purchases = admission by non-denial of allegation
If reply asserts 'absence of supplier default is fatal to demand' → acceptance that credit inadmissible u/s 16(2)(c) once default shown
The Correct Approach
CORRECT Categoric statement required — make position clear in no uncertain terms
"Demand based entirely on mismatch of balances is rejected in its entirety and allegations made are disputed for being contrary to law and facts and on the following further grounds…"
Unequivocal response brings clarity → enables AA to proceed on rival claims of both sides
Role of Dissatisfaction
Satisfaction of NJ is NOT for taxpayer or Proper Officer to decide
Dissatisfaction is for taxpayer to PLEAD
Taxpayer's omission to plead = denial of redressal; also due to acquiescence in later proceedings
1.12 — Vagueness of Denial
REJECTION & DENIAL OF ALLEGATIONS MUST BE UNAMBIGUOUS — VAGUENESS MISLEADS ADJUDICATING AUTHORITY
Effect of Vague Denial
RISK AA may be misled as to taxpayer's actual position qua demand
EXAMPLE SCN on goods given as gifts; taxpayer's reply asserting ITC has been reversed — ambiguous whether: (i) admits output tax payable on gifts OR (ii) asserts output tax admitted but discharged by ITC reversal
Politeness ≠ Ambiguity
Cultural urge to leave communication confusing when disagreeing
Politeness in written communication is different from leaving it ambiguous
Why Clarity Matters
Adjudication is the FIRST step — not the last — in the long process until final resolution of lis
Clear position of each party → AA proceeds on rival claims
Proper Officer — Issue of Jurisdiction
NOTE Where notice issued after audit enquiry or inspection inquiry in Central GST dept —
Proper Officer who issued the notice is NOT the Proper Officer authorized to adjudicate upon the same notice
1.13 — Rights, Remedies and Safeguards
GST = SELF-ASSESSMENT REGIME (Sec 59) — GREATEST SAFEGUARD AND GREATEST PERIL FOR TAXPAYER
Rights — Key Provisions
Section 155 — burden on taxpayer only for ITC eligibility
'All other matters' — burden placed on Revenue by implication
Unless taxpayer volunteers to take up burden — it resides on Revenue
Revenue must impeach self-assessment by reliable material for valid reasons — able to stand scrutiny in appeal
Remedies — Within Jurisdiction
Contained in methodology for conferring jurisdiction on Proper Officer
Laid down specifically in each provision
EVEN Proper Officer cannot travel beyond scope of given provision
EXAMPLE Inspection u/s 67(1)(a) requires: (i) pre-existing reasons to believe (ii) those reasons form basis of authorization (iii) Officer not below Jt. Commissioner (iv) inquiry only re authorized evasion points
Safeguards — Mandatory Procedures
Adjudication safeguards — Sec 75
Penalty safeguards — Sec 126
Operate even against pursuit of potentially outstanding dues
Sec 75(7) Grounds on which SCN issued = only basis for Order. Attempts to 'save demand' → Order illegal, violation of s.75(7)
Absence of Pre-Conditions = No Jurisdiction
RULE Existence of ALL pre-conditions → lawful exercise of authority
Absence of even one → action lacks jurisdiction
Any discovery tainted with same illegality (if agitated; not acquiesced)
PURPOSE Every 'Notice to Show Cause' is an opportunity to show cause — WHY the allegations levelled against Noticee must NOT be adjudicated upon and be visited with the full extent of consequences laid down in law. Taxpayers must familiarize themselves with rights, remedies & safeguards — and also know how to locate them in given provisions and identify steps to exercise them.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 1 ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Mohinder Singh Gill v. Chief Election Commissioner — AIR 1978 SC 851
Supreme Court of India | 1978
Key Ratio / Principle
'Civil consequence' undoubtedly covers infraction of not merely property or personal rights but of civil liberties, material deprivations and non-pecuniary damages. In its comprehensive connotation, everything that affects a citizen in his civil life inflicts a civil consequence.
When to Use: To establish that any adverse tax action has 'civil consequences' — triggering the duty of judiciousness on every empowered Officer, not courts alone.
Sakal Papers (P) Ltd. v. Union of India — AIR 1962 SC 305
Supreme Court of India | 1962
Key Ratio / Principle
Legitimacy of the result intended to be achieved does not necessarily imply that every means to achieve it is permissible; for even if the end is desirable and permissible, the means employed must not transgress the limits laid down by the Constitution.
When to Use: When Revenue argues 'we were protecting interests of Revenue' while bypassing due process — means must remain within constitutional limits regardless of the legitimacy of the end goal.
Juhi Industries (P) Ltd. v. State of Jharkhand — CA 1991/2021
High Court | 2021
Key Ratio / Principle
Even where all steps — SCN, adjudication, Orders, rectification application and rectification — were completed, failure of 'due process' at any stage led the High Court to quash the entire proceedings.
When to Use: To argue that failure of due process at any step renders the entire proceeding liable to be quashed — even if the substantive demand may otherwise have merit.
Nazir Ahmad v. King Emperor — AIR 1936 PC 253
Privy Council | 1936
Key Ratio / Principle
Where a power is given to do a certain thing in a certain way, the thing must be done in that way or not at all.
When to Use: To challenge procedural defects — SCN issued without simultaneous DRC-1 summary, or without mandatory pre-notice consultation. Power not exercised in the prescribed way is no power at all.
Dr. (Mrs.) Binapani Dey v. State of Orissa — AIR 1967 SC 1269
Supreme Court of India | 1967
Key Ratio / Principle
Principles of natural justice must be followed even if there is no express requirement in the law to do so — not only in quasi-judicial functions but also in administrative functions such as rejection of registration application or its cancellation.
When to Use: When Revenue argues NJ need not be followed because the statute does not expressly require it — it is implied in every administrative action adversely affecting rights.
Menaka Gandhi v. Union of India — AIR 1978 SC 597
Supreme Court of India | 1978
Key Ratio / Principle
Violation of principles of natural justice is a failure of due process and renders the process arbitrary. When Executive action is arbitrary, it violates Articles 14, 19 and 21. Violation occurs right at the threshold by not following due process — not by a wrong conclusion on merits.
When to Use: When notice was defective or personal hearing not properly granted — establish that the proceeding is constitutionally infirm from inception, regardless of merits of demand.
Board of High School, etc. v. Chitra — AIR 1970 SC 1039
Supreme Court of India | 1970
Key Ratio / Principle
Whether a duty arises in a particular case to issue a show cause notice before including a penalty does not depend on the authority's satisfaction that the person to be penalised has no defence but on the nature of the order proposed to be passed.
When to Use: Against the argument that SCN or hearing was a 'useless formality' because outcome was obvious — the nature of the order determines the duty to issue notice, not the merits of defence.
SL Kapoor v. Jagmohan — AIR 1981 SC 136 & Swadeshi Cotton Mills v. UOI — AIR 1981 SC 818
Supreme Court of India | 1981
Key Ratio / Principle
The principles of natural justice know of no exclusionary rule dependent on whether it would have made any difference if natural justice had been observed. The non-observance of natural justice is itself prejudice to any man and proof of prejudice independently of proof of denial of natural justice is unnecessary.
When to Use: When Revenue argues denial of NJ caused no prejudice since the result would have been the same — shut down this argument firmly: non-observance is itself the prejudice, no further proof needed.
Ridge v. Baldwin — 1964 AC 40
House of Lords (applied in Indian jurisprudence) | 1964
Key Ratio / Principle
The same person cannot possibly be permitted to be judge, jury and executioner. Fairness is the hallmark of adjudication; if that is absent, the entire process is reduced to an empty formality or a wooden public relations exercise where the mind is already made up.
When to Use: To challenge bias — e.g., the Officer who investigated also adjudicates; or audit party issues notice and same Officer adjudicates upon it.
Oryx Fisheries (P) Ltd. v. UOI — 2011 (266) ELT 422 (SC) & CCE v. Brindavan Beverages (P) Ltd. — 2007 (213) ELT 487 (SC)
Supreme Court of India | 2011 / 2007
Key Ratio / Principle
Notice issued under an incorrect provision of law cannot be cured by filling-in-the-blanks or accommodating errors; incurability of such a defect is fatal to the demand.
When to Use: When SCN invokes a wrong provision as the cause of action — the demand is fundamentally defective, incurable, and must fail regardless of the merit of underlying allegation.
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 1 — Background
⚖️ Chapter 2 — Sections 25 & 29

⚖️ Chapter 2 — Notices under Sections 25 and 29

GST Litigation Reference — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
2.1 Overview
2.2 Anatomy of a Notice
2.3 Limitations of Scope
2.4 Rejection of Application
2.5 Repeated Applications
2.6 Option for Composition
2.7 Option for Provisional Assessment
2.8 Suspension of Registration
2.9 Suo Motu Cancellation
2.10 Revocation of Cancellation
2.11 Voluntary Cancellation
2.12 Appealable Orders
2.13 Circulars and Instructions
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
2.1 — Overview
REGISTRATION — SERIES OF ADMINISTRATIVE ACTIONS; DUE PROCESS IN CHAPTER VI OF CGST ACT
Scope of Chapter 2
Registration — Sec 25 & 27
Suspension & Cancellation — Sec 29
Revocation — Sec 30
Appeal for restoration — Sec 107
NJ Still Applies
Rigours of demand notice ≠ applicable here
Requirement to 'put at notice' and ensure no miscarriage of justice is NOT lost in these proceedings
Registration — Due Process Flow
① Apply in REG1 Part A → Upload docs in Part B
② Acknowledgement in REG2
→ Notice of deficiencies in REG3 "within 7 days"
③ Respond to deficiencies "within 7 days" in REG4
→ Reject due to unresolved deficiencies in REG5
④ If not rejected → Registration in REG6 "within 7 days"
Post-registration: Aadhaar authentication mandatory
Purpose of These Deliberations
Precursor to deliberations on demand notices
Invitation to consider underlying principles — NOT a restatement of statutory provisions
2.2 — Anatomy of a Notice
PROCEDURE FOR REGISTRATION ALSO AFFECTS RIGHTS OF TAXPAYERS — NJ MUST BE FOLLOWED
Adverse Consequences of Non-Registration
CANNOT Claim Input Tax Credit — ITC only to 'registered person'
CANNOT Generate e-waybill
CANNOT Issue tax invoice to collect GST from Customers
BUT Output tax can be demanded from any 'taxable person' — even unregistered
Constitutional Angle
Vinod Kumar v. CST (WP 1553/2021) — Uttarakhand HC
"Denial of GST registration affects chances of employment / executing works → affects right to livelihood → violative of Article 21 if denied without remedy to appeal"
Three Situations Requiring 'Notice'
(a) Deficiencies in application for registration
(b) Proposal to suspend or cancel registration
( c) Justification for revocation of suspended registration
Role of Proper Officer
Application assigned to Central or State/UT GST dept for verification
Must prevent prejudice to Revenue while acting as 'trade facilitator'
Short point: determine questions of fact that entail grant of registration
2.3 — Limitations of Scope
ROLE OF PROPER OFFICER u/s 25 IS NOT THAT OF AN INVESTIGATOR — SCOPE IS LIMITED
What Officer Cannot Do
CANNOT Decisively show prejudice to Revenue at registration stage — business yet to commence
CANNOT Use misadventures under earlier laws as sufficient reason to reject
NOT PERMITTED Enquiry into pre-registration activities of Applicant
Legitimate Grounds to Reject / Scrutinize
(a) Applicants are benami — acting for other persons
(b) Insufficient to establish identity of Applicant & business premises
(c) Registration threshold already crossed — applying to overcome investigation
(d) Purpose: to claim and transfer ITC only
(e) Incorrect category — regular, casual, non-resident or ISD
(f) Goods proposed to be dealt with are prone to evasion
Purpose of Notice in Registration
Allow Applicant opportunity to satisfy Proper Officer of correctness, completeness & reliability of documents
No investigative work involved
Applicant's reply is to be binary — not involving complex questions of law
Due Process Applies Equally
Notices in proceedings from Sec 25 are as much a 'due process' of law as a demand notice
Time limits are short — extensive investigative work is NOT involved
2.4 — Rejection of Application
ORDER OF REJECTION IN REG5 IS A 'DECISION OR ORDER' — APPEALABLE + MUST BE A SPEAKING ORDER
Appellate Right
RIGHT Appeal against Order of Rejection — Sec 107(1)
Grounds on which rejected — open for consideration by Appellate Authority
Unusual for Applicant to appeal rather than file fresh application — but legally available
Speaking Order Requirements
Must state reasons for finding that application merits rejection
Supported by documents or response from Applicant showing insufficiency
Must bring out insufficiency of documentation required to establish identity or location
What is NOT Germane at Rejection Stage
NOT RELEVANT Correctness of aggregate turnover
NOT RELEVANT Classification & forecast of turnover
NOT RELEVANT Nature of supplies
NOT RELEVANT Financial background or skill to engage in business
NOT RELEVANT Doubts about bona fides of Applicant
Scope of Rejection — Confined To
Objective of application for registration only
Establishing identity of Applicant and location of business premises
2.5 — Repeated Applications Without Reply to Notice
FILING FRESH REG1 AFTER ALLOWING PREVIOUS APPLICATION TO LAPSE — INVITES CLOSER SCRUTINY
The Problem
Where REG3 deficiency notice issued → Applicant obliged to respond within 7 days in REG4
RISK Omitting to respond → application lapses under rule 9(4)
Filing fresh REG1 = 'application history' will be examined by Proper Officer
What Scrutiny Reveals
Reasons for omission to respond in REG4 in previous application
Essential information may have been misstated or incongruous with supporting documents
Unexplained 'repeat applications' → closer verification; response less likely to be accepted readily
Best Practice
Exhibit transparency in responding to notices about the reason for the repeat application
2.6 — Option for Composition
COMPOSITION (Sec 10) IS AN OPTION FOR TAXPAYER — COMMUNICATED BY 'INTIMATION' NOT APPLICATION
Legal Framework
Sec 10 — legislative mandate to opt for composition
Rules 3 to 7 — due process for composition
Only for 'registered person' — no difference in REG1 application whether opting for composition or not
Process
After REG1 Part B submitted → 'intimation' of decision to avail composition is to be made
Composition is taxpayer's option — communicated to Proper Officer by 'intimation'
Scope of Verification
Limited review of eligibility to composition only
NOT About threshold limit of turnover
Only the category of composition — as declared by Applicant
Response to REG3 on Composition
EXAMPLE Bakery (manufacturer + trader + eatery) — Circular 27/1/2018-GST clarifies bakery = akin to restaurant for composition u/s 10(1)(b)
REG3 notices on composition eligibility → same level of responsibility & risk as demand notices; clarity & transparency required
2.7 — Option for Provisional Assessment
PROVISIONAL ASSESSMENT (Sec 60) — FOR WHEN MATERIAL INFORMATION IS UNAVAILABLE; NOT A SUBSTITUTE FOR ADVANCE RULING
When Available
TRIGGER Person is "unable" to carry out self-assessment
Material information having bearing on self-assessment is not available
NOT Doubts about interpretation of law — that is for Advance Ruling (Ch. XVII)
Process
Apply in ASMT1 under Rule 98
Notice in ASMT2 — for additional information
Respond in ASMT3 — firmly establish maintainability
Order in ASMT4 — Speaking Order required; appealable u/s 107(1)
Proper Officer's Limited Scope
(a) Maintainability — confusion about law vs truly missing information
(b) Conditions — bond + security for differential tax
(c) Conclusion of provisional assessment
Examples: Fit vs Not Fit
FIT HSN 2306 bran — identity of product cannot be known without lab test; exempt or 5%?
NOT FIT Ready-to-eat savouries in sweet meat shop — doubt is about interpretation (goods vs services), not about unavailable material fact
2.8 — Suspension of Registration
SUSPENSION (Rules 21, 21A, 22) — PRE-EMPTIVE STEP BEFORE CANCELLATION; NO NOTICE REQUIRED BEFORE SUSPENDING
Nature of Suspension
RULE Suspension is now the FIRST step in all instances — delinquency or voluntary cancellation
No violation of NJ — no finality in suspension; full restitution possible
Taxpayer not prejudiced by lapse of credit or any other manner during suspension
Post-Suspension Flow
① Post suspension → Notice in REG17 or REG31
→ Reply "within 7 days" in REG18 (extended to 30 days for suspension)
② Satisfactory → Suspension withdrawn in REG20
② Not satisfactory → Registration cancelled in REG19
REG31 — Post-Decisional Notice
Issued 'after' suspension under Rule 21A(2A)
Legally valid as 'post decisional hearing notice'
Causes stated in REG31 will generally be self-explanatory; answers must be substantiated
What Suspension Stops
Issue tax invoice and collect tax
File returns
Generate e-waybills
Claim refund
Avail other portal facilities
STRATEGY Not the time to question jurisdiction or validity of suspension causes. Address and redress delinquencies swiftly. Response must address all outstanding tax, interest and late fee. Where delinquencies removed — withdrawal will not normally be denied. Loss of livelihood / cancellation of Customer contracts = NOT good reasons for withdrawal.
2.9 — Suo Motu Cancellation
CANCELLATION IS FINAL — POST-SUSPENSION NOTICE IN REG31 BORROWS FROM PRE-CANCELLATION NOTICE IN REG17
Five Explicit Delinquencies u/s 29(2)
Legislature has specified 5 delinquencies — taxpayers well-informed through s.29(2) itself
Vigilantibus non dormientibus jura subveniunt — law assists the vigilant, not those asleep over their rights
CONSEQUENCE Cannot complain of 'inadequate notice' when any of these 5 delinquencies are permitted to remain unresolved
Proper Officer's Position
Not answerable if registrations cancelled where specified delinquencies exist
Discretion exercised does not militate against validity of action
No right against discrimination between delinquents inter se
Taxpayer's Response — REG18
MUST Reply in REG18
MORE IMPORTANTLY Redress and regularize outstanding delinquencies
Satisfactory response + redressal → cancellation dropped in REG20; not satisfactory → Speaking Order in REG19
Liabilities on Cancellation
(a) Outstanding tax, interest, late fee & penalties
(b) Sec 29(5) — credits availed
Final returns u/s 45 — within 3 months
NOTE REG19 may omit demanding all dues — but this does NOT eclipse taxpayer's liability u/s 29(5)
Sec 93(1) — recovery permitted even from taxpayer's estate; Sec 29(3) — determination permitted long after cancellation
2.10 — Revocation of Cancellation
NOT EVEN CANCELLATION IS FINAL — Sec 30 + Rule 23 PROVIDE MECHANISM FOR REVOCATION
Revocation Flow
① Registration cancelled in REG19
② Apply for revocation "within 30 days" in REG21
③ If not satisfied → Notice for justification in REG23
④ Reply "within 7 days" in REG24
⑤ Revocation allowed in REG22
⑤ Revocation application rejected in REG5
What Revocation Does NOT Require
NOT NEEDED Elaborate pleadings — essence is evident in the title of the prescribed form
Emotional pleas for justice due to loss of livelihood — unnecessary
Three Conditions for Grant — No Reason to Refuse
(a) REG21 filed within time limit
(b) All outstanding undisputed dues discharged
(c) Declaration in REG24 — "said registration is still required in view of intention to continue said business"
To reject in REG5 → Speaking Order required; furnishes grounds for appeal
Revenue's Remedy if Aggrieved by Revocation
Revocation in REG22 → restoration goes back to date of suspension; time-barred credits under s.16(4) become admissible
Revenue may prefer departmental appeal u/s 112(3) to Appellate Tribunal
OR initiate revisionary proceedings u/s 108
Taxpayer can resist u/s 108 proceedings with reasonable expectation of success — due to immediacy of prejudice
2.11 — Voluntary Cancellation
VOLUNTARY CANCELLATION (Sec 29, Rule 20) — CAREFUL EVALUATION REQUIRED BEFORE CANCELLING
Critical Warning
WARNING Output tax with interest and penalty can be demanded — limitation permitting — long AFTER registration is cancelled
Input tax credit CANNOT be allowed if registration stands cancelled
Possible Liabilities on Cancellation
(a) Arrears of tax, interest & penalties
(b) Late fee — monthly, annual or final returns
( c) Dues under Sec 29(5)
Process After REG16 Filed
Proceedings skip to post-suspension stage along with all attendant disabilities
If reason to reject detected → notice in REG17 + rest of procedure follows
Voluntarily cancelled registration may not qualify for revocation — but where it does, revocation is a step toward redressal
2.12 — Appealable Orders
ORDER REJECTING REVOCATION APPLICATION (REG5) IS APPEALABLE UNDER Sec 107(1)
Taxpayer's Appeal Right
Revocation rejected in REG5 → File appeal within 3 months in APL1
Order rejecting cancellation = adjudication order → appealable u/s 107(1)
Revenue's Position
NO APPEAL Departmental appeal not permissible — Revenue NOT aggrieved by order rejecting cancellation
Revenue may become aggrieved when revocation is ALLOWED — restoration in REG22 revives time-barred credits u/s 16(4)
Effect on Payments under Sec 29(5)
Revocation does NOT entitle reversal of all payments made u/s 29(5)
Only to the extent credit is admissible u/s 18(1)
Finality of Cancellation
Real finality comes only when time limit for revocation passes
Taxpayer should achieve proper winding down to be free from liabilities coming to light later — when limitation for rectification or appeal may have passed
2.13 — Circulars and Instructions
IMPORTANT CIRCULARS & INSTRUCTIONS — REGISTRATION JURISPRUDENCE
Succession on Death
Circular 96/15/2019-GST dated 28 Mar 2019 — procedure for transition of registration on succession due to death of taxpayer
Revocation Guidelines
Circular 148/4/2021-GST dated 18 May 2021 — guidelines regarding revocation of cancelled registrations
Extension of Revocation Time
Notification 34/2021-Central Tax dated 29 Aug 2021 — extension of time limit for revocation
Circular 158/14/2021-GST dated 6 Sept 2021 — applicable procedures for extended revocation
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 2 ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Vinod Kumar v. Commissioner of State Tax — WP 1553/2021
Uttarakhand High Court | 2021
Key Ratio / Principle
If the petitioner is denied a GST registration number, it affects his chances of getting employment or executing works. Such denial of registration of GST number, therefore, affects his right to livelihood. If he is denied his right to livelihood because of the fact that his GST Registration number has been cancelled, and that he has no remedy to appeal, then it shall be violative of Article 21 of the Constitution as right to livelihood springs from the right to life as enshrined in Article 21 of the Constitution of India.
When to Use: To resist arbitrary rejection or cancellation of registration — establish that denial of registration is not merely an administrative act but an assault on the constitutional right to livelihood under Article 21.
Vigilantibus Non Dormientibus Jura Subveniunt — Legal Maxim
Established Legal Maxim | Applied in GST — Sec 29(2)
Key Ratio / Principle
The law assists those who are vigilant about their rights and not those who are asleep over them. When any of the five specified delinquencies under section 29(2) are permitted to occur and remain unresolved, taxpayer must not express surprise or complain about 'inadequate notice' when Proper Officer proceeds with cancellation as authorized by Legislature.
When to Use: As a double-edged sword — Revenue invokes this when cancelling for delinquencies; Taxpayer invokes this to show vigilance in filing REG21 for revocation within time and discharging dues promptly.
Nemo Judex in Causa Sua — Rule Against Bias (Section 30 — Express Power of Review)
Constitutional Principle | Applied in Revocation u/s 30
Key Ratio / Principle
Although the power of the same Proper Officer to review his own cancellation order (in revocation under section 30) appears to touch the line of violating the rule nemo judex in causa sua, this power of review is express in the law and is to allow relief to the taxpayer. Adverse outcome in violation of natural justice alone comes up for consideration before Courts.
When to Use: If Proper Officer denies revocation arbitrarily — argue that the power of review under section 30 is for the benefit of the taxpayer; any adverse use must conform to natural justice and a Speaking Order is mandatory for rejection.
SMWP 3/2020 — Suo Motu Writ Petition + Sec 168A Notifications
Supreme Court of India | 2020 (COVID-era extension)
Key Ratio / Principle
Taxpayer's appeals for restoration of registration are being entertained on equitable considerations, especially citing SMWP 3/2020 of Apex Court read with notifications issued under section 168A — allowing extension of limitation periods during extraordinary circumstances.
When to Use: When taxpayer has missed the 30-day window for filing revocation application in REG21 due to COVID or extraordinary circumstances — argue equitable extension of limitation under SMWP 3/2020 and applicable section 168A notifications.
Dr. (Mrs.) Binapani Dey v. State of Orissa — AIR 1967 SC 1269 (Applied to Registration)
Supreme Court of India | 1967
Key Ratio / Principle
Principles of natural justice must be followed even if there is no express requirement in the law to do so — not only in quasi-judicial functions but also in administrative functions such as rejection of application for registration or its cancellation.
When to Use: In all registration proceedings — to establish that even purely administrative decisions like rejection of REG1 application or cancellation of registration attract natural justice: notice, hearing and a Speaking Order are mandatory.
General Principle — Speaking Order Mandatory for REG5 Rejection (Sec 107 Appeal Right)
Section 107(1) CGST Act | Rule 21A and Rule 22
Key Ratio / Principle
Order of Rejection in REG5 must be a Speaking Order — it must state the reasons for the finding that the application merits rejection, duly supported by documents or response from Applicant, which brings out the insufficiency of documentation. Without a Speaking Order, the grounds for rejection cannot be open for consideration by the Appellate Authority under section 107(1).
When to Use: When REG5 rejection does not contain reasons or grounds, or when rejection is based on irrelevant criteria (turnover forecasts, financial background, bona fides) — argue that the rejection order is non-speaking and liable to be set aside; file appeal under section 107(1).
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 2 — Notices under Sections 25 and 29
⚖️ Chapter 3 — Section 63

⚖️ Chapter 3 — Notice under Section 63

GST Litigation Reference — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
3.1 Overview
3.2 Sec 63 Not Competing with Sec 62
3.3 Sec 63 Not Competing with Sec 67
3.4 Taxable Person Not Holding Registration
3.5 Authority to Investigate Absent
3.6 Registration Ousts Jurisdiction
3.7 Best Judgment is Not Wild Guesswork
3.8 Basis of Best Judgment
3.9 Interest and Penalty Not Imposable
3.10 Reply More Onerous
3.11 Pre-Emptive Powers Imperil Taxpayers
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
3.1 — Overview
SECTION 63 — BEST JUDGMENT ASSESSMENT OF UNREGISTERED PERSONS; DIFFERENT JURISPRUDENCE FROM Sec 73 & 74
Key Distinction from Sec 73 / 74
UNIQUE Sec 63 eclipses Sec 73 & 74 — empowers Proper Officer to determine tax on basis of estimates and guesswork
This is the most telling distinction of Sec 63 and the most striking LIMITATION of Sec 73 / 74 by implication
Notice accompanied by DRC1 (not DRC7) — Rule 100(2); 12th provision under rule 142(1) mandating ASMT14 + DRC1
What Sec 63 Can Demand
ONLY Tax liability — not interest, not penalty
NO INTEREST Without info about 'time of supply' — any imputed interest would be arbitrary and illegal
NO PENALTY Sec 63 is limited to "tax liability" alone
Jurisdiction Triggers
Unlike Sec 64 — which requires 'evidence' to invoke jurisdiction
Sec 63 — something less than evidence; something more than mere suspicion
Notice: ASMT14 + DRC1 → Adjudicated → Order: ASMT15 + DRC7 → DRC7 is 'notice of recovery' u/s 79 → Appealable u/s 107(1)
Taxpayer's Position
Opportunity to reply must be given — even when liability is indubitable
Must give utmost attention to these proceedings and timelines — DRC7 issued at end triggers recovery u/s 79 unless stayed by appeal
3.2 — Sec 63 Not Competing with Sec 62
SEC 62 (NON-FILER) vs SEC 63 (UNREGISTERED) — DIFFERENT OBJECTS; NO OVERLAP
Section 62 — Flow for Non-Filers
① Notice in GSTR3A → file outstanding returns
Non-filing within 15 days → Order in ASMT13 + DRC7
File valid returns within 60+60 days with interest u/s 50 & late fee u/s 47 (₹100/day) → Order in ASMT13 vacated u/s 62(2)
Non-filing beyond 60+60 days → Recovery in DRC13 (if appeal not filed)
Key Distinction — Sec 62 vs 63
Sec 62 — pre-emptive order permitted; can be vacated by filing returns within time
Sec 63 — commences with NOTICE, not pre-emptive order
Sec 62 target: registered person who failed to file; Sec 63 target: unregistered person
Harsh Reality of Sec 62
PERIL Order in ASMT13 remains valid even if actual dues already discharged but returns filed beyond 60+60 days
Recovery can proceed — not even a willing Proper Officer can provide relief
Only stay u/s 107(7) on filing timely appeal before AA can prevent recovery
LESSON GST expects everyone to adhere to procedure and leave justice to take care of itself
3.3 — Sec 63 Not Competing with Sec 67
SECTION 63 AND SECTION 67 OCCUPY EXCLUSIVE, NON-OVERLAPPING AREAS OF OPERATION
Threshold to Invoke
Sec 67: 'Reasons to believe' = more than suspicion, less than evidence; requires authorization from Joint Commissioner
Sec 63: Not fettered by 'reasons to believe'; needs (i) undischarged liability and (ii) person is aware of it — something more than suspicion but less than evidence
Rank of Officer
Sec 67: Under oversight of Joint Commissioner
Sec 63: Commercial Tax Officer / Superintendent up to Deputy Commissioner — no authorization needed from superior
When to Use Which
MISAPPLICATION Using Sec 63 estimates & guesswork when Sec 67 ingredients exist = misapplication of law
CORRECT Where evasion of tax detected → Sec 67 is appropriate, not Sec 63
Where credible info available but not sufficient for Sec 67 authorization → Sec 63 becomes available
Legislative Design
Sec 63 in Chapter XII; Sec 67 in Chapter XIV — deliberate placement in different chapters
Sec 63: to be used sparingly — not when more accurate proceeding u/s 67 is available
RISK Proper Officer may be tempted to bypass Sec 67 (requires JC authorization) and use Sec 63 — this would be misapplication of law
3.4 — Taxable Person Not Holding Registration on Date of Notice
SEC 63 APPLIES ONLY TO PERSONS WHO ARE UNREGISTERED ON DATE PROCEEDINGS ARE INITIATED
Two Specified Persons
(a) One who is unregistered — and info available about undischarged liability
'Failure' = deliberate omission to register though aware; NOT bona fide exemption claim u/s 23(1)
EXAMPLE (a) Person liable u/s 24 as per intelligence cannot be acted upon under Sec 63 without further investigation — Sec 63 does not permit investigation
Two Specified Persons (contd.)
(b) One who was registered but cancelled u/s 29(2) — and info available about undischarged liability
Proper Officer who cancelled the registration has jurisdiction to issue notice
EXAMPLE (b) GSTR2A data showing inward supplies above threshold limit even post date of cancellation → can be acted upon under Sec 63
Jurisdictional Proper Officer
Notice must state how jurisdiction is derived — merely appearing in Circular 3/3/2017-GST (5 Jul 2017) is not enough
For Sec 63: jurisdiction guided by geographic territory — not by Common Portal allocation
Both Central and State/UT GST Officers enjoy limited authority u/s 63 r/w s.6(2)(b) — over unregistered persons in their territory or persons whose registration they cancelled
Critical Condition
CONDITION Taxable person must NOT be registered as on date of initiation of proceedings u/s 63
Once registered → respective GST dept assigned on Common Portal exercises administrative oversight; Sec 63 jurisdiction ends
3.5 — Authority to Investigate Absent
SECTION 63 CONFERS NO INVESTIGATIVE AUTHORITY — DELIBERATE LEGISLATIVE INTENDMENT, NOT OVERSIGHT
What Officer CANNOT Do
CANNOT Issue summons to cause taxable persons to submit pre-registration documents
CANNOT Call for bank statements, income-tax returns, financial statements, etc.
EXAMPLE Person compelled to submit pre-registration info disclosing threshold exemption error — cannot be brought under Sec 63; pre-registration info not prescribed at time of granting registration
Consequence of Illegal Collection
PARADOX Illegally collected information CAN be used to issue notice
But collection of info without authority of law = insurmountable hurdle in adjudication if taxable person questions jurisdiction
Taxpayer's Rights
RIGHT u/s 160(2) — actively disobey unauthorized investigations
Withdraw from participating in unauthorized investigations
Respectfully decline to submit to any directions calling for documents or records
FORFEITURE WARNING
Entering objections on merits WITHOUT questioning jurisdiction = forfeiture of jurisdictional remedies in all further proceedings (appeal / revision)
3.6 — Registration Ousts Jurisdiction of Proper Officer
AS SOON AS SPECIFIED PERSON OBTAINS REGISTRATION — PROPER OFFICER LOSES JURISDICTION UNDER SEC 63
Effect of Registration
JURISDICTION ENDS No sooner than registration obtained — Proper Officer loses Sec 63 jurisdiction
CANNOT Launch investigation into pre-registration transactions of now-registered person
NO AUTHORITY From Sec 22 to 29 to demand pre-registration documents or investigate earlier period liability
What Officer Can Do After Registration
PERMITTED Obtain authorization from Joint Commissioner → pass orders u/s 64
Or issue notice u/s 73 or 74 based on available information — but NOT use estimates & guesswork of Sec 63
Why
GST is a self-assessment regime (Sec 59) — pre-emptive interference allowed only in very few specific circumstances
Sec 63 is suited only for persons evading registration — an unreliable & inaccurate methodology; inappropriate once registration obtained
3.7 — Best Judgment is Not Wild Guesswork
BEST JUDGMENT ≠ WILD GUESSWORK — PROPER OFFICER MUST APPLY INFORMATION RESPONSIBLY
Five Parameters Officer MUST Consider
(a) Nature of business of taxable person
(b) Taxable value most reasonably supported by information collected
(c) Determination of most appropriate HSN code and effective tariff
(d) Application of appropriate time and place of supply
(e) Allowance for average input tax credit
Examples of Wild Guesswork — Invalid
INVALID ASMT14 + DRC1 demanding tax on GSTR2A inward supply value + adding 100% margin
INVALID Turnover from TDS-TCS / IT / bank statements with GST rate of 28% — no correlation to actual outward supplies
INCURABLE ASMT14 demands tax as supply of goods when information indicates supply of services — will eventually be dismissed
Example of Valid Determination
VALID ASMT14 demands tax as works contract services but denies exemption on supplies to Government
Valid because taxable person is responsible to demonstrate applicability of exemption — burden shifts to taxpayer
Principle
Tax liability determined must show that information collected has been applied in an expeditious manner
Liability not sustained in appeal due to fatalities in exercising these powers = mockery of legislative wisdom
3.8 — Basis of Best Judgment
BEST JUDGMENT = DEMAND FOR POSSIBLY NON-EXISTENT TURNOVER — ROOT CAUSE IS TAXPAYER'S OWN OMISSION
What Forms the Basis
Information seized by Proper Officer raising question of undischarged liability = forms the basis
Passionate exercise based on logical inference — sufficient
Third-party data can also be a basis
Unlike Sec 62 / 64
Sec 62 / 64 — pre-emptive orders permitted before notice
Sec 63 — commences with notice, not with an order
Latitude permitted to Proper Officer is NOT without checks and balances — no instructions to guide but no blanket authority either
Taxpayer's Obligation in Reply
MUST Clarify factual misinformation, if any, in the notice
CAUTION Do NOT expand scope of notice by introducing new material not forming part of notice — enthusiasm backfires especially for those who believe no tax liability exists
3.9 — Interest and Penalty Not Imposable
DEMAND FOR INTEREST AND PENALTY UNDER SEC 63 IS INCONGRUOUS WITH BEST JUDGMENT BASIS
Why Interest Not Imposable
CANNOT Without information about 'time of supply' — any interest calculation would be arbitrary and illegal
Sec 75(9) saving provision also cannot permit confirmation of interest or penalty under Sec 63
Why Penalty Not Imposable
CANNOT Sec 63 is limited to demanding "tax liability" alone — penalty not permitted
Latitude allowed in law does not authorize 'audit' of records of unregistered persons
Form ASMT15 — Fresh Ground for Appeal
ALERT Form ASMT15 contains a table with provision for interest and penalty
Form and rule cannot override the statute — no such authority exists
Table in ASMT15 demanding interest/penalty = fresh grounds on jurisdiction to be raised in appeal. Same issue exists in ASMT13 vs language in Sec 62.
3.10 — Reply More Onerous
REPLYING TO SEC 63 NOTICE IS MORE ONEROUS THAN TO SEC 73 / 74 — BURDEN OF PROOF IS LOWER ON REVENUE
Why Revenue's Burden is Lower
Sec 63 in Chapter XII — general discipline of Chapter XV (demand provisions) is NOT applicable
Allegations in ASMT14 must rise above suspicion but NOT bear impeccable evidence — lower threshold
Four Reasons Reply is Onerous
(a) Demand admittedly based on 'best judgment' — unimpeachable premise is absent
(b) Omission to register is also a fact — difficult to deny
(c) Reasons for non-registration are NOT within scope of notice
(d) Exposing reasons for non-registration = expands scope of notice; assertion about exemption eligibility shifts onus onto taxpayer
Taxpayer's Best Strategy
FOCUS ON (i) Jurisdiction challenges — is Proper Officer the correct jurisdictional officer?
FOCUS ON (ii) Validity of information forming basis — not the conclusion alone
Greatest resource: demand is based on 'best judgment' which is NOT an 'unimpeachable' premise
CONTRAST
Sec 73 / 74 — Revenue must establish case with requisite evidence before taxpayer justifies self-assessment.
Sec 63 — Revenue needs only 'something more than suspicion'; taxpayer bears heavier practical burden in reply.
3.11 — Pre-Emptive Powers Imperil Taxpayers
TAXPAYERS ARE NOT FREE FROM PERIL OF SEC 63 — DEFENCE MUST ADDRESS CRUDITIES PERMITTED IN THE PROVISION
The Peril
DANGER Illegally collected 'information' can support a legally valid notice
Taxpayer must attend to conclusions the information feeds — not just scour the sources to doubt the information
Expectation of refinement (as in Sec 73 / 74) will be visited with adverse consequences — no sympathy in appellate proceedings
How to Approach Defence
TAILORED DEFENCE Address the crudities that are permitted in Sec 63
Do not expect the standards of Sec 73 / 74 to apply here
Each case needs examination on its own facts — but jurisdiction challenges and quality of information forming basis are the primary battlegrounds
Arbitrariness = Downfall of Demand
GOOD NEWS Arbitrariness in taxing ingredients (HSN, time of supply, place of supply, etc.) will itself bring about the downfall of the demand in appeal
Best judgment which is not 'unimpeachable' = the greatest resource available to taxpayer in these proceedings
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 3 ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Nazir Ahmad v. King Emperor — AIR 1936 PC 253 (Applied to Sec 63)
Privy Council | 1936
Key Ratio / Principle
Where a power is given to do a certain thing in a certain way, the thing must be done in that way or not at all. Section 63 proceedings must be initiated only via ASMT14 + DRC1 together. Notice without the accompanying DRC1 summary, or DRC1 issued subsequently, is no notice at all in the eyes of law.
When to Use: When ASMT14 is issued without simultaneous DRC1 summary — challenge the notice as fatally defective; power under Sec 63 not exercised in the prescribed way is no power at all.
Principle — Estimates and Guesswork Are the Limitation of Sec 63, Not Its Strength
Sec 63 CGST Act | Rule 100(2)
Key Ratio / Principle
Use of 'estimates and guesswork' is the most telling indication of the distinction of notices under section 63 — which by implication is the most striking limitation of notices issued under section 73 or 74. Best judgment is admittedly not the most accurate method. Where credible information is available, Revenue's interests would be better served by investigation under section 67.
When to Use: To challenge the quantum of demand in ASMT14 — argue that best judgment cannot contain imaginary time and place of supply; any arbitrariness in taxing ingredients will itself bring about the downfall of the demand in appeal.
Principle — Section 63 Cannot Compete with Section 67 When Evasion is Detected
Sec 63 r/w Sec 67 CGST Act | Chapters XII and XIV
Key Ratio / Principle
When ingredients necessary to invoke exceptional powers in section 67 are found to exist, legislative wisdom in permitting use of 'estimates and guesswork' to arrive at tax liability on best judgment basis under section 63 would be a misapplication of law. Where 'evasion of tax' is detected, proceedings under section 67 are appropriate — not section 63.
When to Use: When Revenue has used Sec 63 to avoid securing Joint Commissioner's authorization required under Sec 67 — argue that Proper Officer has misapplied the law by resorting to estimates and guesswork when a more accurate proceeding was available and required.
Principle — Registration Ousts Sec 63 Jurisdiction Immediately
Sec 63 r/w Sec 59 CGST Act
Key Ratio / Principle
No sooner than a specified person obtains registration, Proper Officer loses jurisdiction under section 63. GST is a self-assessment tax regime and pre-emptive power of interference is allowed by Legislature in very few and specific circumstances. To assume legislative oversight and launch unguided investigations would be a direct violation of the express authority conferred on registered taxpayers in section 59.
When to Use: When notice under Sec 63 is issued after the person has already obtained registration, or when pre-registration period is sought to be investigated after registration is granted — argue immediate loss of jurisdiction and violation of Section 59.
Principle — No Investigative Authority Under Sec 63; Taxpayer May Resist u/s 160(2)
Sec 63 r/w Sec 160(2) CGST Act
Key Ratio / Principle
There is nothing in section 63 that authorizes 'investigation'. Omission to confer investigative authority is not due to legislative oversight but deliberate intendment. It would be in accordance with section 160(2) for specified persons to actively disobey any unauthorized investigations attempted and respectfully decline to submit to any directions calling for documents or records.
When to Use: When Proper Officer issues summons or calls for bank statements, IT returns, financial statements, or any pre-registration documents under Sec 63 — cite this principle and section 160(2) to decline compliance; also raise this as a jurisdictional ground in reply to ASMT14.
Principle — Interest and Penalty Not Imposable Under Sec 63; Form ASMT15 Cannot Override Statute
Sec 63 CGST Act | Form ASMT15 | Sec 75(9)
Key Ratio / Principle
Demand for interest and penalty is incongruous with demands based on best judgment determination of 'tax' liability. Not even the saving provision in section 75(9) can permit confirmation of demand for interest or penalty. Form and rule cannot feed the statutory provision in the Act — there is no such authority. The table in Form ASMT15 providing for interest and penalty can itself offer fresh grounds on jurisdiction to be raised in appeal.
When to Use: When Order in ASMT15 demands interest or penalty over and above tax — challenge the demand for interest and penalty as without jurisdiction; form cannot override the statute; raise this as an additional ground in appeal against ASMT15 + DRC7.
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 3 — Notice under Section 63
⚖️ Chapter 4 — Section 73

⚖️ Chapter 4 — Notice Under Section 73

GST Litigation Reference — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
4.1 Overview
4.2 Anatomy of Notice
4.3 Pre-Notice Consultations
4.4 Limitation
4.5 Statement of Demand
4.6 Accompanying Summary
4.7 Service of Notice
4.8 Validity of Service
4.9 Concessional Penalty
4.10 Burden of Proof
4.11 Deficiencies in Notice
4.12 Discrepancies in Notice
4.13 Omission to Object
4.14 Non-Denial = Admission
4.15 Post-Adjudication Penalty
4.16 Interest/Penalty Only Demand
4.1 — Overview : Notice Under Section 73
SECTION 73 — PURPOSE & FRAMEWORK
When Issued
Liability detected & remains undischarged
Scrutiny
Audit
Inquiry
Due Process
Put taxable person at notice of allegations
Permit answering allegations
Fair trial by adjudication
Flow: Part A → Part B
Observation → DRC-1A Part A (demand)
Disputed → SCN + DRC-1
No verbal instructions — only written due process
Taxpayer Safeguards
All rights preserved
All remedies allowed
Section 75 safeguards apply
4.2 — Anatomy of a Notice
NOTICE U/S 73 — ANATOMY
5 Actionable Causes
(a) Tax not paid
(b) Tax short-paid
(c) ITC wrongly taken
(d) ITC wrongly utilized
(e) Refund erroneously sanctioned
NOTE
Interest & penalty ONLY if underlying tax/credit/refund demand exists
Notice Must Contain
Allegations
Evidence in support
All info to inform taxable person
Appropriate cause of action
Quantified demand
Form, Manner & Time
Clear & unambiguous
Issued in prescribed FORM
In prescribed MANNER
Within prescribed TIME
✗ Letter ≠ valid notice
No substitute for prescribed form
5 Anatomy Elements
① Distinct Person
② Impugned Transaction
③ Actionable Cause (in law)
④ Demand (quantified)
⑤ Disposal by adjudication
Cryptic / Deficient Notice
FATAL Sec 160(1) cannot cure incurable notice
2A v 3B mismatch ≠ cause of action (only suspicion)
REMEDY Object to incompleteness — demand dismissal at adjudication or in appeal
4.3 — Pre-Notice Consultations
PRE-NOTICE CONSULTATIONS — SEC 73(5)
Mandate & Purpose
Mandatory before SCN u/s 73(1)
Explore avoiding litigation
Penalty = NIL if liability + interest discharged
If availed → NO notice required at all
Forms Used
DRC-1A Part A
Rule 142(1A) — Brief demand description by Revenue
DRC-1A Part B
Rule 142(2A) — Accept or Reject by Taxpayer
Strategy — Part B Reply
WARN Do NOT submit 20-page reply
Do NOT prematurely disclose defences
Only state: Accept or Reject
Revenue's decision to issue notice already made at Part A stage
Sec 74(5) Trap
ALERT Dept often issues DRC-1A under Sec 74(5) even when Sec 74 not attracted
Taxpayer may discharge tax + interest 'as if' under Sec 73(5) and then agitate for Sec 75(2) relief
Important Rule
Sec 73(5) in statute since 1 Jul 2017
Rule 142(1A) enabled from 9 Oct 2019
Sec 74(5) penalty NOT applicable where Sec 74 preconditions (evasion) absent
4.4 — Limitation
LIMITATION — SEC 73(10)
All 5 Steps Must Conclude Within End Date
① Pre-notice consultations concluded
② Notice served
③ Reply received
④ Personal hearing conducted
⑤ Speaking Order passed
Demand TypeNotice Start DateOrder End Date
Tax not paid / short paid
ITC wrongly taken / utilized		33 months from due date of annual return for FY36 months (3 years) from due date of annual return
Erroneous Refund33 months from date of refund sanction36 months from date of refund sanction
Extended End Dates (Notification 9/2023-CT, 31 Mar 2023)
FY 2017-18
→ 31 Dec 2023
FY 2018-19
→ 31 Mar 2023
FY 2019-20
→ 30 Jun 2024
NOTE No change for Sec 74 or Sec 76
✗ SEC 73 CANNOT BE USED WHEN

Evasion investigated u/s 67 → Must use Sec 74  |  Penalty u/s 122(1)/(3) for unregistered persons  |  Demand period exceeds 3 years prescribed
4.5 — Statement of Demand (SOD)
STATEMENT OF DEMAND — SEC 73(3)
When Used
Same issue / non-compliance exists for subsequent tax period
Operates 'as if' notice u/s 73(1) for that period
What SOD Contains
Reference to earlier SCN
Demand for subsequent tax period
Accompanied by Form GST DRC-2
Mutatis mutandis — all grounds of original SCN apply
Conditions for Valid SOD
Same issue & same grounds as original SCN
Tax periods need NOT be sequential
✗ FATAL SOD adds 'additional grounds' not in original SCN → SOD becomes non-existent
Rights & Limits
No pre-notice consultation required for SOD
DRC-2 accompaniment mandatory
Taxpayer has NO vested right to demand SOD instead of fresh SCN
4.6 — Accompanying Summary (Form DRC-1)
ACCOMPANYING SUMMARY — SINE QUA NON
Mandatory Requirement
SCN u/s 73(1) → Must be with DRC-1
[Rule 142(1)(a)]
SOD u/s 73(3) → Must be with DRC-2
[Rule 142(1)(b)]
Fatal Defects
✗ SCN titled 'DRC-1' ≠ notice + summary (both are independent documents)
✗ Corrigendum "treat DRC-1 as SCN" = still invalid
Rule 142(1) = "along with" — belated summary = fatal (Juhi Industries, Jharkhand HC)
Rule: "Along With"
Cannot be issued subsequently
Not curable by corrigendum or addendum
Mandatory requirements = cannot be treated as directory
4.7 — Service of Notice
SERVICE OF NOTICE — SEC 169
What is Service
NOT mere dispatch of notice
Mode that ENSURES notice actually reaches taxpayer
14 Modes — Sec 169
Must choose most expeditious mode
NOT SUFFICIENT Portal posting / email / SMS alone
Must confirm actual receipt by taxpayer
Non-Service Consequences
Adjudication Order = doubtful validity
'Date of realization' (recovery action) = date of service for appeal limitation
AA cannot dismiss appeal as belated if non-service established
4.8 — Validity of Service
VALIDITY OF SERVICE — MULTIPLE ASPECTS
SEC 160(2) BAR — Taxpayer LOSES right to object to validity if:
(a) Replied on merits of notice  |  (b) Omitted to question legality / validity
6 Aspects for Validity of Service
(a) Valid form / format prescribed
(b) Ingredients satisfied in prescribed form
(c) Issued by authorized Proper Officer
(d) Authorized transaction & tax period
(e) Issued to valid Distinct Person
(f) Served in most expeditious mode
CONSEQUENCE Failure in ANY ONE of above = notice non est in law
STRATEGY Taxpayer must place on record 'admitted date of service' — not date of order. Dismissal of appeal equating date of order = date of service is a question of law agitable up to Tribunal
4.9 — Concessional Penalty
CONCESSIONAL PENALTY — STAGES
STAGE 1 — Pre-Notice
Sec 73(5)
Penalty = NIL
Pay tax + interest only
STAGE 2 — Post-Notice
Sec 73(8)
Pay within 30 days of SCN
Penalty = NIL
STAGE 3 — Post-Adjudication
Sec 73(9)
Penalty = 10% of tax
Min ₹10,000
(Sec 122(2)(a))
STRATEGY Taxpayers often wait for SCN to assess quality/merits before deciding to accept. Stage 2 window (30 days) gives opportunity to evaluate then discharge with NIL penalty.
4.10 — Burden of Proof
BURDEN OF PROOF — SEC 155 + COMMON LAW PRINCIPLES
Statutory Allocation — Sec 155
TAXPAYER
Eligibility of ITC availed
REVENUE
All other matters
7 Common Law Principles Applicable to GST
(a) Sec 56
Official notice of extant law/trade/science taken even if not in SCN
(b) Sec 58
Undisputed facts need no proof
(c) Sec 101
Person making assertion bears burden to prove it
(d) Sec 102
New material → onus shifts to person introducing it
(e) Sec 103
Exemption claim → burden on taxpayer with special knowledge
(f) Sec 24-32
Oath statement on facts = presumption in favour of Revenue
BUT NOT on interpretation of law
(g) Sec 24-32
Admission before GST Officer NOT protected by self-incrimination rule
(h) Sec 114 ill.(e) — Dept actions presumed bona fide BUT must be tested for jurisdiction validity
4.11 — Deficiencies in Notice (Non-Compliance with Due Process)
⛔ Fatal Deficiencies⚠️ Non-Fatal Deficiencies
Officer is NOT Proper Officer to issue SCNDRC-1A not issued (only in offence cases u/s 132)
SCN issued before date of authorization conferring authorityForm of DRC-1 varies
DRC-1A proceedings omittedSCN invokes section with cause of action but does not specify exact sub-section
SCN not accompanied by DRC-1Documents relied upon are authored by taxpayer, not secured independently
SCN only asserts violation — does NOT state cause of actionDocuments relied upon not disclosed or furnished
Service of SCN not valid—
KEY Acquiescence = forfeiture of remedy. Object in earliest proceedings. Sec 160(2) bars later objections if taxpayer replied on merits.
4.12 — Discrepancies in Notice (Fatalities Within an Otherwise Valid Notice)
⛔ Fatal Discrepancies⚠️ Non-Fatal Discrepancies
Valid SCN issued pursuant to invalid proceedingsSCN issued under "section 73/74" when allegations relate to 'evasion of tax'
SCN not descriptive of allegations AND contains no discernible reference to any section in lawSCN not descriptive of allegations but makes reference to Sec 16(2) for credit mismatch
SCN issued under Sec 31, 35, etc. where non-compliance has occurred (wrong section)SCN describes cause of action sufficiently to put taxpayer at notice
SCN issued under Sec 65—
RULE Discrepancies cannot be furnished by Adjudicating Authority due to Sec 75(7) safeguards. SCN on incorrect provision cannot be corrected — must be dropped (Sec 161 + 75(7)).
4.13 — Omission to Object
OMISSION TO OBJECT — CONSEQUENCES
DANGER
Taxpayer cannot assume relevant provisions — must object
Objecting late (at Tribunal/HC) shows bad faith
RULE
Object to discrepancies BEFORE replying on merits
Rule 112 only bars new 'evidence' — NOT new grounds
ANXIETY
Fear: Revenue may withdraw and reissue within limitation
SCN on incorrect provisions cannot be corrected u/s 161 — must drop demand
PRINCIPLE
Adversarial proceedings — interplay of allegation and defence
Due process sets course — taxpayer has statutory opportunity to guide proceedings
4.14 — Non-Denial Implies Admission
NON-DENIAL = ADMISSION?
RISK
Silence during response period = 'no contest' inference
Slowly appearing in Adjudication Orders
RULE
Express denial is best. Clear, unambiguous denial for each allegation
Politeness ≠ non-denial only if unambiguous
WHAT CAN BE ADMITTED
Facts only (act or omission)
✗ CANNOT ADMIT
Interpretation of facts
Tax treatment
Interpretation of law
LIMITS
Non-denial ≠ admission when allegations not supported by evidence
Taxpayer can 'opt out' from claiming eligible credits but CANNOT consent to denial of credit
4.15 — Post-Adjudication Penalty
POST-ADJUDICATION PENALTY — SEC 73(9)
LOSS OF CONCESSION
If taxpayer does NOT carry demand in appeal after adverse order → Sec 73(5)/(8) concession LOST
PENALTY QUANTUM
Max 10% of tax (Sec 122(2)(a))
Min ₹10,000
GAP IN LAW Sec 122(2)(a) uses "tax due" — no specific provision for credit or refund penalty
SEC 73 vs 74
Sec 73 = notice provision only
Penalty LEVIED by Sec 122(2)(a)
Sec 73(11) penalty applies where default ≤ 30 days and price is 'all inclusive'
ARGUMENT
Challenge penalty on credit/refund — no express statutory basis in Sec 73(1) or 122(2)(a)
4.16 — Demand for Interest or Penalty Only
INTEREST / PENALTY ONLY DEMAND — CONSTRUCT
RULE
No standalone demand for interest or penalty alone without primary demand for tax/credit/refund
APPROACH
Deposit-Demand-Appropriation
(a) Issue notice demanding tax
(b) Acknowledge payment as 'deposit'
(c) Demand interest & penalty
(d) Appropriate deposit against dues on adjudication
TRANSITION CREDIT
Interest on ineligible transition credit
Sec 50(1) = unpaid tax
Sec 50(3) = credit wrongly utilized
Transition credit = neither of above → No legislative mechanism to demand interest → Demand must FAIL
ARGUMENT
Sec 2(62)-(63) — transition credit NOT listed as input tax credit
Cannot treat as ITC for interest but not for other purposes — no express fiction in law
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 4 — Notice Under Section 73
⚖️ Chapter 5 — Section 74

⚖️ Chapter 5 — Notice under Section 74

GST Litigation Reference — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
5.1 Overview
5.2 Special Circumstances
5.3 Anatomy of a Notice
5.4 Exception to Pre-Notice Consultations
5.5 Pre-Notice Penalty
5.6 Post-Notice Penalty
5.7 Post-Adjudication Penalty
5.8 Additional Aspect About Evasion
5.9 Disputed Tax Period
5.10 Implication of Cum Tax Demand
5.11 Circulars and Instructions
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
5.1 — Overview
NOTICE U/S 74 — OVERVIEW; SPECIAL CIRCUMSTANCES; SELF-ASSESSMENT BASIS OF GST
Core Principle
RULE S.74 invoked ONLY when 'special circumstances' exist — S.73 & S.74 are mutually exclusive; no surplusage in legislative drafting
✗ NEVER No demand permitted on verbal instructions under any provision
S.73 & S.74 cannot overlap or be applied interchangeably — circumstances of invocation are clearly legislated
DRC1A → SCN Flow
Observation in (i) Scrutiny (ii) Audit (iii) Review or (iv) Inquiry
→ Demand in DRC1A Part A
→ Demand rejected in Part B
→ SCN + DRC1 Issued
Start Date of LIS
GST Self-assessment regime — lis commences when taxpayer first discloses tax position in earliest document filed
NOT WHEN Proper Officer first came to know of taxpayer's position
START DATE Earliest document filed under GST — not date of cognizance by Revenue
End Date — Crystallisation
END DATE Law declares end date — Proper Officer must initiate, convene & conclude findings within time
Taxpayer: accept & discharge OR agitate in appeal
CONSEQUENCE Appeal omitted → demand 'crystallised' → falls into recovery
5.2 — Special Circumstances
SPECIAL CIRCUMSTANCES — THREE LISTED, TWO BY OPERATION OF OXFORD COMMA; REVENUE'S FIVE-STEP BURDEN
Oxford Comma — Three Becomes Two
GRAMMAR Comma placed after "fraud" before "or" → fraud ring-fenced in its own class
No comma between "wilful-misstatement" & "suppression" → one common class; both require end objective: to evade tax
(a) FRAUD — standalone; end objective irrelevant once established
(b) Wilful-misstatement OR Suppression — both require "to evade tax" as end objective
FRAUD — Defined
(i) Something believed to be untrue is carried out
(ii) If disclosed — would alter others' opinion of the transaction
(iii) Contemporaneously recorded in altered form
(iv) Overlaid/presented to go undetected under normal scrutiny
KEY Error ≠ Fraud. Fraud requires actions contrary to belief
Wilful-Misstatement & Suppression
WILFUL-MISSTATEMENT Assert transaction as one thing when in fact another; apply tax treatment on incorrect assertion — no device to conceal
SUPPRESSION Knowing prescribed disclosure will not procure advantage → something done to avoid transparent disclosure
≠ Disclosing a substitute = wilful-misstatement, NOT suppression
Revenue's Five-Step Burden
(i) Identify ONE special circumstance
(ii) Choose that ONE
(iii) Collect evidence for chosen one
(iv) Make election in notice
(v) Support with clinching evidence
FATAL Invoking 'all' / 'any' = non-application of mind → remedy u/s 75(7)
Special Exclusions from S.74
PSU S.74 SCN cannot issue to PSUs — end objective of evasion cannot be established (savings go to Consolidated Fund)
DIVERGENT VIEWS Where Tribunals / Courts expressed divergent views — S.74 SCN cannot be sustained
SOURCE Revenue using taxpayer's own records to canvass alternate interpretation → not compatible with 'special circumstances'
Rule of Last Antecedent (RLA)
RLA Normally 'words of qualification' apply only to immediately preceding word in statute
BUT Oxford comma after 'fraud' overrides RLA — "to evade tax" extends to BOTH wilful-misstatement AND suppression
PRINCIPLE No rule of statutory construction is inalienable from its context — Ref: Justice GP Singh
5.3 — Anatomy of a Notice
ANATOMY OF NOTICE U/S 74 — JURISDICTION, ONE-WAY DEEMING FICTION, LIMITATION & SOD RULE
Why S.74 Notice is Mandatory
PURPOSE Procures jurisdiction to raise demand in 'special circumstances' + additional time + statutory minimum penalty
CONTRADICTION Special circumstances alleged BUT notice issued u/s 73 → grave contradiction; clear misapplication of law
CONTRADICTION Notice issued u/s 73 pursuant to investigation u/s 67 → contradictory
TAXPAYER Where notice is u/s 73 despite special circumstances → assail validity; inherent admission of 'no evasion'
S.75(2) — One-Way Relief Only
S.75(2) S.74 notice not sustained → deemed as if S.73 notice; taxpayer gets full S.73 relief
CONVERSE FALSE No provision to deem S.73 notice as S.74 notice if special circumstances emerge later in proceedings
EMBARGO S.75(7) + 2nd Proviso S.107(11) → authority CANNOT enhance liability beyond notice
CANNOT Omission to issue S.74 notice cannot be cured by S.75(2)
Limitation Table — S.74(2) & S.74(10)
DemandNotice StartOrder End
Tax not paid / short paid / credit wrong54 mts. from due date of annual return60 mts. (5 yrs)
Erroneous refund54 mts. from date of refund sanction60 mts. from refund sanction
NOTE No extension u/s 168A for S.74 notices for FY 2017-18
KEY S.74 can also be issued WITHIN 3 years of relevant date — if special circumstances exist; extended period is not the only trigger
SOD — S.74(4) Special Rule
S.74(4) SOD issued u/s 74(3) for subsequent period (where earlier notice was u/s 74) → deemed demand u/s 73
BENEFIT All relief under S.73(8) and S.73(11) applies to this SOD even though issued with reference to S.74
CAUTION Do not overlook this difference — especially when S.74(1) notice and S.74(3) SOD are passed in same proceeding
5.4 — Exception to Pre-Notice Consultations
PRE-NOTICE CONSULTATIONS — APPLICABLE EVEN FOR S.74; TWO EXCEPTIONS CARVED OUT IN RULES
General Rule
RULE Pre-notice consultations apply EVEN for S.74 notices — no assumption of guilt inherent in 'due process'
S.73(5) & S.74(5) — both require pre-notice consultations; Rule 142(1A) amendment ('shall' → 'may', Oct 2020) does NOT eclipse this statutory requirement
RULE 142(1A) 'Shall communicate' → 'May communicate' from 15 Oct 2020; amendment in rules does not override statute
Exception 1 — Untraceable Offenders
SITUATION Taxpayers not traceable after committing offences punishable u/s 132
Mandatory DRC1A would allow racketeers to evade consultations then claim bypass in later proceedings
EXCEPTION Rules amended to allow discretion ('may') — sufficient leeway already in statute
Exception 2 — Erroneous Refund Recovery
SITUATION Refund sanctioned pending in departmental appeal; notice issued for recovery via 'dual notice procedure'
Taxpayer has opposed the departmental appeal → pre-notice consultations = empty formality
RESULT Notice for erroneous refund can be issued without pre-notice consultations — whether u/s 74 or 73
5.5 — Pre-Notice Penalty
PRE-NOTICE PENALTY — S.74(5) + DRC1A; DANGER OF PAYMENT WITHOUT FORM DRC1A
Pre-Notice Settlement — DRC1A
PROCESS Revenue must issue Form GST DRC1A before notice — taxpayer given opportunity to discharge
BENEFIT Discharge: Full demand + Full interest + 15% penalty → proceedings concluded
S.73 In S.73 proceedings — pre-notice penalty = NIL (not 15%)
⚠ Danger — Payment Without DRC1A
PERIL Officers insist payment via DRC3 at 15% penalty without issuing DRC1A — misleads taxpayer into believing proceedings concluded
RISK Without DRC1A — finality u/s 74(6) NOT available — demand can be revived via S.74(7) citing non-discharge of applicable penalty
REMEDY S.75(2) relief available if special circumstances held unsustainable in later proceedings
5.6 — Post-Notice Penalty
POST-NOTICE PENALTY — S.74(8) — 30-DAY WINDOW; PENALTY COMPARISON AT ALL THREE STAGES
30-Day Window After Notice
S.74(8) 30-day window after notice — discharge Full demand + Full interest + 25% penalty → proceedings concluded
STRATEGY Notices with deficiencies are fatal to demand — examine grounds before deciding to discharge
Penalty at All Three Stages
StageDemandInterestS.74 %S.73 %
Pre-notice (DRC1A)FullFull15%NIL
Post-notice (30 days)FullFull25%NIL
Post-adjudicationFullFull50%10%
Judicial Question on Percentages
QUESTION Whether statutory percentages bind Courts in quasi-judicial adjudication — yet to be settled
POSITION Limits appear binding on Revenue — cannot demand MORE than specified when payment made at appropriate stage of proceeding
Tactical Option
STRATEGY Discharge dues and penalty up to S.73 limit (at appropriate stage) AND contest S.74 notice — seeking relief u/s 75(2) if special circumstances not sustained
5.7 — Post-Adjudication Penalty
POST-ADJUDICATION PENALTY — S.74(9) / S.74(11); MINIMUM 25%, MAXIMUM 100%; S.74(11) REDUCED PENALTY
Adjudication Requirements
REQUIREMENT Existence of special circumstances must be accepted in adjudication before demand is confirmed on merits
PENALTY LEVY Penalty for special circumstances under S.122(2)(b) — NOT under S.74(1) itself
RANGE Min = 25% per S.74(8) | Max = 100% per S.122(2)(b)
QUASI-JUDICIAL Proper Officer determines penalty quantum independently — NOT 'acting under dictation'
S.74(11) — Reduced Penalty After Order
S.74(11) Deposit within 30 days of Order communication → pay 50% of DEMAND as penalty (NOT 50% abatement of penalty in Order)
Leviable Max.AdjudicatedReduced (S.74(11))
10010050 ✓
1006050 ✓
10040NA ✗
NOTE Deposit to ECL alone does NOT suffice — amount must be appropriated against dues admitted
S.67 Investigation — Key Signal
CONTRA-INDICATOR Where inspection / search & seizure conducted u/s 67 — issuing notice u/s 73 is a contra-indicator of jurisdiction to undertake S.67 proceedings
5.8 — Additional Aspect About Evasion
EVASION — FOUR INGREDIENTS NOTICE MUST ESTABLISH; BONA FIDE BELIEF ≠ MALA FIDE INTENT; ANIMUS BAKED IN
Four Ingredients of Evasion
(a) Non-payment of tax
(b) Awareness of existence of liability
(c) Device employed to conceal detection
(d) Gains derived from misadventure
CRITICAL Notice must verifiably SHOW benefit derived — mere allegation insufficient
