PRINCIPLE Evasion without motivation = foolishness, not mischief
Non-Collection Defence
IF Notice does not allege tax collected from customers → Revenue must demonstrate HOW evasion perpetuated
BENEFIT 1 Cum-tax computation under Rule 35 becomes available
BENEFIT 2 Non-collection + non-payment = no gains → bona fide belief in non-taxability admitted → notice relegated to S.73
Bona Fide Belief ≠ Mala Fide
PRINCIPLE Bona fide belief (even if wrong) ≠ wilful mischief — taxpayer can be wrong and still bona fide
Courts themselves revise earlier views — pursuing incorrect interpretation is not always mala fide
TEST View plausible + honestly applied with full disclosure → evasion allegation cannot easily sustain
BOLAM TEST Even if no prudent person would entertain such view — still fails to satisfy 'special circumstances' for S.74
Animus — Intent Baked In
KEY Animus (intent to evade) is baked into 'evasion of tax' — cannot be proved by presumption or assumed in every S.74 case
"Being stupid is not violation of law"
EXAMPLE RCM on ocean freight — settled by Apex Court (UOI v. Mohit Minerals) → SCN must be u/s 73, NOT S.74
EXAMPLE ITC on construction services — Safari Retreats (Ori. HC) → S.74 not sustainable even if taxpayer disagreed
5.9 — Disputed Tax Period
DISPUTED TAX PERIOD — SELF-ASSESSMENT QUA TAX PERIOD; ADMISSION TRAP IN REPLY; S.41 OLD / NEW
Core Rule — Tax Period Specificity
S.2(106) Demand in GST = per 'tax period' — NOT financial year or block of tax periods
S.59 Self-assessment mandated qua each tax period — merging of tax periods unknown in GST
TRAP Stating liability discharged in Tax Period 5 = admission that Tax Period 1 liability was left undischarged
Admission Danger in Reply
Disclosure that arrears discharged in later period = admission of undischarged liability for disputed tax period
Interest compensation becomes due to Revenue for the delay in discharge
RULE Reply to notice must focus only on the specific 'tax period' for which demand is made
Pre / Post 1 Oct 2022 — S.41 Change
UP TO 30 SEPT 2022 S.41-Old — availment AND utilization of ITC circumscribed; taxpayer must mind 'identity' of tax period
FROM 1 OCT 2022 S.41-New — concerns only 'availment'; utilization governed entirely by S.49; sea change in position
Circular 172/4/2022-GST Para 6 — clarifies ECrL utilization across tax periods in self-assessment and proceedings
S.49 — Ledger Framework (Key)
SectionOperation
S.49(1)Cash → ECL
S.49(2)Credit in 3B → ECrL
S.49(3)ECL → any liability
S.49(4)ECrL → output tax
S.49(5)Order of utilization in ECrL
S.49(8)Order of settlement: arrears, current, demands
PRINCIPLE S.49 permits cross-business utilization — no 'one-to-one' correlation required in GST
5.10 — Implication of 'Cum Tax' Demand
CUM TAX DEMAND — IMPACT ON SPECIAL CIRCUMSTANCES; WHEN AVAILABLE; NOTICE FAILURE FROM OMISSION
Two Critical Questions
Tax collected from customers but not discharged?
Tax NOT collected AND not discharged?
KEY Absence of allegation of non-collection = important defence opportunity
RESULT Non-collection + non-payment = no gains to taxpayer = contra-indicator of evasion intent
Cum-Tax Computation — When Available
CONDITION Available ONLY IF on record that price is inclusive of all taxes — NOT automatic
STRONG All-inclusive price apparent on record → omitting cum-tax = 'error apparent on record' → correctable u/s 161 without full appeal
IN GST Only cum-tax computation survives — ITC restoration NOT available due to S.16(2)(d) affirmative action requirement
Notice Failure — Cum-Tax Omission
FATAL OMISSION Notice omitting cum-tax computation = Revenue's own assertion that benefit accruing to taxpayer was not revealed in inquiry
CAUTION Contract stating 'GST exempt' but turns out taxable → cum-tax NOT available (price = whole transaction value)
Supplier may recoup under S.64A of Sale of Goods Act — but mere non-inclusion of tax ≠ entitlement to cum-tax
5.11 — Circulars and Instructions
IMPORTANT CIRCULARS — DEMAND & RECOVERY JURISPRUDENCE; PARI MATERIA PROVISIONS
(a) Master Circular
Circular 1053/2/2017-CX dated 10 Mar 2017
Central Government's acceptance of judicial authorities under pre-GST central laws
Provisions pari materia with Chapter XII (Demand & Recovery) of GST Act — carry same judicial interpretation
(b) & (c) Amending Circulars
Amending Circular 1076/2/2020-CX dated 19 Nov 2021
Amending Circular 1079/3/2021-CX dated 11 Nov 2021
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 5 ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
UOI v. Mohit Minerals (P) Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | [2022] 138 taxmann.com 331 / 61 GSTL 257 / 92 GST 101 (SC)
Key Ratio / Principle
Where the law on a point has been settled by the Apex Court in favour of the taxpayer (here: RCM on ocean freight), demand must be raised under Section 73 and NOT under Section 74. A settled legal position — even if previously contested — cannot form the basis of 'special circumstances' for invoking Section 74.
When to Use: When S.74 SCN is issued on a matter settled by Apex Court or High Court in taxpayer's favour — challenge S.74 as jurisdictionally void; the precondition of 'special circumstances' cannot exist where a court has upheld the taxpayer's position.
Safari Retreats (P) Ltd. v. Chief Commissioner — CGST
Orissa High Court | [2019] 105 taxmann.com 324 / 74 GST 500 / 25 GSTL 341 (Ori.)
Key Ratio / Principle
Where a taxpayer entertains a view that is supported by a High Court judgment — even if the taxpayer does not personally agree with it — Section 74 SCN cannot be sustained. A view sanctioned by judicial authority, whatever the outcome on further appeal, negates the ingredient of 'special circumstances' required to invoke Section 74.
When to Use: When Revenue alleges wilful-misstatement or suppression where a High Court judgment exists supporting the taxpayer's position — argue that judicial sanction of the view negates the 'special circumstances' ingredient at the very threshold.
Principle — Bolam's Test of Reasonableness Applied to GST Evasion
Legal Doctrine — Applied to S.74 CGST Act by Analogy
Key Ratio / Principle
Even a view entertained by a taxpayer which no prudent person might entertain (read Bolam's test of reasonableness) would still fail to satisfy the 'special circumstances' needed to support a demand under Section 74. Being stupid is not a violation of law. Imprudent interpretation is not the same as fraudulent intent or wilful mischief.
When to Use: When Revenue argues the taxpayer's position was 'implausible' or 'unreasonable' — invoke Bolam's test to show that even an unreasonable but honest view does not satisfy the mental element of evasion required by S.74.
Principle — Rule of Last Antecedent (RLA) + Oxford Comma Doctrine
Canon of Statutory Construction | Ref: Justice GP Singh — Principles of Statutory Interpretation
Key Ratio / Principle
The oxford comma after 'fraud' removes fraud from the operation of the Rule of Last Antecedent — thereby making the end objective 'to evade tax' applicable to both wilful-misstatement and suppression. A mere wilful-misstatement without the end objective of tax evasion cannot satisfy the requirements of Section 74. No rule of statutory construction is inalienable from its context.
When to Use: When Revenue alleges wilful-misstatement alone without pleading or proving the end objective of evasion — argue the notice lacks the essential jurisdictional ingredient; demand relief u/s 75(7) for non-application of mind.
Principle — Section 75(2) One-Way Deeming Fiction; No Reverse Operation
S.75(2) CGST Act, 2017 | S.75(7) | Second Proviso to S.107(11)
Key Ratio / Principle
Section 75(2) allows a Section 74 notice to be treated as a Section 73 notice if special circumstances are not sustained in later proceedings. But the converse is not true — there is no provision that deems a Section 73 notice to be a Section 74 notice if special circumstances come to light later. There is an express embargo in section 75(7) and the second proviso to section 107(11) against enhancing liability beyond that originally proposed in the notice.
When to Use: (a) To obtain S.73 relief even after S.74 notice fails on special circumstances — invoke S.75(2) to have demand recomputed as if under S.73. (b) To resist any attempt by Revenue to upgrade a S.73 notice to S.74 in adjudication or appellate proceedings.
Principle — Section 75(7) Remedy for Non-Application of Mind; Omnibus Invocation Fatal
S.75(7) CGST Act, 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
Revenue must identify the specific special circumstance, choose one, collect evidence for that one, make an election to allege it in the notice, and support it with clinching evidence. Failure at any of these five steps entitles the taxpayer to remedy under Section 75(7). Revenue cannot invoke 'all' of these special circumstances or 'any' of them in omnibus fashion — the ingredients for fraud are vastly different from those for wilful-misstatement and suppression, and each requires entirely different evidence.
When to Use: Whenever SCN invokes fraud + wilful-misstatement + suppression simultaneously in a kitchen-sink approach — invoke S.75(7); taxpayer cannot be left to disprove all alternate arguments; non-application of mind as to which specific circumstance is alleged is itself fatal to the demand.
Principle — Section 74(4): SOD Always Deemed Section 73; Concessional Relief Preserved
S.74(3) / S.74(4) / S.73(8) / S.73(11) CGST Act, 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
A Statement of Demand issued under Section 74(3) in respect of a subsequent period, where an earlier notice was issued under Section 74, is deemed to be a demand under Section 73 as per Section 74(4). All attendant relief under Section 73(8) and Section 73(11) — including NIL penalty at pre-notice stage and 10% penalty at post-adjudication stage — applies to such SOD, even though it is issued with reference to a Section 74(1) notice.
When to Use: When both a Section 74(1) notice and a Section 74(3) SOD are issued in the same proceedings — separately claim Section 73 benefits (NIL penalty / 10% penalty) for the SOD portion; do not allow Revenue to treat the SOD as attracting S.74 penalty rates.
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 5 — Notice under Section 74
⚖️ Chapter 6 — Section 74A

⚖️ Chapter 6 — Notice under New Section 74A

GST Litigation Reference — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

6.2 Terminal Date for Old Notice of Demand

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
6.1 — Date of Commencement
SECTION 74A — BRAND NEW PROVISION; THREE CONDITIONS FOR OPERATIONALISATION; CAUSE-OF-ACTION TEST
Three Conditions to Operationalise
(i) Central Act enacted
(ii) ALL State Acts also enacted
(iii) Notified on a COMMON date
SCOPE Applies to demands "pertaining to" FY 2024-25 onwards — cause-of-action must arise in FY 2024-25
Cause-of-Action Test — Which Section?
RULE Cause-of-action "pertains to" FY 2023-24 or earlier → S.73 or S.74 continues; CANNOT switch to S.74A
RULE Cause-of-action arises in FY 2024-25 or later → S.74A applies; notice may be issued much later than within FY 2024-25
KEY WORDS "Tax pertaining to" inserted in S.73(12) and S.74(12) — particularly illuminating for transition
Misapplication — Fatal Consequence
FATAL S.74A notice issued for demand "pertaining to" FY 2023-24 or earlier = disastrous misapplication of law
S.75(10) being unaffected by S.74A — any misapplication is fatal to the demand
TAXPAYER Satisfy yourself as to correct applicability of S.74A at earliest opportunity — do not cause prejudice to your own case
Examples from Text
EXAMPLE 1 Output tax not paid in FY 2019-20 — S.74 SCN by 30 Sept 2025 — must NOT be issued under S.74A ("pertains to" FY 2019-20)
EXAMPLE 2 Erroneous refund sanctioned Dec 2019 — S.74 SCN by 30 Jun 2024 (54 mts. from refund order) — must NOT be issued under S.74A
6.2 — Terminal Date for Old Notice of Demand
S.73 AND S.74 NOTICES CONTINUE EVEN AFTER FY 2024-25 — PARALLEL OPERATION WITH S.74A
Parallel Operation — Old & New
KEY RULE S.73 and S.74 remain in statute — issued even after FY 2024-25 for cause-of-action "pertaining to" FY 2023-24 or earlier
UNAFFECTED Limitation, concessional penalties (pre and post notice), all protections continue unabated for old notices
CRITICAL Locate the 'terminal date' for each old notice under S.73/S.74 — issuing under wrong provision is disastrous to the demand
No Switch to S.74A
RULE Where cause-of-action "pertains to" FY 2023-24 or earlier — notice of demand will NOT switch to S.74A soon after new section comes into effect
TAXPAYER Revenue's plea of acquiescence by taxpayer may not succeed if misapplication of law is raised in Court — challenge at earliest opportunity
6.3 — Ingredients to Jurisdiction
S.74A vs S.73 vs S.74 — COMPARISON; ONLY LIMITATION DIFFERS; NEARLY NOTHING NEW
Basic Ingredients Comparison
DescriptionS.73S.74S.74A
Demand for tax, refund or creditYESYESYES
Special circumstances applyNOYESBOTH
Statement-of-demand allowedYESYESYES
Limitation & Penalty Comparison
DescriptionS.73S.74S.74A
Limitation 1 — issue SCN33 mts.54 mts.42 mts.
Extension of limitation 1NO*NO*NA
Limitation 2 — pass Orders36 mts.60 mts.54 mts.
Extension of limitation 2NONOYES, 6 mts.
Pre-notice consultationYESYESYES
Pre-notice concessional penaltyYESYESYES
Post-notice concessional penaltyYESYESYES
Post-notice limitation30 days30 days60 days
Post-order concessional penaltyYESYESYES
Post-order limitation30 days30 days60 days
* Special extension under S.168A excepted for S.73/S.74
Key Insight — What is New?
INSIGHT Except for limitation numbers — S.74A has nearly nothing that distinguishes it from S.73/S.74
The only plausible justification for introduction = 42-month limitation — applicable to demands that would otherwise have fit within S.73
REALITY With expanded definition of "suppression" — every FY 2024-25 onwards notice will anyway be issued under S.74A with allegation of special circumstances
RELIEF S.75(2) will also apply to demand under S.74A — taxpayer can claim read-down to regular demand
6.4 — New Limitation to Issue Notice and Conclude Adjudication
LIMITATION FRAMEWORK — 42 MONTHS FORWARD; 12 MONTHS TO ADJUDICATE; JC EXTENSION; VESTED RIGHT ON LAPSE
Forward vs Backward Counting
S.73/S.74 Limitation 'worked BACKWARD' — last date to conclude adjudication is fixed; issue-notice date derived from that end date
S.74A(2) Limitation 'worked FORWARD' — 42-month period counted from date cause-of-action accrues (date of commencement of lis)
RESULT Proper Officer need NOT wait near end of 42 months — may issue notice as soon as enquiry is sufficient to carry demand into adjudication
S.74A(7) — 12-Month Adjudication Limit
S.74A(7) 12 months from date of notice → complete adjudication; each notice has its OWN individual deadline
BENEFIT Eliminates urgent disposal of notices due to delay in issuing — unlike S.73/S.74 where delay caused compressed adjudication time
BURDEN Proper Officer must now track due date for every INDIVIDUAL notice — not one collective end date per FY
JC Extension — Conditions & Challenge
EXTENSION Only by Joint Commissioner or higher rank — max. 6 months additional; reasons must be recorded in writing on file
Grant of extension is justiciable — can be challenged in a Court; not included in S.121 as non-appealable decisions
RTI File noting by PO (reasons for delay) + JC (satisfaction for extension) are 'public record' under RTI Act — can be demanded by taxpayer
DANGER Any ante dating of extension request will be exposed when files called by JC — back-dating never supported
Vested Right on Lapse of Limitation
S.75(10) Delay in completing adjudication = fatal to demand; vested right accrues to taxpayer on lapse of limitation
Neither a willing taxpayer nor a lethargic one can acquiesce to time-barred adjudication
REMEDY Order passed beyond limitation without justifiable extension → S.161 rectification + S.54 refund of any payment/recovery
TAXPAYER Monitor lapse of limitation qua each notice — in appeal, verify limitation counting after excluding call-book period
6.5 — No New Ingredients
EXACTLY SAME SPECIAL CIRCUMSTANCES IN S.74A(5) — FOUR-FOLD EVASION TEST; ACCEPT-REJECT-REPLACE APPROACH
S.74A(5) — Two Clauses
CLAUSE (i) Demand in regular circumstances — without special circumstances
CLAUSE (ii) Demand in special circumstances — fraud / wilful-misstatement / suppression to evade tax
SAME Special circumstances are EXACTLY same as in S.74 — entire discussion from Ch. 5 applies here
Four-Fold Evasion Test (Recap)
(i) Non-payment of tax
(ii) Knowledge of liability to pay
(iii) Active concealment to evade detection
(iv) Gains from misadventure
RULE Non-payment without gains from misadventure will NOT tantamount to evasion
Examples — Evasion vs Not Evasion
EVASION Collect 18% and pay only 12% — IS evasion of tax
EVASION RREP project claiming ITC and utilizing the same — IS evasion
NOT EVASION Claiming Safari credits — NOT evasion (HC decision of Ori. HC supports this view)
NOT EVASION Omission to include non-Government subsidy in transaction value — NOT evasion (even if price advantage helped secure contract)
ACCEPT-REJECT-REPLACE Approach
APPROACH Even in notice of demand under S.74A(5) — allegations and interpretation of law must be met with "ACCEPT-REJECT-REPLACE" approach
Without evasion — underlying proceedings cannot be carried out by Officers under S.67 but only by Proper Officer under S.61 or S.65
6.6 — Discharge of Demand
DISCHARGE OF DEMAND UNDER S.74A — PRE-NOTICE, POST-NOTICE & POST-ORDER PENALTY TABLE
Pre-Notice Stage — S.74A(8)/(9)(i)
MANDATED Pre-notice consultations required even under S.74A(8)/(9) — mimics S.73/S.74 with minor relaxations
DescriptionRegular DemandSpecial Circumstances
Pre-notice consultationALLOWEDALLOWED
Pre-notice penalty & limitation0% [S.74A(8)(i)]15% [S.74A(9)(i)]
Post-Notice & Post-Order Stage
DescriptionRegular DemandSpecial Circumstances
Post-notice penalty & limitation0% in 60 days [S.74A(8)(ii)]25% in 60 days [S.74A(9)(ii)]
Post-order penalty & limitation10%* in 30 days [S.74A(5)(i) & 74A(11)]50% in 60 days [S.74A(9)(iii)]
* minimum of ₹10,000 each
60-Day Window — Key Change
BENEFIT Post-notice and post-order windows extended to 60 days under S.74A — was 30 days under S.73/S.74
RULE 142 Amendment in Rule 142(1)(a)/(b)/(1A) required to give effect to issue pre-notice and post-notice summary of demand on Common Portal
6.7 — Same Situations, Same Limitation and Same Penalty
NO NEW AMENDMENT TO S.122(2) — PENALTY QUANTUM ALREADY IN S.74A(8)/(9)/(11); JUDICIAL QUESTION ON DICTATED PENALTIES
No New S.122(2) Needed
PRINCIPLE No amendment proposed to S.122(2) — penalty quantum already specified in S.74A(8)/(9)/(11)
S.122(2) will continue to furnish power of imposition to S.74A — it will NOT become obsolete after terminal date of S.73/S.74
NOTE S.74A(8)/(9) only GUIDE adjudication as to quantum of penalty — actual power of imposition still resides in S.122(2)
Judicial Question — Dictated Penalties
QUESTION Whether Courts can impose penalty BELOW statutory percentages — yet to be settled
Apex Court in State of Gujarat v. Saw Pipes Ltd. (2023) 5 Centax 205 (SC) — judiciary precluded from determining penalties specified in statute
OPEN Remains to be seen if Apex Court will revisit — equality is not treating everyone equally; treating equals, equally; one-size-fits-all may rub against equality before law
6.8 — Order of Adjudication
S.74A(1)/(3) NOTICES ADJUDICATED UNDER S.74A(6) BY SPEAKING ORDER; S.75 SAFEGUARDS APPLY
Adjudication Requirement
S.74A(6) Notice of demand under S.74A(1)/(3) must be adjudicated by a speaking order
SAME GUIDANCE All guidance on examining ingredients needed to substantiate allegations and locate incurable fatalities applies to S.74A notices too
PNJ Principles of natural justice + rights, remedies and safeguards in S.75 are applicable to notice of demand under S.74A
S.75 Safeguards — Applicability to S.74A
S.75SafeguardS.74A
(1) Stay by CourtLimitation excludes stay periodMade applicable
(2) Read-down SCN to S.73Limitation and penalty alteredVia S.74A(2A)
(3) Directions to POCompliance within 2 yearsNot applicable
(4) Grant hearingAudi alteram partemIn S.74A(6)
(5) AdjournmentSame as aboveInherent right
(6) Speaking orderSame as aboveIn S.74A(6)
(7) Demand confined to SCNSafeguard vs refinement of SCNInherent right
(9) Penalty explicitInterest is automaticBuilt-in S.74A(8)/(9)
(10) Limitation to adjudicateLapse fatal to demandMade applicable
(11) Call-bookPower to maintain call-bookMade applicable
(12) Recovery of arrearsSystem-generated noticesMade applicable
(13) Multiplicity of penaltiesRemoves aggravated penaltiesMade applicable
* See Circular 185/17/2022-GST dated 27 Dec 2022
6.9 — Definition of Suppression
SUPPRESSION — EXPANDED DEFINITION TRANSPLANTED FROM S.74 EXPLANATION 2 TO S.74A; LIMITS OF EXPANSION
Definition — Transplanted
CHANGE Definition of "suppression" in Explanation 2 to S.74 — proposed to be omitted and transplanted by way of Explanation after S.74A
Effective date of S.74A = effective date of omission of current definition in S.74
PRESERVED Super-added ingredient "to evade tax" continues in S.74A — no expansive language can shift burden of proof from Revenue to taxpayer
Ingredients & Limits of Expanded Definition
IngredientImpactLimitation
Non-declaration in Returns / Statements / Report / Other docUnrectified errors → presumption of suppressionBona fide belief in correctness will mitigate presumption
Inaccurate statementUnder S.61 and S.65 (but NOT S.67-70)Defence in post-notice stage cannot alter SCN
Report (S.150)Information prepared under say S.150—
Other document furnishedMisrepresentation in pre-notice stage or system generated noticesDefence in post-notice stage cannot alter SCN
Failure to furnish info in writing asked by POOmission to appear/record statement under S.70IO is NOT Proper Officer under S.67-70; section-less letters "seeking information" are without prejudice*
* See CBIC Instructions 1/2023-24-GST (Inv.) dated 30 Mar 2024
Taxpayer's Response
COMMON FAILURE Taxpayers acquiesce and fail to agitate the validity of suppression allegation — do NOT do this
ACTION Put suppression allegation too to trial — avail remedy in law for redressal; same as any other ground in notice of demand
6.10 — Power to Impose Penalty
POWER OF IMPOSITION MUST BE LOCATED IN ACT — S.122(2) IS THE SOURCE; OMISSION TO INVOKE = FATAL TO PENALTY
Legislative Mandate Required
RULE To impose any tax, interest, penalty or any other impost — locate Legislative mandate in the ACT (not Rules or delegated legislation)
Power of imposition opens with words: "there shall be levied" or "shall be liable to"
EXAMPLES S.9(1) — "there shall be levied a tax" | S.122(2) — "shall be liable to a penalty"
S.122(2) — Power Source for S.74A
FATAL OMISSION No penalty can be imposed under S.74A(5)/(8) unless "power of imposition" invoked in notice of demand by referring to S.122(2)(a) or (b)
RULE OF LAW S.74A(8)/(9) only guides quantum of penalty in adjudication — does NOT confer power to impose; cannot 'fill in the blanks'
CONCLUSION S.122(2) will continue to furnish power of imposition to S.74A — no new provision in S.122 needed; S.122(2) will NOT become obsolete after terminal date of S.73/S.74
Legislative Will — Not Commonsense
PRINCIPLE 'Legislative Will' in express language of the Act cannot be substituted with what appears reasonable to commonsense
WARNING If Legislature relied on commonsense — even that would have had to be manifest in the Act itself; 'rule of law' prevails over common sense
6.11 — Special Features of New Provision
S.73 vs S.74 vs S.74A — COMPREHENSIVE COMPARISON; VERY LIMITED DISSIMILARITIES; NO ALTERATION IN DUE PROCESS
Due Process — All Three Provisions
DescriptionS.73S.74S.74A
No notice, no demandYESYESYES
Burden of proof on RevenueYESYESYES
Specific cause-of-action invokedYESYESYES
Officer must be "proper"YESYESYES
Pecuniary limits applyYESYESYES
Separation of powers for PNJYESYESYES
Pre-SCN consultationYESYESYES
SCN with summary of demandYESYESYES
Post-SCN adjournmentYESYESYES
Adjudication with independenceYESYESYES
Speaking orderYESYESYES
Power of rectificationYESYESYES
Support use of appellate remedyYESYESYES
Limitation & Penalty Differences Only
DescriptionS.73S.74S.74A
Limitation to issue SCN33 mts.*54 mts.*42 mts.
Limitation for post-SCN resolution30 days30 days60 days
Quantum of pre-SCN penalty **0%15%0%/15%
Quantum of post-SCN penalty **0%25%0%/25%
Limitation to pass Orders36 mts.60 mts.54 mts.
Extension of limitationNILNIL6 mts.
Quantum of post-Order penalty **10%50%10%/50%
Limitation for post-Order resolution30 days30 days60 days
* Special extension under S.168A excepted  |  ** Alternate values for bona fide demands and demands involving mala fides
Conclusion
FINDING Deep search reveals very limited dissimilarities and NO alteration in powers vested or due process of law in this new provision
CAUTION Great anguish expressed that S.74A would reduce burden on Revenue — this is NOT so; burden of proof remains on Revenue; self-assessment of taxpayer retains same sanctity
6.12 — No Alteration of Extant Jurisprudence
NO NEW JURISPRUDENCE FOR S.74A; S.75 DISCIPLINE APPLIES; "PERSON CHARGEABLE WITH TAX" IS STILL THE NOTICEE
Same Jurisprudence Applies
RULE No new or special jurisprudence applies to notice of demand under S.74A — it remains in Chapter XV, not elsewhere
Upon payment of pre-notice or post-notice extent of tax + interest + penalty — proceedings are "deemed to be concluded" — consistent with S.73(8)/S.74(8)
S.75 Entire body of discussion on 'general discipline' in S.75 applies to adjudication under S.74A with same force and authority
"Person Chargeable with Tax" = Noticee
KEY DISTINCTION "Person(s) liable to discharge tax" (successors) ≠ "Person(s) chargeable with tax" (original Noticee)
RULE Without successor being admitted in S.74A as Noticee — mere existence of recovery powers does NOT ensure lawful recovery; PNJ must be satisfied
CASE Trelliborg India (P) Ltd. v. State of Karnataka (WP 15620/20204) — Courts have directed Revenue to reinitiate proceedings against persons liable to discharge tax; but without such person as Noticee, demand will not go into recovery
6.13 — Mother Notice v. Child Notice
MOTHER NOTICE (S.74A(5)(ii)) → CHILD NOTICES (ALWAYS S.74A(5)(i)); AGGRAVATED PENALTIES NOT APPLICABLE TO CHILD NOTICES
Mother Notice — First Tax Period
MOTHER NOTICE Notice of demand issued for FIRST tax period of a continuing infraction — after thorough enquiry; under S.74A(5)(ii)
CHILD NOTICE Subsequent notices for later tax periods (same grounds — infraction continuing) — these are Statement of Demand under S.74A(3); always under S.74A(5)(i)
Key Difference from S.74(4)
IMPORTANT Unlike S.74(4) — S.74A does NOT contain any express provision that every SOD for subsequent period (where S.74A(1) notice was issued) is deemed to be under S.73
RESULT Aggravated penalties will NOT apply to such child notices — 'special circumstances' cannot be alleged yet again when earlier Mother Notice has already been issued
OPEN QUESTION Whether omission of S.74(4) equivalent in S.74A is deliberate attempt to impute continued mala fides or not necessary — to be tested in course of time
Call-Book — S.75(11)
S.75(11) Amendment permits call-book procedure applicable to limitation under S.74A(7) for completing adjudication in respect of notices under S.74A(1)/(3)
Where exact same issue is already pending before a superior forum — limitation to complete adjudication is eclipsed provided notice placed in call-book 'without prejudice'
TAXPAYER Confirm no errors in counting of limitation after exclusion of call-book period — determine whether demand is payable and to what extent
6.14 — Eventual Resolution of Dispute
RESOLUTION OF LIS — DISPUTE AND LITIGATE v. ACCEPT AND DISCHARGE; NO SHORT-CUTS ONCE DUE PROCESS INITIATED
Resolution Flow — LIS to Appeal
LIS → Mother-SCN 1 under S.74A(5)(ii) → Child-SCN 2,3.... under S.74A(5)(i)
Mother-SCN: Jurisdiction to conduct inquiry → Disclose investigative material → Ingredients to attract demand → Affirm evasion → Cum-tax computation → Impeach self-assessment → Adjudicate/Appeal
CHILD-SCN Call-book for 'x' years (until disposal of Mother-SCN)
Dispute & Litigate v. Accept & Discharge
DISINCENTIVE Inherent disincentive to litigate = mounting interest burden; no remedy to deposit demand 'under protest' or pre-deposit more than S.112(8) permits
CAUTION Low success in early stages (adjudication and first appeal) — possibility of favourable outcome best assessed when litigation strategy is implemented correctly
PRINCIPLE New grounds that become available as case moves from strength-to-strength are relevant to inform taxpayer whether course correction is required
No Short-Cuts to Resolution
RULE Once due process initiated — there are NO short-cuts to resolution
CANNOT NOT possible for anyone to assure taxpayer to 'drop the demand' unless speaking order under S.74A(7) is passed dropping demand
FINALITY Even a favourable order does NOT imply finality unless Revenue's remedy of appeal under S.107(2)/S.112(3) or S.108(2)(b) are also exhausted within limitation
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 6 ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name / section.
Principle — S.74A Applies Only to Demands "Pertaining to" FY 2024-25 Onwards; Cause-of-Action Test is Decisive
S.74A r/w S.73(12) & S.74(12) CGST Act | Transitional Provisions
Key Ratio / Principle
The key words "tax pertaining to" are peppered in all affected provisions. Terminal date for a notice of demand under section 73 or 74 refers to the underlying cause-of-action. Where the cause-of-action "pertains to" FY 2023-24 or earlier, notice of demand cannot be issued under section 74A — the demand does NOT pertain to FY 2024-25 or later. Any misapplication of section 74A is fatal to the demand, with section 75(10) remaining unaffected.
When to Use: When Revenue issues S.74A notice for demands pertaining to FY 2023-24 or earlier — argue that the cause-of-action test fails; S.74A jurisdiction is not attracted; S.75(10) operates to make such misapplication fatal to the demand.
Principle — Vested Right Accrues to Taxpayer on Lapse of S.74A(7) Limitation; Order Non Est
S.74A(7) r/w S.75(10) r/w S.161 r/w S.54 CGST Act, 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
Section 74A(7) specifies a 12-month period of limitation to complete adjudication from date of notice. Delay in completing adjudication will be fatal to demand as provided in section 75(10). Neither a willing taxpayer nor a lethargic one can acquiesce to a time-barred adjudication. Any adjudication order passed beyond limitation and without justifiable extension will come within the purview of rectification under section 161 and refund under section 54 for any payment/recovery pursuant to such order that is non est.
When to Use: When adjudication order is passed beyond the 12-month limitation under S.74A(7) without JC extension, or where extension was granted without 'good and sufficient reasons' recorded in writing — challenge the order as non est; invoke S.161 for rectification and S.54 for refund of any recovery.
Principle — JC Extension of Adjudication Limitation is Justiciable; RTI Route Available
S.74A(7) Proviso r/w S.121 CGST Act | RTI Act, 2005
Key Ratio / Principle
Grant of extension by Joint Commissioner to complete adjudication is justiciable — it can be challenged in a Court. The extension is not included in section 121 as a non-appealable decision. Recording of reasons in writing is to enable the same being put to test for fairness and reasonableness, and not arbitrariness or bias. File noting by Proper Officer (reasons for delay) and JC (satisfaction for extension) are 'public records' under the RTI Act and can be demanded by taxpayer.
When to Use: When extension of adjudication limitation is granted — (i) immediately object by letter to JC citing specific reasons; (ii) reiterate objection in 'record of personal hearing'; (iii) in appeal, compel Appellate Authority to record finding on validity of extension; (iv) obtain extension file through RTI to examine JC's satisfaction.
Principle — Aggravated Penalties Do NOT Apply to Child Notice (SOD under S.74A(3))
S.74A(3) r/w S.74A(5) CGST Act, 2017 | Contrast with S.74(4)
Key Ratio / Principle
The 'special circumstances' cannot be alleged yet again when an earlier notice under section 74A(5)(ii) has already been issued for tax period(s) not including the tax period(s) covered by this statement of demand. Aggravated penalties will NOT apply to such child notices. Unlike section 74(4) which expressly deems subsequent SODs as section 73 demands, section 74A does not contain any such express provision — but the jurisprudence of non-application of aggravated penalties to child notices remains the same.
When to Use: When Revenue attempts to apply aggravated penalty (50%) to Child Notice/SOD under S.74A(3) for subsequent tax periods — argue that special circumstances cannot be re-alleged for each subsequent period; aggravated penalties are restricted to the Mother Notice only.
Principle — Omission to Invoke S.122(2) in S.74A Notice = Fatal to Penalty Impost
S.122(2)(a)/(b) r/w S.74A(5)(i)/(ii) r/w S.74A(8)/(9) CGST Act, 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
No penalty can be imposed under section 74A(5) or (8) unless the "power of imposition" is invoked in the notice of demand by specifically referring to section 122(2)(a) or (b). The reduced quantum of penalty appearing in section 74A(8)/(9) only guides adjudication as to the quantum to be considered — it does not itself confer the power to impose. Where 'rule of law' prevails, there is no authority to 'fill in the blanks' and assume that the power of imposition also resides in section 74A when plain language only addresses quantification.
When to Use: When notice under S.74A fails to specifically invoke S.122(2) as the source of power to impose penalty — challenge the penalty impost as being without jurisdiction; S.74A(8)/(9) provides only the quantum, not the power; power must be separately and specifically invoked from S.122(2).
State of Gujarat v. Saw Pipes Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | (2023) 5 Centax 205 (SC)
Key Ratio / Principle
Judiciary is precluded from determining penalties that are specified in statute. Where the Legislature has prescribed the quantum of penalty, the Courts cannot substitute their own assessment of what is a fair or appropriate penalty. However, this principle may be revisited since equality is not treating everyone equally — it is treating equals, equally — and where unequals are involved, a one-size-fits-all approach may rub against this principle of equality before law.
When to Use: (a) To resist Adjudicating Authority imposing penalty below statutory percentages in S.74A(8)/(9) — cite Saw Pipes that statutorily prescribed penalties bind Courts. (b) Conversely, argue that unique facts of each case warrant a different approach — flag the unequals argument as grounds for Apex Court to revisit this decision.
Trelliborg India (P) Ltd. v. State of Karnataka
Karnataka High Court | WP 15620/20204
Key Ratio / Principle
Courts have directed Revenue to reinitiate proceedings against "person(s) liable to discharge tax" (successors-in-interest or transferee-of-business). However, without such successor being the "Noticee" in section 74A proceedings — admitted through proper notice, adjudication, and appeal — mere existence of recovery powers does not ensure lawful recovery. The deliberate statutory distinction between "person(s) chargeable with tax" (original Noticee) and "person(s) liable to discharge tax" (successors) cannot be assumed to be legislative oversight.
When to Use: When Revenue attempts to recover from successors-in-interest or transferee-of-business without first issuing notice of demand under S.74A to such person as Noticee — challenge recovery as without jurisdiction; principles of natural justice must be satisfied even if not expressly provided by statute; power of recovery cannot be invoked without first concluding adjudication.
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 6 — Notice under New Section 74A
⚖️ Chapter 7 — Section 76

⚖️ Chapter 7 — Notice Under Section 76

GST Litigation Reference — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
7.1 Overview
7.2 Pre-Requisites to Notice
7.3 Composite Demand Notice
7.4 Absence of Limitation
7.5 Recovery of Credit Loss
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
7.1 — Overview : Notice Under Section 76
SECTION 76 — TAX COLLECTED BUT NOT APPLICABLE / NOT COLLECTIBLE
What Sec 76 Covers
Tax collected but NOT applicable
Tax NOT collectible but still collected
Trigger: Observation in (i) Audit or (ii) Inquiry → SCN + DRC-1
Key Link — ITC & Output Tax
RULE ITC cannot be denied UNLESS output tax is first shown to be inapplicable
When Revenue accepts output tax discharged → ITC CANNOT be denied
Leaving output tax undisputed = Revenue's acceptance of its validity → ITC demand becomes self-defeating
Procedural Features
NO PRE-NOTICE CONSULTATIONS
Rule 142(1A) excluded deliberately — NOT legislative oversight
DRC-1 accompanying summary still required under Rule 142(1)
Taxpayer CANNOT demand pre-notice consultations u/s 76 as a matter of 'due process'
STRATEGIC GROUND
Omission to invoke Sec 76 where applicable = contra-indicator of facts entertained by Revenue → raises doubts about correctness / completeness of demand
7.2 — Pre-Requisites to Notice
PRE-REQUISITE: "AMOUNT REPRESENTING AS TAX" COLLECTED
What Triggers Sec 76
Amount specified "as tax" in invoice or other document
Whole of such amount liable to be remitted to Government
'Collected' ≠ 'Realized' — amount levied on transaction even if not received = sufficient
5 Situations — 'As Tax' Arises
(a) Tax charged due to ignorance of exemption
(b) Tax charged at rate higher than applicable
(c) Correct rate but higher taxable value
(d) Correct rate but subsidy not excluded from value
(e) Wrong classification — goods vs services or vice versa
Credit Note — Limited Remedy
REGISTERED RECIPIENT
Credit note generally satisfies — when duly reported and credit restricted
UNREGISTERED RECIPIENT
Credit note generally fails to satisfy — Sec 76 action still warranted
Sec 34 imposes time limit to issue credit notes — cannot always be issued
Silent Contract / Bona Fide Mistake
SILENT CONTRACT
No presumption that tax has been collected — but taxpayer's own return filing establishes understanding
BONA FIDE MISTAKE
Does NOT excuse Sec 76 application — unless taxpayer can establish 'no profiteering'
MAFATLAL PRINCIPLE
SC in Mafatlal Industries v. UOI (1996):
State may retain excess tax under parens patriae — rather than allowing taxpayer to retain or find customer for refund
7.3 — Composite Demand Notice
COMPOSITE DEMAND — SEC 76 + SEC 74 TOGETHER
Why Composite SCN Needed
GST is NOT a fungible tax
Tax charged on goods ≠ can be cross-cancelled with tax payable on services
Art 265 bars collecting tax contrary to law — 'cross-cancellation' of wrong tax with right tax = wrong tax unaddressed in notice
Non-Fungibility Rule
'Right tax v. Wrong tax' — double compliance imposed by Sec 77 CGST r/w Sec 19 IGST
Paying 'any tax' irrespective of classification = renders tariff classification otiose
Circular 172/3/2022-GST (6 Jul 2022) appears to endorse — though Sec 76 reference evaded in Issue 1
Fictitious Invoice Scenario
SCN u/s 74 for fictitious inward supplies — ITC availed and utilized on fictitious outward supplies
DEFENCE
Either both inward AND outward supplies are fictitious — or both are factual
Revenue's non-denial of outward supplies = compels invocation of Sec 76 for outward supplies — else ITC demand under Sec 74 is incoherent
REAL ESTATE EXAMPLE
Developer collected 18% (on 2/3 of price) + availed restricted ITC
Composite SCN:
(i) Output tax u/s 76 — allow credit for cash paid
(ii) Inadmissible ITC u/s 74
7.4 — Absence of Limitation
SECTION 76 — NO TIME LIMIT SPECIFIED
ABSENCE
Sec 76 — no time limit like Sec 73(10) or 74(10)
Sec 75(10) does NOT apply to Sec 76 demands
PRINCIPLE
Res judicata demands no right to statutory action can exist without time limit unless expressly excluded by Legislature
COMPOSITE SCN RULE
Where Sec 76 is part of composite demand notice — time limit of the primary demand (Sec 73/74) automatically flows to Sec 76 demand also
OPEN QUESTION
Authoritative judicial pronouncement awaited on limitation for standalone Sec 76 demands — position not yet settled
7.5 — Recovery of Credit Loss
RECOVERY OF CREDIT LOSS — CONTRACT LAW MEETS SEC 76
Right to Collect Tax
NOT STATUTORY
No provision in CGST Act vesting Supplier with statutory right to demand tax reimbursement from Recipient
CONTRACTUAL
'GST extra' in contract = Price excludes GST → Recipient must pay separately
Rate Revision — Sec 64A SGA
'GST 12%' in contract + rate revised to 18% → Sec 64A Sale of Goods Act → Recipient obliged to pay additional 6%
Rate decreases 12% → 5% → Supplier CANNOT continue recovering 12% from Recipient
EXCEPTION
'Firm price' contract — bars Sec 64A application for rate revision
ITC Loss Recovery (Unison Metals Principle)
Exemption notified mid-contract → Supplier must reverse ITC u/s 18(4) → credit loss say 11%
SEC 76 APPLIES
Charged as CGST-SGST or IGST in invoice = "as tax" = bar operates
SEC 76 NOT APPLIES
Charged as "ITC recovery" or equivalent = NOT "as tax" = bar does NOT operate
Such ITC recovery becomes part of Price — included in exempt supply turnover for Sec 17(2) compliance
PRACTICAL EXAMPLE
RWA issues:
(i) Tax invoice + 18% GST — members with maintenance >₹90,000/yr
(ii) Bill of supply + 14% 'credit recovery' — members below threshold
= Sec 76 averted for (ii)
⇧ Back to Index
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 7
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name before the officer.
Mafatlal Industries Ltd. v. Union of India
Supreme Court of India | (1996) 89 ELT 247
Since the State will eventually use the excess tax amount towards public expenditure, when a taxpayer collects more than the applicable rate, rather than allow the taxpayer to retain it or find the Customer who paid the tax to refund it, the State may retain such tax — supported by the principle of parens patriae.
USE WHEN: Taxpayer seeks refund of excess tax collected arguing unjust enrichment — Revenue can invoke this to retain amount or appropriate it under Sec 76 / Sec 54(10)
Unison Metals Ltd. v. CCE
Larger Bench, CESTAT | (2006) 204 ELT 323
Unless the amount collected is represented 'as tax', the bar under the provision does not operate. The critical test is whether the amount is described in the invoice as tax — not merely that the amount corresponds to a tax rate.
USE WHEN: Supplier has recovered ITC loss or cost escalation from Recipient — argue that if described as 'ITC recovery' and not 'as tax', the bar in Sec 76 does NOT operate and the amount is legitimate contractual recovery
Article 265 + Article 300A — Constitutional Embargo
Constitution of India
No tax shall be levied or collected except by authority of law. No person shall be deprived of his property save by authority of law. Tax collected contrary to law — whether wrong type, wrong rate, or on wrong person — cannot be retained by the collector.
USE WHEN: Revenue permits 'cross-cancellation' of wrong tax with right tax — argue that Art 265 bars such adjustment and Sec 76 notice is mandatory to address the wrong tax collected
⚜️ Key Principles & Arguments for Personal Hearings
ITC demand without establishing output tax inapplicability = self-defeating
When Revenue has accepted output tax discharged by taxpayer, input tax credit availed to discharge that same output tax cannot be denied — the demand becomes incoherent and self-defeating
USE WHEN: ITC is challenged by Revenue while output tax liability is not disputed — demand they first invoke Sec 76 for output tax before denying ITC
Omission to invoke Sec 76 = contra-indicator
Where Sec 76 ought to have been invoked but was not, this omission is a contra-indicator of the facts entertained by Revenue and raises doubts about the correctness or completeness of the demand under Sec 74
USE WHEN: Revenue issues Sec 74 SCN for ITC without issuing Sec 76 SCN for corresponding output tax — this omission itself is a ground to challenge the ITC demand
Parens patriae
"Parent of the nation" — State acts as guardian to retain excess tax rather than let taxpayer benefit from over-collection
NOTE: Revenue may cite this principle — be prepared with the counter that Sec 76 requires proper notice before appropriation and taxpayer retains right to establish 'no profiteering'
Res judicata (applied to limitation)
"A matter already judged" / "No action without limitation" — no right to statutory action can exist without a time limit unless expressly excluded by Legislature
USE WHEN: Revenue issues standalone Sec 76 notice after several years — argue that in absence of prescribed limitation, principle of res judicata demands a reasonable time limit be implied
GST is not a fungible tax — 'Right tax v. Wrong tax'
Tax charged on supply of goods cannot be cross-cancelled with tax payable on supply of services. Each must be addressed separately under the correct provision.
USE WHEN: Revenue allows cross-cancellation of wrong tax type with right tax due — argue that Sec 77 CGST r/w Sec 19 IGST imposes 'double compliance' and Sec 76 is mandatory for the wrong tax
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 7 — Notice Under Section 76
⚖️ Chapter 8 — System Generated Notices

⚖️ Chapter 8 — System Generated Notices

GST Litigation Reference — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
8.1 Validity
8.2 Rule 88C and Rule 142B
8.3 Options for Response 1 (Rule 88C)
8.4 Rule 88D
8.5 Options for Response 2 (Rule 88D)
8.6 Validity of Adverse Inference
8.7 Presumption of Arrears from Turnover Admitted
8.8 Service and Belated Response
8.9 Effective Date and Period of Coverage
8.10 Auto-Generated Appropriation
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
8.1 — Validity
SYSTEM GENERATED NOTICES — VALIDITY; USELESS FORMALITY THEORY; NEW GENUS OF NOTICES
S.75(12) — Useless Formality Theory
S.75(12) Explanation inserted from 1 Jan 2022 — closest to 'useless formality theory' of Administrative Law
Theory: when nothing more remains to be said by taxpayer — notice to show cause is NOT necessary; its absence does not render consequent actions unfair or opposed to natural justice
USE CASE Taxpayer has admitted and disclosed output tax in GSTR1/IFF but omitted to discharge in GSTR3B/4
Half-Way Remedy — System Notice
REMEDY System generated notices issued with very short duration to respond
CONSEQUENCE Non-response or dissatisfactory response → authority to initiate aggressive recovery measures
STATUS No authorities yet uphold validity of this new genus of notices — taxpayers may need to swiftly come to terms with them
Courts' Position
COURTS FROWN Courts frown upon any demand without a notice of demand — but explanation inserted shows resolve to press forward
REMAINING CHALLENGES (i) System generated character (ii) Short duration allowed to respond (iii) Potentially misleading 'suggested responses' — may be entertained by Courts but NOT with same rigour as notice-free recovery
8.2 — Rule 88C and Rule 142B
RULE 88C — OUTPUT TAX MISMATCH (GSTR1 vs GSTR3B); RULE 142B — RECOVERY PROCEDURE; DRC1B + 9 RECOVERY MODES
Self-Assessment Framework
ASSESS Liability self-assessed resides in invoice under S.31
DISCLOSE Liability disclosed in GSTR1/IFF under S.37
DISCHARGE Liability discharged in GSTR3B/4 under S.39
PRESUMPTION Admitted liability in GSTR1 → presumption in favour of Revenue that liability disclosed is undisputed and fallen in arrears once due date for GSTR3B passes
Rule 88C — From 26 Dec 2022
TRIGGER Difference between liability disclosed (GSTR1) and discharged (GSTR3B) for a tax period 'exceeds a certain percentage'
→ Notice in Form GST DRC1B-Part A issued on Common Portal
RESPONSE 7 days to respond in Form GST DRC1B-Part B (options on Common Portal)
Rule 88C(3) Recovery under S.79 authorized where (i) no explanation offered OR (ii) explanation not acceptable
Rule 142B — Recovery Procedure (4 Aug 2023)
PROCESS PO reaches conclusion under Rule 88C(3) → Issues Form GST DRC1D (intimation of recovery) on Common Portal
AFTER DURATION Recovery set in motion:
(a) DRC9 — officer owing money to taxpayer
(b) DRC10 — e-auction of taxpayer's goods
(c) DRC13 — person holding taxpayer's funds
(d) DRC15 — Court to execute decree
(e) DRC16 — restrain disposal of property
(f) DRC18 — District Collector (land revenue)
(g) DRC19 — Magistrate (fine)
(h) Recover from surety
(i) DRC24 — Liquidator (company in liquidation)
Concern — No Application of Mind
CONCERN 'Application of mind' prior to issuance reduced to non-essential — trait of system-based tax administration
IF VALID If notice bears indicia of valid demand and disposal is fair — non-involvement of Officer in issuing it does not seem to invalidate
CONCERN Authorizing recovery action due to possible dissatisfaction with response — without an appealable order — creates incompleteness that could engender highhandedness
8.3 — Options for Response 1 (Rule 88C — Output Tax Mismatch DRC1B)
RULE 88C — DRC1B RESPONSE OPTIONS; SOME INCULPATORY; CHOOSE WITH CIRCUMSPECTION
(a) Excess Liability — Earlier Tax Periods
Excess tax discharged in earlier tax periods — taxpayer ought to claim refund; cannot unilaterally withhold output tax in current period
RISK Circular 26/26/2017-GST dated 29 Dec 2017 not withdrawn but issued at nascent stage — may not be applicable in current sophisticated return-refund process
(b) Earlier Period Transactions Reported Now
Earlier period transactions reported now — though tax already discharged earlier; requires excess of 3B over R1 in 'earlier tax period'
CAUTION Cannot be readily selected — information of all earlier tax periods readily available to validate; further questions arise if GSTR1 was via amendment or afresh
(c) Errors in Data — Amendment Later
SAFE OPTION Sufficiently vague response that cannot be readily rejected without further enquiry; follow-up enquiry not permitted in these proceedings
RESULT Recovery action CANNOT be taken up if this response is selected — finds support in Instruction 1/2022-GST dated 7 Jan 2022
WARNING All responses must be provided in good faith
(d) Mistake in Reporting Advances
May hold off recovery action presently — but attracts enquiry into receipt voucher issued in earlier period and corresponding tax invoice in current period
BURDEN New information introduced → burden of proof on person introducing it; counter-party entitled to express dissatisfaction at anything less than impeccable quality
(e) Any Others — Use with Circumspection
ADMISSION This response admits that reason for mismatch is none of the earlier reasons — yet one that does not deserve recovery action
CAUTION Must be selected with circumspection; must be one that can truly impress with details appended in support; use with full justification only
8.4 — Rule 88D
RULE 88D — ITC MISMATCH (GSTR3B vs GSTR2B); NO DIRECT RECOVERY; TRIGGERS S.73/74 NOTICE; FULL CHAPTER XV RIGHTS RESTORED
Rule 88D Trigger — From 4 Aug 2023
TRIGGER ITC claimed in GSTR3B by Recipient where tax charged by Suppliers is NOT reflected in GSTR2B
Unmatched credits (above threshold to be notified) → liable to be reversed with interest OR offer explanation that credit satisfied S.16(2) vesting conditions
→ System generated notice in Form GST DRC1C-Part A on Common Portal; 7 days to respond in DRC1C-Part B
Rule 88D(3) — Key Difference from Rule 88C(3)
UNLIKE 88C(3) Rule 88D(3) does NOT authorize direct recovery under S.79
INSTEAD Authorizes Proper Officer to issue notice under S.73 or S.74 where (i) no explanation offered OR (ii) explanation not acceptable
CRITICAL Notice must be issued by JURISDICTIONAL Proper Officer authorized under S.61 — NOT the Proper Officer discharging duties under S.65 or S.67
RESULT Entire repertoire of rights, remedies and safeguards applicable to notice of demand under Chapter XV become available to taxpayer
8.5 — Options for Response 2 (Rule 88D — ITC Mismatch DRC1C)
RULE 88D — DRC1C RESPONSE OPTIONS; CHOICE OF RESPONSE NOT TO BE TAKEN LIGHTLY; CAN RAISE PRESUMPTION AGAINST TAXPAYER
(a) Credit of Earlier Period in Current Period
Credit matched in earlier period but not availed (non-receipt of supplies) — now claimed in current period
OUTDATED New GSTR3B requires all credit in GSTR2B to be claimed in 4(A)(1) and reversed in 4(B)(2) — this response may be outdated
(b) Credit Omitted in Earlier Period
SAFE Sufficiently vague response that cannot be readily faulted without further enquiry
PRINCIPLE Indicates very real possibility in law that taxpayers CANNOT be presumed to be in default based on linear comparison of data
(c) & (d) Import / SEZ Credits
OUTDATED (c) Import credits (ICEGATE linked to GSTIN) — outdated; not likely to be found acceptable
OUTDATED (d) SEZ supply credits — outdated in view of completion of portal linking
(e) Excess Reversal Now Reclaimed
Rules admit possibility that credit reversed (other than Rule 37/37A) can be reclaimed
RISK Selection of this response could attract review of compliance with S.16(4) in current tax period
(f) & (g) Credit Reversed — Now Reclaimed
(f) Verifiable with Rule 37 reporting + Table 6H of GSTR9; not readily selectable as taxpayers generally claimed net credit in earlier periods
(g) Rule 37A unmatched credit — verifiable on Common Portal; not readily selectable without exposing immediate validation
(h) & (i) Errors / Any Others
(h) DANGEROUS "Errors in report credit — to be rectified later" — implicitly contains admission of facts sufficient to raise presumption against taxpayer's innocence
(i) DANGEROUS "Any others" — admits reason is none of the earlier ones; effectively admits wrong-doing; use only with extreme circumspection and detailed substantiation
8.6 — Validity of Adverse Inference
ADVERSE INFERENCE — POOR REPLY ≠ REVENUE'S BURDEN; BUT TAXPAYER'S OWN ADMISSION CREATES PRESUMPTION AGAINST ITSELF
Revenue's Burden — Not Displaced by Poor Reply
PRINCIPLE No adverse inference can be made against taxpayer solely on unimpressive response against impressive allegation — burden of proof rests equivocally on the one making the allegation
RULE Poor reply is NOT a substitute for Revenue's burden to bring home the allegations
Taxpayer's Own Admission — Presumption Against Self
KEY When faced with taxpayer's own assertions as to existence of liability — Revenue is not making an allegation but acting upon taxpayer's OWN admission
PRESUMPTION Admitted liability disclosed in GSTR1 cannot be left undischarged in GSTR3B unless GSTR1 itself is asserted AND shown to be erroneous
RESULT Omission to discharge admitted liability raises presumption in favour of Revenue — this is 'presumption against taxpayer by taxpayer's own assertions'
8.7 — Presumption of Arrears from Turnover Admitted
RULE 88C TRIGGER — GSTR1 TAX PAYABLE > GSTR3B TAX PAYABLE; RESPONSE IN DRC1B-PART B IS KEY; IPSI DIXIT NO ARREARS
Rule 88C Explicit Trigger
TRIGGER 'Tax payable' in GSTR1 exceeds tax payable in GSTR3B by certain limit (as GSTC may recommend) → DRC1B-Part A issued
EXAMPLE GSTR1: Turnover ₹100, IGST ₹18; GSTR3B: Turnover ₹100, IGST ₹5 → same turnover but mismatch in tax liability admitted vs discharged
RESPONSE "Classification error" in DRC1B-Part B fits within "Reason 3" — avoids consequences of direct recovery under Rule 88C(3)
Response Strategy
PRINCIPLE Merely because there is difference between GSTR1 and GSTR3B does NOT ipsi dixit mean there is an undisputed arrear
KEY Response in Part B must place facts correctly AND indicate absence of any liability
CBIC INSTRUCTION Instruction 1/2022-23-GST dated 7 Jan 2022 (in context of S.75(12)) — no occasion to rush to invoke S.79 as there could well be bona fide reasons for difference in returns not entailing any undischarged arrears
8.8 — Service and Belated Response
SERVICE CANNOT BE CHALLENGED; BELATED RESPONSE BETTER THAN NO RESPONSE; COURTS HELP THOSE WITH CLEAN HANDS
Service — Cannot Be Challenged
RULE With recovery authorized under Rule 88C(3) and notice authority under Rule 88D(3) — question of 'service' of system-generated notices CANNOT be raised
NO EXCUSE Lack of awareness of computers; business in poorly connected locations; other such grounds — do NOT come to taxpayer's rescue
Belated Response — Better Than None
STRATEGY Instead of omitting to provide response — offer even belated response after 7 days; Common Portal may accept it; delay may be condoned
NON-PROSECUTION Non-prosecution may appear as neglect on part of taxpayer seeking redressal
COURTS Courts would come to rescue of taxpayers who 'come with clean hands' — grounds to assail recovery action should be after filing response on Common Portal; only delay then remains to be explained, not wrong-doing
8.9 — Effective Date and Period of Coverage
EFFECTIVE DATE — PROSPECTIVE OR RETROSPECTIVE? NO VESTED RIGHT IN PROCEDURE; LIS STARTED WHEN GSTR3B FILED
Effective Date — Key Question
QUESTION Whether transactions after start date only can be included in these system-generated notices — OR as rules of procedure will cover past transactions within limitation
If limitation under S.74 is outer limit — will these rules enjoy operation from 1 Jul 2017?
RULE DATES Rule 88B: 5 Jul 2022 (effective 1 Jul 2017) | Rule 88C: 26 Dec 2022 | Rule 142B & 88D: 4 Aug 2023 | S.16(2)(aa) operational: 1 Jan 2022
No Vested Right in Procedure
PRINCIPLE 'There is no vested right in matters of procedure' — rules of procedure can cover past transactions
LIS COMMENCEMENT LIS commenced not when system-generated notices were issued but when underlying infraction first appeared on Common Portal when GSTR3B was filed
CAUTION Validate 'tax period' covered when system-generated notices are issued — notice extending application from an anterior date may be impermissible; Government's deliberate approach in not following Rule 88B's retrospective approach for later rules is significant
8.10 — Auto-Generated Appropriation
RULE 142(2A) — DRC1A PART C REPLACES DRC4; RIGHT TO REFUND CANNOT BE ECLIPSED; RESIST COERCIVE TACTICS
New Rule 142(2A) — From 10 Jul 2024
EARLIER Appropriation of discharge required Proper Officer to issue DRC4 as affirmative step against payment in DRC3
NOW Payment at pre-notice stage via DRC3 → DRC1A-Part C issued as acknowledgement-cum-acceptance; DRC4 no longer required
RESULT With auto-generated DRC4 or PO-generated DRC1A-Part C — taxpayer is less likely to be in a position to dispute liability after making payment
Right to Refund — Cannot Be Eclipsed
PRINCIPLE Where it is undoubtedly clear that not even a willing taxpayer can accept and discharge a non-existent liability — no amount of automation in prescribing innovative appropriation modes can eclipse the right to refund
EARLIER REMEDY Taxpayers who discharged liability under compulsion — could submit refund application under 'other' category in RFD1 within S.54 limitation
UPHILL TASK Taxpayers have uphill task to dispute such payments now — but the right to refund is NOT extinguished by automation of appropriation
Resist Coercive Tactics
COERCIVE TACTIC 1 Misleading taxpayer into believing closure of proceedings on voluntary payment via DRC3 — RESIST
COERCIVE TACTIC 2 Inducing taxpayer into assuming more severe consequences if liability not discharged immediately — RESIST; it is time taxpayers stand up against such tactics
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 8 ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the section / instruction.
Principle — 'Useless Formality Theory'; Admitted Liability in GSTR1 Undischarged in GSTR3B Creates Presumption Against Taxpayer
S.75(12) r/w Explanation from 1 Jan 2022 | CGST Act, 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
Section 75(12) and the explanation inserted from 1 Jan 2022 embody the 'useless formality theory' of Administrative Law — where there remains nothing more to be said by taxpayer, notice to show cause is not necessary and its absence does not render the consequent actions unfair or opposed to natural justice. Admitted liability disclosed in GSTR1 cannot be left undischarged in GSTR3B unless GSTR1 itself is asserted as well as shown to be erroneous. Revenue is not making an allegation — it is acting upon the taxpayer's own admission. This is presumption against taxpayer by taxpayer's own assertions.
When to Use: In reply to DRC1B-Part A / DRC1C-Part A — where GSTR1 liability genuinely differs from GSTR3B due to classification error or other legitimate reasons, assail the system-generated notice on merits; show the GSTR1 is erroneous OR that there is a legitimate reason for the difference; failure to do so will cement the presumption against taxpayer.
Principle — Poor Reply is NOT a Substitute for Revenue's Burden; Adverse Inference Cannot Be Made Solely on Unimpressive Response
S.75(12) r/w General Principles of Administrative Law | Burden of Proof
Key Ratio / Principle
No adverse inference, on account of non-prosecution of demand or inadequate prosecution in the nature of negligence, can be made against taxpayer in law based solely on unimpressive response against an impressive allegation, when the burden of proof rests equivocally on the one making said allegation. Poor reply is not a substitute for Revenue's burden to bring home the allegations. The burden of proof in GST proceedings rests on Revenue — system-generated notices do not shift this burden.
When to Use: When adjudicating authority in S.73/S.74 notice (arising from Rule 88D(3)) attempts to confirm demand solely based on taxpayer's weak response to DRC1C — argue that weak response is not an admission; Revenue must independently discharge burden of proof; poor reply cannot substitute for Revenue's case.
Principle — Rule 88D(3) Requires Jurisdictional Proper Officer Under S.61; NOT Officer Under S.65 or S.67
Rule 88D(3) r/w S.61 / S.65 / S.67 CGST Act, 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
The notice of demand under section 73 or 74 that must be issued upon unacceptable response to DRC1C-Part B (under rule 88D(3)) must be issued by the jurisdictional Proper Officer of the registered taxpayer authorized to discharge duties under section 61 — and NOT Proper Officer discharging duties under section 65 or 67. With this, the entire repertoire of rights, remedies and safeguards applicable to notice of demand under Chapter XV become available to taxpayer.
When to Use: When notice under S.73/S.74 arising from Rule 88D(3) is issued by a Proper Officer authorized under S.65 (audit) or S.67 (inspection) — challenge the notice as issued by wrong Proper Officer; only jurisdictional PO under S.61 has authority; additionally, assert all Chapter XV rights, remedies and safeguards immediately.
CBIC Instruction 1/2022-23-GST dated 7 Jan 2022 — Bona Fide Reasons May Exist for GSTR1 vs GSTR3B Difference
CBIC Instruction 1/2022-23-GST dated 7 Jan 2022 | S.75(12) CGST Act, 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
There is no occasion to rush to invoke recovery powers under section 79 as there could well be bona fide reasons for the difference between GSTR1 and GSTR3B, yet not entailing any undischarged arrears. Merely because there is a difference between GSTR1 and GSTR3B does not ipsi dixit mean there is an undisputed arrear. The response provided in DRC1B-Part B is key — it must place the facts correctly and indicate the absence of any liability.
When to Use: When Revenue uses Rule 88C(3) to initiate recovery action without proper examination of taxpayer's response — cite this CBIC instruction to argue that Revenue must first examine whether bona fide reasons exist for the difference; hasty recovery action based purely on GSTR1 vs GSTR3B mismatch is not contemplated by law.
Principle — Right to Refund Cannot Be Eclipsed by Automation; Coercive Payment Via DRC3 Can Be Disputed
Rule 142(2A) from 10 Jul 2024 r/w S.54 CGST Act, 2017 | RFD1 'Other Category'
Key Ratio / Principle
When it is undoubtedly clear that not even a willing taxpayer can accept and discharge a non-existent liability, no amount of automation in prescribing innovative modes of appropriation (auto-generated DRC4 or DRC1A-Part C) can eclipse the right to refund. Taxpayers who discharged liability under compulsion may submit refund application under 'other' category in RFD1 within limitation under section 54. Coercive tactics to mislead into believing closure of proceedings on voluntary payment via DRC3, or to induce assumption that severe consequences will follow non-payment, must be resisted.
When to Use: When taxpayer has made payment under compulsion/coercion in response to system-generated notice or inquiry proceedings — challenge the auto-appropriation; file refund claim in RFD1 under 'other' category within S.54 limitation; argue that automation cannot create liability where none exists in law; right to refund is protected by S.54 irrespective of the mode of appropriation.
Principle — 'Errors in Data to be Amended Later' (Response (c) to Rule 88C) Avoids Recovery; No Follow-Up Enquiry Permitted
Rule 88C(3) CGST Act | CBIC Instruction 1/2022-GST dated 7 Jan 2022
Key Ratio / Principle
The response "Errors in data reported to be amended later" is a sufficiently vague response that cannot be readily rejected without further enquiry — and such follow-up enquiry is NOT permitted in these proceedings under rule 88C. Recovery action CANNOT be taken up if this reason for the mismatch is selected by taxpayers, and it finds support in CBIC instruction 1/2022-GST dated 7 Jan 2022. All responses must be provided in good faith and taxpayers must be sure the response can truly be substantiated with details appended in support.
When to Use: When faced with DRC1B-Part A notice and the genuine reason for GSTR1 vs GSTR3B mismatch is a data error to be corrected — select response (c); rule 88C(3) cannot trigger recovery on this basis; no follow-up enquiry is permitted; Revenue must issue fresh notice under S.73/S.74 if it wishes to pursue the matter further.
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 8 — System Generated Notices
⚖️ Chapter 9 — Refund Notice
CHAPTER 9

Refund Notice

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index — Click to Navigate

9.1 Overview 9.2 Pre-Requisites to Notice 9.3 Purpose of Notice 9.4 Unjust Enrichment 9.5 Mischief of Credit Notes 9.6 Second Round of Refund Proceedings 9.7 Composite Refund Notice 9.8 Protective Action 9.9 Protective Notice 9.10 Erroneous Refund Along with Interest 9.11 Refund to Unregistered Persons 9.12 Circulars and Instructions ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

9.1 Overview

⬆ Index
Refund Notice — Nature & Character
Character of Proceeding
DISTINCT Crisp Proceeding Taxpayer fully seized of facts. No dispute or lis pending — only 'right to refund' is taken for adjudication.
CANNOT No pre-notice consultations — this notice does not involve any demand.
Procedural Flow
RFD-2 Application Acknowledged
↓
RFD-8 Notice Issued
↓
RFD-6 Order (Speaking Order)
Speaking Order Mandate
ALWAYS Even if refund is denied, Order must be a Speaking Order — it affects rights of taxpayer.
CONDITION Where refund application validly accepted → must conclude in an Order.

9.2 Pre-Requisites to Notice

⬆ Index
Pre-Requisites — Valid Refund Application
RFD-2 = Validity Gate
ESTABLISHED IF RFD-2 issued Validity of refund application confirmed. Once RFD-2 issued, limitation no longer applies. Speaking Order must still be passed to reject.
RFD-3 — Deficiency Memo
CATASTROPHIC RFD-3 issued = Refund 'as if never filed' No provision to 'reply to' RFD-3. No authority to restore. Brings application to irretrievable end.
What qualifies as Deficiency?
ONLY Prima facie incomplete / incoherent applications. NOT matters requiring application of mind to law/facts.
WARNING Para 11 of Master Circular 125 — RFD-3 will NOT be re-issued if rectified application has same deficiencies. Taxpayer blindsided.
Interest Risk on Rejection
RISK Rejection of refund risks interest payable u/s 56 OR in equity if rejection was on frivolous grounds bearing bad faith on part of Revenue.
Taxpayer's Imperative
ACTION Persist until RFD-2 is issued. Only then is the right to refund protected. Frivolous RFD-3 rejections: Proper Officer answerable in judicial review — especially when rectified applications again rejected and limitation lapses.

9.3 Purpose of Notice

⬆ Index
Purpose of Refund Notice (RFD-8)
Six Ingredients in RFD-8
MUST SHOW 1. Distinct Person / Claimant
2. Accrual of 'Right to Refund'
3. Evidence of Accrual
4. Application within Limitation
5. Documents to verify claim
6. Unjust Enrichment
Natural Justice Foundation
PRINCIPLE In any quasi-judicial proceeding where adverse consequences may follow, person entitled to be 'put at notice' and allowed opportunity to answer on grounds stated.
Notice ensures refund applications not dismissed unheard and allows Appellate Authority to consider basis of any adverse finding.
RFD-2 Does Not Create Presumption
NO PRESUMPTION Issuance of RFD-2 does NOT raise presumption of admissibility. Proper Officer still examines on touch points to establish refund is not illegal or erroneous.
Objective of Proper Officer
CORRECT VIEW Objective is NOT to find ways to reject. Speaking Order in RFD-6 contains grounds — even seemingly unjust rejections are strictapplication of rules of procedure, not discretion.

9.4 Unjust Enrichment

⬆ Index
Unjust Enrichment — Framework
State Cannot Claim Unjust Enrichment
PRINCIPLE Apex Court in Mafatlal Industries (para 74) Unjust enrichment does NOT apply to State — principle of parens patriae. Article 39 of Constitution proscribes State from perpetuating unjust enrichment.
Exemptions — Proof Not Required
EXEMPT No proof of enrichment needed for:
(i) Refund of cash balance in ECL
(ii) Unregistered taxpayers
(iii) Certain cases of exports
Presumption u/s 49(9) — Refund of Output Tax
PRESUMPTION For refund of output tax paid — incidence presumed to have been passed on. Presumption is rebuttable.
Evidence Required to Rebut (a to e):
(a) Nature & circumstances of payment
(b) Underlying commercial arrangements
(c) Refundable amount NOT included in commercial price
(d) Accounting records — carried value in financial statements (year of payment + year of refund)
(e) Independent accountant's certificate verifying above
INSUFFICIENT Mere self-declaration OR accountant's certificate without basis NOT acceptable. Assertion must be verifiable.
Default Procedure — CWF Credit
DEFAULT u/s 49(9) r/w s.54(5): Refund sanctioned BUT amount credited to Consumer Welfare Fund (CWF) u/s 57 r/w Rule 97, unless presumption is rebutted.

9.5 Mischief of Credit Notes

⬆ Index
Credit Notes — Suo Motu Refund Mischief (Section 34)
Revenue's Perspective
IMPACT Suo motu sanction of refund — that is the impact of credit notes from Revenue's perspective. Indiscreet use of s.34 attracts enquiry into correctness of tax adjusted.
Burden of Proof on Taxpayer
3 CONDITIONS Credit notes must comply with (i) Circumstances, (ii) Time Limit, (iii) Conditions under s.34.
IRRELEVANT DEFENCES (a) Correction of erroneous invoice
(b) Cancellation & reissue
(c) Absence of mala fides
None of these are relevant. Transaction must be squarely placed within s.34 with documentary evidence.
Double Assertion Burden
LOGIC Tax invoice disclosed u/s 37 (GSTR-1) + liability discharged u/s 39 (GSTR-3B) = unequivocal assertion. Withdrawing this assertion requires twice as credible evidence to:
(i) demonstrate reasons for first assertion
(ii) establish no further errors in second assertion.
Zero-Rated Supplies — Extra Caution
CASCADING In respect of zero-rated supplies consequences cascade. Taxpayer must demonstrate: (i) non-recurring failure of invoice procedures, (ii) contemporaneous documentary support of customer interactions available on file.
NOTE Appellate Authority loath to verify documents if not verified during adjudication. Failure to discharge burden of proof at reply stage = great disadvantage.

9.6 Second Round of Refund Proceedings

⬆ Index
Second Round — After Appeal Success
Accrual of Right to Refund
ONCE ONLY Actionable cause arises once — at the time of export/eligible SEZ supply/deemed export. Right to refund is then complete.
RFD-1 filing = Affirmative action of intention
RFD-2 grant = Revenue's admission subject to verification
After Appeal Allowed
KEY No NEW right accrues to Claimant. Appellate Authority furnishes interpretation (of facts or law) to be adhered to by Proper Officer. Proper Officer continues proceeding and sanctions refund.
s.75(3): Proper Officer may comply with directions of Appellate Authority within 3 years — NOT treated as remand.
Fresh Application (Online System)
MASTER CIRCULAR 125 (Para 24) Due to online processing, fresh refund application must be filed under option: "On Account of Assessment / Provisional Assessment / Appeal / Any other order". Will NOT be rejected as time-barred.
Example: Refund for 2017-18, filed June 2019, rejected Aug 2020, appeal allowed Aug 2022. Fresh application Sept 2022 → not time-barred.
Proper Officer Restricted in 2nd Round
CANNOT RAISE In second round RFD-8, Proper Officer cannot raise:
(i) Limitation grounds
(ii) Findings of Appellate Authority (no review jurisdiction)
CAN RAISE Only matters not considered in first round or in appeal.
IF IMPROPER Departmental appeal u/s 107(2) OR revisionary proceedings u/s 108 is proper recourse. Until then, Proper Officer obliged to process as per law.
LIMITATION MECHANISM Time-barred applications rejected → amount debited recredited into Electronic Credit Ledger via PMT-3 under Rule 93. Right to refund not lost but remedy of collecting refund barred.

9.7 Composite Refund Notice

⬆ Index
Composite Refund Notice — Scope & Extended Demand
Scope of Verification — Proper Officer
ONLY Proper Officer must limit verification to matters bearing on whether to 'accept or reject' the refund.
CANNOT EXAMINE Outstanding compliance / classification / valuation of outward supplies / inter-branch tax / reversal u/s 17(2) — these fall under Audit u/s 65 r/w s.2(13).
Ineligible Credit Visible to PO
VISIBLE (a) Mismatch of credit (2A/2B v. 3B)
(b) Blocked credits availed u/s 17(5)
ONLY REMEDY Reject refund to that extent by recomputing admissible refund in formula. NOT welcome to issue notice demanding payment.
Master Circular 125 (Para 20-21) — Extended Scope
CONTROVERSY MC 125 authorises Proper Officer to issue composite RFD-8 simultaneously demanding:
(a) Rejection of refund for ineligible ITC
(b) Recovery of ineligible ITC as wrongly availed — u/s 73 or 74 + interest + penalty
Order in RFD-6 (u/s 54 r/w s.73/74)
DRC-07 — liability entered in electronic liability register
OR — Voluntary payment via DRC-03 u/s 73(5)/74(5) r/w Rule 142(2) avoids demand notice
Claimant's Vigilance Required
ALERT Where refund is related to ITC, Claimant must be vigilant. Given limited time under Rules 90–92 and interest burden u/s 56, Proper Officer may not entertain legal submissions at this stage. Extended scope must not come as surprise.

9.8 Protective Action

⬆ Index
Protective Action — Where Refund Sanctioned Erroneously
Three Remedies for Revenue
OPTIONS (a) Departmental appeal u/s 107(2) — within 6 months from refund order
(b) Revisionary proceedings u/s 108 — within 3 years against refund order
(c) SCN u/s 73 — within 3 years; u/s 74 — within 5 years, demanding repayment
KEY RULE Omission to initiate one does not preclude others. Lapse of shorter limitation does NOT bar action with longer time limit.
'Dual Notice Procedure' — Mandatory
WHY NEEDED Departmental appeal only reaches a finding (of law/fact). Success before Appellate Authority does NOT automatically authorise recovery. Notice u/s 73/74 still required.
LAW MINISTRY POSITION Based on Apex Court decisions — dual notice procedure well-established as 'due process'. Continues in GST.
Protective notice adjudication may be kept in abeyance (call-book) until departmental appeal disposal.
PRACTICE Protective notice commonly issued u/s 73 (not 74) at time of sanctioning refund when there is doubt about procedure.
Example: IDS refund for fertilizer — credit from (i) higher tax on packing material and (ii) lower taxable value due to subsidy to agriculturist. SCN u/s 73 on allegation that parameter (ii) is inadmissible.
Exception — Revisionary Proceedings
IF u/s 108 Where erroneous refund taken up in revisionary proceedings AND order (i) stayed AND (ii) fresh orders rejecting refund:

(a) Validly reject pending refund applications based on revisionary order
(b) Proceed to recover against APL-4 issued in revisionary proceedings
Taxpayer's Checklist Post-RFD6
SCENARIO A Protective notice u/s 73 along with RFD-6 → Reply on allegations made.
SCENARIO B RFD-6 without protective notice → Watch for 6 months if departmental appeal u/s 107(2) filed. If filed but no s.73 notice → agitate failure of mandatory dual notice procedure.
SCENARIO C No appeal filed within 6 months of RFD-6 → Watch for 3 years for RVNT in revisionary proceedings. If issued → respond to revisionary proceedings.

9.9 Protective Notice

⬆ Index
Protective Notice — Section 84 Framework
Section 84 — Two Mandates
(a) ENHANCEMENT "Another notice" must be issued to extent of enhancement of demand brought in appeal/revision. s.107(11): Appellate Authority can confirm/modify/annul. s.108(1): Revisionary Authority can enhance/modify/annul.
(b) REDUCTION Notice "not necessary" in case of reduction of demand — intimation of reduced demand in DRC-25 suffices.
Due Process Location
KEY POINT Appeal/revision only clears 'interpretation'. Demand due process is located in Chapter XV (Demands & Recovery) — NOT in Chapter XIII (Audit) or Chapter XVIII (Appeals & Revision).
Revenue's success in departmental appeal / revisionary proceedings may not lead to recovery unless DRC-7 is issued — acknowledged in Circular 423.
APL-4 ≠ DRC-7
CRITICAL GAP APL-4 does NOT contain provision to appropriate pre-deposit. Rule 142(5) does not reference s.107/108/113/117/118. APL-4 cannot be equated with DRC-7 ('notice for recovery') to activate s.79 recovery.
Only a statutory amendment (not a trade-friendly circular) can allow connection between APL-4 and s.79 recovery.
Rule 161 Amendment — Scope Clarified
CLARIFICATION Amendment to Rule 161 replacing "order" with "intimation or notice" for s.84 accommodates IBC proceedings only — Circular 187/19/2022-GST dated 27 Dec. 2022. Does NOT make DRC-25 a substitute for SCN to Corporate Debtor u/s 63/73/74/76. DRC-25 continues where DRC-7 halted — not about enhancement.
IMPASSE 'Notice of demand' ≠ 'Notice for recovery'. Circular 423 position stands — dual notice procedure mandatory in GST.

9.10 Erroneous Refund Along with Interest

⬆ Index
Erroneous Refund — Recovery & Recrediting Mechanism
Section 74 — Rarely Applicable
CAUTION Demand for repayment of erroneous refund rarely sustainable u/s 74 by imputing mala fides. Mischief by taxpayer not impossible but standard is high.
PMT-3 Recrediting — Two Scenarios
SCENARIO 1 — ECrL Refund of credit claimed and ECrL debited → on rejection (credit otherwise admissible) → recredited into ECrL via PMT-3 under Rule 86(4).
SCENARIO 2 — ECL Refund claimed debited in ECL → on rejection → recredited into ECrL via PMT-3 under Rule 87(11).
Payment by Mistake of Fact or Law
