PURPOSE Refund due to "payment by mistake" = restoration of status quo ante only. Cannot result in encashment of unutilized credit. Rule 86(4A) from 23 Mar. 2020 makes PMT-3 applicable. Unjust enrichment does not apply to State.
Rule 86(4B) — PMT3A Procedure (from 5 Jul 2022)
CONDITIONS Where refund erroneously sanctioned and taxpayer accepted repayment: Recrediting in same mode via PMT-3A — subject to: (i) Repayment in cash via DRC-3 (ii) Along with interest and mandatory penalty
Example: Refund ₹300 (CGST ₹100 + SGST ₹50 + IGST ₹150) → Recredited via PMT-3A only if cash repayment ₹300 via DRC-3 + 18% interest + penalty ₹30.
Payments During Investigation
ENTITLED Payment made during investigation liable to be refunded. Where RFD-2 issued, rejection on grounds of:
(i) Payment during investigation
(ii) Payment admits liability
(iii) No provision to sanction
— are impermissible and illegal.
Rejection must be for non-compliance with conditions/restrictions — provided no DRC-7/DRC-25 at finality or DRCID self-assessed liability.
Export Services — Repatriation Failure
DOUBLE IMPACT Failure to repatriate convertible foreign exchange:
(i) Denial of zero-rated benefit + recovery 'in cash' with interest
(ii) Demand for output tax as inter-State supply
RELIEF Rule 96A(1)(b): Commissioner may relax repatriation time limit → directory, not mandatory. Belated repatriation ≠ non-repatriation. Zero-rated benefit available. Netting off receivables with payables (RBI master directions) = repatriation.

9.11 Refund to Unregistered Persons

⬆ Index
Refund to Unregistered Persons — New Rule 89(2)(kb)
When Eligible
CONDITION Unregistered person who bore incidence of tax AND time under s.34 to collect credit note from Supplier has passed.
PRE-CONDITION Exhausting remedy of adjustment u/s 34 — not in statute but expressly imposed via Circular 188/20/2022-GST dated 27 Dec. 2022.
Procedure
MECHANISM One application per Supplier mandated. Relevant date: date of cancellation of contract of supply (not date of payment). Sanction by Proper Officer to whom 'temporary PAN-linked registration' is mapped.
Details submitted under Rule 89(2)(ka). Certificate under Rule 89(2)(m) dispensed with for such claimants.
Retroactive Coverage
EFFECTIVE Statutory amendments from 26 Dec. 2022 → unregistered persons can claim refund for cancellation of services from 27 Dec. 2020. Limitation relaxation — Notification 13/2022-Central Tax dated 5 Jul. 2022 (eclipses from 1 Mar. 2022) — available to unregistered claimants too.

9.12 Circulars and Instructions

⬆ Index
Key Circulars — Chapter 9 Jurisprudence
Circular 423/56/98-CX
SUBJECT Applicability of 'dual notice procedure' in case departmental appeal is filed against erroneous refund sanctioned.
Date: 22 Sept. 1998
Master Circular 125 — Key Paras
Para 9-12 Deficiency memo procedure (Rule 90(3))

Para 11 RFD-3 NOT re-issued for same deficiencies in rectified application

Para 20-21 Composite notice — refund rejection + demand for ineligible ITC u/s 73/74

Para 24 Fresh application after appeal success — "On Account of Appeal/Assessment" option
Other Circulars
Circular 187/19/2022-GST
27 Dec. 2022 — Rule 161 amendment for IBC proceedings. DRC-25 not substitute for SCN to Corporate Debtor.

Circular 188/20/2022-GST
27 Dec. 2022 — Exhausting s.34 remedy as pre-condition for unregistered persons.

Notification 13/2022-Central Tax
5 Jul. 2022 — Limitation relaxation for unregistered claimants.

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 9: Refund Notice

⬆ Index
These ratios, principles, and Latin maxims are for use in personal hearings, written submissions, and appellate arguments on refund matters. State them confidently, cite the case name/circular, and link them to the precise facts of your client's case.

⚖️ Key Legal Authorities & Ratios

Mafatlal Industries Ltd. & Others v. Union of India & Others
Supreme Court of India (Constitution Bench) | Para 74
Unjust enrichment does not apply to the State by the principle of parens patriae. Even in the absence of an express provision, Article 39 of the Constitution proscribes the State from perpetuating unjust enrichment against a taxpayer.
🎯 Use When: Revenue claims refund cannot be sanctioned due to unjust enrichment on State's part, or where refund has been credited to CWF wrongly.
Circular 423/56/98-CX
Central Excise | Dated: 22 September 1998 | Law Ministry Position — Apex Court Decisions
The 'dual notice procedure' — departmental appeal coupled with protective notice under Section 73/74 — is a well-established due process for recovery of erroneously sanctioned refund. Success in departmental appeal alone does not authorize recovery without a separate notice of demand under Section 73 or 74. Judicial authorities acknowledged herein cannot be lightly dismissed in GST.
🎯 Use When: Revenue attempts recovery of refund on strength of departmental appeal alone, without having issued protective notice. Argue non-issuance of dual notice is fatal to recovery.
Master Circular 125 (Para 20-21) — Composite Notice Jurisdiction
CBIC Circular | GST | Para 20-21
Proper Officer's jurisdiction during refund verification is limited to accepting or rejecting the refund. Demand for recovery of ineligible ITC via composite RFD-8 under Section 54 r/w Section 73/74 is an extended step — and where such composite notice is issued, claimant may voluntarily discharge liability via DRC-03 before notice is served to avoid demand proceedings.
🎯 Use When: Composite refund notice received demanding both rejection + ITC recovery. Argue PO exceeded jurisdiction of verification under refund rules; alternatively use DRC-03 route proactively.
Section 75(3) CGST Act — Appellate Authority Direction; 3-Year Compliance
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017
Where appeal is allowed, Proper Officer is permitted to comply with directions of Appellate Authority within three years — this is not treated as a remand. No new right accrues to the claimant — the originally accrued right is canvassed afresh. Proper Officer in second round cannot raise limitation, cannot re-examine findings of Appellate Authority.
🎯 Use When: Revenue raises limitation in second round refund proceedings or attempts to re-examine merits already decided by FAA/GSTAT. Cite Section 75(3) and Master Circular 125 Para 24 to shut down both objections.
Section 84 CGST Act — Dual Notice & DRC-25 Limitation
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | r/w Rule 142(5) & Circular 187/19/2022-GST
APL-4 cannot be equated with DRC-7 as 'notice for recovery' to activate Section 79 recovery machinery. Rule 142(5) does not include reference to Section 107/108. DRC-25 is not a substitute for a show cause notice of demand — it continues recovery from the stage where DRC-7 was halted, not a fresh notice of demand for enhanced demand.
🎯 Use When: Revenue attempts recovery via APL-4 without issuing separate DRC-7/notice of demand after departmental appeal success. Argue APL-4 ≠ DRC-7; statutory amendment needed; recovery is barred.
Section 49(9) r/w Section 54(5) CGST Act — Presumption of Unjust Enrichment
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017
For refund of output tax paid, there is a legal presumption that the incidence of tax has been passed on. This presumption is rebuttable — not by a mere self-declaration or accountant's certificate without basis, but by comprehensive evidence covering commercial arrangements, accounting records, and independent certification.
🎯 Use When: Preparing refund applications of output tax. Ensure a robust accountant's certificate covering all five parameters (a) to (e) is filed upfront to rebut presumption and avoid CWF credit.
Rule 96A(1)(b) CGST Rules — Belated Repatriation
CGST Rules, 2017 | r/w RBI Master Directions (Current Account Regulations)
Commissioner is empowered to relax the time limit for repatriation of export proceeds — making the time limit directory and not mandatory. Belated repatriation cannot be equated with non-repatriation. Zero-rated benefit available on such export of services. Netting off of receivables with payables as permitted by RBI constitutes valid repatriation.
🎯 Use When: Revenue denies zero-rated refund for service exports citing delay in repatriation of foreign exchange. Argue directional nature of time limit; produce RBI netting approval.

🔤 Latin Maxims — Chapter 9

Parens Patriae
Meaning: "Parent of the Nation" — the State stands in the role of a protector of those who cannot protect themselves. By this principle, the State cannot claim unjust enrichment against its citizens.
Use in Hearing: "The Apex Court in Mafatlal Industries has held that by the principle of parens patriae, the doctrine of unjust enrichment does not apply to the State. Article 39 of the Constitution proscribes the State from perpetuating unjust enrichment. Crediting refund to CWF without proper finding of enrichment is constitutionally impermissible."
Lis
Meaning: A dispute or legal controversy. In refund proceedings — unlike demand notices — there is no lis pending at the time of filing. The dispute emerges only once the 'right to refund' is taken for adjudication.
Use in Hearing: "In refund proceedings there is no lis at the threshold — the taxpayer is fully seized of facts and is merely 'put at notice'. Pre-notice consultations are therefore not warranted and the proceedings are of a distinct and crisp character."
Prima Facie
Meaning: "At first sight" or "on the face of it". Used in context of deficiency memo — RFD-3 is issued only for prima facie incomplete and incoherent applications, not for matters requiring application of mind to law and facts.
Use in Hearing: "The issuance of RFD-3 is limited to prima facie deficiencies apparent on the face of the application — not to substantive enquiry into eligibility. Any rejection on merits at this threshold stage is a usurpation of jurisdiction reserved for the adjudicating stage in RFD-8."
Suo Motu
Meaning: "On its own motion" — without being prompted. Used here to highlight that credit notes under s.34, if not strictly compliant, operate as suo motu sanction of refund from Revenue's perspective — an automatic tax reduction without adjudication.
Use in Hearing: "Issue of credit notes is, from Revenue's perspective, equivalent to suo motu sanction of refund. The legislature has therefore placed high standards under Section 34, and indiscriminate use draws mandatory scrutiny of circumstances, time limits and conditions."
Mutatis Mutandis
Meaning: "With the necessary changes having been made" — the due process applicable to refund claims applies equally to claims of tax adjustment via credit notes, with the appropriate modifications.
Use in Hearing: "The 'due process' in relation to refund claims applies mutatis mutandis to claims of tax adjustment via credit notes. The same rigour of proof required for refund sanction is demanded for admission of credit notes under Section 34."
Mala Fides
Meaning: "Bad faith" — wrongful intent. Used in two contexts: (i) Revenue's frivolous rejection of refund on bad faith grounds invites equity-based interest; (ii) Section 74 demand for erroneous refund on imputation of mala fides on taxpayer is rarely sustainable.
Use in Hearing: "Rejection of this refund on patently frivolous grounds constitutes bad faith on the part of Revenue and exposes the Department to interest claims in equity. Conversely, Section 74 invocation against our client requires imputation of mala fides — a high threshold not met on these facts."
Status Quo Ante
Meaning: "The state of things as they were before" — restoration to the original position. Refund due to "payment by mistake of fact or law" is only to restore status quo ante; it cannot result in encashment of unutilized credit.
Use in Hearing: "The purpose of refund in the case of payment by mistake is the restoration of status quo ante. It is not an occasion for the claimant to convert unutilized credit into cash — only the amount paid erroneously in cash may be refunded."
Sine Qua Non
Meaning: "Without which not" — an indispensable condition. Repatriation of convertible foreign exchange is the sine qua non for availing zero-rated benefit on export of services.
Use in Hearing: "While repatriation of export proceeds is sine qua non for zero-rating, the time limit for such repatriation is directory under Rule 96A(1)(b), and belated repatriation — as established by our client — cannot deprive the claimant of zero-rated benefit."
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 9 — Refund Notice
⚖️ Chapter 10 — e-Way Bill Notice
CHAPTER 10

Notice for e-Way Bill

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index — Click to Navigate

10.1 Overview 10.2 Binary Verification 10.3 Alteration of Scope by Circular 10.4 Extra Legislative Enquiry 10.5 Cryptic Notice 10.6 Misplaced Responses 10.7 Pleading for Lenience 10.8 Secure Release Immediately 10.9 Essential Fact to be Documented 10.10 Remediation in Appeal 10.11 Risks of Revisionary Proceedings 10.12 Circulars and Instructions ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

10.1 Overview — Section 129 Proceedings

⬆ Index
e-Way Bill Notice — Section 68 r/w Section 129
Nature of Proceedings
UNIQUE CONSTRAINT Carried out under severe constraints of time and information. Due process of a whole new order compared to other GST notices.
Interception in presence of person-in-charge of conveyance — not Supplier or Recipient. No clarity on form of supply, classification or valuation expected.
RESULT Proper Officer constrained to lean in favour of imposing penalty when prima facie discrepancies observed.
Sole Point for Consideration
ONLY TEST Compliance with Section 68 — produce documents prescribed in Rule 138A. That is the short (or lone) point for consideration.
Procedural Flow — MOV Forms
Interception → Statement in MOV-1
↓
Verification in MOV-2 → Report in MOV-4
↓
Release in MOV-5
OR
Detention in MOV-6
↓
Notice in MOV-7 → Bond in MOV-8
↓
Order in MOV-9
Governing Circulars
PROCEDURE Circular 41 — Due process of interception, examination, detention, notice, order

Circular 49 — Amendment to Circular 41

Circular 64 — Guidance on bona fide vs mala fide non-compliance; manner of exercising discretion in penalty

10.2 Binary Verification

⬆ Index
Binary Nature of Verification — Rule 138A
The Binary Test
ONLY TWO OPTIONS Either prescribed documents under Rule 138A are available — or they are not. There is no third possibility that law admits.
IF AVAILABLE Documents are real and available → Proper Officer has no further authority to test propriety of self-assessment. Launching into associated investigation is clearly barred.
IF NOT AVAILABLE Pre-defined course of action in s.129(1)(a) or (b) must be proceeded with. No discretion.
Penalty Scenarios — MOV-7
ScenarioMOV-7 WordsPenalty
Invoice available, EWB missings.129(1)(a)200% of tax
Neither invoice nor EWBs.129(1)(b)Higher of 50% of market value OR 200% of tax
Provisional Release — MOV-5 / MOV-8
RARELY FOLLOWED Law permits provisional release in MOV-5 on bond in MOV-8 u/s 129(1)(c). Rarely seen in practice especially when taxpayers are located outside the State.
Appeal — Pre-Deposit Trap
CRITICAL TRAP Payment of penalty = pre-condition for release. Appeals entertained u/s 107(1) treating payment as 100% pre-deposit for s.107(6) purposes (notwithstanding finality in s.129(3)).
WARNING — APL-1 In APL-1, amount discharged must be reported as 'disputed' penalty (NOT 'undisputed' demand in Table 15) — otherwise additional pre-deposit under proviso to s.107(6) becomes necessary.

10.3 Alteration of Scope by Circular 64

⬆ Index
Circular 64 — New Category: Mala Fide Movement
What Circular 64 Introduces
NEW CATEGORY Creates a category of non-compliance beyond plain words of s.68 and s.129: "Mala fide movement" — discrepancy in documents must palpably point to movement with intent to evade tax.
Issued u/s 168 — taxpayers may cite this circular where misapplication is faced. Provides guidance to Proper Officer in a transparent manner.
Two-Edged Effect
TAXPAYER BENEFIT Illustrations in Circular 64 open up possibility for taxpayers to seek nominal penalty — allows pre-defined course in s.129(1)(a)/(b) to be reconsidered by PO.
BURDEN SHIFT Having admitted bona fide transactions deserve lenience — establishing bona fides becomes onerous, discretionary and arbitrary. PO cannot reject bona fides lightly.
Non-Compliance Classification
CERTAIN RULE Every non-compliance must be classified as either:
(a) Bona fide non-compliance — inadvertent, no intent to evade
(b) Mala fide non-compliance — intent to evade palpable
PRACTICAL REALITY Nearly every instance of interception and penalty can be expected to conclude not before reaching Appellate Authority.

10.4 Extra Legislative Enquiry

⬆ Index
Delegate Cannot Expand Authority — Doctrine
Core Legal Doctrine
TRITE LAW The 'delegate' — one vested with authority — cannot expand the scope of that authority while exercising it. To do so = attempt at legislating. No emergency can authorize expansion of authority.
Delegate must act within the scope of authority vested. Intercepting Officers fuelled by earlier tax regime experience cannot 'sense' evasion and expand limited powers conferred by Legislature.
What Proper Officer Cannot Do
BARRED MOV-7 admitting Rule 138A compliance but alleging undervaluation or misclassification = Extra-legislative enquiry. NOT within terms of reference of s.68 r/w s.129.
ALSO BARRED Collecting intelligence (Inter-State Intelligence Report re 'non-existent' Supplier, eVaahan Report from Toll Plaza) and issuing comprehensive notice for fraudulent transaction u/s 129 and 122(1A) = usurpation of authority. Will be dismissed as excess of authority in s.68 r/w s.129.
Correct Procedure If Intelligence Available
PROPER ROUTE Secure authorisation u/s 67 to same or another Proper Officer → Launch proper inspection → Issue notice for all attendant actionable causes u/s 74 r/w s.129 (extending to s.130 and s.132).

10.5 Cryptic Notice

⬆ Index
Cryptic Notice — Validity Standard under Section 160(1)
Sufficiency Standard
VALID IF Notice need NOT be elaborate. Saving provision in s.160(1): sufficient if "intents, purposes and requirements" are conveyed to a knowledgeable taxpayer who carries out self-assessment of liability.
Valid MOV-7 Example: "Conveyance intercepted on…, consignment vide tax invoice…dated… without complete address in EWB Part A, hence detained. Penalty proposed 200% of tax u/s 129(1)(a)." — This is sufficient.
When Notice Can Be Faulted
ONLY IF Short description can be faulted ONLY if it is impossible for a knowledgeable taxpayer to comprehend:
(i) Violation involved
(ii) Allegation and evidence to support
(iii) Action proposed
(iv) Opportunity — time, place and authority — to present defence

10.6 Misplaced Responses

⬆ Index
What to Say — and What NOT to Say — in Reply to MOV-7
✓ Correct Response — 5 Points
INCLUDE (a) Facts of the transaction
(b) Availability of EWB — valid and current or expired
(c) Plea of reasons for EWB deficiency — clerical error is most common
(d) Movement covered by valid invoice and entered in contemporaneous records of all parties
(e) Plead that full penalty equates all deficiencies to be mala fide — contrary to judicial authorities and administrative instructions
PRINCIPLE Take position on facts available — supported by evidence available at the time of interception from records produced.
✗ Incorrect Response — Avoid These
DO NOT INCLUDE Loading MOV-7 reply with court decisions from other States/UTs or complex legal principles — does NOT advance taxpayer's cause before Proper Officer for release.
IRRELEVANT Long cumbersome responses pleading bona fides by way of:
(i) Consignor is a genuine person
(ii) Consignment urgently required
(iii) Detention affecting livelihood
(iv) GST is a simple tax
(v) EWB portal too complicated
(vi) Knowledgeable staff not affordable
All genuine difficulties — but all irrelevant for MOV-7 purposes.
CONTRADICTION Extracting information from taxpayer's records but then imposing full penalty imputing mala fides is a contradiction — use this in response.
Golden Rule
PRINCIPLE Do not plead hardship. Do not appeal to good conscience.

Confine response to the limited terms of reference in Section 129. Proper Officer would then be well within contours of law to allow the relief prayed.

10.7 Pleading for Lenience

⬆ Index
Discretion in Penalty — Why Lenience Pleading Fails
Discretion — Root of Corruption
INSIGHT Discretion in penalty is the root of corruption. Society's demand to eliminate corruption = demand to eliminate discretion. But when inadvertent slips occur, taxpayers plead lenience — a contradiction.
Pleading lenience or parading innocence has worked for a long time — but not anymore. GST limits enquiry to compliance with prescribed documents in Rule 138A.
Standard for Bona Fide Determination
MANDATORY Once non-compliance is located — examine if bona fide or mala fide. Determination of bona fides cannot be:
(i) Undisclosed
(ii) Secretive
(iii) Whimsical
Must be based on cogent reasons and reliable material taken on record.
NO ROOM Legislature allows no room for use of undisclosed discretion in bona fide determination.

10.8 Secure Release Immediately

⬆ Index
First Priority — Secure Release of Detained Consignment
Why Immediate Release is Critical
DANGER OF DELAY Delay raises new presumption against taxpayer's claim. Permitting detention can lead to belief that e-auction u/s 129(6) may be justified.
WHY NOT EXPECT RELIEF FROM PO KI Shepard v. UOI AIR 1988 SC 686 (Apex Court):
"Once a decision has been taken, there is a tendency to uphold it and representation may not really yield any fruitful purpose."
→ Same PO who recorded MOV-6 unlikely to reconsider MOV-7 findings. Next available Officer = Appellate Authority.
Route 1 — Bond in MOV-8
IF PO WILLING File application u/s 129(1)(c) for release against bond in MOV-8. Every detention must be followed by such an application — regardless of whether PO informed taxpayer of this option or whether application is allowed or not.

Will furnish grounds in appeal.
Route 2 — Payment Under Protest
IF BOND NOT POSSIBLE Appearance of Consignor/Consignee must be placed on record. Application u/s 129(1)(c) = bulwark against PO invoking s.129(1)(b). Then make payment 'under protest' of penalty u/s 129(1)(a).
KEY PRINCIPLE Payment of penalty under protest = NOT admission of wrongdoing. Only to secure release of consignment with right to audience in appeal.
What is Irrelevant
IRRELEVANT Financial hardship of taxpayer — of no relevance when non-compliance is undisputed and bona fides debatable. Arguments on bona fides best canvassed before a different Officer — i.e., Appellate Authority.

10.9 Essential Fact to be Documented

⬆ Index
The One Essential Fact — Owner Has Come Forward
The Essential Fact
MUST DOCUMENT "Owner of the goods has come forward to attend to these proceedings."

Proper Officer must be 'put at notice' of this essential fact — even when penalty imposed is not agreeable.
Why It Matters — Critical Distinction
CRITICAL Where essential fact is 'placed on record' → relief will not evade taxpayer.

Where omitted → Proper Officer's finding in MOV-9 that s.129(1)(b) is attracted cannot be easily impeached in later proceedings. Uphill task to establish s.129(1)(a).
COMMON PROBLEM MOV-6 and MOV-9 often addressed to person in MOV-1 — usually the driver. Later proceedings addressed by owner (Consignor/Consignee) depending on passage of title at point of interception.
Three-Step Mandatory Checklist
ON EVERY INTERCEPTION (a) Application seeking option u/s 129(1)(c) is filed

(b) Authorization to 'driver' to act on behalf of owner (Consignor/Consignee) is issued

(c) Application for provisional release against bond in MOV-8 is filed
If these three are clearly documented — even if refused by PO — taxpayer's interests will be secure. Then deposit penalty demanded in MOV-9 and secure release.

10.10 Remediation in Appeal

⬆ Index
Appellate Stage — MOV-9 Must Stand Scrutiny
Appellate Authority's Examination
STANDARD Appellate Authority examines PO's actions on basis of material on record contrasted with the 'essential fact' documented and grounds put forward by taxpayer. MOV-9 must stand scrutiny — not merely display PO's strict action.
NOTE Every appeal does not obligate AA to interfere and overturn PO's decision. But close-minded and hasty proceedings will be exposed.
What Makes Appeal Compelling
STRONG CASE IF Case carefully guarded by:
(i) Documenting facts surrounding interception
(ii) Placing relevant material on record
(iii) Showing bona fides
(iv) Questioning presumption of mala fides
(v) Demonstrating prejudice in rejection of application u/s 129(1)(c)
(vi) Payment of penalty 'under protest'

→ Makes it too compelling for AA to 'save the demand' for penalty.
Grounds of Appeal — Correct Focus
NOT JUST LENIENCE Grounds of appeal must bring above aspects to attention — not be limited to pleading lenience.
CAUTION Impoverished yet favourable order in appeal will be short-lived if grounds for entertaining appeal and reconsideration not shown to be justified — Orders of AA are open to revisionary proceedings.

10.11 Risks of Revisionary Proceedings

⬆ Index
Revisionary Authority — Wider Latitude than Appellate Authority
Appellate Authority vs Revisionary Authority
KEY DISTINCTION Appellate Authority: NOT empowered to 're-do' adjudication carried out by Proper Officer.

Revisionary Authority: Free to 're-do'. Wields more latitude than Appellate Authority or even Appellate Tribunal.
Prejudice to Revenue — Trigger for Revision
RISK Prejudice to Revenue apparent in nearly every AA order overturning PO's full penalty. Often AA imposes penalty under an altogether different provision — 'to do justice'. This invites revisionary proceedings.
Example: MOV-9 imposed penalty u/s 129 of ₹10 lakhs. AA set aside u/s 129(1)(a) for absence of mala fides, imposed nominal ₹10,000 u/s 125. → Revisionary Authority can re-examine the entire matter.
Strategic Implication for Taxpayer
STRATEGY Keep revisionary proceedings in mind at the time of submitting first response against MOV-7. The first response must be crafted with revisionary scrutiny in view — not just for immediate release.
Revisionary Authority is NOT the last fact-finding authority — that privilege belongs to Appellate Tribunal. But Revisionary Authority wields more latitude than both AA and Appellate Tribunal.

10.12 Circulars and Instructions

⬆ Index
Key Circulars — Chapter 10 Jurisprudence
Circular 41/15/2018-GST
DATE: 13 Apr 2018 Entire procedure of interception, examination, detention, notice and order under s.68 r/w s.129 — Chapter XVI of Rules. Guides 'due process'. Amended by Circular 49.
Circular 49/23/2018-GST
DATE: 21 Jun 2018 Amendment to Circular 41. Updated procedure for interception proceedings.
Circular 64/38/2018-GST
DATE: 14 Sept 2018 Fresh guidance to Proper Officer to examine bona fide cases involving non-compliance. Introduced mala fide/bona fide classification. Issued u/s 168 — taxpayers may cite directly.

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 10: Notice for e-Way Bill

⬆ Index
These ratios, principles, and Latin maxims are for use in personal hearings before FAA, GSTAT, and written responses to MOV-7. State them confidently and link them to the precise facts of interception. In e-Way Bill hearings — speed, brevity and precision matter more than eloquence.

⚖️ Key Legal Authorities & Ratios

KI Shepard v. Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1988 SC 686
Once a decision has been taken, there is a tendency to uphold it and representation may not really yield any fruitful purpose. Independence of perspective is therefore essential for effective review of the decision to detain.
🎯 Use When: Arguing that the same Proper Officer who recorded MOV-6 (detention warranted) cannot be expected to reconsider MOV-7 effectively — independence of Appellate Authority is the appropriate forum. Use to justify why you are appealing rather than relying on reconsideration by the same PO.
Section 160(1) CGST Act — Saving Provision for Cryptic Notice
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017
A notice is not invalid merely because it is brief or cryptic — provided it conveys the intents, purposes and requirements to a knowledgeable taxpayer who carries out self-assessment. Short notice cannot be faulted unless it is impossible for such a taxpayer to comprehend the violation, allegation, proposed action and opportunity to defend.
🎯 Use When: Revenue argues that a brief MOV-7 is invalid or insufficient. Cut both ways — use to show that the brief notice gave sufficient notice to taxpayer, or conversely, where the notice is genuinely incapable of comprehension, challenge its validity on this standard.
Circular 64/38/2018-GST — Bona Fide vs Mala Fide Standard
CBIC Circular | Dated: 14 September 2018 | Issued under Section 168
Non-compliance must be classified as either bona fide or mala fide. Where documents are available but carry a deficiency, discrepancy must palpably point to movement with intent to evade tax before mala fide inference is drawn. Bona fide non-compliance deserves lenient treatment and imposition of nominal penalty. Taxpayers may cite this circular directly where misapplication is faced.
🎯 Use When: Revenue imposes maximum penalty under s.129(1)(a) or (b) for a mere technical deficiency — incomplete address, minor EWB error, expired EWB where consignment was genuine. Cite Circular 64 to demand bona fide classification and argue that equating all deficiencies to mala fides is contrary to this circular.
Delegate Cannot Expand Authority — Trite Law of Administrative Jurisdiction
Well-Established Principle | Applied in EWB Context: Section 68 r/w Section 129
The delegate — one vested with authority — cannot expand the scope of that authority while exercising it. To do so would be to attempt legislation. No emergency can authorize expansion of authority. The scope of verification in EWB proceedings is limited to compliance with Rule 138A — not valuation, classification, or extended intelligence-based enquiry.
🎯 Use When: MOV-7 alleges undervaluation or misclassification despite Rule 138A compliance, OR where PO has collected intelligence and issued comprehensive notice u/s 129 and 122(1A). Argue excess of jurisdiction — PO should have proceeded under s.67 for inspection instead.
Section 129(1)(c) CGST Act — Right to Provisional Release on Bond
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017
A detained consignment may be provisionally released on execution of bond in MOV-8. Application for this option must be made and placed on record on every detention — regardless of whether PO informs taxpayer of this option or whether the application is allowed. Filing the application furnishes grounds in appeal and acts as a bulwark against invocation of s.129(1)(b) higher penalty.
🎯 Use When: Arguing in appeal that PO wrongly imposed s.129(1)(b) penalty (higher of 50% of market value or 200% of tax) when taxpayer had filed application for s.129(1)(c) release — demonstrating owner came forward — making s.129(1)(a) the applicable provision.
Payment Under Protest — Not Admission of Wrongdoing
Settled Legal Principle | Applicable in GST EWB Proceedings
Payment of penalty as a pre-condition for release of consignment is not admission of wrongdoing of the kind that attracts the full force of penalty. It is payment only to secure release of consignment with the right to audience in appeal. Such payment constitutes 100% pre-deposit for purposes of s.107(6) and the appeal is maintainable.
🎯 Use When: Revenue argues that payment of penalty estops taxpayer from contesting the demand in appeal, or that payment amounts to admission. Also use to prevent Revenue from arguing additional pre-deposit is required under s.107(6) proviso — but ensure amount reported as 'disputed' in Table 15 of APL-1.

🔤 Latin Maxims — Chapter 10

Prima Facie
Meaning: "At first sight" or "on the face of it." Proper Officer is constrained to lean in favour of imposing penalty when prima facie discrepancies are observed — given the constraints of time and information at the point of interception.
Use in Hearing: "The Proper Officer's jurisdiction at the point of interception is limited to examining prima facie compliance with Rule 138A. Where documents are present and real, even if carrying a minor deficiency, there is no prima facie case for the imposition of maximum penalty which requires a finding of intent to evade."
Bona Fide / Mala Fide
Meaning: "Good faith" / "Bad faith." The central classification in EWB proceedings post-Circular 64. Bona fide non-compliance = inadvertent, no intent to evade. Mala fide non-compliance = discrepancy palpably points to intent to evade tax.
Use in Hearing: "The discrepancy in this case — an incomplete address in EWB Part A — is a clerical error with no bearing on the identity of the Supplier, Recipient, or value of goods. The movement was bona fide in every respect. Imposition of maximum penalty equates all deficiencies to mala fides, which is expressly contrary to Circular 64 and the judicial authorities cited therein."
Delegatus Non Potest Delegare
Meaning: "A delegate cannot delegate" — and, by extension, a delegate cannot expand the authority that was delegated. Proper Officer under s.68/129 cannot step into the shoes of an officer exercising inspection powers under s.67.
Use in Hearing: "It is trite law that delegatus non potest delegare — the Proper Officer vested with authority under s.68 r/w s.129 cannot expand that authority to conduct a valuation or classification enquiry. Such enquiry falls within s.67 jurisdiction. The notice in MOV-7 alleging undervaluation is an excess of jurisdiction and liable to be quashed."
Status Quo Ante
Meaning: "The state of things as they were before." Provisional release on bond restores the status quo ante — goods are returned to their lawful movement pending adjudication of the penalty.
Use in Hearing: "The object of Section 129(1)(c) is to restore status quo ante — allowing the consignment to reach its destination while the dispute on penalty is adjudicated. Refusal to entertain the application for provisional release despite the owner coming forward is prejudicial to taxpayer and defeats this statutory purpose."
Audi Alteram Partem
Meaning: "Hear the other side." A fundamental principle of natural justice — notice must provide genuine opportunity to present defence. Even in summary EWB proceedings, the taxpayer must be given opportunity to comprehend the violation, allegation, proposed action and time/place to defend.
Use in Hearing: "The principle of audi alteram partem demands that even in summary proceedings under Section 129, the notice in MOV-7 must be capable of enabling a knowledgeable taxpayer to comprehend the allegation and mount a defence. A notice that is not so capable is invalid despite the saving provision in s.160(1)."
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 10 — Notice for e-Way Bill
⚖️ Chapter 11 — Penalty Notice
CHAPTER 11

Notice for Penalty

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index — Click to Navigate

11.1 Overview — Chapter XIX 11.2 Pre-Requisites to Notice 11.3 Exception to Pre-Notice Consultations 11.4 Accompanying Summary 11.5 Notice Under Alternative Sections 11.6 Statutory Definition of Infraction 11.7 Concept of Animus 11.8 General Discipline on Penalty 11.9 Personal Penalty to Director 11.10 Penalty Under Section 52 11.11 Waiver of Penalty Notice 11.12 Circulars and Instructions ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

11.1 Overview — Chapter XIX Penalty Framework

⬆ Index
Penalty — Nature, Chapter XIX Map & Critical Debate
Nature of Penalty
PUNITIVE Penalty is intended to be punitive — high enough to:
(i) Rob perpetrator of fruits of misadventure
(ii) Deter others from the same misadventure
Chapter XIX — Section Map
SectionPurpose
122Specific penalty
123Non-filing of information returns*
124Non-filing of statistical information**
125General penalty
127Non-specified penalty
129Penalty — consignment without prescribed documents
130Redemption fine in-lieu of confiscation
*s.150 info returns not yet prescribed | **s.151 format not notified
Critical Debate — s.74(1) vs s.122(2)(b)
RAGING DEBATE s.74(1) appears to impose penalty on its own. BUT due to express language in s.122(2)(b) — penalty is only imposable under s.122(2) for all demands against registered persons. No penalty under s.74(1) independent of s.122(2)(b).
CANNOT Notice for penalty under s.122(1)/(1A)/(3) cannot operate in isolation — cannot operate de hors safeguards in s.75. Cannot be issued independent of administrative discipline due to s.126(3) mandate.
KEY QUESTION Can Chapter XIX penalty notice be independently issued — or must it be issued conjointly with s.73 or s.74?
Rule 142(1) — Cannot Opt Out
NO OPT-OUT Requirement of valid notice cannot be 'opted out' by indiscreet taxpayer merely because Rule 142(1) lists all 7 penalty provisions to be accompanied by summary in DRC-1.

11.2 Pre-Requisites to Notice

⬆ Index
Pre-Requisites — Animus is the Central Ingredient
Not Every Demand = Penalty
NOT AUTOMATIC Not every demand will automatically and irrefutably attract penalty. Infraction of law must go beyond mere 'non-payment of tax or inadmissible claim of credit'.
REQUIRED Necessary ingredient of animus or intent to commit such infraction of law must be shown.
No Mechanical Imposition
VOID IF MECHANICAL Penalty of, say, 10% under s.73(11) imposed in all cases — liable to be struck down as mechanical application. Must be shown in each case based on cogent reasons and reliable material taken on record.
Bad SCN: Demanding output tax for GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B mismatch "along with applicable interest and penalty of 10% as per applicable provisions." — Penalty part unsustainable.
Due Process for Penalty Determination
ADVERSE CONSEQUENCE Penalty determination is an 'adverse consequence' — cannot be determined without following 'due process' with full force of natural justice — not less than general principles of 'fairness of procedure'.
s.73(5) RELIEF Where infraction dispensed with on payment without contest within 30 days of notice — it would be discriminatory to demand penalty mechanically when such dispensation option exists.
Example: SCN u/s 73 for inadmissible exemption (unregistered person, exempt goods) → bona fide misapplication → no penalty if tax discharged with interest.
Incurable Deficiency
FATAL Omission to bring allegations sufficient to support the ingredients needed to fit the facts within the operative penalty provision = incurable deficiency — demand unsustainable in adjudication or appeal.

11.3 Exception to Pre-Notice Consultations

⬆ Index
Pre-Notice Consultations — Applicability Map Across All Penalty Sections
s.122(1) and s.122(2) — Applies
APPLICABLE Pre-notice consultations apply to s.73/74 — confirmed by s.142(1A). Since penalty under s.122(1)/(2) requires notice under s.73/74, pre-notice consultation courtesy automatically extends to these penalty proceedings.
s.122(1A) and s.122(3) — By Extension
BY EXTENSION Notice for penalty u/s 122(1A)/(3) usually conjoint with notice for demand against another taxable person / registered person → pre-notice consultations apply by extension.
s.52, s.63, s.76 — Does NOT Apply
EXPRESSLY EXCLUDED Pre-notice consultations do not apply to s.52, s.63, s.76 — confirmed from language of s.142(1A).
s.125 and s.127 — Not Expressly, Yet...
NUANCE Not expressly applicable in s.125/127 but taxpayers not prejudiced — in demand cases penalty brings lis to speedy conclusion, but in s.125/127 notices, penalty itself is the lis.
s.129 and s.130
DIFFERENT MANNER s.129: Pre-notice consultation conducted differently — penalty for extraneous actions, prima facie case made on detention in MOV-6.
s.130: 'Redemption fine' — taxpayer 'opts' to discharge to overcome loss of property by confiscation.
Pre-Notice = Vested Right
PRINCIPLE Pre-notice consultation is a remedy and a vested right. Cannot be bypassed or omitted due to oversight unless expressly excepted in the statute. Taxpayer's objections begin from this point and continue till conclusion of proceedings.

11.4 Accompanying Summary

⬆ Index
DRC-1 — Mandatory Companion to Penalty Notice
Mandatory Requirement
RULE 142(1) Notice for penalty MUST be accompanied by summary of demand made in DRC-1. Demands in GST — whether for tax, credit, refunds or penalties — must appear on Common Portal to be recovered without exception.
DRC-1 Alone Is Insufficient
NOT ENOUGH Merely issuing accompanying summary (DRC-1) in case of demand for penalty will NOT meet the exacting standards of 'due process'. DRC-1 must accompany a notice issued in accordance with law — not substitute it.
Both Documents = First Step in Law
FIRST STEP Both notice + accompanying summary = first step in setting the law in motion. Failure in this first step is not only incurable but also fatal to recoverability of this demand.
Notice must contain all 'ingredients' necessary to show how exactly were taxpayer's omissions satisfying the ingredients in the statutory definition of the specific penalty proposed.
Animus Must Be Shown
PENALTY ≠ UNIVERSAL Penalty does NOT apply in every instance of non-payment of tax (or credit or refund) unless the required animus is shown to exist.

11.5 Notice Under Alternative Sections

⬆ Index
Plurality of Penalty Sections — Embargo under s.75(13)
Rule on Plurality
CANNOT Notice demanding penalty cannot be issued under two or more provisions unless the infraction of law authorises separate and independent actionable causes.
s.75(13) EMBARGO Even though notice may take cognizance of penalty under different provisions — adjudication must conclude with any one of them.
Danger of Plurality in Notice
DOUBLE-EDGED Plurality of actionable causes seems prudent for Revenue — but it can be seen as:
(i) Incomplete enquiry
(ii) Ambiguity in notice

Result: Allegations may be insufficient to adjudicate and confirm penalty under any of those specific provisions.
TRAP FOR TAXPAYER Guilt-ridden taxpayers who choose to 'plead leniency' fail to notice the incompleteness of enquiry or ambiguity in the notice. Transaction must be clearly identified + infraction shown to exist precisely.

11.6 Statutory Definition of Infraction

⬆ Index
Section 122(1) — 21 Offences: Act / Purpose / Outcome Framework
Core Rule
MUST FIT Every non-payment ≠ evasion. Transaction considered infraction must fit the statutory definition precisely. Statutory definition of offence must be demonstrated in notice. Evidence of the 'invisible ingredient' necessary to implicate taxpayer must be adduced.
Section 122(1) — All 21 Offences Mapped
ClauseAct or OmissionAttempted PurposeOutcome Achieved
(i)Supplies taxable goods/services without invoiceConceals liabilityTaxes owed not discharged
(ii)Issues tax invoice without actual supplyAsserts existence of actual suppliesEnables credit to Recipient
(iii)Collects tax via invoice but retains >3 monthsRetains taxes collected and utilises moniesTDS owed not discharged
(iv)Collects tax on non-taxable supply, retains >3 monthsConceals trail about taxable suppliesTaxes owed not discharged
(v)Omits to deduct TDS or deducts less or fails to remitConceals trail about taxable supplies made by SupplierTaxes owed (by Supplier) not discharged
(vi)Omits to collect TCS or collects less or fails to remitConceals trail about taxable supplies made by SupplierTaxes owed (by Supplier) not discharged
(vii)Avails ITC without actual inward supplyClaims credit freely available to utilizeCarries credit ready to utilize or actually utilizes
(viii)Claims refund by misrepresenting eligibilityClaims and collects refund by misrepresentationRefund inadmissible but for misrepresentation
(ix)Takes or distributes credit as ISDTransfers credit to other distinct personsDistinct persons utilize credit and discharge output tax
(x)Prepares/presents false accounts or recordsConceals liability or renders undetectableLiability goes undetected
(xi)Omits to registerOmits to discharge liabilityTaxes owed not discharged
(xii)Registers with false informationMisleads Revenue about taxable person/transactionAttaches liability to some other person
(xiii)Obstructs Proper Officer in discharge of dutiesRenders detection of liability impossibleTaxes owed not discharged
(xiv)Transports taxable goods without prescribed documentsConceals liability or renders undetectableTaxes owed not discharged
(xv)-(xvii)Suppresses turnover / Fails to maintain records / Fails to furnish info demanded by POConceals liability or renders undetectableTaxes owed not discharged
(xviii)Supplies or transports goods liable to confiscationOmits to discharge liabilityTaxes owed not discharged
(xix)Issues invoice belonging to another personClaims credit freely available to utilizeCarries credit ready to utilize or actually utilizes
(xx)Tampers with evidenceConceals liability or renders undetectableLiability goes undetected
(xxi)Disposes of/tampers with goods detained (s.129), seized (s.67(2)/130(1)) or attached (s.83)Renders detection impossible — active steps to cause disappearanceRenders impossible to fasten demand
KEY EXAMPLES s.122(1)(i): Issuing bill of supply (not tax invoice) is self-assessment that exemption is admissible — not ipso facto penalty under (i)(a). Must show action incompatible with self-assessment that exemption is not admissible.
s.122(1)(iii): Non-remittance due to lethargy or clerical error ≠ offence unless monies collected are mingled with working capital. Collection = actual realization — not accrual by issuing invoice.
s.122(1)(iv): Misunderstanding about non-taxability incompatible with this offence. Requires knowledge of non-liability + deceptive collection + non-remittance + appropriation.

11.7 Concept of Animus

⬆ Index
Animus — The Mental Element Required for Penalty
What is Animus
DEFINITION Penalty requires prior knowledge of consequences of misadventure when self-assessment was exercised and advantageous interpretation adopted which stands disproved in proceedings.

NOT 'intention to evade' (that is s.132 super-added ingredient) — but lack of belief in truth and accuracy of interpretation adopted during self-assessment.
Animus + Benefit = Mischievous Application
ESCALATION Animus alone = misapplication of law
Animus + accrual of benefit or advantage = mischievous application of law → supports imposition of penalty
s.134 Section 134 raises rebuttable presumption of mental state — but only for prosecution under s.132. No such presumption exists for offences under s.122 to s.129.
Who Bears Penalty — Section Map
APPLICABILITY s.122(1): Taxable persons
s.122(1A): 'Master mind' of invoice-credit-refund racket
s.122(2): Registered persons (facing demand u/s 73/74)
s.122(3): Any person
UNREGISTERED PERSONS Facing demand u/s 73 (not 74) → NO penalty under s.122(2) or s.122(1).
Facing notice u/s 74 (deliberate mischief) → Penalty under s.122(1) — if misadventure meets any of the clauses.
KEY DISTINCTION Animus needed for s.122(1) is not required when taxpayer is already registered and liable under s.73/74 (s.122(2) truncated burden). Mere allegation of liability against unregistered taxable persons is insufficient to impose penalty under s.122(1).

11.8 General Discipline on Penalty — Section 126

⬆ Index
Section 126 — Four Key Learnings on Penalty Discipline
(a) Curable Omissions — No Penalty
EXEMPT Curable omissions not amounting to offence — where existence of animus and outcome from misadventure are absent — will not attract penalty provided same are duly redressed and all dues discharged.
(b) Absence of Animus — Insufficient
NOT SUFFICIENT Absence of animus shown by use of deliberate device to conceal liability OR render it undetectable — and non-payment due to gross negligence — will not be sufficient to attract penalty.
(c) Mitigation — Permitted
PERMITTED Mitigation of liability to penalty is permitted by exonerating actions of taxpayer. Mitigating factors depend on facts and circumstances of each case.
(d) Hearing — Mandatory
MANDATORY No imposition of penalty without granting opportunity to be heard. Nature and grounds in notice, extent of opportunity and procedure will vary by section in Chapter XIX. Non-speaking Orders frustrate due process and render penalty unsustainable.

11.9 Personal Penalty to Director

⬆ Index
Section 122(3) — Natural Persons Behind Legal Entity Mischief
Legal Entity Actions = Natural Persons' Actions
PRINCIPLE All actions of legal entities are the outcome of actions by individuals — Directors in Companies or Partners in Firms. No action attributable to legal entities is systemic without the active engagement by one or more natural persons.
Conjoint Penalty under s.122(3)
COMPATIBLE Allegations of deliberate actions attracting penalty against legal entity = entirely compatible with penalty under s.122(3) against natural persons (Directors/Partners).
OMISSION IS FATAL Omission to impose penalty under s.122(3) against natural persons = good grounds to assail the allegation against legal entities independently. Failure to identify the mind behind the mischief indicates deficiencies in inquiry against the legal entity.
Revenue's Obligation
DUAL OBLIGATION Revenue is welcome to forego action against natural persons — but cannot omit to address the role of natural persons in describing the process of discovering liability of legal entities. Lenience against natural persons + strictness against legal entities = strange application of this law.

11.10 Penalty Under Section 52 — E-Commerce Operators

⬆ Index
Section 52(12) — Penalty on E-Commerce Operator (TCS)
Quantum and Trigger
PENALTY Up to ₹25,000 each in addition to any penalties under s.122 — for failure to furnish information demanded in notice u/s 52(12) within 15 days of such notice. Notice precedes penalty.
Scope of Notice
Scope of Operator ActivityFailure Attracts Penalty
Supplies notified under s.9(5)s.52 not applicable
Supplier supplies through OperatorDetails of such supplies omitted to be furnished within 15 days of notice
Operator permits Supplier to use Operator's premises as 'additional place of business'Details of stock of transactions through Supplier from such premises omitted within 15 days of notice
Notice Must Be Confined
DISTINCTION Unlike penalty from infraction of law, penalty u/s 52 demands notice confined to scope of information that may be sought by PO (not below rank of Deputy Commissioner). Omission to collect TCS exposes Operator to deposit dues u/s 52(2) — even if recourse against Supplier is no longer effective.

11.11 Waiver of Penalty Notice

⬆ Index
Waiver of Notice — 'Promises of Closure' — Extreme Caution Required
The Concept
UNWRITTEN RULE When no lis exists and taxpayer consents to demand — nothing more to be done. Taxpayer can 'opt out' of adjudication by plain-paper letter (or email) accepting liability, waiving requirement for notice and even right to appeal or claim refund.
Official acceptance in Master Circular 1053 under earlier tax regime — being imported into GST piecemeal. GST does not contain express provision extending legislative approval to its continuation.
The Danger — 'Promises of Closure'
EXTREME CAUTION Taxpayers must be extremely cautious when 'promises of closure' are made — even well-meaning functionaries are subject to compulsions. "When in doubt about promise, doubt the promise."
Example: ₹1,00,000 detected in audit (s.65). Promised closure if tax + interest discharged after ADT-2. After payment → SCN issued demanding 25% penalty on grounds: (i) s.74(6) closure forfeited due to non-payment of 15% penalty under s.74(5) before SCN, and (ii) audit detection = 'special circumstances' to invoke s.74.
Correct Approach
SAFE ROUTE When law allows formal pre-notice consultations — no justification to act on any such promise. Taxpayers must avail opportunity to discharge outstanding dues and avoid litigation — but not without DRC-1A.

11.12 Circulars and Instructions

⬆ Index
Key Circulars — Chapter 11 Jurisprudence
Master Circular 1053/3/2017-CX
DATE: 10 Mar 2017 Earlier tax regime. Discusses aspects relating to notice, special circumstances, call-book cases, adjudication. Waiver of notice practice has official acceptance here — being imported into GST piecemeal.
Circular 31/5/2018-GST
DATE: 9 Feb 2018 Contains aspects from Master Circular 1053 transplanted into GST regime. Amended by Circular 169/1/2022-GST dated 12 Mar 2022.

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 11: Notice for Penalty

⬆ Index
These ratios, principles, and Latin maxims are for use in personal hearings and written submissions in penalty proceedings. In every penalty matter — first test the notice for sufficiency of ingredients before defending on merits. A notice that does not plead animus, statutory definition of infraction and the 'invisible ingredient' is fatally deficient.

⚖️ Key Legal Authorities & Principles

Section 122(2)(b) CGST Act — Penalty Only Under s.122(2) for Registered Persons
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | r/w Section 74(1)
Despite the language of s.74(1) appearing to impose penalty independently, the express language in s.122(2)(b) mandates that penalty is only imposable under s.122(2) for all demands against registered persons. No penalty under s.74(1) can operate independent of and to the prejudice of s.122(2)(b).
🎯 Use When: Revenue imposes penalty under s.74(1) independently, treating it as self-operating. Argue that s.122(2)(b) is the exclusive penalty provision for registered persons and s.74(1) cannot operate independently to the prejudice of s.122(2)(b). The raging debate on this point exists and should be raised at the earliest opportunity.
Section 126(3) CGST Act — No Penalty Without Hearing; Non-Speaking Order Fatal
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Chapter XIX
Section 126(3) mandates that a notice be issued without giving opportunity to be heard — but no due process for giving such opportunity is contained anywhere in Chapter XIX. Therefore notice for penalty under s.122(1)/(1A)/(3) cannot operate in isolation or de hors safeguards in s.75. Non-speaking orders frustrate due process and render penalty imposed unsustainable.
🎯 Use When: Penalty is imposed without a speaking order, or where the order merely states the section without applying facts to the ingredients of the offence. Cite s.126(3) and s.75 to argue the penalty order is unsustainable on account of absence of due process and non-speaking character.
Section 75(13) CGST Act — Adjudication Must Conclude Under One Provision
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017
The embargo in s.75(13) requires that even though a notice may take cognizance of penalty actionable under different provisions, adjudication must conclude with any one of them. Plurality of penalty sections in a notice can be seen as incomplete enquiry or ambiguity — resulting in allegations being insufficient to adjudicate and confirm penalty under any of those specific provisions.
🎯 Use When: Notice proposes penalty under two or more provisions alternatively (e.g., s.122(1)(i) and s.122(1)(ii) together). Argue s.75(13) embargo; challenge notice as ambiguous — Revenue has not committed to a specific provision and therefore has not satisfied the ingredients of any one provision with specificity.
Principle — Penalty Cannot Be Imposed Mechanically
Well-Established Legal Principle | Applied to s.73(11) and All Penalty Provisions
Penalty cannot be imposed mechanically. Even where all cases have the same infraction of law attracting the same extent of penalty by law of precedent, this cannot be assumed — it must be shown in each case to meet the high standards of application of mind, based on cogent reasons and reliable material taken on record. Mechanical imposition of 10% penalty in all demand cases is liable to be struck down.
🎯 Use When: SCN demands tax and adds "along with applicable penalty of 10% as per applicable provisions" without separately pleading animus or infraction. Argue mechanical imposition without application of mind to animus in the specific case is fatal to the penalty demand.
Section 134 CGST Act — Rebuttable Presumption Only for Section 132 Prosecution
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017
Section 134 raises a rebuttable presumption of the existence of the requisite mental state — but only for prosecution of offences under s.132. No such presumption exists in law in respect of offences from sections 122 to 129. Revenue must affirmatively establish animus in each case for penalty under these sections.
🎯 Use When: Revenue relies on presumption of intent or attempts to shift burden to taxpayer to disprove animus in a s.122 penalty proceeding. Cite s.134 to establish that the presumption is limited to s.132 prosecution and does not apply to civil penalty proceedings under s.122.
Principle — Notice Must Plead Statutory Definition + Invisible Ingredient
Derived from Chapter XIX — Sections 122 to 126 r/w Section 75
Statutory definition of offence must be demonstrated in the notice and evidence adduced of the 'invisible ingredient' necessary to implicate the taxpayer. The transaction must be shown to fit the statutory definition precisely — alleging infraction of law is only an opinion or interpretation unless the act or omission discovered satisfies all elements of the clause relied upon. Incurable deficiency in notice = unsustainable demand in adjudication and appeal.
🎯 Use When: Notice alleges "supplied without invoice" (s.122(1)(i)) but does not show that supplies were concealed and taxes went undischarged. Or alleges "issued invoice without supply" (s.122(1)(ii)) but does not show that Recipient actually availed ITC. Challenge sufficiency of notice before defending on merits — the invisible ingredient (outcome + concealment) is the litmus test.
Unregistered Taxable Person + Section 73 Notice = No Penalty Under s.122(1) or s.122(2)
Statutory Construction | Section 122(1), 122(2) CGST Act, 2017
Unregistered taxable persons facing demand for tax (but not credit or refund) under s.73 will not attract any penalty under either s.122(2) or s.122(1). Penalty under s.122(1) requires deliberate mischief warranting notice under s.74 — mere allegation of liability against unregistered persons under s.73 is insufficient to impose penalty under s.122(1).
🎯 Use When: Penalty is demanded against unregistered persons in a s.73 demand matter. Argue that s.122(2) applies only to registered persons and s.122(1) requires the misadventure to meet the ingredients of its specific clauses — not a truncated burden that applies to registered persons under s.73/74.

🔤 Latin Maxims — Chapter 11

Animus
Meaning: "Intent" or "mental state" — the guilty mind or intent behind an action. In GST penalty proceedings, animus is the central ingredient distinguishing a mere error in self-assessment from an offence attracting penalty. It is not 'intention to evade' (that is s.132) but the lack of belief in the truth and accuracy of the interpretation adopted during self-assessment.
Use in Hearing: "The sine qua non of penalty under s.122 is the presence of animus — the ingredient that distinguishes a case of misapplication of law from a case of mischievous application of law. The notice nowhere alleges and demonstrates the existence of animus on the part of the assessee. In its absence, the penalty demand is not sustainable."
De Hors
Meaning: "Outside of" or "beyond the scope of." Used in Chapter 11 to describe penalty notices that operate outside the safeguards on administrative discipline contained in s.75 — i.e., notice for penalty that operates de hors the protections afforded by the statute.
Use in Hearing: "The notice for penalty under s.122(1) cannot operate de hors the safeguards on administrative discipline contained in s.75 of the CGST Act. The mandate of s.126(3) requires that even in Chapter XIX penalty proceedings, the due process of notice and opportunity to be heard must be scrupulously followed."
Mens Rea
Meaning: "Guilty mind" — the mental element of a crime or offence. In GST, mens rea is the invisible ingredient that must be demonstrated in the notice to bring home the penalty charge. Without pleading and proving mens rea, the statutory definition of the offence is not complete.
Use in Hearing: "Penalty under s.122 demands proof of mens rea — the guilty mind that converts a mere error in self-assessment into a mischievous application of law. The notice issued in this case does not plead the mental element required for the specific clause of s.122(1) relied upon, rendering the penalty charge fatally deficient."
Audi Alteram Partem
Meaning: "Hear the other side." The foundational principle of natural justice that applies with full force to penalty proceedings. Section 126(3) mandates opportunity to be heard before any penalty imposition — and this right is not less favourable in the statute than general principles of fairness of procedure allow.
Use in Hearing: "The principle of audi alteram partem is the bedrock of penalty proceedings under Chapter XIX. S.126(3) mandates opportunity to be heard before penalty imposition. A non-speaking order which does not apply facts to the statutory ingredients of the offence, or which dispenses with hearing on the ground of urgency, violates this fundamental rule and renders the penalty unsustainable."
In Terrorem
Meaning: "As a warning" or "to inspire terror/deterrence." Penalty by its very nature is intended to be in terrorem — punitive enough to not only deprive the perpetrator of the fruits of the misadventure but also deter others from attempting the same. This dual character justifies the high standards for its imposition.
Use in Hearing: "Penalty under Chapter XIX is intended to operate in terrorem — to punish the perpetrator and deter others. For this very reason, the standards for its imposition are exacting: the statutory definition of the infraction must be precisely satisfied, the invisible ingredient of animus demonstrated, and due process followed meticulously. These high standards are the counterpart to the severity of the punishment."
Lis
Meaning: "A dispute or legal controversy." In demand cases, penalty is a tool to bring the lis to a speedy conclusion (s.73(5)/74(5)). In proceedings under s.125 and s.127 however, penalty itself is the lis — a critical distinction affecting the applicability of pre-notice consultations.
Use in Hearing: "In proceedings under s.125 and s.127, the penalty itself is the lis — unlike demand cases where penalty is merely ancillary to the tax demand. The purpose of pre-notice consultations is therefore equally applicable in these proceedings, even though not expressly provided for, because to deny this remedy where penalty is the primary subject matter of proceedings would be to deny the taxpayer a vested right without express statutory authority."
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 11 — Notice for Penalty
⚖️ Chapter 12 — Confiscation

⚖️ Chapter 12 — Notice for Confiscation

GST Litigation Reference — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
12.1 Overview
12.2 Seizure Must for Confiscation
12.3 Exigencies Absent
12.4 Waiver of Demand Notice
12.5 Redemption Fine
12.6 Provisional Release
12.7 Appellate Remedy
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
12.1 — Overview : Notice for Confiscation
CONFISCATION — DUE PROCESS MANDATORY
What is Confiscation
Compulsory transfer of property in offending goods to Government
Property vests in Government PERMANENTLY — cannot be transferred back
Due Process Required
Valid notice mandatory
Ingredients of 'offence' must be alleged + supported by evidence
Notice → Adjudication → Appellate relief
Seizure vs Confiscation
Seizure
Can be done in hurry even if grounds not firm
Reversible — can be released
Confiscation
Must NOT be done in hurry
Irreversible — property vests in Govt permanently
USELESS FORMALITY
Theory has EVEN LESS application in offence/confiscation cases — near impossibility of restitution + urgency of diversion makes due process even more critical
TAXPAYER'S REMEDY
After confiscation — only remedy is to participate in auction and purchase the same goods
12.2 — Seizure Must for Confiscation
SEIZURE — MANDATORY PRE-CONDITION FOR CONFISCATION
Physical Custody Rule
Physical custody MUST for confiscation u/s 130
If physical custody lost for ANY reason → confiscation proceedings UNAUTHORIZED even if amply justified
Authorized Seizure Only
Sec 67(2) — Search and Seizure
Sec 129(1) — Detention of goods in transit
Sec 35(6) unaccounted stock during audit u/s 65 → seizure NOT permitted without Sec 67(2) authorization
IMPORTANT
GST ≠ NDPS Act or Customs Act
Power of confiscation permitted by Legislature with great circumspection — only in cases of blatant violation
DISAPPEARANCE OF GOODS
Taxpayer deliberately causes disappearance after detention/seizure/attachment → only penalty u/s 122(1)(xxi)
Does NOT authorize retrieval or substitutionary confiscation
12.3 — Exigencies Absent
SEC 130 CONFISCATION — REQUIRES INVESTIGATION u/s 67
Origin of Sec 130 Notice
Must be outcome of investigation u/s 67
Audit u/s 65 revealing unaccounted stock → stock not available at time of audit → confiscation NOT enabled
Finance Act 2021 — Substitution
Sec 130 operates as concomitant of proceedings under other provisions of the Act
Any proceeding exposing misadventure can attract Sec 130
NOT every Officer is Proper Officer to initiate Sec 130 proceedings
DOCTRINE OF ELECTION
Proper Officer under Sec 129 is NOT authorized to take action under Sec 130
Notice in MOV7 renders confiscation impossible — doctrine of election applies
EXAMPLE
Consignment intercepted → owner discharges penalty u/s 129(1)(a) → proceedings conclude u/s 129(5)
Once penalty discharged — detention no longer permitted → without detention, confiscation NOT permissible
12.4 — Waiver of Demand Notice
WAIVER OF NOTICE — ILLEGAL IN GST
ABSOLUTE RULE
No provision for waiver of notice in GST — except Sec 73(5) / 74(5)
Not even a willing taxpayer can waive 'due process'
Waiver = Illegal + Ineffective
Waiver of notice is incompatible with any dispute about demand
Revenue cannot claim to have been misled by taxpayer's waiver
Taxpayer is NOT barred from withdrawing or rescinding any 'waiver of notice' — even if issued in writing
DANGER
Waiver of demand notice → implies admission of underlying offence → forms basis for confiscation proceedings
Taxpayers are welcome to admit demand but resist confiscation
COMMON TRAP
In Sec 67 investigations — taxpayer 'motivated' to admit liability → waiver followed by insistence to pay DRC-3 u/s 74(5) + 15% penalty
This is misapplication of law
RULE
Await the notice
Then consider defence
No assurances to be entertained in pre-notice inquiry without frittering away rights, remedies and safeguards
12.5 — Redemption Fine
REDEMPTION FINE — OPTION TO PAY IN LIEU OF CONFISCATION
When Option Available
AFTER notice for confiscation is issued
During adjudication — option exercised and duly recorded in writing
CANNOT be exercised prematurely — before notice = without full information about underlying offence
What Election Does
Does NOT foreclose contest on confiscation
Exercise 'without prejudice' — obtain release then continue to agitate confiscation
Subject to furnishing suitable security u/s 67(6)
MISLEADING TACTIC
Taxpayers misled into believing there is a short urgency window for this option
There is NO urgency once goods are seized — Revenue's interests are secure
EXCEPTION
Only urgency = articles covered by Sec 67(8) r/w Rule 141 (perishable/hazardous goods specified in Notification 27/2018-CT)
12.6 + 12.7 — Provisional Release + Appellate Remedy
PROVISIONAL RELEASE + APPELLATE REMEDY
Provisional Release — Sec 67(6)
In Revenue's interest to provisionally release goods — to avoid burden of care and protection
Only permits custody and safe-keeping — NOT appropriation by taxpayer
Goods provisionally released may be recalled under Rule 140(2) if needed in confiscation proceedings
Confiscation proceedings cannot be delayed to frustrate taxpayer's compliance with provisional release conditions
Sec 130 — Finance Act 2021
Non obstante clause eliminated — Sec 130 now operates in consonance with other proceedings
Confiscation must operate along with proceedings under Sec 67
Confiscation detached from Sec 129 → ONLY Sec 67 can furnish pre-requisite of seizure for confiscation
Appellate Remedy
Confiscation ≠ summary proceeding
Full appellate relief available regardless of how inquiry was conducted
Paying redemption fine ≠ waiver of appeal — right of appeal preserved
Absolute stay u/s 107(7) [or 112(8)] operates as bulwark unless accelerated recovery u/s 78 justified
Revenue initiating Sec 79 recovery soon after adjudication without awaiting appeal = challengeable
Summary — Confiscation Flow + Key Rules
StageRuleSection
Investigation / DiscoveryMust be initiated u/s 67 — not audit u/s 65Sec 67
SeizurePhysical custody mandatory — authorized only u/s 67(2) or 129(1)Sec 67(2), 129(1)
Provisional ReleaseRevenue should provisionally release to avoid custody burden — taxpayer furnishes securitySec 67(6), Rule 140
Notice for ConfiscationValid notice u/s 130 mandatory — ingredients of offence must be alleged and provedSec 130
Redemption Fine OptionAFTER notice issued — during adjudication — without prejudice to appealSec 130(2)
Adjudication OrderSpeaking order required — confiscation must be satisfactorily establishedSec 130
AppealFull appellate remedy available — Sec 107(7) stay automatic unless accelerated recoverySec 107(7), 112(8)
RecoveryOnly after final determination in appeal — not immediately after adjudicationSec 78, 79
⇧ Back to Index
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 12
Key principles for use in personal hearings and objections in confiscation proceedings. State these confidently.
No Seizure = No Confiscation
Physical custody over offending goods is a mandatory pre-condition for confiscation proceedings. If physical custody is lost for any reason, confiscation proceedings will be unauthorized even if amply justified. No provision authorizes substitutionary confiscation of equivalent goods.
USE WHEN: Revenue attempts confiscation without current physical custody of goods — seizure under Sec 67(2) is the only valid route; object to proceedings as legally barred
Waiver of Notice is Illegal and Rescindable
Not even a willing taxpayer can waive 'due process'. Legislature has mandated that no demand or penalty proceeding is permitted without granting opportunity of hearing. Waiver of notice is incompatible with any dispute about a demand. A taxpayer is not barred from withdrawing or rescinding any waiver of notice, even if issued in writing.
USE WHEN: During investigation, if taxpayer or counsel has inadvertently made any statement amounting to 'waiver of notice' — this can be formally rescinded; Revenue cannot rely on it as substitute for proper notice
Redemption Fine Does NOT Waive Right of Appeal
Taxpayer's election to pay redemption fine does not foreclose contest on confiscation. The option must be exercised 'without prejudice' to the right of appeal. No prejudice will be caused to taxpayer's right of appeal with the exercise of option to pay redemption fine.
USE WHEN: Revenue claims that by paying redemption fine the taxpayer has accepted confiscation — expressly state option exercised 'without prejudice' and carry matter in appeal
Sec 107(7) — Absolute Stay Operates as Bulwark
Unless accelerated recovery under section 78 has taken place for good and sufficient reasons, absolute stay under section 107(7) [or 112(8)] will operate as a bulwark against frustration of 'fruits of litigation' to taxpayer.
USE WHEN: Revenue initiates recovery action u/s 79 immediately after adjudication without awaiting appeal — cite Sec 107(7) and demand stay of recovery; approach Appellate Authority urgently
Confiscation Proceedings Must Follow Sec 67 Investigation — Not Audit
Notice under section 130 must be the outcome of an investigation initiated pursuant to section 67. It would be remarkable that audit under section 65 could result in discovery of circumstances to attract confiscation under section 130. Validity of notice under section 130 ties in very closely with validity of proceedings that permit verification of matters that reveal ingredients attracting confiscation.
USE WHEN: Revenue issues confiscation notice based on audit findings under Sec 65 — challenge jurisdictional validity of the notice as Sec 67 investigation is the only valid pre-requisite
⚜️ Key Latin Maxims and Principles
Non obstante
"Notwithstanding" — Finance Act 2021 eliminated the non obstante clause in Sec 130, making confiscation operate in consonance with (not independent of) other proceedings
USE WHEN: Arguing that confiscation proceedings cannot be initiated independently of Sec 67 investigation — the elimination of non obstante clause makes Sec 130 dependent on underlying proceedings
Fait accompli
"An accomplished fact" — exercising redemption fine option is NOT a fait accompli; it does not conclude the proceedings or waive any right
USE WHEN: Revenue argues that payment of redemption fine conclusively ends the matter — counter that it is a conditional act, exercised without prejudice, and appeal rights remain fully intact
Doctrine of Election
Having elected to proceed under Sec 129 (detention in transit) by issuing MOV7, the Proper Officer cannot simultaneously or subsequently elect to proceed under Sec 130 (confiscation) for the same goods
USE WHEN: Revenue issues both MOV7 and Sec 130 notice for same goods — cite doctrine of election; the earlier election under Sec 129 bars confiscation proceedings
Concomitant
"Accompanying / naturally associated with" — Sec 130 after Finance Act 2021 operates as concomitant of proceedings under Sec 67; it cannot stand alone
USE WHEN: Arguing that Sec 130 notice without a valid underlying Sec 67 investigation = notice issued without jurisdiction and hence non est in law
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 12 — Notice for Confiscation
⚖️ Chapter 13 — Preparation to Reply

⚖️ Chapter 13 — Preparation to Reply

GST Litigation Reference — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
13.1 Overview
13.2 Test the Notice
13.3 Scrutinize Allegation
13.4 Construct of Allegation
13.5 Allegations by Presumption
13.6 Contradictory Allegations
13.7 Rejection of Records
13.8 Merits of Rejecting Books
13.9 Taxing Ingredients
13.10 Acting Under Compulsion
13.11 Acting Under Dictation
13.12 Internal Validation
13.13 External Validation
13.14 Influence of Allied Laws
13.15 Post-Notice Continuation
13.16 Fatal Errors Pre-Notice
13.17 Statements Recorded
13.18 Assertions Rejected
13.19 Cross-Examination
13.20 Elaborate Reply
13.21 Short Reply
13.22 Ex Parte Orders
13.23 Reply Other Than Merits
13.24 Case Laws Overload
13.25 Earlier Tax Regime
13.26 Reliance on AARs
13.27 Overseas Case Laws
13.28 Assumptions about GST
13.29 Fallacy: Basic Features
13.30 Vague ≠ Arbitrary
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
13.1 — Overview
PREPARATION TO REPLY — MINDSET RESET
Common Misconceptions
Adjudication readily accepts taxpayer's position
Parading innocence = defence
Blaming advisors/team is useful
Correct Mindset
Revenue has duty to challenge every tax position
GST has plurality of tax positions — alternate view always possible
Proper Officer ≠ enemy, ≠ friend — has statutory duty
Proper Officer's Role
NOT assessing officer — GST is self-assessment
Designated for each provision
Proceedings not by non-designated officer = invalid
Limitation as Clock
Revenue on a clock — must issue and conclude within limitation
Finality demands notices be issued — jus in rem vs jus in personam
13.2 — Test the Notice (Checklist)
Checklist Part A — Unregistered Noticee
ParameterSec 63 routeSec 64 routeSec 67 routeOther
Underlying proceedings636467OTHER
Notice for demand under63737476
Notice for penalty under122125127OTHER
Accompanying summaryDRC1DRC1DRC1OTHER
Pre-notice consultations—DRC1ANONE—
Checklist Part A — Registered Noticee
ParameterSec 61-62-64Sec 65Sec 67Other
Underlying proceedings61-62-646567OTHER
Notice for demand under—737476
Notice for penalty under122125127OTHER
Accompanying summaryDRC7DRC1-2DRC1OTHER
Pre-notice consultations—DRC1ANONE—
13.3 — Scrutinize Allegation and Supporting Evidence
ALLEGATION — WHAT IS IT?
NOT
Suspicion
Actionable cause
A fact
IS
Interpretation of acts/omissions affecting correctness of self-assessment
Accusation backed by evidence
Fact vs Opinion
FACT — undeniable by both sides
OPINION — if deniable, it is NOT a fact
Taxpayer must enlist facts vs opinion in each allegation
Checklist Part B
For each demand: Is it Fact / Opinion / Evidence? (YES/NO)
Applies to: Issue A, Issue B, Interest, Penalty 1, Penalty 2
Checklist Part C
For each demand: Accepted / Rejected on Facts / Rejected on Law
Undisputed opinion = becomes fact needing no further proof
13.4 — Construct of Allegation
CONSTRUCT OF ALLEGATION — INVESTIGATE HOW DEMAND IS BUILT
Taxpayer's Approach
Investigate HOW demand has been constructed in allegation
Test it against the contours of the provision under which issued
COMMON TRAP
Rushing to answer — even non-taxability claim = admission that demand not discharged
RULE
Truthful reply ≠ leaving notice unquestioned. Must question construct of demand first.
PRINCIPLE
Careful analysis of construct will reveal inherent shortcomings in notice
13.5 — Allegations by Presumption
ALLEGATIONS BY PRESUMPTION — NO DEMAND PERMISSIBLE
RULE
No presumption or conjecture in demand u/s 73, 74, 76
Exceptions to Notice Requirement
(a) Sec 62 — Best judgment (non-filer)
(b) Sec 64 — Summary assessment (JC auth)
(c) Sec 121 — Pre-emptive orders
FATAL PRESUMPTIONS
2A vs 3B mismatch → Supplier defaulted
Third-party data → taxpayer liable
Supplier's GSTR-1 → RCM payable by Recipient
USELESS FORMALITY
Doctrine rejected in GST — every demand must follow valid notice + due process
13.6 — Contradictory Allegations
CONTRADICTORY ALLEGATIONS — DOCTRINE OF ELECTION
Self-Defeating Notice
Notice with internal contradictions = self-defeating
Not a vehicle to canvass every possible interpretation
DOCTRINE OF ELECTION
Quod approbo non reprobo
"That which I approve, I cannot disapprove"
Revenue chooses one path → abandons all others
DEFENCE
Show that at least one other interpretation is more correct than Revenue's
→ Notice bears self-defeating contradiction
APPLIES TO TAXPAYER TOO
Doctrine of election operates in self-assessment — cannot pursue alternative paths
13.7 + 13.8 — Rejection of Taxpayer's Records & Merits
REJECTION OF TAXPAYER'S RECORDS
RULE
Books of account cannot be rejected routinely — NO provision in GST law to reject and substitute
Why Books Cannot Be Rejected
Revenue can only access books in audit (Sec 65) or seizure (Sec 67(2))
Proper Officer ≠ Assessing Officer
Cannot 'invent' values — taxable value is ONE of several taxing ingredients
QUOD APPROBO NON REPROBO
If Revenue uses taxpayer's documents to support demand — cannot simultaneously reject those same documents
CIT v Motor & General Stores
AIR 1968 SC 200
Taxing statute applied per legal rights of parties — form of transaction governs, not 'substance'
13.9 — Taxing Ingredients
Output Tax Demand — 11 Ingredients Required
(a) Nature of alleged transaction (investigative description)(b) Coverage within definition of 'supply'
(c) Object — goods or services — and basis(d) Outside any exclusions from supply
(e) HSN code under applicable tariff notification(f) Outside any applicable exemption
(g) Time of supply(h) Place of supply
