(i) Taxable value — price as sole consideration(j) Imputed value — where price NOT sole consideration
(k) Applicability of cum-tax treatment
ITC Demand — 7 Ingredients to Deny Credit
(a) Credit barred — Sec 16(1) — no permitted end use(b) Credit divests — Sec 16(2) vesting conditions
(c) Credit fails — Sec 17(1) intended end use(d) Credit inadmissible — Sec 17(2) change of end use
(e) Credit blocked — Sec 17(5) embargo(f) Credit inadmissible — Sec 18(4)
(g) Credit liable to be repaid — Sec 18(6)
RULE Anything less = demand suffers vice of presumption, conjecture and surmise. Guesswork NOT permitted u/s 73, 74, 76.
13.10 + 13.11 — Acting Under Compulsion / Dictation
COMPULSION vs DICTATION — BOTH FATAL TO NOTICE
COMPULSION
Revenue compulsion to protect interests → biased adjudication
Sec 168 instructions guide — but cannot replace independent finding
DICTATION
SCN based only on third-party data / analytics reports / ITR mismatch
= Abdication of investigative work
ITR vs GST turnover mismatch → SCN without inquiry = dictation
GSTR9 NIL reversals → SCN assuming 6A has pre-reversal credits = dictation
PRINCIPLE
"Justice must not only be done but must appear to have been done"
Menaka Gandhi v UOI
SEPARATION
Audit/Investigation ≠ Adjudication
Circular 31/5/2018-GST (amended by 169/1/2022) — GST Audit/Intelligence officers cannot preside over adjudication
13.12 + 13.13 — Internal Validation + External Validation
VALIDATION OF NOTICE — TWO LEVELS
Internal Validation
Notice must be free of internal contradictions
Test: Are allegations coherent and harmonious?
SCN "73 and 74" together = ambiguous and incurably erroneous
Fictitious supplier + no proceedings against supplier + demand on recipient = internal contradiction
External Validation
Validity greatly affected by validity of underlying proceedings
Sec 61 → Sec 73/74
Sec 65 → Sec 73/74
Sec 67 → Sec 73/74/76
Legitimate demand in illegitimate proceedings = tainted
KEY RULE
Taxpayer must NOT reply to notice without first subjecting it to external validation
"Unless the question is correct, no answer is warranted"
13.14 — Influence of Allied Laws
ALLIED LAWS — GST TREATMENT IS INDEPENDENT
Rule
GST treatment NOT altered by how another statute treats same transaction
Divergent treatment under two statutes is possible and permissible
CRITICAL
Facts presented to one regulator cannot be altered when presented to another regulator
WELFARE vs TAX STATUTE
Welfare statute → substance of transaction considered
Tax statute → form given to transaction governs (strict interpretation)
COMPARISON TABLE
Sec 61, 65, 67 underlying proceedings → different scope and noticee (see Para 13.14 table)
13.15 — Post-Notice Continuation of Enquiry
POST-NOTICE — AUDIT/INVESTIGATION MUST STOP
RULE
Once notice issued — audit/investigation CANNOT continue
Notice sets boundaries/framework of lis
TAXPAYER DUTY
'Politely decline' invitation to extend fact-finding beyond notice
Oryx Fisheries + Brindavan Beverages — Apex Court
TRAP
Introducing new documents/material after notice = 'shifting of onus' to taxpayer to prove admissibility
PRINCIPLE
Adjudication = trial of allegations in notice
NOT a search for the truth
13.16 — Fatal Errors in Pre-Notice Stage
Fatal Errors Taxpayer May Have Already Committed
(a) Submitting books in 'unnamed' proceedings(b) Submitting pre-registration info in registration proceedings
(c) Admitting valid 'service' of notice(d) Filing 'nil' returns in hurry or under compulsion
(e) Multiple rounds of written replies to notice in ASMT10(f) Detailed reply to observations in ADT2
(g) Omission to apply u/s 67(10) for disclosure of records during investigation(h) Admitting ingredients to support demand for tax or penalty
KEY Admitting 'service' of notice is more harmful than admitting liability. Preparations to reply must account for what has already been done or disclosed.
13.17 — Taxpayer's Statements Recorded
STATEMENTS RECORDED — HANDLE WITH CARE
What Can Be Admitted
(a) Facts from personal knowledge
(b) Facts from official records
CANNOT ADMIT
(c) Interpretation of facts
(d) Interpretation of actions
(e) Opinion on tax position
(f) Admission of ingredients
(g) Confession
(h) Interpretation of motives
PROPER OFFICER ASSURANCES
Will NEVER give valid assurance of:
Closure of proceedings
Immunity from prosecution
'Plain paper' closure report
RULES FOR SUMMONS
(a) Attend — not avoiding
(b) Disclose facts truthfully
(c) Avoid interpretation unless reliably aware
(d) Comply with document directions
(e) Payment only against DRC1A
13.18 — Taxpayer's Assertions Rejected
NOTICE = REJECTION OF TAXPAYER'S ASSERTIONS
Rejection of taxpayer's assertions — Notice implies rejection of: all ingredients to tax/credit, tax treatment adopted, books, contracts, business model, allied law treatment, returns and records
KEY PRINCIPLE Longer these facts have existed and the more reliable they are — the more complicated it becomes for Revenue to reject them. Perception of bias makes rejection more burdensome.
CAUTION Do NOT submit 100-page replies to ASMT10, ADT2, DRC1A — elaborate premature replies expose defences and become 'relied upon material' to draft better quality SCN
13.19 — Cross-Examination
CROSS-EXAMINATION — TOOL TO IMPEACH EVIDENCE
When Needed
(a) Third-party witness oath statement
(b) Third-party expert statement
(c) Investigating Officer conclusions
(d) 'Special circumstances' assertions
(e) Taxing ingredients assertions
(f) Financial statements from third-party
Forms of Cross-Examination
(a) In-person questioning with recorded response
(b) Interrogatory — send questions, secure written response
CONSEQUENCE
If cross-examination refused — material and conclusions based on it must be excluded from consideration
EXPERT OPINION
Must contain only facts + basis of extracting facts
Must NOT contain opinion to be reached by Proper Officer
Auditor's classification opinion ≠ valid expert evidence
13.20 + 13.21 — Elaborate Reply vs Short Reply
ELABORATE vs SHORT REPLY — WHAT WORKS
ELABORATE REPLY RISKS
Repetitions
Shifting of onus
Exposes defences prematurely
Adjudication 'saves' demand by filling blanks
SHORT REPLY RULE
"If worth saying — say in few words. If not possible in few words — probably not worth saying."
Length ≠ quality
What Each Proceeding Requires
Sec 61 (ASMT10): Explain discrepancy in returns only
Rule 101(4): Accept or reject observations
Sec 65(6) ADT2: No response required
Sec 67 (Report/Notice): No response required
SHORT REPLY STRUCTURE
Identity of Noticee → Accept/Reject demand → Objections on law → Objections on facts → Objections on evidence → Rebuttal on facts/law → Relief sought
13.22 + 13.23 — Ex Parte Orders + Reply Other Than on Merits
EX PARTE ORDERS + REPLY OTHER THAN ON MERITS
EX PARTE ≠ ALWAYS ADVERSE
Without taxpayer's reply, records may themselves show notice unsustainable
Absence of party ≠ decision must favour Revenue
OVERDOING REPLY
New info/material in reply → adjudication travels beyond notice → demand gets 'saved'
Over-reply does more harm than ex parte order
REPLY OTHER THAN MERITS
Exhaust ALL preliminary objections BEFORE replying on merits:
Jurisdiction → Validity → Due process → Limitation → Service
JUHI INDUSTRIES
Jharkhand HC struck down Adjudication Orders where 'due process' was without authority of law — even without raising in earlier proceedings
13.24–13.27 — Case Laws: Overload, Earlier Regime, AARs, Overseas
CASE LAWS — CORRECT USE IN GST REPLIES
Case Law Overload
Every proposition finds support somewhere
Not every decision is binding in context of notice interpretation
EARLIER REGIME
GST ≠ old wine in new bottle
Marked departure from VAT/Service Tax
Earlier regime cases = limited applicability in GST
AARs
NOT quasi-judicial
NOT binding precedent
No precedent value except for Applicant-taxpayer
Do NOT cite AARs in replies
OVERSEAS (CJEU)
Persuasive only — not binding
Useful for: nature of transaction, terms of trade, contract interpretation
EU VAT ≠ GST — no force-fit
ALLIED LAWS IN GST
Indian Contract Act, ToP Act, Evidence Act, Customs Act, Limitation Act, CrPC, Registration Act, General Clauses Act — all applicable
13.28–13.30 — Assumptions / Basic Features Fallacy / Vague ≠ Arbitrary
GST ASSUMPTIONS — WHAT IS CORRECT vs FALLACY
FALLACY
GST has inherent 'basic features' traceable to statute = INCORRECT
No such contemporanea expositio available
Parliamentary debates ≠ substitute for express words
INALIENABLE TRUTHS
Self-assessment regime — Revenue cannot bypass
ITC is indefeasible right — Supplier's default = State's responsibility
Unjust enrichment does NOT apply to Revenue
Form governs over substance (unless sham)
Vague ≠ Arbitrary
Vague = taxpayer WITH GST knowledge cannot grasp allegations
Arbitrary = vagueness goes to root and destroys demand completely
Must show EXACT level of unintelligibility — not merely state 'arbitrary'
Arbitrariness as defence must be advanced with great circumspection — ends further defence if raised alone
⇧ Back to Index
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 13
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name before the officer.
CIT v. Motor and General Stores (P.) Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1968 SC 200
"In the absence of any suggestion of bad faith or fraud, the true principle is that the taxing statute has to be applied in accordance with the legal rights of the parties to the transaction. When the transaction is embodied in a document, the liability to tax depends upon the meaning and content of the language used in accordance with the ordinary rules of construction."
USE WHEN: Revenue attempts to reject taxpayer's books/documents and substitute its own 'logical reasoning' for determining taxable value or transaction nature
Narayan Bhagwantrao Gosavi Balajiwale v. Gopal Vinayak Gosavi
Supreme Court of India | 1960 AIR SC 100
"Where, however, parties have joined issue and have led evidence and the conflicting evidence can be weighed to determine which way the issue can be decided, the abstract question of burden of proof becomes academic."
USE WHEN: Officer argues burden of proof lies entirely on taxpayer — demonstrate that once Revenue leads evidence and taxpayer joins issue, burden becomes academic
Menaka Gandhi v. Union of India
Supreme Court of India | (ibid)
"Justice must not only be done but must appear to have been done."
USE WHEN: Adjudication Order appears to have been passed under dictation or revenue compulsion — challenge independence and objectivity of adjudication
Juhi Industries (P.) Ltd. v. State of Jharkhand
Jharkhand High Court | [2022] 141 taxmann.com 416 / 93 GST 521
Notice issued without accompanying DRC-1 summary is incomplete and non est in law. Mandatory requirements of law cannot be treated as directory — proceeding to adjudicate without accompanying summary is fatal to the demand.
USE WHEN: SCN not accompanied by DRC-1 / issued on different date than DRC-1 — demand is non est in law
NKAS Services (P.) Ltd. v. State of Jharkhand
Jharkhand High Court | [2021] 131 taxmann.com 230
Adjudication Orders where 'due process' was found to be without authority of law were struck down — reposing faith in Rule of Law even where taxpayer had not objected to due process violations at adjudication stage.
USE WHEN: In appeal / personal hearing — arguing that non-compliance with due process is incurable even if not raised earlier at adjudication stage
Nazir Ahmed v. King Emperor
Privy Council | (ibid)
When the law prescribes that certain things be done in a certain manner, those things must be done in that manner or not at all.
USE WHEN: Revenue argues substantial compliance is enough — press that mandatory requirements cannot be bypassed by any alternative procedure
Dilip Kumar v. CC
Supreme Court of India | (2018) 9 SCC 1
Exemptions must be given strict interpretation — once exemption found applicable, full play must be extended to the coverage of exemption.
USE WHEN: Revenue seeks to restrict scope of exemption notification — cite for both strict construction AND full play once eligibility established
State of Kerala v. Mother Superior Adoration Convent
Supreme Court of India | (2021) 5 SCC 602
Once exemption is found to be applicable, full play must be extended to the coverage of that exemption.
USE WHEN: Revenue accepts exemption eligibility in principle but tries to limit its scope or coverage
Megha Engineering & Infrastructures Ltd. v. CCT
Telangana High Court | WP 44517/2018
Notice is not required to recover unpaid interest — recovery of interest without notice is permissible.
NOTE: Revenue may cite this. Counter with Mahadeo Construction below.
Mahadeo Construction Company v. UOI
Jharkhand High Court | WP(T) No. 3517/2019
Without serving a valid notice, recovery of unpaid interest is invalid.
USE WHEN: Revenue proposes to recover interest without issuing formal notice — cite to demand proper notice before any recovery of interest
⚜️ Latin Maxims for Personal Hearings
Quod approbo non reprobo
"That which I approve, I cannot disapprove"
USE WHEN: Revenue uses taxpayer's documents to compute demand but simultaneously alleges those documents are unreliable — cannot do both
Jus in rem
"Right against the world" — tax positions must not be left unchallenged
USE WHEN: Explaining why Revenue was right to issue notice — but their method of doing so was flawed
Jus in personam
"Right against a specific person" — self-assessment is not immune from challenge
USE WHEN: Taxpayer's self-assessment is challenged — acknowledge Revenue has right to challenge, but must follow due process
Prima facie
"At first sight / on the face of it"
USE WHEN: Arguing Revenue has failed to establish even a prima facie case to rebut taxpayer's self-assessment
Non est (non est in law)
"Does not exist" — notice treated as if never issued
USE WHEN: Notice has fatal defect — SCN without DRC-1 / invalid service / issued by non-Proper Officer
Mutatis mutandis
"With necessary changes having been made"
USE WHEN: Statement of Demand (SOD) refers to earlier SCN — all grounds of SCN apply mutatis mutandis
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 13 — Preparation to Reply
⚖️ Chapter 14 — Adjudication Jurisprudence
CHAPTER 14

Jurisprudence of Adjudication

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index — Click to Navigate

14.1 Overview — Section 75 14.2 Limitation on Disposal 14.3 Impatience in Adjudication 14.4 Belated Adjudication — Call Book 14.5 Wisdom in Adjournment 14.6 Reasons for Adjournment 14.7 Personal Hearing 14.8 Authorized Representative 14.9 Grounds in Notice and Order 14.10 Facts-in-Issue 14.11 Irrefutable Facts 14.12 Irrelevant Facts 14.13 Grounds Modified or Withdrawn 14.14 Bar on Improvement 14.15 Relief in Absence of Special Circumstances 14.16 Remand Sans Power of Remand 14.17 Orders to Be Speaking 14.18 Consequential Demand Order 14.19 Interest Automatic, Not Penalty 14.20 Special Modes of Computing Interest 14.21 Plurality of Penalty Barred 14.22 Limitation Not to Commence 14.23 Circulars and Instructions ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

14.1 Overview — Section 75: The Most Important Safeguard

⬆ Index
Section 75 — Taxpayer's Shield in Adjudication
Why Section 75 is Supreme
PRIMACY Section 59 is the 'most powerful provision' — unseats PO from 'Assessing Officer' role. Section 75 safeguards taxpayer's interests — areas of safety and remedies allowed as matters of right. No matter which provision of this law, 'due process' must be followed.
Adjudication Procedural Flow
Observation in (i) scrutiny (ii) audit (iii) review or (iv) inquiry → Demand in DRC-1A Part A
↓
Demand rejected in Part B → SCN + DRC-1
↓
Receive taxpayer's reply → Conduct Personal Hearing
↓
Pass Speaking Order

14.2 Limitation on Disposal — Section 75(10)

⬆ Index
End Dates — Notice Must Be Dropped If Order Not Passed in Time
Time Limits — Generic Table
NoticeOrderSCN+DRC1DRC1A*ASMT10/ADT2
Section 7336 mts.33 mts.32 mts.31 mts.
Section 7460 mts.54 mts.53 mts.52 mts.
Section 74A54 mts.42 mts.41 mts.40 mts.
*1 mt. (2 mts. in 74A) allowed for post-notice discharge. Absence of limitation does not justify pre-notice discharge of demand at lesser limitation.
Section 73 — FY-wise Dates
FYLast date GSTR9Last date SCNLast date Order
2017-18*5/7 Feb 202030 Sept 202331 Dec 2023
2018-19*31 Dec 202031 Jan 202430 Apr 2024#
2019-2031 Mar 202131 May 202431 Aug 2024#
2020-2128 Feb 202230 Nov 202428 Feb 2025
2021-2231 Dec 202230 Sept 202531 Dec 2025
2022-2331 Dec 202330 Sept 202631 Dec 2026
2023-2431 Dec 202430 Sept 202731 Dec 2027
2024-25NEW SECTION 74A
*9/2023-Central Tax 31 Mar 2023 | #56/2023-Central Tax 28 Dec 2023
Section 74 — FY-wise Dates
FYLast date GSTR9Last date SCNLast date Order
2017-185/7 Feb 20205/7 Aug 20245/7 Feb 2025
2018-1931 Dec 202030 Jun 202531 Dec 2026
2019-2031 Mar 202130 Sep 202531 Mar 2026
2020-2128 Feb 202231 Aug 202628 Feb 2027
2021-2231 Dec 202230 Jun 202731 Dec 2027
2022-2331 Dec 202330 Jun 202831 Dec 2028
2023-2431 Dec 202430 Jun 202931 Dec 2029
NOTE Date of 'service' ≠ date of 'issue'. Statutory 3 months cannot be replaced as 90 days. View that last date to pass order is 28 Feb 2024 → last date for SCN is 28 Nov 2024 (not 30 Nov) is NOT accepted.
Vested Right on Limitation
APEX COURT Ramlal & Ors. v. Rewa Coalfields Ltd. 1962 AIR SC 361 — A vested right accrues based on limitation and it cannot be unseated unless expressly permitted by Legislature. No demand contrary to the applicable dates will survive.

14.3 Impatience in Adjudication

⬆ Index
30-Day Contemplation Period — Section 73(8) / 74(8)
The 30-Day Right
BARRED It would be contrary to s.73(8) or s.74(8) to schedule personal hearing sooner than 30 days from date of service of notice. Taxpayer is permitted 30 days to 'contemplate' whether to avail post-notice concessional penalty.
Adjournment is Available
RIGHT Once decision is made to 'dispute and litigate', taxpayer must avail the time allowed in law to consider the most expeditious approach. Even if adjournment is required, the same may be requested.

14.4 Belated Adjudication — Call Book (Section 75(11))

⬆ Index
Call Book Cases — Section 75(11) Exception to s.75(10) Embargo
When Call Book Applies
CONDITION s.75(10) embargo will NOT operate to the prejudice of Revenue's interests when a decision adverse to Revenue holds field in the case of one taxpayer and is pending consideration before a higher forum in appeal. Notices issued but not adjudicated — s.75(10) 'suspended' when in 'call book'.
Notices MUST Still Be Issued
MANDATORY Notices must be issued to all taxpayers notwithstanding the pending appeal. If notices NOT issued — lapse of limitation will cause loss of remedy against all other taxpayers.
Example: SCN u/s 73 for credit blocked u/s 17(5)(c)/(d) for letting out immovable property. Revenue already in appeal before Apex Court against Safari Retreats (Orissa HC decision). SCNs may be kept in call book and saved from expiration u/s 75(10) by operation of s.75(11).
Expression to Place on File
ON FILE Adjudication is not to be carried out in view of the pendency of the Principal Case before higher forum. Adjudication would add pendency without the benefit of wisdom of that higher forum which is seized of the exact issue.

14.5 Wisdom in Adjournment

⬆ Index
Adjournment — A Right Inherent in Opportunity to Be Heard
Adjournment = Right, Not Privilege
RIGHT Adjournment is as much a right as the right inherent in opportunity to be heard. Declining adjournment effectively denies opportunity to hear the taxpayer. Adjournments must be fairly considered subject to 'end date' applicable.
Adjournment Benefits Both Sides
IN NEW REGIME Adjournment within statutory limitation may benefit both sides due to: (a) Publication of clarifications and circulars (b) Pronouncement of decisions by Courts on admitted questions (c) Policy decisions pending before GST Council.
Examples: 24% interest revised to 18% per rule 88B; GST on ocean freight pending Council; Solar power plants valuation issue; HAM highway projects pending Council reconsideration of Circular 150/6/2021.
Cap on Adjournments
CAPPED s.75(5): Adjournments allowed for 'sufficient cause' — capped at three (3) adjournments. This is neither statutory minimum nor failure of justice. Failure to grant personal hearing, right at threshold of adjudication, becomes grounds for appeal if Adjudication Order is miscarriage of justice.

14.6 Reasons for Adjournment — Esha Bhattacharjee Principles

⬆ Index
Esha Bhattacharjee v. MC of Raghunathpur Nafar Academy (2013) 12 SCC 649 — 18 Principles
Principles (a) to (i)
(a) Reasonableness, practicality and fairness must underpin disposal of all requests
(b) Consider possibility that injustice may be legalized by mechanical denial — especially if accompanied by sworn affidavit
(c) Due regard to facts and circumstances of each case — no straight-jacket formula
(d) Judiciousness must prevail over procedure and technicalities
(e) Dilatory tactics cannot be assumed; neglect not condoned
(f) Dismissal for insincerity must be substantiated
(g) Requests must not be routinely entertained
(h) Liberal approach should not engender misuse
(i) Delay may be long but satisfactorily explained, or short but unsubstantiated — neither as matter of routine
Principles (j) to (r)
(j) Explanation of delay must be supported by documents and cogent reasons — not cleverly crafted excuses
(k) Justice to taxpayer causes prejudice to Revenue — cannot be allowed except to meet ends of justice
(l) Fraud on Court/authority must not be allowed
(m) Consequential relief that accrues must be visualized by being perceptive
(n) Different standards when considering private taxpayer vs instrumentalities of State
(o) Condonation is not a matter of right but a remedy to ensure due consideration of principal issues
(p) Objective criteria must be adopted and discussed in writing while disposing application
(q) Discretion cannot be exercised arbitrarily but to ensure justice
(r) Extent of condonation allowance must not exceed statutory boundaries even in meritorious facts

14.7 Personal Hearing — Section 75(4)

⬆ Index
Personal Hearing — Salutary Principle of Natural Justice
When Must Hearing Be Given
MANDATORY Opportunity to be heard must be allowed even when not required but not expressly barred. Salutary principle of natural justice — Dr. (Mrs.) Binapani Dey v. State of Orissa refers. s.75(4) mandates when: (a) Request is made in writing; OR (b) Adverse decision is contemplated.
Preliminary Letter — 6 Points
BEST PRACTICE When reply yet to be submitted but hearing is scheduled, file preliminary letter covering: (a) Acknowledge service of notice and date (b) Affirm time permitted u/s 73(8)/74(8) to avail concessional penalty (c) State detailed written reply by way of objections to be filed (d) Demand personal hearing after detailed reply is filed (e) State acknowledgement is NOT acceptance of validity of notice (f) Demand copy of specific authorization granted to Proper Officer.
Effect of Filing Preliminary Letter
PROTECTION Filing preliminary letter will not prejudice taxpayer's approach to notice but is a welcome step to preserve safeguards available in law. Adverse decision is 'contemplated' when personal hearing is scheduled — this alone triggers s.75(4) hearing obligation.

14.8 Authorized Representative

⬆ Index
Section 116 — Authorized Representative — Quality of Representation
Who May Appear
ENTITLED Taxpayer may appear personally or engage Authorized Representative (AR) having qualifications listed in s.116 — based on written authorization with specific reference to the given notice.
Three Qualities PO Must Assess
PO MUST ASSESS (a) Familiarity with facts of taxpayer's case (b) Knowledgeable in law and capable of understanding binding effect of statements made during hearing (c) Satisfy PO in manner of conducting proceedings — comportment, attitude and presentation.
PO's Obligations
PO OBLIGATION Must inform taxpayer if AR NOT found satisfactory. Adjudication Order must show Revenue has not derived advantage to the prejudice of taxpayer due to deficiency in quality of representation. Contradiction between AR's oral admission and written reply cannot be relied upon without taxpayer being entitled to claim prejudice.

14.9 Grounds in Notice and Order — Section 75(7)

⬆ Index
Grounds — Same in Notice Must Be Same in Order (Section 75(7))
Section 75(7) — Profound Mandate
APEX COURT Jurisprudence re incurability of defects laid down in Oryx Fisheries and Brindavan Beverages — fortified in s.75(7). Grounds on which notice is issued are the same grounds on which Adjudication Order must be passed. List grounds in notices and test if Order has veered off course — assail for direct violation of s.75(7).
Facts vs Grounds — Distinction
CRITICAL DISTINCTION Grounds = allegation adduced by evidence. Grounds are NOT facts. Facts = undisputed realities perceived by senses, admitted as true for further proceedings — not opinion. Grounds are constructed upon facts. "Because of....." in notice points to grounds.
Revenue's Limitations
REVENUE CANNOT Add or improve upon grounds in adjudication. Can only withdraw or abandon. If notice contains an averment expressed as accepted conclusion "because of...", those are grounds — Revenue is limited to them.
Taxpayer's Position on Grounds
CAUTION Taxpayers may enter any grounds in defence even contrary to caution in deliberations. But enthusiastic new grounds may afford Revenue grounds to 'save demand' during adjudication. Grounds not in notice but introduced by taxpayers in rebuttal — cannot alter overall appearance of notice but may implicitly admit new grounds.

Consider 'doctrine of election' — once new grounds are entered, they cannot be withdrawn to avoid consequences.
SECTION 75(7) EXAMPLES — DEMAND CANNOT BE CONFIRMED
SCN demands output tax on 'supply of goods' u/s 9(1) r/w 7(1)(a) → Cannot confirm as 'supply of services' u/s 9(1) r/w 7(1A) or Schedule II
SCN demands credit reversal for supplies consumed by employees u/s 17(5)(g) → Cannot confirm invoking s.17(5)(b)
SCN demands output tax on subsidy received — Payer not State/Central Govt → Cannot confirm as composite/mixed supply (only as additional consideration for original supply)
SCN demands repayment of erroneous refund on wrong formula ground → Cannot confirm for failure of 'place of supply' condition for zero-rating

14.10 Facts-in-Issue

⬆ Index
Facts-in-Issue — When Conclusively Established, Bear on Grounds Canvassed
Definition
DEFINITION Grounds are not facts — but closely linked. Facts-in-issue = those which, when conclusively established, will have a bearing on the grounds (allegation supported by evidence) canvassed by Revenue.
Key Facts-in-Issue Examples
Whether outward supplies are goods or services simplicitor → bearing on allegation for output tax demand

Whether inward supplies consumed by employees are 'personal use' or 'tools-of-trade' → bearing on blocked credit reversal

Whether subsidy payer is instrumentality of Government → bearing on undervaluation demand

'Place of supply' question in respect of zero-rated supplies → bearing on RFD-8 — POS is integral to EOS

Whether s.50 applies to ITC or transition credit also → bearing on interest demand for reversal

14.11 Irrefutable Facts

⬆ Index
Irrefutable Facts — Admitting the Obvious Serves the Taxpayer
The Principle
PRINCIPLE Enthusiasm to litigate sometimes glazes attention to irrefutable facts. Thoughtfulness in making admissions sometimes propels taxpayers to deny the obvious. Admitting the obvious may do more good than giving credit to such admissions in replies. Clearly lay out the factual matrix — do not make a mockery of this approach.
Examples of Irrefutable Facts
Bank paying advocate fee for issuing legal notice to defaulting borrower → cannot deny paying fee even if debited to customer's account

Developer holding Landowner's property under provisional attachment u/s 83 → cannot deny holding property when joint development projects are underway

Taxpayer distributing Diwali gifts → cannot deny distributing gifts — but can resist reversal demand based on output tax duly discharged on gifts in course of business (not voluntary contribution)

14.12 Irrelevant Facts

⬆ Index
Irrelevant Facts — Not Germane to the Issue — Eliminate from Reply
Definition of Irrelevance
IRRELEVANT IF Not germane to the issue involved. Irrelevance is not that information is not pertaining to taxpayer or transaction — but that it is not germane to the issue. As important as locating facts-in-issue is eliminating irrelevant facts.

Note: Certain facts considered irrelevant may become relevant if they are discovered to lead grounds for taxpayer's defence.
Irrelevant Facts — Examples
Mode of transportation used (when demand is for output tax on income without dispute that GST is not discharged) — irrelevant

Exact location where inward supplies delivered for employee use (when demand is for reversal of blocked credit) — irrelevant

Budgetary/non-budgetary nature of Government Subsidy Scheme (when demand is for output tax on undervaluation) — irrelevant

Overseas customer name and location (when demand is for repayment of erroneous refund due to formula error) — irrelevant

Reasons for accounting of incentives as 'other income' vs reducing inventory value (when demand is for output tax on incentives) — irrelevant

Whether medicines distributed are life-saving drugs or cosmetic treatments (when demand is for reversal of credit on 'free samples') — irrelevant

Location of vaccination drive (when demand is for output tax on Employer supplying healthcare) — irrelevant

14.13 Grounds Modified or Withdrawn

⬆ Index
Modification or Withdrawal of Grounds — Revenue vs Taxpayer Rights
Revenue — Cannot Modify, Can Withdraw
BARRED Grounds in notice cannot be 'modified' — modification = alter the quality of grounds without substituting. This is within the bar in s.75(7). Revenue at greater liberty to withdraw grounds altogether, signifying willingness not to agitate demand on those grounds. 'Fine tuning' without substituting a better ground is also barred.
Example: MOV-7 demanding penalty u/s 129(1)(b) 'modified' in adjudication by confirming u/s 129(1)(a) — barred. Tantamount to not putting taxpayer at notice for cause of action under s.129(1)(a).
Taxpayer — Can Add, Modify or Withdraw Defence
PERMITTED Taxpayers may add, modify or withdraw grounds in defence. Rule 112 bars introduction of new evidence but not new grounds in appellate proceedings. Grounds of defence are without prejudice to one another. Must be cautious not to follow 'any ground that pleases the Court' approach.
'WITHOUT PREJUDICE' A salutary provision flowing from s.23 of Indian Evidence Act — anything stated "without prejudice" and found inadmissible later would not prejudice the remainder of proceedings.

14.14 Bar on Improvement

⬆ Index
Bar on Improvement — Section 75(7) r/w Section 160(1)
What is 'Improvement'
BARRED Adjudicating authority barred from 'improving' grounds in notice. Improvement = altering quality of grounds without substituting the same. Within the bar in s.75(7). Bias to 'save the demand' may cause PO to commit this mistake — supplies grounds in taxpayer's appeal.
Balance with s.160(1)
BALANCE Must be balanced with latitude permitted in s.160(1). 'Fine tuning' the ground in notice (without substituting with a better ground) = permitted. Substituting with a better ground = barred improvement.
Example: MOV-7 on ground that Part A of EWB was 'incorrect' — 'improved' in adjudication by confirming penalty on ground that information required in Part A exists but is 'insufficient' to establish destination of movement. This is an improvement — barred.

14.15 Relief in Absence of Special Circumstances — Section 75(2)

⬆ Index
Section 75(2) — When s.74 Notice Lacks Valid 'Special Circumstances'
The Ground — Special Circumstances
MUST BE ADJUDICATED 'Special circumstances' is an important ground in s.74 notice — must be adjudicated based on cogent reasons and reliable material on record. Cannot be alleged by presumption or adjudicated by assumption. Taxpayer free to admit liability and interest but oppose allegation of 'special circumstances' — this itself can be the issue in appeal.
Section 75(2) Deeming Fiction
RELIEF Where notice u/s 74 but existence of 'special circumstances' not upheld in appeal → demand is mandated by s.75(2) to be recomputed 'as if' notice was always issued u/s 73. Taxpayer becomes entitled to relief by way of concessional penalty as applicable at pre-notice and post-notice stages — if attendant conditions u/s 75(3) or 75(8) were already complied. Circular 185/17/2022-GST dated 27 Dec 2022 provides scenarios.
Bona Fide Position + Judicial Authorities Overturned
COMPREHENSIVE RELIEF Where taxpayer agitates s.74 demand on ground that 'special circumstances' not proved AND tax position was bona fide and plausible supported by judicial authorities which are now overturned → comprehensive relief admissible even if not pleaded in appeal. Grounds of defence must include 'reliefs admissible under s.75(2)' to take within its sweep complete relief.

14.16 Remand Sans Power of Remand — Section 107(11) & Section 75(3)

⬆ Index
Appellate Authority — No Remand But 'Directions' → PO Passes Consequential Orders
No Power of Remand
NO REMAND Appellate Authority is without 'power of remand' under s.107(11). Appeal does not empower AA to substitute PO and 're do' adjudication. AA will reach a 'finding' on facts-in-issue and issue 'directions'. AA is not enjoined with facilities to verify documents, contracts etc. — this information must be forthcoming from Adjudication Order.
Section 75(3) — Consequential Orders
PO MANDATE 'Consequential orders' must be passed by PO pursuant to 'directions' based on new findings reached by AA or Appellate Tribunal. PO mandated to pass such orders within 2 years from date of service of Order of AA or Appellate Tribunal. No new cause of action or right accrues to taxpayer — 'directions' are authoritative interpretation that ought to have always applied.
REFUNDS EXCEPTION In refunds, Master Circular 125 requires fresh refund application — this is an exception to s.75(3) mandate, not the rule. Taxpayer need NOT remind PO but plain-paper request u/s 75(3) would help. Taxpayer NOT required to wait indefinitely.

14.17 Orders to Be 'Speaking'

⬆ Index
Speaking Order — Anatomy and Mandatory Contents
What is a Speaking Order
DEFINITION Speaking Orders ≠ elaborate orders. Elaborate orders can be 'non-speaking'. Short orders must be sufficiently 'speaking'. Must: (i) State 'factual matrix' clearly (ii) Discuss allegations and evidence adduced (iii) Reach a 'finding' on probative value (iv) Give full consideration to validity of notice, jurisdiction, and merits. Non-speaking orders tend to erode eventual recoverability of demand as case progresses in appeal.
Adjudication = Trial of Grounds
PRINCIPLE Adjudication is NOT to parrot all grounds and objections and 'do as pleased' to 'save the demand'. Adjudication is a trial of the grounds raised in notice, in the light of the defence put forward by taxpayer. PO may be convinced by some and not others — that is the adjudication.
Contents Matrix of Adjudication Order
DescriptionRevenue (SCN)Taxpayer (Reply)Added/Modified/WithdrawnAdmitted and Relied Upon
FactsNA
Facts-in-issueNANANA
AllegationNA
EvidenceNA
RebuttalNA
EvidenceNA
New materialNA
EvidenceNA
Position of lawNA
Case lawNA
Finding to reach
Inquiry (discuss)
Findings reached
Order

14.18 Consequential Demand Order — DRC-7 and APL-4

⬆ Index
DRC-7 — Notice for Recovery | APL-4 — Modified DRC-7
DRC-7 with Adjudication Order
MANDATORY Every Adjudication Order must be accompanied by summary in DRC-7 which operates as 'notice for recovery' under Rule 142(6). When Adjudication Order is appealed on making necessary pre-deposit, recovery is stayed by operation of law u/s 107(7).
APL-4 — Modified Demand
s.75(8) — STANDS MODIFIED When Adjudication Order is 'modified' by AA (or Appellate Tribunal or Court), s.75(8) declares demand "shall stand modified" — survives for revised value. AA mandated by Rule 113(1) to issue 'modified DRC-7' = APL-4. No application by taxpayer needed. In modification by Appellate Tribunal, 'modified APL-4' required by Rule 113(2) from jurisdictional PO.
Partial Relief — Further Appeal
FURTHER APPEAL Where taxpayer secures partial relief from AA, may still be aggrieved to extent demand remains confirmed — may prefer appeal before Appellate Tribunal. For recovery of disputed amount to be stayed → taxpayer required to make necessary pre-deposit to enjoy stay u/s 112(8). All recovery action taken by jurisdictional PO — taxpayer must ensure APL-4 is issued by PO.

14.19 Interest Automatic, Not Penalty

⬆ Index
Interest vs Penalty — Fundamental Contrast under Section 75(9)
Interest — Compensatory and Automatic
AUTOMATIC Interest is compensatory in nature. Omission in Adjudication Order to confirm liability to pay interest does NOT prejudice Revenue. s.75(9): interest is payable even if Adjudication Order omits to confirm levy of interest. DRC-7 issued must be noted.
Penalty — NOT Automatic
NOT AUTOMATIC s.75(9): Omission to levy penalty — even if applicable and demanded in notice — will not be payable unless specifically imposed levy in Adjudication Order. Penalty not automatic — even altered by s.73(11) or s.74(1).
Second Notice for Penalty
SECOND NOTICE Levy of penalty inadvertently omitted in Adjudication Order cannot be saved by s.161 proceedings in absence of 'full and due consideration' on penalty issue. Supplementary notice not permitted (not prescribed). Revised notice not permitted (demand no longer in dispute). Second Notice permissible — making Adjudication Order as 'relied upon' document — confined to sole question of levy of penalty. Must be adjudicated and carried in appeal — not kept in call book.

14.20 Special Modes of Computing Interest

⬆ Index
Section 50 — Six Special Interest Computation Scenarios
(a) Belated Return — Net Tax Rule
PROVISO TO s.50(1) r/w Rule 88B(1) Output tax discharged via belated periodic returns → interest only on 'net tax' payable for tax period by debiting ECL. Where sufficient ITC balance to discharge output tax → interest only on excess output tax over ECrL balance utilized at time of filing. Applicable from 1 Jul 2017.
Example: Output tax ₹10 lakhs, balance in ECrL ₹8 lakhs. Return filed belatedly. Interest at 18% on ₹2 lakhs only.
(b) Belated Return — ECL Relief
PROVISO TO Rule 88B(1) Where sufficient balance in ECL also available for that tax period — no interest payable if delay is merely on account of filing returns and not retaining cash representing tax payable. Notified from 10 Jul 2024 — ought to be effective from 1 Jul 2017 given it is clarificatory. Refer 53rd GSTC meeting.
Example: Output tax ₹10 lakhs, ECrL ₹8 lakhs, ECL ₹3 lakhs. Return filed belatedly. No interest payable except late fee.
(c) Pursuant to Notice of Demand
FULL INTEREST Relief under (a) and (b) will NOT be applicable where output tax payable pursuant to any notice of demand. Interest at applicable rate on (i) entire amount of demand admitted and discharged (ii) from relevant 'time of supply' up to 'date of payment'. Output tax can be discharged by utilizing ITC available on 'date of discharge' even if not available on 'time of supply' — new s.41 from 1 Oct 2022.
(d) RCM — Self-Invoice Belated
RCM Output tax on RCM basis discharged via belated self-invoice → interest on (i) entire amount (ii) from relevant 'time of supply' up to 'date of payment'. Circular 211: belated payment of RCM output tax does not prejudice credit. Not likely to survive limitation in s.16(4) — computed from 'relevant time of supply' not 'actual belated date of self-invoice'. Note: Circular 211 advantages will not apply after proposed amendments come into effect.
(f) Wrongly Availed but NOT Utilized
NO INTEREST ITC wrongly availed but not utilized — identified as liable to reversal (for any reason) — interest will NOT be payable as inadmissible credit remains unutilized. Relief under Rule 88B(3) applicable. Exception: Rule 37 where admissible credit that subsequently becomes inadmissible due to non-compliance with post-condition — interest payable from date of originally claiming credit.
(g) Wrongly Availed AND Utilized — Without Notice
INTEREST PAYABLE ITC wrongly availed and utilized — identified as liable to reversal without any notice of demand → interest is payable as inadmissible credit has been utilized. Interest on (i) entire amount of liability admitted and reversed (ii) from 'date of claim' of said credit up to 'date of payment'.
(h) Wrongly Availed AND Utilized — With Notice
FULL INTEREST ITC wrongly availed and utilized — identified and demanded by notice of demand → interest payable on (i) entire amount admitted and reversed (ii) from 'date of claim' up to 'date of payment'. Impugned credit inadmissible ab initio yet utilized gives Revenue two options: (i) demand output tax as outstanding due to non-est discharge by non-existent credit, OR (ii) demand reversal of inadmissible credit. Treatment of interest depends on which option is elected by Revenue in issuing notice.

14.21 Plurality of Penalty Barred — Section 75(13)

⬆ Index
Section 75(13) — One Act Cannot Be Punished Under Multiple Provisions
The Rule
RELIEF While one act or omission may give rise to multiple actionable causes — and penalty may be permitted under more than one provision — s.75(13) declares penalty shall not be "imposed" under various provisions available. Gives primacy to s.73 or s.74 for levy of penalty. Where penalty is imposed under s.73 or s.74 — no penalty "shall be" imposed under other provisions.
Scope of Exclusion
SCOPE Penalty under s.73/74 is actually levied under s.122(2). Cause of action in s.122(2) subsumes all offences by registered taxpayer under Chapter XIX except s.129 and s.130. Relief under s.75(13) limited in exclusion it provides to a registered person only.
DOES NOT APPLY TO Persons liable to penalty under s.122(1A) or s.122(3) will not suffer notice under s.73 or s.74 → will not enjoy the exclusion. Exclusion does not apply to persons issued notice under s.63 or s.76. Great deliberation possible to identify defects — for "the same act or omission" is the circumscribing condition to avail this exclusion.

14.22 Limitation Not to Commence — Section 75(1)

⬆ Index
Stay Orders — Limitation Clock Paused Under Section 75(1)
When Limitation Does Not Commence
EXCLUSION s.75(10) extinguishment applies when notice is issued and adjudication is pending. s.75(1) creates new exclusion to operation of limitation where a Court or Appellate Tribunal stays: (a) service of notice; OR (b) issuance of orders. Duration of stay is excluded in considering respective time limits.
Court Stay — Writ Jurisdiction
WRIT JURISDICTION Court stay of 'issuance of notice' is unusual — granting such relief is not pursuant to a 'decision or order' appealable under s.117 or s.188 → comes up in Writ Petition under Articles 226-227. Courts seldom enthusiastic to admit petitions when notice itself is not issued. Where Courts pass interim orders to maintain status quo → PO precluded from adjudication → limitation will not commence.
Appellate Tribunal Stay — Limited Application
LIMITED s.75(1) refers to Appellate Tribunal. Only 'decision or order' under s.107 or s.108 is appealable to Appellate Tribunal. Prior to issue of notice — no decision or order under s.107/108 that is appealable. After issue of notice — 'decision or order' is to be passed by PO which is appealable first to AA under s.107 (no direct appeal to AT under s.112). Thus limited application of this exclusion in statutory appeals — with remedy in s.75(11), reckless exclusion can result in lapse of limitation and loss of even legitimate demands.

14.23 Circulars and Instructions

⬆ Index
Key Circulars — Chapter 14 Jurisprudence
962/5/2012-CX
DATE: 28 Mar 2012 | Earlier Regime Benefit of credit admissible when bona fide claim of exemption overturned by Court. Applicable to continue in GST.
213/3/2019-ST
DATE: 5 Jul 2019 | Service Tax Reversal of common credits not attracted when taxable value reduced due to abatement — affirmed long after service tax was repealed. Applicable to continue in GST.
185/17/2022-GST
DATE: 27 Dec 2022 Provides different scenarios and manner of re-computation of demand when relief under s.75(2) is to be allowed. Issued to remove confusion about re-computation when demand is mandated to be recomputed 'as if' notice always issued u/s 73.

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 14: Jurisprudence of Adjudication

⬆ Index
This is the most powerful Arsenal in the series — Chapter 14 is the master chapter on adjudication rights. Every paragraph contains a principle that can be deployed in personal hearings, written submissions, grounds of appeal, and revisionary proceedings. Use these with precision — cite the section, principle, and case by name.

⚖️ Key Legal Authorities & Principles

Ramlal & Ors. v. Rewa Coalfields Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | 1962 AIR SC 361
A vested right accrues based on limitation and it cannot be unseated unless expressly permitted by Legislature. No demand contrary to the applicable dates of SCN issuance and Order passing under sections 73, 74 or 74A will survive — the end-date regime is absolute and carries the full force of this settled position.
🎯 Use When: Adjudication Order is passed and served after the 'end date' in s.73(10) or s.74(10). Argue that the taxpayer's vested right on limitation stands crystallised, cite this Supreme Court authority, and the notice will have to be dropped. Also cite when Revenue argues that the 3-month period in SCN limitation can be read as 90 days — the statutory language controls, not arithmetical equivalence.
Oryx Fisheries Pvt. Ltd. & Brindavan Beverages Pvt. Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | Cited in GST context via Section 75(7)
Jurisprudence regarding incurability of defects, discrepancies and deficiencies in notice was laid down by the Apex Court in these cases — now fortified in s.75(7). The 'grounds' on which a notice is issued are mandated to be the same 'grounds' on which the Adjudication Order is passed. This is a profound Legislative direction — Adjudication Order that veers off course is in direct violation of s.75(7) and is assailable on that ground.
🎯 Use When: Adjudication Order confirms demand on a ground not raised in SCN, or changes the legal basis of the demand (e.g., SCN demands u/s 7(1)(a) but Order confirms u/s 7(1A)). Cite s.75(7) and these cases to argue the Order is void ab initio for transgressing the mandatory ground-identity rule.
Esha Bhattacharjee v. Managing Committee of Raghunathpur Nafar Academy
Supreme Court of India | (2013) 12 SCC 649
The basis on which requests or applications for adjournment may be entertained as 'sufficient cause' under s.75(5) must meet 18 principles — including that reasonableness, practicality and fairness must underpin disposal; mechanical denial of applications may legalize injustice; dilatory tactics cannot be assumed; discretion cannot be exercised arbitrarily; and dismissal for insincerity must be substantiated. Declining adjournment effectively denies the opportunity to be heard.
🎯 Use When: Proper Officer refuses to grant adjournment and passes an ex parte order, or grants fewer adjournments than what sufficient cause warranted. Cite this judgment in grounds of appeal to argue that the refusal of adjournment was arbitrary, violated s.75(5), and constituted a miscarriage of justice at the threshold of adjudication — independent of merits of the demand.
Dr. (Mrs.) Binapani Dey v. State of Orissa
Supreme Court of India | Cited in s.75(4) context
Opportunity to be heard must be allowed even when not required but not expressly barred — it is a salutary principle of natural justice. Section 75(4) imposes an interesting obligation to grant personal hearing when request is made in writing OR when adverse decision is contemplated — these are two independent triggers, either of which suffices.
🎯 Use When: Proper Officer passes adverse order without granting personal hearing, claiming it is not mandatory or that taxpayer did not request it. Cite s.75(4) and this authority to argue that scheduling of personal hearing itself constitutes contemplating adverse decision — triggering the obligation to grant hearing; the Order is unsustainable for violation of this mandate.
Section 75(2) CGST Act — Re-computation Deeming Fiction When Special Circumstances Not Proved
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | r/w Circular 185/17/2022-GST
Where notice is issued under s.74 but existence of 'special circumstances' is not upheld in appeal, the demand is mandated by s.75(2) to be recomputed 'as if' the notice was always issued under s.73. Taxpayer becomes entitled to relief by way of concessional penalty as applicable at pre-notice and post-notice stages if the attendant conditions under s.75(3) or s.75(8) were already complied with. Special circumstances cannot be alleged by presumption or adjudicated by assumption.
🎯 Use When: Notice is issued under s.74 and taxpayer contests the existence of 'special circumstances'. Plead separately that s.74 is not warranted and that even if the demand is confirmed, s.75(2) relief must be given. Include 'reliefs admissible under s.75(2)' as a specific ground in reply to notice and in appeal — this ensures the complete s.73 consequential relief is taken within the sweep of the adjudication or appeal.
Section 75(9) CGST Act — Interest Automatic; Penalty Not Automatic
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017
Interest is compensatory in nature and is payable even if the Adjudication Order omits to confirm levy of interest — it is automatic. Penalty, by contrast, is not automatic — even if applicable and demanded in notice, penalty will not be payable unless it is specifically imposed levy in the Adjudication Order. This fundamental distinction cannot be altered even by section 73(11) or 74(1).
🎯 Use When: (1) Revenue attempts to collect interest despite Adjudication Order not specifically mentioning it — cite s.75(9) to show interest is automatic and DRC-7 must be noted. (2) Revenue attempts to collect penalty based on notice alone when Adjudication Order does not specifically levy penalty — cite s.75(9) to show penalty is not payable and Revenue's only remedy is a Second Notice confined to the penalty issue.
Section 75(13) CGST Act — Plurality of Penalty Barred
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017
While one act or omission may give rise to multiple actionable causes and penalty may be permitted under more than one provision, s.75(13) declares that where penalty is imposed under s.73 or s.74, no penalty "shall be" imposed under other provisions of Chapter XIX for the same act or omission. This gives primacy to s.73 or s.74 for levy of penalty against registered persons.
🎯 Use When: Notice demands penalty under both s.73/74 and additionally under s.125 or s.127 or other Chapter XIX provisions for the same transaction. Cite s.75(13) to argue that once penalty is imposed under s.73/74, no further penalty shall be imposed under other provisions — the embargo is absolute for "the same act or omission". Great deliberation possible to identify defects in arriving that this condition is satisfied.
Section 107(11) r/w Section 75(3) — Remand Sans Power of Remand
Statutory Provisions | CGST Act, 2017
Appellate Authority is without power of remand under s.107(11). AA will reach a finding on facts-in-issue and issue directions. Consequential orders must be passed by Proper Officer within 2 years from date of service of AA's or Appellate Tribunal's Order. No new cause of action or right accrues — the directions are authoritative interpretation that ought to have always applied to the original cause of action.
🎯 Use When: After winning in appeal, Proper Officer fails to pass consequential orders or attempts to re-adjudicate the matter afresh as if no appeal was decided. Cite s.75(3) to demand consequential orders within 2 years. Also use when revenue attempts to issue a second notice on the same ground already decided by AA — argue AA's 'directions' are binding on PO and he cannot deviate from them without filing a further appeal.

🔤 Latin Maxims — Chapter 14

Audi Alteram Partem
Meaning: "Hear the other side." The foundational rule of natural justice — no person shall be condemned without being heard. In adjudication, this encompasses not just the right to file a reply but the right to a personal hearing, the right to an adjournment for sufficient cause, the right to present evidence, and the right to a speaking order that gives reasons for the finding.
Use in Hearing: "The principle of audi alteram partem is not merely satisfied by the issuance of a notice and acceptance of a reply. It demands a genuine opportunity to be heard — which includes the right to personal hearing under s.75(4) once adverse decision is contemplated, the right to adjournment for sufficient cause under s.75(5), and the right to a Speaking Order that demonstrates application of mind to the defence placed on record. Any order passed in violation of these rights is unsustainable regardless of the merits of the demand."
Ex Parte
Meaning: "From one party only" — proceeding and adjudicating without hearing the other side. In GST adjudication, an ex parte order may be passed when taxpayer fails to appear for personal hearing despite repeated opportunity — but such orders are vulnerable if adjournment was wrongly refused or hearing was not properly scheduled.
Use in Hearing: "An ex parte Adjudication Order passed on available information and records, without granting further adjournments, is not automatically sustainable. The question of whether the taxpayer was genuinely afforded opportunity to be heard — including whether adjournment requests for sufficient cause under s.75(5) were properly considered per the 18 principles in Esha Bhattacharjee — must be the first ground of challenge in appeal."
Non Obstante
Meaning: "Notwithstanding" — a legislative device that makes one provision override another despite any contrary provision. Section 75 uses non obstante clauses in several sub-sections (e.g., s.75(9) — interest payable notwithstanding omission in Order) to create absolute rules that cannot be overridden by other provisions or by the parties.
Use in Hearing: "The non obstante language of s.75(9) makes interest automatic and penalty non-automatic — regardless of what the Adjudication Order says or does not say. These are absolute statutory positions that cannot be altered by the conduct of the parties, the terms of the notice, or the convenience of Revenue. Interest will always follow the demand; penalty will never follow without a specific finding in the Order."
Functus Officio
Meaning: "Having discharged one's duty" — once an authority has exercised its jurisdiction and passed an order, it is functus officio and cannot revisit or re-open the same matter. In GST adjudication, once the Adjudication Order is passed, Proper Officer is functus officio on the matters decided — he cannot supplement or improve the order under s.161 except for errors apparent on the face of the record.
Use in Hearing: "Once the Adjudication Order is passed, the Proper Officer is functus officio on the issue of penalty. Levy of penalty inadvertently omitted in the Adjudication Order cannot be saved by s.161 proceedings in the absence of 'full and due consideration' on the issue of penalty in the Adjudication Order — it would not be an error 'apparent on the face of the record'. The only permissible route is a Second Notice confined to that sole question."
Sans (French Legal Term)
Meaning: "Without" — used in the expression "remand sans power of remand" to describe the unique GST mechanism where Appellate Authority achieves the functional equivalent of remand (finding + directions to Proper Officer) without having the formal power of remand under s.107(11).
Use in Hearing: "The Appellate Authority operates on the principle of 'remand sans power of remand' — it reaches a finding on facts-in-issue and issues directions which are binding on the Proper Officer, who must then pass consequential orders within 2 years under s.75(3). The Appellate Authority's directions are a declaration of the authoritative interpretation that ought to have always been applied — not a new right accruing to the taxpayer."
Without Prejudice (s.23, Indian Evidence Act)
Meaning: Anything stated "without prejudice" — if found inadmissible later — would not prejudice the remainder of the proceedings. A salutary provision that allows taxpayers to place positions on record and explore compromise without those positions being used against them in the event the compromise fails or the position is abandoned.
Use in Hearing: "This reply is filed strictly 'without prejudice' to all rights and remedies available in law — including the right to challenge the validity, jurisdiction, and sufficiency of the notice in these proceedings or in any appellate proceedings. Acknowledgement of this notice is NOT acceptance of its validity. All objections as to validity of notice, jurisdiction of Proper Officer, and limitation are expressly reserved."
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 14 — Jurisprudence of Adjudication
⚖️ Chapter 15A — Reply to Notices

⚖️ Chapter 15 (Part A) — Reply to Notices

GST Act 2017 | Strategy, Jurisdiction, Evidence & Rebuttal — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
15.1 Overview
15.2 From Strength to Strength
15.3 Statutory Provision Dictates Approach
15.4 Jurisdiction and Proper Officer
15.5 Illegal Evidence
15.6 Bona Fide Demand in Mala Fide Proceedings
15.7 Question-the-Question
15.8 Revenue's Evidence
15.9 Impeaching Revenue's Evidence
15.10 Admissibility of Evidence
15.11 Statements on Oath
15.12 Probative Value of Evidence
15.13 Evidence Impeached by Denial
15.14 Rebuttal and Evidence
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
15.1 — Overview
REPLY TO NOTICES — ADVERSARIAL LITIGATION; MULTIPLE ROUNDS; STRATEGY FROM THE FIRST STEP
Nature of Adjudication
NOT 'Due process' of adjudication is NOT a 'friendly match' — it is adversarial litigation in every sense
KEY Neither side should assume quiet surrender or crude settlement. PO is not the 'last authority'
REALITY Notice and reply is just the BEGINNING — concludes only when decisive ruling is rendered or adversary concedes
Revenue's 5 Avenues
(a) Adjudication by Proper Officer
(b) Departmental appeal before FAA
(c) Revision of orders prejudicial to Revenue by RA
(d) Departmental appeal before Appellate Tribunal
(e) Statutory appeal to HC/SC on question of law
Multiple Rounds Principle
CAUTION Not even a favourable order in adjudication is cause for celebration — Revenue can carry it in departmental appeal (6 months) or revision (3 years)
RULE Reply to notice must be approached with strategy that entertains possibility of adverse outcome in early rounds
EACH ROUND Must be approached based on the relief that authority is empowered to allow in that round
Nature of Adjudication
NOT Adjudication is NOT a search for truth
IS Adjudication is a TRIAL of the interpretation canvassed by Revenue
PERIL Raising constitutional validity of provision before PO alone = demand confirmed; PO is creature of statute, barred from sitting in judgment over vires of law
15.2 — From Strength to Strength
CASE MUST BE CARRIED FROM 'STRENGTH TO STRENGTH' — ASSUMPTION vs REALITY DIAGRAM; 8 KEY QUESTIONS
Assumption vs Reality Gap
ASSUMPTION Taxpayer assumes straight upward progress (Line A) from adjudication to appellate stage
REALITY Reality curve (Line B) dips before it begins to climb — adjudication stage is the dip
RISK Taxpayers averse to litigation stop along line A-B due to alarm and disappointment at adjudication outcome, failing to appreciate the 'reality' of progress
Why Adjudication Dips
Adjudication = trial of Revenue's interpretation canvassed in notice — not a search for truth
As allegations are 'chipped away' at each stage, outcome becomes clearer — requires perseverance and patience with entire 'due process'
Care must be guarded even from taxpayers giving up midway and accepting a 'compromise' — there is no 'short cut' resolution that exists in this law
8 Questions to Ask at Each Stage
(a) Are the right objections placed on record at this stage?
(b) Have allegations and evidence in support been put to test?
(c) Is the scope of notice contained or expanded by new material?
(d) Do findings narrow down the facts-in-issue involved?
(e) Are grounds urged (in appeal) sharpening focus on findings to reach?
(f) What avenues are still left open that may 'save the demand'?
(g) Does the record bear correct facts, eliminate irrelevant facts, revolve around correct statutory provisions?
(h) Is the correct relief prayed?
15.3 — Statutory Provision Dictates Approach
'WHICH SECTION?' — MOST IMPORTANT QUESTION; DICTATES OFFICER, APPROACH & RELIEF AVAILABLE
'Which Section?' Question
MOST IMPORTANT Answer to 'which section?' reveals the approach to be followed in replying to the notice
EXAMPLE Notice u/s 63 requires one approach — this approach cannot be applied if notice is u/s 76
ALSO DICTATES 'Which section?' also dictates 'which Officer?' — not every Officer is the Proper Officer; when Officer is not 'proper', notice and everything following will be 'improper'
Acquiescence Validates Invalid Notice
DANGER Right to question validity of any notice will be lost by delay and by omitting to question its validity — due to embargo in s.160(2)
FATAL By replying to SCN on merits, taxpayer has acquiesced and rendered the SCN (and consequent AO) 'valid' as per s.160(2)
EXAMPLE SCN u/s 74 with estimates and guesswork → Use of estimates compatible with s.63 (best judgment), NOT s.74 → Appeal allowed on this ground
Next Round Mentality
DUE PROCESS With several rounds of adversarial litigation laid down in law — sights must be set on the 'next round' while working on reply in 'this round'
RELIEF Relief that can be easily allowed in appeal may be impossible in adjudication — each round must be milked for what it is empowered to deliver
15.4 — Jurisdiction and Proper Officer
JURISDICTION — 'COMPOUND' JURISDICTION PYRAMID; JURISDICTIONAL TABLE; VALIDITY OF AUTHORIZATION
Compound Jurisdiction Pyramid
LEVEL 1 Territorial jurisdiction (geographic territory)
LEVEL 2 Administrative mapping (Centre or State/UT) — registered taxpayer is mapped
LEVEL 3 Statutory jurisdiction under the relevant provision of law
LEVEL 4 Administrative authorisation (i) qua taxable person and (ii) qua tax period
Jurisdictional Table
NoticeRegn.Proper Officer
S.52, S.63CentreCentre (Note 1)
S.63NoneEither A (Note 2)
S.73-74CentreCentre (Note 1)
S.74-76CentreCentre (Note 1)
S.74-76CentreEither B (Note 3)
S.122NoneEither B (Notes 2+3)
S.129Either/noneEither A (Note 2)
S.130Either/noneEither B (Notes 2+3)
Note 1: State & State Officer | Note 2: Within respective territory | Note 3: Authorized in INS1
Validity of Authorisation
⚠ KEY Uncertainty about 'validity of jurisdiction' goes to heart of notice — any invalidity can bring entire proceedings to grinding halt
RULE Where jurisdiction is lacking, notice is incurable and must be dropped — even if there is merit in underlying demand
EXAMPLE Karnataka — JC issues 'Assignment' to DC specifying (i) distinct person (ii) tax period(s) (iii) S.73 or 74 (iv) pecuniary limit → taxpayer must demand copy of Assignment before replying
DEMAND Unless every step in jurisdiction table is tested and satisfied, taxpayer is welcome NOT to enter into merits — include demand for copy of authorization in preliminary letter
Simultaneous Jurisdiction — S.6(2)
S.6(2) Anti-evasion action u/s 67: simultaneous jurisdiction vested in officers of both Central AND State/UT GST departments
MAPPED TAXPAYER Taxpayer mapped to Central GST → audit u/s 65 by Central GST Audit Commissionerate only; State GST officers NOT authorized
Validity of jurisdiction is not 'simple' but 'compound' — territory + administrative mapping + statutory provision + specific authorisation
15.5 — Illegal Evidence
ILLEGAL EVIDENCE — 'EXTRA LEGISLATIVE' METHODS; 8 ALARMING PROVISIONS; OUTSTANDING LIABILITY AREAS
What 'Illegal' Means Here
DEFINED 'Illegal' = evidence collected using 'extra legislative' methods due to misapplication of prescribed procedure for evidence collection
NOT BARRED Evidence obtained illegally is NOT barred from being relied upon to issue notice — but objections must be raised RIGHT AT THE POINT when illegal attempts are observed
ACTION Objections to use of illegal means to collect evidence must be made in preliminary letter / preliminary reply — not as afterthought
8 Alarming Provisions for Illegal Evidence
(a) Inspection u/s 71 without 'reasons to believe' any outstanding liability
(b) Inspection u/s 67 — demanding 'voluntary' submission of books referencing outstanding liability
(c) Summons u/s 70 without authorisation u/s 67 to conduct 'inquiry' in respect of any evasion of tax
(d) Letters of instruction u/s 61 calling for books in relation to ASMT10 issued
(e) Survey of unregistered persons — verbal instructions to 'voluntarily' submit books for s.63 proceedings
(f) Verbal instructions during registration grant for outstanding liability in prior period for s.63 proceedings
(g) Verbal instructions during 'test purchase' u/s 67(12) to expose unaccounted stock under s.35(6) for s.73/74
(h) Notices akin to summons calling for 'information' u/s 150 — applicable only to large carriers of data (Railways, banks, etc.); no notification yet
Outstanding Liability Areas
Output TaxITCInterest
RCM u/s 9(3)Credit mismatchUnpaid arrears
RCM u/s 9(4) (up to 13 Oct 2017)Inward supplies not used in businessArrears discharged belatedly
Deemed supply (multi-GSTIN)Time-barred creditsTax discharged on debit notes via 3B
Staff recoveries (credits in expense accounts)Reversal of common creditsInterest paid on 'net tax' basis (via DRC3)
Other income in P&LBlocked credits
Value adjustments
15.6 — Bona Fide Demand in Mala Fide Proceedings
TAINTED PROCEEDINGS — EVEN BONA FIDE DEMAND CANNOT SURVIVE; LAWFULNESS IS THE TEST; AUDIT REPORT TRAP
Core Principle
RULE When proceedings are 'tainted', it does not matter if the underlying demand is real and due
ADMIN LAW Not even a bona fide demand can legitimize mala fide proceedings — rule of Administrative Law
EQUALLY Relief NOT pleaded cannot be allowed — not even in interests of justice; justice = the lawful process of getting to the outcome
Taxpayer's Common Error
OVERCOME BY Taxpayers overcome by bona fides of demand overlook legislative mandate that 'no demand is permitted except by issuing a notice as per law'
PAYMENT Taxpayers gladly pay up even when demand cannot lawfully be made — without questioning validity of proceedings
Audit Report Trap — S.65(7)
TRAP Report containing 'observations' under s.65(6) but not titled ADT2 — consequence in law remains same once s.65(6) is invoked
DANGER Taxpayers hopelessly hopeful that 'detailed reply' will make the notice 'go away' — this prejudices future defence by exposing grounds available prematurely
CORRECT No statutory provision in Central GST for PO conducting audit u/s 65 to direct 'detailed reply to audit report in ASMT2' — taxpayer is not obliged; await pre-notice consultations via DRC1A as s.65(7) renders PO 'functus officio' once ADT2 is released
15.7 — Question-the-Question
QUESTION-THE-QUESTION — 3 PERILS OF RUSHING INTO MERITS; UNDERSTAND 'WHAT' AND 'HOW' FIRST
The Core Principle
PRINCIPLE Before launching to answer the question, it is most prudent to 'question-the-question' — because ambiguity about the question will expose taxpayer to perils
PERIL (a) Forfeiture of right to demand Revenue satisfactorily discharge its 'burden of proof' about interpretation canvassed
PERIL (b) Implicit admission of allegation (in notice) left undisputed
PERIL (c) Shifting of 'onus of proof' onto taxpayer for introducing new or additional material and information
Understand 'What' and 'How'
RULE Taxpayer must read plain words of notice with clear mind, free of all preconceptions, and locate 'within' that notice the 'what and how' about the demand
COMMON ERROR Taxpayers 'already know the question' — they imagine the question and then answer it. Taxpayer's role is to 'answer the question', not 'imagine the question'
RCM EXAMPLE SCN alleges RCM on royalty not paid. Taxpayer objects to royalty being taxable — thereby admitting (i) payments made to Authority and (ii) RCM not discharged. Essential fact left open and incomplete — taxpayer has admitted what Revenue needed established
Examples of Missing 'Essential Facts'
Demand for CGST-SGST without specifying 'place of supply' and its basis → demand is biased, arbitrary and erroneous, formed purely on presumption and conjecture
Demand for output tax without specifying 'HSN code' → fatal to a well-reasoned confirmation of demand in adjudication
Demand for RCM without specifying 'time of supply' → incurable; demanding interest from 31 Mar 20XX implies 'time of supply' assumed to be last day of FY
PRELIMINARY Taxpayer submitting preliminary objections gives Revenue opportunity to consider defects, deficiencies and discrepancies — and if turned down, those become compelling grounds of defence
No Statutory Provision — Examples
QUESTION THE NOTICE No statutory provision in Central GST Act to issue SCN demanding interest on inadmissible transition credit availed, utilised and repaid
No statutory provision for jurisdictional PO to call for books/records even if return discrepancies are blatant — after ASMT11 reply, PO must refer for audit u/s 65/66, or inspection u/s 67, or issue SCN u/s 73/74
No statutory provision for PO conducting audit u/s 65 to direct 'detailed reply to observations in report issued u/s 65(6)'
15.8 — Revenue's Evidence
REVENUE'S EVIDENCE — ALLEGATIONS ≠ EVIDENCE; BURDEN OF PROOF ON REVENUE; TWO-STEP PROCESS FOR TAXPAYER
Allegations ≠ Evidence
FUNDAMENTAL Allegations are mere conjecture — even if very logical — unless evidence is adduced to support them
Amounts appearing in financial statements as 'income' does NOT evidence that it is the result of 'supply', much less 'taxable supply'
EXAMPLE Income in TDS reports (AIS/26AS) = Customer's admission of liability towards Supplier — NOT evidence of (i) supply made (ii) taxable goods/services (iii) time of supply (iv) place of supply (v) taxable value
Burden of Proof
RULE One who makes the assertion bears the burden of proof — necessary for taxpayers to dispute allegation and allow Revenue to discharge its burden
UNDISPUTED Allegations do not require any evidence if left undisputed — omission to plead = implicit admission = Revenue's burden discharged
KEY Disputing Revenue's assertion does NOT require 'proof' to be produced — only 'pleading' and taking a position that Revenue's evidence is disputed
Two-Step Process for Taxpayer
STEP 1 Plead that Revenue's evidence is disputed — allow Revenue to demonstrate reliability of its evidence
STEP 2 (only then) Once Revenue establishes reliability of its evidence → taxpayer introduces evidence in rebuttal (disprove it)
COMMON ERROR Taxpayers, consumed by enthusiasm, introduce evidence to disprove Revenue's evidence without even checking if Revenue has successfully made out a prima facie case in the notice
15.9 — Impeaching Revenue's Evidence
IMPEACHING REVENUE'S EVIDENCE — PLEADING INADMISSIBILITY FIRST; 4 FACTORS FOR ADMISSIBILITY; FLOW DIAGRAM
Pleading Inadmissibility — First Step
STEP 1 Pleading inadmissibility is the FIRST step in impeaching Revenue's evidence
OMIT THIS Omit this step only to discover a presumption in favour of Revenue starts to emerge — pleading is essential
ADJUDICATION Must reach a finding on admissibility of Revenue's evidence — not from lack of counter-evidence by taxpayer, but from the notice itself and substantiating admissibility on 4 factors
4 Factors for Admissibility
(a) Contemporaneously occurring
(b) For compatible purposes
(c) By unrelated parties
(d) Nothing to the contrary available on record
Flow: Revenue vs Taxpayer
FLOW A Revenue's evidence entered → Taxpayer pleads inadmissibility → Revenue unable to establish admissibility → Taxpayer NOT obliged to introduce rebuttal evidence
FLOW B Revenue's evidence entered → Taxpayer pleads inadmissibility → Revenue's evidence prima facie established as admissible → Taxpayer to introduce evidence in rebuttal
ERROR Revenue's evidence entered → Taxpayer introduces evidence in rebuttal (skipping inadmissibility plea) → Revenue excused from establishing admissibility
Illegal Evidence in GST
GST RULE Rigorous rules of Indian Evidence Act (s.24-30) are NOT applicable in GST proceedings — even illegally secured evidence is admissible in GST
NOT POLICE PO is not a 'Police Officer' — statements made before PO are NOT protected under s.24-30 of Evidence Act
THEREFORE Illegal or extra legislative methods must be met with strong objection and active disobedience in replies to notices — this is the only protection available
15.10 — Admissibility of Evidence
ADMISSIBILITY — CONTEMPORANEOUS EVIDENCE; TWO WAYS TO BECOME ADMISSIBLE; 'CLINCHING' EVIDENCE DEFINED
What is Admissible Evidence
RULE Evidence is admissible if it is CONTEMPORANEOUS — not when specially prepared for purposes of present enquiry
Contemporaneous evidence prepared for (i) compatible purposes (ii) by disinterested third-parties → HIGH probative value
Collected without disclosing circumstances of present enquiry → IMPECCABLE admissibility
Two Ways Evidence Becomes Admissible
(a) DISPUTED Where it is disputed → it must be proved to be reliable
(b) UNDISPUTED Where it is left undisputed → it will be presumed to be reliable
BOTH SIDES Evidence does not become admissible merely because the Party introducing it — whether Revenue or taxpayer — considers it admissible
'Clinching' Evidence Defined
DEFINED Evidence will be considered 'clinching' when NO OTHER possibility can be explained by the said material introduced as evidence
MULTIPLE POSSIBILITIES When possibilities increase, each gets attached with a probability — making evidence prima facie unreliable and of loose evidentiary value
MOST EVIDENCE Most material touted to be 'evidence' fails on all counts — yet not a whisper is found in reply (to notice) about its doubtful probative value
Assumption-Requirement Gap
SPECTRUM Revenue's case lies at 'assumption' end — taxpayer's response exposes distance Revenue must travel to reach 'requirements' end to receive law's approval
REPLY FUNCTION Taxpayer's reply erects obstacles to keep Revenue's case from reaching from 'assumption' to 'requirements' end
ERROR Taxpayer's reply 'entering into merits' switches to 'assumption' end — exposing taxpayer's self-assessment for adjudication to examine at 'requirement' end
15.11 — Statements on Oath
STATEMENTS ON OATH u/s 70 — EX FACIE UNRELIABLE; THIRD-PARTY STATEMENTS; RELIABILITY INVERSELY RELATED TO TIME GAP
Why Statements are Ex Facie Unreliable
UNRELIABLE Summons proceedings: questions put, answers recorded — person called after swiftly recollecting events surrounding subject matter of investigation = ex facie unreliable
DEPONENT Most Deponents are not 'fully' knowledgeable about the goings-on in business; Senior functionaries passively involved in tax/regulatory compliance are not knowledgeable about interpretation
RULE Gap between date of summons and date of events under inquiry, and reliability of statements recorded, are INVERSELY related — larger the gap, less reliable
Statements Must Be Limited
RULE Statements-on-oath should be limited to 'facts' which are answered either from personal/first-hand knowledge or based on impersonal/record-based reported-knowledge
EXPERT OPINION Director's statement that 'indivisible works contracts bifurcated into PO for goods + WO for installation services' = 'expert interpretation' by Director who is neither GST expert nor contractual documentation expert → no respectable evidentiary value
S.136 s.136 does not disbelieve statements recorded without good reason — and this is not limited to prosecution u/s 132 but for all applicable actionable causes under any proceeding in law
Third-Party Statements
NEARLY ALWAYS When Deponents are third-parties, their statements are nearly always unreliable
Not sufficient to show third-party was familiar with the facts — must also show that the relationship between Deponent and taxpayer is not 'associated' such that there are reasons for Deponent to be less truthful (well-wishers) or inimical and ill-willed
EXAMPLE SCN relied on SOA of warehouse-keeper that goods received without invoices — without corroboration, this is just one of many possibilities including invoices delivered to Corporate Office with only goods to warehouse
15.12 — Probative Value of Evidence
PROBATIVE VALUE — TESTED FOR RELIABILITY; WHAT DOES IT PROVE; INNOCENCE IS NOT THE QUESTION
Testing Probative Value
RULE Every material introduced as evidence deserves to be tested for its probative value — whether reliable and if so, to what end and what does it prove
EXAMPLE Income appearing in books of account = proof only that said amount is NOT a current-year liability — may result from (i) unpaid suppliers (ii) reversal of previous year expenses (iii) supplier due reductions (iv) non-supply transactions (v) supply not leviable to tax (vi) supply leviable but accounted correctly
MULTIPLE POSSIBILITIES When possibilities increase → each gets probability → makes evidence prima facie unreliable of loose evidentiary value to support liability, much less demonstrate all 'taxing ingredients'
Innocence is NOT the Question
NOT THE Q Taxpayer's innocence is not at all the question in the notice — just as adjudication is not the search for truth
THE QUESTION Putting the 'notice to trial' and examining whether interpretation canvassed by Revenue is tenable or not — this is the 'assumption-requirement' gap
VALLEY Reply erects obstacles to keep Revenue's case from crossing the valley from 'assumption' to 'requirements' end — interpretation of Revenue OR taxpayer admitted only if it meets the taxing ingredients in the law as per rules of procedure
15.13 — Evidence Impeached by Denial
IMPEACHMENT BY DENIAL — NO PROOF REQUIRED TO DENY; CLOUD OF SUSPICION; DOCTRINE OF MERGER IN APPEAL
No Proof Required to Deny
RULE Impeachment does NOT require evidence to disbelieve an assertion — to expect taxpayer to produce evidence to disbelieve would imply burden of proof shifted to taxpayer
WHAT IS EXPECTED Revenue is expected to canvass an alternate interpretation and threaten a different tax treatment — NOT leave taxpayer to run around disproving Revenue's case
DENIED EVIDENCE When Revenue's evidence is 'denied' — comes under a cloud of suspicion; PO must restore it in adjudication by showing prima facie case is made out as alleged in notice; grounds on which evidence was entertained and considered admissible must stand challenge when taxpayer carries AO in appeal
Effect in Appeal — Doctrine of Merger
MERGER If evidence is admitted even though denied by taxpayer — by operation of 'doctrine of merger', this evidence flows from notice into AO and will be answerable in appeal
AA/GSTAT POWER AA and GSTAT have inherent powers to carry out "such further inquiry as may be necessary" to arrive at proper finding about interpretation canvassed and evidence adduced
REVERSIBILITY Evidence disbelieved = sufficient to put burden back on Revenue to satisfy in appeal that taxpayer's disbelief was baseless; taxpayers cannot lightly disbelieve Revenue's evidence
15.14 — Rebuttal and Evidence
