REBUTTAL — WHEN IT ARISES; STATE FACTS WITHOUT REASONS; DO NOT ENTER INTO MERITS WITHOUT PREJUDICE
When Rebuttal Arises
TRIGGER When Revenue's evidence has put forward a prima facie case and supports the allegation in the notice — THEN it is time to put forward taxpayer's view
LONG ROAD Bringing evidence in support of taxpayer's position as rebuttal starts with (i) discussion on facts (ii) law (iii) binding nature of precedents/instructions (iv) taxpayer's interpretation when prevailing legal position applied to admitted/asserted facts
ELECT Taxpayer may elect to 'respectfully decline' to enter into merits until findings are reached whether notice must survive or be dropped due to fatalities itself
State Facts — Without Reasons
RULE Self-assessment must be demonstrated given undisputed facts of transactions taken on record — stated in plain language WITHOUT adding reasons
REASONS = SELF-DOUBT Adding reasons overlays stated explanation of self-assessment with 'self-doubt' — reasons are justification, not explanation; important but thin line
EXAMPLE Replies to ASMT10: specific — discrepancy 'accepted or rejected' and consequent liability 'admitted or denied'; no requirement to submit detailed justification of correctness of self-assessment
EXAMPLE Replies to ADT2: to-the-point — observation 'accepted or rejected' and consequent demand 'admitted or denied'; no need to disclose possible defences that could be taken
Do NOT Enter Merits Without Prejudice
NOT ADVISABLE It is NOT advisable to enter into merits 'without prejudice' for two reasons:
(a) Acrimonious findings on validity of notice may be reached to 'save the demand' due to bias/disbelief that defects/discrepancies in notice are actually incurable
(b) Attention may be diverted from fundamental question of validity of notice and into merits to somehow confirm the demand
APPEARANCE OF BIAS Executive Officers entrusted with adjudication/appellate functions who continue to report to Executive Superiors are not free from 'appearance of bias' — decisions in Madras Bar Association 1 to 4 affirm this; even Member Technical in GSTAT is not free from appearance of bias
Proving Innocence NOT Required
RULE There is no requirement to 'prove innocence' — facts must be stated; facts are available in transaction documents and associated records
Facts when applied to law → furnishes the applicable treatment. If PO disputes facts — NOT for taxpayer to prove those facts; tables are turned and it falls on PO to show 'how' facts entertained by taxpayer are incorrect
SHORT REPLY Preliminary reply is NOT a poor reply and elaborate reply is NO assurance of favourable outcome — 'short preliminary reply' is the right approach
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 15 (Part A) | Reply to Notices ⇧ Index
These principles are for use in personal hearings at adjudication, appellate and revisionary stages. State them confidently and cite the statutory source.
Principle 1 — Adjudication is Trial of Revenue's View, Not Search for Truth [Para 15.1]
GST Act 2017 | General Litigation Principle
Key Ratio / Principle
Adjudication is not a search for the truth but a trial of the interpretation canvassed by Revenue. Reply to notice is not the last step — it is the place where defences are put forward with eyes set firmly on a 'day in Court' when findings reached by the Proper Officer in adjudication will be put to the test. Grounds to be urged in adjudication are vastly different from those to be urged in appeal — each round must be approached based on the relief that authority is empowered to allow.
When to Use: At every hearing — to orient the Bench/Officer that the exercise is a trial of Revenue's case, not an enquiry into taxpayer's guilt; to set the tone that each stage has its own distinct purpose and relief.
Principle 2 — Acquiescence by Replying on Merits Validates an Invalid Notice under S.160(2) [Para 15.3]
Section 160(2), CGST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
The right to question the validity of any notice will be lost by delay and by omitting to question its validity due to the embargo in section 160(2). By replying to a show-cause notice on merits, taxpayer has acquiesced and rendered the SCN (and consequent Adjudication Order) 'valid' as per section 160(2). Whether this embargo can really overcome the right to a valid notice is yet to be tested — but as it stands today, acquiescence validates an otherwise invalid notice and hence, the rest of the proceedings.
When to Use: At preliminary stage — to justify raising jurisdictional and procedural objections FIRST before entering into merits; to establish that silence and entry into merits is itself an admission of validity of the notice under s.160(2).
Principle 3 — Compound Jurisdiction Pyramid: Territory + Mapping + Statutory Provision + Specific Authorisation [Para 15.4]
Section 6(2), Section 67, Section 160(2), CGST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
Question of jurisdiction is not 'simple' jurisdiction but 'compound' jurisdiction as it touches "territory + administrative mapping + statutory provision + specific authorization." Omission to be mindful of this jurisdictional pyramid can cause irreparable prejudice to taxpayer's interest. Where jurisdiction is lacking, notice is incurable and must be dropped immediately — even if there is merit in the underlying demand which is not exposed by prematurely 'entering into merits' in the reply.
When to Use: As the first line of defence in SCN reply — demand copy of specific authorisation (Assignment letter) before proceeding to reply on merits; if authorisation is absent or defective, the entire proceedings are without jurisdiction regardless of merit in underlying demand.
Principle 4 — Bona Fide Demand Cannot Legitimise Mala Fide Proceedings [Para 15.6]
Administrative Law | GST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
When proceedings are 'tainted', it does not matter if the underlying demand is real and due. When it comes to 'validity' of proceedings, not even a bona fide demand can legitimize mala fide proceedings. There is yet another rule that relief NOT pleaded cannot be allowed — not even in the interests of justice. Justice, therefore, properly understood is not the eventual outcome, especially in taxation matters, but the lawful process of getting to the outcome.
When to Use: When Revenue contends that since the demand is real and due, procedural defects should be overlooked — invoke this principle that the lawfulness of proceedings is a threshold requirement independent of the merits of the underlying demand.
Principle 5 — Question-the-Question: Never Rush into Merits; First Locate 'What' and 'How' [Para 15.7]
Section 73/74, CGST Act 2017 | Litigation Strategy
Key Ratio / Principle
Before launching to answer the question, it is most prudent to 'question-the-question' because ambiguity about the question will expose taxpayer to perils of: (a) forfeiture of right to demand Revenue satisfactorily discharge its burden of proof, (b) implicit admission of allegation left undisputed, and (c) shifting of onus of proof onto taxpayer. Taxpayer's role is to 'answer the question' — not 'imagine the question and then proceed to answer.' Taxpayers must grasp exactly 'what' is the allegation and 'how' has it been supported in the notice.
When to Use: As foundational approach in every SCN reply — insist on identifying the specific allegation and the specific evidence adduced in support; expose every missing 'essential fact' in the notice (POS, HSN, time of supply, etc.) before entering into merits.
Principle 6 — Allegations are Mere Conjecture Without Evidence; Burden Remains on Revenue [Para 15.8]
Section 73/74, CGST Act 2017 | Indian Evidence Act principles (persuasive)
Key Ratio / Principle
Allegations are mere conjecture, even if they are very logical, unless evidence is adduced to support them. One who makes the assertion bears the burden of proof — it is necessary for taxpayers to dispute the allegation and allow Revenue to discharge its burden. Disputing Revenue's assertion does not require 'proof' to be produced by taxpayer. Disputing Revenue's assertion is a position taxpayer takes regarding the evidence adduced in support of allegations made in the notice. Taxpayers are merely required to 'plead' and take this position that Revenue's evidence is disputed.
When to Use: In every personal hearing where Revenue presents evidence — first plead the allegation is disputed; allow Revenue to establish prima facie admissibility; only after Revenue has made out a prima facie case does taxpayer need to introduce rebuttal evidence.
Principle 7 — Illegal Evidence Must Be Met with Strong Objection and Active Disobedience [Para 15.5 / 15.10]
Section 67, 70, 71, CGST Act 2017 | Indian Evidence Act S.24-30 (not applicable in GST)
Key Ratio / Principle
Evidence obtained illegally is NOT barred from being relied upon to issue notice in GST proceedings. Rigorous rules of the Indian Evidence Act (sections 24 to 30) are not applicable in GST proceedings. Proper Officers are not 'Police Officers' — statements made before them are NOT protected under sections 24 to 30 of the Evidence Act. Therefore, illegally or extra-legislatively secured evidence is admissible in GST. For this reason, illegal or extra legislative methods must be met with strong objection and active disobedience in replies to notices — this is the only protection available.
When to Use: When SCN is based on evidence collected through extra-legislative methods (verbal instructions, s.71 inspections without 'reasons to believe', s.70 summons without s.67 authorisation, etc.) — raise specific objection in reply pointing out the absence of statutory authority; this objection must be made right at the point when illegal attempts are observed.
Principle 8 — Admissibility Must Be Established from Notice Itself; Taxpayer Not Obliged to Rebut Until Prima Facie Case Made [Para 15.9]
GST Act 2017 | Evidence Law Principles
Key Ratio / Principle
Adjudication must reach a finding on admissibility of Revenue's evidence — not from the lack of evidence to the contrary brought by taxpayer, but from the notice itself and by substantiating its admissibility on the factors of: (a) contemporaneously occurring, (b) for compatible purposes, (c) by unrelated parties, and (d) nothing to the contrary on record. If Revenue is unable to establish admissibility of its evidence, taxpayer is NOT obliged to introduce rebuttal evidence. Taxpayer's inadmissibility plea is sufficient. Most material touted to be 'evidence' fails on all four counts.
When to Use: In every adjudication — plead that specific documents/data relied upon by Revenue as evidence fail on one or more of the four admissibility factors; this forces the Proper Officer to reach a finding on admissibility before proceeding with adjudication.
Principle 9 — Statements on Oath are Ex Facie Unreliable; Reliability Inversely Related to Time Gap [Para 15.11]
Section 70, Section 136, CGST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
Summons proceedings and the manner in which they are conducted is rife with doubt and anxiety, and persons summoned being called upon to answer after swiftly recollecting the events surrounding the subject matter of investigation which is recording as their statements-on-oath — is ex facie unreliable. The gap between date of summons and date of events under inquiry, and reliability of statements recorded, are inversely related — larger the gap, less reliable the statement. Statements-on-oath should be limited to 'facts' answered from personal and first-hand knowledge or from impersonal record-based reported-knowledge.
When to Use: When SCN is based primarily on statement-on-oath — challenge the probative value: (a) date of events vs date of summons gap; (b) whether Deponent had first-hand knowledge; (c) whether Deponent is a third-party with possible bias; (d) whether statement is from personal knowledge or merely anxiety-driven admission.
Principle 10 — There is No Requirement to Prove Innocence; Facts Must Be Stated; If PO Disputes Facts, Burden Shifts to PO [Para 15.14]
GST Act 2017 | Litigation Strategy
Key Ratio / Principle
There is no requirement to 'prove innocence.' Facts must be stated. Facts are available in transaction documents and associated records. Facts when applied to the law furnishes the treatment applicable. There is no question of proof of correctness of facts. Facts are undisputable. And if Proper Officer disputes the facts, it is NOT for taxpayer to prove those facts. Tables are now turned — it falls on Proper Officer to show 'how' facts entertained by taxpayer are incorrect. A preliminary reply is not a poor reply and an elaborate reply is no assurance of a favourable outcome.
When to Use: In personal hearing to resist cross-examination on admitted facts — state factual position clearly in plain language; if PO disputes the stated facts, demand that PO specify which facts are disputed and how; the burden to disprove taxpayer's stated facts lies on the Revenue, not on taxpayer to establish those facts.
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Chartered Accountants, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 15 (Part A) — Reply to Notices | GST Act 2017
⚖️ Chapter 15B — Reply (Para 15.15–15.36)

REPLY TO NOTICES — PARA 15.15 to 15.36

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 TABLE OF CONTENTS — Click to Navigate

15.15 Discussion on Facts 15.16 Restore Facts 15.17 Expose Conjecture 15.18 Summarize Facts-in-Issue 15.19 Discussion on Law 15.20 Evidence via Affidavit 15.21 Binding Nature of Circulars 15.22 Binding Nature of Decisions 15.23 Prayer on Findings 15.24 Personal Hearing 15.25 Readiness for Later Proceedings 15.26 Business Description ≠ HSN 15.27 Introducing New Material 15.28 Relevance of Circulars 15.29 Case Law — MORE 15.30 Case Law — LESS 15.31 Essentials in Reply 1 15.32 Essentials in Reply 2 15.33 Without Prejudice Replies 15.34 With Prejudice Replies 15.35 Alternate Pleas Replies 15.36 Circulars & Instructions Issued ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
§ 15.15 — DISCUSSION ON FACTS
⬆ Index
Discussion on Facts — Taxpayer's Reply Strategy
Prima Facie Case by Revenue
Reply must attend to 'facts' as they are
Clear the air — limit consideration to undisputed facts only✓ GOAL
Address disputed facts separatelyIF disputed
Undisputed Facts — First Layer
Form the foundation before erecting the rest of the structure
Reference admissions — express or implicit — from within the notice itself
Avoids debate all over again on admitted aspects✓ TACTIC
Three Categories of Facts
Facts about legal entity & distinct person
Facts about transaction & identity of transaction documents
Facts about treatment applied & disclosures in statutory records
Arranging Facts Properly
Taxpayer's own treatment during disputed period + disclosure in contemporary records → raises presumption of reliability✓ KEY
'Distinct person' identity — if multiple registrations / similar names — must be specifically identified✗ RISK
Reply must 'disclose' treatment — NOT justify or elaborate to convinceRULE
Tax position ≠ tax treatment. Treatment is given; position is the interpretation adopted as 'basis'✗ CONFUSE
Avoid synonymous expressions with profound legal significance used in common parlance✗ DANGER
Elimination of Irrelevant Facts
Irrelevant facts, even if undisputed, must be identified and eliminated
Example: SVLDR Scheme admission in SCN demanding RCM on security services — undisputed but irrelevant; law & ingredients not comparable under two legislations
§ 15.16 — REPLY TO RESTORE FACTS
⬆ Index
Restoring Omitted / 'Cherry-Picked' Facts
Why Revenue Omits Facts
OversightIF
Deliberate bias✗
Facts not advancing Revenue's case may be overlooked but may materially guide taxpayer's interpretation of law
How to Restore
State without hope or assumption of Revenue's deep understanding✓ APPROACH
Restore omitted facts — reorganize entire factual matrix (taxpayer + transaction + compliance)
Innocuous omissions include: assertions under allied laws that are pari materia and accepted by regulators✓ INCLUDE
Examples of Restorable Facts
SCN on ITC reversal HSN 9954: restoration = show construction NOT capitalized in books per Rules & AS
SCN on output tax HSN 9993 denying healthcare exemption: restore fact of 'clinical establishment' registration
§ 15.17 — REPLY TO EXPOSE CONJECTURE
⬆ Index
Conjecture Admitted as 'Facts' in Notice — How to Expose
What is Conjecture in Notice
Assumptions based on lack of understanding of trade practices✗ PROBLEM
Peculiar practices in each trade are poorly appreciated by outsidersROOT CAUSE
Expose Trade Practice — Examples
'Wastage' in jewellery trade ≠ actual gold lost. As composite supply, cost added to taxable value. But accounting treatment of expenditure does not attract credit reversal
Clubhouse charges by Developer ≠ membership fee. It is recovery of construction cost. Not separate supply. Lower 12% rate cannot be denied by overlooking this fact
Scrap sale by authorized service centre ≠ supply of goods. Amount is additional consideration for vehicle repair. No title passes to Buyer. 18% demand under HSN 9989 only possible by omitting this trade practice
Rule for Reply
Facts from allied laws + domain understanding must be exposed in reply to eliminate assumptions, conjecture and surmise✓ RULE
§ 15.18 — REPLY TO SUMMARIZE FACTS-IN-ISSUE
⬆ Index
Facts-in-Issue — The Decisive Facts for Outcome
What are Facts-in-Issue
Facts that bear upon the outcome — without conjecture, without embellishments✓ DEFINITION
When irrelevant facts are eliminated early, 'findings to be reached' emerge clearly for adjudication
Examples of Facts-in-Issue
SCN on mismatched credit (2A v. 3B): FII = whether suppliers defaulted AND recovery proceedings failed and attained finality. Without this, denying credit is mere assumption on S.16(2)(c)
SCN on tour operator HSN 9985 / 5% without credit: FII = (i) do services fit 'tour operator' definition? (ii) should output tax at 18% have been demanded under s.76?
SCN on penalty u/s 129(1)(a) on OMV basis: FII = whether movement was by way of sale, lease, rental or not at all. Without this, demand based on assumption
§ 15.19 — DISCUSSION ON LAW
⬆ Index
How to Discuss Law in Reply — Approach and Pitfalls
What NOT to Do
Reproduce copious extracts from law — bulk-up reply✗ AVOID
List all statutory provisions then zero in — unnecessary✗ WRONG
What to Do
Extract relevant portions only — highlight key words bearing on context✓ DO
Plainly state applicable statutory provisions✓ DO
Discuss law to lead up to taxpayer's understanding and lay out background for tax treatment adopted✓ DO
Interpretation — Critical Component
Extend discussion to 'interpretation' — statutory definitions + connected provisions
Purpose: expose misapplication of statutory provisions while making allegations in the notice✓ PURPOSE
Do NOT falter at this stage — incorrect statutory provisions applied → goes downhill from here✗ WARNING
Fatal Errors — Examples
Admitting 'intermediary agent' status + POS determined under s.13(3) instead of 13(8) — fatal error in defence unless deliberate strategy✗ FATAL
Using 'voucher' loosely in reply without stating nature (instrument with obligation / RBI approved PPI). Once asserted as voucher, treatment applicable cannot be disputed✗ FATAL
§ 15.20 — EVIDENCE VIA AFFIDAVIT
⬆ Index
Affidavit as Evidence — When, How, Limits
When Affidavit is Needed
Assertions that ought NOT to have contemporaneous documentary or other material evidenceTRIGGER
Simply assert 'existence and reliability' of those facts + support with sworn Affidavit✓ REMEDY
Authorized Signatory (not AR who acts under instructions) must execute
Effect of Affidavit
Revenue must accept assertions as true OR impeach with contrary evidence✓ FORCE
Risk of perjury too great for Revenue to disbelieve lightly✓ STRENGTH
Good place for Affidavit: 'date of service' of notice (for time-bar ground)BEST USE
When NOT to Use Affidavit
Where contemporaneous evidence is available — avoid Affidavit for those facts✗ DON'T
Not warranted → decline demand to 'support denial of allegation' with Affidavit✗ DECLINE
Affidavit vs Certificate vs Opinion
Expert certificates: enjoined with risk of professional misconduct if facts asserted non-existent/unreliable
Certificates ≠ 'opinion'. Opinion states state-of-things + methodology of discovery
Indirect evidence = evidence of secondary things existing only when primary things exist. Do not introduce for facts requiring direct evidence✗ RULE
Examples
Inadmissible credit not availed: NO contemporaneous evidence. Inferred from records showing only admissible credits availed. Taxpayer cannot be faulted for not 'claiming and reversing' inadmissible credits
GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B difference ≠ non-payment of tax. Must show which data is more reliable. No demand without real underlying taxable supply. Mere data difference = only suspicion begging enquiry
Supply without invoice allegation: needs red-handed evidence OR indirect evidence of (i) inward supply docs, (ii) outward movement of goods, (iii) customer confirmation, (iv) stock shortage
§ 15.21 — BINDING NATURE OF CIRCULARS
⬆ Index
Circulars — Binding Force on Proper Officer
Which Circulars are Binding
Under section 168 — expressly binding✓ BINDING
Without reference to s.168 — no less binding. Purpose: avoid administrative anarchy from divergent treatment✓ ALSO BINDING
Taxpayer's Obligation in Reply
Place on record (i) existence and (ii) applicability of all extant circulars and instructions✓ MANDATORY
Any departure by Proper Officer will leave much to be explained in appeal
Pre-GST / Earlier Tax Regime Circulars
Can illuminate understanding of law IF field of law occupied by those circulars is 'splitting image' of GSTCONDITION
Example: Circular 423 (mandatory SCN even after departmental appeal) — judicial authorities under s.11A of Central Excise (splitting image of s.73 CGST) cannot be dismissed merely because of earlier tax regime context
§ 15.22 — BINDING NATURE OF DECISIONS
⬆ Index
Hierarchy of Judicial Decisions — Binding Force
Supreme Court
Applies to ALL authorities in ALL States/UTs including all High Courts✓ SUPREME
Final and binding even if it appears to need reconsideration✓ FINAL
Later Apex Court decision may overturn earlier decision of same CourtNOTE
High Courts
Binding over all authorities within respective State/UT✓ BINDING
HC of one State with better Bench composition prevails over earlier HC of another State especially when that earlier decision was consideredIF
Divergent views by two HCs — final/binding over their respective States if co-equal judicature
Per incuriam decision: final/binding until carried in Review PetitionUNLESS
Case Law Usage Rules
Present case law after proposition is presented and argued✓ SEQUENCE
Thrusting decisions for every sentence = 'brow beating' — denies Proper Officer independent consideration✗ AVOID
Include decisions on both sides — it would be impossible to conceal a contrary decision (s.108 + long limitation). Present correctly and point to taxpayer's subscribed interpretation✓ FAIR PLAY
Experts advise: compile both sets — help Proper Officer consider taxpayer's position in light of most expeditious current decision
§ 15.23 — PRAYER ON FINDINGS TO BE REACHED IN ADJUDICATION
⬆ Index
Prayer on Findings — Guiding the Adjudicator
Why Point to Findings
Complete adjudication requires pointing to 'findings to be reached'✓ PURPOSE
Overburdened Proper Officer limited by time from PH to drafting order — this portion is immense assistance✓ PRACTICAL
Even if 'obvious' — stating it does no harm. Superfluity never killed any reply
Examples of Findings-to-be-Reached
SCN denying credit on capital goods for electricity (captive): Finding = credit admissible only when output from capital goods itself is taxable OR when output from plant is taxable
SCN on credit for capital goods used in Gwalior building maintenance: Finding = whether 'one to one' correlation is applicable for credit utilisation under same GSTIN
§ 15.24 — PERSONAL HEARING
⬆ Index
Personal Hearing — Rights, Practice, Record
Legal Right
s.126(3) opportunity to be heard is perfected by demand in writing for 'personal hearing' under s.75(4)✓ PROVISION
Request may be made right at the start — when acknowledging service of noticeEARLY
Record of Personal Hearing
Cryptic/standardized record is disservice to mandate of granting PH — appears as empty formality✗ PROBLEM
Carry plain-paper 'record of PH' with key arguments in short sentences — including on limitation and jurisdiction. PO may stamp/sign and attach✓ PRACTICE
Since AR argued, record authored by AR is more accurate✓ TIP
When to Skip PH
Only if taxpayer is confident absence won't prejudice outcome OR has no hope at this stageCONDITION
§ 15.25 — READINESS FOR LATER PROCEEDINGS
⬆ Index
Reply as Foundation for Appeal — Readiness Principle
Reply Serves Dual Purpose
Primary: Adjudication✓
Secondary: Foundation for Appeal✓
Appellate Authority's Scrutiny
AA will call adjudication records — confirm what grounds were raised before PO and what findings were reached
Changing approach in later stages: frowned upon by AA as failure of defence. May refuse to entertain✗ RISK
Be sure of approach from the very beginning — right from pre-notice stages✓ RULE
Adjudication Order in Appeal
In appeal, it is the Adjudication Order (not the notice) that is 'at large'
AA entertains appeal only if AO is shown to be deserving interferenceTHRESHOLD
AA is NOT to 'redo' adjudication — only to examine if AO is perverse in law or procedure causing injustice by misapplication of law
Purpose of Reply on Findings
Expose pitfalls in reaching relevant 'finding on facts' agitable before AA seeking intervention and remediation✓ STRATEGY
§ 15.26 — DESCRIPTION OF BUSINESS DOES NOT DICTATE HSN
⬆ Index
Business Description ≠ HSN for Output Tax Computation
The Principle
Business of appellant does not substantiate HSN to compute output tax on supplies allegedly escaping self-assessment✓ RULE
Business activities described at registration ≠ bar against making supplies unconnected to those described✓ PRINCIPLE
Examples
Works contract RP reporting 'other income': No assumption that 'other income' is taxable under HSN 9954 without more material on record
Real estate developer reporting turnover in Table 5 of GSTR-9: No assumption taxable under HSN 9954. More material needed: output tax rate, ratio to Table 4, GSTR-9C reconciliation
Self-Assessment Principle
Authority to self-assess requires exercise be carried out truthfully. Truthfully ≠ accurately. Not patently erroneous on facts or mischievously inaccurate on law → always truthful✓ PROTECTION
Submitting reconciliation implicitly admits wrongdoing and discharges Revenue's burden. RP now must disprove Revenue's case — easily rejected as 'not satisfactory'✗ TRAP
Passion to protect Revenue does NOT authorize bypassing due process. No PO can claim more passionate than Parliament which permitted self-assessment✓ ARGUMENT
Undisputed Allegation Consequence
Where allegations left undisputed — burden of proof on Revenue stands discharged. Not even AA or GSTAT is free to travel beyond order-at-large✗ FATAL
§ 15.27 — INTRODUCING NEW MATERIAL
⬆ Index
New Material — Risks, Obligations, Burden of Proof
Core Obligation in Adjudication
One of most important objectives of reply = place on record ALL material that will be referred in appeal✓ OBJECTIVE
Taxpayers often omit to 'place on record' relevant documents for determining any question of law or fact✗ COMMON ERROR
Risk of Introducing New Material at Appellate Stage
AA/AT could dismiss introduction due to embargo in Rule 112✗ RISK
'Remand with directions' only possible with AT (s.113(1)) — NOT AA (s.107(11))NOTE
Burden of Proof Shift
Introducing new material in later proceedings shifts onus to taxpayer to prove facts sought to be established by such new material✗ SHIFTS BURDEN
Also takes away Revenue's burden to establish its own allegations in the notice✓ HELPS REVENUE
Common Law Principles (Indian Evidence Act)
(a) One who makes assertion bears burden to prove it
(b) One who asserts exclusion bears burden to prove exclusion
(c) One retaining special information bears burden to disclose and prove it
§ 15.28 — RELEVANCE OF CIRCULARS
⬆ Index
Circulars — Taxpayer's Right to Accept, Reject or Use Selectively
Taxpayer's Right re Circulars
Welcome to DISREGARD interpretation in circulars — due to disagreement or lack of reasoning✓ RIGHT
Welcome to TAKE ADVANTAGE of favourable circulars — even if ratio is doubted✓ RIGHT
Circulars with Gaps/Ambiguity
Circular 164/48/2018 — 'can be adopted' (directory not mandatory) — especially retrospective application
Circular 177/9/2022-TRU — sale of plots 'after' development not liable. But sale 'before' and 'during' development mysteriously skirted
Circular 171/3/2022 — bogus invoice issuer not liable to output tax (no supply) but doesn't address s.76 (invoice issued 'with tax')
Withdrawn Circulars
Circular 105/24/2019 on post-supply discounts — withdrawn. Practices now open for Revenue to canvass; decided on facts in each case✗ WITHDRAWN
When Circular Conflicts with Judicial Authority
CCE v. Ratan Melting (2008) 231 ELT 22: mere existence of circular cannot curtail implementation of incorrect interpretation. Justice demands such interpretation be jettisoned✓ RATIO
CBIC Circular 1006/13/2005-CX: Officers authorized to deviate from circulars inconsistent with judicial authorities. Pending reconsideration, Officers must comply with judicial authorities✓ CBIC ITSELF
§ 15.29 — CASE LAW AUTHORITIES — MORE
⬆ Index
Case Law Overload — Why 'More' is Dangerous
The Fallacy of 'More Decisions = More Power'
Nothing more grotesque in a reply than case law overload✗ GROTESQUE
Binding precedent in GST will develop only after 10+ years — until Revision Petitions under s.117/118 reach Courts
Citation of many decisions may do more harm than good✗ HARM
Proper Officers who find this tiresome may overlook even good decisions that are squarely on point but lying undetectable in this mix✗ RISK
Approach Consideration
Case law overload is a matter of approach — cannot be applied without considering Proper Officer's likely reactionCONSIDER
Some taxpayers have tasted success with this approach — so it cannot be universally condemnedCAVEAT
§ 15.30 — CASE LAW AUTHORITIES — LESS
⬆ Index
Case Law — Precise Use, Types of Binding Precedent
Rule: What to Cite
Case law = required only after proposition is presented and argued well✓ SEQUENCE
Cite only the final ruling — not 'arguments' appearing in decisions✓ ONLY RULING
There is a tradition: certain decisions MUST be cited even if not directly relevant — Proper Officers frown at replies missing these essentials✓ TRADITION
Three Types of Binding Precedent
(a) Decision on facts — binding where facts are identical before any subordinate authority✓ FACTUAL
(b) Interpretation of law — binding even if facts not identical, as long as same provision is being interpreted✓ LEGAL
(c) Ratio of approach to interpretation of comparable provision — binding where provision is pari materia✓ PARI MATERIA
Example (a): Taghar Vasudev Ambrishva v. AAAR [2022] — exemption under entry 12 for residential dwelling. Binding in Karnataka; persuasive elsewhere unless contrary decision of jurisdictional HC
Example (b): Vinod Kumar v. CSGST SA 123/2022 (UK) — cancellation of registration = starvation, violation of Art.21. Applies regardless of business/classification
Example (c): Munjaal Manishbhai Bhatt v. UOI [2022] — fixed abatement by notification cannot sustain where actual value available. Applies wherever fixed abatement applied and option to rely on actual value disallowed
Must-Cite Decisions
EWB matter: Asstt. Commissioner v. Satyam Shivam Papers (P) Ltd. [2022] 134 taxmann.com 241/90 GST 479 (SC)
Prosecution for fake bill racketeering: P.V. Ramana Reddy v. UOI [2019] 104 taxmann.com 407/73 GST 727 (Telangana)
Online gaming: State of Maharashtra v. Gurdeep Singh Sachar SLP (Crl.) 42282/2019 & Varun Gumber v. UT of Chandigarh CWP 7559/2017
§ 15.31 — ESSENTIALS IN A REPLY 1 — ISSUE-WISE POSITION MATRIX
⬆ Index
Issue-wise Position: Accept / Reject & Challenge / Deny & Replace
For Each Issue in Notice
"Accept and Proceed" — accept position and proceed✓
"Reject and Challenge" — reject and contestIF
"Deny and Replace" — deny revenue's position, replace with correct position✗ REPLACE
Checklist of Issues
Distinct Person / Legal Entity
Jurisdiction (Underlying proceedings / PO / Notice)
Facts / Allegations / Evidence
Cause-of-action / Sufficient & complete taxing ingredients
Scope and limitations
Position of law / Circulars & instructions / Judicial authorities
Tax treatment (adopted / canvassed by Revenue / distinguishing points)
Findings to be reached / Contrary instructions / Contrary authorities
Key Principle
Once a point is asserted, it becomes an indelible part of taxpayer's defence. Adding explanation ≠ more reliable. Omitting further explanation ≠ erode reliability✓ PRINCIPLE
§ 15.32 — ESSENTIALS IN A REPLY 2 — INGREDIENT-WISE TABULATION
⬆ Index
Ingredient-wise Tabulation of Demand — Precision Defence
Why Tabulate Ingredients
Basis of demand (DRC1/ASMT14/RFD8/MOV7/MOV10) confirms ingredients considered infraction. Specificity in affirming ingredients also disaffirms infraction of other ingredients✓ POWER
Tabulation brings facts-in-issue into sharp focus — prevents extraneous/new matters from entering adjudication. s.75(7) safeguard✓ S.75(7)
Example Ingredients — Section 54 (Refund)
Eligibility, RFD-1 within limitation, RFD-2 acknowledgement, Computation under applicable rule, Max refund admissible, ECL/ECrL debit, Statement A/B, CA certificate on unjust enrichment, Documentary proof (FIRC/eBRC/LEO)
Example Ingredients — Section 67-74 (Demand)
Date of inspection, INS1, Subject of evasion, Audit matters covered, Objections to INS1, Summons, Multiple summons, Statement recorded, Docs collected, Copy of statement, Statement is 'relied upon doc' in SCN, Retraction filed
Under s.74: Basis of intelligence (1st party/3rd party), Proceedings u/r.86A, s.83, Recovery action prior to SCN, RFD1 against pre-SCN recovery, Possibility of 'double recovery'
Example Ingredients — Section 129-130 (Detention/Confiscation)
s.129: Consignor/Consignee registered, Point of interception in reasonable route, Owner present/via carrier, Statement of driver, Copy of statement, Date MOV6, Date MOV7, Conveyance detained, Reason for detention
s.130(1): Supply in contravention with intent to evade / Non-accounting of goods / Supply without registration / Contravention with intent to evade / Use of conveyance — each with Noticee's position: Denied / Not Applicable / YES
§ 15.33 — 'WITHOUT PREJUDICE' REPLIES
⬆ Index
'Without Prejudice' — Protection and Strategy
What 'Without Prejudice' Means
Caveat forestalls prejudice that actions may cause — even if addressee is unmindful of protection in law✓ PROTECTION
'Without Prejudice' marking CANNOT be contradicted by entering into merits. Such reply vacates the reservation✗ WARNING
When to Use
Notice is appreciably wanting in jurisdiction, limitation or arbitrariness — no wisdom in replying on meritsIF NOTICE DEFECTIVE
Mark reply 'without prejudice' where entering into merits may not be necessary in face of defects, discrepancies and deficiencies in notice✓ STRATEGY
GSTAT Power — Important Distinction
AA does NOT enjoy power of remand (s.107(11)). AT does — s.113(1)✓ KEY
Where notice held valid but no findings on merits: demand will NOT be confirmed in vacuuo without hearing taxpayer on merits. AT may direct taxpayer to address on merits for first time or remand for fresh adjudication
But where findings reached on merits due to taxpayer's premature reply on merits: AO will almost certainly eclipse any redressal of grievance about notice invalidity✗ ECLIPSE
§ 15.34 — 'WITH PREJUDICE' REPLIES
⬆ Index
'With Prejudice' — The Default Position and Three Approaches
Default Position
Every reply is 'with prejudice' unless marked 'without prejudice'✓ DEFAULT
Taxpayer exposed to consequences of contents — averments made/omitted, evidence introduced/omitted, authorities cited/omitted
Three Strategic Approaches
'ACCEPT AND DISCHARGE' — accept liability subject to benefit under s.73(8)/74(8) r/w s.75(2)✓ APPROACH 1
'SOFT AND TRADITIONAL' — contest demand but avoid contesting validity of notice/proceedings. Assist adjudication by introducing all requested materialAPPROACH 2
'FIRM AND JUDICIOUS' — contest demand firmly even if AO adverse; relief available in law after proper appreciation of facts-on-recordAPPROACH 3
Taxpayers often prefer 'mid path' due to fear of retribution. But POs carry out administrative duties — not personal agenda. No forum will 'do justice' if taxpayer failed to remain vigilant and avail rights
§ 15.35 — 'ALTERNATE PLEAS' REPLIES
⬆ Index
Alternate Pleas — Strategy, Danger, Conditions
What are Alternate Pleas
Where two or more possible approaches to rebuttal exist — matter of strategy whether to go with everything or pick one line of attack
There is 'no single rule that has universal applicability' — great practitioners in litigation follow no consistent approach. Wisdom prevails✓ FLEXIBILITY
Multiple Grounds vs Multiple Reasons
Multiple GROUNDS of attack = matter of strategy✓ STRATEGY
Multiple REASONS for taking one tax position = can be prejudicial to taxpayer's defence✗ DANGEROUS
Danger of Alternate Pleas
Completely divergent defences may render both unreliable✗ RISK
Example (RCM s.9(4)): Asserting 'no liability' under s.9(4) AND 'taxing ingredients absent' are completely different positions. Only ONE to be taken to avoid contradiction
Soft denial or omission to deny = construed as non-denial. Make taxpayer's position unambiguous when it comes to denial of liability✗ RULE
§ 15.36 — CIRCULARS AND INSTRUCTIONS ISSUED (STATE & CBIC)
⬆ Index
State & CBIC Instructions — Persuasive Value Across States
CBIC Circulars / Instructions
CBIC Circular No. 183/15/2022-GST, 27 Dec. 2022
CBIC Instruction No. 2/2022 GST, 22 Mar. 2022
CBIC Circular No. 1006/13/2005-CX, 21 Sept. 2015 — limitations to binding nature of circulars
Maharashtra State GST Instructions
CST/JC(HQ)-5/Scrutiny/Issues/Fine No. B1, 25 Feb. 2022 (Internal Circular 2A of 2022)
CST/JC HQ-5/Adm. Instr. GST/UAO/B-2, Mumbai, 25 Feb. 2022 (Internal Circular 3A of 2022)
Other State GST Instructions
Delhi: F.No. 1(2)/DIT/L&J/Misc.) 2019-20/77-79, 11 Feb. 2022
Tamil Nadu: No. JC(HQ-5)/GST/Recurrent SCN/22-23 ADM-/B-74, 17 Nov. 2022 (Trade Circular 12-T of 2022)
Tamil Nadu: Circular No. 12/2022 (LW/12/30440383/2022), 26 Sept. 2022
Tamil Nadu: Circular No. 8/2024, 29 Aug. 2024
⚔️ HEARING ARSENAL — RATIOS FOR PERSONAL HEARINGS
⬆ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Narayan Bhagwantrao Gosavi Balajiwale v. Gopal Vinayak Gosavi
Supreme Court of India (cited in Para 15.33)
"Where findings have been reached on merits due to a premature reply on merits, such an Adjudication Order will almost certainly eclipse any redressal of grievance about the invalidity of the notice and illegality of earlier proceedings."
When to Use: Arguing at AT that notice was invalid — if AO reached merits, use this to show eclipse of invalidity. Alternatively, use to prevent Revenue from arguing taxpayer's 'without prejudice' defence was waived.
CCE v. Ratan Melting & Wire Industries
Supreme Court — (2008) 231 ELT 22 | Para 15.28
"Mere existence of a circular cannot curtail implementation of an incorrect interpretation. Justice demands that such interpretations be jettisoned and, if need be, the matter be carried before the appropriate Court for a ruling on correct interpretation — and circulars that are outdated or irrelevant should therefore be struck down."
When to Use: Where Proper Officer follows an old circular contrary to current judicial pronouncements. Cite to compel PO to apply judicial authority over the circular.
Taghar Vasudev Ambrishva v. Appellate Authority for Advance Ruling
Karnataka High Court — [2022] 135 taxmann.com 287/91 GST 750/63 GSTL 445 (Kar.) | Para 15.30
"Exemption under Entry 12 in respect of residential dwelling will be available no matter who occupies the premises for use as residence."
When to Use: In any matter involving denial of residential exemption on ground of identity of occupant — lessee, sub-lessee, etc. Binding in Karnataka; persuasive elsewhere.
Vinod Kumar v. CSGST
Uttarakhand High Court — SA 123/2022 (UK) | Para 15.30
"By cancellation of registration, taxpayer may suffer starvation and because of denial of livelihood for want of GST registration and is left without remedy — this is a violation of Article 21."
When to Use: In any SCN / proceedings threatening cancellation of registration. Cite to restrain Proper Officer from passing orders that deny taxpayer's right to livelihood.
Munjaal Manishbhai Bhatt v. UOI
Gujarat High Court — [2022] 138 taxmann.com 117/92 GST 327/62 GSTL 262 (Guj.) | Para 15.30
"Fixed abatement imposed by notification cannot be sustained where the actual value to be excluded (towards sale of land) is available in the facts of the given case."
When to Use: In any matter involving real estate where Revenue seeks to deny actual deduction for land value by insisting on fixed abatement. Cite to claim actual deduction from composite supply price.
Asstt. Commissioner v. Satyam Shivam Papers (P.) Ltd.
Supreme Court — [2022] 134 taxmann.com 241/90 GST 479 (SC) | Para 15.30
Mandatory citation in any EWB (e-way bill) related SCN or proceedings. This is a 'must cite' decision acknowledged as essential by the author.
When to Use: In every SCN / hearing involving detention/penalty for e-way bill related contraventions. Failure to cite is noted adversely by Proper Officers.
P.V. Ramana Reddy v. UOI
Telangana High Court — [2019] 104 taxmann.com 407/73 GST 727 | Para 15.30
Mandatory citation in SCN involving prosecution for fake bill racketeering. Acknowledged as a 'cannot be missed' decision — laying down foundational rights of accused in GST prosecution proceedings.
When to Use: In any prosecution SCN or proceedings alleging fake invoicing / ITC fraud. Must be cited as foundational authority on rights of Noticee in prosecution.
State of MP v. Orient Paper Mills Ltd.
Supreme Court — AIR 1977 SC 687 | Para 15.22
"Royalty is feudalistic euphemism for purchase price of State resources."
When to Use: Understanding that even a SC decision can be overturned by a later SC decision (Titaghur overturned Orient Paper). Cite to show hierarchy and caution against over-relying on a single precedent that may have been overturned.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Para 15.15–15.36 | GST Reply to Notices
⚖️ Chapter 16 — Relief in Adjudication

⚖️ Chapter 16 — Relief in Adjudication

GST Litigation Reference — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
16.1 Relevance in Replying
16.2 Pleadings
16.3 No Relief Beyond Pleadings
16.4 Moulding Relief
16.5 Injustice in Due Process
16.6 Creature-of-Statute Effect
16.7 Decisions Distinguished
16.8 Non-Binding Circulars
16.9 Adjudication in Offence Cases
16.10 Perspective Bias
16.11 Shortest Route
16.12 Adverse Orders
16.13 Defective Orders
16.14 Non-Speaking Orders
16.15 Consequential Relief
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
16.1 — Relevance in Replying to Notices — Complete Reply Structure
COMPLETE REPLY — 9 COMPONENTS IN SEQUENCE
① Acknowledge 'date of service'
→
② Pleading on demand (issue-wise)
Accepted or Denied
→
③ Pleading on allegations (issue-wise)
Disputed or Undisputed
↓
④ Pleading on evidence (issue-wise)
Admitted or Challenged
→
⑤ Pleading on cause-of-action (issue-wise)
Valid or Invalid
→
⑥ Summary of grounds (alleged) in SCN
↓
⑦ Impeach Revenue's case with evidence to disprove
→
⑧ Advance Taxpayer's case with evidence
→
⑨ Summary of pleadings (rebuttal) in reply
↓
Summary of findings to be reached (in adjudication)
→
Relief Sought
(Specific + General)
16.2 + 16.3 — Pleadings + No Relief Beyond Pleadings
PLEADINGS — STRATEGY IS EVERYTHING
Adjudication ≠ Last Step
Reply must set sights on grounds for appeal also
Contains preparatory work needed for later stages
Pleadings Must
Be cautious and based on facts-on-record
Embody the defence strategy
Summarise findings to be reached
NOT too much or too little of available defences
NO RELIEF BEYOND PLEADINGS
Proper Officer BARRED from travelling beyond notice
Relief limited to pleadings in reply
Sec 75(7) operates as bulwark
PRAYER (MANDATORY)
(i) Specific relief based on findings to be reached
(ii) General — "pass such orders as may be deemed fit and proper in facts and circumstances"
REALITY CHECK
Proper Officer's bias is undeniable — notice is Revenue's own case
'Truth does not matter — facts do'
16.4 + 16.5 — Moulding Relief + Injustice in Due Process
MOULDING RELIEF + DUE PROCESS INJUSTICE
Moulding Relief
Courts of Equity — can travel beyond notice to prevent miscarriage of justice
Proper Officer = creature of statute — CANNOT do justice by extra-legislative methods
PRINCIPLE
"Follow the procedures — justice will take care of itself"
Proper Officer and AA are not 'gatekeepers of justice' — only functionaries of procedure
Injustice in Due Process
Procedures are heartless — cannot accommodate 'second chances'
GST voted for heartless procedures
Government has shown humility — retrospective corrections: Rule 88B, Sec 43A, Rule 89(5), Rule 86A/B
16.6 — Creature-of-Statute Effect
CREATURE OF STATUTE — LIMITS OF ADJUDICATION
What this Means
Every statutory functionary = creature of statute
Cannot sit in judgment over vires of the statute
Grounds — Facts vs Law
FACTS Must be agitated from adjudication onwards — cannot be omitted at earliest opportunity
LAW Can be urged at any time and any stage until final disposal
EXAMPLE
Time-bar of SCN → can be raised in appeal even if not raised in adjudication
Classification of goods → cannot change stance in appeal if not disputed in adjudication
RULE
Pray only relief that can be allowed
Grounds must only be those the authority concerned can address and redress
16.7 + 16.8 — Decisions Distinguished + Non-Binding Circulars
CASE LAWS + CIRCULARS — HOW OFFICER MAY DEAL
Binding Force of Decisions
(i) Applicable on 'all fours' — fully binding
(ii) Applicable 'in principle' only
(iii) Applicable to 'dissimilar' facts — can be distinguished
OFFICER REFUSING
Must state 'why' with valid basis
Routinely refusing binding authorities → swift decision in appeal against such failure
Circulars — Sec 168
Have statutory force
Cannot be substituted by Officer's own interpretation
3 ways to challenge circular application: (a) Facts not comparable to illustration (b) Extrapolation beyond circular (c) Circular contains limiting words
TAXPAYER'S REMEDY
Even if Officer distinguishes — rejection of cited decisions gives grounds for appeal
Taxpayer not prejudiced ultimately
16.9 — Adjudication in Offence Cases
PENALTY IN GST — NOT AUTOMATIC
3 REASONS PENALTY ≠ AUTOMATIC
(a) Sec 75(7) — refers to 'interest' only, not 'penalty'
(b) Sec 126(3) — mandatory enquiry into degree, severity, mitigating factors
(c) Sec 135 — provides 'presumption' NOT 'assumption' of mental state
ANIMUS REQUIRED
Unless required animus (intent) shown to be present before act/omission alleged — penalty CANNOT be automatically applied
Registered vs Unregistered
Registered: Sec 122(2) — lesser concession when demand confirmed
Unregistered: Sec 122(1) — far more labour required to show mischief in act/omission
STRATEGY
Penalty is better addressed in APPEAL — allow adjudication to proceed with overconfidence and fail to find necessary animus
16.10 — Perspective Bias
THREE PERSPECTIVES TO FACTOR INTO DEFENCE STRATEGY
① Proper Officer's Perspective
Accountable for revenue leakage
Limitation running out — must issue notice when in doubt
9 factors cause perspective bias: complex law, plural views, multiple circulars, retrospective changes, bona fide assertions, mischievous taxpayers, limited access to books, AAR divergence, new learning
② Taxpayer's Perspective
3 types: (a) Vigilant — honest position challenged (b) Popular position — perplexed (c) Mischievous — surprised at detection
6 reasons to litigate: not viable to pay, no money, precedent risk, market practice, someone said fight, quarterly results
③ Appellate Authority's Perspective
AA leaves Adjudication Order undisturbed UNLESS taxpayer shows palpable misapplication of facts to law
Bake AA's perspective into defence strategy from the reply stage itself
KEY INSIGHT
"Patient's confidence is directly connected to Surgeon's confidence"
All that remains in the end are the facts — not the truth
16.11 — Shortest Route to Favourable Decision + Litigation Journey
SHORTEST ROUTE TO FAVOURABLE DECISION
Principle
Find, identify, locate shortest route → present it well
Reply must present defences that advance the case — NOT parade taxpayer's convictions
Skilful reply = charts course to 'eventual' relief piecemeal — within limits of each authority
ADJUDICATION ≠ END OF ROAD
Prepare for long journey
Decision to 'dispute and litigate' demands perseverance
Without conviction = only enthusiasm, not perseverance
Complete Litigation Journey
ASMT-10 / ADT-2 / INS1
→
DRC-1A
↓
SCN+DRC-1 / SOD+DRC-2
→
Adjudication Order DRC-7
↓
APL-1 + DRC-3
→
Appellate Order + APL-4
↓
RVN-1 + Stay Order
→
Revisionary Order + APL-4 (new 1)
↓
APL-5 + DRC-3 (addl. pre-deposit)
→
Appellate Order + APL-4 (new 2)
↓
Revision Petition to HC / SC
16.12 — Adverse Orders — Silver Lining
⛔ Failure in Reply🔒 Consequence — Halts Appellate Relief
Validity of notice accepted + legality of jurisdiction admittedJurisdiction no longer open for consideration in appeal
Demand disputed but allegations NOT deniedUndisputed allegations cannot be disputed in appeal
Evidence in support of allegations not challengedEvidence admitted — excuses Revenue from burden of proof
Facts stated in SCN not reviewed or restatedFacts admitted in SCN become final, binding and inalienable
New material and information introducedConclusions from such material liable to be rejected if reliability in doubt
Tax position in self-assessment explained with reasonsDemand sustained by fault-finding in self-assessment
No dispute raised on POSNature of demand (CGST-SGST or IGST) not to be disputed
No dispute raised on TOSInterest quantification accepted
Bona fides of inward supplies pleaded for credit admissibilityFailure of credit-conditions accepted
Additional grounds of defence not included in pleadingsNo new grounds to be allowed in appeal
Dispute raised on valuationLevy of tax no longer in dispute
KEY Adjudication is NOT search for truth — it is 'notice on trial'. An adverse order is not always a 'bad' order — a well-planned reply steers adjudication to arrive at a 'good' order even if immediately 'adverse'.
16.13 — Defective Orders — Advantages in Appeal
⚠️ Adverse Order Situation✅ Advantage This Creates in Appeal
Demand confirmed under Sec 73Admission that 'evasion of tax' absent in self-assessment
Demand confirmed under Sec 74 without discussing reliability of evidence for 'special circumstances'Relief u/s 75(2) available + fresh inquiry into special circumstances almost barred
Demand confirmed under Sec 63Use of estimates and guesswork and lack of evidence admitted
Fatal defects, discrepancies and deficiencies in SCN unaddressedCompels redressal before examining issues on merits
Grounds in Order beyond grounds in SCNShortcomings in SCN to support demand admitted
Haste and bias in OrderFailure of adjudication exposed
Dispute about facts in SCN not redressed or shown to be untrueConfirms demand to be contrary to facts
⛔ When Adjudication Orders Are Defective
(a) Notice was defective yet demand confirmed(b) Error in identity of distinct person
(c) Order passed beyond limitation period (Sec 75(10))(d) Order travelled beyond grounds in notice
(e) Order travelled beyond issues in notice
CAUTION For defective orders — file appeal AND keep eye on limitation u/s 107(1). Sec 161 rectification application does NOT stop limitation clock for appeal running.
16.14 + 16.15 — Non-Speaking Orders + Consequential Relief
NON-SPEAKING ORDERS + CONSEQUENTIAL RELIEF
Non-Speaking Orders
Violate principles of fairness in adjudication
Impose enormous burden on Appellate Authority for fact-finding
Appeal is the ONLY remedy — whether speaking or non-speaking
Non-speaking = affords additional grounds in appeal
NOT a ground to send back to Proper Officer for fresh adjudication
Consequential Relief
Primary Relief = Finding directly sought in adjudication
Secondary Relief = Consequential benefit flowing from primary finding
Sec 74 suppression NOT established → demand recomputed for shorter limitation under Sec 73
Registration suspension withdrawn → refund applications for past periods now open (Sec 54)
STRATEGY
Recognize and identify potential consequential (secondary) relief while drafting reply
Include in the prayer so it is not missed
⇧ Back to Index
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 16
Key principles and phrases for personal hearings. State these confidently before the Adjudicating Officer.
Creature of Statute Principle
"We are creatures of the statute and for this reason are excluded from sitting in judgment over the vires of statutory provisions."
USE WHEN: Officer claims power to go beyond notice or decides on grounds not in SCN — remind that Proper Officer is a creature of statute and bound by Sec 75(7)
No Relief Beyond Pleadings — Sec 75(7)
Proper Officer is barred from travelling beyond the notice and must even limit the relief to pleadings in reply. Section 75(7) operates as a bulwark against any curative attempt during adjudication.
USE WHEN: Officer attempts to cure defects in notice during adjudication proceedings — cite Sec 75(7) explicitly
Penalty is NOT Automatic — Sec 126(3)
Section 126(3) mandates inquiry into degree, severity and mitigating factors before imposing penalty. Section 135 provides only a 'presumption' and not an 'assumption' of mental state. Unless the required animus is shown to be present before the act or omission alleged, penalty cannot be automatically applied.
USE WHEN: Adjudicating Officer is about to confirm penalty routinely — challenge the lack of specific findings on animus and mitigating factors
Binding Authorities Must Be Distinguished With Valid Basis
Refusing to follow binding authorities is indiscipline and must be avoided by Proper Officers. Even distinguishing of decisions cannot be without valid basis. Routinely refusing binding authorities will invite swift decision in appeal — a failure that cannot be condoned, not even to save the demand.
USE WHEN: Officer refuses to follow a binding HC/SC decision — demand that reasons for distinguishing be recorded in the Order, and carry the matter in appeal
Circulars Have Statutory Force — Sec 168
Circulars issued under section 168 have statutory force and cannot be undermined by Proper Officer substituting a new interpretation, even if better or more accurate.
USE WHEN: Officer ignores a favorable CBIC Circular in adjudication — cite Sec 168 and the specific circular; demand it be considered
Adjudication is 'Notice on Trial' — Not Search for Truth
Adjudication is not a search for the truth — it is the notice on trial. The findings must be confined to the allegations and cause of action as stated in the notice. Evidence and material not part of the notice cannot be introduced at adjudication stage to 'save the demand'.
USE WHEN: Officer introduces new evidence or new grounds at personal hearing stage — object that adjudication is confined to the four corners of the notice
Law of Grounds — Facts Must Be Raised at Earliest Stage
Facts not disputed during adjudication and relied upon to confirm demand are rendered undisputed in appellate proceedings. Dispute about facts cannot be omitted to be agitated at the earliest opportunity. But dispute about law can be urged at any time and at any stage until final disposal.
USE WHEN: Framing the scope of personal hearing — identify and press all factual disputes at adjudication itself; legal grounds can be preserved for appeal
⚜️ Key Principles and Phrases for Personal Hearings
"Follow the procedures — justice will take care of itself"
Proper Officer and Appellate Authority are not gatekeepers of justice — only functionaries of procedure
USE WHEN: Appealing to the officer's sense of equity — remind that their duty is to follow the procedure faithfully, and just outcomes flow from that
"Truth does not matter — facts do"
Adjudication is based on facts on record, not on what taxpayer knows to be true
USE WHEN: Structuring the reply and hearing — insist that every factual claim of Revenue must be supported by admissible evidence on record
"Patient's confidence is directly connected to Surgeon's confidence"
Taxpayer's confidence in litigation depends entirely on quality and conviction of the practitioner
REMINDER: For self — prepare thoroughly; a confident, well-prepared advocate creates a favorable impression even in adverse hearings
Vires
"Within the powers" — creature of statute cannot question validity of the statute itself
USE WHEN: Distinguishing between questioning legality of action (permissible) vs challenging validity of the statute (not before Proper Officer)
Speaking Order
Order that discloses the reasoning and basis for the conclusion reached — ratio must be knowable
USE WHEN: Requesting at personal hearing that a speaking order be passed addressing each point of objection raised in reply
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 16 — Relief in Adjudication
⚖️ Chapter 17 — Outcome in Adjudication

CHAPTER 17 — OUTCOME IN ADJUDICATION

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 TABLE OF CONTENTS — Click to Navigate

17.1 Relevance in Replying to Notices 17.2 Revised Notice or Fresh Notice 17.3 Person Aggrieved 17.4 Remedies to Aggrieved Persons 17.5 Rectification of Apparent Errors 17.6 Taxpayer's Appeal 17.7 Restrictions under Section 121 17.8 Maintainability of Appeal 17.9 Payment under Protest 17.10 Payment = Admission of Liability 17.11 Direct Writ Petition 17.13 DRC3A — Immediate Steps ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
§ 17.1 — RELEVANCE IN REPLYING TO NOTICES
⬆ Index
Anticipating Outcome — Sharpening the Reply Strategy
Why Anticipate Outcome
Understanding relief in adjudication helps formulate and sharpen approach in replying to notices✓ PURPOSE
Knowing 'what next' (outcome) or having 'options' available can greatly influence approach in replying to notice✓ BENEFIT
Reply to notice must bake in necessary components of 'appellate strategy'. Foresight and planning = underlying message✓ RULE
Positivist Realist Approach
Steer clear of 'perspective bias' — be pleased with any outcome that advances taxpayer's case✓ MINDSET
If outcome unfavourable: examine if groundwork (i) brought out all material on record, (ii) only undisputed facts considered, (iii) relevant provisions and authorities appliedCHECKLIST
If outcome favourable: no time to celebrate unless AO is unassailable on law and merits. Revenue may carry in departmental appeal or revisionary proceedings✗ CAUTION
Classic Example — Wrong Reply Strategy
SCN on 'other income' as output tax → Taxpayer explains it is reduction in price by financial credit note → Fresh SCN issued demanding reversal of credit to extent payment not made. Result: Detailed reply triggered a new demand!
SCN on 'other income' → Taxpayer objects taxing ingredients absent; GST not tax on income; no demand by presumption of supply from accounting disclosure → AO confirmed citing reply 'incomplete'. Appeal ALLOWED — burden of proof on Revenue to demonstrate taxable supplies was not discharged✓ CORRECT APPROACH
§ 17.2 — REVISED NOTICE OR FRESH NOTICE
⬆ Index
No Provision in Law for 'Revised Notice' — Taxpayer's Rights
Two Mischievous Responses to a Good Reply
Revised notice — mischievous response✗ NO PROVISION
Fresh notice — surprisingly humble responseWITH CONDITIONS
Three Demands Taxpayer Must Make
(a) Speaking Order must be passed on notice already issued✓ DEMAND
(b) Simultaneous prosecution of same fact-in-issue via original + revised notice = misapplication of law✓ GROUND
(c) Fresh notice only after Speaking Order is passed withdrawing original notice — subject to applicable limitation✓ CONDITION
No Corrigendum / Addendum / Errata
These are also NOT permitted in law because defects, deficiencies and discrepancies are INCURABLE✗ INCURABLE
These methods permitted ONLY to correct typographical errorsEXCEPTION
Alteration of grounds = reapplication of mind to support demand by substituting grounds with new ones. Doctrine of election shows 'change of mind' → not a typographical error✗ NOT TYPO
§ 17.3 — PERSON AGGRIEVED
⬆ Index
Who is Aggrieved — Revenue's Perspective & Post-Audit
Key Principle
Person is aggrieved with the OUTCOME (of adjudication) — NOT with the grounds on which outcome is arrived✓ PRINCIPLE
Understanding Revenue's perspective while replying = what would Revenue's grounds be if AO were favourable to taxpayer?✓ FORESIGHT
Four Triggers for Revenue's Grievance
(a) Outcome favourable to taxpayer
(b) Contradictory views by different authorities
(c) Acceptance of view 'as final' not approved internally
(d) Objections raised in 'post audit' of AO
Post-Audit — Revenue's Internal Review
EVERY AO favourable to taxpayer will most likely be taken up in internal 'post-audit' — independent review of documents placed on record. A quality assurance review by peers✗ ASSUME THIS
Revenue cannot suffer adverse outcome and do nothing — especially with plurality of remedies available in law✓ REALITY
§ 17.4 — REMEDIES TO AGGRIEVED PERSONS
⬆ Index
Comparative Remedies Matrix — Taxpayer vs Revenue
Remedy Comparison Table — FAA Level
RemedyTaxpayerRevenue
Statutory AppealAllowedAllowed
Time Limit3 months6 months
Delay+ 1 month+ 1 month
Deposit10% of disputed tax/credit/refundNIL
Interim ReliefBalance stayedIssue SCN *
Final OutcomeConfirm, reduce or drop demand in notice
Remedy Comparison Table — Tribunal Level
RemedyTaxpayerRevenue
Statutory AppealAllowed (against PO/AA/RA orders)Allowed
Time Limit3 months6 months
DepositAdditional 10%NIL
Interim ReliefBalance stayed—
Delay+ 45 daysNo limit
Memo of Cross **45 days45 days
Final OutcomeConfirm, modify, annul Order or remand
Revision of Order — Revenue's Most Powerful Tool
ParameterRevenue
AllowedYES
Time Limit3 years
CoversOrders of PO or Appellate Authority
Interim Relief (for Revenue)IMMEDIATELY stay operation of Orders
Revenue's Most Dangerous Remedy — Revision + Stay
Revisionary proceedings give Revenue IMMEDIATE STAY of favourable AO. Result: subsequent notices will NOT be adjudicated based on precedence of that favourable AO. Refund applications also disposed adversely✗ DEVASTATING
* and ** Notes
* Revenue's interim relief at FAA level = issue SCN against erroneous refund sanctioned in AO
** Non-appealing party to file 'memorandum of cross objections' within 45 days after notice of other party's appeal before AT
§ 17.5 — RECTIFICATION OF APPARENT ERRORS (SECTION 161)
⬆ Index
Rectification under s.161 — Scope, Limits, Limitation Traps
What is Rectification
Errors that do NOT require reappreciation of material taken on record or otherwise redo the adjudication✓ DEFINITION
Applies not only to AO under Chapter XV but also to decisions from administrative or quasi-judicial functions in Chapter VI, XI or XII (except those excluded in s.121)✓ SCOPE
s.161 = limited purpose remedy against perpetuating mistakes contrary to intent of its author. NOT power to review
NOT a Fit Case for Rectification
Admitting declarations from Suppliers collected after date of AO (Circular 183/15/2022-GST)
Admitting eBRC collected from bank after date of AO
Reconsidering reply submitted manually stated as 'no reply filed online'
AO passed relying on record and ex parte — asserting non-service of email for PH
Rejecting ITC as goods not 'used in course or furtherance of business'
SCN demanding output tax on 'discount received' but AO confirming reversal of credit under rule 37
SCN demanding 5% on husk sale but AO confirming 18% as additional consideration for job-work
SCN on ITC as gifts but AO confirming as taxable outward supply
SCN in RFD-8 on credit mismatch but AO on intermediary services not zero-rated
FIT Cases for Rectification
Arithmetic error in computing turnover and/or demand✓ FIT
AO dropping demand in 'discussions & findings' but confirming in operative 'order'✓ FIT
AO confirming IGST when SCN demanded CGST-SGST✓ FIT
Limitation Traps — CRITICAL
Time lost pursuing s.161 is 'WITH PREJUDICE' to limitation in s.107(1)/112(1)✗ DANGER
Taxpayer NOT barred from simultaneously pursuing s.161 + s.107/112. Mention rectification in SoF for transparency✓ SAFE APPROACH
Appeal must be counted from date of REJECTION of rectification — NOT from date of original order. Counting from rejection order = INVALID plea✗ INVALID
Where rectification ALLOWED under s.161 → limitation to file appeal under s.107(1)/112(1) counted from date of DRC-8✓ FROM DRC-8
Making s.161 application ADMITS validity of authority to entertain. Relief sought is 'with prejudice' to (i) s.107(1)/112(1) remedy AND (ii) acquiescence as to validity of jurisdiction. Taxpayer PRECLUDED from later questioning jurisdiction under s.161✗ ACQUIESCENCE TRAP
If DRC8 is challenged and held illegal → DRC7 springs back to life → recovery proceeds. But taxpayer will have lost statutory appeal against DRC7. Must question DRC8 'with prejudice' to other remedies
§ 17.6 — TAXPAYER'S APPEAL
⬆ Index
Right to Appeal vs Maintainability of Appeal
Maintainability Covers
(a) Limitation of time by statute within which appeal must be preferred✓ FIRST TEST
(b) Order requires interference to set right illegality, error or impropriety✓ SECOND TEST
Grounds Matrix — Essential vs Insufficient
Essential Grounds to AppealInsufficient Grounds to Appeal
Illegal on facts or lawUnhappy with outcome
Erroneous on facts or lawUnable to pay the demand
Impropriety on procedure in lawYet another view is possible
Merely filing appeal will not bring relief. Such appeals dismissed on admission → lend weight of AA's consideration to basis of AO✗ BACKFIRE
§ 17.7 — RESTRICTIONS UNDER SECTION 121
⬆ Index
Non-Appealable Decisions/Orders — s.121 and Beyond
Under s.121 — Not Appealable
Appeal by overlooking s.121 will NOT be condoned✗ FATAL
Once such appeals admitted, they too must be dismissed by Speaking Order
Beyond s.121 — Also Not Appealable
(a) Refusal of provisional assessment (s.60)
(b) Refusal to withdraw provisional attachment (s.83)
(c) Refusal of provisional release of seized goods
When non-appealable outcome arises in adjudication → must be considered in approach in the reply itselfPLAN AHEAD
Rights Reminder
Rights are available only to those who avail them and take affirmative action — NOT those who while away and do precious little to secure them✓ PRINCIPLE
§ 17.8 — MAINTAINABILITY OF APPEAL
⬆ Index
Testing Appeal for Grounds of Maintainability
The Mandatory but Tested Next Step
Appeal = mandatory 'next step'. But MUST NOT be filed merely because it is statutorily permitted✓ DISTINCTION
AO (or Appellate Order) must be tested for 'grounds of maintainability' of appeal
All appeals filed in accordance with procedure = valid. But all appeals are liable to be dismissed unless grounds are valid and demonstrated✓ TEST
The Realistic Approach
Prudent taxpayers will chart out future course of each notice — a few steps ahead before even taking the first step. This is not pessimism. This is being realistic✓ WISDOM
§ 17.9 — PAYMENT UNDER PROTEST
⬆ Index
Payment under Protest in GST — No Express Provision, High Risk
Modes of Payment in GST
(a) GSTR-3B — 'admitted' liability✓
(b) DRC-3/DRC-7 — 'admitted' demand✓
(c) APL-1 — 'disputed' demand✓
Amount left as cash balance in ECL ≠ payment. Requires acknowledgement of dues + appropriation✗ NOT PAYMENT
No Provision for 'Payment under Protest' in GST
No provision in statute expressly permitting payment without admitting liability and forestalling interest✗ NO PROVISION
Common sense from earlier regime says it's permitted — but no judicial support specifically in GST context yet
DRC-3 payment = not disputed but undisputed demand discharged → PO could issue DRC-4 per rule 142(2)✗ TRAP
(a) No option to deposit disputed demand at pre-adjudication stage
(b) Incremental deposit of disputed demand NOT permitted during appellate stage via APL-1✗ TWO LIMITS
DRC-3 Deposit — Double Risk
Deposit via DRC-3 runs independent of dispute. If limitation under s.54 from date of deposit runs out without refund application → will NOT be refunded/adjusted against demand✗ LIMITATION RISK
When unjust enrichment declared not applicable to State → taxpayer bears this misadventure of payment outside APL-1
Where payment discharged against DRC-7 (s.129): file RFD-1 route + tag refund appeal with substantive question in appeal for joint disposal✓ SAFE ROUTE
§ 17.10 — PAYMENT OF DEMAND = ADMISSION OF LIABILITY (Rule 142 Amendments w.e.f. 10 Jul. 2024)
⬆ Index
Four Aspects of Liability via DRC-3 + 2024 Amendments to Bring Finality
Four Key Aspects of Liability via DRC-3
(a) Creation of liability on common portal
(b) Admission of liability
(c) Discharge of liability
(d) Appropriation by Proper Officer
Pre-2024 vs 2024 Flow — Comparative
P1 (Pre-2024): Liability ascertained → Payment via DRC-3 → Appropriation by PO via DRC-4
P2 (2024): Liability ascertained → Payment via DRC-3 → Auto-generated DRC-4✓ AUTO
Q1 (Pre-2024): DRC-1A Part A → Reply in DRC-1A Part B + Payment via DRC-3 → Appropriation by PO via DRC-4
Q2 (2024): DRC-1A Part A → Reply + DRC-3 → Appropriation intimated via DRC-1A Part C✓ INTIMATION
R1 (Pre-2024): Liability discharged via DRC-3 → Liability created by PO in Liability Ledger via PMT-1 → Appropriation by PO via DRC-4
R2 (2024): Liability admitted in Liability Ledger via DRC-3A → Discharge via DRC-3 → Auto-generated DRC-4✓ DRC-3A TRIGGER
DRC-3A — The Masterstroke Against DRC-3 + RFD-1
Rule 142(2A) & (2B) from 10 Jul. 2024: Taxpayer's affirmative action via DRC-3A makes it impossible to later resile from liability and file RFD-1✗ FINALITY
BUT: not even a willing Taxable Person can admit non-existent liability. Liability must be traceable to s.9(1) or s.5(1). 'No supply, no tax' — books/returns are not proof without corroboration (Mafatlal Industries)✓ COUNTER
Note: Where PO has issued DRC-5 under rule 142(3) and concluded proceedings, NO application in DRC-3A will be permitted
Dispute + Refund Tagging Strategy Flow
Discharge disputed demand
→
Demand agitated + RFD-1 filed
↓
Appeal against adverse order on demand
→
Appeal against order on refund
↓
Tag both appeals for joint hearing and disposal
→
Appeal on demand allowed → secures right to claim refund, saved from limitation due to appeal
§ 17.11 — DIRECT WRIT PETITION
⬆ Index
Writ Petition — When to Approach HC, Grounds, Right & Wrong Reasons
Two Conditions to Approach HC
(a) Notice deserves Court's intervention to stop march of injustice✓ CONDITION 1
(b) Remedy necessary cannot be allowed in adjudication or in appeal✓ CONDITION 2
Nine Grounds for WP Maintainability
(a) Omission to exercise vested authority
(b) Exercise of non-existent authority
(c) Exercise by unauthorized official (unvested)
(d) Exercise in excess of limits to vested authority
(e) Exercise in derogation of preconditions to vested authority
(f) Exercise of authority illegally vested (ultra vires)
(g) Exercise contrary to 'due process' prescribed
(h) Exercise in haste or with pre-judged mind
(i) Exercise in violation of principles of natural justice
Four WRONG Reasons to Approach HC
(a) To avoid 10% + 10% pre-deposit in statutory appeal
(b) To prevent Revenue from exposing transaction details and triggering fresh proceedings in other States
(c) To bypass time and effort of due process
(d) To secure a binding interpretation for ongoing transactions
Five RIGHT Reasons — When Adjudication/Appeal Not Efficacious
(a) Validity of law itself — PO/AA as 'creatures of statute' not empowered to sit in judgment over it
(b) Relief sought cannot be allowed by PO or AA
(c) Violation of natural justice — entertaining illegal proceedings is injustice irrespective of fair consideration
(d) Consequences irreversible and urgent intervention impossible within due process
(e) Constitutionality of provisions affecting society — cannot be left to instrumentality of Government
Key Concepts — Locus / Laches / Maintainability
Locus: Only aggrieved Legal Entity has locus to approach HC
Laches: Unexplainable delay = laches. Courts won't entertain inordinately delayed petitions
Maintainability: Injustice must 'jump out of the pages'. Must show 'demand for justice' was made before PO and turned down
Petition must also contain 'RELIEF' that no other forum is empowered to allowESSENTIAL
Critical Limitation Effects of WP
Time in WP proceedings excluded in computation of limitation for notice under s.75(1)✓ BENEFIT
Time in WP proceedings does NOT pause interest liability if demand confirmed eventually✗ INTEREST RUNS
Relief = 'Stay of notice': if allowed, will EXTEND limitation under s.75(2)
Relief = 'Quash the notice': if allowed, will END the notice. Fresh notice for same period may followCRITICAL DISTINCTION
Writ Petition — Process Flow
SCN issued
→
Evaluate: grievance + SCN fit for HC redressal?
↓
WP filed
→
Petition + enclosures proper; Office objections cleared
↓
Admission hearing (1st)
→
Preliminary arguments on maintainability of WP; Respondent plea for dismissal
↓
Interim Orders (2nd)
→
Arguments on irreparable prejudice to Petitioner due to injustice by Respondents
↓
Main Petition (3rd)
→
Arguments on grounds in WP + permanent relief. Disposal: 1 year or more
§ 17.13 — IMMEDIATE STEPS AFTER ADVERSE ORDER (DRC-3A / Rule 142(2B))
⬆ Index
Stage-wise Action Required under Rule 142(2B) — After Adverse Orders
Two Immediate Steps for Taxpayer
Step 1: File online 'application' in DRC-3A to identify the DRC-3 which relates to pre-deposit✓ STEP 1
Step 2: File manual undertaking before PO to file appeal once Appellate Tribunal is operational✓ STEP 2
Steps NOT required if PO has uploaded DRC-5EXCEPTION
Stage-wise Mode of Deposit + Rule 142(2B) Action Required
Stage of DisputeMode of DepositAction under Rule 142(2B)?
Adverse order by PODRC-13 *NO
Adverse EWB order in MOV-9DRC-3YES
Adverse refund order in RFD-6PMT-3NO
Appeal pending before FAADRC-3YES
Appeal not filed before FAADRC-13 *NO
Adverse order by FAADRC-3YES
Adverse order by FAAAPL-1NO
Adverse order by FAACourt directionsNO
Adverse order by RADRC-3YES
Adverse order by RACourt directionsNO
* file RFD-1 and collect RFD-2
⚔️ HEARING ARSENAL — RATIOS FOR PERSONAL HEARINGS | CHAPTER 17
⬆ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings, GSTAT arguments, and Writ Petition drafting. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Mafatlal Industries Ltd. v. Union of India
Supreme Court of India — cited in Para 17.10
"Not even a willing Taxable Person can admit non-existent liability. The entire jurisprudence is well explained by the Apex Court — liability must be traceable to section 9(1) or 5(1). No matter what is stated in books or returns, the adage 'no supply, no tax' cannot go wrong except where the Taxable Person does not claim non-existence of liability even if books or returns appear to indicate otherwise. Books of account are not proof of any transaction without corroboration and returns can be and are quite often not free of errors."
When to Use: Where Revenue argues that DRC-3 payment via Rule 142(2A)/(2B) amounts to admission of liability for non-existent supply. Cite to show that no affirmative action can create liability where supply itself is absent.
Principle on Burden of Proof — Revenue's Duty to Demonstrate Taxable Supplies
Appellate Principle from Para 17.1 — illustrative AA order
"The Adjudication Order fails to discharge the burden of proof on Revenue to demonstrate taxable supplies in 'other income' appearing in financial statements. This burden cannot be shifted to taxpayer. GST is not a tax on income appearing in financial statements without showing the existence of taxable supplies qua the said amount of 'other income'. No demand arises by presumption of supply in accounting disclosure in financial statements."
When to Use: In any matter where Revenue is raising demand based on accounting entries (other income, sundry receipts, miscellaneous income) without identifying the actual taxable supply. Use at personal hearing and in SOF of APL-1 to establish that burden lies on Revenue.
Doctrine on Revised Notice — No Provision in Law
Doctrinal Principle — Para 17.2
"There is no provision in law to 'revise' a notice that is already issued. Once a notice is issued, it must culminate in a Speaking Order. There is also no provision for 'corrigendum' or 'addendum' or 'errata' to be issued because defects, deficiencies and discrepancies in notice are incurable. Alteration of grounds in a notice is not typographical but a reapplication of mind to support demand by substitution of grounds with new ones — the Doctrine of Election showing a 'change of mind' has taken place."
When to Use: When Revenue issues a revised notice or corrigendum trying to rectify a defective SCN. Cite to insist on Speaking Order on original notice before any fresh notice can be issued, subject to fresh limitation.
Rectification Limitation Trap — DRC-8 and Acquiescence
Doctrinal Principle — Para 17.5
"Making an application under section 161 admits the validity of authority to entertain this application. The relief sought in this application will be 'with prejudice' to (i) remedy under section 107(1)/112(1) and (ii) acquiescence as to validity of jurisdiction to entertain such application. Taxpayers will be precluded from questioning the existence and validity of jurisdiction to entertain such application due to acquiescence."
When to Use: Where a client is considering filing a rectification application — cite to advise simultaneous filing of appeal as a protective measure. Also useful before GSTAT to show that DRC-8 proceedings, if held illegal, revive DRC-7 and that taxpayer acted with full prejudice awareness.
Writ Jurisdiction — Nine Grounds of Maintainability
Constitutional Law Principle — Para 17.11
"The grievance must show: omission to exercise vested authority; exercise of non-existent authority; exercise by unauthorized official; exercise in excess of limits; exercise in derogation of preconditions; exercise of authority illegally vested (ultra vires); exercise contrary to due process prescribed; exercise in haste or with a pre-judged mind; or exercise in violation of principles of natural justice."
When to Use: In any preliminary objection to an SCN before approaching HC — establish which of these nine grounds applies to make the Writ Petition maintainable. Also useful at FAA/GSTAT to show that jurisdictional questions can be carried to a Court of Equity even when not at large before them.
Stay vs Quash — Critical Limitation Difference in Writ
Doctrinal Principle — Para 17.11
"Relief prayed before the Writ Court for 'stay' of notice and consequent adjudication proceedings, which if allowed, will 'extend' the limitation as provided in section 75(2). But where relief prayed is to 'quash' the notice, which if allowed, will 'end' the notice and not save the effect of limitation in respect of fresh notice issued for the same disputed period after taking into account the grounds on which High Court quashed the notice issued earlier."
