When to Use: At the time of drafting a Writ Petition — choose relief prayer carefully. 'Stay' extends limitation protecting taxpayer from demand becoming final while 'quash' kills the notice but opens door to fresh notice at limitation advantage to Revenue.
Post-Audit Risk — Revenue's Inevitable Internal Review
Strategic Principle — Para 17.3
"Taxpayers must assume that every Adjudication Order where the outcome is favourable to taxpayer will most likely be taken up in an internal verification exercise called 'post-audit'. Purpose is to examine whether the Adjudication Order merits 'acceptance' by Revenue based on independent internal review. Revenue cannot suffer an adverse outcome in Adjudication Order and not do anything about it, especially where there is a plurality of remedies available in law."
When to Use: When advising a client after a favourable AO — immediately review the order for any weakness that can be attacked by Revenue in revision (with immediate stay power within 3 years). Use to justify making the reply and adjudication water-tight from the beginning.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 17 — Outcome in Adjudication | Para 17.1–17.13
⚖️ Chapter 18 — Remedy of Appeal

CHAPTER 18 — REMEDY OF APPEAL

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 TABLE OF CONTENTS — Click to Navigate

18.1 Relevance in Replying to Notices 18.2 Standard Operating Procedure 18.3 Purpose of Appeal to AA 18.4 Departmental Appeal 18.5 Taxpayer's Exposure in Counter 18.6 Condonation of Delay 1 18.7 Defective Appeal 1 18.8 Order 'At Large' in Appeal 18.9 Proceedings Not 'At Large' 18.10 Issue 'At Large' in Appeal 18.11 Burden of AA to Verify Evidence 18.12 Curative Powers of AA 18.13 Framing of Facts-in-Issue 18.14 Exclude Irrelevant Facts 18.15 Good Reason to Be Confident 18.16 Withdrawal of Appeal 1 ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
§ 18.1 — RELEVANCE IN REPLYING TO NOTICES
⬆ Index
Decision to Dispute & Litigate — When NOT Advisable vs When Justified
When NOT to Dispute & Litigate✗ AVOID
(a) Demand not of material amount
(b) Demand correct and payable
(c) Likelihood of favourable outcome is low
(d) Demand can be passed on and creditable to Customers
(e) RCM demand creditable by taxpayer (self)
(f) No precedent value in securing sound interpretation of law
When Wisdom Lies in Contesting
Educate about general probabilities at each stage before deciding defence approach✓ RULE
Approach to defence is closely connected with outcome in persevering with 'due process' in law✓ PRINCIPLE
Deposit 'Under Protest' — Mounting Interest Risk
Not expressly in law — arises from 'equitable remedy' outside the law. Approved by Courts for decades under earlier tax regime
GST is strict on procedure — promptly file refund application under 'other payments' after deposit 'under protest' to save from rigours of limitation✗ CAUTION
Until 'equitable remedy' receives express approval of Courts in GST context — file refund application promptlySAFE COURSE
§ 18.2 — STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP) FOR APPEAL
⬆ Index
Audit / investigation → Potential demand of tax, credit, interest & penalty quantified in writing
→
'Accept & Pay' demand + interest but dispute penalty (review future transactions)
→
'Evaluate & Contest' demand — prepare for adverse orders initially (relief in appeal)
↓
'Pay but contest' — deposit tax under protest (refundable, non-interest bearing)
→
Pre-notice DRC-1A issued → accept & discharge with interest and concessional penalty
→
Option to pay rejected → SCN issued "Time: T"
↓
Adjudication after detailed reply/objections filed. Max. time for orders: 36 mts (normal) / 60 mts (special) Time: T+~1yr | Odds: <10%
→
Appeal against adverse order to FAA — within 3 months (pre-deposit 10% of disputed tax)
→
FAA passes orders within 1 year (directory, not mandatory) after PH Time: T+~2yr | Odds: <20%
↓
Appeal against FAA adverse order to Tribunal — within 3 months (additional 10% pre-deposit)
→
Tribunal: public hearing (physical/virtual). Taxpayer's interpretation of law & facts appreciated → Authoritative decision Time: T+~5yr | Odds: >70%
SOP — Five Key Steps for Taxpayer
Step 1
File appeal within 3 months from 'date of service' of Adjudication Order
Step 2
Submit certified copy of AO to collect APL-2 (rule 108(3))
✗ Absence of APL-2 = Appeal non est in law
Step 3
APL-1 must be accompanied by pre-deposit
Capped at ₹20 Cr. each CGST & SGST
Step 4
Belated appeal permitted within 1 month (COD) — each day of delay explained with Affidavit. No questions re: normal time
Step 5
Personal hearing to be granted before disposal. Speaking Order + APL-4 to be issued
§ 18.3 — PURPOSE OF APPEAL TO APPELLATE AUTHORITY
⬆ Index
Right to Appeal vs Purpose of Appeal — Critical Distinction
Do NOT File Appeal Merely Because
(a) Unhappy with outcome of adjudication
(b) Unable to afford payment of demand confirmed
(c) Another conclusion is possible by applying law to given facts
(d) Capable of filing appeal (to defer payment)
File Appeal Only to Show AO is
(a) Bad in law for misapplying provisions
(b) Erroneous in comprehending facts
(c) Arriving at conclusion impossible in law or on facts
(d) Contrary to judicial authorities and administrative instructions
The Threshold for AA Intervention
AA must conclude that interference is JUSTIFIED, NECESSARY and WARRANTED — and WITHOUT such interference, injustice will be done✓ TEST
Just because another view can be taken by AA — does NOT mean it must be taken. That is misplaced understanding of appellate remedy
Appeal is 'admitted' in law but does NOT compel AA to 'entertain' itDISTINCTION
Default rule of AA = 'to dismiss' all appeals — entertaining an appeal itself raises a presumption in favour of taxpayer in revisionary/further appeal that AO merited interference✗ DEFAULT
Penalty Cases — Specific Warning
Routine invoking of AA interference in penalty cases to demand 'another look' = misapplication of s.107. Not condoned. Revenue is prejudiced by non-dismissal of appeals without 'good and sufficient' reasons✗ PENALTY
§ 18.4 — DEPARTMENTAL APPEAL
⬆ Index
Departmental Appeal — Who Files, When, Three Lines of Defence
Who Can File Appeal (s.107 — APL-3)
(a) Registered person
(b) Unregistered person
(c) Proper Officer
Department's Three Lines of Defence
(a) Revisional proceedings (s.108)
(b) Issue notice under s.73 or s.74
(c) File departmental appeal under s.107(3)
Decision Authority & Timeline
Decision on which route: Commissioner (per Commissionerate) or Joint Commissioner (per Division) under s.107(2)
Departmental appeal must be filed by PO within 6 months from date of Adjudication Order✗ 6 MONTHS
§ 18.5 — TAXPAYER'S EXPOSURE IN COUNTER (TO DEPARTMENTAL APPEAL)
⬆ Index
Counter to Departmental Appeal — Minimal & Precise Strategy
What a Good Counter Must Be
(a) Point-wise — affirming or disaffirming the grounds✓ FORMAT
(b) State clearly 'extent' of such disaffirmation✓ FORMAT
Elaborate Counter — Six Risks Created
(a) Failure to object to 'maintainability' of appeal
(b) Failure to object to 'appellate interference' in this appeal
(c) Failure to object to 're-adjudication' attempted
(d) Omitting to dispute grounds-of-appeal in dept's appeal
(e) Admission of new grounds raised for first time in this appeal
(f) Introduction of new or additional material in rebuttal
Expansion of Lis — The Trap
Taxpayer ends up expanding scope of lis — so innocuously that focus shifts to 'saving demand' and this failure evades attention in later appeals too✗ TRAP
Best approach: OBJECT TO DEPARTMENTAL APPEAL IN ITS ENTIRETY rather than file extensive 'counter'. Less damage ensues✓ BEST TACTIC
Counter ≠ Memorandum of cross-objections under s.112(5). These are very different
§ 18.6 — CONDONATION OF DELAY 1
⬆ Index
Belated Appeal — Condonation, Limits & Ramlal Principle
Statutory Framework
Belated appeal permitted up to maximum 1 month under s.107(4) after due date under s.107(1)/(2/3)✓ MAX 1 MONTH
Limitations Act, 1963 — s.5 and s.14: "sufficient cause" must be shown to justify delay
Ramlal & Ors. v. Rewa Coalfields Ltd. — SC Principles
(a) Non-filing within normal time NOT questionable
(b) Every day of delay to be explained with Affidavit
(c) Reasons cited — verified; rejected if not satisfactory
(d) Condonation allowed only by Speaking Order
When limitation lapses — counterparty gets a VESTED RIGHT which cannot be denied by routine/mechanical condonation without 'good and sufficient' reasons
Beyond Maximum Condonation Period — Fatal
AA has NO statutory authority to condone beyond s.107(4) period — not even if reasons are ample. Appeal must be dismissed as fatally belated✗ NO POWER
Singh Enterprises v. CCE 2008 (221) ELT 163 (SC): where limitation specifically provided in statute, admitting appeals albeit for 'sufficient cause' would render statutory provision otiose. AA denuded of authority — barred from even examining the cause, much less its sufficiency
§ 18.7 — DEFECTIVE APPEAL 1
⬆ Index
Incurably Defective Appeal — Four Fatal Defects
Four Incurable Defects
(a) APL-1 not verified by Appellant
(b) Certified copy of AO not appended
(c) Pre-deposit of disputed tax not made
(d) APL-2 not issued within time (rule 108(3))
APL-2 — The Critical Date Determinant
Date of actual filing of APL-1 = date of APL-2
If APL-2 issued but Appellant has NOT filed COD application — AA cannot hear appeal without first regularizing the delay✗ SEQUENCE
Delay beyond s.107(4) due to misunderstanding of rule 108(3) → AA cannot entertain — not even in interests of justice✗ FATAL
Taxpayer's interest: confirm date of APL-2 in departmental appeal to see if delay in securing APL-2 has been fatal to DEMAND✓ TIP
Rule 108(3) Amendment — Clarity Provided
(a) Certified copy NOT required where AO uploaded online✓ ONLINE
(b) Certified copy may be 'self-certified' where AO NOT uploaded online✓ OFFLINE
§ 18.8 — ORDER 'AT LARGE' IN APPEAL
⬆ Index
Identifying the Correct 'Order Impugned' — No Mistake Permitted
Core Principle
Appeal = redressal of 'any' decision/order — NOT 'all' decisions/orders on a subject matter
Only 'a' decision or order that taxpayer is aggrieved by is 'at large' before AA/AT — examined on basis of grounds pleaded and not anything else or more✓ CORE
Accuracy in locating 'order to be impugned' must not be incorrect✓ CRITICAL
What AA/AT CANNOT Do
NOT permitted to 're-do' original proceedings even where appellate intervention is warranted✗ NOT RE-DO
Original proceedings concluded cannot be reviewed — AA/AT NOT vested with powers of adjudicating authority✗ CANNOT REVIEW
Which Order is 'At Large' — Examples
RFD-6 sanctioned by PO → RA stayed it via RVN1 → RA issued APL-4 dated 31 Mar. 2023. Order 'at large' = RA's order/APL-4 (NOT RFD-6). If Court quashes RA proceedings for lack of jurisdiction → RFD-6 springs back and must be given effect under s.75(3) by issuing RFD-5
MOV11 issued based on MOV6. Court quashes MOV11 due to omission to conclude proceedings under s.129 vide MOVs7-9. MOV7 cannot be issued if goods released on deposit under protest. Deposit also ordered to be refunded from date of Court order
If appeal filed against order already substituted by later order of superior authority → later order is 'at large', not former. Choice of order to impugn is 'with prejudice' — perils lie in over-enthusiastic drafting error
§ 18.9 — PROCEEDINGS NOT 'AT LARGE' IN APPEAL
⬆ Index
What Cannot Be Agitated in Appeal — Jurisdiction Boundary Rules
Valid Underlying Proceedings — Prerequisite
Every notice of demand must trace to an authorized action: scrutiny (s.61), BJ assessment (s.62/63/64), audit (s.65), inquiry (s.67), interception (s.68), or s.125/127✓ MUST TRACE
Notice with undisclosed underlying proceedings = bad in law. PO must have exercised authority lawfully✗ INVALID
Proceedings NOT at Large — Examples
Central GST Officer cannot audit taxable person mapped to State administration (and vice versa). CGST officer issuing ADT1/2 or SCN under s.73 for data mismatch = Officer improper, action without jurisdiction✗ JURISDICTION
ADG-DGGI issuing SCN under s.73 disaffirms 'evasion of tax'. Action improper although officer proper → without jurisdiction✗ JURISDICTION
Post-Adjudication — What AA/AT Can Enter Into
Once notice adjudicated — AA/AT CANNOT enter into validity of original proceedings
But CAN enter into validity of jurisdiction exercised — if officer acted without authority or proceedings were beyond extant authority✓ CAN
Jurisdictional aspects are 'at large' before a Court of Equity (Writ) even when not at large before AA/AT✓ WRIT ROUTE
Jurisdiction + Merits Both in Reply — Important Result
If reply contains objections on (i) jurisdiction AND (ii) merits, and AO finds (a) erroneously on jurisdiction and (b) confirms on merits → AA/AT must examine and reach findings on BOTH sets of issues SEPARATELY✓ BOTH ISSUES
With jurisdiction answered AND merits in taxpayer's favour → appeal may be allowed on merits without overturning jurisdiction finding. If only jurisdiction raised → compels finding on jurisdiction → itself appealable → s.117/118 SQoL questionSTRATEGIC NOTE
What FAA CANNOT Entertain (Against DRC-7)
Why ASMT-10 not issued before SCN? (ASMT-10 not a precondition for s.73/74)
How did JC authorize inspection for FY 202X when audit concluded? (s.65 audit doesn't estop s.67 inspection)
Why SCN even though ASMT-12 issued on same issue? (ASMT-12 doesn't estop SCN under s.73/74 if s.61 findings erroneous)
How was SCN issued on data mismatch without audit? (where evidence supports findings, SCN can be issued without formality of audit)
§ 18.10 — ISSUE 'AT LARGE' IN APPEAL
⬆ Index
Issue-wise Scope of Appeal — Boundaries of Lis
Issue-wise Appeal Principle
Only issues within the impugned order that taxpayer is aggrieved with are 'at large' under s.107(1)/112(1)
Issues taxpayer is NOT aggrieved with are 'at large' for Revenue to carry in appeal under s.107(3)/112(4)
Issue-wise rectification also permitted under s.108(1). Limitation for rectification is also issue-wise (unlike statutory appeal)✓ NOTE
Once rectification is filed → 'with prejudice' to (i) limitation on right to appeal and (ii) validity of jurisdiction to entertain it✗ PREJUDICE
Expansion of Scope — Not Permitted
Issues not agitated — AA, RA, and AT are BARRED from entering into them. Not 'at large' before them✗ BARRED
Tendency to call for books/records in name of 'fact-finding' = improper expansion of scope in appellate/revisionary proceedings✗ IMPROPER
Acquiescence under s.160(2) only touches notice, order or communication — NOT before AA, RA or AT
Strategic Rule on Grounds
Questions on facts: ONLY up to AT (last fact-finding authority). Questions of law: before AA, AT and Courts✓ BOUNDARY
NOT every objection that can be raised MUST be raised. Only (i) most suited to secure desired outcome and (ii) grounds on which relief prayed may be entertained✓ SELECT
'Throw everything possible in appeal and let's see which one sticks' = outdated and irresponsible litigation strategy✗ OUTDATED
§ 18.11 — BURDEN OF APPELLATE AUTHORITY TO VERIFY EVIDENCE
⬆ Index
Evidence in Appeal — AA's Duty, Probative Test, Scope Limitation
AA's Obligation on Evidence
Evidence introduced in appeal (by both sides) NOT admitted without AA being satisfied it has persuasive value✓ THRESHOLD
Once evidence tendered is entertained by AA → becomes AA's own evidence in later proceedings (revision/second appeal). Appellant-taxpayer no longer answerable for reliance placed✓ KEY EFFECT
AA must examine probative value of evidence and record reasons for placing reliance✓ MANDATORY
What AA Examines
It is the ADJUDICATION ORDER (not the notice) that is 'at large' before AA
AA examines AO closely — identify grounds on which AO is passed — compare with grounds urged by Appellant in APL-1
Very common for Appellant to abandon grounds in APL-1 or APL-3. AA limits scope of inquiry to grounds urged in disposing appealNOTE
Section 107(11) — 'Such Further Inquiry'
Permits AA to call for information/documents in support of grounds urged in APL-1 or APL-3✓ POWER
Calling for information must be limited to establishing reliability of evidence in support of grounds urged — NOT expanded scope✗ LIMIT
§ 18.12 — CURATIVE POWERS OF APPELLATE AUTHORITY
⬆ Index
AA's Curative Powers — Scope, Limits, and What AA CANNOT Do
Questions of Fact — Test for Reliability
Facts ≠ opinion. Existence admitted beyond reasonable doubt — not merely on probability
Evidence tested for reliability on: (i) source — first/third party, (ii) contemporaneous record or specially prepared, (iii) no contradictory alternates available, (iv) procured from ordinary course of trade✓ FOUR TESTS
Once facts entertained in appeal → become part of AA's own findings in Order — referred in later proceedings✓ EFFECT
Limits on AA's Power When Interference is Justified
AA's powers limited by: (i) grounds in Adjudicating Order AND (ii) grounds in pleadings of Appellant-taxpayer✓ LIMITED BY
AA is BARRED from 'switching' statutory provisions to more accurately support the demand✗ BARRED
Example: Penalty confirmed under s.129 CANNOT be reduced by substituting with s.125 penalty. If not justified, may be reduced but under s.129(1)(a) itself. Such reduction endorsed in Circular 64
What AA CANNOT Do
NOT undertake to 're-do' adjudication already concluded✗
NOT 'fill in the blanks' in the AO✗
NOT attempt to 'do justice' by travelling beyond the points about 'grounds'✗
Example: Penalty under s.129 CANNOT be reduced by reaching conclusion 'no intention to evade' when EWB was absent and non-compliance undisputed by taxpayer. In absence of material omitted by PO, relief in appeal = arbitrary and illegal (liable to be overturned in revision)
§ 18.13 — FRAMING OF FACTS-IN-ISSUE
⬆ Index
Identifying Facts-in-Issue in Appeal — Grounds Tracking Matrix
What Must Be Identified
(a) Grounds in notice
(b) Grounds relied upon in Adjudication Order
(c) Grounds urged in APL-1
(d) Grounds added / modified / withdrawn
Grounds Tracking Matrix
Grounds in SCN
(issue-wise)		 AO
(Retained/Abandoned)		 Urged in APL-1 Added/Modified/Withdrawn
…………      
Present summary of grounds in appeal and confirm during Personal Hearing so that (i) grounds in APL-1 not beyond AO terms and (ii) grounds considered not beyond those admissible in AO✓ PURPOSE
Why This Matters
AA will be in position to decide facts bearing on issues in appeal — prevents distractions✓ BENEFIT
Common to find orders addressing facts NOT in issue. Once FII identified → eliminate issues not relevant to grounds in APL-1 or APL-3
§ 18.14 — EXCLUDE IRRELEVANT FACTS
⬆ Index
Irrelevant vs Relevant Facts in Appeal
Irrelevant Facts — AA Will Discard
Common: Appellant presents 'all' facts in SOF of APL-1 — AA will eliminate 'irrelevant facts'✗ OVER-PRESENTATION
Submissions 'not germane' to appellate proceedings will be listed and discarded in the Order — cannot be urged as 'error of omission' by AA later
Example: Appeal on refund claim → admissibility of credit under s.17(5) blocked credit = NOT fact-in-issue. At time of sanction of refund, PO not permitted to audit ECrL (unless raised in RFD-8, per para 20 of Circular 125/44/2019-GST dated 18 Nov. 2019)
Relevant Facts — What Qualifies
Facts connected with the issue under consideration AND contributing in a meaningful way to course of adjudication and eventual outcome✓ DEFINITION
Example (same appeal): Suspension of registration and subsequent restoration by same PO = RELEVANT FACTS to permit claim of credits. Revenue can take advantage of denial under s.16(4) when cancellation decision (causing blockage of login to claim credit) is overturned by AA by restoring registration
§ 18.15 — GOOD REASON TO BE CONFIDENT
⬆ Index
Why Appellate Remedy Inspires Confidence — The Refinement Process
The Refinement Principle
Issues progress from 'observation' in scrutiny/audit/investigation → 'demand' in notice. Refinement occurs at each stage✓ REFINEMENT
Adjudication = place where facts crystallize, allegations become static and taxpayer's position disclosed✓ ADJUDICATION
Short point in notice becomes SHORTER STILL in appeal. Revenue's interpretation has received one rendering in AO. Anxiety and stress have passed. Only finite set of data-points remain✓ APPEAL
Practical Takeaway
Relief that is to come in appeal is worth knowing — review preparation in reply accordingly✓ CONFIDENCE
§ 18.16 — WITHDRAWAL OF APPEAL 1
⬆ Index
Withdrawal of Appeal — Consequences, Time Window, Pre-Deposit Recovery
Basic Framework
Only appeal before AA (NOT AT) can be withdrawn. Via prescribed form bearing suffix 'W'. Both taxpayer appeal and Revenue application can be withdrawn✓ ONLY AA
Withdrawal is ALWAYS 'with prejudice' — adverse outcome must be suffered; liability confirmed in AO must be discharged FORTHWITH✗ WITH PREJUDICE
Recovery action under s.79 initiates WITHOUT additional time allowance. Entire disputed demand (+ incremental interest since AO date until withdrawal) goes into recovery✗ RECOVERY
Rule 109C — Time Window for Withdrawal
Time window: (i) AFTER filing appeal BUT BEFORE earlier of (ii-a) issuance of sequel-notice by AA and (ii-b) disposal of appeal under s.107(11)
Practically: window will not remain open beyond date when PH is scheduledPRACTICAL
Withdrawal 'subject to approval' of AA within 7 days. 'Re-appeal' is permitted subject to original limitation✓ RE-APPEAL
Limitation for second-time appeal: excludes period from date of AO until date of first-time filing
Pre-Deposit Recovery — No Auto-Refund
APL-1/APL-3-W has NO automatic mechanism to re-credit or refund pre-deposit✗ NO AUTO
Taxpayer MUST file refund application for pre-deposit under category 'pursuant to order of appeal'✓ ACTION
Pre-deposit for RCM demand paid from ECrL: must be re-credited to ECrL (demand in cash from ECL, not credit). Same for s.9(5) or tariff conditions not allowing creditSPECIAL
Adjustment of pre-deposit with undischarged arrears NOT permitted within current system functionalities✗ NOT POSSIBLE
⚔️ HEARING ARSENAL — RATIOS FOR PERSONAL HEARINGS | CHAPTER 18
⬆ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before FAA, GSTAT and Courts. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Ramlal & Ors. v. Rewa Coalfields Ltd.
Supreme Court of India — cited in Para 18.6 and Para 18.7
"When the time to file an appeal lapses, the counterparty gets a vested right — an advantage or benefit from such failure — which cannot be denied by condonation of delay in a routine and mechanical manner without 'good and sufficient' reasons. A vested right accrues to the Respondent when an appeal filed is fatally delayed beyond the time permitted, if any, in the statute for condonation."
When to Use: Where departmental appeal is filed belatedly — cite to establish that a vested right has accrued to taxpayer-Respondent and condonation must be denied as a matter of right.
Singh Enterprises v. Commissioner of Central Excise
Supreme Court — 2008 (221) ELT 163 | Para 18.6
"Where the period of limitation is specifically provided in the statute, admitting appeals albeit for 'sufficient cause' would render the statutory provision otiose. The Appellate Authority being denuded of authority to condone (due to lapse of maximum time permitted) is barred from examining the cause, much less its sufficiency."
When to Use: Where appeal (taxpayer's or department's) is filed beyond the maximum condonable period under s.107(4). Cite to shut the door completely — AA has NO jurisdiction to entertain, period.
Standard Dismissal Language — Appellate Authority (Para 18.3)
Illustrative AA Order Drafting — Para 18.3 of this Chapter
"Appellant has not shown that the Adjudication Order is erroneous on law and seeks intervention by Appellate Authority canvassing an alternate interpretation. While another interpretation is possible, without showing the error in the interpretation adopted by the Adjudicating Authority, intervention by Appellate Authority is not justified. This appeal is a misplaced attempt demanding Appellate Authority enter into the shoes of Proper Officer who has already examined all material evidence and considered all submissions by Appellant as stated in the Adjudication Order."
When to Use: At GSTAT or in written submissions — cite this as the standard the AA order fell short of. If AA dismissed without applying this test, show how AA overstepped or under-applied the threshold for interference.
Principle on 'Order At Large' — Chapter 18.8
Doctrinal Principle — Appellate Jurisprudence | Para 18.8
"It is only 'a' decision or order that taxpayer is aggrieved by that is 'at large' before the Appellate Authority or Appellate Tribunal to examine on the basis of 'grounds' pleaded and not anything else or anything more. The appellate authority(ies) not being vested with powers of adjudicating authority(ies), original proceedings concluded cannot be reviewed."
When to Use: To resist AA/AT from travelling beyond the impugned order or expanding the scope of inquiry. Cite to confine proceedings to the four corners of the grounds pleaded in APL-1.
Principle on AA's Curative Powers — Chapter 18.12
Doctrinal Principle — Appellate Jurisprudence | Para 18.12
"When the Adjudication Order is found to justify interference in appellate proceedings, the Appellate Authority's powers will be limited by (i) grounds in the Adjudicating Order and (ii) grounds in the pleadings of Appellant-taxpayer. The Appellate Authority is barred from 'switching' statutory provisions in order to more accurately support the demand."
When to Use: Before GSTAT — where FAA either switched statutory provisions to support demand differently than PO, or travelled beyond grounds in AO and APL. Use to attack the FAA order as made without jurisdiction.
Principle on Judicial Discipline — Chapter 18.9
Doctrinal Principle on Jurisdiction | Para 18.9
"Proceedings underlying any notice of demand must be traceable to any authorized action of Proper Officer. Notice of demand with undisclosed underlying proceedings is bad in law because the Proper Officer issuing notice of demand must have exercised authority lawfully."
When to Use: Where SCN is issued without any traceable authorized proceeding (no audit, no inquiry, no inspection, no scrutiny) — challenge the very root of the notice in any proceedings.
Principle on 'Facts-in-Issue' in Appeal — Chapter 18.13
Doctrinal Principle — Para 18.13 and 18.14
"Appellate Authority or Revisionary Authority or Appellate Tribunal are barred from entering into issues not 'at large' before them. It is important for Appellants to feel compelled to permit expansion of scope of inquiry. Not even a willing Appellant can acquiesce to the exercise of non-existent or transferred jurisdiction."
When to Use: Where AA or RA is going beyond the grounds in APL-1 or raising new issues. Cite to stop the expansion and confine proceedings to pleaded grounds only.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 18 — Remedy of Appeal | Para 18.1–18.16
⚖️ Chapter 19 — Sequel Notice (AA)
CHAPTER 19

'Sequel Notice' by Appellate Authority

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index — Click to Navigate

19.1 Introduction — Section 107(11) 19.2 Fact-Finding via Further Inquiry 19.3 Update Original Demand 19.4 DRC-1A Bypassed 19.5 Acquiescence Not Curative 19.6 First Proviso 19.7 Second Proviso 19.8 Calling of Books Barred 19.9 Update Notice v. Upgrade Notice 19.10 Altogether New Notice Barred 19.11 Sequel Not Free from Defects 19.12 Safety of Limitation 19.13 Safety of Original Grounds 19.14 Readiness for Tribunal ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

19.1 Introduction — The Remarkable Authority in Section 107(11)

⬆ Index
Section 107(11) — AA's Power to Issue 'Sequel Notice' of Demand
What is This Authority
REMARKABLE GST law authorises Appellate Authority to issue 'notice of demand' outside of Chapter XV — within Chapter XVIII. Placed here as bulwark against AA attempting to 're-do' audit or investigation. Not an invention of GST — analogous to almost-never-used s.128A(3) of Customs Act.
Source of Authority
SECOND PROVISO Does not expressly state 'notice to show cause' must be issued by AA — but mandating that no order confirming additional liability may be passed "unless the appellant is given a notice" → AA is enjoined with solemn duty. Notice of demand flows from s.107(11) itself. Limitation under s.73 or s.74 applied to this notice to safeguard taxpayer's interest.
RULE 109C Introduced from 26 Dec 2022 — permits withdrawal of appeal "at any time before issuance of show cause notice under sub-section (11) of section 107" → confirms Government's understanding of legislative intent in s.107(11).
Reformatio in Peius Problem
PRINCIPLE VIOLATED? Latin maxim Reformatio in peius = change for the worse. Person who appealed adverse order cannot be left with greater grief than suffered before. This principle is part of fair procedure — inherent in natural justice — and a principle of equity. Jaswal Neco Ltd. v. CC 2015 (322) ELT 561 elucidates this principle.

s.107(11) appears to be deliberate departure from this principle. To be workable, s.107(11) only permits 'updating the demand' — NOT 're-adjudication'.
AA's Excluded from s.2(4)
EXCLUDED Appellate Authority while NOT authorized to discharge duties of Adjudicating Authority (due to exclusion in s.2(4)) — express legislative empowerment cannot be dismissed lightly. Deliberate expression of legislative will in these provisos must be given meaning and full play until read down by Courts.

19.2 Fact-Finding via Further Inquiry — Limits

⬆ Index
AA is Fact-Finding Authority — But NOT Adjudicating Authority of Higher Rank
What is 'At Large' Before AA
CRITICAL RULE Before Appellate Authority, it is only the Adjudication Order that is 'at large' — NOT the show cause notice or the books of account that were audited or inspected. Appellate Tribunal, in turn, only examines the Order-in-Appeal — not the Adjudication Order or SCN.
What AA Cannot Do
BARRED If AA were to discharge functions of PO under s.61, s.65 or s.67 or under s.73, s.74 or s.76 → appeal will transform into 'second round' of audit, inspection or adjudication — neither of which is permissible. Confirming altogether new demand on new grounds taxpayer was never put at notice about = legislative wisdom in s.2(4) expressly excluded AA from definition of 'adjudicating authority'.
PRINCIPLE Power to issue a notice is to canvass a certain interpretation based on certain facts taken on record. Allegations in notice cannot be altered in appeal — when adjudication itself is expressly circumscribed by s.75(7), nothing in s.107(11) or s.113(1) can accomplish more. New demand = due process in Chapter XV + limitation must apply.
Example: SCN demanding output tax at 28% on diamonds adjudicated ex parte. Not appealed within limitation under s.107(4). Before AT, taxpayer cannot agitate demand on silver being illegal — AT must confine inquiry to whether AA's order dismissing appeal as barred by limitation is bad in law — only the 'order-in-appeal' is at large.

19.3 Update Original Demand — Scope of Sequel-Notice

⬆ Index
Sequel-Notice = Sub-Set of Original SCN — Refinement, NOT New Notice
The Golden Rule
MUST BE Sequel-notice under s.107(11) must be a sub-set of the notice issued under s.73, s.74 or s.76 and a refinement of the demand. It can overcome any incomplete portions of the demand — but only those founded on the grounds contained in the original notice. Contours of refinement cannot alter the actionable cause.
Permitted — Update Example
PERMITTED SCN demanding output tax at 28% on lease of motor car — facts not disputed, demand disputed on correctness of classification subject to correctness of engine capacity adopted in SCN. AA permitted to issue sequel-SCN demanding cess of 12% applicable to motor car based on 'opinion' that interpretation in order of adjudication is correct as per Rules of Interpretation.
Barred — Upgrade Example
NOT PERMITTED SCN demanding output tax at 18% on 'other income' being discounts received from suppliers. Reply disputing output tax (asserting no supply attracting s.9(1) and that rule 37 should have been invoked for credit reversal). AA NOT permitted to issue sequel-SCN altering demand of output tax into reversal of ITC discovered in reply to original SCN.

Reason: This changes the actionable cause — from output tax demand to credit reversal.

19.4 DRC-1A Bypassed — What This Means for Taxpayer

⬆ Index
Sequel-Notice Issued Under s.107(11) — Not Under s.73/74 — DRC-1A Does Not Apply
DRC-1A Not Attracted
BYPASSED
Supplement to original SCN permitted under s.107(11) bypasses Rule 142(1A) — even though 'demand' is for tax, credit or refund.

Not issued under s.73 or s.74 — issued under s.107(11).

Does NOT attract the operation of Rule 142(1A).

Great caution must be exercised in exercising this exceptional authority — Authority in s.107(11) is very limited and cannot be legislative duplication of original proceedings itself.
Prejudice — Elesh Agarwal HC
ALLAHABAD HC 2023
Elesh Agarwal v. UoI & Ors. (2023) 151 taxmann.com 538

When liability is disputed by taxpayer, omission of pre-notice consultations — although contrary to due process prescribed — does not cause any prejudice to taxpayer.

Innovative manner of setting aside taxpayer's claim that irreparable prejudice is caused when due process of DRC-1A consultations is bypassed by Proper Officer.

The reasoning: when original demand is disputed, the formality of pre-notice consultation adds no practical benefit — making it a 'useless formality'.
Original SCN vs Sequel-Notice — What Applies
Description Original SCN
u/s 73 or 74		 Sequel-Notice
u/s 107(11)
Pre-notice consultations (DRC-1A) ✓ Applicable ✗ NOT applicable
Concessional penalty — pre-notice stage ✓ Applicable ✗ NOT applicable
Concessional penalty — post-notice stage ✓ Applicable ✗ NOT applicable
Post-adjudication concessional penalty (within 30 days of Order) ✓ Applicable ✗ NOT applicable
Right to appeal against Adjudication Order ✓ Available ⚠ Via AT only
Limitation protection under s.73(10)/74(10) ✓ Full protection ✓ Tied to original
Sequel ≠ Corrigendum or Addendum
IMPORTANT DISTINCTION
Sequel-notice is NOT a corrigendum or addendum to the original notice — because it is not issued by the Proper Officer who issued the original notice.

There is no curative power with Appellate Authority to redress fatal defects in original notice.

'Useless formality theory' may operate — since the original demand (and hence the additional demand now made) being disputed, no real prejudice would be caused when, even by design, no procedure for pre-notice consultations is necessary.

19.5 Acquiescence Not Curative

⬆ Index
Taxpayer's Own Admissions Feed the Sequel-Notice — Extreme Caution in Replies
Acquiescence — Three Forms
DANGER Acquiescence = implied acceptance of allegations by:
(i) Leaving it undisputed
(ii) Omitting to question correctness
(iii) Admitting probative value of evidence adduced

Facts admitted in adjudication proceedings, even before matter reaches AA, affords material that may be relied upon to issue sequel-notice.
What Feeds the Sequel-Notice
FEEDS SEQUEL Overenthusiastic replies — even 'without prejudice' replies — make material available for sequel-notice. Alternative interpretations presented as 'more accurate' must be left as a possibility to create doubt — NOT as the only possibility by introducing evidence.
Example 1: SCN denying credit u/s 17(5)(g) for corporate gift articles. Reply asserting 'transfer of property' in articles to support credit claim → exposes liability to output tax by admitting ingredients for output tax.
Example 2: SCN for output tax on discounts from suppliers. Reply asserting failure to pay full value of Supplier invoice → exposes liability to reversal of ITC. This is precisely what AA cannot issue a sequel-notice for (different actionable cause) — but taxpayer's admission in reply creates the mischief.
Sequel-Notice Not Curative
PROTECTION Sequel-notice is NOT a cure for all ills in original notice. Curative power cannot be tucked away in a proviso potent enough to supplant the entire Chapter XV. Legislative intent to do so must be more expressly manifest. Placement of authority in the proviso is deliberate — must be read as a sub-set of authority in the sub-section to further its objects of "confirming, modifying or annulling the decision or order appealed against".

19.6 First Proviso — Enhancement of Existing Impositions Only

⬆ Index
First Proviso — Only 'Existing' Impositions Can Be Enhanced
Core Rule
KEY 'Enhancement' presupposes the demand for fee/penalty/redemption fine or 'reduction' of refund/credit was already the subject-matter of dispute in the appeal. 'Existing' impositions alone can be 'enhanced'. If an altogether new imposition is to be made → Appellate Authority is NOT the Proper Officer.
(a) Fee — Drafting Disconnect
ANOMALY Late fee u/s 47 does not have a machinery provision to demand it in Chapter XV. Drafting disconnect — AA permitted to enhance demand for fee when PO is left without authority to demand late fee in the first place.
(b) Penalty — Cannot Enhance to Different Provision
BARRED Statute prescribes penalty percentages — no occasion for AA to enhance when PO is already without authority to use discretion. Penalty under an altogether different provision is not permissible — it would not satisfy the pre-condition of 'existing demand' for penalty in order to fit within authority to 'enhance'.
(c) Redemption Fine — PO's Discretion Stands
BARRED Discretion permitted to PO under s.130(2) and 'upper limit' in its proviso — cannot be undone by AA merely because discretion was exercised. Appeal is not to redo adjudication but to prevent miscarriage of justice. Authority to adjudicate redemption fine is vested with PO in Chapter II.
(d) Refund & (e) Credit — Limits
LIMITS Refund: Order sanctioning refund → departmental appeal u/s 107(3) → AA cannot reduce beyond extent prayed. AA cannot trespass into Revisionary Authority u/s 108.

Credit: Order allowing credit (dropping demand) → departmental appeal → AA reducing further = new notice, impossible. Order rejecting credit (confirming demand) → taxpayer's appeal → AA reducing further = impossible without a new notice which is barred.

19.7 Second Proviso — Additional Demand from Records of Current Proceedings

⬆ Index
Second Proviso — 'Any' Tax, Credit or Refund — But from Records, Not New Inquiry
The Outer Limit
CONSTRAINT 'Any' tax, credit or refund in second proviso does not indicate pre-existing demand. Yet AA cannot make altogether new demand due to exclusion from s.2(4). Additional demand must be the outcome of proceedings before AA — NOT discovered by AA by carrying out altogether fresh proceedings under Chapters XI to XIV.
Material Must Flow from Records
SOURCE Material necessary to support additional demand must flow out of the records in current proceedings — namely: (i) Notice and relied upon documents from underlying proceedings in Chapters XI to XIV (ii) Reply to notice (iii) Appeal memo (iv) Additional submissions by taxpayer. No material outside these records can be introduced by AA to support 'opinion'.
Leary Principle
LEARY PRINCIPLE When law assures taxpayer of fair adjudication and right to appeal, taxpayer cannot be expected to make do with an unfair (or worse, a missing) adjudication and a fair appeal. Fundamental guarantee — AA cannot substitute for missing adjudication.
Opinion Must Be Reached During Appeal
CANNOT No opinion can be reached in vacuo. Opinion without any material = guesswork, estimation and conjecture. Opinion based on wisdom = suspicion at best. Opinion must be reached during the course of disposal of the appeal — not after calling for additional books or records.

19.8 Calling of Books Barred

⬆ Index
AA Cannot Call Books and Records — Judicial Notice vs New Material
Books Barred
BARRED In the course of issuing sequel-notice, AA is NOT authorized to call for books and records to undertake a more thorough examination. There is a gulf of difference between: (i) Conducting inquiry to reach finding on facts that are subject-matter of dispute, AND (ii) Launching altogether new investigation to discover new liability.
Judicial Notice — Permitted Exception
PERMITTED Taking judicial notice (s.56 of Evidence Act) = state of law or science or other matters of established knowledge = NOT 'new material' being introduced.
Example: SCN demanding reversal of credit on sweet meat stall due to mismatch. AA can take judicial notice that sweet meats supplied are taxable as supply of services under para 6(b) to Schedule II and confirm demand — notwithstanding certificates produced under Circular 183/15/2022-GST. Differentiating 'new material' from 'judicial notice' is key.
Adverse Inference — Not Permissible
NOT PERMISSIBLE It is not permissible in law that adverse inference be drawn to support an unsubstantiated demand based on guesswork, estimation and conjecture — however compelling the circumstances or however confident any authority may be about additional liability. Wisdom of Legislature in using 'enhancement' in first proviso but not second, and 'any' in second, cannot re-enact the whole of Chapters XI to XIV into s.107(11).

19.9 Update Notice v. Upgrade Notice — The Critical Distinction

⬆ Index
Update = Permitted | Upgrade = Barred | Doctrine of Election Applies
Update (Permitted)
PERMITTED Refinement of the demand on the same grounds in original notice. Overcoming incomplete portions of demand. Sub-set of original actionable cause. Consistent with facts, evidence and actionable cause in original notice.
Upgrade (Barred)
BARRED Upgraded sequel-notices cause prejudice where they operate in nature of 'review' of PO's decision in issuing original notice. Power of review resides in s.107(2) in respect of adjudication order — reviewing the notice itself is barred by s.75(7) even for PO — cannot be permitted to AA.
Example 1: SCN under s.73 cannot be 'upgraded' to SCN under s.74 — would attract consequences of additional penalty or blocking of credit.
Example 2: SCN demanding credit reversal for mismatch u/s 16(2)(c). Appeal filed enclosing certificates under Circular 183/15/2022-GST. AA cannot then issue sequel on ground of s.16(4) by making fresh discovery.
Doctrine of Election
DOCTRINE Sequel-notices that are upgraded — not just updated — operate in nature of 'review' of PO's decision. Revenue's election of actionable cause in original notice binds both PO and AA. Sequel-notice cannot disaffirm the actionable cause invoked in original notice. Sequel-notice cannot be issued under s.76.

19.10 Altogether New Notice Barred

⬆ Index
Sequel-Notice Must Operate Within Confines of Original Notice — Comparison Table
Original Notice vs Sequel Notice
DescriptionOriginal NoticeSequel Notice
FactsYESNO
AllegationsYESNO
Evidence in supportYESNO
Actionable causeYESNO
DemandYESYES
Why Altered Facts = New Notice
FATAL Taxpayer's right to face his accusers is the purpose of a notice. If accusers alter the notice → taxpayer having reached appeal stage will be left without right of fair adjudication. Appeal is remedy against perverse adjudication — not opportunity for re-adjudication.

If facts altered → sequel-notice becomes new notice whose adjudication is bypassed.
If new actionable cause invoked → defence against earlier actionable cause is rendered otiose. 'Shifting the goal post' = irreparable prejudice.
Active Disobedience — Admin Law
STRATEGY Active disobedience is a mandate in Administrative Law. Declining from submitting to illegal and unsanctioned requests for introducing new material is legitimate. Opinion must be made on material taken on record — NOT material requested but not brought on record. Penalty under s.122(1)(xvii) is not a threat — it is another proceeding altogether. Taxpayer must extend full cooperation for legitimate inquiries.

19.11 Sequel Not Free from Defects

⬆ Index
Test the Sequel-Notice — Same as Testing Original SCN
Defective Sequel-Notice
ADVANTAGE Ex parte disposal of appeal after issuance of sequel-notice could itself prove advantageous to taxpayer. Revenue cannot allege non-prosecution to support any adverse inference derived from a defective sequel-notice. Exercise of testing the original notice applies to sequel-notices on all fours.
Delegate Cannot Expand Authority
TRITE LAW Sequel-notices involve the added element whether demand is traceable to material available on record — so as not to suffer from 'delegate expanding authority while exercising authority delegated by statute'. No one can claim to be more passionate about Revenue's interests than Parliament. Due process of law is not one to be treated lightly.
Example: Order confirmed demand when reply only objected to validity of notice. Order stated "taxpayer has not submitted reconciliation but has been harping that notice is defective" — this itself is sufficient admission that (i) defects in notice are valid and (ii) adjudication found nothing to overturn these defects. Taxpayer can introduce reconciliation in appeal — but AA cannot demand preparation and production of such reconciliation.
Two Things to Check Before Replying
CHECKLIST (i) Examine if attempt is being made to cure defects in original notice via sequel-notice — which is an admission in itself
(ii) Examine which material in reply to original notice and submissions in adjudication proceedings feed the sequel-notice

This exercise is must before beginning to reply to sequel-notices. Elaborate reply to notice does not assure favourable outcome. A notice is not a search for the truth but an attempt to canvass yet another view possible in the given factual matrix.

19.12 Safety of Limitation

⬆ Index
Sequel-Notice Must Be Issued and Order Passed Within Original Limitation Period
Limitation Rule
MANDATORY Sequel-notices must be issued AND order passed within the original period of limitation under s.73 or s.74. This limitation is tied to the section under which the original notice was issued.
Cannot Change Section
BARRED Sequel-notice cannot be issued under s.74 when original notice was issued under s.73 — that vice would make it an altogether new notice.
Example: SCN under s.73 in Jun 2023 demanding output tax for 2017-18 on 'other income' in financials alleged to be supply of service — cannot be refined by issuing sequel-notice in Dec 2023 (limitation has elapsed).
Consequential Benefits Travel with Limitation
TRAVELS WITH Concessional penalty and admissibility of credit applicable to demand under s.73 will mutatis mutandis be applicable to sequel-notice. SCN demanding output tax on RCM basis under s.74 with bar on ITC → bar continues in respect of additional demand via sequel-notice. Relief under s.75(2) secured from Appellate Tribunal will cover both original AND additional demand — with exclusion from operation of the bar on claiming credit.

19.13 Safety of Original Grounds

⬆ Index
Grounds in Sequel-Notice Must Match Original Notice — Alteration = New Notice
Alteration = New Notice
FATAL Alteration of grounds in sequel-notice compared to those in original notice renders it a new notice. Consequences attendant to the grounds in original notice must be maintained while refining demand. If AA alters grounds → taxpayer left remediless and original notice becomes open-ended and ever-changing.
Alternate Grounds = Void Notice
BOTH VOID If notice has alternate grounds → bad in law — neither of them can survive. If notice indicates deliberate election to pursue one course of action by invoking relevant actionable cause → sequel-notice cannot disaffirm the actionable cause invoked.
Example 1: SCN demanding reversal of credit for non-compliance with s.16(2)(aa) AND in alternative s.16(2)(c) → void notice.
Example 2: SCN demanding penalty u/s 74(1) traceable to s.122(2)(b) where allegation is claiming ITC without actual delivery — attracts penalty u/s 122(1)(vii). Sequel-SCN cannot then propose penalty u/s 122(1)(vii) — such sequel would itself disaffirm the demand confirmed in order of adjudication.

19.14 Readiness for Tribunal

⬆ Index
Appellate Tribunal — Last Fact-Finding Authority — Definitive Position Required
AT is Last Fact-Finding Forum
FINALITY Appellate Tribunal is the last fact-finding authority. Facts found until that stage will be put to the test. Taxpayer's approach before AT must be definitive and clear. Affirmation of one thing is a disaffirmation of all other connected things. Sequel-notice has the potential to disaffirm allegations in original notice if it is an upgrade rather than update.
Tracking Framework — Grounds Chart
Grounds in SCN (issue-wise)Order of Adjudication (RETAINED / ABANDONED)Urged in Appeal Memo (APL-1)Added / Modified / WithdrawnAA Introduced in Sequel-Notice
…………
This tracking framework demonstrates whether jurisdiction exercised by AA in issuing sequel-notice was valid and proper. Determination of whether sequel-notice authority is traceable to first proviso or second proviso is a question of law before AT.
Writ vs Statutory Appeal
CAUTION Taxpayers stunned by this authority often rush to High Court in writ petition. Courts of Equity do not readily share taxpayer's emotion — they objectively consider whether injustice is meted out — likely to uphold this authority and relegate taxpayers to seek relief on merits in statutory appeal. The existence of this authority is certain — what remains to unfold is how it will be exercised and taxpayer's readiness to respond.

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 19: Sequel Notice by Appellate Authority

⬆ Index
Chapter 19 deals with the most dangerous and least-understood power in GST appellate law — the AA's authority to issue a 'sequel notice' under s.107(11). Every taxpayer who has won in adjudication and faces an appeal by Revenue, or who has filed an appeal against an adverse order, must know these principles cold. A poorly drafted reply to the original SCN can feed the very sequel-notice that destroys the case. Use this Arsenal to test the sequel-notice before responding and to agitate its validity before the Appellate Tribunal.

⚖️ Key Legal Authorities & Principles

S. Sundaram Pillai & Ors. v. VR Pattabiraman & Ors.
Supreme Court of India | (1985) 1 SCC 591
Authoritative words on the scope and extent of statutory construction of explanation and provisos used in drafting legislation. A proviso is ordinarily intended to operate as a qualification or an exception to the main provision — it cannot expand the scope of the main section. Such construction of the provisos in s.107(11) could leave the appellant worse off after filing appeal than before it.
🎯 Use When: Arguing before the Appellate Tribunal that the sequel-notice issued by AA exceeded the authority available under the provisos to s.107(11). Cite this authority to establish that the provisos cannot be construed to expand the main section's authority so as to enable AA to raise altogether new demands that were never the subject-matter of the original SCN or the appeal proceedings.
Jaswal Neco Ltd. v. Commissioner of Customs
Supreme Court of India | 2015 (322) ELT 561
Elucidates the principle of Reformatio in Peius — a person who has appealed against an adverse order cannot be left with greater grief than that suffered earlier. Prohibition of reformatio in peius is a part of fair procedure and is considered inherent in principles of natural justice — it is not just a procedural guarantee but a principle of equity itself.
🎯 Use When: Appellate Authority proposes to issue sequel-notice that is an upgrade rather than an update — i.e., proposes altogether new and higher demand that was never canvassed in the original SCN. Cite this principle and Jaswal Neco to argue that s.107(11) must be construed to permit only 'updating the demand' — and not 're-adjudication' — failing which the very filing of an appeal by taxpayer would expose him to greater liability, which violates the fundamental guarantee of fair procedure.
Elesh Agarwal v. Union of India & Ors.
Allahabad High Court | (2023) 151 taxmann.com 538
When liability is disputed by taxpayer, omission of pre-notice consultations — although contrary to due process prescribed — does not cause any prejudice to taxpayer. This innovative reasoning is used to set aside the claim that irreparable prejudice is caused when the DRC-1A process is bypassed by virtue of the sequel-notice being issued under s.107(11) rather than s.73/74.
🎯 Use When: Challenging the sequel-notice on the ground that DRC-1A pre-notice consultation was not followed before the sequel-notice under s.107(11). Revenue will cite this decision to counter the prejudice argument — so taxpayer must pre-empt this by focusing instead on the structural limits of the sequel-notice (not being a new notice, not allowing new grounds, etc.) rather than solely on procedural bypass.
Section 2(4) CGST Act — Appellate Authority Excluded from 'Adjudicating Authority'
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017
The definition of 'adjudicating authority' expressly excludes Appellate Authority and Appellate Tribunal. For Appellate Authority to discharge functions of Proper Officer under s.61, s.65, s.67, s.73, s.74 or s.76 — the appeal would transform into a 'second round' of audit, inspection or adjudication, neither of which is permissible. A new demand confirmed by AA on new grounds that taxpayer was never put at notice about — that too within original limitation — violates this fundamental demarcation.
🎯 Use When: Sequel-notice by AA proposes altogether new and different demand on entirely new grounds. Cite s.2(4) to argue that AA has trespassed into the exclusive domain of the Proper Officer — the authority to raise new demands following Chapter XV due process. The placement of AA's demand authority in Chapter XVIII (not Chapter XV) is a deliberate legislative design to make such notice a sub-set of the original notice — not a substitute for an original SCN.
Section 75(7) CGST Act — Grounds in Notice = Grounds in Order
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017
Allegations in notice cannot be altered in appeal. When adjudication itself is expressly circumscribed by s.75(7) — that the grounds on which notice is issued are the grounds on which the order must be passed — nothing in s.107(11) or s.113(1) can accomplish more. Power to issue a notice is to canvass a certain interpretation based on certain facts taken on record.
🎯 Use When: Sequel-notice introduces new grounds, new facts, or new allegations that were not in the original SCN. Cite s.75(7) to argue that even Proper Officer is prohibited from changing grounds between SCN and Order — and this constraint cannot be lower for Appellate Authority. Sequel-notice that violates s.75(7) standards is as deficient as a notice that violates s.75(7) — assailable on the same grounds.
Rule 109C CGST Rules — Right to Withdraw Appeal Before Sequel-Notice
CGST Rules, 2017 | Introduced from 26 Dec 2022
Rule 109C permits withdrawal of appeal at any time before issuance of show cause notice under sub-section (11) of section 107. This confirms Government's understanding that AA can issue a show cause notice under s.107(11) — and further, that taxpayer has the right to withdraw appeal before such notice is issued to avoid the risk of a sequel-notice. Once sequel-notice is issued, the right to withdraw falls away.
🎯 Use When: Appeal is pending before FAA and there are signs that the AA is likely to issue a sequel-notice demanding higher tax on the basis of material in the appeal records. Advise client to consider withdrawal of appeal under Rule 109C before the sequel-notice is issued — especially where the original Adjudication Order was favourable on most grounds and the risk of sequel-notice exceeds the benefit of the relief sought in appeal.

🔤 Latin Maxims — Chapter 19

Reformatio in Peius
Meaning: "A change for the worse" — a person who has appealed against an adverse order cannot be left worse off as a result of filing the appeal than he was before it. This principle is inherent in natural justice and a principle of equity itself. Section 107(11) is a deliberate legislative departure from this principle — but only to the extent of 'updating the demand', not 're-adjudication'.
Use in Hearing: "The sequel-notice under s.107(11) must be read in the light of the Latin principle of reformatio in peius — which holds that a person who has appealed against an adverse order cannot be left worse off as a result of filing that appeal. Section 107(11) is a deliberate statutory departure from this principle — but only to the extent expressly permitted — which is 'updating the demand', not raising an altogether new demand on new grounds. The sequel-notice as issued has exceeded even this exceptional departure and is assailable on this ground."
In Vacuo
Meaning: "In a vacuum" — used in the chapter to describe an opinion reached without any material on record. No opinion can be reached in vacuo. Opinion without material is guesswork, estimation and conjecture. The AA's 'opinion' necessary to trigger the sequel-notice must be reached during the course of disposal of the appeal — based on records of the current proceedings.
Use in Hearing: "The second proviso to s.107(11) requires Appellate Authority to reach 'an opinion' before issuing the sequel-notice. No opinion can be reached in vacuo. Opinion without any material is guesswork, estimation and conjecture. The 'opinion' of the Appellate Authority must be reached during the course of disposal of the appeal — based on material forming part of the appellate records — and cannot be supported by directing the appellant to produce books and records that do not already form part of those records."
Mutatis Mutandis
Meaning: "With the necessary changes having been made." Used in Chapter 19 to state that concessional penalty and admissibility of credit applicable to a demand under s.73 will mutatis mutandis apply to the sequel-notice issued under s.107(11) — since the sequel-notice is a sub-set of and traceable to the original SCN under s.73.
Use in Hearing: "The sequel-notice under s.107(11) is a sub-set of the original SCN issued under s.73. Consequently, concessional penalty options available under s.73(5) and s.73(8) will apply mutatis mutandis to the demand made in the sequel-notice. Similarly, where relief under s.75(2) is secured from Appellate Tribunal in respect of the original demand, the same relief will extend to the additional demand confirmed via the sequel-notice — both being traceable to the original s.73 notice."
Otiose
Meaning: "Serving no practical purpose" — redundant or ineffective. In Chapter 19, if a new actionable cause is invoked in the sequel-notice, the defence offered against the earlier actionable cause would be rendered otiose — serving no purpose in the new proceedings on the new cause.
Use in Hearing: "If the sequel-notice invokes a new actionable cause — different from the one elected in the original SCN — the entire defence placed on record in reply to the original SCN and in the grounds of appeal would be rendered otiose. The taxpayer would be denied the benefit of the defence already placed on record, and would be left to face an altogether new demand without any fair adjudication in Chapter XV. This 'shifting of the goal post' causes irreparable prejudice that cannot be remedied even in further appellate proceedings."
Leary Principle
Meaning: When law assures a taxpayer of fair adjudication and a right to appeal, the taxpayer cannot be expected to make do with an unfair (or worse, a missing) adjudication and a fair appeal. The appeal is meant to remedy a perverse adjudication — not to substitute for a missing or deficient one. The sequel-notice under s.107(11) cannot be used to cure fatal defects in the original notice.
Use in Hearing: "The Leary Principle holds that when law assures taxpayer of fair adjudication and a right to appeal, taxpayer cannot be expected to make do with a missing adjudication and a fair appeal. The sequel-notice under s.107(11) cannot be used to cure fatal defects in the original SCN — curative power cannot be tucked away in a proviso potent enough to supplant the entire Chapter XV. Legislative intent to do so must be more expressly manifest than what is discernible in the second proviso."
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 19 — Sequel Notice by Appellate Authority
⚖️ Chapter 20 — Relief in Appeal
CHAPTER 20

Relief in Appeal

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index — Click to Navigate

20.1 Standard Operating Procedure 20.2 Scope of Permissible Relief 20.3 Limitations in Powers of AA 20.4 Relief Limited by Pleadings and Prayer 20.5 Inherent Powers 20.6 Scope for Review and Appeal 20.7 Directions of AT or Court 20.8 Consequential Effects of Relief 20.9 Dismissal — Appeal Does Not Merit Interference 20.10 Strict v. Beneficial Interpretation 20.11 Tariff v. Exemption Notification 20.12 Time Limits — Limitation or Prescription 20.13 Suo Motu Writ Petition by SC 20.14 Circulars — Distinguishable or Binding 20.15 Case Law Authority — Binding or Not 20.16 Questions About Jurisdiction 20.17 Proceedings in Appeal 20.18 Speaking Orders in Appeal ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

20.1 Standard Operating Procedure for Appellate Authority

⬆ Index
AA's 4-Step SOP in Disposing an Appeal
Step 1 — Identify Grounds
STEP 1 Identify grounds in pleadings. Locate grounds that are withdrawn by forfeiture or election of a particular line of pleading.
Step 2 — Examine Prayer
STEP 2 Examine 'prayer' in APL-2 or APL-3. Common trap: Generic prayers such as (i) "allow appeal in interests of justice" and (ii) "pass such orders as may be deemed fit and proper" — may limit relief.
Step 3 — Allow Only Permissible Relief
STEP 3 Allow relief that is permissible based on grounds. If prayer is generic → allow relief only if grounds in pleadings are found satisfactory and relief prayed is compatible with pleadings.
Step 4 — Revenue Prejudice Check
STEP 4 Consider if Order is prejudicial to interests of Revenue — take additional steps to ensure 'discussion and findings' sufficiently support the Order. AA must only interfere with Adjudication Order if and to the extent merited.

20.2 Scope of Permissible Relief

⬆ Index
What AA Will and Will NOT Do — Illustrative Scope
What AA Will Do
WILL (a) Show appeal is not defective
(b) Show appeal is not on restricted matters (s.121)
(c) Show appeal deserves intervention
(d) Prevent misinterpretation of law
(e) Address misunderstanding of facts and misapplication of law
(f) Address misplaced use of discretion
(g) Address omission to discharge duties
(h) Check extra-legislative measures in attempt 'to do justice'
What AA Will NOT Do
WILL NOT (a) Re-adjudicate the notice
(b) Travel beyond Adjudication Order and examine notice
(c) Re-appreciate evidence to reach different conclusion
(d) Entertain appeal to reach another conclusion on same facts
(e) Re-assess tax liability

AA is not authorized to re-assess tax liability — Appellant-taxpayers often make mistake of pleading unlimited powers.
Preparation for AA
PREPARATION (a) List all facts-in-issue that need to be decided
(b) Take on record grounds in notice that are (i) retained or (ii) abandoned in Adjudication Order and (iii) pleaded in APL-1
(c) Limit relief to pleadings and prayer by Appellant
Dismissal — When No Interference Warranted
COMMON OUTCOME Very common for AA to dismiss appeals on ground that interference is NOT warranted considering the manner in which 'decision or order' is passed in adjudication. Even on dismissal, Speaking Order required u/s 107(12) due to 'doctrine of merger'.

20.3 Limitations in Powers of Appellate Authority — Section 107(11)

⬆ Index
Section 107(11) — Confirm, Modify or Annul — Nothing More
Three Powers — Exhaustive
s.107(11) AA can only:
(i) Confirm = retain original demand
(ii) Modify = reduce original demand to some extent
(iii) Annul = completely drop entire original demand

Nothing more. No power to enhance.
s.107(11) vs s.108(1) — Ejusdem Generis
GP SINGH RULE s.107(11): "confirming, modifying or annulling"
s.108(1): "enhancing, modifying or annulling"

When 'words of rank' are used, words of higher rank cannot be inserted without violating ranking order. AA relying on 'modifying' to 'enhance' demand is barred by this rule of statutory construction. Legislature used 'enhancing' only in s.108 (Revision) — deliberate departure cannot be undone.
Further Inquiry — Boundaries
BARRED Power of "such further inquiry" u/s 107(11) exercised with great caution so as not to travel beyond the Adjudication Order — subject only to grounds urged in APL-1. Any 'discovery' of additional liability during hearing of appeal → must be informed to PO for necessary action in separate proceedings — not confirmed by AA.
Radiant Enterprises (P) Ltd. v. JC (Appeals-I) (2023) 147 taxmann.com 245 (Cal.) — AA is not empowered to travel beyond Adjudication Order and enter into matters contained in books of account or refund application. Cannot frame new issues not before AA. Even issues in Adjudication Order not agitated by either side are NOT 'at large' before AA.
Abandonment of Grounds
CAUTION Appellant-taxpayers are free to abandon grounds — even inadvertently. Will NOT be restored by AA unless application for amendment of grounds is moved by Appellant-taxpayer and allowed by AA. Once abandoned — deemed forfeited.

20.4 Relief Limited by Pleadings and Prayer

⬆ Index
Doctrine of Election — Taxpayer's Choice of Grounds Binds AA
AA Cannot Go Beyond Pleadings
BARRED AA will NOT travel beyond grounds of appeal in APL-1 or APL-3 and allow relief based on some other more advantageous grounds that could have been pleaded. Appellant-taxpayer's wisdom to choose grounds cannot be undermined or substituted by altogether fresh grounds by AA.
Wrong Ground = Wrong Outcome
CONSEQUENCE
Example: In appeal against rejection of refund on SEZ supplies (zero-rated), Appellant pleaded that refund is admissible based on 'delivery location' in EWB into the Zone. AA may NOT be permitted to allow relief prayed.

But if Appellant had pleaded availability of endorsed invoice from Specified Officer of SEZ → AA would be bound to consider this unimpeachable evidence and examine possibility of allowing appeal.
Imperative — Careful Pleadings and Prayer
LESSON Careful consideration of pleadings and prayer is imperative for fair consideration and disposal of appeal. This is often lost sight of by taxpayers. Relief permissible = relief that is sought (pleading AND prayer) — both must be aligned.

20.5 Inherent Powers — Section 151 CPC r/w Section 111

⬆ Index
Inherent Powers of Appellate Authority — Section 111 List
Source of Inherent Powers
SOURCE Judicial proceedings by AA are enjoined by 'inherent powers' under s.151 of CPC. Available in every proceeding declared to be 'judicial' in nature. Listed in s.111 (in context of Appellate Tribunal).
8 Inherent Powers (a to h)
(a) Summoning and enforcing attendance of any person and examining him on oath
(b) Requiring discovery and production of documents
(c) Receiving evidence on affidavits
(d) Requisitioning any public record/document (subject to s.123 and s.124 of Indian Evidence Act)
(e) Issuing commissions for examination of witnesses or documents
(f) Dismissing a representation for default or deciding it ex parte
(g) Setting aside order of dismissal for default or order passed ex parte
(h) Any other matter which may be prescribed
Examples of Use
EXAMPLES Per Incuriam: Appeal decided without considering a HC decision — within inherent powers, AA can recall order and pass fresh order.

Withdrawal: Appellant submitting written request for withdrawal → appeal may be "dismissed as withdrawn, with prejudice".

20.6 Scope for Review and Appeal — Forum Hierarchy

⬆ Index
Appellate Hierarchy in GST — AA → AT → HC → SC
Forum Hierarchy
FAA — First forum of appeal. Questions of fact + law.
↓
GST Appellate Tribunal (s.112) — Last forum on questions of FACT.
↓
High Court (s.117) — Questions of LAW only.
↓
Supreme Court (s.118)
Revisionary Risk to AA Orders
RISK Orders of AA are open to review under s.108 of CGST Act. If AA's Orders were to be overturned in revisionary proceedings → entire exercise of AA may be futile and relief extended to Appellant merely illusory.

Where AA considers appeal allowable — re-examine if there are grounds for revisionary authority to consider it prejudicial.
PREJUDICIAL IF
GroundYes/No?Detail
IllegalYes/NoHOW?
ImproperYes/NoHOW?
Not taken into account factorsYes/NoWHICH?
Adverse observations by CAGYes/NoWHAT?
Questions of Law Examples
QOL EXAMPLES 2A v. 3B mismatch: Whether 2A can be introduced retrospectively from 1 Jul 2017 → QOL — can be carried in revision petition to HC/SC.

Belated credit s.16(4): Whether vested credits can be divested by delay in filing returns → QOL — can be carried to HC/SC.

20.7 Directions of Appellate Tribunal or Court

⬆ Index
AA Bound to Follow Directions of AT or Court — Section 113
Power of Remand — AT has, AA Does Not
KEY DISTINCTION AA is bound to carry out directions of AT or Court — in view of power of remand available in s.113 to Appellate Tribunal. AA does NOT enjoy power of remand under s.107(11). Directions will be specific — whether to reconsider appeal with certain findings on facts or law.
AA Cannot Deviate from AT/Court Directions
ABSOLUTE AA cannot proceed against directions of AT or Court — not even in the interests of justice.
Example: Appeal dismissed on limitation (delay beyond s.107(4)) may be directed by AT or Court to be admitted and considered on merits. AA then only addresses merits — not limitation. Revenue welcome to challenge this direction and demand objections be recorded — affording opportunity to carry grievance to HC/SC. But AA must proceed on merits, as directed.

20.8 Consequential Effects of Relief in Appeal

⬆ Index
Primary Relief → Secondary Relief — Windfall Gains or Hidden Losses
The Concept
PRINCIPLE Granting relief on one issue (Primary Relief) may bring windfall gains on another issue which is not in dispute (Secondary Relief) — due to the finding of fact reached and nature of relief granted. AA must consider consequential effects of any finding reached.
Registration Cancellation Example
PRIMARY → SECONDARY Primary: Appeal allowed in 2022 re registration cancelled in 2018. Restoration implies PO's grounds for cancellation were erroneous.

Secondary: Appellant who could not file returns and claim credit — now has grounds to (i) claim ITC contrary to s.16(4) and (ii) oppose any demand for violation of s.16(4) — placing default on PO for wrongful cancellation.
Mismatch Credit Example
SECONDARY RELIEF
Example: Appeal allowed re mismatch of credit claimed (2A v. 3B) on ground that Adjudication Order travelled beyond scope of SCN. By allowing appeal, Adjudication Order is set aside and credit becomes admissible. PO's failure to raise right grounds in SCN — AA will not dismiss appeal to 'save the demand'.

20.9 Dismissal — If Appeal Does Not Merit Interference

⬆ Index
Admitted vs Entertained — Objective vs Subjective Criteria
Power vs Obligation
DISTINCTION Power = exercised where it is essential — where omission would be failure to deliver what is necessary.
Obligation = must be discharged in every instance — where omission would itself be a failure.

AA has power — NOT obligation — to interfere. Only exercises where Adjudication Order 'deserves' interference.
Admitted (Objective) vs Entertained (Subjective)
Objectivities (Admitted)Subjectivities (Entertained)
Form of appeal correctMistake of facts or law
Condition of pre-deposit metMisapplication of law
Verification completeExercise of discretion omitted
Limitation satisfiedErroneous findings reached
Valid APL-2 issuedFindings reached erroneously
Condonation (if any) within limitAuthorities not followed
When All Appeals Are First Liable to Dismissal
ALL APPEALS Appeal is NOT to 'substitute' wisdom of AA for PO's. Where NOT shown that PO has failed — even when discretion is exercised — interference NOT justified. Appeal liable to be dismissed. Taxpayer's grievance must show that failure in Adjudication Order will perpetuate and cannot be redressed except with AA's interference.

Taxpayers must guide proceedings to a point where confirming demand would be impossible without exposing Adjudication Order to interference.

20.10 Strict v. Beneficial Interpretation

⬆ Index
Strict Interpretation is the Rule — Beneficial Interpretation Barred for AA
Strict Interpretation — The Rule
RULE Intentions of lawmaker are best understood by the words used in legislation. There is no room for intendment. Therefore, 'strict' interpretation is the rule. Absurdity must be shown — and absurdity is not the possibility of one absurd result but one that does disservice to the entire provisions.
Beneficial Interpretation — Barred for AA
BARRED Beneficial interpretation must be assumed to be barred in AA's hands. AA is 'creature of the statute' — not permitted to sit in judgment about validity of any provision of law. Can only sit in judgment about application of law to facts or exercise of powers without authority.

Beneficial interpretation = reading statutory provisions to support pro-taxpayer or pro-Revenue outcome. Only High Court enjoys this latitude.
Example: Appeal re belated credit s.16(4) is liable to be dismissed by AA — although HC may direct that form of claiming transition credit is subordinate to substantive right. To allow this appeal on similar reasoning = travelling beyond statute. HC alone enjoys this latitude.
Judicial Notice — AA Can Take
PERMITTED Judicial notice of state of the law may be taken even if not urged by taxpayer.
Example: PO demanded interest u/s 50 on 'gross tax' liability. Due to retrospective amendment, interest payable on 'net tax' liability (belated filing of returns). AA cannot ignore this state of law and prejudice taxpayer — must take judicial notice of retrospective amendment.
Saving demand by correct law: If PO invoked relevant provision but interpreted incorrectly → AA free to apply correct treatment. Example: SCN invoked s.15(4) → Adjudication applied Rule 27 → AA free to apply Rule 30 after showing OMV cannot be reliably established. This is taking 'official notice' of extant law.

20.11 Interpretation of Tariff v. Exemption Notification

⬆ Index
Tariff Notification → Exemption Notification — Carve-Out Relationship
Goods vs Services — Interpretation Principles
PRINCIPLES Goods: Customs Tariff Act principles apply to GST tariff notification for supply of 'goods'
Services: Explanatory Notes principles apply to GST tariff notification for supply of 'services'

Mistake in notice between goods and services = INCURABLE.
TARIFF SHOPPING BARRED No person permitted to engage in 'tariff shopping' — paying higher rate of output tax to enjoy ITC not available at lower rate. Conditional exemptions are NOT optional — mandatory. Credit claimed on payment of higher rate = forfeited u/s 76; credit liable to be demanded u/s 74.
Exemption = Carve-Out of Tariff
RELATIONSHIP Entries in exemption notification are traceable to entries in tariff notification. Scope of exemption entries is to 'carve out' from scope of tariff entries.
Exemption Entry 66 (Educational Institution): Traceable to Entry 30 of Tariff Notification under HSN 9992. NOT all activities of Trust/Society running educational institutions are exempt — only those (i) in its capacity as educational institution and (ii) supplied to students, faculty or staff.
General purpose articles: When used for special purposes do NOT become special purpose articles eligible for exemption. Candle sold near Church to devotees = general purpose article, not 'pooja samagri'.
RCM Entries Traceable to Tariff
RCM LINKAGE RCM notification entries traceable to tariff notification entries. Tax payable u/s 9(1) collected from Recipient u/s 9(3).

Entry 5AA RCM: Traceable to Entry 12 of Tariff. If property remains 'residential dwelling' → RCM when Recipient is Registered Person. If change-of-use → forward charge basis.

Entry 2 RCM (advocate): Will NOT be attracted if supply is eligible to exemption under Entry 45 of Exemption Notification.

20.12 Time Limits — Limitation or Prescription

⬆ Index
Limitation (Remedy Lost) vs Prescription (Right Lost) — Critical Distinction
Limitation — Remedy Lost, Right Intact
LIMITATION Time limit operates as 'limitation' where the right is not actionable — cannot be carried to seek directions from a Court. Right remains intact but remedy in law is lost due to delay.
Example: RFD-1 NOT filed within 2 years → Accumulated ITC will NOT be lost — it continues to be available for utilization later. But the (actionable) right to claim refund will be lost.

Time limit in s.37, s.39 or s.44 → attracts late fee u/s 47 but once late fee paid, filing will not be 'void' or 'defective' as defined in s.2(117).
Prescription — Right Itself Lost
PRESCRIPTION Time limit operates as 'prescription' where the right itself will be lost — more correctly called 'extinctive prescription'.
Example: Time limit in s.16(4) cannot be regularized — delay results in permanent loss of ITC even when all other conditions of s.16(2) are fulfilled. The right itself is lost.
When taxpayer loses ITC by extinctive prescription → State gains right by correlative 'acquisitive prescription'. Study ss.25 and 27 of Limitation Act for deeper understanding.

20.13 Suo Motu Writ Petition by SC — COVID Limitation Extension

⬆ Index
SMWP 3/2020 + Section 168A — COVID Limitation Relaxation Framework
SMWP 3/2020 — SC Suo Motu
SMWP 3/2020 At COVID lockdown (Mar 2020), Apex Court suo motu took cognizance — 15.03.2020 to 28.02.2022 period excluded for limitation in all judicial or quasi judicial proceedings.

Final disposal via MA 21/2022:
(I) Period 15.03.2020 to 28.02.2022 stands excluded
(II) Balance limitation as on 03.10.2021 available from 01.03.2022
(III) Cases where limitation expired during 15.03.2020–28.02.2022 → 90 days from 01.03.2022
(IV) Also covers Arbitration Act, Commercial Courts Act, NI Act
Section 168A — Provision-Specific Extension
s.168A Central Government promulgated s.168A vide TOLA Act 2020 from 31 Mar 2020 — to issue provision-specific changes in limitation. Several notifications issued starting with 35/2020-CT dated 3 Apr 2020.

Circular 157/13/2021-GST dated 20 Jul 2021 — extension applies NOT only to taxpayers but also to departmental action.
Specific Extension — FY 2017-18
2017-18 Notification 13/2022-CT dated 5 Jul 2022 revised vide 9/2023-CT dated 31 Mar 2023:
(a) Demand u/s 73 extended to 31 Dec 2023 (previously 30 Sept 2023)
(b) Refunds u/s 73 — excluded period 1 Mar 2020 to 28 Feb 2022

SMWP vs s.168A: Once s.168A came into statute and specific notifications issued — SMWP must make way for will of Legislature. Higher courts may 'mould relief' allowing greater of the two.

20.14 Circulars — Distinguishable or Binding

⬆ Index
Circulars Are Binding — But AA Must 'Adopt' Reasons as Its Own, Not Act Under Dictation
Binding but Not Dictation
RULE All circulars issued by Commissioner are 'binding'. But AA cannot be seen to be 'acting under dictation'. When issue revolves around circular → AA must 'adopt' those reasons "as if they were AA's own reasons" for passing Order.
Distinguishability
CHECK While adopting reasoning in any circular — care must be taken to examine if the facts in the appeal are comparable with those in the circular. AA must apply the most current judicial authorities on any aspect before it. AA being a quasi judicial functionary cannot act under dictation flowing from circulars or other instructions.

20.15 Case Law Authority — Distinguishable or Binding

⬆ Index
