Hierarchy of Binding Precedent — What Binds Whom
Supreme Court
BINDING — ALL INDIA Applies to whole of India. Later SC decision preferred over earlier on a given issue. Later decision rendered without considering earlier decision = still binding unless specifically overruled. The two decisions cannot be applied based on convenience — later decision alone binds.
High Court Hierarchy
STATE HC = BINDING Decision of State (or UT) HC = binding within State. Decision of other HCs = binding when no decision of State HC on given issue. Later decision of other HCs is NOT binding in State if earlier State HC decision subsists — even if providing different interpretation. Bench composition also provides superior binding authority.
Earlier Tax Regime Decisions
CAUTION Decisions under earlier tax regime have almost NO bearing in GST. To rely on those — provisions of such law and GST must be shown to be 'pari materia'. One purpose of new tax regime is to depart from practices of earlier regime. Application by logical reasoning = misplaced.
CJEU Decisions — Not Binding
NOT BINDING (a) CJEU is not an Indian Court
(b) EU VAT is not a statute but a 'treaty'
(c) CJEU binding on sovereign nations only because it interprets the treaty
(d) EU VAT = Common Law + Civil Law; India = Common Law only

Useful only for detailed examination of facts (business model, purpose of arrangement, terms and conditions, admitted reasons) — not for legal principles.

20.16 Questions About Jurisdiction

⬆ Index
Actions Without Authority of Law Are Illegal — Jurisdiction = Officer × Provision
Officer v. Taxpayer
ILLEGAL IF Actions taken without authority of law are illegal. Illegally secured evidence may be used by Revenue — but illegal use of authority cannot be condoned, not even in appeal.
Example 1: SCN by PO below rank of Assistant Commissioner levying penalty u/s 122(1A) → beyond Authorization-Assignment issued to such PO.
Example 2: SCN by Enforcement Officer (above rank of AC) under s.73 → implies demand does not involve evasion of tax or credit → SCN lacks jurisdiction.
Officer v. Provision
ALSO ILLEGAL Jurisdiction is not only limited to Officer v. Taxpayer — also applies to Officer v. Provision. 'Jurisdictional pyramid' must be referenced.
Example 1: SCN u/s 73 by State GST Officer to taxpayers registered with Central GST department not involving evasion of tax.
Example 2: Adjudication Order in MOV-9 demanding tax in respect of undervaluation of supplies → beyond scope of enquiry in ss.68 and 129.

20.17 Proceedings in Appeal — Last Full Forum on Facts

⬆ Index
AA = Probably Last Forum for Full Consideration on Facts — Avail This Opportunity to Fullest
Why AA is Critical
KEY AA is probably the last forum where the dispute or lis will receive full consideration on facts. GST Appellate Tribunal will only inquiry into failure of consideration by AA — will not re-examine documents and records to establish facts.
What Appellant Must Do
AVAIL FULLY Appellant-Respondent must avail this opportunity to fullest extent, including:
— Making new pleadings
— Adducing evidence in support
— Withdrawing any pleadings

AA will guide the course of lis to reach proper finding on QOF and QOL.
AA's Limits
HELPLESS ONLY FOR AA is helpless only to extent appeal challenges the validity of the law itself. Challenge to validity of proceedings — entertained in right earnest. AA may caution Appellant-Respondent in event of omission to avail opportunity to make full and true disclosure of all material facts.

20.18 Speaking Orders in Appeal

⬆ Index
AA's Speaking Order — Anatomy and Special Points
Three Special Points for AA
SPECIAL (a) Validity of original proceedings not questioned in earliest opportunity → taxpayer barred from raising this question in appeal due to acquiescence
(b) Allegations made in notice responded by rebuttal and defence but NOT denied and disputed in reply
(c) New or additional material not forming part of notice introduced by taxpayer in rebuttal and defence
Flow of Speaking Order in Appeal
Appeal filed in order (form, limitation, pre-conditions) → Appeal not liable to be dismissed
↓
Grounds (pleaded/withdrawn) clearly identified → Evidence examined and accepted/rejected
↓
Facts-in-issue proved/not-proved/disproved → Appellant-Respondent heard (PH conducted and recorded)
↓
Pleadings-rebuttal discussed → Finding(s) reached (QOF-QOL)
↓
Order (operative part) pronounced
Order Must Steer Clear
BOTH SIDES Order of AA must steer clear of:
(i) Any bias to 'save the demand'
(ii) Travelling beyond boundaries of law 'to do justice'

Care must be taken to separately record matters of assistance in further proceedings — identify points of dispute (facts-in-issue) and expound manner in which AA found the same to be reliable.

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 20: Relief in Appeal

⬆ Index
Chapter 20 is the master chapter on appellate strategy — from the SOP of AA to Speaking Orders in appeal. Every practitioner appearing before FAA must know these principles. The most powerful tool is knowing the limits of AA's powers (confirm/modify/annul — nothing more) and using those limits to protect the taxpayer from revisionary proceedings that may undo hard-won appellate relief.

⚖️ Key Legal Authorities & Principles

Radiant Enterprises (P) Ltd. v. JC (Appeals-I)
Calcutta High Court | (2023) 147 taxmann.com 245
Appellate Authority is not empowered to travel beyond the Adjudication Order and enter into matters contained in books of account or refund application. AA cannot frame new issues that are not before it for consideration. Even issues contained in the Adjudication Order that are not agitated by either sides are also not 'at large' before the Appellate Authority. A much-needed judicial authority to quell misplaced enthusiasm in appellate proceedings to 'save the demand' beyond boundaries laid down by Legislature.
🎯 Use When: AA in appellate proceedings attempts to examine books of account, raise new issues, or confirm demand on grounds not raised in SCN or Adjudication Order. Cite this HC decision along with s.107(11) to argue that AA's jurisdiction is strictly limited to the Adjudication Order and grounds raised in APL-1 — anything beyond is without jurisdiction.
Section 107(11) CGST Act — Confirm, Modify or Annul — Cannot Enhance
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | r/w GP Singh on Principles of Statutory Interpretation
AA can only confirm (retain original demand), modify (reduce original demand to some extent), or annul (completely drop entire original demand) — and nothing more. The word 'modifying' in s.107(11) cannot be read to include 'enhancing' — Legislature used 'enhancing' only in s.108(1) for Revisionary Authority, and this deliberate departure cannot be undone. When 'words of rank' are used in statute, words of higher rank cannot be inserted without violating the ranking order (GP Singh principle).
🎯 Use When: AA in adjudicating appeal proposes to confirm demand on a higher amount or on new grounds beyond those in the Adjudication Order. Cite s.107(11) and GP Singh's ejusdem generis principle to demonstrate that 'modifying' in s.107(11) can only mean reducing — not enhancing — the original demand. If Revenue relies on AA's 'modification' to enhance, argue this is beyond the statutory authority of s.107(11).
Objective vs Subjective Criteria — Admitted vs Entertained
Principle | Applied in GST Appeal Proceedings | Para 20.9
Appeals are 'admitted' when objective criteria are fulfilled (form correct, pre-deposit met, verification complete, limitation satisfied, valid APL-2 issued, condonation within limit). But appeals are 'entertained' only when subjective criteria are shown to exist (mistake of facts or law, misapplication of law, exercise of discretion omitted, erroneous findings reached, authorities not followed). Appeal is not to substitute wisdom of AA for PO's — where PO has not failed, interference is not justified.
🎯 Use When: Preparing grounds of appeal. The grounds must be framed to satisfy the 'subjective' criteria — not just assert that the demand is wrong. Each ground must show that the failure in the Adjudication Order is of a kind that will perpetuate and cannot be redressed except with AA's interference. Generic grounds like 'demand is contrary to law' without linking to the specific failure of PO will be dismissed.
Section 151 CPC r/w Section 111 CGST Act — Inherent Powers of Appellate Authority
Statutory Provisions | Civil Procedure Code & CGST Act, 2017
Judicial proceedings by AA are enjoined by inherent powers under s.151 CPC. These include: summoning any person and examining on oath; requiring discovery and production of documents; receiving evidence on affidavits; requisitioning public records; issuing commissions for examination of witnesses; dismissing for default or deciding ex parte; setting aside ex parte orders. A decision rendered per incuriam (without considering a binding HC decision) can be recalled within inherent powers and fresh order passed.
🎯 Use When: (1) AA has passed an order without considering a relevant HC or SC decision — apply for recall on ground of per incuriam using inherent powers. (2) AA has dismissed appeal for default without granting proper opportunity — apply for restoration of appeal. (3) AA needs to direct production of documents critical to establishing facts-in-issue — cite these inherent powers to support the application.
Strict Interpretation — Rule for Appellate Authority; Beneficial Interpretation — Barred
Settled Principle | Applied in GST Appellate Proceedings | Para 20.10
Intentions of lawmakers are best understood by the words used in legislation — there is no room for intendment. Therefore strict interpretation is the rule. Beneficial interpretation (reading statutory provisions to support a pro-taxpayer or pro-Revenue outcome) must be assumed to be barred in Appellate Authority's hands. AA is a 'creature of the statute' — not permitted to sit in judgment about validity of any provision of law. Only Courts of record (HC and SC) can 'mould relief' to prevent absurdity.
🎯 Use When: (1) Taxpayer asks AA to allow appeal by reading a provision beneficially — AA will decline citing this principle; taxpayer must instead go to HC. (2) Revenue asks AA to dismiss appeal by creating an adverse interpretation through beneficial reading — cite this principle to argue AA must follow strict interpretation and this serves to set aside the demand. (3) AA proposes to take judicial notice of state of law — cite the interest on net tax example to show AA can and must take judicial notice of retrospective amendments even if not urged by taxpayer.
Limitation vs Prescription — Extinctive vs Acquisitive
Settled Legal Distinction | Applied in GST Credit and Refund Context | Para 20.12
Limitation: the right remains intact but the remedy in law is lost due to delay — actionable cause cannot be carried to Court. Prescription (extinctive): the right itself is lost due to delay. When taxpayer loses ITC by operation of extinctive prescription in s.16(4), the State gains this right to real property by the correlative acquisitive prescription by operation of the same law. The distinction between these two concepts determines whether ITC continues to be available for utilization (limitation) or is permanently forfeited (prescription).
🎯 Use When: (1) Arguing that ITC lost due to s.16(4) cannot be regularized by AA — the right itself is lost by prescription, unlike refund limitation where only the remedy is lost. (2) Arguing that RFD-1 filed beyond 2 years does not extinguish the underlying ITC — it only loses the actionable right to refund, while credit continues to be available for utilization. (3) Use the acquisitive prescription concept to show why the State takes an especially strong interest in s.16(4) — it is not merely procedural but vests the right in the State.

🔤 Latin Maxims — Chapter 20

Ejusdem Generis
Meaning: "Of the same kind or nature." Where general words follow specific words in a statute, the general words are interpreted to apply only to things of the same kind as those specifically enumerated. Applied in Chapter 20 to the words 'confirming, modifying or annulling' in s.107(11) — 'modifying' must be read in the same category as 'confirming' and 'annulling', which means reducing or neutralising the demand — NOT enhancing it. The word 'enhancing' is deliberately used only in s.108(1).
Use in Hearing: "The principle of ejusdem generis, as explained by GP Singh on Principles of Statutory Interpretation, applies to the words 'confirming, modifying or annulling' in s.107(11). All three words operate in the same direction — they can only retain, reduce or extinguish the original demand. 'Modifying' cannot be stretched to mean 'enhancing' without violating the ranking order in which these words are used. The Legislature used 'enhancing' only in s.108(1) — this deliberate departure cannot be undone by reading into 'modifying' something more."
Per Incuriam
Meaning: "Through lack of care" — a decision rendered per incuriam is one that was decided without considering a relevant binding authority (statute or precedent) that if it had been considered, would have led to a different result. Such a decision does not have binding precedential value. In appeal proceedings, an order of AA that fails to consider a binding HC decision is per incuriam and can be recalled using inherent powers.
Use in Hearing: "The order of Appellate Authority is rendered per incuriam — it fails to consider the binding decision of this Hon'ble High Court in [case name] which squarely covers the issue at hand and which, if considered, would have led to a different conclusion. In exercise of inherent powers under s.151 of the CPC read with s.111 of the CGST Act, we request that this order be recalled and a fresh order be passed after due consideration of this binding authority."
Pari Materia
Meaning: "On the same subject matter" — statutes that are on the same subject are to be read together and interpreted consistently. Applied in Chapter 20 to decisions under earlier tax regime — which can be relied upon in GST only if the provisions of such earlier law and GST are shown to be 'pari materia'. The purpose of a new tax regime is to depart from practices and interpretations of the earlier regime — making application of earlier decisions by mere logical reasoning misplaced.
Use in Hearing: "The decision cited by Revenue under the earlier tax regime is not applicable to the present proceedings. For a decision under the earlier regime to be persuasive in GST, the provisions of such earlier law and GST must be shown to be pari materia — on the same subject matter, with the same language, similar structure and identical purpose. Since GST is a conscious legislative departure from the earlier tax regime, reliance on earlier decisions by mere logical reasoning is misplaced and this decision must be distinguished."
Extinctive Prescription / Acquisitive Prescription
Meaning: Extinctive prescription = a time limit by which a right itself is extinguished by delay, not merely the remedy. Acquisitive prescription = the correlative process by which another party acquires that right. When taxpayer loses ITC under s.16(4) by extinctive prescription, the State gains the right by acquisitive prescription by operation of the same law. This is more than procedural — it is a vesting of title in the State.
Use in Hearing: "The time limit in s.16(4) operates as an extinctive prescription — not a mere limitation. The distinction is fundamental: in limitation, the right remains intact but the remedy is lost. In prescriptions, the right itself is extinguished. When input tax credit is permanently forfeited by operation of s.16(4), the State acquires the right to that credit by acquisitive prescription. No amount of equitable reasoning, no HC direction, and certainly no AA order can restore a right that has been extinguished by operation of law."
Lis
Meaning: "A dispute or legal controversy." In appellate proceedings, the lis is the dispute as defined by the grounds raised in APL-1. AA's jurisdiction is confined to the lis before it. AA cannot travel beyond the lis to frame new issues, examine new documents, or confirm demand on new grounds — even if doing so would 'save the demand'. Appellate Authority is probably the last forum where the lis will receive full consideration on facts.
Use in Hearing: "The lis before this Appellate Authority is defined by the grounds raised in APL-1 against the specific findings in the Adjudication Order. The question of [new ground raised by Revenue] does not form part of the lis before this forum — it was neither alleged in the SCN, nor adjudicated in the Adjudication Order, nor raised in the grounds of appeal or in the departmental response. For AA to frame this as a new issue would be to travel beyond the lis and exercise jurisdiction of a Proper Officer — which is expressly barred by s.2(4) read with s.107(11)."
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 20 — Relief in Appeal
⚖️ Chapter 21 — Revisionary Proceedings

⚖️ Chapter 21 — Revisionary Proceedings

GST Act 2017 | Section 108 — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
21.1 Relevance in Replying to Notices
21.2 Decision or Order
21.3 Officer Subordinate
21.4 Suo Motu
21.5 Information Received
21.6 Request from Commissioner of State Tax
21.7 Conflict with Section 107(2)
21.8 Call for and Examine
21.9 Prejudicial to Interests of Revenue
21.10 Illegal Decision or Order
21.11 Improper Decision or Order
21.12 Pre-Existing Factors
21.13 Post-Arising Factors
21.14 CAG Adverse Observations
21.15 Prerequisites to Invoke Powers
21.16 Doctrine of Election
21.17 Stay Operation of Orders
21.18 Further Inquiry
21.19 Immediately Stay — Refund Orders
21.20 Substitutionary Orders
21.21 Enhance
21.22 Modify
21.23 Annul
21.24 Bar on Proceedings
21.25 Mischievous Appeals
21.29 Enhanced Remedy for Revenue
21.30 Checklist
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
21.1 — Relevance in Replying to Notices
REVISIONARY PROCEEDINGS — WHY IT MATTERS WHILE DRAFTING SCN REPLIES
Golden Rule for Replies
RULE Reply must contain ONLY what is necessary for the proceedings at hand — nothing more
Must be limited to authority of the officer before whom it is filed
PURPOSE Section 108 analysis equips taxpayer to review reply-drafting approach and pre-notice enquiry strategy
Danger of Over-Enthusiasm
⚠ PERILOUS Over-disclosing facts in SCN reply is dangerous
File can be called for examination by Revisionary Authority to determine whether prejudice is caused to Revenue
Too much information on record = ammunition for RA to initiate revision proceedings and overturn favourable order
21.2 — Decision or Order
WHAT QUALIFIES AS 'DECISION OR ORDER' — REVISIONARY AUTHORITY u/s 2(99); SORTING PROCESS
Trigger for RA u/s 108
WHO Revisionary Authority [s.2(99)] can call records of any proceeding
PURPOSE Verify whether decision/order is proper and in accordance with law
TIMING Records callable ONLY AFTER decision/order is passed — NOT during pendency of proceedings
EXCEPTION Orders NOT appealable u/s 121 are excluded from revisionary proceedings
IS a Decision or Order
YES Decision to reject provisional release of goods lawfully seized = decision/order (even if not appealable u/s 121)
All adjudication orders passed by Proper Officers = covered
NOT a Decision or Order
NO Grant of authorisation to conduct audit u/s 65 = administrative function, NOT adjudication
Administrative functions cannot be taken up in revisionary proceedings
Sorting Process
FAVOURABLE Orders favourable to taxpayer → Must be examined for prejudice to Revenue
UNFAVOURABLE Orders unfavourable to taxpayer → Generally not prejudicial to Revenue
PROCESS All orders go through 'sorting' to determine whether revision is merited
21.3 — Officer Subordinate
OFFICERS SUBORDINATE TO REVISIONARY AUTHORITY — TWO CATEGORIES; CONSTITUTIONAL CONCERN; SAFEGUARDS
Category (a) — Proper Officers
Acting as presiding officers in adjudication proceedings
Applying mind → reaching findings on facts → pronouncing applicable law
Category (b) — Appellate Authority
Appellate Authority lower in rank than Revisionary Authority
Rule 109A → lists AA of certain rank authorised to hear adjudication orders of corresponding Proper Officers
Constitutional Concern
CONCERN Executive Officer (RA) reviewing/overturning Appellate Authority orders → offends separation of Judiciary and Executive
Immediacy of 'stay' power → sets at naught success in adjudication → 'Jarring'
BUT Legislative wisdom: avoiding illegality/impropriety → cannot be dismissed lightly
Safeguards — Validity
SAFEGUARD Power vested in high-ranking official → discrimination difficult to attach
Notice required + opportunity to be heard + Speaking Order mandatory
Revenue has dual remedy: Departmental Appeal [s.107(2)] + Revisionary Proceedings [s.108]
21.4 — Suo Motu
SUO MOTU INITIATION — OWN MOTION OF RA; DECISION vs ORDER; FACT-IN-ISSUE; EXAMPLES
Flow of Information
Imperative to track all decisions/orders in administrative, adjudication & quasi-judicial proceedings by subordinate Officers
KEY Q Can a 'decision' about a fact-in-issue be prejudicial even if resultant 'order' is unfavourable to taxpayer?
Fact-in-Issue — Defined
S.3 IEA "Any fact from which — either by itself or with other facts — existence, non-existence, nature or extent of any right, liability or disability necessarily follows"
DECISION = Reasons resulting in an order; may be about existence of certain facts or state of affairs in a business transaction
Illustration — Goods vs Services
DECISION Contract decided as supply of goods → 5% demanded
IF WRONG Actually services → 18% applicable
Decision is prejudicial → Order (though confirming 5% demand) is subject to revision
Examples
AA reducing penalty beyond PO's allowance → attempt to 're-do' adjudication → prejudice → suo motu revision to restore AO
IT dept info on TP adjustment under GAAR accepted by taxpayer → deemed supplies between distinct persons → refunds liable to revision
21.5 — Information Received
INFORMATION RECEIVED — ANY SOURCE ADMISSIBLE; INDEPENDENT APPLICATION OF MIND; RTI SCOPE
Source of Information
ANY Any source is admissible — no restriction on source under Section 108
VAT precedent: Even Proper Officer or trade competitors were valid sources
IMMUNITY RA not answerable for source in judicial review — only answerable for satisfaction as to 'prejudice'
Obligation on RA
NOT EVERY Every piece of information need not be acted upon
MUST Show independent application of mind → conclusion that prejudice is caused
PRUDENCE Information received must be attended to — not ignored; noting on file required
RTI File noting of RA comes within scope of Right to Information Act
Examples
DGARM/EIU intelligence set aside by AA citing bona fide purchases → prejudice = failure to consider factors on record → revision
Gap in export invoice value vs adjusted export turnover → non-repatriation ~2 years → unless approved u/r 96A → GST on inter-State supplies (export of services fails s.2(6) IGST)
21.6 — Request from Commissioner of State Tax
CROSS-GOVERNMENT MECHANISM — CENTRAL ↔ STATE/UT CROSS-REFERRAL FOR REVISIONARY PROCEEDINGS
Order by Central Govt Officer
✗ CANNOT State/UT Revisionary Authority cannot take up revision
MUST Commissioner of State/UT Tax → send 'request' to Central Govt Revisionary Authority
Order by State/UT Govt Officer
✗ CANNOT Central Govt Revisionary Authority cannot take up revision
MUST Commissioner of Central Tax → send 'request' to State/UT Revisionary Authority
Nature of 'Request'
NOT NEEDED Prior confirmation that order is prejudicial is NOT necessary to make a request
This exchange = facility to ensure prejudice is not left unattended due to unavailability of cross-empowerment
LEGISLATIVE CRAFT Fosters coordination between Central & State/UT administrations
Chain Use of Exchange
Revisionary proceedings in one case → same issue → forms basis for request in another case
Requesting Commissioner may share all details to assist RA in reaching findings and proceeding if satisfied
21.7 — Resolution of Conflict with Section 107(2)
REVIEW [S.107(2)] vs REVISION [S.108] — CRITICAL DISTINCTIONS; NO OVERLAP
Review — S.107(2)
TIME LIMIT 6 months from date of order
NATURE ADMINISTRATIVE proceeding — NOT quasi-judicial
PURPOSE Only to identify Revenue is aggrieved → direct PO to approach AA; NOT to alter order
TAXPAYER Rights NOT adversely affected → no opportunity to taxpayer required
LIMIT Does NOT permit 'further inquiry'; no new demand in appellate proceedings [s.107(11)]
Revision — S.108
TIME LIMIT 3 YEARS from date of order to initiate + hear + pass revisionary orders
NATURE QUASI-JUDICIAL — can adversely affect taxpayer's rights
POWERS (i) Stay operation + (ii) Pass substitutionary orders → taxpayer entitled to hearing
RVN1 MUST State: (i) points of prejudice, (ii) nature of further inquiry, (iii) extent of proposed alteration
ALLOWS "Such further inquiry as may be necessary"
Conclusion
✗ NOT OVERLAP S.107(2) proceedings NOT to be treated as overlapping with S.108 proceedings
These are distinct remedies — separate purposes, separate timelines, separate natures
21.8 — Call for and Examine
POWER TO CALL FOR AND EXAMINE — SCOPE; TAXPAYER'S ROLE; LIMITS ON RA; PROCEDURE AFTER EXAMINATION
Scope — What Can Be Called
AT LARGE Entire file — correspondence during inquiry, notice, reply, all documents — whether or not relied upon
The order itself + everything before it is open to RA
Taxpayer's Role
NOT REQUIRED Taxpayer NOT involved until notice in RVN1 is issued
Proceedings are NOT pre-emptive — take effect only AFTER decision/order is concluded by PO
Limits on RA
✗ CANNOT Act entirely on information alone → must examine all available material on record
FATAL Mechanical invocation or acting under dictation/compulsion → taints entire proceedings
COURTS Have not hesitated to strike down entire proceedings if jurisdictional validity not established at first step
Procedure After Examination
NO ACTION If no further action warranted → reasoned file noting (not Speaking Order) sufficient
ONCE RVN1 Law is 'set in motion' → must conclude with Speaking Order
PRE-RVN1 Proceedings dropped by internal file noting on file
21.9 — Prejudicial to Interests of Revenue
PREJUDICIAL TO INTERESTS OF REVENUE — MEANING; WHAT IS & IS NOT PREJUDICE; EXAMPLES
What Prejudice IS NOT
✗ NOT RVN1 cannot be issued merely because outcome is favourable to taxpayer
✗ NOT Every favourable order cannot ipso facto be 'prejudicial to Revenue'
✗ NOT Mere reduction in Revenue is no reason to invoke exceptional jurisdiction
What Prejudice IS
DEFINED 'Unjustified disfavour' to Revenue — misapplication of law, misunderstanding of facts, or omission in inquiry
NOTE Even demand-confirming order can be prejudicial if tax rate confirmed was lower than correct rate
BENCHMARK RA considers broad industry-agnostic + industry-specific issues as comparison benchmark
Prejudice EXISTS — Examples
AA allowing credit note adjustment without verifying s.34 circumstances in each case → prejudice
PO sanctioning export-of-services refund without establishing place of supply outside India → prejudice
AA restoring registration after 3 years on equity grounds → unclaimed credit s.16(4) → prejudice
NO Prejudice — Examples
PO sanctioned refund per formula, within limitation, credit matched → no prejudice → no revision
AA dismissed dept appeal on interest on transition credit (no statutory provision) → no prejudice
PO dropped demand on alternate supplier confirmation → no prejudice
Interest not paid on belated returns (no underlying order), ASMT-10 but no SCN/order, Landowners not paying GST (no order) → no revision in all these cases
21.10 — Illegal (Decision or Order)
ILLEGALITY AS GROUND FOR REVISION — MEANING; DISTINCTION FROM IMPRUDENT INTERPRETATION; EXAMPLES
What 'Illegal' Means
DEFINED Untenable interpretation of law — NOT merely another way of interpreting; impossible and impermissible reading
Also covers erroneous understanding of facts
CRITICAL Illegality must be in the RESULTANT decision or order — not just in the reasoning process
Erroneous ≠ Illegal Resultant Order
NUANCE Erroneous interpretation of law in exemption granted on non-taxable transaction → untenable interpretation, BUT resultant order NOT illegal
Interpretation accepted in one period ≠ binding precedent; Revenue may deviate in later periods
Examples — Illegal → Revision
PO dropped Cess demand (bona fide mistake: credit of Cess under IGST column) → extra-legislative concession → illegal → prejudice
AA admitted time-barred appeal beyond 30 days even after Circular 157/13/2021-GST → fatally time-barred → illegal → prejudice
PO allowed transition credit refund u/s 140 but computation contrary to express formula in rules → illegal → prejudice
21.11 — Improper (Decision or Order)
IMPROPER — INCONGRUOUS/INCOMPATIBLE WITH LAW; WIDER THAN ILLEGALITY; EXAMPLES
Definition
DEFINED Decision/order that is incongruous or incompatible with the law — even without being illegal
WIDE POWER Section 108 gives much latitude to RA to revise on ground of 'improper' — does not require illegality
Examples — Improper → Revision
AA dropped demand for reversal of transition credit via GSTR-3B → improper appreciation of facts + misapplication of law → prejudice
PO accepted refund in plain-paper application after 26 Sept 2019 (online RFD-1 implemented) → improper procedure contrary to master circular → prejudice
AA imposed penalty u/s 125 substituting PO's demand for max penalty u/s 129(1)(a) → improper understanding of appellate interference scope → prejudice
21.12 — Not Taken into Account Pre-Existing Factors
PRE-EXISTING FACTORS OMITTED — MEANING OF 'FACTORS'; CURATIVE POWERS OF RA; EXAMPLES
What are 'Factors'
BROAD Not only facts but also facts-in-issue — aspects of fact/law bearing on existence or non-existence of other facts impacting taxability
RULE All factors affecting the decision/order must be taken into consideration by PO
Taxpayer's Strategy — Double-Edged
CAUTION Omission raised in taxpayer's reply to notice → can itself become source of information for RA to initiate revision
RA can take taxpayer's rebuttal submissions as 'new disclosures' in further inquiry and overturn favourable order
RA's Wide / Curative Powers
CURATIVE Acts as curative powers for fatalities in notices → demand dropped in adjudication or first appeal
Powers extend to Executive Officer orders AND First Appellate Authority orders
Examples
AA dropped royalty demand on minor minerals — overlooked pending SAIL's case in SC → sub judice → prejudice
PO allowed 15% concessional penalty when payments made after SCN u/s 74 → improper application → prejudice
21.13 — Not Taken into Account Post-Arising Factors
POST-ARISING FACTORS — REMARKABLE POWER; 3-YEAR LIMIT; MATERIAL FACT REQUIREMENT; FACTOR MATRIX
What are Post-Arising Factors
REMARKABLE Factors that emerge SUBSEQUENTLY after the decision/order — within 3-year time limit — are permitted as basis for revision
Not limited to higher judiciary decisions — also includes retrospective changes in procedures
EXAMPLE Rule 96B (from 23 Mar 2020) → operates as restriction on refunds filed both after and pending before this date
'Material Fact' Restriction
RESTRICTED Post-arising factors must be 'material facts' — new court decisions/interpretations are NOT material facts
Superimposing new court/Apex Court interpretation = 're-doing' adjudication → beyond material facts → cannot authorise revision
Factor Matrix
FactorPre-existingPost-arising
FactsYESYES
Interpretation of FactsYESYES
LawYESNO*
Interpretation of LawYESNO*
Amendment to LawYESNA
Retrospective AmdtYESNA
CircularsYESNO*
Court DecisionsYESNO*
Apex Court DecisionYESNO*
*Superimposing these = re-doing adjudication → beyond 'material facts'
Safeguard & Purpose
SAFEGUARD Opportunity to be heard will be given → meets natural justice standards
Purpose: prevent prejudicial orders bringing windfall gains to adventurous taxpayers when all others discharged taxes lawfully
21.14 — Adverse Observation by CAG
CAG ADVERSE OBSERVATIONS — AUDIT OF TAX AUTHORITIES' RECORDS; DAP AS BASIS FOR REVISION
CAG's Jurisdiction
AUDIT OF Records of TAX AUTHORITIES — not directly of taxpayers
Taxpayer's records audited only as they appear on files of tax authorities
Effect of Adverse Observations
REMEDY Any adverse observation by CAG can be remedied by taking up revisionary proceedings
DAP Draft Audit Para (DAP) finalised by CAG after final discussions → must be carried in revisionary proceedings [Circular 1053/2/2017-CX dated 10 Mar 2017]
21.15 — Prerequisites to Invoke Powers
PREREQUISITES TO INVOKE SECTION 108 — BASIC (BOTH) + ADDITIONAL (AT LEAST ONE); 5-CONDITION TEST
BASIC — Both Must Exist
CONDITION 1 Prejudice to Revenue
CONDITION 2 + Affects INTERESTS of Revenue (something MORE than mere reduction in Revenue)
ADDITIONAL — At Least One
A Illegal
B Improper
C Not taken into account factors (pre-existing or post-arising material facts)
D Adverse observation by CAG
5-Condition Test for 'Interests of Revenue'
(a) Causes misapplication of statutory provisions
(b) Becomes a precedent for other taxpayers to rely upon
(c) Engenders continuation of such application
(d) Perpetuates leakage of tax revenue
(e) Is an incorrect application of plain words of statute
RVN1 Testing
TEST Every RVN1 must be tested: BASIC (both) + ADDITIONAL (at least one) present?
BURDEN Big burden on RA to demonstrate — RA's decision itself is appealable (only after conclusion of proceedings, not ad interim)
21.16 — Doctrine of Election
DOCTRINE OF ELECTION — SPECIFICITY IN RVN1; ONE ADDITIONAL REQUIREMENT ONLY; TAXPAYER TACTIC
Core Principle
DOCTRINE Choosing one ground implies all other grounds are manifestly not suited to be invoked
Applies to Revenue in all adversarial litigation and administrative functions; also to taxpayer's self-assessment & reply strategy
Application to RVN1
✗ CANNOT RA cannot 'cast the net wide' and allege MORE THAN ONE additional requirement
FATAL Multiple allegations = ambiguity = arbitrary = illegal; taxpayer unable to put forward defence
✗ CANNOT Non-specific allegation = no notice at all; 'Revised RVN1' cannot be issued in law
MUST RVN1 must explain basis for BOTH basic requirements + WHICH ONE additional requirement was most compelling
Taxpayer's Tactic
TACTIC Attentively pick up on 'slip between cup and lip' — error of election in RVN1 → challenge validity at threshold
BURDEN Once jurisdiction validly exercised → burden of proof is on RA to 'bring home' the allegation
21.17 — Stay Operation of Such Decision or Order
STAY ORDER — PRE-EMPTIVE POWER; TIMING; EFFECT; 3-MONTH GUIDELINE; CASCADING EFFECT
When & How Stay Passed
IMMEDIATELY Upon determination of prejudice — even BEFORE RVN1 is issued to taxpayer
Prevents taxpayer from 'retaining, receiving or appropriating' benefits of favourable order
GUIDELINE Stay order should be passed within 3 months from date of order (aligned with s.78/107(1) timelines)
Effect of Stay Order
EFFECT 1 Benefit of favourable order not available to taxpayer
EFFECT 2: Deferred for other tax periods involving similar issue until revisionary proceedings concluded
3-Month Timing Note
NOTE Stay after 3 months does NOT bar revisionary proceedings — statutory limitation is 3 YEARS
Taxpayer who already retained benefits → may need to 'cease and desist' prospectively
Cascading Effect
(i) Pending SCNs/inquiries adjudicated in line with new interpretation adopted in revisionary proceedings
(ii) New inquiries/notices for subsequent tax periods to avoid demands becoming time-barred u/s 73
21.18 — After Making Such Further Inquiry
FURTHER INQUIRY — MOST REMARKABLE ASPECT OF S.108; SOURCES; STRATEGIC WARNING FOR TAXPAYERS
Sources for Further Inquiry
SOURCE 1 Taxpayer's own reply to notices — where facts/law challenged — provides invaluable material to overturn orders that went in taxpayer's favour due to shortcomings in notice
SOURCE 2 RA suo motu identifies shortcomings in notices → inquires to set right prejudice caused to Revenue
Strategic Warning for Taxpayers
⚠ WARNING Too much information in SCN reply = ammunition for RA to overturn AO or AA orders in revisionary proceedings
SCOPE RA empowered to 'right every wrong' in earlier proceedings including before First Appellate Authority
TIME Must be speedily concluded within 3 years from date of said decision/order
21.19 — Immediately Stay Operation of Orders
PRE-EMPTIVE STAY — CONSEQUENTIAL EFFECTS ON REFUND ORDERS; TAXPAYER'S REMEDY
Consequential Effects — Refund Orders
(a) PO must issue notice of demand for erroneous refunds already sanctioned
(b) PO must reject PENDING refund applications of taxpayer
Taxpayer's Remedy Against Stay
✗ NO APPEAL No appeal can be preferred against stay order itself
REMEDY Appeal u/s 112 preferred only against FINAL orders in revisionary proceedings
ALTERNATIVE Validity of revisionary proceedings can be assailed in appeal before Appellate Tribunal
RATIONALE Allowing appeal against stay order would render pre-emptive power conferred by Legislature otiose
21.20 — Pass Such New Substitutionary Orders
SUBSTITUTIONARY ORDERS — APL-4 UNDER RULE 109B; SPEAKING ORDER; FURTHER APPEAL; RECOVERY
Nature of Substitutionary Orders
Based on 'further inquiry' + after considering taxpayer's reply to RVN1
FORM RA must also issue APL-4 under Rule 109B
NATURE Must be a Speaking Order → subject to further appeal u/s 107(1) or 112(1)
APL-4 and Recovery
APL-4 New demand order → recovery proceedings if further appeal not filed within time
Amount in APL-4 = 'final amount of demand confirmed' [Rule 109B] = 'amount payable' u/s 78
RECOVERY Recovery u/s 79 follows if no further appeal preferred
21.21 — Enhance (Said Decision or Order)
ENHANCE — UNIQUE POWER OF RA; ENTIRE ISSUE AT LARGE; LIMIT: NO NEW ISSUES
Comparison with AA
AA CANNOT 2nd Proviso to s.107(11) → AA does NOT have power to 'enhance' demand
RA CAN Section 108 → RA PERMITTED to 'enhance' the demand
NOTE Adjudicating authority cannot confirm demand more than amount in SCN
Scope & Limit
SCOPE Entire issue including investigation, adjudication, and first appeal is 'at large' before RA
LIMIT Must not take up 'new issues' not integral to the proceedings under revision — else legal impediment
21.22 — Modify (Said Decision or Order)
MODIFY — INCLUDES REDUCTION; TAXPAYER CAN INDIRECTLY ACCESS RA'S POWERS; THIN LINE vs APPEAL
Scope of Modification
Quantum of demand + other aspects: interpretation of place of supply, classification, etc.
NOTE Since 'enhance' is covered in 21.21, 'modify' primarily refers to REDUCTION of demand
Taxpayer's Indirect Route
PERMITTED No bar on reduction of demand in revisionary proceedings
ROUTE Taxpayer NOT barred from indirectly accessing RA's remedial powers to correct 'prejudicial fatalities' in PO's findings
CONDITION Must demonstrate that order's continuation is 'prejudicial to interests of revenue'
THIN LINE This is NOT a substitute for a time-barred appeal — must be carefully demonstrated
21.23 — Annul (Said Decision or Order)
ANNUL — COMPLETE SETTING ASIDE; PRE-CONDITION; PROSPECTIVE EFFECT ON SUBSEQUENT TAX PERIODS
Meaning of Annulment
NOT JUST Dropping demand — also setting right any interpretational fatalities
PRE-CONDITION Even to annul → must show continuation of decision/order is prejudicial to interests of Revenue
Prospective Impact
Interpretation fatality in order → if similar interpretation in subsequent periods → far greater loss to Revenue
PURPOSE Revisionary powers are remedial not just for immediate proceedings but for all future proceedings following similar interpretation
21.24 — Bar on Revisionary Proceedings
BAR ON REVISIONARY PROCEEDINGS — S.108(2); TIME LIMITS; APPEAL BAR; POINTS RAISED & DECIDED
Time Limits — Section 108(2)
Order TypeNot BeforeOuter Limit
Adjudication Order (PO)6 months from order date3 years from order date
Appellate Authority OrderNA3 years from order date
Re-start (stalled by appeal)—1 year from Appellate Order* (subject to 3-yr outer limit)
*if any point in AO was NOT raised & decided in Appeal — but cannot exceed outer limit of 3 years in clause (b)
Appeal Already Filed
BARRED When appeal already filed against order → revisionary proceedings barred on points raised & decided
OPEN Points NOT raised and decided in appeal → CAN be taken up in revisionary proceedings (within 3-year outer limit)
Period Excluded from 3 Years
Period of any stay order granted against revisionary orders in statutory appeal
Period of stay granted by Courts of Equity against RVN1 notice
21.25 — Mischievous Appeals
MISCHIEVOUS APPEALS — BARING REVISIONARY JURISDICTION; S.108(2) SAFEGUARDS; REAL MERITS
Nature of Mischief
TACTIC Taxpayer preferring appeal to bar RA's revisionary jurisdiction — even when not aggrieved or only nominally aggrieved
NOTE S.108(2) carefully crafted to prevent this tactic; AA/Tribunal can dismiss mischievous appeals
Real Merits of S.108(2)
(a) When appeal filed → revisionary proceedings barred
(b) When appeal disposed → points 'raised and decided' must be examined
(c) If those points NOT the ones causing prejudice → revisionary proceedings can proceed on OTHER undecided points
AA/Tribunal can DISMISS mischievous appeal if satisfied it is not genuine — and revisionary proceedings can then proceed
21.29 — Enhanced Remedy for Revenue
REVENUE'S DUAL REMEDY — APPEAL + REVISION; RISK OF OVERLAP; RVN1 CANNOT BE IN ABEYANCE
Two Available Remedies
S.107(2) Departmental review → appeal before First Appellate Authority
S.108 Revisionary proceedings → set right prejudice caused by any decision/order
Risk of Overlap
RISK Procedures under 107(2) and 108 may tend to overlap → risk of omission when overlap occurs
SUGGESTION Systematic portal procedure: after each order → (i) Acceptance (ii) Appeal (iii) Revision
RVN1 — Critical Notes
MUST RVN1 cannot be issued routinely — requires careful consideration of nature of prejudice
CANNOT RVN1 cannot be kept in abeyance → forfeiture of opportunity to remedy prejudice under s.108
NOTE If RVN1 NOT issued → call book time-extension benefit NOT available for revisionary proceedings
Time — Stay Period Exclusion
Period of stay granted against revisionary orders in statutory appeal (or by Courts of Equity against RVN1) is excluded from 3-year time limit for completion
21.30 — Checklist
STEP-BY-STEP CHECKLIST FOR REVISIONARY PROCEEDINGS — FINDINGS → STAY → RVN1 → INQUIRY → ORDER
STEP 1 — Findings in Order
ADVERSE Adverse findings → NO JURISDICTION for revision under s.108
FAVOURABLE Favourable findings → PREJUDICIAL → Proceed to Step 2
FAVOURABLE But lawfully so → NOT prejudicial → No action required
STEP 2(a) — Pass Stay Order
TIMING Pass BEFORE taxpayer retains, receives, appropriates benefits
GUIDELINE Pass within 3 months from date of order
CANNOT Stay cannot be vague — must be after careful consideration as basis could be called into question in later proceedings
STEP 2(b) — Issue RVN1
Point-wise / Issue-wise; select SPECIFIC reason for prejudice:
Illegal → Yes/No → to state how
Improper → Yes/No → to state how
Factors not taken into account → Yes/No → state which factors and how findings are prejudicial
CAG adverse observation → Yes/No → state DAP reference
STEP 2(c)/(d) — Inquiry & Limits
INQUIRY Issue detailed notice → collect response → reach point/issue-wise findings → pass Speaking Order: Enhance/Modify/Annul
NOTE Points NOT in RVN1 → taxpayer free to retain benefits on those points
CANNOT Adverse points/issues CANNOT be taken up in revision — would be without jurisdiction u/s 108
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 21 | Revisionary Proceedings ⇧ Index
These principles are for use in personal hearings before the Revisionary Authority, FAA, or GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the statutory source.
Principle 1 — Over-Disclosure in SCN Reply is Perilous [Para 21.1]
Section 108, CGST Act 2017 | Strategic Drafting Principle
Key Ratio / Principle
Reply to notice must contain that much information as is necessary for purposes of the proceedings at hand and up to the limits of authority before who is to consider the reply. Over enthusiasm can be perilous when the file is called for examination to determine whether prejudice is caused to Revenue in revisionary proceedings.
When to Use: When advising clients on how much to disclose in SCN reply — the same disclosures that defeat a demand in adjudication may become the ammunition for RA to initiate revision and overturn the favourable order.
Principle 2 — Administrative Function is NOT a Decision or Order [Para 21.2]
Section 108 read with Section 2(99), CGST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
Grant of authorization to conduct audit under Section 65 is not a 'decision or order' because it is an administrative function and not an adjudication function. Revisionary proceedings can only be initiated against a completed 'decision or order' — not against administrative acts or orders passed during pendency of proceedings.
When to Use: When challenging RA's jurisdiction where the act sought to be revised is administrative in nature. Also where RA attempts to intervene during pendency of proceedings — jurisdiction arises only AFTER decision/order is passed.
Principle 3 — Review is Administrative; Revision is Quasi-Judicial [Para 21.7]
Section 107(2) vs Section 108, CGST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
Review by Commissioner does not involve any quasi-judicial proceeding but only an administrative proceeding. However, 'revision' under Section 108 is a quasi-judicial proceeding as it could affect rights of taxpayer adversely — through power to stay operation of the order and pass substitutionary orders. Taxpayer is therefore entitled to opportunity of being heard. Notice in RVN1 must clearly state: (i) points of prejudice, (ii) nature of further inquiry, and (iii) extent of proposed alteration.
When to Use: When challenging revisionary proceedings on natural justice grounds — RVN1 notice is mandatory and must be specific. Also to resist any attempt by RA to treat its role as merely administrative.
Principle 4 — Mere Prejudice is Insufficient; 5-Condition Test for 'Interests of Revenue' [Para 21.15]
Section 108, CGST Act 2017 — Prerequisites to Invoke Powers
Key Ratio / Principle
Mere prejudice to Revenue is not sufficient — it must cause prejudice AND affect the interests of Revenue. For an order to be prejudicial to 'interests' of Revenue it must: (a) cause misapplication of statutory provisions, (b) become a precedent for other taxpayers to rely upon, (c) engender continuation of such application, (d) perpetuate leakage of tax revenue, and (e) be an incorrect application of the plain words of the statute. Every favourable order cannot ipso facto be treated as prejudicial — prejudice is 'unjustified disfavour' to Revenue.
When to Use: When RA invokes Section 108 merely because outcome of adjudication or appellate order is favourable to taxpayer — demand that the high threshold of 'interests of Revenue' be demonstrated with specificity against all 5 conditions.
Principle 5 — RVN1 Cannot be Vague; One Additional Requirement Only [Para 21.16]
Section 108, CGST Act 2017 — Doctrine of Election
Key Ratio / Principle
Revisionary Authority cannot 'cast the net wide' and allege more than one 'additional requirement'. Ambiguity in RVN1 makes it arbitrary and hence illegal because taxpayer is now unable to put forward a defence. Specificity is essential to validity of notice. Non-specific allegation is no notice at all. A 'revised RVN1' cannot be issued in law. RVN1 must explain the basis for both basic requirements and which ONE additional requirement was most compelling.
When to Use: Whenever RVN1 simultaneously invokes 'illegal' + 'improper' + 'factors not taken into account' in kitchen-sink fashion — challenge validity of notice at threshold; non-specific allegation = no notice at all.
Principle 6 — Courts Strike Down Proceedings Where Jurisdiction Not Established [Para 21.8]
Section 108, CGST Act 2017 — Judicial Review
Key Ratio / Principle
Courts have not hesitated to strike down entire proceedings if the first step required to invoke powers under this section — to demonstrate validity of jurisdiction exercised — are not brought home successfully where said decision or order is taken up for revisionary proceedings. If it is found that Revisionary Authority has mechanically invoked section 108 or acted under dictation or compulsion, it will taint the entire proceedings even though there may be some real findings involved.
When to Use: When filing Writ against revisionary proceedings — to invoke the principle of invalid exercise of jurisdiction; mechanical action or action under dictation is independently sufficient to void the entire proceedings.
Principle 7 — Post-Arising Court Decisions Cannot Authorise Revision [Para 21.13/21.15]
Section 108, CGST Act 2017 — Material Facts Doctrine
Key Ratio / Principle
Superimposing interpretation from court decisions or Apex Court decisions that arise subsequent to the said decision or order tantamounts to re-doing adjudication or appeal, which is beyond 'material facts' that can authorise revisionary proceedings. Post-arising court decisions, interpretations of law, and circulars are NOT valid grounds for revisionary proceedings — they are specifically excluded from the list of post-arising factors.
When to Use: When RA relies on a subsequent Apex Court/HC judgment or CBIC Circular (issued after the impugned order) to invoke Section 108 — challenge the jurisdictional basis as beyond material facts; superimposing such interpretation = re-doing adjudication.
Principle 8 — No Appeal Against Stay Order; Challenge Through Final Revisionary Order [Para 21.19]
Section 108 read with Section 112, CGST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
Taxpayers will be greatly prejudiced by such 'stay' order but no appeal can be preferred against such order. Appeal under Section 112 may be preferred only in respect of orders to be finally passed in these proceedings. Validity of revisionary proceedings can be assailed in appeal before Appellate Tribunal. To allow appeal against this stay order would render the pre-emptive power conferred on Revenue by the Legislature otiose.
When to Use: When taxpayer is aggrieved by stay order under Section 108 — clarify that remedy lies before Appellate Tribunal against final revisionary order; simultaneously pursue writ jurisdiction if stay causes irreparable prejudice pending revisionary proceedings.
Principle 9 — Reduction of Demand Permissible; Taxpayer's Indirect Route via Section 108 [Para 21.22]
Section 108, CGST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
Since there is no bar on reduction of demand in revisionary proceedings, taxpayer would NOT be barred from indirectly accessing remedial powers of Revisionary Authority under Section 108 to correct any 'prejudicial fatalities' in the findings reached by the Proper Officer. If reduction of demand were barred, the law ought to have stated so explicitly. Since it is not barred explicitly, it is permitted — provided the basic premise of decision or order causing prejudice to interests of revenue is established.
When to Use: Innovative argument where appeal is time-barred but the impugned order contains a clear interpretational error — if it can be shown the error itself is prejudicial to interests of Revenue, invoke Section 108 indirectly for correction; a thin but real line from a time-barred appeal.
Principle 10 — Points NOT in RVN1 Cannot be Revised; Adverse Points Are Without Jurisdiction [Para 21.30]
Section 108, CGST Act 2017 — Checklist
Key Ratio / Principle
Where notice in RVN1 does not make reference to any point or issue, taxpayer is free to retain, receive or appropriate benefits of favourable order that are NOT included in the points or issues taken up in revisionary proceedings. Adverse points or issues in respect of order under revision cannot be taken up in revisionary proceedings even if there were something illegal or improper in said decision or order, as it would be without jurisdiction under Section 108.
When to Use: When RA attempts to expand scope of revision beyond what was mentioned in RVN1, or when RA attempts to revise adverse (unfavourable to taxpayer) findings — both are without jurisdiction; confine proceedings strictly to points stated in RVN1.
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Chartered Accountants, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 21 — Revisionary Proceedings | Section 108 GST Act
⚖️ Chapter 22 — Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT)

⚖️ Chapter 22 — Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT)

GST Act 2017 | Sections 109–114 — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
22.1 Relevance in Replying to Notices
22.2 Creatures of the Statute
22.3 Structure of GSTAT
22.4 Full Bench or Division Bench
22.5 Single Member Bench
22.6 Reference to Third Member
22.7 Appealable Orders
22.8 Appeal Before Erroneous Bench
22.9 Types of Appeals
22.10 Limitation
22.11 Pre-Deposit
22.12 Statement of Facts
22.13 Grounds of Appeal
22.14 Defective Appeals
22.15 Paper-Book
22.16 Departmental Representative
22.17 Limitations in Powers of GSTAT
22.18 Power of Inquiry by GSTAT
22.19 Fruits of Earlier Preparations
22.20 Law of Precedent
22.21 Conclusion
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
22.1 — Relevance in Replying to Notices
GSTAT — WHY ITS STRUCTURE MATTERS WHILE DRAFTING SCN REPLIES
GSTAT — Final Forum for Facts
KEY GSTAT is the LAST forum where questions of FACT will be examined
By the time notice reaches GSTAT — facts should be well established, allegations and evidence brought into the open
BEYOND GSTAT Questions of LAW can be carried to High Court and Supreme Court — but NOT questions of fact
Strategic Implication
REALITY 'Points of disagreement' on facts are clearly exposed at GSTAT — the final determination that remains is also exposed
Understanding structure and contours of GSTAT's inquiry powers is ESSENTIAL to good SCN reply drafting from the very first stage
22.2 — Creatures of the Statute
ALL AUTHORITIES INCLUDING GSTAT ARE CREATURES OF STATUTE — JURISDICTION LIMITS; WHAT GSTAT CAN AND CANNOT DO
All Authorities — Creatures of Statute
RULE All authorities until GSTAT — including GSTAT itself — are created by the statute
✗ CANNOT Sit in judgment over the constitutional vires (validity) of the statute itself
What They CAN Do
✓ CAN Entertain questions about validity of ACTIONS by instrumentalities of the State
✓ CAN Examine incongruity of rules with provisions of the statute
✓ CAN Examine incoherent circulars contrary to plain words of the statute
What They CANNOT Do
✗ CANNOT Question validity of the Act or the Rules themselves
GSTAT not empowered to sit in judgment over constitutional validity of sections 16(2) and 16(4)
✗ CANNOT Condone delay beyond maximum condonable duration in section 112(6) — even for good and sufficient reasons
Practical Implication
KEY Q Whether the right questions have been brought up in the manner in which the notice has been carried until GSTAT?
Although notice is not 'at large' before GSTAT — these insights greatly illuminate approach that ought to be taken in replying to notices at all stages
22.3 — Structure of GSTAT
GSTAT STRUCTURE — NATIONAL TRIBUNAL; PRINCIPAL BENCH + STATE BENCHES; COMPOSITION; TRANSFERS
Principal Bench — New Delhi
Finance Act 2023 One Principal Bench at New Delhi
4 Members: President + 1 Judicial Member + 2 Technical Members (Centre + State)
S.114 All benches under administrative and financial superintendence of President at New Delhi
Anti-Profiteering Finance (No.2) Act 2024: Principal Bench to preside over anti-profiteering adjudication u/s 171 until sunset clause notified
State Benches
States may request u/s 109(4) → Bench designated at that State by President
NOT Benches of respective States/UT — they are 'extension counters' of Principal Bench
4 Members: 2 Judicial Members + 2 Technical Members (Centre + State)
Multiple State Benches possible within one State — based on volume of appeals, range of issues, location of litigants
National Tribunal Character
PURPOSE Harmonious interpretation of law across the country
PREVENTS States developing independent jurisprudence as seen in AAR structure
Ensures delivery of justice to taxpayers not located in State Capital cities
Transfer of Members
GENERAL Members may be transferred across different States in different State Benches
EXCEPTION Technical Member (State administration) → transferred only between Benches within the SAME State
22.4 — Full Bench or Division Bench
FULL BENCH / DIVISION BENCH — 2 MEMBERS AT ANY TIME; BENCH 'A' AND BENCH 'B'; BIAS CONCERNS
Division Bench — Composition
RULE Principal Bench and State Bench always presided by 2 Members at any time
Since full Bench has 4 Members → likely to be Bench 'A' and Bench 'B' conducting proceedings simultaneously
REFERRED AS 'Division Bench'
Bias Concern — Technical Member
OPEN Q Whether orders from State dept must be presided by Bench with Technical Member from Central dept (or vice versa) — President's decision awaited
SAFEGUARD All Members superannuate from GSTAT after tenure u/s 110(9) → no concerns about bias in justice dispensation
Technical Member brings decades of qualifying experience in VAT/Central Excise departments — adds value in those areas
22.5 — Single Member Bench
SINGLE MEMBER BENCH — S.109(8); THREE CONDITIONS; DEMAND TYPES; OPEN QUESTIONS
Three Conditions — ALL Must Exist
(i) Demand involves tax, credit, fine, fee or penalty
(ii) Amount is LESS than Rs.50 lakhs
(iii) No question of law is involved
Demand Types — SM Powers
DemandRemarks
TaxOutput tax incl. RCM; refund of tax
CreditITC wrongly availed/utilised; refund of credit
FineRedemption fine u/s 130(2)
FeeLate fee u/s 47
PenaltyPenalty under various provisions
Open Questions & Transfer
OPEN Cancellation of registration and best judgment orders NOT expressly included in SM's powers
OPEN Refund of output tax/credit may be beyond SM's powers but could be included — clarity awaited from President's listing instructions
TRANSFER Appeal before Division Bench discovered to be within SM jurisdiction → Division Bench may transfer without entertaining; but no bar on Division Bench hearing such appeals
TACTIC If Appellant raises a question of law — even in SM cases — SM would hear the appeal; also depends on whether Appellant raises question of law
22.6 — Reference to Third Member
REFERENCE TO THIRD MEMBER — DIFFERENCE OF OPINION; MAJORITY VIEW; LARGER BENCH CONCEPT
Reference Procedure
WHO President may refer appeal to be heard by a Larger Bench
Within same State Bench → 1 Member hears the point(s) of difference between first-hearing Members
ABSENT If such Members unavailable → point(s) of difference put before 1 Member of State Bench in another State
After Reference Decision
OUTCOME Combined majority view = the decision
Bench that FIRST heard appeal passes the operative portion of the order
LIMIT S.109 limits reference to Third Member only — does not conceive a full 3-Member Larger Bench situation as under earlier tax regime
22.7 — Appealable Orders
APPEALABLE ORDERS BEFORE GSTAT — FAA + RA ORDERS; POS → PRINCIPAL BENCH; RA'S TWO ISSUES
Orders Appealable to GSTAT
FAA Orders of FAA u/s 107(11) → appealable before GSTAT
RA Orders of Revisionary Authority u/s 108(1) → appealable before GSTAT
POS INVOLVED If Place of Supply (POS) is one of the issues → entire order must be carried to PRINCIPAL BENCH at New Delhi (not State Bench)
Subject to restrictions in s.121 — appeals permitted on question of law or question of fact or both
RA Orders — Two Issues
(a) Exercise of JURISDICTION to take up a matter in revisionary proceedings
(b) FINDING reached in revisionary proceedings
22.8 — Appeal Filed Before Erroneous Bench
ERRONEOUS FORUM — NOT FATAL; S.14 LIMITATION ACT; EXCLUSION vs EXTENSION; 5 INGREDIENTS
Filing Before Erroneous Forum
NOT FATAL Filing appeal before erroneous forum does not destroy the appellate remedy
Duty of the forum: either redirect appeal to correct forum OR advise Appellant to file before correct forum
CAUTION Appellant must NOT 'withdraw' appeal from erroneous forum — must only request 'direction to transfer' to correct forum
Section 14 — Limitation Act
S.14 Limitation in suspended animation when bona fide litigant prosecutes rights before forum that cannot entertain for want of jurisdiction
RIGHT S.14 is a right — NOT a relaxation (unlike S.5 which is relief for 'sufficient cause')
DISTINCTION S.5 'extends' limitation; S.14 'excludes' time from computation of limitation
MANDATORY Condonation of delay (S.5) = discretionary; Exclusion (S.14) = mandatory
5 Ingredients of S.14
(i) Remedy sought must be the SAME
(ii) Both forums must have same authority and jurisdiction
(iii) Error was bona fide and satisfactorily explained
(iv) First appeal filed within statutory limitation
(v) Parties to the lis are the same
Key Cases & Limits
ITC Ltd. v. CCE (1997) 97 ELT 456 — Time lost in incorrect forum excluded when appeal came before correct forum
CST v. Parson Tools AIR 1970 All. 428 — S.14 extended to taxation; includes authorities with 'trappings' of a Court
LIMIT Writ remedy dismissed → cannot claim S.14 exclusion in statutory appeal unless Courts allowed it at dismissal
GST Time lost in rectification u/s 64(2)/s.161/representations to GSTC → CANNOT be excluded from limitation u/s 107(1)
22.9 — Types of Appeals
TYPES OF STATUTORY APPEALS BEFORE GSTAT — APL 5/6; TAXPAYER vs REVENUE vs EITHER
Statutory Appeal Types
SectionWho FilesForm
112(1)By TaxpayerAPL 5
112(4)By RevenueAPL 5
112(5)By EitherAPL 6
Note: Refer para 15.4 for details and timelines of this remedy
GSTAT as Final Fact-Finding Forum
GSTAT Final forum to examine facts and place on record the FINAL POSITION on facts
WARNING Facts taken on record by GSTAT CANNOT be denied in later proceedings
RISK Omission to dispute misplaced assertions = fait accompli — these become the facts on which all further questions of law are examined
22.10 — Limitation
LIMITATION — COMMENCEMENT; TIME LIMITS; CIRCULAR 224's 'U-TURN'; PRE-DEPOSIT BEFORE LIMITATION STARTS
Commencement of Limitation
RODO 2019 Appeals to GSTAT commence on date President enters office [Central GST (Ninth RODO) Order, 2019 dated 3 Dec 2019]
Circular 132/2/2020 No precipitative action by Revenue in the interregnum — taxpayer must inform Revenue about decision to rely on this circular
Time Limits
TAXPAYER 3 months — extendable by further 3 months on sufficient cause u/s 112(6)
REVENUE 6 months — no outer limit [proposed 2024 amendment: Revenue must file application showing 'sufficient cause' for belated appeal]
CROSS-OBJECTIONS 45 days limitation
Circular 224 — 'U-Turn'
CIRCULAR 224 Stunning 'U-turn' — requires taxpayer to IMMEDIATELY declare intention to appeal via undertaking before jurisdictional PO u/s 79
Also requires pre-deposit on Common Portal via "Services → Ledgers → Payment towards demand" — independent of APL 5
CONTRARY Requirement to deposit BEFORE limitation expires is contrary to prevailing jurisprudence — until lapse of limitation, nothing in impugned order can be given effect [Ramlal v. Rewa Coalfields Ltd.]
Revenue's Condonation
S.112(6) References to sub-sections (1) and (5) → Revenue's appeal may be condoned indefinitely — "wheels of State turn slowly"
2024 Amendment "Application under sub-section (3)" now included in condonation scope in s.112(6)
Taxpayer welcome to OPPOSE Revenue's belated appeal application where cause adduced appears insufficient
22.11 — Pre-Deposit
PRE-DEPOSIT u/s 112(8) — FORM APL-5; ECrL; RULE 142(2B); 20%→10% AMENDMENT; PENALTY-ONLY CASES
When & How Pre-Deposit
S.112(8) Required 'to file' appeal — but when limitation not yet commenced → no occasion for taxpayer to pre-deposit in anticipation
FORM Prescribed form = APL 5 (NOT DRC 3)
ECrL Pre-deposit can be made from ECrL balance [Circular 172/4/2022-GST dated 6 Jul 2022 para 6, esp. due to s.41-New]
Rule 142(2B) — New Procedure
Applicable to all appeals filed before AA where pre-deposit made via DRC 3 — requires pre-deposit in anticipation of GSTAT appeal to be deposited on Common Portal
Applies to amounts deposited: (i) voluntarily to prevent recovery (ii) pursuant to recovery via DRC13 (iii) pursuant to Court directions
NOTE DRC 3 was makeshift arrangement for manual APL1/5 filings — now superseded
20% → 10% Amendment
AMENDED S.112(8)(b) — pre-deposit reduced from 20% to 10% of disputed demand
ALREADY PAID MORE If already deposited more than 10%: (i) apply DRC 3A for whole amount + pleadings in APL5, OR (ii) apply DRC 3A for 10% + file RFD1 for excess + ensure RFD2 issued
PIQUANT SITUATION Extant law had 20%; Circular 224 already in force; amendment yet to come into effect simultaneously across all State GST Acts
Penalty-Only Demand
SPECIAL RULE Proposed proviso to s.112(8): (i) pre-deposit of 10% of penalty payable (ii) IN ADDITION to pre-deposit 'payable' under proviso to s.107(6)
Revenue's pre-deposit: No outer limit → interesting if s.112(6) allows indefinite condonation of Revenue's delay
22.12 — Statement of Facts
STATEMENT OF FACTS (SoF) — APL 5/6 TABULAR FORM; BREVITY IS KEY; FACTS vs RELEVANT FACTS
Form and Contents
FORM APL 5 or APL 6 — tabular form + SoF + GoA + verified by Appellant
SoF IS Appellant's position on facts to the extent considered RELEVANT to the case
SoF IS NOT A history lesson for the Bench. Not about Appellant's life story. Not a place for disputed facts — those are Grounds
Brevity is the Test
CONTRA INDICATOR Very bulky appeal document = contra indicator — implies Revenue has a good case that grievance cannot be readily brought out
SIGNAL Elaborate SoF implies proceedings until this stage have not gone well — parties haven't reached consensus on facts
RULE Facts will be clear when Appellant traces history from pre-notice stage — but must be BRIEF
Facts vs Relevant Facts
MUST SoF must bring out only RELEVANT facts — not all facts
PROCLIVITY Appellants tend to be very wordy — perhaps to impress someone, which is usually NOT the Bench
Refer paras 12.10-12.12, 13.15-13.18 and 16.11 for deliberation on facts vs relevant facts distinction
22.13 — Grounds of Appeal
GROUNDS OF APPEAL (GoA) — REASONS NOT ARGUMENTS; 3×3 MATRIX OF ERRORS; PLEADING IS ESSENCE
What GoA IS and IS NOT
GoA IS Reasons for Appellant's grievance against order of FAA/RA — the 'point of law' on which Appellant is aggrieved
GoA IS NOT Arguments. GoA is not to contain detailed explanation of ratio in case laws
GoA IS NOT Reasons why or how FAA/RA went wrong — that is for arguments at hearing
GoA — 3×3 Error Matrix
ON LAW (a) Wrong provisions applied OR (b) Right provisions wrongly applied OR (c) Wrong provisions wrongly applied
ON FACTS (a) Wrong facts considered OR (b) Right facts wrongly considered OR (c) Wrong facts wrongly considered
Key Principles for GoA
PLEADING Pleading is the essence — expose foundational error → outcome is inevitable
GSTAT (and AA) is obliged to reach a FINDING on each ground — failure of earlier authorities glossed over merges into GSTAT order
CANNOT Relief cannot be allowed by GSTAT on grounds that could have been urged but NOT urged in GoA
RULE 112 Embargo on additional evidence — NOT additional grounds; selection of grounds is still free
GoA Strategy
APPROACH Being DISCREET is suggested — substantive grounds convey quality; exhaustive grounds convey something else — impressions matter
Case law citations and ratio extraction belong in Paper-book and Synopsis — NOT in GoA
DO NOT PLEAD Matters GSTAT cannot preside over (save for HC/SC); matters that are of no avail
22.14 — Defective Appeals
DEFECTIVE APPEALS — RULE 110(4); FATAL vs NON-FATAL DEFECTS; LIMITATION IMPACT
Rule 110(4) — Key Amendment
From 10 Jul 2024 Limitation counted AFTER defects noted by Registry are cleared — time spent in discovery, intimation and resolution of defects → appeal could pass limitation and be rendered non est
PARALLEL Like Rule 90(3) proviso for RFD1 → time between RFD1 defects and RFD3 intimation excludable (from 18 May 2021); administrative delay at Registry cannot prejudice Appellant's substantive appeal remedy
Fatal Defects
FATAL Appeal not in prescribed Form (APL5/6/7)
FATAL Error in title of Respondent
FATAL Appeal not verified
FATAL Pre-deposit deficient or omitted
FATAL Mandatory attachments (self-certified order) omitted
FATAL Appeal past limitation (per date of service admitted)
FATAL Application for condonation of delay omitted
FATAL Affidavit in support of CoD application
Non-Fatal Defects
NOT FATAL Pre-deposit made earlier (recovery or conditional stay)
NOT FATAL Statement of facts contains misstatements
NOT FATAL Grounds of appeal need amendment
NOT FATAL Prayer not in harmony with pleadings
NOT FATAL Paper-book not submitted
NOT FATAL Authorization not included in appeal memo
NOT FATAL Documents in support of affidavit
Tactical Notes
TACTIC Defects in REVENUE's appeal can operate as important defence for Appellant — "what is good for the goose must be good for the gander"
REGISTRAR Determining appeals as defective is itself a question meriting consideration by GSTAT — Registrar cannot stand on same plane as Members of the Bench
Right to appeal is substantive — nature of defects (fatal vs non-fatal) must be carefully examined
22.15 — Paper-Book
PAPER-BOOK — WITHIN 30 DAYS; CONTENTS; BREVITY; DIGITAL DIRECTION
Contents & Timing
WITHIN 30 DAYS Of filing appeal — Appellant to submit paper-book in sufficient copies
May include: notice, reply, AO, FAA/RA order, documentary rebuttal evidence already submitted, new material
AVOID Tendency to submit documents in multiple volumes routinely even when not relevant to GoA grounds
Key Principles
RULE With paper-book available, APL 5/6 has no excuse to be burdened with unnecessary Annexures and Exhibits
SoF and GoA must be brief — long-winded appeal document is NOT a test of high-quality preparation
SKILL Bring out the central issue in shortest possible time — one hearing may be all that is allowed (few minutes to as much as Bench permits)
DIRECTION APL 5/6 being online → digitized paper-book likely to be prescribed — paperless paper-book is the direction
22.16 — Departmental Representative
DEPARTMENTAL REPRESENTATIVE — WHO IS RESPONDENT; RANK; INTERNAL ADMINISTRATION
Who is the Respondent
APPEAL vs FAA ORDER Respondent = Proper Officer who passed the ADJUDICATION ORDER (not FAA itself)
APPEAL vs RA ORDER Respondent = Revisionary Authority — whose notice in RVN1 contains the grounds assailed
Rank & Administration
Centre: Rank of Superintendent to Commissioner
State: Rank of Assistant or Deputy Commissioner
NOTE Matter of internal administration — NOT questionable by Appellant
22.17 — Limitations in Powers of Appellate Tribunal
GSTAT'S LIMITATIONS — CANNOT ENHANCE; CANNOT 'RE-DO'; LATEST JUDICIAL POSITION ALWAYS APPLIED
Cannot Enhance Demand
✗ CANNOT GSTAT cannot 'enhance' the demand — only demand confirmed in FAA/RA order is before GSTAT
ONLY RA Only Revisionary Authority can 'enhance' the demand [compare s.107 vs s.108 vs s.113]
4 Conditions for GSTAT to Support
(a) Shows intervention by GSTAT is warranted/justified
(b) Limits relief sought to pleadings in GoA
(c) Evidence adduced for examination to reach findings on facts-in-issue
(d) Discusses prevailing legal position for applicability to facts
Cannot Re-Do; Omissions Fatal
✗ CANNOT GSTAT cannot 're-do' the proceedings assailed
INCURABLE Omission of material grounds incurable by GSTAT unless amendment of GoA sought, opposed and allowed
FACT Facts left undisputed must NOT be overturned
What GSTAT CAN Do
LIBERTY Apply the most current and up-to-date position of law based on LATEST judicial authorities applicable to given facts
✗ CANNOT Decide based on lack of updated information of judicial authorities rendered as on date of consideration
Decision of GSTAT subject to further appeal on questions of law u/s 117 before HC; or u/s 118 before SC; + HC's judicial review powers
22.18 — Power of Inquiry by Appellate Tribunal
S.111(2) — INHERENT POWERS FROM CPC S.151; WIDE POWERS TABLE; SCOPE LIMITS
Powers — s.111(2) + CPC s.151
PowerPurpose
Summon & examine on oathValidate unestablished assertions — NOT to investigate new demands
Require discovery of documentsCall for contemporaneous documents to evidence probative value of assertions
Receive evidence on affidavitEntertain evidence with risk of perjury — without elaborate fact-finding (unjust enrichment, cost of ops, export realization date, etc.)
Requisition public recordsTake judicial notice of assertions before other regulators: RERA, Sub-Registrar, Income-tax, MCA, etc.
Commission examination of witnessDirect private deposition of CA, RV, or other certificate issuers
Dismiss for default or ex parteDismiss defective appeals or appeals marked defective by Registry
Setting aside dismissal/ex parteRestore dismissed appeals, rectify per incuriam orders, transfer to SM, early hearing applications, batch disposal
Any other matterImpose costs, issue directions, make reference request to President
Scope Limits
✗ CANNOT GSTAT (or AA) cannot expand scope of notice nor improve grounds raised in the notice
APPEALABLE GSTAT orders subject to: (i) judicial review for impropriety and (ii) statutory appeal as revision petition before Division Bench of HC u/s 117
Alteration of nature of demand in notice during appellate proceedings = question of law → GSTAT's decision not final on this
22.19 — Fruits of Earlier Preparations
FRUITS OF EARLIER PREPARATIONS — QUALITY OF SCN REPLY YIELDS RESULTS AT GSTAT; ADMISSIONS CANNOT BE RETRACTED
How Earlier Prep Yields Fruits
GSTAT STAGE Air is either cleared of all misunderstanding on facts OR exact area of misunderstanding on facts is crystallised
Application of law to facts would either have been: canvassed in notice → considered by FAA → or reconsidered in revisionary proceedings → now before GSTAT
Greatest Peril
PERIL Imprudent preparation in reply to notice OR over-enthusiastic reply exposing facts beyond those in notice → only confirmed at GSTAT stage
ADMISSIONS Admissions by taxpayer — whether under misinformation or failure to dispute erroneous allegations — CANNOT be retracted at GSTAT
RULE GSTAT obliged to render decision based on 'undisputed facts' — will NOT launch into fresh investigation
22.20 — Law of Precedent
LAW OF PRECEDENT AT GSTAT — NATIONAL BINDING FORCE; PER INCURIAM; AAR; ALLIED LAWS; PARI MATERIA
GSTAT as National Tribunal
BINDING Decisions of GSTAT in other States = binding precedent — all Proper Officers in all States must follow
DISAGREEMENT Bench differing from earlier decision must determine the point of difference and expressly disagree — later decision then binding
PER INCURIAM Decision without considering earlier binding decision = per incuriam → not binding force for that omission
FAA and AAR Decisions
FAA Decisions of FAA NOT binding on GSTAT
AAR Decisions of AARs have NO binding force — advance ruling is a 'bargain' that offers certainty NOT necessarily accuracy; not even binding on distinct persons of same applicant
TWIST GSTAT not bound to follow AARs but WILL impose AAR ruling's interpretation against the applicant who obtained it
Allied Laws & Earlier Regime
BINDING Allied law decisions binding on interpretation of law referred in GST
LIMITED Earlier tax regime decisions = limited binding force — only when statutory provisions shown to be pari materia
NOTE GST has deliberately deviated from using pari materia expressions used in earlier regime — deliberate departure cannot be equated with materially different expressions
EXAMPLES CST Act inter-State sale decisions ≠ binding on POS u/s 10 IGST; VAT 'burden of proof' decisions ≠ binding on s.16(2) read with s.155 CGST
CJEU & Conclusion on Precedent
CJEU Force of CJEU decisions on GST proceedings is doubted with detailed reasons
CAUTION Do not assume forceful interpretation will be accepted unless it can be shown the rendering forum has legal force over GSTAT
SKILL Choice of precedent to rely upon is skill — burdening paper-book with 200 case laws does not guarantee favourable outcome
22.21 — Conclusion
CONCLUSION — GST NOTICES ARE A MARATHON; ALL NOTICES REACH GSTAT; FIRST STEP DETERMINES OUTCOME
Marathon — Not a Sprint
REALITY Handling GST notices is often assumed to be a short sprint — this deliberation exposes the MARATHON nature of the exercise
RISK Detours are possible if approach in pre-notice stage of proceedings is without full view of the lifespan of the notice
DESTINY ALL notices will reach GSTAT where they meet their end — who this favours depends on the FIRST STEP taken at self-assessment stage
Key Reminders
CANNOT GSTAT cannot be seen acting under compulsion or dictation
OMISSION Omission to rely on binding authorities due to lack of information cannot prejudice Appellant — GSTAT will furnish the interpretation from binding authorities
WISDOM It takes more than innocence to persuade GSTAT to do justice — the first step at self-assessment stage matters most
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 22 | Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) ⇧ Index
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings before GSTAT, FAA, and during filing of appeals. State them confidently and cite the statutory source.
Principle 1 — GSTAT is the Last Forum for Facts; Questions of Law Alone Survive [Para 22.1]
Section 112, CGST Act 2017 | General Appellate Jurisdiction
Key Ratio / Principle
Appellate Tribunal or GSTAT is the last forum where questions of fact will be examined. By the time the notice reaches here, facts should be well established and allegations and their evidence brought out into the open. Questions of law can be carried further to High Court and Supreme Court but NOT questions of fact. Understanding the structure and contours of inquiry that Appellate Tribunal is empowered to carry out are essential.
When to Use: At every stage from SCN reply onwards — to keep focus on establishing facts correctly from the beginning. Undisputed facts at GSTAT cannot be reversed. Facts must be established at the earliest stage.
Principle 2 — GSTAT Cannot Question Constitutional Validity of Statute; Cannot Condone Delay Beyond S.112(6) [Para 22.2]
Section 109, Section 112(6), CGST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
All authorities until the Appellate Tribunal are creatures of the statute and are without authority to sit in judgment over the vires of the statute itself. Appellate Tribunal is not empowered to condone delay beyond maximum condonable duration allowed in section 112(6) even for good and sufficient reasons.
When to Use: (a) When challenging constitutional validity — save it for HC/SC; do not waste GSTAT proceedings on it. (b) To oppose Revenue's application for condonation of delay beyond 6 months — the bar is absolute and not capable of being relaxed even on good cause.
Principle 3 — Section 14, Limitation Act: Exclusion is Mandatory; Not Condonation [Para 22.8]
Section 14, Limitation Act 1963 | ITC Ltd. v. CCE (1997) 97 ELT 456 | CST v. Parson Tools & Plants AIR 1970 All. 428
Key Ratio / Principle
Section 5 'extends' limitation whereas Section 14 'excludes' time from computing limitation. Condonation of delay is discretionary for sufficient cause — but exclusion under Section 14 is mandatory. Relief under Section 14 is a right and not a relaxation. Time lost in appeal filed before incorrect forum was excluded in ITC Ltd. v. CCE when appeal came before the correct forum. Lapse of limitation procures a vested right to counterparty that cannot be lightly denied.
When to Use: When appeal before erroneous forum was filed within limitation — claim mandatory exclusion of that period u/s 14 before correct forum. Also use to oppose Revenue's attempt to condone delay as though it were a discretionary exercise.
