Principle 4 — Admissions Cannot Be Retracted at GSTAT; Undisputed Facts Are Final [Para 22.19]
Section 112, CGST Act 2017 | General Appellate Principles
Key Ratio / Principle
Facts taken on record by Appellate Tribunal cannot be denied in later proceedings. Admissions by taxpayer — whether under misinformation or failure to dispute erroneous allegations — cannot be retracted because Appellate Tribunal will be obliged to render a decision based on 'undisputed facts' and will not launch into a fresh investigation. This is the greatest peril of imprudent preparation in reply to notice.
When to Use: To impress upon clients the absolute importance of disputing every wrong factual allegation in SCN replies. Also to argue at GSTAT that undisputed facts recorded at earlier stages are conclusive — cannot be re-opened unless fraud or misrepresentation is established.
Principle 5 — GoA Must Be Specific; Relief Cannot Be Granted on Unpleaded Grounds [Para 22.13]
Section 112, Rule 112, CGST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
Grounds of Appeal are 'reasons' for Appellant's grievance — NOT arguments. Relief cannot be allowed by Appellate Tribunal on grounds that could have been urged but were not urged in Grounds of Appeal. Omission of material grounds is incurable by Appellate Tribunal unless amendment of GoA is sought, opposed and allowed. The Appellate Tribunal (as should the Appellate Authority) is obliged to reach a 'finding' on each ground. Rule 112 places embargo on additional evidence — NOT additional grounds.
When to Use: (a) To stress importance of comprehensive GoA at filing stage — omitted grounds are lost permanently. (b) To resist Revenue's attempt to introduce new grounds at hearing stage. (c) To demand a specific finding from GSTAT on each ground that has been urged.
Principle 6 — GSTAT Cannot Enhance Demand; Cannot Re-Do Proceedings [Para 22.17]
Section 113, CGST Act 2017 | Compare s.107 vs s.108 vs s.113
Key Ratio / Principle
Appellate Tribunal does not have authority to 'enhance' the demand because the demand confirmed in FAA or RA alone is before the Appellate Tribunal. It is only the Revisionary Authority who can 'enhance' the demand. Appellate Tribunal cannot 're-do' the proceedings assailed. If satisfied that intervention is warranted, the pleadings taken alone must support the eventual order to be passed.
When to Use: When GSTAT appears inclined to travel beyond the demand in the impugned order — cite the specific limitation of s.113. Also use to resist Revenue's argument that GSTAT should infer a higher demand not confirmed below.
Principle 7 — AAR Decisions Have No Binding Force; But Are Imposed Against the Applicant [Para 22.20]
Section 112, CGST Act 2017 | Section 103, AAR Provisions
Key Ratio / Principle
Decisions of Advance Ruling Authorities have no binding force because advance ruling is a 'bargain' that offers certainty and not necessarily accuracy of interpretation. Rulings pronounced do not even have binding force on other distinct persons of the same applicant. Appellate Tribunal is not bound to follow AARs — but Appellate Tribunal will impose the interpretation contained in any ruling against the applicant who obtained it.
When to Use: (a) When Revenue relies on an AAR ruling against taxpayer — argue it has no binding force on GSTAT. (b) When a taxpayer who obtained an unfavourable AAR comes to GSTAT — argue GSTAT is free to take a different view, but may impose the AAR's interpretation against that applicant.
Principle 8 — Per Incuriam Rule; Later Decision Without Considering Earlier Decision Has No Binding Force [Para 22.20]
Section 112, CGST Act 2017 | Doctrine of Per Incuriam
Key Ratio / Principle
As a National Tribunal, decisions of Appellate Tribunals in other States will be binding precedent. Where earlier decisions are not considered and a different decision is rendered by the Appellate Tribunal, then this later decision will not have binding force for omitting to consider the earlier decision and is said to be per incuriam. It is not possible for any Bench to disregard earlier decisions without discharging the duty of expressing their disagreement.
When to Use: When a Division Bench renders a decision that contradicts an earlier binding GSTAT decision without considering it — challenge the later decision as per incuriam, press for reference to Larger Bench or for express acknowledgment of disagreement with stated reasons.
Principle 9 — Defects in Revenue's Appeal Are a Defence; What Is Good for the Goose [Para 22.14]
Rule 110(4), CGST Rules 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
Defects in Revenue's appeal could themselves operate as an important defence for Appellants because "what is good for the goose must be good for the gander." Right to appeal is substantive and existence of defects being a question of law and fact — removal of defects being capable of adversely affecting limitation — requires careful examination of the nature of defects so-called.
When to Use: When Revenue files a defective appeal — systematically check all fatal defects listed (form, verification, pre-deposit, limitation, mandatory attachments, CoD application). Fatal defects in Revenue's appeal are a complete answer. Non-fatal defects may be pressed to force removal that impacts limitation.
Principle 10 — Circular 224's Pre-Deposit Requirement Contrary to Ramlal v. Rewa Coalfields [Para 22.10]
Ramlal v. Rewa Coalfields Ltd. | CBIC Circular 224/18/2024-GST
Key Ratio / Principle
The requirement in Circular 224 to deposit amounts in anticipation of appeal before limitation has even commenced is contrary to the prevailing jurisprudence that until lapse of limitation, nothing in the impugned order can be given effect — and that is an unquestionable and vested right of the litigant as held in Ramlal v. Rewa Coalfields Ltd. Lapse of limitation procures a vested right to counterparty that cannot be lightly denied.
When to Use: When Revenue attempts to initiate recovery action or treats pre-deposit as defaulted before limitation has expired — invoke Ramlal's principle that until limitation lapses, no recovery is permissible; the vested right to exhaust appellate remedy cannot be defeated by circular.
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Chartered Accountants, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 22 — Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) | Sections 109–114, CGST Act 2017
⚖️ Chapter 23 — Tribunal Procedure

⚖️ Chapter 23 — Tribunal Procedure

GST Act 2017 | GSTAT Appeal Filing & Procedure — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
23.1 Standard Operating Procedure
23.2 Appeal Online Only But Offline by Order
23.3 Appeal Memorandum — Table X
23.4 Appeal Memo — Not to Include
23.5 Without Pre-Deposit, Appeal Not Filed
23.6 Date of Order vs Date of Communication
23.7 Memorandum of Cross-Objections
23.8 Reply to Departmental Appeal
23.9 Cross Appeals
23.10 Classification of Supply in Dispute
23.11 Computational Tables
23.12 Statement of Facts 2
23.13 Grounds of Appeal 2
23.14 Appeal Non Est if Fatal Defects
23.15 Paper-Book 2
23.16 Synopsis
23.17 Compilation
23.18 Condonation of Delay 2
23.19 Withdrawal of Appeal 2
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
23.1 — Standard Operating Procedure
GSTAT — STEP-BY-STEP SOP FOR FILING & PROSECUTING APPEAL BEFORE APPELLATE TRIBUNAL
Step 1(a) — Taxpayer Appeal
WHO Taxpayer aggrieved by FAA or RA order
TIME 3 months from date of service of order
CONDONABLE By further 3 months on sufficient cause
Step 1(b) — Cross-Objections
WHO Taxpayer not in appeal but responding to departmental appeal
TIME 45 days from date of service of copy of departmental appeal
CONDONABLE By further 3 months
Step 2(a) — Filing Appeal
FORM APL5 — memorandum of appeal cum challan for pre-deposit
PRE-DEPOSIT 10% of disputed tax (revised 2024 from 20%) — not including interest and penalty — as determined in APL4 issued by AA or RA
PENALTY-ONLY No further pre-deposit apart from that already 'PAYABLE' under proviso to s.107(6); pre-Finance Act 2025 appeals = 10+10% of penalty
Step 2(b) to Step 4
STEP 2(b) Cross-objections in APL6 — no pre-deposit; on grounds to assail those urged in departmental appeal (APL7)
STEP 3 Provisional acknowledgement by Registry → final acknowledgement (APL2) after removal of defects; filing fee payable
STEP 4 Paper-book in 4 sets filed with Registry to assist during presentation (compulsory — rule specifics to be known when GSTAT Rules notified)
23.2 — Appeal Online Only But Offline by Order
RULE 110 OVERHAULED FROM 10 JUL 2024 — ONLINE MANDATORY; OFFLINE ONLY BY REGISTRAR'S ORDER
Default Rule — Online Only
RULE Rule 110 (from 10 Jul 2024) — appeals to GSTAT must be 'online only' unless Registrar issues special/general order
EXCEPTION Registrar 'by general or special order' can accept manual appeals — for specified duration, subject to conditions and restrictions
Practical Note
Detailed guidance to be available once GST Appellate Tribunal (Procedure) Rules are notified
Illustrative pleadings for constructing the framework of matters involved in drafting appeals provided in Part Two of the publication
23.3 — Appeal Memorandum (Table X)
APPEAL MEMORANDUM — SHORT AND ESSENTIAL; TABLE X: KEY MATTERS TO INCLUDE; BULKY APPEAL = CONTRA INDICATOR
Core Principle
AVOID Popular but erroneous practice: filing bulky appeal memo with documents not relevant for limited filing purposes
Bulky appeal = may impress onlookers but does NOT make difference to outcome
NOTE Registry will direct removal of irrelevant material at filing
Table X — What to Include
ItemRequirement
1. Appeal memo(i) Date of service [col.6] (ii) Verification signed by Appellant (iii) Prayer
2. Pre-depositAdditional 10% of disputed demand (ARN No.....) [col.14(b)]
3. AuthorizationPower of Attorney on non-judicial stamp paper (₹100) in name of individual CA / CMA / CS / Advocate — NOT firm-name
4. Order appealed againstCertified copy of impugned order
5. Filing feeARN No.........
NOT TO INCLUDEAdjudication order | SCN | Case law
Two-Rule Golden Principle
RULE 1 Material relevant for 'filing' appeal → include in appeal memo
RULE 2 Material relevant for 'presenting' appeal → include in paper-book
WRONG "When in doubt, include it in appeal memo" — this is a misunderstanding that must be avoided
23.4 — Appeal Memo Not to Include
WHAT MUST NOT BE IN APPEAL MEMO — THREE CATEGORIES; WHY LIMITING CASE LAW CAN BE DANGEROUS
(a) Previous Adjudication Order
NOT TO INCLUDE Previous adjudication order which has gone on to result in the impugned order being disputed in this appeal
EXAMPLE Impugned OIO (Annexure A) + APL1 & synopsis in personal hearing (Annexures B1/B2) which culminated in Impugned OIA → do NOT append all these
(b) SCN & Related Forms
NOT TO INCLUDE Show-cause notice, ADT2, INS1/2, ASMT10, ASMT13, etc.
WHY Process of disposal does not involve sitting and reading everything filed. Relevance aids presentation — material to be filed at appropriate stages
(c) Case Law
NOT TO INCLUDE Case law — full or extracts — in appeal memo
DANGER Citing specific case law in GoA can be overcome if that case is later distinguished — leaving no alternative ground open
BETTER GoA enabling all decisions up to date of disposal to be pressed into service at hearing is more powerful than GoA citing specific limited authorities
23.5 — Without Pre-Deposit, Appeal Not Filed
PRE-DEPOSIT MANDATORY TO FILE APPEAL — NON EST WITHOUT IT; TWO SCENARIOS FOR PRE-PAID DEMAND
Core Rule
NON EST Without payment of pre-deposit, appeal CANNOT be filed; s.112(6) negative construct makes appeal 'as if never filed'
NOT VALID Payment made 'under protest' cannot be considered as 'pre-deposit' — Common Portal does not permit APL5 with unpaid pre-deposit
JURISDICTION Not even interests of justice can vest GSTAT with authority to admit appeal that was never filed — functionary cannot take cognisance of that which law does not recognise to exist
Where Demand Pre-Paid (HC Directions / PO Assurance)
OPTION (a) Pay pre-deposit once again if online functionality does not proceed without it in table 14(b); file APL5
OPTION (b) File APL5 without further pre-deposit if online functionality permits and sufficient pre-deposit already made — even if appeal listed as 'defective' for Bench to admit on account of pre-deposit made under Court directions
NOTE Updated procedure in Rule 142(2B) must be considered as soon as online functionality is operational to avoid any prejudice
23.6 — Date of Order vs Date of Communication of Order
DATE OF ORDER ≠ DATE OF COMMUNICATION — TWO DIFFERENT EXPRESSIONS; MODES OF SERVICE; PLEADINGS
Two Distinct Concepts
DATE OF ORDER APL5 col.4 (col.5 in APL1) = date when order was issued by Proper Officer
DATE OF COMMUNICATION APL5 col.6 (col.7 in APL1) = date when order was brought to attention of Appellant
PRINCIPLE Legislature does not employ two different expressions to mean the same thing — full and due meaning must be discovered and applied to each
Revenue's 4 Dates vs Appellant's Counter
REVENUE Will cite: (i) date stated as date of order (ii) date of digital signature of PO (iii) date of upload on Common Portal (iv) date of postal acknowledgement
APPELLANT Must counter with: (a) incompatibility of e-service with background & familiarity of Appellant with online functionalities (b) auditable log of online access (c) verifiable response to recovery action (signed postal ack., bank debit, etc.)
FAIRNESS Revenue cannot take advantage of Appellant's lapse unless shown to be unusual and readily attributable to negligence; choice of service mode must not reek of unfairness
Modes of Service — S.169
ITeS PERSON May NOT be excused for omitting to locate order sent via email or on Common Portal
SWEEPER SUPPLIER Not well versed with IT systems — expected to receive service only in post or in-person; portal access only on due dates of filing returns
RULE Choice of mode of service must be proportionate to nature of business and background of Appellant
Infructuous vs Non Est Appeal
ONCE APL5 FILED With "xx-xx-xxxx" in col.6 → appeal validly admitted; whether date is erroneous can only be determined at post-admission stage by Bench
INFRUCTUOUS Appeal dismissed as infructuous = as if never filed; pre-deposit may be hit by limitation but refundable if dismissed on merits
NON EST S.107(6)/112(8) negative construct — appeal without pre-deposit = non est; effect of limitation in s.107(1)/112(1) also renders appeal non est
23.7 — Memorandum of Cross-Objections
CROSS-OBJECTIONS IN APL6 — WHEN, WHY, HOW; TESTING CRITERIA FOR DEPARTMENTAL APPEAL; TIMELINE
Trigger
WHEN Revenue aggrieved by decision/order → PO makes application in APL7 u/s 112(4) → disposed as statutory appeal
TAXPAYER Intimated with copy of APL7 → must file memorandum of cross-objections in APL6 within 45 days (condonable by 3 months)
NOT MANDATORY Cross-objections not mandatory — but advisable; taxpayer still entitled to be heard during disposal of departmental appeal even without APL6
Testing Criteria — Departmental Appeal
CriteriaWhat to Check
Form of APL7Check correctness in all respects
Commissioner's directions copyAvailable to taxpayer to confirm defects/discrepancies
Limitation for Commissioner's review6 months from date of 'passing' (NOT 'communication') of order
MaintainabilityMere dissatisfaction insufficient; gross misapplication of law + intervention needed to prevent maladministration
Computation & pecuniary limitsWithin Circular 207/2/2024-GST dated 26 Jun 2024
Issues agitatedIdentified in Commissioner's directions — whether deviated in APL3/7
Rebuttal vs Shifting Burden
RULE Objections placed on record in rebuttal after Revenue's presentation at personal hearing
SKILL REQUIRED Resist inadvertent slip of taking over burden of proof that rests on Revenue — great skill needed to limit submissions to rebuttal only
TACTIC APL6 Grounds must attend to: (i) maintainability of departmental appeal (ii) form, timelines, pecuniary limits (iii) grounds in Commissioner's directions and APL7
APL6 — Limits
NOT SECOND CHANCE APL6 is NOT a 'second chance' for taxpayer to file statutory appeal
Issues where findings adverse to taxpayer were reached in impugned order — NO further consideration possible via APL6; these become FINAL
HEARD TOGETHER APL7 and APL6 heard by the Bench together — disposed as if both were statutory appeals
23.8 — Reply to Departmental Appeal
REPLY TO DEPARTMENTAL APPEAL — WITHOUT FILING APL6; RULE 15A OF CESTAT; 6 GROUNDS TO COVER
Nature & Purpose
VALUABLE Taxpayer-respondent permitted to submit objections in advance — prior consideration of these objections adds value during presentation, after Revenue's case is presented
NOT AN APPEAL This 'reply to appeal' is not an appeal and will NOT attract any filing fee
BASIS Rule 15A of CESTAT Procedure Rules — expected to be applicable in GSTAT Procedure Rules also
6 Grounds the Reply Must Cover
(i) Jurisdiction
(ii) Limitation
(iii) Maintainability and justification of intervention
(iv) Restriction in scope of inquiry limited to facts-in-issue
(v) Evidence in support of grounds urged
(vi) Updated judicial authorities
23.9 — Cross Appeals
CROSS APPEALS vs CROSS-OBJECTIONS — KEY DISTINCTION; BOTH SIDES AGGRIEVED; TAGGED TOGETHER
Cross Appeals — Definition
SITUATION Taxpayer appeals u/s 112(1) on issues decided in Revenue's favour + departmental appeal u/s 112(4) on issues decided in taxpayer's favour → 'cross-appeals'
TAGGED Cross-appeal filed by both parties will also be tagged with respective memorandum of cross-objections (APL6) in respect of issues in impugned order in their favour
TEST Whether taxpayer's appeal is cross-appeal or memorandum of cross-objections depends on: (i) provision under which filed and (ii) limitation applicable under that provision
Cross Appeals ≠ Cross-Objections
DISTINCTION Cross-appeal u/s 112(1) by taxpayer cannot be treated as memorandum of cross-objections u/s 112(5)
When both parties file respective appeals → referred to as 'cross-appeals' not 'cross-objections'
EXAMPLE 5 issues A-E: Revenue appeals A/B/C (in favour of taxpayer) → Taxpayer may file APL6 on A/B/C; Taxpayer appeals D/E (in favour of Revenue) → Revenue may file APL6 on D/E
23.10 — Classification of Supply in Dispute
CLASSIFICATION IN APL5 — STRATEGIC CARE NEEDED; OVER-DISCLOSURE CAN CAUSE PREJUDICE
The Risk
RISK Where classification of taxable supplies is in dispute — a very pointed description of business in SoF can cause prejudice to findings to be reached in appeal
CANNOT Classification of supply-in-dispute cannot be answered from APL5 col.8(ii) / APL1 col.9(ii) alone
RULE Classification of supply-in-dispute must be stated in harmony with GoA
Safe vs Dangerous Wordings
DANGEROUS Stating "works contract services" / "construction services" in appeal on 18% demand — exposes tariff classification & exemption ingredients → prejudice
SAFER Stating "classification indeterminate and disputed" — may NOT cause prejudice
DANGEROUS Rule 37 ITC reversal appeal — stating "sale of solar power" exposes inadmissibility of credit even if suppliers are settled
SAFER State "classification not in dispute" — retains appeal within narrow confines of facts-in-issue and prevents prejudice
23.11 — Computational Tables
COMPUTATIONAL TABLES — APL5 COLS 8(iv)/13/14/15; AUTO-POPULATED BUT MUST BE EDITED; ERRORS ARE 'WITH PREJUDICE'
Nature & Importance
RULE All computational tables must be prepared in spreadsheet and imported reliably into APL5 [col.8(iv), 13, 14, 15] and APL1 [col.9(iv), 14, 15, 18]
CRITICAL Undisputed amounts found outstanding in these tables → entire appeal liable for dismissal as non est
WITH PREJUDICE Errors in computational tables = affirmation by Appellant; correction requires amendment of appeal invoking inherent powers — not allowed as routine, counter-party entitled to object
Auto-Population Warning
AUTO-POPULATED APL5 and APL1 fields auto-populated from previous proceedings data — BUT these fields are 'editable' and MUST be edited to ensure correct data
RULE 142(2B) Procedure to adjust disputed demand can lead to inadvertent misstatement of values in tables — care required
IDENTIFIED ERROR If auto-populated data in APL5 has identified error → Appellant MUST raise this in GoA so GSTAT can issue directions for rectification
APL4 Under GSTAT
GSTAT DIFFERENCE Unlike AA and RA who issue final demand APL4 themselves, APL4 in case of GSTAT orders is to be issued by Proper Officer under Rule 113(2)
EWB matters where entire payment deposited via DRC3 'under protest' — amounts paid and disputed must be clearly recorded in tables so substantive relief is not lost behind technical online functionalities
23.12 — Statement of Facts 2
SoF — DRAWS ATTENTION TO RELEVANT FACTS & AWAY FROM IRRELEVANT; TABLE Y; HSN TRAP; SUMMARY FORMAT
Purpose of SoF
SoF MUST Draw attention (i) to relevant facts and (ii) away from irrelevant facts
NOT LIFE STORY Irrelevant = facts that have no bearing on issues to be decided
ASSIST SoF must assist during in-person presentation by showing: nature of demand, jurisdiction of original proceedings, disputed tax period, dates of service, cause-of-action invoked, power of demand exercised
HSN/Classification Trap
FATAL ERROR Where exigibility to tax is in dispute — making affirmative statement to derive HSN code while describing activities of Appellant in SoF can commit fatal error
SAFE "...is engaged in providing services of goods transport agency" = states taxable activity explicitly — safer
SAFER "...is engaged in various business activities such as trading, investment, transport of goods services with/without RCM option" = broader, less exposing
Summary Format — Table Y
AFTER SoF Summary of proceedings in tabular form eliminates requirement to address details repetitively and enables quick reference during presentation
SoF COVERS Business description | Participation in original proceedings | Due process of law | Pre-notice payments | Adjudication and appeal findings | Summary
REMAND GSTAT (but NOT AA) may remand matter with directions for verification — where remand not necessary, include self-authenticated computations in SoF itself
Demand Summary Template
ItemDetail
Date of SCN...........
Date of service of SCN...........
Date of OIO...........
Date of service of OIO...........
Total demand*₹ ......crores
Underlying proceedingsSection 67/.....
Date of APL1...........
APL within limitationYES
Date of OIA...........
Total revised demand*₹ ......crores
Relief u/s 75(2)YES/NO
*excluding interest and penalty
23.13 — Grounds of Appeal 2
GoA — SELECT NOT EVERYTHING; TABLE Z FRAMEWORK; BROAD-BASED GoA IS BETTER STRATEGY
Selection Principle
BAD ADVICE "Throw everything you have and see which one sticks" — shows absence of clear strategy
RULE Only grounds that advance cause of Appellant and form key to strategy must form part of APL5
BROAD GoA GoA broad enough to enable all decisions up to date of disposal to be pressed into service is BETTER than GoA limited to specific authorities which may be distinguished
Table Z — Bad in Law
...insofar as it is barred by limitation...
date of service of.....is beyond limitation specified in......
nature of demand is incompatible with allegation of special circumstances of....
Table Z — Contrary to Law
...insofar as law applied (to facts) is wrong:
cause-of-action invoked not applicable to facts alleged....
ingredients to attract tax are not forthcoming in the infraction alleged....
...insofar as law wrongly applied (to facts):
interpretation contrary to plain words of statute....
interpretation contrary to extant instructions mandating that....
Table Z — Contrary to Facts & Illegal
...insofar as facts considered are wrong:
evidence adduced is insufficient to support alleged infraction....
absence of evidence renders infraction based on conjecture....
omission of correlative allegations implicitly disaffirms primary infraction....
...illegal...insofar as penalty imposed routinely without:
giving due weightage to facts attendant to new tax regime....
showing needed animus to establish alleged offence....
...insofar as contrary to judicial authorities: relied upon distinguishable / rendered sub-silentio / contrary to directly applicable decision in....
23.14 — Appeal Non Est If Not Free of Fatal Defects
PROVISIONAL ACK ≠ VALID FILING; APL2 IS MUST; FATAL DEFECTS = NON EST; REGISTRAR ≠ BENCH
APL2 — Mandatory
DATE OF FILING Provisional acknowledgement = date of filing — provided defects are removed and APL2 is issued
NOT SECURE Right to appeal is not secure just because provisional acknowledgement has been issued
MUST Taxable Person must NOT rest content until APL2 is issued — whether filed online or offline
NON EST Appeal filed with fatal defects = non est; GSTAT is denuded of authority to entertain appeals with incurable fatal defects [Singh Enterprises v. CCE ibid — Apex Court]
Registrar vs Bench
CANNOT Absolute authority is NOT vested in Registry (to exclusion of Bench) — includes Joint-Registrar, Deputy Registrar, Assistant Registrar
BENCH GSTAT will examine defective appeals and determine severity and fatality of defects — this balance lies with the Bench
TACTIC Explanation that does not satisfy Registry but satisfies Bench is possible — rigours of filing appeal cannot be fit into a straight-jacket; rules 110(4) and 111(4) both apply
Vested Right of Revenue
CAUTION Fatal defects can pass maximum condonable period u/s 112(6) — making appeal non est and beyond GSTAT's authority
Revenue can object to GSTAT entertaining defective appeals — may even carry admission of appeal for judicial review before HC
VESTED RIGHT Lapse of limitation procures a vested right to Revenue when appeal passes limitation — Revenue not one to lightly allow liberal view being taken
23.15 — Paper-Book 2
PAPER-BOOK — BOUND DOCUMENT; SERIALLY NUMBERED; RELEVANT TO GoA ONLY; BREVITY IS AN ART
What Paper-Book IS and IS NOT
IS Material to be relied upon during presentation; serially numbered from top (oldest document = page 1)
IS NOT A compilation of case laws and judicial decisions (that is 'Compilation')
IS NOT A history of the matter since inception — only that part of history relevant to grounds of appeal
CAN INCLUDE ASMT10-11 or ADT2 if grounds involve examination of facts made available to PO yet impossible interpretation arrived at
Key Rules & Principle
AVOID Urge to bulk-up paper-book — a judge once remarked several volumes must be to "harass the judges"
RULE MoA of appellant-company — include only where form of organisation is relevant (e.g., RBI Branch Office approval); exclude where organisation is not in dispute
WISDOM "If it is worth being said, it must be done briefly" — brevity is an art that comes with practice
23.16 — Synopsis
SYNOPSIS — ADVISABLE NOT MANDATORY; POINTERS FOR ORDER DRAFTING; 7 ELEMENTS; 3 COPIES
Nature & Purpose
ADVISABLE Not mandatory — contains key points of consideration to aid in drafting order, if arguments find favour with Bench
VALUABLE Especially where orders are NOT pronounced in open Court and reserved to be drafted in chambers
COPIES 3 copies: 1 to each Member on Bench (2 Members) + 1 to Respondent
DISTINCTION Synopsis ≠ Paper-book; Synopsis = 'pointers' for drafting order — NOT a 'draft order'
7 Elements of Synopsis
(a) Issues in dispute
(b) Facts NOT in dispute
(c) Facts disputed but prayed to be admitted based on corroborative material placed on record
(d) Interpretation applicable with extracts from latest judicial authorities + binding nature of those authorities
(e) Distinction of judicial authorities on issues involved but NOT applicable — reasons detailed during presentation
(f) Findings to be reached on grounds urged in APL5
(g) Conclusion by reaching those findings in favour of Appellant
Drafting Rule
ELEGANCE Must flow elegantly like orders passed by Tribunals/Courts — contains arguments explaining how relevant law is applied to relevant facts and eliminating irrelevant ones
Each argument traceable to one or more GoA; relevant facts identified and brought out; ingredients applied to statutory provisions to arrive at applicable tax treatment
BREVITY Synopsis must not be too elaborate — elaborate synopsis invariably contains repetition and is likely to go unread; brevity is an art
23.17 — Compilation
COMPILATION — BOUND-BOOK OF JUDICIAL AUTHORITIES; INCLUDE ADVERSE DECISIONS TOO; POINTS OF DISTINCTION IN SYNOPSIS
Nature
WHAT Bound-book (one or more volumes) comprising judicial authorities to be cited in support of arguments at personal hearing
PAGINATED Must be paginated in separate volumes so relevant volume and page number can be referred during presentation
ACCOMPANIES Synopsis — compilation goes along with synopsis
Include Adverse Decisions Too
EXPERT OPINION Decisions against Appellant should NOT be omitted from presentation
STRATEGY Separate volume may be submitted containing compilation of pro-Revenue decisions — along with arguments distinguishing those decisions showing how they do NOT apply in Appellant's context
FORMAT Points of distinction included in separate sub-part within synopsis, referring to decisions submitted in separate volume of compilation
23.18 — Condonation of Delay 2
CONDONATION — NOT AS OF RIGHT; 3 CONDITIONS; EXPLANATION vs REASONS; BALANCE OF INJUSTICE TEST
3 Conditions for Condonation
(a) Every day of delay is explained
(b) Explanation is plausible and truthful
(c) Explanation is supported by evidence and Affidavit
NOT RIGHT Condonation within statutory limit u/s 112(6) will NOT be condoned as a matter of right — only if above 3 conditions met
Explanation vs Reasons
DISTINCTION 'Reasons for delay' ≠ 'Explanation of delay'
Reasons = may be understood as excuses (inadvertence, lack of mindfulness)
Explanation = justification despite impediments and constraints beyond care and mitigation
DRAMATIC ≠ GOOD Dramatic explanation (ill-health, bereavement, marriage) is unwarranted — truthful explanation accomplishes more
Balance of Injustice Test
BALANCE (i) Injustice to Appellant by non-exercise of discretion (reasons for delay explained with evidence/Affidavit) vs (ii) Displacement of vested right of Revenue (accrued on lapse of normal period)
Ramlal v. Rewa Coalfields A vested right is created in hands of counter-party when other party delays beyond statutory limitation; this vested right is not to be displaced lightly
GST UPPER LIMIT GST has imposed 'upper limit' for condonation — seeking condonation beyond this limit = seeking exercise of non-existent power which is an impossibility in law
Discretion — How Exercised
NOT ABSOLUTE Existence of power to condone does not mean it must be exercised every time — only to consider the 'explanation' and admit the appeal
VIGILANT ONLY Law comes only to rescue of the vigilant — not dormant — unless dormant compels GSTAT to exercise discretion
NOT ABSOLUTE RIGHT Right to appeal can be fettered with pre-deposit and time limitation. Res judicata is paramount; upholding this may deprive taxpayer of remedy — but right to appeal is not absolute
23.19 — Withdrawal of Appeal 2
WITHDRAWAL — INHERENT POWER u/s 111(2); NOT TO BE TAKEN LIGHTLY; TABLE OF REASONS; COUNTER-PARTY'S RIGHTS
Procedure
PERMITTED Appeal filed and APL2 issued → withdrawal permitted; identical to Rule 109C procedure
INHERENT POWER Withdrawal is an inherent power stated in s.111(2) of GSTAT
REFUSAL POSSIBLE GSTAT may refuse to permit withdrawal if proceeding with appeal entails declaration of law and finding on facts that may operate as precedent — precluding Appellant from litigating same issue on new grounds
Grounds for Withdrawal — Table
ReasonRemark
Acceptance of FAA orderLikely to operate as binding precedent for current & subsequent periods
Acceptance of GSTAT/Court decision on similar subjectSame — binding precedent risk
Need to re-file after rectifying mistakes in appealRequired rejection with liberty to re-file within original limitation
Amount < monetary limit u/s 112(6)OPTION LIKELY TO FALL INTO DISUSE — taxpayer cannot be denied appeal if facts disputed or question of law
Amount < Board/Commissioner monetary limitApplies only in departmental appeal pending monetary limit authorization
Any other reasonCOULD BE MOST FREQUENTLY USED OPTION
Counter-Party's Rights
NOT LIGHTLY Withdrawing appeal is not to be taken lightly by Taxable Person
GROUNDS MUST (i) Dissuade any reason to reject + (ii) Present no prejudice to counter-party
COUNTER-PARTY Entitled to object to withdrawal — may show (a) justification that ends of justice better served + (b) irreparable prejudice if rejected
BALANCE GSTAT must strike balance (subject to judicial review) where right to withdraw is not unjustifiably denied vs prejudice to counter-party due to non-declaration of law
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 23 | Tribunal Procedure ⇧ Index
These principles are for use in personal hearings before GSTAT at every stage of filing, prosecution and withdrawal of appeals. State them confidently and cite the statutory source.
Principle 1 — Appeal Without Pre-Deposit is Non Est; Not Even Justice Can Cure It [Para 23.5]
Section 112(6), Section 112(8), CGST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
The negative construct of language in section 112(6) makes it clear that the appeal is 'as if' it was never filed without pre-deposit. Not even interests of justice can vest the Tribunal with authority to admit an appeal that was never filed in the eyes of law. A functionary under the law cannot take cognisance of that which the law itself does not recognise to exist. Payment made 'under protest' cannot be considered as 'pre-deposit'.
When to Use: (a) When opposing Revenue's attempt to have GSTAT condone non-payment of pre-deposit. (b) When Revenue files APL7 without proper pre-deposit — press this principle to oppose admission. (c) To educate client that pre-deposit is mandatory jurisdiction-conferring requirement.
Principle 2 — Date of Order ≠ Date of Communication; Limitation Runs from Communication [Para 23.6]
Section 112(1), Section 169, CGST Act 2017 | Statutory Construction Principle
Key Ratio / Principle
Legislature is not one to deliberately employ two different expressions while referring to the same thing. When two different expressions are employed, unless shown to have no other meaning, their full and due meaning must be discovered and applied respectively. 'Date of order' refers to date when order was issued by Proper Officer. 'Date of communication of order' refers to date when the same was brought to attention of the Appellant. Revenue cannot take advantage of Appellant's lapse unless it can be shown that the lapse was unusual and readily attributable to negligence.
When to Use: When Revenue argues limitation ran from date of order (upload/digital signature date) — invoke this principle to establish limitation runs from actual date of communication/service; lead evidence of auditable access log, postal acknowledgement, bank debit as counter to Revenue's dates.
Principle 3 — Mode of Service Must Be Proportionate to Nature of Business of Appellant [Para 23.6]
Section 169, CGST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
Modes of service are specified in section 169, but the choice of mode must not reek of unfairness. Choice of mode of service must be proportionate to the nature of business and background of Appellant. A registered person engaged in ITeS may not be excused for omitting to locate an order sent via email or on Common Portal. But a registered person supplying sweepers to Municipality who is not well versed with IT systems and accesses portal only on due dates of filing returns may be expected to receive service only in post or in-person.
When to Use: When limitation is challenged on the ground of delayed discovery of the order — establish the background of Appellant vis-a-vis IT familiarity; proportionate mode of service must be shown not to have been followed; unfair service mode = invalid service = limitation not triggered.
Principle 4 — Broad GoA Better Than Citing Specific Case Law in GoA [Para 23.4 / 23.13]
Section 112, CGST Act 2017 | Advocacy Principle
Key Ratio / Principle
Limiting grounds in Grounds of Appeal to specific authorities can be more easily overcome than grounds enabling all decisions up to date of disposal of appeal being available to be pressed into service at the time of hearing. And even when there is a decision of a higher Court, the points of distinction cannot be lightly brought out. These points of distinction offer grounds to assail the order before Appellate Tribunal. Case law (full or extracts) must not be included in appeal memo — paper-book and synopsis are the places where authorities may be cited.
When to Use: When drafting GoA — draft broadly without citing specific case names; this prevents Revenue from having the GoA dismissed by distinguishing the specific case cited. All decisions up to date of disposal remain available for use in arguments.
Principle 5 — Provisional Acknowledgement Is Not Enough; APL2 Is the Only Valid Acknowledgement [Para 23.14]
Rule 110(4), Rule 111(4), CGST Rules 2017 | Singh Enterprises v. CCE ibid
Key Ratio / Principle
Provisional acknowledgement will be considered as date of filing of appeal provided the defects in the appeal at the time of filing are non-fatal defects. Right to appeal is not secure just because provisional acknowledgement has been issued. Appeal that contains fatal defects will not be entertained lightly by the Bench as to unseat a vested right created in the State on passing of limitation. Appeal filed will not be considered valid unless acknowledged in APL2 after removal of curable defects. Taxable Person must NOT rest content until APL2 is issued.
When to Use: (a) When Revenue's APL7 has only provisional acknowledgement and contains fatal defects — press for non-admission. (b) To impress on client the absolute necessity of pursuing APL2 issuance and not treating provisional receipt as conclusive proof of valid filing.
Principle 6 — Condonation is Not a Right; Every Day Must Be Explained; Vested Right of Revenue [Para 23.18]
Section 112(6), CGST Act 2017 | Ramlal v. Rewa Coalfields Ltd.
Key Ratio / Principle
Delay within the statutory condonable limit in section 112(6) will not be condoned as a matter of right. It will be condoned only if: (a) every day of delay is explained, (b) explanation is plausible and truthful, and (c) supported by evidence and Affidavit. The Apex Court has held in Ramlal v. Rewa Coalfields Ltd. that a vested right is created in the hands of counter-party when the other party delays beyond statutory limitation, and this vested right is not to be displaced lightly. Law comes only to the rescue of the vigilant and not the dormant.
When to Use: (a) When opposing Revenue's application for condonation of delay — press for day-by-day explanation + Affidavit; mere plausible reasons insufficient. (b) When client seeks condonation — ensure Affidavit with specific evidence for each day of delay is prepared; distinguish 'reasons' from 'explanation'.
Principle 7 — Errors in Computational Tables Are 'With Prejudice' — Affirmation by Appellant [Para 23.11]
Section 112, CGST Act 2017 | Rule 113(2), CGST Rules 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
Amounts stated in computational tables in APL5 form the basis of lis. Once undisputed amounts are located in these tables and found to be outstanding, the entire appeal will be liable for dismissal as non est. Any errors in these computational tables will be 'with prejudice' as it tantamounts to affirmation by Appellant. Correction of any data in these computation tables will require amendment of appeal invoking inherent powers of Appellate Tribunal — these applications will not be allowed as a matter of routine and counter-party will be entitled to make their objections known.
When to Use: (a) When opposing Revenue's attempt to correct erroneous computational tables in their APL7 mid-proceedings — resist amendment as 'with prejudice' affirmation. (b) For client advise — verify every figure in APL5 computational tables before filing; auto-populated data must be manually checked and edited; any known errors must be raised in GoA itself.
Principle 8 — SCN u/s 73 = Admission of No Evasion; DGGI Jurisdiction u/s 67 Negated [Para 23.16 — Synopsis Example]
Section 67, Section 73, Section 74, CGST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
SCN issued under section 73 is an affirmation of absence of 'evasion of tax'. DGGI lacks jurisdiction to investigate under section 67 of CGST Act which permits only matters involving 'evasion of tax' to be authorized by Proper Officer. Therefore, where investigation was conducted under section 67 but SCN was issued under section 73 — there is a fundamental contradiction: either the investigation was without jurisdiction, or the SCN is issued under the wrong provision.
When to Use: Where DGGI conducted investigation under section 67 but ultimately SCN is issued under section 73 — press this principle at GSTAT: either the s.67 investigation was without jurisdiction (as no evasion exists per s.73 SCN), or the s.73 SCN is incorrect and should be challenged as an inherent admission of absence of evasion.
Principle 9 — Cross-Objections Are Not a Second Chance; Adverse Findings on Issues Not Appealed Are Final [Para 23.7]
Section 112(5), CGST Act 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
APL6 (memorandum of cross-objections) is NOT a 'second chance' for taxpayer to file statutory appeal. Issues where findings adverse to taxpayer were reached in the impugned order — no further consideration can be reached on those issues via APL6. Those issues become final, and liability left undisputed (by not preferring statutory appeal) is payable in full. But issues which Revenue has carried in appeal may present grounds to rebut the grounds urged in departmental appeal.
When to Use: When taxpayer client has not filed appeal but Revenue has filed APL7 — advise that APL6 cross-objections can only rebut grounds in APL7; adverse findings on undisputed issues are final and payable; timely statutory appeal u/s 112(1) is the only way to contest all issues.
Principle 10 — Classification of Supply in SoF Must Be Strategically Drafted to Avoid Prejudice [Para 23.10]
Section 112, CGST Act 2017 | Advocacy / Drafting Principle
Key Ratio / Principle
Where the classification of taxable supplies is in dispute, a very pointed description of the business in Statement of Facts is capable of causing prejudice to the findings to be reached in appeal. Classification of supply-in-dispute cannot be answered from the information contained in APL5 columns alone. Classification of supply-in-dispute must be stated in harmony with the Grounds of Appeal. Stating "classification indeterminate and disputed" may not cause prejudice, whereas stating a specific supply classification exposes tariff restrictions, exemption ingredients and admissibility of credit.
When to Use: When drafting APL5/APL1 where classification is in dispute — use "classification not in dispute" or "classification indeterminate and disputed" as appropriate to prevent GSTAT or opposing party from using SoF itself to prejudge the classification issue before arguments are heard.
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Chartered Accountants, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 23 — Tribunal Procedure | GSTAT Appeal Filing & Prosecution
⚖️ Chapter 24 — Applicable Jurisprudence

Chapter 24 — Applicable Jurisprudence

Pages 419–444 | Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Table of Contents

24.1 Justice v. Fairness 24.2 Power of Moulding Relief 24.3 Notice Ends Inquiry 24.4 Purposive Construction 24.5 Doctrine of Merger 24.6 Tax Avoidance 24.7 Mandatory or Directory 24.8 Litigation in Anticipation of Amnesty 24.9 Limited Relief from Permanent Credit Loss 24.10 Legality of Accord & Forfeiture 24.11 Ineffectiveness of Relief from Court 24.12 Obiter Dicta by Courts 24.13 Relevancy of Facts 24.14 Relevancy of Tax Period 24.15 Binding Nature of Judicial Authority 24.16 Rule of Law 24.17 Appropriateness of Forum Selection 24.18 Understanding Taxpayer's Business 24.19 Authorities that Illuminate Understanding ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

24.1 · Justice v. Fairness

⬆ Index
Justice v. Fairness — Appellate Authority's Role
Legislature's Design
Limited space for Appellate
Authority to dispense 'justice'
BARRED
Extra-legislative methods
or procedures to achieve
outcome — prohibited
For 'Just' Treatment
REQUIRES
Strictly in accordance with
'procedures laid down in law'
Leary Principle — J. Meggary
Leary v. Northern Union (1970) 2 All ER 713:
"If rules and law combine to give member right to fair trial AND right of appeal, why should he be told to be satisfied with an unjust trial and a fair appeal?"
PRINCIPLE
Appellate Authority cannot
travel beyond the lis
between taxpayer and Revenue
Justice vs Fairness — Answer
ADAGE
"Follow procedure laid
down by Legislature —
justice will take care
of itself"

24.2 · Power of Moulding Relief

⬆ Index
Power of Moulding Relief — Who Has It?
Concept
Authority of a Court of Equity
to travel beyond statute and
invent solution to redress grievances
Who Can Mould Relief?
ONLY
Supreme Court
or High Court
(highest court in State/UT)
CANNOT
Judicial and quasi-judicial
functionaries — NOT at
liberty to attempt such feat
Consequence of Violation
Attempt at moulding by lower
authority = violation of
statute law itself
Will be upset by next
appellate forum for being
contrary to law
Role of Appellate Authority
Objectively examine:
(i) Revenue's allegations
(ii) Taxpayer's defence
Determine treatment from
application of law to
undisputed facts
CANNOT
Carry out audit
or investigation
LIMITED TO
Establish probative value
(truth/reliability) of evidence
adduced by both sides
Bias Warning
CAUTION
AA must steer clear of
role played as Proper Officer
while discharging appellate duties

24.3 · Notice Ends Inquiry

⬆ Index
Notice Ends Inquiry — SCN as Final Boundary
When Inquiry Ends
RULE
Departmental / Enforcement
/ Proper Officer enquiry
ENDS when SCN is issued
Officer is satisfied: facts to
locate non-compliance and
evidence gathered
What Adjudication Is Based On
Based on allegations in SCN
+ defence by taxpayer
By designated Proper Officer
under Section 5 assignment
Post-SCN Prohibition
CANNOT
No enquiry/inquiry/proceeding
can continue once notice issued
CANNOT
Adjudicating Authority
cannot extend process
of collecting new facts
VIOLATION
Entertaining new facts
on different grounds =
violates Section 75(7) CGST
Notice as Framework
KEY RULE
Notice lays down framework
and boundaries within which
outcome of lis must be decided
Contrast: CrPC Section 222 allows
new allegations in charge-sheet —
no such power in GST
EP Royappa v. State of TN
AIR 1974 SC 555:
"Equality and arbitrariness are sworn enemies; where operative reason for State action is extraneous and outside area of permissible considerations, it is mala fide exercise of power — hit by Articles 14 and 16."

24.4 · Purposive Construction

⬆ Index
Purposive Construction — Golden Rule / Mischief Rule
What It Is
Rule of construction where
meaning given based on
'purpose' of provision
Also called:
Golden Rule of Interpretation
/ Mischief Rule
NOT Rule of First Resort
WARNING
In taxation & contract law,
literal construction is
rule of first resort
Purposive construction must
be attempted with great
circumspection only
Purposive vs Literal — When Applied
ONLY IF
Literal construction leads
to outcome that is
impossible or absurd
(not merely burdensome)
Step (a): Take facts in context
Step (b): Read plain words
Step (c): Arrive at treatment
Step (d): Check if impossible/absurd
Step (e): Continue with literal if not
IF IMPOSSIBLE
Find reliable source of 'purpose'
— resist urge to 'invest' purpose
to suit particular requirement
ITC — Purposive Construction Example
POWERFUL
Bona fide purchases cannot
be doubted to deny ITC if
supplier defaults in paying tax
Delay in filing GSTR-3B
cannot result in permanent
loss of credit — Section 16(4)
is illogical and against
'basic features' of GST

24.5 · Doctrine of Merger

⬆ Index
Doctrine of Merger
Core Rule
Grounds upheld in adjudication
order and also upheld in appeal
→ merge into Appellate Order
Grounds supported by
Appellate Authority become
Order of Appellate Authority
When Doctrine Applies
APPLIES WHEN
(i) Finding receives consideration
(ii) AND approved expressly
by Appellate Authority
DOES NOT APPLY
Mere dismissal of appeal
does NOT merge everything
in preceding order
Ouster of Grounds
Grounds considered & rejected
by Appellate Authority →
ousted from appellate order
Works Both Ways
STRATEGIC TIP
Doctrine operates against
taxpayer AND Revenue.
Replying to notice must
consider 'which ground will
be admitted at which stage'
till final disposal

24.6 · Tax Avoidance NEW

⬆ Index
Tax Avoidance vs Tax Evasion — GST Context
The Debate — Losing Ground
Distinction almost pointless
due to multiplicity of
retrospective amendments
and clarifications in GST
REVENUE'S APPROACH
Revenue claims nearly
everything under Section 74
even where proving 'special
circumstances' is impossible
Motivation for Litigation
(a) Plurality of
interpretations in GST
(b) Tax rates far higher
than business margins
(c) Interest rate
prohibitively high
(d) Long limitation
to issue notices
(e) No one convinced
with bona fides
(f) Pre-deposit far
lower than earlier
(g) Well-planned defence
greatly improves outcome
(h) Anxiety over
self-assessment
4 Key Aspects — Evasion Test
1. Non-payment of tax / wrongful claim of credit/refund
2. Knowledge of existence of liability
3. Use of device to conceal or blend — detection impossible
4. Gains derived by (mis)adventure interrupted in inquiry
Golden Rule — No Gain, No Evasion
POWERFUL ARGUMENT
"No gain, no evasion"
Without showing 'gains',
allegation of evasion
remains mere conjecture
Misinterpretation of law
cannot be saddled with
mischief in taxpayer's
intention or animus

24.7 · Mandatory or Directory NEW

⬆ Index
Mandatory vs Directory Provisions
Mandatory
NATURE
Cannot be negotiated with
Must be complied — binding
Non-compliance = denial
of effect from operation
of that provision
TEST
Nature: Affects rights
in law or benefits allowed
Provision: Act (seldom Rules)
Consequence: Loss/denial/forfeiture
Directory
Can be negotiated — must
be complied to extent feasible
Non-compliance = remediation
by suitable methods —
NOT loss of effect
TEST
Nature: Affects procedures
Provision: Act or Rules
Consequence: Remediation
(late fee / compensatory)
Internal Validation Test
MANDATORY IF
Instances found in law
which are (i) not possible to
be affected or (ii) not permitted
to be affected by non-compliance
DIRECTORY IF
No such internal
validation possible
Section 16(4) — Analysis
COLLISION CASES
(a) Clubs/associations — ITC denied
without saving clause when
S.7(1)(aa) introduced retrospectively

(b) Restoration of registration
on appeal — credit loss during
cancellation period

(e) S.16(2)(b) delivery completion
conflicts with S.16(4)

(g) Bona fide exemption claim
overturned by superior court

(h) QRMP taxpayers barred
from filing returns
for Oct by Nov. deadline
REMEDY WITHOUT STRIKING DOWN
'Read down' timelines in
Section 16(4) to be
directory and not mandatory
ASMT-11 and ADT-2
Explanation in ASMT-11 not found satisfactory does NOT give authority to proceed with recovery under Section 79. Response to ADT-2 does NOT authorize recovery — both merely authorize notice under Section 73 or 74.

24.8 · Litigation in Anticipation of Amnesty

⬆ Index
Litigation in Anticipation of Amnesty
The Belief
Government engenders belief
that every decade will see
Tax Disputes Settlement Scheme
with amnesty at discounted amount
The Trap
WARNING
If one pays up, they will
NOT qualify for refund
should amnesty be announced
Who Will Qualify
ONLY
Notices that have been
'disputed and litigated'
will qualify — NOT those
'accepted and discharged'
Issues Aggravating Pendency
Output TaxITCInterest
RCM u/s 9(3)Credit mismatchUnpaid arrears
RCM u/s 9(4)Inward supplies not used in businessArrears discharged belatedly
Deemed supply (multi-GSTIN)Time barred creditsTax on debit notes via 3B
Staff recoveriesReversal of common creditsInterest on 'net tax' via DRC3

24.9 · Limited Relief from Permanent Credit Loss NEW

⬆ Index
Section 16(4) — Collision Courses & Remedies
Structure of Section 16(4)
Time limit in Section 16(4)
NOT in Section 16(2)(d)
— deliberate placement
ARGUMENT
Belated returns are still
valid returns under S.2(117)
— credit in belated returns
cannot be invalid
9 Collision Cases
(a) Cancelled registration restored in appeal
(b) Clubs/assoc. — S.7(1)(aa) retrospective
(c) Credit allowed to Transferee in arrangement
(d) Delivery completion conflict S.16(2)(b)
(e) Exit from composition — credit on stock
(f) Withdrawal of exemption — credit on stock
(g) Bona fide exemption overturned by court
(h) QRMP scheme — Oct filing barred by Nov.
(i) IP/IRP — credit on Corporate Debtor's supplies
Late Fee — Curative Effect
ARGUMENT
Payment of 'late fee' has
curative effect on delay
in filing returns
When delay is redressed
and return is valid, nothing
in Section 16(4) can stand
in way of credit
Section 16(5) & 16(6)
S.16(6) — introduced to
accommodate credit loss
during registration cancellation
period restored in appeal
S.16(5) — saves belated
credits for FY 2017-18
to 2020-21 (condition:
filed by 30 Nov. 2021)

24.10 · Legality of Accord & Forfeiture NEW

⬆ Index
Doctrine of Election — Accord, Forfeiture & Section 128A
Doctrine of Election
Election of one among two or
more alternate courses of action
— excludes all others
Accord (Section 168 / Section 11A)
Government's 'election to
exercise restraint' — resolves
divergence of views without
demanding tax retroactively
Section 11A — where:
(i) Govt satisfaction
(ii) Generally prevalent practice
not paying GST / paying less
(iii) Based on GCTC recommendation
(iv) Via notification — elect
to exercise restraint from demand
Section 128A — Forfeiture Scheme
COVERS
Disputes July 2017 to March 2020
Sections 73 (and S.74 + S.75(2))
Demand must NOT have attained finality
OBLIGATIONS
(i) Revenue free to pursue
further appeal/revision
(ii) Taxpayer must withdraw
appeal or writ — condition
for availing relief
NO BENEFIT
Findings in withdrawn
orders WILL be precedent
— allegations will NOT
Promissory Estoppel — Apex Court
Motilal Padampat Sugar Mills v. State of UP AIR 1979 SC 621 (further refined in UOI v. Godfrey Phillips India Ltd. AIR 1986 SC 806) — upheld doctrines of promissory estoppel and legitimate expectation with exceptions: (i) equity (ii) public interest (iii) change of policy (iv) obligation imposed by law.

24.11 · Ineffectiveness of Relief from Court NEW

⬆ Index
Why Court Relief Is Often Ineffective
What Courts Do
Courts set aside order and
remand for fresh adjudication
on PNJ grounds
RESULT
Proper Officer on re-adjudication
is least likely to reverse conclusion
— passes detailed speaking order
confirming demand
Maintainability Problem
Lofty pleadings lie beyond
authority of PO or AA
to entertain
Fail to establish necessity
of Court intervention on
questions of fact or law
Many earn disfavour of Court;
those that pass hurdle
relegated back to PO
Four Practical Examples
PNJ violation — order quashed;
PO directed to grant personal hearing;
No genuinely fair consideration
afresh in 2nd round
Jurisdiction — SCN quashed;
Fresh SCN may be barred
by limitation (S.75(1)
not applicable to SCN 2)
FAA dismisses in limine
for time-bar — Court directs
FAA to examine on merits
without going into time-bar
Forum Selection Principle
KEY ADVICE
Taxpayers must choose
forum for good & sufficient
reasons — not merely to
overcome pre-conditions
in statutory appeal

24.12 · Obiter Dicta by Courts NEW

⬆ Index
Obiter Dicta — "Said by the Way"
What It Is
Averments made by Court in
course of admitting or even
dismissing petitions
Binding Effect
DANGER
Become binding on subordinate
authorities including
Appellate Tribunal to follow
If averments are on facts,
taxpayer is remediless except
writ appeal or SLP to
Apex Court
Practical Examples
Carousel trading dispute:
Court dismisses petition
but records finding that
"Mr. A conceptualized and
directed the misadventure"
→ Nearly impossible to
wriggle out before any
subordinate authority
Strategic Implication
LESSON
Grounds for maintainability
of WP need NOT be same
as grounds to press for
relief from Court
Great skill required in
harmoniously blending
maintainability grounds
with relief grounds

24.13 · Relevancy of Facts NEW

⬆ Index
Relevancy of Facts — Art of Saying Only What Matters
The Test of Relevancy
PRACTICAL TEST
Picture an impatient elder
loathe to hear 'what you
have to say' — only say
things that secure vice-like grip
over this audience gasping
for more of what is yet
to be presented
Effect of Irrelevant Information
DANGER
Irrelevant information
PREJUDICIAL to outcome
in adjudication or appeal
Authority may enter into
irrelevant facts and limit
inquiry to those facts —
not incorrect to refuse expanding scope
Relevancy in Law
Evidence Act Sections 5 to 55
(Bharatiya Shakshiya Adhiniyam
Sections 3 to 50) —
dedicated to relevance of facts
Important to grasp contours
of relevance of facts so as
not to forfeit relief available
if aspect was preserved
during proceedings

24.14 · Relevancy of Tax Period for Cause-of-Action NEW

⬆ Index
Tax Period Relevancy — Demand cannot be in Wrong Tax Period
Core Rule
FUNDAMENTAL
Not even lawful demand
can be made in wrong
tax period — will suffer
for being unlawful and
without authority of law
Section 42 — Pre-Oct 2022
Tax period of great significance
prior to 1 Oct 2022 — barred
utilization of unaccrued credit
Credit in tax period 'B' could NOT
discharge liability of period 'A'
Post-Oct 2022: Section 42 omitted;
output tax on forward charge
can be discharged regardless
of 'tax period' when credit accrued
SCN in Wrong Tax Period — Examples
SCN in FY 2022-23 denying ITC on
non-compliance with Rule 37
(180 days) — cause-of-action
arises in FY 2023-24 →
NO cause-of-action in FY 2022-23
SCN in FY 2022-23 denying ITC
for Dec. 2021 — cause-of-action
in FY 2021-22 →
PO can only enquire into
cause-of-action occurring in FY 2022-23
Fatal Defect
LETHAL WEAPON
Invalidity of PO's actions
due to wrong tax period
is a fatal defect touching
validity of notice and
jurisdiction exercised
Cause-of-action to issue
notice is NOT omnibus —
demand cannot be made
in any tax period at will

24.15 · Binding Nature of Judicial Authority NEW

⬆ Index
Binding Nature — Judicial Authority in GST
Doctrine of Merger (Extended)
PO's order merges into
FAA's order when FAA upholds
adjudication without speaking discussion
EXCEPTION
FAA dismisses due to limitation
— ratio in adjudication does NOT
secure FAA's authority
(res judicata / finality does
not apply to PO's findings)
High Court Decisions — Binding Force
Binding in whole country
unless contrary decision
of jurisdictional HC
Two contrary HC decisions:
Later in time is binding
Later HC considered earlier
and differed: binding force
is greater
Co-equal judicature (1v1 or 2v2):
'later is better' rule

Unequal (1v2): Larger bench prevails

Later by smaller bench contrary
to earlier: per incuriam or
sub silentio
Revenue Deviation from Binding Authority
IF REVENUE WANTS TO DEVIATE
(i) Issue notice contrary to prevailing authority
(ii) Desist from adjudicating
(iii) Keep notices in call-book
under Section 75(11)
(iv) Pursue to next superior
authority to secure view
overturning earlier authorities

24.16 · Rule of Law NEW

⬆ Index
Rule of Law — Traceable to Article 14 on Equality
Equality ≠ Uniformity
CAUTION
Unequals treated equally
= inequality
Criteria for segregation must
show intelligible differentiation
+ rational nexus with object
Arbitrariness = Antithesis
Arbitrariness in Executive
action violates Article 14
If Art. 14 violated
→ Art. 21 also violated
Arts. 14+21 violated
→ violation of Art. 19
not far behind
Menaka Gandhi — Starting Point
Menaka Gandhi v. UOI AIR 1978 SC 597 — great starting point to study development of extant jurisprudence on rule of law where multiple concurring, distinguishing and dissenting views are traced.

24.17 · Appropriateness of Forum Selection

⬆ Index
Forum Selection — Exclusionary by Nature
Core Rule
KEY
Selection of forum
EXCLUDES all others
Omitting one forum gives
counter-party defence to
demonstrate 'error of forum'
before entering merits debate
Departmental Remedies Table
RemedyKey Implication
S.107(3) — Application to AA by RevenueReview complete within 6 months; only 'legality or propriety' of order
Revision of adjudication or AA orderSuo motu; only where (a) illegal (b) improper (c) omits material facts (d) CAG observation; NOT merely prejudicial
S.112(4) — Application to AT by RevenueReview within 6 months of communication
WP due to non-constitution of ATTerms will be 'with prejudice'; additional pre-deposit on dismissal to file statutory appeal
Invalid Jurisdiction
FATAL
If statutory basis for invoking
any remedy is not satisfied,
it touches question of
validity of jurisdiction
Superior authority barred
from entertaining application
and presiding over it

24.18 · Understanding Taxpayer's Business

⬆ Index
Understanding Taxpayer's Business — Fatal if Wrong
The Divergence
CONFLICT
Taxpayer describes business
based on core strengths
Fiscal laws describe business
based on principal source
of revenue
Misconceptions are inevitable
and must be guarded against
in replying to notices
Consequence of Wrong Identity
Failure to establish identity
of taxpayer's business accurately
= irreparable damage to position
canvassed in notice
ECOM Business
NOTE
ECOM companies and
internet economy businesses
offer most unreliable
explanation for tax purposes
Collect T&Cs from website/app
and derive facts needed to
determine tax treatment —
not rely on taxpayer's description

24.19 · Authorities that Illuminate Understanding

⬆ Index
Authorities that Illuminate — Criteria for Reliance
Why Few Reliable Authorities
Most decisions post-2017 are
before Constitutional Courts
that 'mould relief' in face
of administrative excesses —
not before Revision Petition
Three Tests for Good Authority
(i) Stood the test of time
(ii) Received unquestioned acceptance
from both sides
(iii) Bears 'first principles'
engrained in due process
of this law
Role in Taxpayer's Preparation
Such authorities could be
an interesting addition to
future editions to guide
taxpayers in preparing their replies
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 24
⬆ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name and citation directly before the Adjudicating Authority, FAA, CESTAT, or GSTAT.
Leary v. Northern Union
J. Meggary | (1970) 2 All ER 713 | UK
"If the rules and the law combine to give the member the right to a fair trial and the right of appeal, why should he be told that he ought to be satisfied with an unjust trial and a fair appeal? Even if the appeal is treated as a hearing de novo, the member is being stripped of his right to appeal to another body from the effective decision to expel him."
When to Use: When adjudication was vitiated by PNJ and Department argues it was cured in appeal — argue that taxpayer has independent right to fair trial at adjudication stage itself; deprivation of that right cannot be cured by appellate fairness.
EP Royappa v. State of Tamil Nadu
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1974 SC 555
"Equality and arbitrariness are sworn enemies; one belongs to the rule of law in a republic while the other, to the whim and caprice of an absolute monarch. Where an act is arbitrary it is implicit in it that it is unequal both according to political logic and constitutional law and is therefore violative of Art. 14. Where the operative reason for State action, as distinguished from motive inducing from the antechamber of the mind, is not legitimate and relevant but is extraneous — it would amount to mala fide exercise of power — and that is hit by Arts. 14 and 16."
When to Use: When Proper Officer or AA has post-notice added new facts or traveled beyond SCN — argue that such action is extraneous and outside permissible considerations, hence a mala fide exercise of power violating Article 14.
Motilal Padampat Sugar Mills v. State of UP
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1979 SC 621
Doctrines of promissory estoppel and legitimate expectation were upheld while carving out exceptions of (i) equity (ii) public interest (iii) change of policy (iv) obligation imposed by law. Government cannot resile from assurance given to taxpayers except on these grounds.
When to Use: When Government retroactively imposes tax on a practice that was previously tolerated or for which assurance was given — invoke legitimate expectation doctrine to argue that demand based on changed position without notice violates Art. 14.
Section 75(7) CGST Act — Statutory Principle
Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017
All allegations are complete and contained in the notice. Adjudicating Authority cannot entertain new facts supporting allegations on different grounds — to do so would be violating Section 75(7) of the Central GST Act. Not even the Adjudicating Authority can extend the process of collecting facts to arrive at new allegations after notice is issued.
When to Use: When Adjudicating Officer or AA tries to introduce new grounds not in SCN during adjudication or hearing — cite Section 75(7) directly and state that the SCN is the four corners of the proceeding and no new fact or allegation can be entertained beyond it.
Menaka Gandhi v. UOI
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1978 SC 597
Arbitrariness in Executive action violates Article 14. If Article 14 is violated, Article 21 is also violated. And when Articles 14 and 21 are violated, violation of Article 19 is not far behind. The rule of law and equality are inseparably connected — artificial and irrational differentiation strikes at the root of equality.
When to Use: In writ petitions or constitutional arguments challenging the arbitrary application of GST provisions or selective enforcement — anchor the argument in the chain of constitutional violations flowing from Article 14 arbitrariness.
Section 16(4) — Directory Not Mandatory
Jurisprudential Principle — Applicable in all ITC cases
Plethora of internal exceptions not expressly provided in Section 16(4) conflict with each of several situations resulting in statute authorizing deprivation of taxpayer's rights creating a situation of impossibility of compliance. Without 'striking down' Section 16(4), the remedy in law is to 'read down' the timelines in Section 16(4) to be directory and not mandatory. Belated returns are valid returns under Section 2(117) — credit in belated returns cannot be invalid. Payment of late fee has curative effect on delay.
When to Use: In any ITC disallowance under Section 16(4) — argue that the provision is directory not mandatory, late fee has cured the delay, and credit in valid belated returns flows lawfully into ECrL under Section 16(2)(d).
Tax Period — Fatal Defect in SCN
Jurisprudential Principle — Para 24.14
Not even a lawful demand can be made in the wrong tax period — it will suffer for being unlawful and without authority of law. Validity of a demand requires such demand to be made in the valid 'tax period'. Cause-of-action to issue notice of demand is not omnibus — invalidity of Proper Officer's actions due to error of relevancy of tax period is a fatal defect touching validity of notice and jurisdiction exercised.
When to Use: When PO issues SCN in a tax period where the cause-of-action has not arisen — raise this as a preliminary ground of jurisdiction to challenge the very foundation of the notice, making the demand bad in law ab initio.
No Gain — No Evasion (Tax Avoidance Principle)
Jurisprudential Principle — Para 24.6
Imprudent self-assessment to arrive at NIL liability is not evasion in itself without knowledge of existence of liability that is overcome by some misadventure masked by use of some device to conceal gains (to be) derived that came to be interrupted by said inquiry. That is, 'no gain, no evasion.' Without showing 'gains', allegation of evasion would remain mere conjecture. Every misinterpretation of law cannot be saddled with mischief in taxpayer's intention or animus.
When to Use: When Department invokes Section 74 alleging fraud/evasion — challenge the jurisdictional fact by asking Revenue to demonstrate actual 'gains' concealed. Without this, invocation of Section 74 is without jurisdictional foundation.
Binding Authority — Safari Credits Principle
Jurisprudential Principle — Para 24.15
Where one HC agrees with taxpayer's position making it bona fide and not implausible, demand under Section 74 cannot be raised as the superadded ingredient of evasion of tax is absent. Revenue cannot lightly disagree with orders of Appellate Authority that have reached finality and become binding against taxpayer on the given issue for all subsequent tax periods — that would be administrative indiscipline by Proper Officer.
When to Use: When Revenue raises demand under Section 74 despite a favourable HC view — argue that with HC view holding the position as bona fide, Section 74 jurisdictional prerequisite of fraud/intent is automatically destroyed.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 24 — Applicable Jurisprudence | Pages 419–444
⚖️ Chapter 25 — Pleadings for Reply to Notices

⚖️ Chapter 25 — Pleadings for Reply to Notices

GST Litigation Reference — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
25.1 Replying to Notices
25.2 Relief Prayed
25.3 Burden of Proof
25.4 Accept and Discharge
25.5 Dispute and Litigate
25.6 Reject Allegations
25.7 Burden on Taxpayer
25.8 Options to Reply
25.9 Departmental Observations
25.1 — Replying to Notices
REPLYING TO NOTICES — FRAMEWORK
Monitor Actively
RISK Ignoring notices = forfeiture of remedies in law
Monitor registered email constantly
Monitor Common Portal with registered credentials
What is a Reply
DEFINITION Statement of position of taxpayer — NOT an explanation
Need not be elaborate — sufficient to disclose position
Even "We do not agree" = valid complete reply
What Reply May Contain
Question the correctness of facts
Question the evidence adduced
Dispute the demand
Reject the allegations — leaving burden on Revenue
Pleadings — Key Rule
"You cannot get what you do not ask"
Pleadings = points of objection in reply
Relief NOT prayed = cannot be allowed even if allowable
Paraphrased or point form — sufficient to convey position
E-Service Issue
Disputed question of law — not definitively resolved
Revenue Recognized mode of service
Taxpayer Least expeditious mode — esp. for non-tech taxpayers
25.2 — Relief Prayed
RELIEF PRAYED — RULES
Every Pleading Must
Point to a specific relief sought
Relief must be allowed by accepting authority
Two Types of Pleading
(a) Challenge to notice — reject allegations
(b) Rebuttal — accept validity of allegation but dispute quantum/treatment
Relief Must Be Specific
✗ VAGUE "Pass such orders as deemed fit and proper" = bare minimum only
✓ SPECIFIC "Refund be sanctioned" or "Demand is time-barred"
"Demand liable to be set aside as contrary to Sec [X]"
GOLDEN RULE
Relief NOT prayed cannot be allowed — even if allowable in law
25.3 — Burden of Proof
BURDEN OF PROOF — IN GST SELF-ASSESSMENT FRAMEWORK
Core Principle
Burden rests on one making allegations
Where self-assessment challenged → burden on Revenue making allegation
Self-Assessment Presumption
Taxpayer trusted to self-assess
Even decision NOT to register = outcome of self-assessment
Nil liability = self-assessment outcome
Sec 23 exemption from registration = also self-assessment
Burden NOT Discharged By
✗ Making allegation alone
✗ Even easily plausible allegation
Revenue must establish case BEFORE taxpayer justifies self-assessment
Burden Discharged Only When
Mountain of evidence commensurate with nature of allegation
Produced AND appended to notice
Simple allegation → some evidence
Severe wrong-doing → substantial evidence
Evidence — What Qualifies
✗ NOT Extract of books of account
✗ NOT Statement on oath alone
WEAKEST Oath statement — esp. if contains admission of wrong-doing or interpretation of law
UNRELIABLE Third-party witness — no cross-examination
25.4 — Accept and Discharge
ACCEPT & DISCHARGE THE DEMAND
WHO DECIDES
Sole choice of Taxpayer
Valid or invalid — does not matter once decided
✗ CANNOT Outsource to advisor or Tax Officer
No mala fides to Officer who informs
A — Mode of Discharge
DRC-3
Credit does NOT flow into ECrL
Future dispute risk
Sec 17(5)(i) r/w 75(2)
GSTR-3B
(RCM only)
Credit flows into ECrL ✓
Debit Note
Supplier → Customer
IF contract permits + customer willing
B — Review Decision
Refund claim within 2 years from payment
GROUNDS
Anxiety
Involuntary
Misunder-standing
VERBAL DEMAND
No verbal demand to accept/discharge
Every demand must be in writing + summary
Before notice → DRC-1A
After notice → DRC-1 or 2
Payment → DRC-3
25.5 — Dispute and Litigate
DISPUTE AND LITIGATE — WHEN & HOW
When to Accept Demand
(i) It is creditable
(ii) Can be passed on to customers
(iii) Not material in amount
(iv) No precedent value for future periods
(v) Clear, unambiguous with mounting interest
All other cases → DISPUTE
How to Dispute
MINIMUM REPLY
"Demand is disputed and further proceedings may be initiated in accordance with law"
No explanation required in pre-notice proceedings (scrutiny / audit / inquiry)
After Disputing
Revenue MUST issue Show Cause Notice u/s 73, 74 or 76
Sec 61(3), 65(7), 67(3) — demand only via SCN
Pre-notice provisions NOT connected to Sec 79 (recovery)
Bank Attachment Threat
MISUSE "Bank account will be attached" without DRC-7 + DRC-13 = unlawful
Reply: "Demand disputed — no demand may be recovered without due process. Further proceedings in accordance with law."
Send copy to supervising officer (not below rank of Joint Commissioner)
25.6 — Reject Allegations
REJECT ALLEGATIONS — DISTINCT FROM DISPUTING DEMAND
Dispute vs Reject
Dispute
Contest the demand amount / liability
Reject
Allege allegations are NOT accepted — no rebuttal needed
What Rejection Does
Makes taxpayer's position known
Does NOT require disproving allegations
Plants burden firmly on Revenue to substantiate prima facie case
DANGER of ELABORATE REPLY
Exposes defences prematurely
Revenue can draft better SCN using disclosed defences
Taxpayer's elaborate reply = fodder for Revenue
Part B of DRC-1A
Limit to: (i) Errors on facts + (ii) Contemporaneous documents
DO NOT ADD Explanation / justification / new material
Decision to issue SCN already taken — Part B will not change it
25.7 — Burden on Taxpayer
BURDEN ON TAXPAYER — LIMITED SCOPE
General Rule
NO BURDEN to prove innocence
Sec 155 cannot impose unnatural burden except Sec 16, 17, 18 pre/post conditions
Where Burden IS on Taxpayer
(a) Sec 34 — conditions for credit notes
(b) Concessional tariff / exemption conditions
(c) Absence of prohibited relations with customers (transaction value)
(d) Maintain contemporaneous records for self-assessment
Self-Assessment Disclosure Chain
Invoice = self-assessment order
GSTR-1 (Sec 37) = discloses liability
GSTR-3B/4 (Sec 39) = discharges liability
GSTR-7/8 = TDS/TCS self-assessed
KEY Once data in GSTR-9 — no occasion to seek explanation of mismatch with GSTR-1 or 3B
25.8 — Options to Reply
OPTIONS TO REPLY — DECISION TREE
FIRST DECISION — Accept or Reject the demand?
ACCEPT
Discharge via DRC-3 or GSTR-3B (RCM)
Reply in prescribed form confirming payment
Reply must be in specific prescribed form for each section (online)
REJECT
Short reply disputing demand
Balanced — sufficient to disclose position, not inculpatory
Scale of reply: 'short yet sufficient' to 'bit more elaborate but not excessive'
Detailed reply only when SCN issued — NOT at pre-notice stage
RULE Each section requires reply in specific prescribed form — GST notices and replies are moving entirely online. Taxpayers must be accustomed to filing replies in prescribed forms.
25.9 — Departmental Observations
DEPARTMENTAL OBSERVATIONS — HOW TO HANDLE
What are Departmental Observations
Letter / undefined notice during scrutiny, audit, or demand
Not a formally prescribed form of notice
Taxpayer's Options
Option A
State communication is not authorized in law
Sec 160(2) — refuse to reply on merits
Option B
Reply — but only when proceedings initiated under specific provisions of law
Specific Provisions
Sec 61 notice → respond in prescribed form
Rule 101(4) findings → respond
Pre-notice consultations → respond
Golden Rules for Reply
"It is the noticee's position that…"
"Demand is disputed and allegations are rejected"
Balanced — sufficient to disclose position, not inculpatory
Taxpayer NOT obliged to introduce new material to prove innocence once demand disputed and allegations rejected
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 25 — Pleadings for Reply to Notices
⚖️ Chapter 27 — Pleadings in Appeal Memo (Para 27.24–27.35)

Chapter 27 — Pleadings in Appeal Memo

Para 27.24–27.35 | Pages 624–645 | Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Table of Contents

27.24 Subsidy — Output Tax on Taxable Value 27.25 Escaped Turnover 1 (GSTR-1 vs 3B) 27.26 Escaped Turnover 2 (Books vs Returns) 27.27 Escaped Turnover 3 (Exemption Denied) 27.28 ITC Reversal on Exempt Turnover 27.29 Reversal of Inadmissible Credit 27.30 Supplier-in-Default 1 27.31 Supplier-in-Default 2 27.32 Penalty for Furnishing False Documents 27.33 Interception — Consignment 27.34 Interception — Conveyance 27.35 Inquiry-Led Demand — Relief Allowed Selectively ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

27.24 · Output Tax on Subsidy — Grounds in Appeal Memo

⬆ Index
Subsidy on Taxable Value — Illustrative Grounds
Ground 1 — Exclusion from Taxable Value
Impugned Order is bad in law insofar as it demands output tax on 'subsidy' received from State Government which is clearly excluded from taxable value under Section 15 read with proviso to Section 2(31). Subsidy is mandatorily disclosed as non-taxable supply in GST returns. Demand is liable to be set aside.
Ground 2 — Lack of Clarity on Transaction
Impugned Order is contrary to law as it does not specify which transaction is being demanded — whether exempted turnover is a separate transaction or the 'subsidy' itself. Without identifying which transaction the 12% tax relates to, demand is contrary to law.
Ground 3 — No HSN Code Commonality
Impugned Order is contrary to facts for demanding tax on exempted turnover without establishing commonality of HSN code between subsidy and supply, especially where time interval exists between supply and accrual of subsidy. No demand can be sustained on estimation, guesswork or conjecture.
Ground 4 — 4 Taxing Ingredients Missing
TRITE LAW
All 4 ingredients must be shown:
Taxable Event
Rate of Tax
Measure of Tax
Person Liable
Order proceeds on "assumption" without establishing supply or its time and place. Merely computing tax does NOT satisfy requirements to fasten liability.
Ground 5/6 — Interest & Penalty
Interest: Demand for interest is erroneous when underlying demand for tax itself is erroneous.
Penalty: Demand for penalty is erroneous when tax demand is erroneous, inapplicable and denied.
Penalty — animus: Demand for penalty is illegal when required animus is not shown to exist.

27.25 · Escaped Turnover 1 — GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B Mismatch

⬆ Index
GST ≠ Tax on Turnover — It is a TAX ON SUPPLY
Foundational Principle
RULE
Turnover in financial statements
is NO affirmation of taxable supplies
Accounting income ≠ taxable
outward supplies
Mere data differences CANNOT
support adverse inference
about escaped turnover
Ground 1 — Timing Differences
