Impugned Order is bad in law for failing to consider timing differences in reporting invoices in GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B. Linear comparison of output tax between GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B is NOT a lawful basis for demand.
Ground 2 — 4 Reasons for Mismatch
Data in GSTR-1 must exclude deferment under Section 37(3)
Liability discharged in earlier tax period must be excluded
Liability reversed and adjusted in subsequent period must be excluded
Net values and offline adjustments permitted vide Circular 26/26/2017-GST dated 29 Dec. 2017 must be excluded
Demand contrary to CBIC Instruction No. 01/2022-GST dated 7 Jan. 2022. Demand is liable to be set aside.
Ground 3 — Section 75(12)
Impugned Order is contrary to Section 75(12) — recovery proceedings under Section 79 will be initiated only for unpaid self-assessed tax per Section 39 returns. No such recovery proceedings were invoked.
Ground 4 — 5 Taxing Ingredients Missing
(i) Category — goods/services
(ii) Taxability
(iii) HSN Code
(iv) Time of Supply
(v) Place of Supply
Without these taxing ingredients, demand cannot be sustained. Demand confirmed is liable to be set aside.

27.26 · Escaped Turnover 2 — Books vs Returns

⬆ Index
Books of Account are NOT Perfect — Source Documents Are Primary
Ground 1 — Output Tax Already Discharged
Impugned Order is bad in law — output tax already discharged in accordance with law. No output tax liability (forward or reverse charge) remains undischarged. Without incidence of tax, demand based on data differences violates known tenets of taxation.
Ground 2 — Category of Revenue Changed
Impugned Order is bad in law and facts for attempting to change category of revenue from what is in financial statements — increasing taxable supply by reducing exempt supply is a desperate attempt to demand tax on total revenue without considering facts in reply to SCN.
Ground 3 — Trial Balance Cannot Be Basis
WITHOUT PREJUDICE: Demanding output tax based on amounts in Trial Balance requires (i) identifying supplies that escaped tax AND (ii) such supplies being liable to tax under Section 9(1)/5(1). Financial statements are prepared on rules of accounting — NOT on 'time of supply'. This converts GST from 'tax on supply' to 'tax on receipts'.
Ground 4 — HSN Code Not Stated
Impugned Order is illegal and contrary to law as tax demand is devoid of classification — fails to mention HSN code. Merely computing tax at 18% does not receive sanction of law. Classification is NOT something that can be assumed.
Ground 5 — Wrong Tax IGST
WITHOUT PREJUDICE: IGST demanded without showing 'place of supply' to support inter-State supply. Payment of 'wrong tax' does not satisfy requirements — Section 77 CGST read with Section 19 IGST demands payment of 'correct tax' again.
Ground 6 — Articles 246A, 265, 300A
Demand for output tax cannot be made on guesswork, estimation and conjecture. Not even a consenting taxpayer can deposit dues without attracting taxing ingredients in law. To do so would fall foul of Articles 246A, 265 and 300A of the Constitution of India.
Ground 7 — Burden of Proof
Section 155 CGST Act: Appellant is responsible only to prove eligibility for ITC. Hence burden of proof on output tax demand allegations rests on Revenue. Without discharging this burden, demand is not sustainable.

27.27 · Escaped Turnover 3 — Exemption Denied

⬆ Index
Exemption Denial — Double Impact on Taxpayer
Foundational Principle
Burden to prove exemption rests on taxpayer — but once prima facie basis established, onus to disprove returns to Revenue
RULE
Exemption cannot be denied by merely rejecting claim — rejection requires 'reasons to believe' in inapplicability
Exemption denial has double impact:
credits forfeited + tax demanded
Ground 1 — Approbate and Reprobate
Impugned Order is bad in law and facts — admitted HSN code in self-assessment but denied exemption under Notification 2/2017-CT (Rate) corresponding to said HSN code. Without showing basis for denial, Revenue cannot approbate and reprobate. Demand based on vague assumptions is illegal.
Ground 2 — 5 Taxing Ingredients Missing
Same as 27.26 Ground 4 — (i) category (ii) taxability (iii) HSN (iv) time of supply (v) place of supply — all missing. Demand cannot be made on guesswork. Articles 246A, 265 and 300A violated.
Ground 3 — Periodic Returns Cannot Be Rejected
WITHOUT PREJUDICE: Impugned Order is contrary to law — demanding output tax based on periodic returns without making out prima facie case against admissibility of exemption. Impugned Order "routinely parrots the allegations in SCN as if acting under dictation." Merely computing tax does NOT discharge burden of proof. This would render all exemptions 'inadmissible-until-proved admissible'.
Ground 4 — IGST Place of Supply
WITHOUT PREJUDICE: IGST demanded without showing 'place of supply' — Section 77 CGST read with Section 19 IGST demands 'correct tax'. Demand liable to be set aside.

27.28 · ITC Reversal on Exempt Turnover

⬆ Index
ITC Reversal — Abatement / Exempt Supply / Section 17(3)
Ground 1 — Abatement ≠ Exempt Supply
Impugned Order is bad in law — abatement under Notification 11/2017-CT (Rate) is a legal method of altering taxable value. Section 17(3) includes "sale of land" and "sale of completed buildings" (Schedule III para 5) — abatement under tariff notification does NOT fall within Section 17(3). Rule 32 also provides instances where no reversal is applicable.
Ground 2 — Retrospective Fiction
WITHOUT PREJUDICE: Impugned Order creates a fiction beyond express words of statute — explanation in Section 17(3) was inserted only from 1 Oct. 2023 and cannot be applied to Disputed Tax Period of 2018-19. Such retrospective application is not permitted in law.
Ground 3 — Section 2(47) Exempt Supply Definition
Impugned Order is contrary to law — Section 2(47) "exempt supply" includes only: (i) nil rate supplies (ii) wholly exempt under Section 11 or (iii) non-taxable supply under Schedule III. Innovatively bringing taxable transactions into exempt supply definition when none of these conditions are satisfied is arbitrary and illegal.
Ground 4 — No RUD Provided
WITHOUT PREJUDICE: Neither SCN nor Impugned Order has provided any Relied Upon Documents (RUD) showing basis of transactions, corresponding value, and tax period for which ITC is liable for reversal. Demand arising from material that are contrary to facts is liable to be set aside.

27.29 · Reversal of Inadmissible Credit

⬆ Index
Inadmissibility of Credit — Statutory Hurdles Must Be Crossed
5 Statutory Hurdles for Inadmissibility
S.16(1)
S.17(2)
S.17(3)/(4)
S.17(5)
S.18(6)
RULE
To routinely deny credit without touching these hurdles would be subjective and liable to be set aside
Ground 1 — Vested & Indefeasible Right
Impugned Order is bad in law — attempts to recover vested and indefeasible ITC. Without unseating the compliance with vesting conditions, self-assessment carried out under Section 59 cannot be lightly disturbed.
Ground 2 — Blocked Credits Assumed
Impugned Order is illegal — proceeds on assumption that ITC are 'blocked credits'. Trite law: to assert a disqualification it is necessary to demonstrate from contemporaneous records. When no records have been referred, assumption of blocked credits is impermissible.
Ground 3 — S.17(5)(c)/(d) Exception
WITHOUT PREJUDICE: Even Section 17(5)(c) and (d) are "subject to exception" — inward supplies that are NOT CAPITALIZED would still be eligible for ITC. Without verifying this fact, rushing to conclusion that blocked credits have been claimed is illegal, unjust and arbitrary.

27.30 · Supplier-in-Default 1 — Demanding Recovery Without Exhausting Remedy Against Supplier

⬆ Index
Supplier-in-Default — No Double Recovery Permitted
Ground 1 — Section 73 Disaffirms Mala Fides
Demand under Section 73 is express disaffirmation of any mala fides on the part of Appellant — yet order proceeds to incorporate all incidents of 'carousel trading' (Circular 171/3/2022-GST dated 6 Jul. 2022). Revenue cannot approbate and reprobate. Entire adjudication is exercise in futility.
Ground 2 — No Finding Against Supplier
Impugned Order is illegal — fails to reach any finding whether Supplier-in-Default has discharged tax, interest and penalty under Section 74 read with Section 93. Trite law: no demand can be recovered twice. No material shows recovery was exercised against supplier without yielding result.
Ground 3 — Instruction 1/2022-23
Impugned Order contrary to Instruction 1/2022-23 dated 7 Jan. 2022 — no recovery action warranted merely when portal data does not support compliance. Also contrary to Press Note dated 4 May 2018 by Board, Ministry of Finance. Confirming demand contrary to extant instructions violates administrative discipline.
Ground 4 — Genuine Outward Supplies
Impugned Order contrary to law — inward supplies alleged from non-existent Suppliers, yet there is admission (due to absence of adverse finding) that outward supplies by Appellant are genuine. It is impossible to effect genuine outward supplies without genuine inward supplies. Investigative failure in SCN and Order.

27.31 · Supplier-in-Default 2 — Section 74 Extended Limitation

⬆ Index
Supplier-in-Default 2 — Challenging Extended Limitation Under Section 74
Ground 1 — Section 74 Special Circumstances Not Established
Demand under Section 74 without establishing 'special circumstances' is a mischievous and mala fide attempt to procure unlawful extension of limitation for a demand that can only be made under Section 73. Demand to the extent of extended period of limitation is liable to be set aside — reduced to NIL.
Ground 2 — Which 3 Special Circumstances?
Impugned Order contrary to law — extended period of limitation in SCN does not establish which of the three (3) special circumstances are alleged and does not demonstrate existence of necessary ingredients.
Ground 3 — Evasion Test Not Met
EVASION REQUIRES ALL 4:
(i) Non-payment of tax
(ii) Knowledge of liability
(iii) Active concealment — detection impaired
(iv) Gains from misadventure
Not every doubtful credit ipsi dixit admits evasion. Empty allegation parroting statutory language defeats Revenue's case.
Ground 4 — Section 75(2) Relief
Impugned Order illegal — relief admissible under Section 75(2) has not been established. Read with Circular 185/17/2022-GST dated 27 Dec. 2022 — said relief is liable to be allowed. Demand to extent of extended limitation is liable to be set aside.
Ground 5 — No Finality of Recovery Against Supplier
Impugned Order illegal — demands ITC reversal without placing on record any material to show investigation against Supplier-in-Default has been initiated and reached finality. No adverse inference can be reached against Appellant where actual delivery of underlying supplies has not been shown to be wholly absent.

27.32 · Penalty for Furnishing False Documents — Section 122

⬆ Index
Penalty Under Section 122 — Three-Element Test Must Be Satisfied
Ground 1 — Time-Barred
Demand for penalty is a demand without mala fides. Penalty cannot exceed underlying cause-of-action. As such, SCN and Impugned Order for Disputed Tax Period is time-barred under Section 75(10). Entire demand for penalty is liable to be set aside.
Ground 2 — Section 122(2) Prevails for Registered Person
Demand for penalty under Section 122 cannot be made in vacuo when demand for credit and interest are not made. Appellant is a registered person — Section 122(2) prevails over Section 122(1). Alleged false declaration refers to Supplier's declaration, NOT Appellant's. No wrongdoing can be imputed to Appellant for submitting declaration made available by Supplier.
Ground 3 — Section 122(1)(x)/(xvii) — 3 Elements
SECTION 122(1)(x) REQUIRES:
(i) Authored falsified documents
(ii) Full knowledge of falsehood
(iii) Authored with intent to evade tax
Appellant fails to satisfy ANY of the conditions. Merely being a taxable person does NOT attract Section 122. Revenue cannot extend scope of Act to include Appellant without showing Appellant themselves generated falsified documents.
Ground 4 — Section 75(13) Double Penalty Bar
Impugned Order contrary to law — imposes penalty on same act twice. Section 75(13) clearly states where penalty is imposed under Section 73, no penalty for the same act or omission shall be imposed under any other provision. Read with Explanation 1(ii) of Section 74(11) — where adjudication under Section 73/74 is concluded, proceedings against all persons liable under Section 122 are deemed concluded.

27.33 · Interception-Led Demands — Consignment

⬆ Index
Section 129 → Section 130 — Sequence Must Be Followed
Statutory Sequence
Section 129 = narrow compass
of Rule 138A infraction
TO INVOKE S.130:
Either detention under S.129
OR seizure under S.67(2)
is essential
CANNOT
Skip S.129 and directly
invoke S.130
Ground 1 — Proceedings Not Concluded Under S.129
Impugned Order is bad in law — directs confiscation without concluding S.129 proceedings (MOV7 notice + MOV9 adjudication order). Proceedings abandoned after MOV6 detention order without factual findings. S.130 demand confirmed without proper investigation. Impugned Order is vague and arbitrary.
Ground 2 — Outdated Procedure (Circular 41/2018)
Impugned Order erroneous — follows procedures of Circular 41/15/2018-GST dated 13 April 2018 which was applicable prior to amendment to S.130 vide Finance Act 2021 with effect from 28 Mar. 2021. Procedure of issuing MOV10 and MOV11 is outdated and contrary to law. Redemption fine imposed without jurisdiction.
Ground 3 — S.130(3) Omitted
WITHOUT PREJUDICE: S.130(3) has been omitted from Section 130 vide Finance Act 2021 (effective 01 Jan. 2022) which provided for levy of tax and penalty "in addition to" redemption fine. In absence of notice in MOV7, levy of tax and penalty equal to 100% of tax is non est and liable to be set aside.
Ground 4 — Bona Fides + No Animus
Where consignment is accompanied by prescribed documents (Rule 138A), demand for penalty under S.129(1) alone cannot be levied. Where facts canvassed by Revenue itself are innovative and demand lacks required animus to show any of the five conditions under S.130, no demand for penalty is applicable.
Ground 5 — Revaluation Not Permitted
No authority to undertake revaluation of consignment in transit. Transaction price under Section 15(1) cannot be rejected without initiating proceedings under Section 67. Notice in MOV10 in absence of MOV7 is based on guesswork, assumption, surmise and conjecture — illegal and without authority of law.

27.34 · Interception-Led Demands — Conveyance

⬆ Index
Conveyance Implicated — Transporter's Non-Complicity Must Be Established
Jurisdictional Requirement
NO INFRACTION UNDER RULE 138A?
→ Detention under S.129 vacated
→ Without lawful seizure, S.130 proceedings = without jurisdiction
Must plead grounds on jurisdiction before entering into absence of ingredients under S.130(1)(i) to (v)
Ground 1 — Confiscation of Conveyance on Supplier's Order
Impugned Order is bad in law — directs confiscation of conveyance merely relying on Order for confiscation of goods of supplier which itself is not in accordance with law (S.129 not concluded). Without factual findings to support S.130(1) allegations, Impugned Order lacks legal authority. Demand vague and arbitrary.
Ground 2 — Bona Fides + Documents Available
Impugned Order is illegal — omits to take cognizance of bona fides of transaction where consignment was accompanied by prescribed documents (Rule 138A + Section 68 requirements). All requirements of S.68 read with S.129 satisfied — redemption fine under S.130(2) cannot survive.
Ground 3 — No Animus for 5 Conditions
Where facts canvassed by Revenue itself are innovative and demand lacks required animus to show ANY ONE of the five conditions under Section 130 of Central GST Act, no demand for fine is applicable.

27.35 · Inquiry-Led Demand — Relief Allowed Selectively

⬆ Index
Partial Relief Allowed — Does NOT Preclude Challenging Defective Notice
Key Strategic Point
IMPORTANT
Acceptance of relief to the extent allowed does NOT bar urging grounds of defective notice and arbitrary adjudication in appeal
Acquiescence at adjudication
can be overcome in appeal
by introducing objections
that ought to have been taken
Ground 1 — Partial Drop Raises No Presumption
Impugned Order is bad in law — to the extent demand dropped by Revenue in Impugned Order raises NO presumption in favour of Revenue as to (i) nature of income (ii) taxability (iii) admissibility of exemption (iv) correctness of point of taxation (v) valuation of allegedly taxable services. Demand dropped without appeal by Revenue has reached finality 'with prejudice'.
Ground 2 — Section 63 Methodology Incompatible
Impugned Order confirms demand computed using methodology permitted in Section 63 which is incompatible with proceedings under Section 73. Impugned Order is illegal and passed without authority of law. Demand confirmed is liable to be set aside in toto.
Ground 3 — Investigation Behind Appellant's Back
Impugned Order is bad in law — confirms demand on basis of investigation conducted behind the Appellant's back. This directly violates principles of natural justice. Revenue is required to safeguard taxpayer's interest and guide them in the process. Complete violation of this duty.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 27 (Para 27.24–27.35)
⬆ Index
These are ready-to-state grounds and ratios for use in personal hearings before FAA, GSTAT, CESTAT. State them with confidence and cite the section/principle directly. Each ground closes with "For this reason, the demand confirmed in the Impugned Order is liable to be set aside."
GST is NOT a Tax on Turnover — It is a Tax on Supply
First Principle | Para 27.25 | Applicable in all escaped turnover matters
"GST is NOT a tax on turnover. It is a tax on supply. Turnover in financial statements is no affirmation of taxable supplies. Financial statements are not a reliable source of evidence in the absence of corroboration. Accounting income cannot be equated with taxable outward supplies. Mere differences in data cannot support adverse inference about turnover escaping tax."
When to Use: Opening statement in every escaped turnover appeal before any forum. Reorients the entire hearing away from accounting data and back to the fundamental character of GST as a transaction-based tax.
4 Taxing Ingredients — Trite Law
Fundamental Principle | Para 27.24 | Applicable to all output tax demands
"It is trite law that in order to attract the incidence of tax, it is necessary that all four (4) ingredients to tax, namely, taxable event, rate of tax, measure of the tax and person liable must be shown. The Impugned Order proceeds on an assumption without establishing the supply involved or its time and place. Merely computing tax does not satisfy the requirements to fasten the liability."
When to Use: When demand is made without establishing the basic levy requirements — stop the argument at the threshold by pointing out that the 4 constitutional elements of a valid tax levy are entirely missing.
Subsidy Excluded from Taxable Value — Section 15 + Section 2(31)
Statutory Principle | Para 27.24
"Subsidy received from State Government is clearly excluded from taxable value under Section 15 read with proviso to Section 2(31) of the Central GST Act. It is mandatory to disclose the amount of subsidy received as income in financial statements and it has been duly disclosed as non-taxable supply in GST returns. For this reason, demand made in the Impugned Order is to be set aside."
When to Use: In all matters where State Government subsidy has been included in taxable turnover — cite the specific exclusion and the disclosure in GST returns as contemporaneous evidence of bona fide treatment.
Trial Balance ≠ Taxable Turnover — GST Cannot Become Tax on Receipts
Jurisprudential Principle | Para 27.26
"To merely compute some tax on 'amounts appearing in Trial Balance' renders this law redundant and converts GST from being a 'tax on supply' to 'tax on receipts'. Amounts appearing in financial statements cannot be relied upon for the reason that they are prepared (i) based on rules of accounting and (ii) NOT based on 'time of supply'. For this reason, the demand for tax is liable to be set aside."
When to Use: When Revenue demands tax by comparing financial statements with GSTR-3B without identifying specific supplies — this argument goes to the root of the levy and is constitutionally sound.
Exemption Parroting — Impugned Order Acting Under Dictation
Jurisprudential Principle | Para 27.27
"Impugned Order routinely parrots the allegations in Impugned SCN as if to be acting under dictation. To merely compute tax does not discharge burden of proof that rests on Revenue. To permit such primitive enquiry would render all exemptions to be 'inadmissible-until-proved admissible'. For this reason, the demand for tax is liable to be set aside."
When to Use: When adjudication order merely repeats SCN grounds without independently examining the exemption claim — powerful language that exposes the lack of independent application of mind by the Adjudicating Officer.
No Gain — No Evasion (Section 74 Challenge)
Jurisprudential Principle | Para 27.31
"Not every claim of doubtful credit ipsi dixit admits evasion of tax. Evasion of tax requires (i) non-payment of tax (or refund or credit) (ii) with knowledge of such liability (iii) active concealment of information or record to impair prompt detection and (iv) gains from this misadventure. Without all of these ingredients, empty allegation parroting statutory language defeats Revenue's case."
When to Use: In every matter where Section 74 has been invoked for ITC disallowance from supplier-in-default — demand Revenue to demonstrate ALL 4 elements of evasion before the extended limitation can be sustained.
Section 75(13) — No Double Penalty Bar
Section 75(13) CGST Act read with Explanation 1(ii) of Section 74(11)
"Section 75(13) of Central GST Act clearly states that where any penalty is imposed under Section 73 of the Act, no penalty for the same act or omission shall be imposed on the same person under any provision of Central GST Act. A conjoint reading of Section 75(13) read with Explanation 1(ii) of Section 74(11) makes it clear that where adjudication under Section 73 or 74 has concluded, proceedings against all persons liable to pay penalty under Section 122 are deemed to be concluded."
When to Use: When separate penalty under Section 122 is demanded alongside adjudication under Section 73/74 — cite this to show that Section 122 proceedings stand automatically concluded once Section 73/74 adjudication is completed.
Revenue Cannot Approbate and Reprobate
Common Law Principle | Para 27.27, 27.30
"Revenue cannot approbate and reprobate. Where the Impugned Order admits the HSN code adopted in self-assessment but denies the corresponding exemption without showing the basis on which exemption is denied, or where the order confirms demand under Section 73 (disaffirming mala fides) yet proceeds to incorporate all incidents of carousel trading — the entire adjudication is rendered an exercise in futility."
When to Use: (a) When HSN code is accepted but exemption denied without reason; (b) When Section 73 is invoked but carousel-trading circular is applied simultaneously — both are classic approbate and reprobate situations.
Section 129 → Section 130 Sequence — MOV7 and MOV9 Mandatory
Statutory Principle | Para 27.33, 27.34 | Interception matters
"Proceedings under Section 129 operate within the narrow compass of infraction under Rule 138A. In order to invoke Section 130, either detention under Section 129 is required or seizure under Section 67(2) will be essential. Where proceedings under Section 129 are abandoned after issuance of MOV6 detention order without factual findings to support Section 130(1) allegations, the Impugned Order lacks legal authority. This renders the Impugned Order vague and arbitrary."
When to Use: In all interception matters where Section 130 confiscation/fine was ordered without completing Section 129 proceedings — challenge the very jurisdiction to invoke Section 130.
Section 130(3) Omitted — Finance Act 2021
Finance Act 2021 | Effective 01 Jan. 2022 | Para 27.33
"Sub-section 3 has been omitted from Section 130 vide Finance Act 2021 notified with effect from 01 Jan. 2022. That sub-section provided for levy of tax and penalty in respect of goods and conveyance to be paid 'in addition to' the redemption fine. In the absence of notice in MOV7, the levy of tax and penalty equal to 100% of tax is non est and liable to be set aside."
When to Use: In all interception matters post 01 Jan. 2022 where 100% tax and penalty is levied in addition to redemption fine — cite the specific amendment to show that this provision no longer exists in law.
Section 155 CGST — Burden of Proof on Revenue for Output Tax
Section 155 CGST Act | Para 27.26
"It is evident from Section 155 of Central GST Act that the Appellant is responsible only to prove eligibility for claiming input tax credit. Hence, the burden of proof with respect to allegations made in respect of demand for output tax in the Impugned Order rests on Revenue. With allegations being disputed by Appellant, burden of proof continues to remain on Revenue to demonstrate the incidence of output tax and hence validity of this demand. Without discharging this burden of proof, demand is not sustainable in law."
When to Use: In any output tax demand based on data differences or assumed turnover — reverse the burden of proof onto Revenue using the express statutory language of Section 155.
Abatement ≠ Exempt Supply — Section 17(3) Scope Limited
Statutory Principle | Para 27.28
"Legal fiction for purposes of reversal of credit is specified in Section 17(3) — covering only 'sale of land' and 'sale of completed buildings' under Schedule III para 5. This does NOT include abatement allowed under tariff notification. Abatement is a legal method of altering the taxable value. Other instances found in Rule 32 provide for no reversal. The Impugned Order makes an assumption that abatement is 'exempt supply' — demand based on such assumption is illegal and liable to be set aside."
When to Use: In real estate / works contract matters where ITC reversal is demanded treating tariff notification abatement as exempt supply — use this to draw the precise boundary of Section 17(3) and keep abatement outside its scope.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 27 — Pleadings in Appeal Memo | Para 27.24–27.35 | Pages 624–645
⚖️ Chapter 27 — Pleadings in Appeal Memo (Para 27.36–27.40)

Chapter 27 — Pleadings in Appeal Memo

Para 27.36–27.40 | Pages 646–665 | Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Table of Contents

27.36 ITC Reversal — Non-Genuine Suppliers 27.37 RNV-1 Against FAA Order 27.38 RNV-1 Against ADT-2 27.39 Recovery of Finalized Demand Before Magistrate 27.40 Affidavit — Condonation of Delay at GSTAT ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

27.36 · ITC Reversal Due to Non-Genuine Suppliers

⬆ Index
Non-Genuine Suppliers — GST Dept's Own Failure Creates the Problem
Foundational Context
Suppliers were granted registration
by GST department
Returns filed were available
for GST Dept scrutiny
Non-filing + non-payment
was in full view of Dept
Cancellation of registration
was by Dept's own
affirmative steps
KEY
Power to demand tax under
S.63 or S.74 was available
with GST Dept throughout
Ground 1 — Section 74 Special Circumstances
Demand under Section 74 without establishing 'special circumstances' is a mischievous and mala fide attempt. Demand within normal limitation would stand reduced to NIL. Extended period demand liable to be set aside.
Ground 2 — 3 Circumstances Not Stated
Extended period invoked in SCN does not establish which of the three (3) special circumstances are alleged and does not demonstrate existence of necessary ingredients. Demand to extent of extended period liable to be set aside.
Ground 3 — Evasion Test (All 4 Elements)
(i) Non-payment of tax
(ii) Knowledge of liability
(iii) Active concealment — detection impaired
(iv) Gains from misadventure
Not every claim of doubtful credit ipsi dixit admits evasion. Empty allegation parroting statutory language defeats Revenue's case.
Ground 4 — Section 75(2) + Circular 185
Relief admissible under Section 75(2) read with Circular 185/17/2022-GST dated 27 Dec. 2022 has not been established. Said relief is liable to be allowed. Demand to extent of extended limitation liable to be set aside.
Ground 5 — No Finding on Supplier Recovery
WITHOUT PREJUDICE: ITC reversal demanded without placing any material on record that investigation against Supplier-in-Default has been initiated and reached finality. Trite law: without material showing actual delivery absent, no adverse inference can be reached against Appellant.
Ground 6 — Genuine Outward Supplies
Impugned Order is contrary to law — inward supplies alleged from non-existent Suppliers yet there is admission (by absence of adverse finding) that outward supplies by Appellant are genuine. It is impossible to effect genuine outward supplies without genuine inward supplies. Investigative failure in SCN and Order.
Strategic Double Warning
WARNING 1
Without pleading Dept's failure to act against supplier, taxpayer-recipient CANNOT overcome S.16(2)(b) presumption favouring Revenue
WARNING 2
If Dept has taken action against supplier → notice to recipient is premature and pre-emptive → risk of double demand → notice is illegal

27.37 · RNV-1 Issued Against Order of Appellate Authority

⬆ Index
Section 108 Revision — Exceptional Jurisdiction — Must Be Challenged First on Jurisdiction
Section 108(1) — 4 Grounds Only
Erroneous + Prejudicial + Illegal
Erroneous + Prejudicial + Improper
Erroneous + Prejudicial + Material facts not taken
Erroneous + Prejudicial + CAG Observation
KEY RULE
Order must be shown to be EITHER erroneous + illegal OR erroneous + improper — NOT both simultaneously
Ground 1 — Multiple Grounds = Ambiguity
Using multiple grounds shows ambiguity in grounds permitted in law. Where RNV-1 uses "erroneous, illegal and improper" — this is gross misapplication of exceptional authority in Section 108. Revenue cannot approbate and reprobate. Revenue must decide WHICH single ground to assail the Order under Revision.
Ground 2 — No Affirmative Finding
Order under Revision does not reach any affirmative finding either in favour of Respondent or Appellant. In the absence of any finding in favour of Appellant, there is no occasion to invoke exceptional jurisdiction under Section 108. RNV-1 is without authority of law.
Ground 3 — Revisionary Authority ≠ Proper Officer
CRITICAL DISTINCTION
Revisionary Authority is excluded from definition of "Adjudicating Authority" in Section 2(4)
Also excluded from definition of "Proper Officer" for S.65/S.67 enquiry/inquiry
Cannot conduct fresh investigation — confined to material already on record
Cannot simultaneously exercise powers as Revision Authority under S.108 AND as Officer superior to PO under S.65/67 read with S.5(2)
Ground 4 — DGGI SCN Abdication
Impugned RNV-1 seeks to overturn Order under Revision by abdicating inquiry under Section 108 to SCN issued by DGGI. To assail findings of Appellate Authority without cogent reasons would render 'prejudice to revenue' as the sole ground — not grounds permitted by statute. No demand for tax can be made in vacuo. Defects in Order are incurably fatal.
Ground 5 — 4 Taxing Ingredients Missing
WITHOUT PREJUDICE: Demand confirmed without finding as to (i) nature of underlying transaction (ii) taxability (iii) HSN code (iv) time of supply (v) place of supply (vi) valuation. Nothing new can be accomplished in revisionary proceedings without this information on record.
Ground 6 — Doctrine of Merger
With the passing of Order under Revision, demand in Order of Adjudication has merged into it by doctrine of merger — finding reached affirmatively that 'no finding is possible' in absence of reliable material at audit and adjudication stage. Original demand cannot be separately revived.
Ground 7 — Tax Period Lapsed
Disputed Tax Period in Order under Revision having passed limitation under Section 73(10) read with Section 75(10). These proceedings are a ruse to overturn findings in the Order under Revision that underlying proceedings relied on estimates and guesswork. Demand suffers due to insufficient information on record.
SC Authority — Govind Saran v. CST
AIR 1985 SC 1041:
"Components of tax are: (i) taxable event attracting levy (ii) person obliged to pay (iii) rate at which imposed (iv) measure/value to which rate applied. If any of these are not clearly ascertainable, it is difficult to say that the levy exists in point of law."

27.38 · RNV-1 Issued Against ADT-2 (Audit Report)

⬆ Index
ADT-2 is NOT a "Decision or Order" — RNV-1 is Premature and Without Jurisdiction
Ground 1 — ADT-2 ≠ Decision/Order
Above-referred Audit Report in Form GST ADT-2 is only a report with findings by Proper Officer under Section 65. It is NOT a "decision or order" subject to proceedings under Section 108(1). RNV-1 is issued even before any "decision or order" has come into existence in law — it is therefore illegal, without authority of law and a nullity in law.
Ground 2 — Section 65(7) Mandatory Step Bypassed
MANDATORY SEQUENCE
Step 1: ADT-2 findings
Step 2: Notice of demand under S.73/74 (mandated by S.65(7))
Step 3: Adjudication under S.73/74
Step 4: Appeal to FAA under S.107
Step 5: ONLY THEN → Section 108(1) revision
These proceedings are patently premature — mandatory step of issuing notice of demand under S.65(7) has been bypassed. RNV-1 has been prematurely invoked. Leary Principle applies — taxpayer has right to due process at each stage.
Ground 3 — Revisionary Authority Excluded from S.2(4)
Revisionary Authority is defined in Section 2(99) and excluded from definition of "Adjudicating Authority" in Section 2(4). Functions vested with Proper Officer who conducted audit under S.65 can be exercised by Superior Officer under S.5(2) — but when exercising powers under S.108(1) you are NOT acting under Section 5(2) and jurisdiction sought is premature and without authority of law.
Ground 4 — Section 112(8) Trap
FATAL CONSEQUENCE
If S.108 proceedings somehow pass adverse order, appeal to GSTAT under S.112(1) cannot be filed — no prior pre-deposit under S.107(6) to make pre-deposit "in addition"
Section 112(8): "no appeal shall be filed, unless...." — registered person rendered WITHOUT REMEDY in law
Without adjudication, revisionary proceedings are contrary to law. Taxpayer is thus deprived of both fair adjudication AND right of appeal.
Ground 5 — Mala Fides to Overcome Limitation
With disputed tax period being FY 2018-19, proceedings initiated solely to overcome bar due to lapse of limitation — Section 75(10) deadline was 31 Jan. 2024 (S.73(1)) and 30 Apr. 2024 (S.73(9)) per Notification 9/2023-CT + 56/2023-CT. This shows mala fides in invoking exceptional jurisdiction under Section 108(1).
Ground 6 — Section 160(2) Bar
Registered Person is barred under Section 160(2) from entertaining or replying to any "notice, order or communication" whose validity and legality is in doubt. Only after validity and legality is established does question of examining on merits arise. These objections are WITHOUT PREJUDICE and Registered Person reserves right to reply on merits when validity stands established.
CIT v. Scindia Steam Navigation
AIR 1961 SC 1633:
"It is well settled that no mandamus will be issued unless the applicant had made a distinct demand on the appropriate authorities for the very reliefs which he seeks to enforce by mandamus and that had been refused."
Leary Principle — Indian Adoption
First cited with approval in Laxmidhar v. State of Orissa AIR 1974 Ori 127 as authoritative in Indian jurisprudence: "If rules and law combine to give member right to fair trial AND right of appeal, why should he be told to be satisfied with an unjust trial and a fair appeal?"

27.39 · Recovery of Finalized Demand Before Magistrate (S.79(1)(g))

⬆ Index
Objections Before Judicial Magistrate — Section 79 Recovery Petition
When This Arises
Demand adjudicated → time to appeal passed → Proper Officer files complaint before Magistrate for recovery via attachment of property under S.85/93 or other relief under S.81
KEY RULE
Just state the position taken in the matter and explain the basis — do NOT complain
Objection 1 — Notice Not Served
Copy of Petition has not been served on Respondent except notice of hearing. Petitioner failed to serve notice of demand under Chapter XV on Respondent. Petition is premature when Respondent has been compliant — filing returns regularly, discharging admitted dues and attending proceedings.
Objection 2 — Section 73 Disaffirms Mala Fides
Petitioner raised demand by issuing notice under Section 73 — which is express affirmation of absence of mala fides. Demand based on estimation, guesswork and conjecture by linear comparison of data — wholly impermissible under Chapter XV to impeach valid self-assessment.
Objection 3 — Ex Parte Order Defective
Respondent suffered adverse Order of Adjudication on ex parte basis — not served in manner commensurate with background of Respondent. Passed in hasty and unjust manner — notice issued and adjudication concluded in haste, although limitation was yet to lapse.
Objection 4 — Audit Officer = Adjudicating Officer (PNJ)
Petitioner violated principles of natural justice by being both (i) the Audit Officer conducting proceedings under Section 65 AND (ii) the Adjudicating Officer who passed the Order of Adjudication under Section 73/74. This is a direct violation of PNJ.
Objection 5 — Statutory Appeal Filed (Section 107(7))
KEY DEFENCE
Statutory appeal under Section 107(1) has been filed — entire disputed demand is deemed stayed in terms of Section 107(7)
Nothing in the entire demand survives at this stage. No prejudice will be caused to Revenue if Petition dismissed as premature.
Objection 6 — Payment Under Protest via DRC-3
Payment of any amount must be via online mode on GSTN Common Portal in Form GST DRC-3 which does NOT have any option as "involuntary" or "under protest". Respondent was compelled to select "voluntary" albeit involuntarily and under protest to stave off repeated follow-up and harassment citing revenue targets.
Refund Entitlement
Amounts deposited "involuntarily and under protest" are liable to be refunded under Section 54 within two (2) years from date of each deposit. Refund application will be filed within limitation.

27.40 · Affidavit — Condonation of Delay at GSTAT

⬆ Index
CoD Affidavit — Explanation (NOT Reasons) of Every Day of Delay
Key Distinction
NOT
Affidavit must NOT contain 'reasons'
MUST HAVE
'Explanation' — of EVERY day of delay
STRUCTURE
First STATE the delay → then EXPLAIN it
(Not long and winded, not cryptic either)
Standard Affidavit Clauses
THAT I am engaged in business under name M/s... holding GST No. ...
THAT taxes are self-assessed under S.59 and duly discharged via S.39 returns
THAT audit conducted under S.65 — duly attended, books made available
THAT SCN + DRC1 served via email but not served in accordance with law
THAT Order of Adjudication passed ex parte with DRC7 under S.73(9)
THAT appeal filed under S.107(1) well within limitation from date of communication
THAT Revenue selected most expeditious mode of 'service' (electronic only) under S.169 — Respondent was misled by 'electronic only' mode unfamiliar in new GST regime
7-Row Illustration Timeline Table
Sl.TimelineReason for Delay
1Up to LAST DATEWas informed remedy was to approach department to rectify errors on facts. Learnt filing appeal before AT was prescribed remedy only after meeting CA.
2From... to...Signed and issued authorization in favour of CA to appear before AT. CA was busy with income-tax return filing assignments.
3From... to...Had to make arrangement for funds to pay pre-deposit due to ongoing financial distress. Appeal memo drafted by AR and sent for signing.
4From... to...After AR relieved from ITR/Tax audit (due date on...), followed up with me. Only then realized appeal papers had to be submitted online on Common Portal apart from making pre-deposit — first time filing statutory appeal in GST.
5From... to...Rushed to file on Common Portal — realized CoD application AND affidavit additionally required. Took help of Notary to draft same and sent scans to AR for assistance in filing.
6...Additional fee for CoD application deposited in ECL.
7...Defects noted by Registrar, AT — attended by AR to satisfaction of Registrar.
Closing Clauses — All Mandatory
THAT no other reason for delay — NOT deliberate or neglect
THAT no prejudice to Revenue if delay condoned
THAT a good case is made out on merits — ends of justice better served by condoning delay
THAT application is submitted before Hon'ble Bench
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Para 27.36–27.40
⬆ Index
These are ready-to-state grounds and principles for use in revision proceedings before Revisionary Authority, recovery proceedings before Magistrate, and GSTAT. State them confidently with the section and citation.
GST Department's Own Failure — Non-Genuine Supplier
Jurisprudential Principle | Para 27.36
"Suppliers were granted registration by GST department, returns filed were available with GST department for scrutiny, non-filing and non-payment of returns was in full view of GST department and cancellation of registration was by affirmative steps taken by GST department. Without confirming whether any steps were taken by GST department against said Suppliers, now held to be in-default, routinely demanding reversal of credit is unlikely to survive in later proceedings."
When to Use: In all non-genuine supplier ITC reversal matters — shift the spotlight from recipient's conduct to department's own regulatory failure. If department failed to act under S.63/74 when it could have, the entire foundation of the demand against recipient collapses.
Govind Saran Ganga Saran v. CST — 4 Elements of Tax Levy
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1985 SC 1041
"The components which enter into the concept of a tax are well known. The first is the character of the imposition known by its nature which prescribes the taxable event attracting the levy, the second is a clear indication of the person on whom the levy is imposed, the third is the rate at which the tax is imposed, and the fourth is the measure of or value to which the rate will be applied for computing the tax liability. If those components are not clearly and definitely ascertainable, it is difficult to say that the levy exists in point of law. Any uncertainty or vagueness in the legislative scheme defined any of the components of the levy will be fatal to its validity."
When to Use: In revision proceedings (RNV-1) and before Magistrate — cite to establish that where SCN and adjudication order lack any of the 4 essential taxing components, the entire levy is constitutionally invalid and the revisionary proceedings have no legally valid foundation to rest upon.
Section 108(1) — Exceptional Jurisdiction — Revenue Must Choose ONE Ground
Section 108(1) CGST Act | Para 27.37
"In order to initiate revisionary proceedings against any decision or order, any ONE of the following reasons must be shown to exist, namely, the order or decision is: (i) erroneous, prejudicial to interest of revenue and illegal; or (ii) erroneous, prejudicial to interest of revenue and improper; or (iii) erroneous, prejudicial to interest of revenue, not taken certain material facts; or (iv) erroneous, prejudicial to interest of revenue, in consequence of a CAG observation. Order under Revision must be shown to either be erroneous and illegal OR erroneous and improper — NOT both simultaneously. To use multiple grounds shows ambiguity in the grounds permitted in law to invoke this exceptional jurisdiction."
When to Use: Whenever RNV-1 uses multiple grounds ("erroneous, illegal and improper") simultaneously — this is a fatal defect in the revision notice itself that goes to jurisdiction and can be raised as the very first preliminary ground.
ADT-2 is NOT a "Decision or Order" — Section 65(7) Mandatory Step
Section 65(6) and 65(7) CGST Act | Para 27.38
"Form GST ADT-2 is only a report of findings by Proper Officer in proceedings under Section 65 of Central GST Act. It is NOT a 'decision or order' that is subject to proceedings under Section 108(1). Proper Officer is obliged to 'put at notice' the Registered Person by way of notice of demand to be issued under Section 73 or 74. These proceedings under Section 108(1) are patently premature and without authority of law — the mandatory step of issuing a notice of demand under Section 65(7) has been bypassed."
When to Use: Whenever a Revisionary Authority issues RNV-1 directly against an Audit Report (ADT-2) without waiting for adjudication — this single argument goes to the entire jurisdiction of the revision proceedings and makes it a nullity in law.
Section 112(8) Trap — Registered Person Left Without Remedy
Section 112(8) CGST Act | Para 27.38
"If revisionary proceedings proceed under Section 108(1) without prior adjudication and pass an adverse order, appeal to the Appellate Tribunal cannot be filed as there is no prior pre-deposit under Section 107(6) to make a pre-deposit 'in addition' as required. Section 112(8) states that 'no appeal shall be filed, unless....' — this renders the Registered Person without a remedy in law. Without adjudication, revisionary proceedings are contrary to law."
When to Use: As a supplementary argument to the ADT-2 ground — demonstrates that if RNV-1 against ADT-2 is allowed to proceed, it destroys the entire appellate structure for the taxpayer, stripping them of their right to appeal to GSTAT.
Leary Principle — Indian Adoption (Laxmidhar v. State of Orissa)
AIR 1974 Ori 127 | First Indian adoption of Leary Principle from Leary v. Northern Union (1970) 2 All ER 713
"If one accepts the contention that a defect of natural justice in the trial body can be cured by the presence of natural justice in the appellate body, this has the result of depriving the member of his right of appeal from the expelling body. If the rules and the law combine to give the member the right to a fair trial and the right of appeal, why should he be told that he ought to be satisfied with an unjust trial and a fair appeal?"
When to Use: In RNV-1 against ADT-2 proceedings where mandatory notice of demand under S.65(7) was bypassed — argue that bypassing the due process deprives taxpayer of both the right to fair adjudication AND the right of appeal to GSTAT, which is constitutionally impermissible.
Section 107(7) — Deemed Stay on Filing of Appeal
Section 107(7) CGST Act | Para 27.39 | Recovery proceedings before Magistrate
"With the statutory appeal under Section 107(1) of Central GST Act having been filed, the entire disputed demand is deemed to be stayed in terms of Section 107(7) of Central GST Act. As such, nothing in the entire demand in said Order of Adjudication survives at this stage and no prejudice will be caused to Revenue if this Petition were to be dismissed, as it was invoked in a premature and unjust manner when several remedies are still available in law for Petitioner to pursue, especially when this Respondent has undertaken to discharge any demand when the same is confirmed in accordance with law and becomes due and payable."
When to Use: Before Magistrate in recovery petition — cite Section 107(7) to show that the moment a statutory appeal is filed, the entire demand stands stayed by operation of law. The recovery petition becomes not just premature but legally unsustainable.
DRC-3 Has No "Under Protest" Option — Payment Involuntary
Practical / Procedural Principle | Para 27.39
"Payment of any amount must be via online mode on GSTN Common Portal vide challan in Form GST DRC-3 which does not have any option as 'involuntary' or 'under protest'. Respondent is compelled to select 'voluntary' as the category for making said deposit albeit involuntarily and under protest — only to stave off repeated follow-up and harassment by Petitioner citing revenue targets imposed by the administration. Such payment does not constitute an admission of liability."
When to Use: In recovery proceedings and in appeal — establish that any payment made on DRC-3 under Revenue pressure was involuntary and does not amount to acceptance of the demand or waiver of the right to challenge it. Refund can be claimed under Section 54 within 2 years.
CoD Affidavit — Explanation Not Reasons (GSTAT)
Practice Principle | Para 27.40
"An affidavit for condonation of delay must not contain 'reasons' but 'explanation' — that too of every day of delay. It requires that the affidavit first STATE the delay (so there is no dispute as to extent of delay and that such delay is not beyond the power of condonation if the explanation is found sufficient), and then proceed to offer the explanation. Not long and winded, but not cryptic either. No prejudice will be caused to Revenue if delay is condoned and a good case is made out on merits in the appeal filed — ends of justice are better served by condoning this delay."
When to Use: In every GSTAT CoD application — use this framework to distinguish explanation from reasons, and to ensure every day of delay is covered chronologically in a table format rather than in a narrative, making the affidavit both comprehensive and concise.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 27 — Para 27.36–27.40 | Pages 646–665
⚖️ Chapter 25 — Pleadings for Reply to Notices

⚖️ Chapter 25 — Pleadings for Reply to Notices

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
25.1 Replying to Notices
25.2 Relief Prayed
25.3 Burden of Proof
25.4 Accept and Discharge
25.5 Dispute and Litigate
25.6 Reject Allegations
25.7 Burden on Taxpayer
25.8 Options to Reply
25.9 Departmental Observations
25.10 Payments via DRC-3 (1 & 2)
25.12 GSTR-2A vs GSTR-3B Mismatch
25.13 GSTR-2A vs GSTR-3B Mismatch 2
25.14 Interest on Belated Filing of GSTR-3B
25.15 URP-RCM Unpaid 2017-18
25.16–25.17–25.18 RCM Unpaid 1-2-3
25.19 Invoice of Earlier Tax Period in Current GSTR-3B
25.20 Tax Paid in Excess and Adjusted
25.21 Credit Notes (with GST) Received
25.22–25.23 Credit Notes (without GST) Received
25.24–25.25 Debit Notes Issued & Output Tax on Debit Notes
25.26–25.27 Credit Notes Issued (Domestic & Zero-Rated)
25.28–25.29 Non-Disclosure of Exempt Turnover & EWB-GSTR Mismatch
25.30 Mismatch GSTR-1 with GSTR-3B
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
25.1 — Replying to Notices: Philosophy & Nature of Reply
"You cannot get what you do not ask" — pleadings are the points of objections in reply. Even a justice-oriented authority cannot allow what was omitted in pleadings.
REPLY TO NOTICE — NATURE, PURPOSE & STRATEGIC PHILOSOPHY
What is a Reply?
A STATEMENT OF POSITION of taxpayer — NOT an explanation of self-assessment
Need not be elaborate — sufficient to disclose position taken
"We do not agree" = as good as any 10-page reply. "We do not agree, because it is not payable" = complete in law
E-Service of Notices
Ignoring GST notices → forfeiture of remedies. Monitor registered email + Common Portal constantly
E-service: recognized mode for Revenue BUT least expeditious for taxpayers not well-versed with computers
Two Ways to Challenge
(a) Challenge notice — reject allegations entirely
(b) Accept validity of allegations — rebut and offer explanation
What Pleadings May Contain
A question as to correctness of facts considered
Challenge to evidence adduced in support of allegations
Dispute the demand AND reject the allegations — leaving burden on Proper Officer to substantiate prima facie case
25.2 Relief Prayed + 25.3 Burden of Proof
RELIEF & BURDEN OF PROOF — TWIN PILLARS OF EVERY REPLY
Relief Must Be Specific
RULE
Relief NOT prayed CANNOT be allowed — even if allowable in law
Vague prayer ("pass such orders as deemed fit") = bare minimum for uninitiated
Specific: demand time-barred / demand set aside / refund sanctioned / POS outside India → these are reliable precedents
Burden of Proof — Key Principles
RULE
Burden rests on one MAKING the allegation — Revenue — NOT on Registered Person suffering it
Burden is NOT discharged by making even a plausible allegation
Discharged ONLY when mountain of evidence commensurate with nature of allegation is produced
Simple allegations → some evidence. Severe wrong-doing → proportionately substantial evidence
What Counts as Evidence?
Books of account are NOT reliable evidence unless corroborated
Statements-on-oath = weakest form of evidence — especially when containing admission of wrong-doing
Third-party witness statements unreliable without opportunity of cross-examination
Self-Assessment Presumption
GST = self-assessment regime. Even decision NOT to register = outcome of self-assessment (Sec 23)
Where self-assessment is challenged — burden rests on Revenue making allegation
Allow Revenue to establish "their case" first before rushing to justify self-assessment
25.4 Accept & Discharge + 25.5 Dispute & Litigate — Strategic Decision
ACCEPT vs DISPUTE — THE TAXPAYER'S STRATEGIC FORK
When to Accept & Discharge
Accept when: (i) creditable (ii) can be passed on to customers (iii) not material (iv) no precedent value for future periods (v) clear and unambiguous
Payment: via DRC-3 or GSTR-3B (RCM). GSTR-3B flows credit to ECrL; DRC-3 does NOT for RCM
Can review within 2 years by making refund claim if acceptance was in anxiety, involuntary or misunderstanding
Decision Cannot Be Outsourced
Accept/discharge decision = choice of TAXPAYER alone — NOT tax officer, NOT advisor
No mala fides can be imputed to tax officer who informs of undischarged liability
Every demand in writing + DRC-1A (pre-notice) or DRC-1/2 (post notice). Payment via DRC-3
When to Dispute & Litigate
ALL other instances — demands beg to be disputed
Minimum reply: "Demand is disputed and further proceedings may be initiated in accordance with law"
No requirement to explain reasons in pre-notice proceedings (scrutiny, audit, inspection)
Bank attachment threat: Sec 83 = provisional attachment, NOT remittance order. Revoked after 1 year or when SCN is issued
DANGER — DRC-1A
Elaborate reply in Part B of DRC-1A can: (i) expose defences prematurely (ii) provide fodder for better-quality SCN (iii) extend shot-in-the-arm to Revenue's case. Limit Part B reply to: (i) factual errors only (ii) contemporaneous documents — nothing more
25.6 Reject Allegations + 25.7 Burden on Taxpayer + 25.8 Options to Reply + 25.9 Departmental Observations
HOW TO REJECT ALLEGATIONS + TAXPAYER'S LIMITED BURDEN
How to Reject
Rejecting ≠ disproving. Rejecting = making position known that allegations are NOT accepted
Magic phrase: "It is the noticee's position that ………" + "Demand is disputed and allegations are rejected"
These two sentences: burden firmly planted on Revenue — taxpayer NOT obliged to prove innocence
Taxpayer's Specific Burden (Sec 155)
NO burden to prove innocence — Sec 155 cannot impose unnatural burden except to show vesting conditions under Sec 16/17/18
SPECIFIC burdens on taxpayer only: (a) Sec 34 credit notes compliance (b) concessional tariff/exemption conditions (c) absence of prohibited relations (d) maintain contemporaneous records
GSTR-9 filed → no occasion to seek explanation of any data mismatch with GSTR-1 or 3B
Options to Reply
Notices must NOT be ignored. But taxpayer has option to "accept or reject" demand
Where liability is disputed — replies range from "short yet sufficient" to "bit more elaborate but not excessive"
Detailed replies reserved ONLY for show cause notice stage — NOT pre-notice
Departmental Observations (Sec 160(2))
Communication via letter/undefined notice — Registered Person may reply: "communication not authorized in law"
Sec 160(2): Registered Person is entitled to question such communication AND refuse to reply on merits — only SCN triggers mandatory response
25.10 + 25.11 — Payment Made via DRC-3: Template Replies
These are the template replies for use when payment has been made via DRC-3. Three variants for "Liability Not Accepted" and one for "Liability Accepted".
DRC-3 PAYMENT — REPLY FRAMEWORK
LIABILITY ACCEPTED
Certain liability has been admitted and discharged vide DRC-3 challan No.___ dated___. It is also noted that applicable interest has not been discharged on this payment.
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Discrepancy noted. Entire demand DISPUTED, allegations REJECTED for being contrary to facts and to law. There is no clarity as to the basis on which interest is demanded.
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Discrepancy noted. Entire demand DISPUTED. In view of above: (i) levy of tax OR (ii) demand of tax is erroneous and liable to be refunded. Registered Person is in process of claiming refund of the same. Demand for interest is rejected.
NOT ACCEPTED 3
Discrepancy noted. Demand is DISPUTED. Allegations rejected for being contrary to facts and to law. There is no clarity as to basis on which interest is demanded. Relevant amendments to interest rate notification as follows: [table of Notifications 13/2017-CT, 31/2020-CT, 51/2020-CT, 18/2021-CT]
SEC 41 AMENDMENT NOTE — With amendment to Sec 41 of CGST Act: there is no requirement to maintain one-to-one correlation with respect to utilization of credit. Liability discharged via DRC-3 (credit) challan means tax is already discharged at the time of discharge. SCN makes a "double demand" of GST on reverse charge basis when payment was already discharged — this is contrary to law.
25.12 — Mismatch of GSTR-2A with GSTR-3B 1 (ITC Demand)
GSTR-2A vs GSTR-3B ITC MISMATCH — REPLY FRAMEWORK
Key Legal Principles
GSTR-2A does NOT authorize reversal of ITC — mere difference does not imply inadmissibility
Inward supplies are bona fide, duly received, properly accounted, paid via normal banking
Input tax credit is bona fide vested right — indefeasible when all vesting conditions satisfied
Burden of proof as to bona fides of inward supplies: Revenue must discharge (Sec 155)
Rule 36(4) Compliance
Rule 36(4) (pre-9 Oct 2019): No demand for reversal of ITC could be made prior to this date — Notification 49/2019-CT dated 9 Oct 2019
Rule 36(4) (post 9 Oct 2019): allows up to 20% of bona fide mismatch uploads — no automatic reversal
Press Release 4 May 2018: No automatic reversal of ITC from buyer for non-payment by supplier
Demand for Reversal Infirmities
GSTR-2A is itself NOT matching with GSTR-9 Table 8A — data is not reliable
Circular 183/15/2022-GST dated 27 Dec 2022 admits GSTR-2A may have reasons for mismatch that are not valid grounds for reversal
Demand for reversal = ipso facto not valid and admissible
Demand based on data before Jan 1, 2022 cannot rely on ASMT-10 subject notice/information
Reply Template Variants
ACC 1 Credit appearing in GSTR-2A for FY in question: credit claimed in GSTR-3B; interest reversed immediately
ACC 2 Discrepancy noted; liability duly discharged; or reversal vide debit entry
REJ 1 Demand disputed; WITHOUT PREJUDICE, discrepancy is immaterial; detailed reconciliation provided
REJ 2 GSTR-2A data itself not matching with GSTR-9; data for comparison is not matching with report downloaded; demand rejected
REJ 3 Entire demand disputed. No burden on Appellant; Sec 155 stands discharged; burden to remain on Revenue
REJ 4 (ITC) Credit claimed for disputed period after satisfying all vesting conditions in Sec 16 — vested and indefeasible right — none of Sec 17/18 bars attracted
25.13 — Mismatch of GSTR-2A with GSTR-3B 2 (Supplier Filed but Omitted GSTR-3B)
GSTR-2A vs GSTR-3B 2 — BORROW REASONS FOR NOT ACCEPTING LIABILITY
Position of Registered Person
No change in accounting treatment / GST law position — no adverse inference
ITC admitted under Sec 16(2) — all vesting conditions satisfied — vested & indefeasible — Sec 17(2)/(4)/(5) NOT attracted
No recovery action against Registered Person without showing (i) proceedings under Sec 79 (ii) final recovery against said Suppliers (iii) lapse of appeal process
Interest + Penalty
Interest demand: subject to Rule 88B — any demand for reversal of ITC, interest only where demand pertains to reversal
Penalty: not sustainable in law without required animus — cannot be imposed in mechanical or routine manner
Where recovery made from Registered Person: double demand — violative of law — any recovery is a double demand
25.14 — Interest on Belated Filing of GSTR-3B
Interest on belated GSTR-3B filing is tantamount to "double demand" — violative of Articles 246A, 265 and 300A of the Constitution of India and a self-contradiction that is unjust and unsustainable as per law and the Government's own interpretation of the applicable statutory provisions.
INTEREST ON BELATED GSTR-3B — REPLY VARIANTS
LIABILITY ACCEPTED
GSTR-3B returns filed on certain dates. Applicable interest discharged and admitted. Challan attached.
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Entire demand disputed. Challan attached. In view of immaterial amount — WITHOUT PREJUDICE — liability duly discharged.
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Interest demand contrary to facts & law. Relevant amendments: Notification 13/2017-CT (interest rates), 31/2020-CT (Feb-Apr 2020 concession), 51/2020-CT (substitution), 18/2021-CT (Mar-Apr 2021 special concession).
KEY PRINCIPLE
In view of GSTC decision (45th Council): interest to be charged only in respect of net cash liability, Sec 50(3) amended retrospectively — interest on "ineligible ITC availed and utilized" not on "ineligible ITC availed"
25.15 to 25.18 — URP-RCM Unpaid (2017-18) & RCM Unpaid 1-2-3
RCM demand in 2017-18 for URP supplies was under Sec 9(4) — a short-lived provision. Supplier's registration status is a fact based on Common Portal — not guesswork. Demands have seldom stated the basis supporting the allegation of infraction.
RCM DEMAND REPLY — CORE DEFENCES ACROSS ALL THREE VARIANTS
Core Legal Defences
Without demonstrable liability — self-assessment carried out under Sec 59 CANNOT be disturbed
Merely selecting "heads of expense" and computing demand = guesswork — not sufficient to make a lawful allegation
Demand for output tax on "reverse charge basis" cannot be made without showing ingredients to attract incidence of tax under Sec 9(1)
Place of Supply Defences
CGST-SGST: assumes place of supply within State — assumption that is not permitted in law
IGST: assumes place of supply outside State — assumption that is not permitted in law
Both: demanding tax without thorough audit — burden of proof that rests on Revenue cannot be done except by audit
RCM 3 — Specified Payments
Impugned SCN makes "double demand": (i) full extent of RCM liability already discharged from ECrL at time of discharge (ii) credit therefor already discharged during disputed tax period
SCN does NOT adduce evidence in support of demand — second time demand on reverse charge basis
Registered Person's position: no change in accounting treatment — it is self-assessment carried under Sec 59 — no adverse inference
Interest + Penalty
Interest: subject to Rule 88B; without showing demand pertains to reversal of ITC — interest not sustainable
Penalty: not sustainable without animus shown under Sec 126 — demand for penalty cannot be imposed in mechanical manner
25.16–25.17 — RCM Unpaid 1 & 2: CGST-SGST vs IGST Specific Defences
RCM UNPAID — SPECIFIC COMMON DEFENCES
LIABILITY ACCEPTED
Liability on RCM duly discharged from time to time. As applicable, no arrears undischarged for disputed period. Discrepancy/finding noted is rejected.
NOT ACCEPTED 1
No demonstrable liability. Sec 9(1) ingredients to attract incidence of tax cannot be made. Self-assessment carried out by Registered Person cannot be interfered with.
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Tax leviable under Sec 9(1) CGST is subject to exemption — notifications 8/2017-CT (Rate), 2/2017-CT (Rate) and 12/2017-Integrated — supplies in column (2) are those specified. Supplier's data on Common Portal may be in error — without determining correctness, demand cannot be raised.
NOT ACCEPTED 3
No output tax liability — forward or reverse charge — that remains undischarged for disputed period. Demand for output tax is violative of known tenets of taxation. Discrepancy/finding noted rejected.
25.19 — Invoice of Earlier Tax Periods Included in Current GSTR-3B
Tax invoice of earlier tax period included and liability discharged in GSTR-3B of later tax periods. Sec 41(2) of CGST mandates ITC be discharged out of mandate in section 41(2). Utilization of credit of later tax periods to discharge liability of earlier tax periods tantamount to unpaid arrears — due along with interest as applicable.
EARLIER PERIOD INVOICE IN CURRENT GSTR-3B — REPLY
LIABILITY ACCEPTED 1
Challan attached. Liability duly discharged.
LIABILITY ACCEPTED 2 — WITHOUT PREJUDICE
Discrepancy noted. Demand disputed. Immaterial amount — attached challan. Liability duly discharged.
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Demand disputed. Tax already discharged via GSTR-3B — earlier tax period invoice & liability discharged in subsequent period. Demand and admission that tax has already been discharged = "double demand" violating Articles 246A, 265, 300A Constitution.
25.20 — Tax Paid in Excess and Adjusted
TAX PAID IN EXCESS — ADJUSTED IN LATER PERIOD
LEGAL FOUNDATION
Output tax paid in excess in earlier period → adjusted by credit note issued → withholding payment to extent of short payment → Section 34 CGST Act allows this. Adjustment is suo motu via credit notes. Returns may be amended under Sec 37 and 39.
LIABLE ACCEPTED 1
Challan or reversal vide debit entry attached. Liability duly discharged.
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Demand disputed. Demand for discharge along with admission that tax already discharged = "double demand" — violative of Articles 246A, 265, 300A Constitution.
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Adjustment of output tax carried out per Circular 26/26/2017-GST (17 Feb 2023 circular — "negative values" in GSTR-3B adjustment permitted). Making a "second demand" without a remedy of refund = ultra vires Articles 246A, 265, 300A.
25.21 — Credit Notes (with GST) Received from Suppliers
Credit notes received from Suppliers must be accompanied by tax invoice. ITC is reversed in GSTR-3B by Registered Person. Credit notes are not affected in GSTR-3B by said credit notes — but ITC claimed to the extent of credit notes must be reversed per Rule 37.
CREDIT NOTES RECEIVED (WITH GST) — REPLY FRAMEWORK
LIABILITY ACCEPTED
Liability duly discharged; reversal or debit entry made.
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Demand disputed. Discrepancy is immaterial. WITHOUT PREJUDICE — challan attached. Detailed reconciliation to be provided.
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Entire demand disputed. GSTR-2A relied for comparison — itself not matching with GSTR-9 for same tax period. GSTR-2A visible to Registered Person on Common Portal — data is itself erroneous. Demand for reversal of ITC rejected.
NOT ACCEPTED 3
Discrepancy noted. Entire demand disputed. Allegations rejected. Suppliers with GSTIN who have inadvertently reported — Circular 183/15/2022-GST — they must also be excluded.
25.22 + 25.23 — Credit Notes (without GST) Received: Inward & Outward
CREDIT NOTES WITHOUT GST — KEY PRINCIPLES + REPLY
Credit Note (No GST) — Inward
Determination of tax treatment by Supplier does NOT bar Registered Person from correctly carrying out self-assessment
Requirement for reversal of credit: (i) not barred Registered Person (ii) Supplier(s) cannot be thrust — Remedy in Recipient is condition applicable to said Supplier(s) NOT fait accompli
Without establishing "failure" of Registered Person to pay value of supplies — merely a contractual revision in price — does NOT attract reversal of credit
Credit Note (No GST) — Outward
Outward credit note without GST must be accompanied by tax invoice — Sec 34 CGST Act
No output tax payable — credit note implies correction of earlier invoice. It is admitted that Supplier cannot issue zero-rated credit note
Unless it can be shown that any credit note is NOT in accordance with Sec 34 — impugning ALL credit notes is arbitrary & contrary to law
Place of Supply Defences
Demand for output tax as CGST-SGST: demands that supplies are within State — assumption not permitted in law
Without showing ingredients to attract output tax under Sec 9(1) — demand for outward supplies cannot be interfered with
Inward supplies do NOT attract incidence of tax — any demand for outward supplies without taxing ingredients is illegal and arbitrary
25.24 + 25.25 — Debit Notes Issued + Output Tax on Debit Notes Discharged Belatedly
Debit notes issued in respect of outward supplies of earlier tax periods. Output tax on debit notes reported and discharged via GSTR-3B of current tax periods. Time of supply of debit note = date of tax invoice originally issued (Sec 12(6)/13(6)). As such, output tax of earlier periods discharged out of credit of later periods — tantamount to unpaid arrears due with interest.
DEBIT NOTES — TIME OF SUPPLY + INTEREST DEMAND
LIABILITY ACCEPTED
Output tax discharged with interest. Challan attached.
ACCEPTED 2 — WITHOUT PREJUDICE
Immaterial amount. Challan attached. Demand disputed in view of immaterial nature.
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Demand disputed. Entire demand for discharge along with admission of already discharged tax = "double demand" — violative of Articles 246A, 265, 300A. Output tax payable discharged out of ECrL without satisfying one-to-one correlation.
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Debit notes issued promptly and contemporaneously. Circumstances well-documented. No delay in issuing or reporting in GSTR-1 or discharging applicable tax via GSTR-3B. When no actionable cause under CGST Act is attracted — demand for interest on output tax payable is arbitrary and contrary to law. Interest rate notifications: 13/2017-CT, 31/2020-CT, 51/2020-CT, 18/2021-CT apply.
25.26 + 25.27 — Credit Notes Issued for Domestic & Zero-Rated Supplies
CREDIT NOTES ISSUED (DOMESTIC + ZERO-RATED) — LEGAL PRINCIPLES
Domestic Sales — Sec 34 Compliance
Credit notes: permitted in five (5) circumstances listed in Sec 34 + subject to two conditions: (i) one credit per tax invoice (ii) issued within Sept. of subsequent financial year
Tax when discharged cannot be summarily denied — Sec 34 statutory provisions apply
Without showing which credit notes are NOT in accordance with Sec 34 — impugning all credit notes is arbitrary. Circular 26/26/3017-GST dated 27 Dec 2017 instructs.
Zero-Rated Supplies — IGST Condition
Credit notes on zero-rated: (i) per Sec 34 in five circumstances (ii) it is one credit → is to be issued for each tax invoice subject to two conditions: (i) it is one credit → issued within Sept. financial year (ii) payment not repatriated → credit liable to be discharged as IGST
When tax was not payable to IGST — correction to extent of original invoice — no correction to tax on which tax is issued
Without showing five specific circumstances and two conditions — demand for IGST in every instance is not valid
25.28 + 25.29 — Non-Disclosure of Exempt Turnover + EWB & GSTR-1/3B Mismatch
NON-DISCLOSURE + E-WAY BILL MISMATCH — KEY DEFENCES
Non-Disclosure: Exempt Turnover
Omission to report exempt/non-taxable supplies is a non-essential omission — voluntary disclosure in annual returns is adequate
Demand for output tax based on non-disclosure presumptuous and conjecture — demand in GSTR-9 table 5 not attracted
Without showing taxing ingredients — demand for output tax under Sec 9(1) or 5(1) IGST cannot be made
E-Way Bill vs GSTR-1/3B
EWB value of outward supplies is GREATER than taxable value in GSTR-3B: difference represents taxable value liable to tax
E-waybill data: (i) inward supplies NOT involving supply (ii) outward supplies net (iii) purchase returns net → EWB much less about taxable turnover than GSTR-3B
Without showing taxing ingredients to attract Sec 9(1) — demand for mismatch of consignment value of EWB with GSTR-3B is not attracted
Data reported in GSTR-3B is reliable — evidenced by unreconciled turnover in GSTR-9/9C being "nil"
25.30 — Mismatch of GSTR-1 with GSTR-3B
GSTR-1 is greater than taxable value in GSTR-3B returns. The difference represents taxable value that is liable to tax. Instruction No. 1/2022 dated 7 Jan 2022 prohibit recovery action merely on the basis of data differences. Rule 88C: specific procedure for data differences between GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B.
GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B MISMATCH — FOUR REPLY VARIANTS
LIABILITY ACCEPTED 1
Tax duly discharged. Challan or debit entry attached.
ACCEPTED 2 — WITHOUT PREJUDICE
Immaterial amount. Challan attached. Detailed reconciliation in support provided.
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Demand disputed. No such inference possible. Without excluding e-way bills and comparing GSTR-1 & GSTR-3B: linear comparison is erroneous. Instruction No. 1/2022 (7 Jan 2022) prohibit recovery on data differences.
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Demand disputed. GSTR-1 not verified for the financial year — comparison faulty. With extended time under Sec 37 — data comparison extends beyond given financial year. Central GST (Second Removal of Difficulties) Order 2018 mandates data comparison over even larger period.
NOT ACCEPTED 4 — Registered Person discharged output tax in accordance with law. No liability on forward or reverse charge basis that remains undischarged. Without incidence of tax, demand for output tax on data differences is violative of known tenets of taxation. Impugned SCN does NOT state statutory provisions: (i) category as goods or services (ii) taxability (iii) HSN code (iv) time of supply (v) place of supply. Demand for CGST-SGST violative of Sec 77 CGST Act + Sec 19 IGST Act.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
⇧ Index
Press Release dated 4 May 2018 — No Automatic Reversal of ITC
There shall not be any automatic reversal of credit from buyer on non-payment of tax by the seller. In case of default in payment of tax by the seller, recovery shall be made from the seller; however reversal of credit from buyer shall also be an option available with revenue authorities to address exceptional situations like missing dealer, closure of business etc.
When to Use: GSTR-2A mismatch ITC demands — cite to show no automatic reversal lies against buyer
Instruction No. 1/2022 dated 7 Jan 2022 — Recovery Not Merely on Data Differences
Recovery proceedings initiated merely on the basis of data differences between GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B are prohibited. The procedure prescribed is under Rule 88C of Central GST Rules — specific procedure must be followed before any recovery can be initiated.
When to Use: GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B mismatch demand — raise this as a preliminary bar to any recovery action
45th GST Council Decision — Interest on Net Cash Liability Only
In the spirit of 45th GST Council meeting resolution dated 17 September 2021 and Sec 50(3) of CGST Act as amended retrospectively: interest is to be charged only in respect of net cash liability, section 50(3) is to be applied by taxpayer on "ineligible ITC availed and utilized" and not on "ineligible ITC availed".
When to Use: Any interest demand on belated GSTR-3B filing or ITC reversal — cite this to show interest basis is flawed
Circular 183/15/2022-GST dated 27 Dec 2022 — GSTR-2A Mismatch Reasons
GSTR-2A data may have various other reasons that explain the mismatch. GSTR-2A data taken for comparison is itself not matching with GSTR-9 table 8A stated in ASMT-10 for the same tax period. Demand for reversal of input tax credit is not valid and proper such demand for reversal of ITC is not valid and admissible.
When to Use: GSTR-2A ITC mismatch demands — shows even Revenue's own circular admits GSTR-2A data unreliability
Section 77 CGST Act + Section 19 IGST Act — Wrong Tax Must Be Corrected
It is the scheme of the GST law that payment of "wrong tax" does not satisfy the requirements of law. Section 77 of Central GST Act read with Section 19 of Integrated GST Act demands payment of "correct tax" again. For this reason, demanding CGST-SGST when IGST was paid (or vice versa) cannot be a valid demand — correct tax must be recovered through proper proceedings.
When to Use: Any demand for CGST-SGST when IGST was paid — or when Revenue simultaneously demands CGST-SGST on top of IGST already paid
Articles 246A, 265, 300A Constitution — Double Demand Prohibition
Demand for output tax along with admission that said tax has already been discharged is tantamount to "double demand" which is violative of articles 246A, 265 and 300A of the Constitution of India and a self-contradiction that is unjust and unsustainable as per law and according to the Government's own interpretation of the applicable statutory provisions.
When to Use: When Revenue demands payment of already-discharged tax in any format — DRC-3, GSTR-3B, credit note, debit note
Rule 36(4) — Notification 49/2019-CT dated 9 Oct 2019
Rule 36(4) was introduced from 9 Oct 2019 by Notification 49/2019-CT and amended by Notification 94/2019-CT dated 11 Nov 2019. Data available for tax period covered by sub-ject proceedings cannot be relied upon for purposes of making demand — without invoking any actionable cause in law, data mismatch cannot be used as basis for reversal of input tax credit.
When to Use: Any GSTR-2A ITC mismatch for periods pre-9 Oct 2019 — no demand could have been made at all; and even post Oct 2019, relaxation of 20% applied
Senco Gold Ltd — Account Settlement as Valid Payment Mode (Para 27.17)
Settlement of creditors includes settlement via 'account adjustment' as recognized in Senco Gold 02/WBAAR/2019-20 dated 8 May 2019. Payment to suppliers is NOT limited to bank payment but also includes payment by return supplies, credit notes, and account settlement.
When to Use: Reversal of ITC on unpaid suppliers — show payment occurred through account settlement, not just banking
Nemo debet bis vexari pro una et eadem causa
No one ought to be twice vexed for one and the same cause.
Use when: Revenue makes "double demand" for the same tax that has already been paid or discharged — especially relevant for DRC-3 payments, GSTR-3B discharge, earlier tax period invoice inclusions.
Ei incumbit probatio qui dicit, non qui negat
The burden of proof lies upon one who affirms, not one who denies.
Use when: Revenue demands taxpayer prove innocence — cite this maxim and Sec 155 to firmly put burden back on Revenue. State: "Demand is disputed and allegations are rejected."
In dubio pro reo
In doubt, in favor of the accused.
Use when: Evidence is ambiguous, data mismatch unexplained, or where Revenue relies on assumptions — benefit of doubt must go to taxpayer in self-assessment regime.
Quod non apparet non est
That which does not appear does not exist.
Use when: Revenue relies on GSTR-2A or e-way bill data as sole evidence without verifying actual invoices — data that does not appear in portal does not mean supply did not occur.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 25B — Mismatch & Pleadings for Reply

⚖️ Chapter 25 (Part B) — Mismatch & Pleadings for Reply to Notices

GST Litigation Reference — Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
25.30 GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B Mismatch
25.31 GSTR-7 vs GSTR-1/3B (TDS)
25.32 GSTR-8 vs GSTR-1/3B (TCS)
25.33 Non-Reversal of Common Credits
25.34 Blocked Credits Availed
25.35 Matched Credits — Absconding Suppliers
25.36 Mismatch of TRAN-1 Credit
25.37 Claim of TRAN-1 Credit in GSTR-3B
25.38 Interest on TRAN-1 Credit Reversed
25.39 Composition Taxpayer Collecting Tax
25.40 Composition — Service Turnover
25.41 Composition — Exceeding Threshold
25.42 Composition — With Credit Balance
25.43 Disposal of Capital Goods
25.44 Write-Off of Inputs & Capital Goods
25.45 Refund of Unmatched Credits to Exporter
25.46 Credit Claimed After Due Date
