25.47 Credit — Payment Not Made to Suppliers
25.48 Output Tax on 'Other Income'
25.49 Credit Blocked Under Rule 86A
25.50 Output Tax in Cash — Rule 86B
25.51 Demand for Interest Only
25.52 Demand for Penalty Only
25.53 Classification Not Correct 1
25.54 Classification Not Correct 2
25.55 Exemption Not Admissible 1
25.56 Exemption Not Admissible 2
25.57 Pure Agency Collections
25.58 Continuous Utilization of Credit
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
25.30 — Mismatch of GSTR-1 with GSTR-3B
GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B MISMATCH — WHY DATA IS ERRONEOUS
Why GSTR-1 Data Cannot Be Relied Upon
Excludes deferment of adjustment permitted u/s 37(3) CGST Act
Must exclude liability discharged in earlier tax period
Must exclude liability discharged in subsequent period — reported in GSTR-9 Part V, Table 14
Must exclude liability reversed and adjusted in subsequent period
Mandatory Exclusions from Comparison
Net values and offline adjustment of negative values — Circular 26/26/2017-GST, para 4
No recovery action permitted for output tax based on mere data differences — CBIC Instructions 1/2022-GST, para 3.2
FATAL FLAW
18-month window (21 months for 2017-18) must be considered for valid comparison. Linear comparison = demand not sustainable in law
GSTR-2A / GSTR-9 Data Issues
GSTR-2A data ≠ GSTR-9 Table 4N and 13 for disputed tax period
GSTR-2A data ≠ GSTR-1 report downloaded by Registered Person
SELF-ASSESSMENT
GST = self-assessment regime. When burden u/s 155 CGST stands discharged, demand based on data differences = contrary to law
ANCILLARY DEMANDS
No output tax demand → Interest demand not sustainable
No output tax demand → Penalty without animus not sustainable
Penalty = subject to Sec 126 discipline — cannot be mechanical
25.31 — Mismatch of GSTR-7 with GSTR-1/3B (TDS)
Query
Total value on which TDS is deducted (GSTR-7) is greater than total taxable value admitted in GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B. Difference alleged as taxable value liable to tax with interest.
TDS MISMATCH — GSTR-7 vs GSTR-1/3B — RESPONSE STRATEGIES
LIABILITY ACCEPTED 1
Challan/debit entry attached — liability duly discharged
LIABILITY ACCEPTED 2
WITHOUT PREJUDICE — challan attached for immaterial amount, demand disputed, year-wise reconciliation provided
Liability NOT Accepted — 3 Grounds
Ground 1: Turnover not admissible to Registered Person — erroneously cited GSTIN of RP by Deductors; turnover reported in GSTR-3B in different period; deduction on non-taxable outward supplies
Ground 2: Without showing taxing ingredients u/s 9(1) CGST or 5(1) IGST, demand based on 'data mismatch' = contrary to law. TDS on 'taxable' goods/services — NOT composition supplies
Common Refutations: Without exemption u/n 2/2017 and 12/2017 (Rate); without demonstrable liability (self-assessment u/s 59 cannot be disturbed); CGST-SGST assumes intra-State place of supply without evidence; IGST assumes inter-State POS without evidence; 18% assumes no exemption/concessional rate/HSN classification
KEY PRINCIPLE
Mere data difference ≠ taxable supply. Full taxability must be established by Revenue — only through audit u/s 65. Adverse inference = illegal
25.32 — Mismatch of GSTR-8 with GSTR-1/3B (TCS)
Query
Total value on which TCS is collected (GSTR-8 by Collectors) is greater than taxable value in GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B. Difference alleged as taxable value liable to tax with interest.
TCS MISMATCH — GSTR-8 vs GSTR-1/3B — SAME STRUCTURE AS TDS
KEY DISTINCTION
TCS collected on NET VALUE of taxable supplies — does NOT exclude exempt supplies
EXCLUSIONS NEEDED
(i) Turnover not admitted as belonging to RP — Collectors erroneously cited GSTIN
(ii) Turnover already reported in GSTR-3B in different period
(iii) Collection even on non-taxable outward supplies
COMMON DEFENCES
Same as 25.31: No taxing ingredients u/s 9(1)/5(1); CGST-SGST POS assumption; IGST POS assumption; 18% HSN assumption; Sec 59 self-assessment not disturbed lightly; adverse inference illegal without Sec 65 audit
GSTR-9/9C RELIABILITY
GSTR-3B data is reliable — evidenced by nil unreconciled turnover in GSTR-9/9C for the financial year. Adverse inference from Collector data = presumptuous and conjecture
25.33 — Non-Reversal of Common Credits
Query
Reversal of credits u/s 17(2) CGST Rules 42 and 43 has not been complied with even though exempt turnover reported in GSTR-9 Annual Returns. Common credit reversal demanded with interest.
NON-REVERSAL OF COMMON CREDITS — RULE 42/43 — RESPONSE MAP
LIA. ACCEPTED 1
Challan/debit entry attached — liability discharged
LIA. ACCEPTED 2
WITHOUT PREJUDICE — immaterial amount. Year-wise reconciliation attached.
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Credit claimed is 'gross' — contrary to law
Total credit in GSTR-3B is LESS THAN total credit in GSTR-2A for relevant period as adjusted by extended duration and permitted for reporting outward supplies
Non-reversal shows credit is net of guesswork — discrepancy rejected
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Credit availed after excluding (i) ineligible credit and (ii) blocked credit
Demand for reversal = 'double demand' — contrary to law
NOT ACCEPTED 3
Inward supplies are bona fide — duly verified, properly accounted, paid via normal banking
ITC is bona fide vested right — indefeasible when all vesting conditions satisfied
Disclosure requirement (Rules 4A(1) & 4B(2)) — GSTR-3B functionality introduced only after 5 Jul 2022, via Notif. 14/2022; until then, 'net of reversal' was common practice
Without invoking actionable cause in law — data mismatch cannot support demand. No demand until total credit excluding ineligible/blocked/common credits established
25.34 — Blocked Credits Availed
Query
Inward supplies include blocked credits u/s 17(5) CGST Act. No reversal of such credits. Reversal demanded with applicable interest.
BLOCKED CREDITS — SEC 17(5) — DEFENCE STRATEGY
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Credit claimed includes 'blocked credits' — allegation about non-reversal is guesswork
Total credit in GSTR-3B < Total credit in GSTR-2A for relevant period as adjusted for extended duration and permitted for reporting outward supplies — discrepancy rejected
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Table 7E of GSTR-9 showing 'NIL' = does NOT authorize inference that blocked credits have been availed and not reversed
Demand cannot be supported merely by referring to reversal data in GSTR-9 — not a reliable indicator
NOT ACCEPTED 3
Inward supplies — bona fide, duly verified, properly accounted, normal banking
ITC is bona fide vested right — indefeasible when all vesting conditions satisfied
GSTR-3B functionality for disclosing (i) total credit and (ii) reversal — introduced only after 5 Jul 2022 via GSTR-3B revised version; Aug 2022 via Common Portal. Until then, 'net of reversal' was common practice
Without invoking actionable cause in law — data mismatch cannot support demand for reversal
25.35 — Matched Credits from Absconding Suppliers
Query
Input tax credit of Rs._____ appearing in ECrL for tax period _____ was blocked u/r 86A. It has been reported that respective Suppliers are bogus. Credit liable to be reversed with interest as per CGST Rules.
ABSCONDING SUPPLIER CREDIT — RULE 86A BLOCKING — DEFENCES
LIA. ACCEPTED 1 & 2
Challan attached / WITHOUT PREJUDICE — liability discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Credit blocked pre-emptively — submitted to higher officers with no response despite reminders
Blocking was a contrary indicator — exceptional power must be exercised with care and attention
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Inward supplies — bona fide, duly verified, normal banking
ITC is bona fide vested right — all vesting conditions satisfied
Supplier(s) in default in Demand Proceedings — (i) no 'nil' recovery, (ii) action in favour of RP, (iii) presumption tilts in favour of bona fides
Without actionable cause — data mismatch + confidential intelligence ≠ support for reversal
Rule 86A of CGST Rules — SAFETY AVAILABLE — due process under Chapter XV, rule 86A(3): RP has NOT approached Appellate Authority — any demand raised in summary fashion is premature
MALA FIDES ALLEGATION
Bogus supplier allegation cannot be based on mala fides with no supporting evidence. No conclusive intelligence gathered. Unless RP's bona fides impeached — no adverse inference
25.36 — Mismatch of TRAN-1 Credit
Query
Transition credit availed on Common Portal in 2017 and 2022 are not matching with (i) closing balance of computation, (ii) amount of transition credit availed, (iii) difference. Transition credit admissible is liable to be reversed with applicable interest.
TRAN-1 CREDIT MISMATCH — LAYERED DEFENCE
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Vested right to claim transition credit — valid and proper under earlier laws u/s 140(1) CGST Act
Mode of making this claim via TRAN-1 or GSTR-3B — not as prescribed but Apex Court has set aside this requirement
New procedure (GSTR-3B in 2022) does not mandate reversal/reclaim — it is evidence of wide prevalence of transition credit via GSTR-3B
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Mala fides allegation — adverse inference drawn from embargo in Sec 160(2) CGST Act = illegal
Transition credit ≠ alter nature of claim. Demanding reversal = without remedy of restoring right — fatal to vested and indefeasible right
Defective portal functionalities — Courts accepted / TRAN-1 credit filing TRAN-1 is neither affirmative action nor opportunity to review validity
If indefeasible credits can flow into ECrL earlier — RP could not have known the month functionality was re-opened
NOT ACCEPTED 3 — Jurisdictional
Correctness of RP's claim of transition credit — valid inquiry u/s 140(1) CGST Act and Sec 140(3) and 140(6) — not permitted under the CGST Act — only Proper Officer to exercise
Demand for interest on transition credit = without authority of law
Mode by which transition credit flows into ECrL cannot be invoked — demand = inadmissible
25.37 — Claim of TRAN-1 Credit in GSTR-3B
Query
Transition credit was permitted to be availed on Common Portal in 2017 and 2022 but not via GSTR-3B. Transition credit claimed via GSTR-3B liable to be reversed with applicable interest.
TRAN-1 CREDIT VIA GSTR-3B — 3-TIER DEFENCE
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Vested right — valid and proper. Mode of making claim via TRAN-1 in not as prescribed — but can neither be supported merely based on mala fides nor be based on adverse inference drawn from preemptive embargo in Sec 160(2)
Verification of transition credit requires consideration: correctness of all provision of earlier laws; admissibility of credit under earlier laws; determination of pre-conditions (both Central & State laws); determination of post-conditions; considering outward supplies proposed in GST by RP
NOT ACCEPTED 2 — Same as 25.36
Mode of claiming transition credit does not alter the nature of claim. Demanding reversal = without remedy of restoring transition credit — fatal
Filing TRAN-1 is neither affirmative action nor opportunity to review validity of credit as originally claimed
Transition = process, not substantive. State should not involve another step to 'transition'. Assisting RP cannot prove costly to RP.
NOT ACCEPTED 3 — Jurisdictional
Mode of claiming transition credit does not alter its nature — Demanding reversal without remedy for restoring = fatal to vested and indefeasible right
Defective functionalities — (i) affidavits filed before Courts; (ii) Apex Court extension granted Dec 2022 — RP could NOT have known functionality was re-opened
If indefeasible credits can flow into ECrL earlier, justification to require RP to redo exercise already carried out much earlier = without authority
25.38 — Interest on TRAN-1 Credit Reversed
Query
Transition credit of Rs._____ claimed incorrectly has been reversed on _____. However such reversal is not accompanied with applicable interest.
INTEREST ON TRAN-1 REVERSAL — TWO POSITIONS
LIABILITY ACCEPTED 1
Challan attached — dated _____ — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Demand for interest on reversal of transition credit is rejected. Demand for interest is not supportable under Chapter XV of CGST Act — power to create legislative provisions needed to provide machinery for recovery of transition credit interest is NOT utilized
Interest is NOT payable especially when interest, where credit is availed but not utilized, is a treatment of law that cannot be different for GST credit and transition credit
25.39 — Composition Taxpayer Collecting Tax
Query
Registered Person is operating under composition of GST. Supplying goods bearing MRP — 'at MRP' implies condition in Sec 10(4) CGST Act — liable to discharge full extent of output tax and applicable penalty.
COMPOSITION — TAX COLLECTING — RESPONSE TREE
LIA. ACCEPTED 1
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
MRP is the 'maximum price' at which goods may be sold — where tax is NOT collectable, goods may be assumed to be sold and collected unless it can be shown that output tax is not collectable
Bills of supply issued clearly show that output tax is NOT collected. Discrepancy observation is liable to be rejected
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Composition under Sec 10 = self-contained code — payable u/s 9 CGST Act and having approved composition
Requirements u/Legal Metrology Act 2009 cannot establish non-compliance with conditions attendant to composition unless shown expressly
'Output tax has been collected' allegation = inference and conjecture. Bills of supply empty proceedings — no evidence forthcoming
Ratio in Amrit Agro Industries Ltd. v. CCE, Ghaziabad 2007 applies mutatis mutandis — case and allegations = contravened in summary fashion
Sec 10(4) CGST Act contravention alleged without substance — RP is not contravened — allegation based on inference and conjecture
25.40 — Composition Taxpayer with Service Turnover
Query
Registered Person has effected outward supply of services which is not permitted when the option of composition is allowed under Sec 10(1) CGST Act. Entire extent of composition liable to be reversed with applicable interest.
COMPOSITION — SERVICE TURNOVER — RESPONSE TREE
LIA. ACCEPTED
Challan attached — dated _____ — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Discrepancy noted is erroneous — demand for output tax is erroneous for the following reasons
Relief from exclusion under the option of composition allowed under second proviso to Sec 10(1) CGST Act has been overlooked
Turnover from services is NOT adjusted for effect of differences in time of accounting of said turnover in financial statements and 'time of supply' provisions in CGST Act
NOT ACCEPTED 2
RP has claimed of composition allowed under option of composition — all conditions attendant to the option satisfied — self-assessment carried out
Turnover from services is not adjusted for effect of differences in time of accounting in financial statements and time of supply in CGST Act — demand is erroneous
25.41 — Composition Taxpayer Exceeding Threshold
Query
Registered Person has exceeded threshold limit. Application of composition availed and enjoyed under Sec 10(1) CGST Act. Entire extent of composition liable to be reversed with applicable interest.
COMPOSITION — THRESHOLD EXCEEDED — 3-TIER DEFENCE
LIA. ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Turnover data NOT adjusted for effect of differences in time of accounting of said turnover in financial statements and 'time of supply' provisions in CGST Act
All conditions of composition satisfied; self-assessment carried out; absence of material to unseat
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Turnover data is not adjusted for effect of differences — erroneous for output tax
After making adjustment — all conditions of composition satisfied; self-assessment carried out; absence of material
NOT ACCEPTED 3
Turnover data not adjusted for effect of differences in time of accounting — erroneous for output tax
RP's position: conditions of composition ARE satisfied. In absence of material to unseat this position — self-assessment carried out. Cannot be lightly disregarded
KEY NOTE
Rs._______ being the excess — composition taxpayer to adjust turnover up to threshold, and excess Rs._____ alone would be liable to output tax. Adjusted for admissible input tax credit — Amrit Agro Industries applies mutatis mutandis
25.42 — Composition Taxpayer with Credit Balance
Query
Registered Person having availed option of composition under Sec 10(1) CGST Act has availed input tax credit in their books of account. Entire extent of composition liable to be reversed with applicable interest.
COMPOSITION — ITC IN BOOKS — CREDIT BALANCE DEFENCE
LIA. ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
ITC in financial statements = erroneous — does NOT prejudice RP's composition option claim
This error has prejudiced composition by erroneous income-tax credit brought in ECrL instead of GSTR-3B — error does NOT operate as 'from option of composition'
Adjusted for the above, turnover up to threshold qualifies for composition and excess Rs._____ alone would be liable to output tax
Where any output tax is demanded — admissible input tax credit be adjusted before making any demand
Section 16(4) of CGST Act — net of adjustments by Registered Person — no liability payable. Amrit Agro Industries applies mutatis mutandis
25.43 — Disposal of Capital Goods
Query
Registered Person has disposed 'capital goods' as appearing from financial statements/EWB generated on Portal. Disposal of capital goods attracts liability u/s 18(6) CGST Act. Output tax discharged is less than payable with applicable interest.
DISPOSAL OF CAPITAL GOODS — SEC 18(6) — DEFENCE
LIA. ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Output tax payable u/s 9(1) CGST Act is levied under Sec 9(1) at the rate mentioned in Notification 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate), dated 28 Jun 2017 read with any exemption Notification 2/2017-Central Tax (Rate), dated 28 Jun 2017 — Registered Person has discharged output tax at such rates only
Net of adjustments as above — there would be no liability payable by Registered Person
Where any output tax is demanded — admissible input tax credit must be adjusted before making demand. Section 16(4) of CGST Act — net of adjustments — no liability payable
Revenue cannot approbate and reprobate — if credit is less than transaction price of outward supplies, demand credit to extent of restriction input tax credit — within scheme of GST law
Credit availed, as reduced to extent prescribed in rules, payable on outward supply of capital goods that cannot be lawfully demanded — being an indefeasible right. 'Taxable supply' having taken place — credit fully and lawfully vested as an indefeasible right
25.44 — Write-Off of Inputs and Capital Goods
Query
Financial statements show inputs and/or capital goods have been 'written off'. Credit claimed in respect of such inputs and capital goods is liable to reversal of credit under section 17(5)(h) read with rules 43 and 44 in accordance with law.
WRITE-OFF OF INPUTS/CAPITAL GOODS — SEC 17(5)(H) — DEFENCES
LIA. ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Input tax credit is admitted u/s 16(1) with all vesting conditions in Sec 16(2) and 16(4) duly satisfied — said credit not exposed to any bar in Sec 17(2) or 17(5) of CGST Act — become vested and indefeasible and cannot be eclipsed readily
There is no evidence brought on record that said inputs or capital goods have been 'written off' except observation from financial statements that inward supplies have been treated as 'period cost' — material difference in fact and law cannot be lightly equated
NOT ACCEPTED 2
No longer available for use as inputs or capital goods — accounting parlance 'written-down' in furtherance of business. Data differences have been marked — cannot be imputed as accounting treatment of law
No change in factual matrix — accounting treatment of any transaction and tax treatment under GST do NOT interfere. RP's position u/s 59 CGST Act — demand not be lightly unseated based on adverse inference
25.45 — Refund of Unmatched Credits to Exporter
Query
Refund has been claimed for 2017-18 of 'net ITC' although the credit claimed in GSTR-3B for the relevant tax period of refund is more than that appearing on the Common Portal. As such, the erroneous refund is liable to be repaid with applicable interest.
REFUND OF UNMATCHED CREDITS TO EXPORTER — REFUND DEFENCE
LIA. ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Input tax credit admitted u/s 16(1) — vesting conditions in Sec 16(2) and 16(4) duly satisfied — not exposed to bar in Sec 17(2) or 17(5) — vested and indefeasible — cannot be eclipsed readily
No evidence on record that said inputs/capital goods have been 'written off' except observation from financial statements
Mere canvassing of 'written-off' expression — accounting difference in fact and law cannot be lightly equated
No change in factual matrix — accounting treatment of any transaction and tax treatment do NOT interfere. Without invoking actionable cause in law — cannot be made
RP's position u/s 59 CGST Act — self-assessment and demand cannot be unseated based on adverse inference
NOT ACCEPTED 2 — Alternative
NOT ACCEPTING LIABILITY IN GSTR-3B WITH GSTR-2A — BORROW RESPECT OF MISMATCH OF GSTR-2A WITH GSTR-3B PROVIDED EARLIER UNDER ANY OF THE ALTERNATIVE ANSWERS
Without examining factual matrix — accounting treatment under GST law does NOT change — any adverse inference is erroneous; RP's position u/s 59 CGST Act cannot be unseated based on adverse inference
25.46 — Credit Claimed After Due Date
Query
GSTR-3B is filed belatedly for 2017-18 and such credit is inadmissible in view of the time restriction to claim credit u/s 16(4) CGST Act. Entire amount to be demanded along with interest.
BELATED GSTR-3B — CREDIT AFTER DUE DATE — MULTI-GROUND DEFENCE
NOT ACCEPTED 1
RP is approved to operate under 'QRMP Scheme' duly approved by Proper Officer — time limit is earlier than the time allowed to file returns for disputed tax period — two provisions are conflicting within the law
When two provisions conflicting — one must make way for the other — the Sec 16(4) restriction to bar ITC would be in derogation of each other
NOT ACCEPTED 2
RP has claimed input tax credit for the disputed tax period — all vesting conditions in Sec 16(2) and Sec 16 CGST Act attracted in GSTR-3B for the disputed tax period — entire amount of input tax credit becomes vested and indefeasible right that cannot be lightly rejected
With allegations being disputed — burden of proof continues to remain on Revenue to demonstrate inadmissibility. Without discharging this burden — demand not sustainable
NOT ACCEPTED 3
RP claimed input tax credit for disputed tax period — all vesting conditions in Sec 16 CGST Act are attracted — entire credit becomes vested and indefeasible right
Claim of input tax credit not earlier than date of completion of Sec 16(2)(b) cannot result in non-compliance with Sec 16(4)
Special instances where ITC is allowed even though no saving clause: Clubs and Associations (Sec 1 Jul 2017 to 31 Jan 2022 — retrospective amendment, Notif 20th Feb 2022); taxpayers who haven't claimed credit under time limit in Sec 18(1)(d); IRPs/Debtors in CIRP — Notification 11/2020; Cancelled-Restored registration cases
Construct of language used for 'non-negative words' in Sec 107(6) CGST Act — may be interpreted to bar the appeal — time limit construed as directory and NOT mandatory. All overlooked and valid returns filed — extent of ITC cannot be overlooked. The extent of input tax credit availed, burden of revenue u/s 2(117) cannot be discharged lightly
25.47 — Credit Claimed When Payment Not Made to Suppliers
Query
Supplier's payments have been delayed beyond 180 days where input tax credit is availed. As such, credit is to be demanded in accordance with law.
CREDIT CLAIMED — PAYMENT NOT MADE TO SUPPLIER — 3-TIER DEFENCE
LIA. ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Input tax credit availed — input invoices duly verified — all dues paid via normal banking channels
Detailed reconciliation — bill-wise statement with payment dates attached
Payments settled belatedly — Registered Person submits bill-wise payment statement with details
NOT ACCEPTED 2
RP claimed input tax credit for disputed tax period — all vesting conditions — entire credit vested and indefeasible right
Without discharging this burden of proof — demand not sustainable
NOT ACCEPTED 3
RP claimed input tax credit — all vesting conditions satisfied — entire credit becomes vested and indefeasible right
Appearance of Creditors Balance does NOT ipsidixit imply any credit condition — no demand for reversal of credit arises
Data appearing in Creditors Balance cannot be relied upon without excluding Suppliers where credit is NOT availed
Settlement of creditors includes settlement via 'account adjustment' as recognized in Senco Gold Ltd. 02/WBAAR/2019
No adverse inference can be drawn without showing that bill-wise 'delay' in settlement of dues is availed by Noticee. Mismatch of data — allegations in Impugned SCN = guesswork, estimation and conjecture
25.48 — Output Tax Payable on 'Other Income'
Query
Financial statements show 'other income' is admitted and reported but output tax on the same has not been discharged. Output tax along with applicable interest is liable to be demanded.
OUTPUT TAX ON 'OTHER INCOME' — WHY DEMAND IS ERRONEOUS
LIA. ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1 — Key Grounds
RP has discharged output tax liability — there is no output tax liability on forward charge basis, whether on forward charge or reverse charge basis — demand based on data differences
Taxing ingredients needed to attract incidence of tax — supply (i) goods or services (ii) place of supply (iii) taxability of supplies (iv) time of supply (v) place of supply. Without these, demand = guesswork, estimation and conjecture
Demand for output tax as CGST-SGST violative of Sec 77 CGST Act and Sec 19 IGST Act
Income appearing in P&L Account = data based on rules of accounting — NOT based on 'time of supply' as permitted in CGST Act; Sec 44 CGST Act
Mismatch of data between P&L Account and GSTR-3B is itself erroneous — (i) P&L Account excludes (i) non-GST income and (ii) P&L not reconciling is permitted under CBIC Circular 26/26/2017-GST, para 4 dated 29 Dec 2017
Output tax liability appearing in GSTR-9 Part V Table 14 must exclude: liability in earlier period; liability discharged in subsequent period; liability reversed and adjusted; deferment adjustments u/s 37(3)
25.49 — Credit Blocked Under Rule 86A
Query
Certain Suppliers where input tax credit is availed are no longer registered and intelligence gathered shows credit is blocked u/r 86A and the same is liable to be recovered in accordance with law.
RULE 86A BLOCKING — EXCEPTIONAL POWER — COMPREHENSIVE DEFENCE
LIA. ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED — Grounds
Invoking rule 86A without any basis in law and directing immediate repayment of such credit = illegal and liable to immediate restoration
On perusal of rule 86A of CGST Act — only 5 reasons for which power can be invoked — falling with these. Use of exceptional power would be contrary to law
Blocking use of ITC = vested and indefeasible rights — in nature of property of Registered Person — would be institutionalized theft bypassing the law
CBEC-20/16/2021-GST/1552 dated 2 Nov 2021 — mandates 'limitations' in paras 3.1.2 thereof. Without valid basis — authority to deny ITC = blatant misapplication of statutory authority
No demand for any liability to be made by 'objective determination' of need for such pre-emptive blocking. No demand for any liability can be made in a 'mechanical manner' contrary to these guidelines
Tax payable in terms of rule 86A = a 'restriction' — not 'arrears'. Falls within operation of Sec 75(12) of CGST Act — implementation requires direction by Commissioner — in absence of any direction, rule 86A cannot be enforced on all taxpayers
SAFETY: Rule 86A safety available in CGST Rules — where show cause notice has already been issued, continuing to block = tantamount to provisional attachment under Sec 83 CGST Act for which no authority exists with Proper Officer
OR: Unless valid notice of demand is issued — credit blocked is to be unblocked u/r 86A
25.50 — Output Tax in Cash Under Rule 86B
Query
Output tax is paid entirely by utilizing input tax credit but attendant facts indicate that 1% of the output tax should have been charged 'in cash' in terms of rule 86B. Such output tax charged 'in cash' is liable to be recovered along with applicable interest.
RULE 86B — 1% OUTPUT TAX IN CASH — MULTI-GROUND DEFENCE
LIA. ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Unequivocal position of Noticee — no output tax liability as alleged in Impugned SCN. Admitted position = 'second demand' in terms of rule 86B = without authority of law — 'second demand' for output tax = demand for 'second levy' of tax
Demand for payment of output tax in terms of rule 86B — keeps with rule 86A of CGST Act — likely evasion of output tax. This rule is seen from the compilation — compliance with rule 86B is paid any of two preceding financial years — 'in cash' — Rules 86B is excluded where income-tax is paid in arrears
NOT ACCEPTED 2 + 3
NOT ACC 2: Rule 86B cannot be given retrospective effect — cannot be 'second demand'. 'Second demand' for output tax is 'second demand' for 'second levy' which is without authority of law
Authority of Noticee to remove this restriction: second proviso to rule 86B — RP not empowered to prescribe — similar to rule 86A — erroneous refund cannot be prescribed
NOT ACC 3: There is no liability to output tax — it is the unequivocal position. In absence of liability — Restrictions in GST are either mandatory and applicable to all taxpayers or directory only when applicable to some. Rule 86B cannot be converted into mandatory restrictions. The restriction imposed is NOT applicable in all cases where circumstances is shown to indicate evasion
Compliance — RP is in filing returns, reporting turnover and maintenance of records for purposes audit — there cannot be evasion when turnover is above 50 lakh rupees. Application of rule 86B is directory and circumstances to invoke rule 86B is NOT shown
25.51 — Demand for 'Interest Only'
Query
GSTR-3B has been filed belatedly along with late fee but interest being mandatory and compensatory in nature is payable without limitation.
DEMAND FOR INTEREST ONLY — THREE RESPONSE OPTIONS
ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Demand for interest based on Sec 73 CGST Act = misapplication of authority — no underlying demand pertains to rule 88B of CGST Rules which appears to have been overlooked
In absence of demand for tax credit — demand for interest NOT sustainable in law without required animus. Demand for interest subject to rule 88B which cannot be overlooked. Penalty cannot be imposed in mechanical or routine manner without showing mala fides
NOT ACCEPTED 3 + 4
NOT ACC 3: Demand for interest is NOT a permitted cause in Sec 73 CGST Act — note of demand for interest is a misapplication of authority of CGST Act. In view, demand for interest = contrary to Sec 75(12) of CGST Rules
Demand for payment of tax (or refund or credit) that is already been discharged — once such payment already made in self-assessment, notice of demand is demanded once again purporting to appropriate the payment already made in self-assessment. In view of the above, discrepancy noted = a misapplication under Sec 75(12) of CGST Act. Demand for interest discharged is a misapplication — contrary to rule 142B of CGST Rules
25.52 — Demand for 'Penalty Only'
Query
GSTR-3B has been filed belatedly along with late fee and interest but mandatory penalty under Sec 73(11) CGST Act has not been discharged. As such penalty is payable being mandatory.
DEMAND FOR PENALTY ONLY — FOUR RESPONSE OPTIONS
ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Demand for 'penalty only' is NOT a permitted cause in Sec 73 CGST Act — note of demand for penalty for any tax refund or credit is a misapplication of authority under CGST Act
In view discrepancy noted subject demand = a misapplication under Sec 75(12) of CGST Rules — contrary to rule 142B of CGST Rules
NOT ACCEPTED 3
Demand for penalty (or refund or credit) = already been discharged — demand for penalty cannot be demanded again purporting to appropriate payment already made in self-assessment. Where there is no underlying demand — notice of demand is a misapplication — contrary to rule 142B of CGST Rules
Demand for payment of tax (or refund or credit) cannot be demanded more once a taxpayer can become 'worse off' when interest is raised with notice after compliance. Mechanical application of Sec 73(8) CGST Act is a contortion of legislative wisdom — contrary to Sec 126 mandated consideration of mitigating factors
NOT ACCEPTED 4
Demand for 'penalty only' is NOT a permitted cause — demand is contrary to general discipline and existence of mitigating factors to be given due consideration mandated in Sec 126 of CGST Act
OR: Demand for 'interest only' is NOT a permitted cause — not sustainable. Demand for interest — where the demand pertains to rule 88B of CGST Rules which appears to have been overlooked — contrary to rule 142B of CGST Rules
25.53 — Classification Not Correct 1 (Works Contracts)
Query
GST is discharged at 5% but works contracts undertaken are liable to tax at 18%. As such differential tax is liable to tax along with interest.
CLASSIFICATION DISPUTE — WORKS CONTRACT — RESPONSE MAP
ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Services classifiable under entry 3(vii) to tariff notification — RP has undertaken works contracts for supply of taxable services. Works contracts are classifiable as taxable service under entry 3(vii) — accepted that this classification be accepted and demand dropped
NOT ACCEPTED 2
Total credit charged and deposited 5% — if differential tax of 13% is demanded — RP would not be able to recover the same from customers and remit to government — GST = indirect tax — result in financial loss
Discrepancy observation may kindly be dropped
NOT ACCEPTED 3 — Comprehensive
Determination of turnover as 'works contract' = a question of law — no clarity as to basis of demand
Self-assessment of liability — RP's position = no liability to tax on forward charge or reverse charge — it is RP's position
Demand for tax based on alternate classification — cannot be imputed or inferred based on description of the registration. Without showing enquiry conducted — assumption cannot be entertained
Demand for differential tax — no tariff entry. Revenue cannot accept and do a 'volte face' to deny correctness of tax so discharged without showing authority
Accepting and appropriating tax already discharged in self-assessment — demand militates against the alternate view canvassed
Reliance placed on AAR is erroneous — as the same does NOT have any precedent value. Reliance placed on decisions under earlier tax regime is also erroneous — substantive provisions referred are NOT pari materia
25.54 — Classification Not Correct 2 (Job Work / Agricultural)
Query
GST is discharged at 5% on supply of taxable goods but said turnover appearing in financial statements being described as sale of by products are liable to tax at 18% differential tax. As such differential tax is payable along with interest.
CLASSIFICATION — AGRICULTURAL / JOB WORK — RESPONSE MAP
ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1 + 2
NOT ACC 1: RP engaged in job-work services — agricultural products received from agriculturists involving treatment or process for carrying raw material — sifting, even though strictly speaking, products belonging to agriculturists who provided it — proceeds from disposal — sale of goods/5%.
In GST, products were disclosed as sale of goods under HSN 9988 and taxable at 5%. Also discharged GST at 5% and tax at 18% — no tariff notification — no adverse inference. Demand for differential tax = no tariff entry
NOT ACC 2: No such inference about turnover in subject of 'works contract' or canvassing supply of services — no clarity as to the basis on which demand is made. No such inference with adducing material in support of — demand is contrary to law
NOT ACCEPTED 3 — Comprehensive
Determination of turnover based on classification — no clarity as to terms of contract — cannot be accomplished without reference to terms of contract
Self-assessment — RP's position = there is no liability to tax — it is RP's position — demand cannot be lightly disturbed
Demand for differential tax — no tariff entry. Revenue cannot accept and do 'volte face' to deny correctness — without showing authority. Accepting and appropriating tax already discharged = against the alternate view canvassed; demand militates against Registered Person's position
Reliance placed on AAR is erroneous — no precedent value. Reliance on decisions under earlier tax regime — not pari materia
25.55 — Exemption Not Admissible 1
Query
Table 5 of GSTR-9 contains exempt turnover reported for being insufficiently explained. As such, output tax on the said turnover is liable to be discharged on the said turnover.
EXEMPTION NOT ADMISSIBLE — 3-TIER DEFENCE
ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
No information about exemption available with RP — merely alleging that exemption of inapplicability of said exemption = prima facie case — there is NO such inference brought on record. RP is NOT required to support inquiry to support a prima facie case of exemption
Demand = a 'roving inquiry' — entire proceedings are without jurisdiction
NOT ACCEPTED 2
No such inference about non-taxability of any exemption or otherwise — can be doubted without adducing material of law. There is no occasion to make adverse inference about inapplicability of any exemption
NOT a 'negative tax' regime — entire proceedings are without jurisdiction. There is no such inference = dropped
NOT ACCEPTED 3 — Comprehensive
Determination of taxability — without showing underlying enquiry conducted and without adducing material to substantiate a finding on this fact = cannot be entertained
Self-assessment — RP's position = no liability to tax — it is RP's position on such self-assessment. It is the position of RP that there is no liability to tax on the relevant tax period — cannot be lightly disturbed
Demand for tax — taxability by rejecting enquiry conducted = without showing underlying enquiry. Merely canvassing an alternate classification without adequate material brought on record — cannot be impugned
Reliance placed on AAR erroneous — no precedent value. Reliance on decisions under earlier tax regime = not pari materia. Entire proceedings = dropped
25.56 — Exemption Not Admissible 2 (Electricity to Lessees)
Query
HSN 9972 applied on certain turnover reported in GSTR-9 and another turnover is claimed as exempt. Financial statements 2022-23 shows RP had land with building in joint development — RP was landowner now has income from lease. This exempt turnover is liable to tax at 18% along with interest.
EXEMPTION NOT ADMISSIBLE 2 — ELECTRICITY / LEASE — DEFENCE
ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
RP is the lessor and exempt turnover is, in fact, on account of sale of electricity to lessee — sample lease agreement dated ______ wherein it is clearly stipulated that RP is liable to make sufficient arrangements for lessee to use and enjoy the demised premises for the permitted use along with all amenities including adequate power supply. RP is NOT an independent power producer — there is an obligation to supply electricity to lessee
NOT ACCEPTED 2
No such inference about non-taxability — can be doubted without adducing material — there is no occasion to make adverse inference about inapplicability of any exemption
Self-assessment — RP's position = no liability to tax. Demands based on conclusions: multi-year data comparison does not support it; absence of prior authorization; conclusions are grossly insufficient; actual inquiry has been abandoned — not a valid demand
Demand for CGST-SGST presumes intra-State supply — without showing nature of inward supplies with Sec 15(1) CGST Act — cannot be derived from data appearing in Table 5 of GSTR-9
Demand for CGST-SGST presumes intra-State supply — demanding IGST presumes all outward supplies have POS outside State. In absence of information — assumption not permitted
NOT ACCEPTED 3 — Comprehensive
Determination of taxability — without showing underlying inquiry, without adducing material to substantiate — cannot be entertained
Reliance placed on AAR erroneous — no precedent value; substantive provisions under earlier tax regime not pari materia
25.57 — Pure Agency Collections
Query
Registered Person is an Architect and Project Management Consultant operating 'escrow account' for payments to contractors. Revenue traceable in GSTR9 — Rs.200 crore for FY is NOT excludable from taxable outward supplies and is payable along with interest.
PURE AGENCY — ESCROW COLLECTIONS — TAXABILITY DEFENCE
ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
Nature of business of RP is PMC — correctly understood that 'escrow account' correctly represents amounts held in trust. RP is recipient of fiduciary supplies addressed to end-Customer — transactions at behest of end-Customer
PMC mandate = value of PMC fee being 'x'% of project cost. And PMC fee being 'x'% — involves another step to be fully informed about the costs incurred
NOT ACCEPTED 2
RP which requires NOT only monitoring inward supplies but exercise legal authority to control quality and timeliness of inward supplies identified by RP but approved by end-Customer — these are the essential aspects of this trade
Exempt turnover is, in fact, on account of sale of electricity to lessee (same lease agreement defence as above applies)
NOT ACCEPTED 3 — Comprehensive
Question about addition to taxable value of outward supplies arises only when any reimbursements are inextricably connected to taxable outward supplies received are NOT claimed to be excluded under rule 33 of CGST Rules
Self-assessment — RP's position = supplies have been determined in accordance with Sec 15(2) of CGST Act and full extent of taxable outward supplies have been discharged. It is the position of RP that data reported in GSTR-3B is reliable — evidenced by the full extent
Innovative inquiry conducted based on logical deductions are contrary to facts. As such — entire proceedings appear to be without jurisdiction in the nature of affirmation whether said proceedings are duly authorized by Proper Officer
25.58 — Continuous Utilization of Credit
Query
Output tax is admitted and reported via ECL and the entire liability has always been discharged via ECrL. As such, the input tax credit claimed is liable to be reversed along with interest.
CONTINUOUS UTILIZATION OF ITC — CHALLENGE TO REVERSAL DEMAND
ACCEPTED
Challan attached — liability duly discharged
NOT ACCEPTED 1
With demand not being payable — demand for interest = not sustainable. Any demand pertaining to rule 88B — demand for interest overlooked. In absence of demand for credit = demand for penalty NOT sustainable in law without required animus
Demand for penalty subject to general discipline in Sec 126 CGST Act which cannot be overlooked — cannot be imposed in mechanical or routine manner without showing mala fides
NOT ACCEPTED 2
ITC is placed on record that prohibits continuous or recurring utilization of admissible input tax credit to discharge output tax liability — demand is illegal without showing the utilization
Without placing material on record — demand would overlook facts such as build-up of inventory by making inward supply of services towards capital goods, expenses — demand for reversal of admissible ITC = contrary to law
NOT ACCEPTED 3
ITC claimed — demand for reversal = illegal without showing the statutory provision that prohibits continuous or recurring utilization. RP that all vesting conditions prior to or subsequent to claim of credit — Sec 16, 17 and 18 have been satisfied
Self-assessment — RP's position based on documentation available — claim of credit is duly authorized inquiry. As such — entire proceedings appear to be without jurisdiction — NO affirmation whether said proceedings are duly authorized
Innovative inquiry based on logical deductions — contrary to facts. Entire proceedings appear to be without jurisdiction in the nature of affirmation whether said proceedings are duly authorized by Proper Officer
⇧ Back to Index
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 25 (Part B)
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name before the officer.
Amrit Agro Industries Ltd. v. CCE, Ghaziabad
Supreme Court of India | 2007 (210) ELT 183
The ratio of this decision applies mutatis mutandis to the present case — allegations of the Registered Person are in support of the position taken by the Registered Person and against the allegation of Revenue. Self-assessment regime mandates that the Revenue must show actual contravention before demanding tax. Guesswork and conjecture are not substitutes for evidence.
USE WHEN: Revenue demands output tax from composition taxpayer citing MRP or exceeding threshold without showing actual evidence of tax collected or threshold breach; or whenever Revenue makes adverse inferences without concrete evidence
Senco Gold Ltd. v. WBAAR
West Bengal Advance Authority | 02/WBAAR/2019 | dated 8th May 2019
Settlement of creditors includes settlement via 'account adjustment' — mere appearance of Creditors Balance in books does not ipsidixit imply outstanding payment to suppliers. Data appearing in Creditors Balance cannot be relied upon without excluding Suppliers where credit is not availed. No adverse inference can be drawn from Creditors Balance without clear evidence of outstanding dues.
USE WHEN: Revenue alleges ITC reversal under Sec 16(2)(b) based solely on Creditors Balance in financial statements — challenge the reliability of Creditors Balance as evidence
CBIC Instructions 1/2022-GST
CBIC Circular | Para 3.2 | dated 7th January 2022
While prohibiting any recovery action in respect of output tax based on mere data differences in GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B — Revenue's own instructions prohibit recovery action based on mere data mismatch. Department is bound by its own circulars and instructions and cannot act contrary to them.
USE WHEN: SCN is issued solely based on GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B mismatch without any independent finding of tax liability — cite to quash the proceedings at the root
Circular No. 26/26/2017-GST
CBIC Circular | Para 4 | dated 29th December 2017
Reporting of 'net values' and offline adjustment of 'negative values' is permitted. This circular mandates that any comparison of GSTR-1 with GSTR-3B must account for such adjustments before concluding any mismatch. Linear comparison without these adjustments is factually erroneous and legally infirm.
USE WHEN: Demand is based on straight-line GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B comparison without accounting for net values and negative value adjustments
Circular No. 169/1/2022-GST / CBEC-20/16/2021-GST/1552
CBIC | Notification/Circular | dated 2nd November 2021
CBIC guidelines issued vide CBEC-20/16/2021-GST/1552 mandates 'limitations' in paras 3.1.2, 3.1.3 and 3.1.4 for blocking of ITC under Rule 86A. Without following these guidelines, blocking of ITC is without authority of law. Department cannot act contrary to its own binding guidelines.
USE WHEN: ITC has been blocked under Rule 86A without following prescribed limitations and objective determination required by CBIC's own guidelines
M/s Dilip Kumar v. Commissioner of Customs
Supreme Court of India | (2018) 9 SCC 1
Exemptions must be given strict interpretation — once exemption found applicable, full play must be extended to its coverage. The benefit of exemption is to be construed strictly but once eligibility is established, the scope of exemption cannot be artificially restricted.
USE WHEN: Revenue concedes exemption eligibility in principle but tries to restrict its scope; or when demanding output tax on turnover appearing in Table 5 of GSTR-9 without establishing non-applicability of exemption
⚜️ Latin Maxims for Personal Hearings — Chapter 25B
Ipsi dixit
"He himself said it" — bare assertion without proof
USE WHEN: Revenue's demand for ITC reversal based solely on Creditors Balance does not show actual payment failure — allegation is ipsidixit without supporting evidence
Pari materia
"Of the same matter" — provisions/decisions must be on the same subject to be comparable
USE WHEN: Revenue cites decisions under earlier tax regime (VAT/Service Tax) — firmly state those are NOT pari materia with GST law and cannot be applied
Mutatis mutandis
"With necessary changes having been made"
USE WHEN: Citing Amrit Agro Industries ratio to composition taxpayer matters — the ratio applies mutatis mutandis to the facts of the present case
Volte face
"Complete reversal of position" — Revenue cannot accept tax collected and then deny the correctness of classification
USE WHEN: Revenue has accepted GST paid at 5% but now demands differential tax at 18% — state that Revenue cannot accept the tax paid and simultaneously deny the classification
Mala fides
"Bad faith" — intention to deceive or defraud
USE WHEN: Revenue alleges blocked credits or absconding supplier credits as fraudulent — demand that Revenue establish mala fides with concrete evidence — mere allegation without evidence is not enough
Bona fides
"Good faith" — genuine, honest intention
USE WHEN: Defending ITC from matched/blocked/absconding supplier credits — establish that all purchases were bona fide, verified through normal banking, and ITC is an indefeasible vested right
Non obstante
"Notwithstanding" — overriding clause
USE WHEN: Arguing that Section 16(5) Finance Act 2024 (retrospective amendment) operates as a non obstante clause — notwithstanding any time limit in Sec 16(4), credit for FY 2017-18 to 2020-21 is validated
⚖️ For professional litigation use only | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 25 Part B — Mismatch & Pleadings for Reply to Notices | Paras 25.30–25.58
⚖️ Chapter 27 — Pleadings in Appeal Memo (Para 27.12–27.24)

⚖️ Para 27.12–27.24 — Pleadings in Appeal Memo

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index of Sections
27.12 ITC — Vested & Indefeasible Right
27.13 Transition Credit Demand
27.14 Common Issues 1 — Output Tax & Deemed Supply
27.15 Common Issues 2 — Interest & Penalty
27.16 Deemed Supply — Corporate Guarantee
27.17 Reversal — Unpaid Suppliers
27.18 Tax on Supplies from Unregistered Suppliers
27.19 Reversal — Invoices Not Available
27.20 Data Mismatch with GSTR-9
27.21 Late Fee Demand
27.22 Interest Only Demand
27.23 Differential Tax — Admitted Turnover
27.24 Output Tax on Subsidy
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
27.12 — ITC Pleadings: Vested & Indefeasible Right
ITC is a vested and indefeasible right recognised by the Apex Court. To deny it requires much more than estimation, guesswork and conjecture. Self-assessment carried out in satisfaction of vesting conditions cannot be lightly disturbed without evidence.
ITC PLEADINGS — KEY GROUNDS IN APPEAL MEMO
Vested & Indefeasible Right
APEX COURT
ITC is vested and indefeasible right — to deny requires far more than guesswork & conjecture
Without unseating compliance with vesting conditions, self-assessment CANNOT be lightly disturbed
Burden of Proof on Revenue
SEC 155
Appellant responsible only to prove eligibility — not to disprove Revenue's allegations
Allegation is NOT on "eligibility" to credit — but on "treatment" of eligible credits → burden stays on Revenue
Not a shred of evidence on record to impeach self-assessment → demand is unsustainable
Credit Notes / GSTR-2A Reversal
Reversal demanded on basis of credit notes in GSTR-2A WITHOUT first confirming Appellant actually availed & utilized that ITC in GSTR-3B
No finding of fact as to tax period when ITC was first availed & utilized
Inadmissible credits already reversed where applicable — no credit retained contrary to law
Specificity & Vagueness Failures
FATAL
Order vaguely states non-compliance with Sec 16 — no specific actionable cause stated
Demands duplicated by canvassing alternate interpretations of same source data
Circular & Press Release dated 4 May 2018 ignored — Government's own directive to eliminate needless litigation
27.13 — Audit-Led Demand of Transition Credit
Transition credit is neither input tax credit nor output tax as defined in the Central GST Act. Rule 121 cannot make Section 73 or 74 applicable for demand and recovery of inadmissible transition credit. Legislature alone must fill gaps in law — courts cannot expand or interpolate.
TRANSITION CREDIT — CORE LEGAL INFIRMITIES
Nature of Transition Credit
NOT input tax credit → NOT output tax → Rule 121 cannot invoke Sec 73/74 for demand
Demand under Sec 73 without statutory authority → proceedings without authority of law
Conditions Must Be Shown
Inadmissibility requires showing that claim falls OUTSIDE Sec 140(1), 140(3), 140(6) [as applicable]
Without showing specific infraction of law — denial of transition credit is arbitrary and illegal
Burden of Proof
Burden of proof for wrongful claim lies with Proper Officer — NOT Appellant
Dissatisfaction with documentation only affirms impoverished verification — not wrongful claim
Abandonment of verification documents does NOT impeach validity of self-assessment
LEGISLATIVE PRINCIPLE
One can interpret law — one CANNOT expand law or fill missing portions, not even to do what ought to be done. Legislature alone must furnish the resolution
27.14 — Audit-Led Demand: Common Issues 1 — Output Tax & Data Mismatch
COMMON ISSUES 1 — OUTPUT TAX DEMAND INFIRMITIES
"Put at Notice" Failure
Revenue has NOT put Appellant at notice regarding:
• Nature of alleged transaction
• Coverage within definition of supply
• Object — goods or services
• Exclusions from supply
• HSN code
• Exemption notification
• Time of supply
• Place of supply
• Taxable value / imputed value
• Inclusive of tax treatment
Third-Party Data Comparison
WITHOUT PREJUDICE
Third-party data (financial statements) NOT prescribed under GST law — not reliable for comparison
Must EXCLUDE: (i) non-taxable income (ii) non-GST income (iii) supplies with tax discharged in earlier period (iv) returns & credit adjustments (v) net values vide Circular 26/26/2017
GSTR-9 is filed "after rectification" — comparing pre-rectification data with GSTR-9 is unreliable & arbitrary
Books vs GST Returns
Books of account follow accounting rules — GST turnover follows "time of supply" → they CANNOT be equated
Sec 35(5) demands RECONCILIATION — which is an affirmation that books are NOT indicative of GST turnover
"Double demand" — comparing third-party data AND then GSTR-9 is grossly incorrect when liability is already discharged
Time / Place / HSN Failures
Time of supply NOT established — different rules for goods vs services — demand estimated on difference values is illegal
CGST-SGST demanded without showing place of supply is intra-State under Sec 8 IGST Act → impermissible
WITHOUT PREJUDICE
Sec 74 does NOT permit guesswork & estimation — errors are INCURABLE
27.15 — Common Issues 2 — Interest & Penalty Grounds
INTEREST & PENALTY — GROUNDS NOT TO BE OVERLOOKED
Interest Demand
SEC 50 + RULE 88B
Retrospective amendment — interest applies only on net cash liability NOT on ITC balance
When underlying output tax / ITC demand itself is not payable — interest demand falls away automatically
Routine computation of interest is ILLEGAL — specific finding of undischarged arrears required first
Penalty Demand
Penalty erroneous when underlying demand itself is erroneous, inapplicable and denied
ANIMUS
Required animus (intent) is NOT shown to exist → without animus, penalty is misapplication of law
Contrary to judicial authorities and administrative instructions binding in law
27.16 — Audit-Led Demand for Output Tax on Deemed Supply (Corporate Guarantee)
Deemed supply is legal fiction. Fiction requires existence of actual supply even between related/distinct persons. Sec 7(1)(c) r/w Para 2, Schedule I — eclipses need for consideration but DOES NOT create supply where none exists.
DEEMED SUPPLY — CORPORATE GUARANTEE GROUNDS
No Supply Exists
Guarantee given by Appellant to Lenders (NOT distinct/related person) on behalf of Debtor → taxing ingredients absent
No supply "to" distinct person — Sec 7(1)(c) demands existence of two distinct persons
No contractual privity between surety (Appellant) and borrower subsidiary → demand arrived by altering facts
Transaction in Money
SEC 7
Transaction in MONEY is neither supply of goods nor supply of services → outside scope of Sec 7
Corporate guarantee = transaction in money to the extent stated → GST on transaction in money is not leviable
Valuation & Time of Supply
Valuation under Notification 52/2023 dated 26 Oct 2023 — NOT in force during Disputed Tax Period → cannot be applied retrospectively
Artificial time of supply created — contrary to Sec 13 CGST Act → cherry-picking from financials is arbitrary
Rules and circulars CANNOT override the Act — where levy absent under Sec 9, machinery provisions cannot create tax
CGST-SGST / Place of Supply
WITHOUT PREJUDICE
CGST-SGST demanded without demonstrating tests to determine place of supply → contrary to law
Corporate guarantee: secondary collateral only — no benefit to Debtor shown — demand based on conjecture
27.17 — Demand for Reversal of Credit on Unpaid Suppliers
ITC REVERSAL — UNPAID SUPPLIERS: GROUNDS
Payments Made
SEC 16(2) + RULE 37
Reversal attracted ONLY when registered person FAILS to pay supplier — where payment made in full, reversal does not sustain
Payment is NOT limited to bank — includes return supplies, credit notes, account settlement of security (Senco Gold 02/WBAAR/2019-20)
Creditors Balance Argument
Appearance of Creditors Balance does NOT ipsi dixit imply non-compliance with credit conditions
Without "bill-wise delay" data, allegations based on guesswork — incompatible with demand under Chapter XV
Must EXCLUDE: (i) Suppliers where credit not availed under Sec 17(2)/17(5) (ii) Creditors settled by account adjustment
180-Day Jurisdictional Issue
If 180-day limit falls in FY 202X+1 — Proper Officer authorized only for FY 202X CANNOT examine FY 202X+1 records
Even Appellate acquiescence CANNOT confer jurisdiction on Proper Officer beyond authorization period
WITHOUT PREJUDICE
Even if paid beyond 180 days — only INTEREST can be demanded; reversal of ITC itself is NOT sustainable. Credit reversed is restorable under Rule 37(4)
27.18 — Audit-Led Demand for Tax on Supplies from Unregistered Suppliers
TAX ON UNREGISTERED SUPPLIER — GROUNDS
Output Tax Already Discharged
No output tax liability — forward charge or reverse charge — that remains undischarged for disputed period
Without incidence of tax, demand for output tax is violative of known tenets of taxation
RCM Conditions Not Met
SEC 9(4)
Demand without showing (i) existence of RCM liability AND (ii) non-payment of said liability
Must first establish levy under Sec 9(1) before making demand under Sec 9(4)
Accounting classification is NOT GST tariff classification — no relevance to establish RCM liability
Time / Place / HSN
WITHOUT PREJUDICE
Time of supply different under RCM — not established → demand set aside in toto
CGST-SGST demanded without showing place of supply — Sec 77 + Sec 19 IGST Act demands correct tax again if wrong tax paid
HSN code not established — classification cannot be assumed — demand set aside in toto
27.19 — Audit-Led Demand: Reversal of Credit — Invoices Not Available for Verification
Notice ENDS audit. New material introduced after notice — especially material available during audit but alleged not produced — can shift burden of proof onto taxpayer unwittingly. This must be exposed in grounds.
REVERSAL ON NON-VERIFICATION OF INVOICES — GROUNDS
Vesting Conditions Satisfied
ITC claimed after satisfying all vesting conditions in Sec 16 — none of Sec 17/18 restrictions attracted
Entire ITC becomes vested and indefeasible right — cannot be lightly rejected
Possession of Invoice
SEC 16(2)(a) + RULE 37
Reversal only when registered person does NOT have a tax invoice in possession
Non-verification of invoice does NOT ipso facto imply reversal
Unless (i) demand for submission specifically made AND (ii) Appellant omitted/evaded submission — no adverse inference lawful
GSTR-2A Not Mandatory
GSTR-2A is a matter of procedure — NOT a mandate in law. Matters of procedure are DIRECTORY not mandatory
Appellant entitled to claim ITC based on satisfaction of vesting conditions even when data does not appear in GSTR-2A
Revenue cannot approbate and reprobate → ITC is bona fide except for non-verification — no other infraction
Burden under Sec 155
SEC 155
When Appellant discharges burden to prove eligibility → burden to prove allegations shifts to Revenue
Demand confirmed without verifying invoices available with Appellant → contrary to law
27.20 — Audit-Led Demand: Data Mismatch with GSTR-9
GSTR-9 DATA MISMATCH — GROUNDS
GSTR-9 is "After Rectification"
GSTR-9 filed "after rectification" — comparing with pre-rectification data is unreliable and arbitrary
Data in GSTR-9 Tables 4N and 13 not matching with disputed tax period downloads — bad in law
Reversals Already Made
SEC 49(4)
Tax on reversals already made in GSTR-3B — further demand contrary to law
Payment through DRC-03 (cause: annual return) does not restrict payment from electronic credit ledger
Admitting reversal made but demanding "reversal in cash" is contrary to law — once reversed, no further liability
BURDEN NEVER DISCHARGED
With allegations disputed — burden to demonstrate incidence of output tax remains on Revenue throughout. Without discharging this burden, demand is not sustainable
27.21 — Audit-Led Demand for Late Fee
LATE FEE DEMAND — JURISDICTIONAL INFIRMITIES
No Jurisdiction under Sec 73
SEC 47 vs SEC 73
Late fee is leviable under Sec 47 — there is NO statutory provision in Sec 73 to demand late fee
Sec 73 permits demand for "tax not paid / short paid / ITC wrongly availed" — NOT for late fee
When Legislature enacts a specific provision, it cannot be invoked for alternate or additional purpose
Late Fee in Isolation
Demanding late fee in isolation WITHOUT any demand for tax or ITC → Sec 73 is not appropriate provision
Where delay in filing is regularized and returns admittedly filed — delay is regularized → demand for late fee illegal
Where tax demand is "undisputed" → Sec 75(12) r/w Rule 142B applies — Sec 73 cannot be bypassed
27.22 — Audit-Led Demand for 'Interest Only'
INTEREST ONLY DEMAND — WRONG PROVISION INVOKED
Sec 73 Cannot Demand Interest Only
No jurisdiction under Sec 73 to demand "interest on delayed filing" — Sec 73 permits demand for tax/ITC only
Demand for "interest only" without any tax — without authority of law → liable to be set aside
Specific Provision Prevails
SEC 75(12)
More specific remedy available under Sec 75(12) — specific provision must prevail over generic Sec 73
Explicit non-inclusive language in Sec 75(12) disaffirms authority under Sec 73 for "interest only"
CORRECT ROUTE
Recovery of interest → Sec 79 r/w Rule 88B and 142B, NOT Sec 73. Where output tax already paid before proceedings — Sec 75(12) applies
27.23 — Inquiry-Led Demand for Differential Tax on Admitted Turnover
Where data to compute demand is available in financials and returns — Proper Officer under Sec 67 has no jurisdiction. In classification disputes, Revenue cannot demand tax under new HSN AND adjust tax paid under old HSN simultaneously — each requires separate notice. Grounds in appeal must NOT introduce new material at this stage.
DIFFERENTIAL TAX — CLASSIFICATION DISPUTE GROUNDS
Self-Assessment Not Impeached
Classification adopted in self-assessment left unimpeached — payment under distress is NOT substitute for Revenue's burden
Without establishing HSN YYYY is more appropriate than HSN XXXX — demand for differential tax is arbitrary & illegal
No Identity of Goods Established
Which outward supply transactions form subject-matter of enquiry NOT identified
Pointing to general inventory and painting with single brush is affront to due process
Impugned Order parroted SCN allegations and acted under dictation
Sec 73 (Bona Fide)
BONA FIDE
SCN issued under Sec 73 = disaffirmation of mala fides → Sec 74 cannot be invoked
Payment under distress + issuance of debit note to customers ≠ admission of wrongdoing or wrong HSN
Three-step classification finding needed: (i) identity of goods (ii) technical basis for classification (iii) terms of supply — ALL absent
27.24 — Inquiry-Led Demand for Output Tax on 'Subsidy'
"Any financial transaction that occurs simultaneously with another and artificially lowers the transacted price — would be a subsidy." Where inquiry discovers flow of subsidy, demand for GST on subsidy is easily foisted because taxpayers report it as exempt or non-GST supply — or don't report it at all.
OUTPUT TAX ON SUBSIDY — APPROACH TO GROUNDS
Key Legal Principles
Taxing ingredients must be established to attract incidence of tax — same grounds as output tax demand apply
Self-assessment of taxability under Sec 59 cannot be easily impeached without evidence bringing transaction within Sec 9 r/w Sec 7 and Sec 12/13
Where "incurable defects" exist in Impugned SCN — Order affirming that illegality is malicious and must be set aside
STRATEGIC NOTE
Grounds on subsidy overlap substantially with grounds on other output tax demands — same infirmities apply: time of supply, place of supply, HSN, nature of transaction. Treat subsidy as a distinct factual sub-set and apply all common grounds accordingly.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
⇧ Index
ITC as Vested and Indefeasible Right — Supreme Court (Referred in Para 27.12)
Input tax credit is a vested and indefeasible right of the taxpayer. To deny this right requires much more than estimation, guesswork and conjecture. Without unseating compliance with vesting conditions, self-assessment cannot be lightly disturbed.
When to Use: Any ITC disallowance matter — open with this as the foundational principle before all other grounds
Senco Gold Ltd. — 02/WBAAR/2019-20 dated 8 May 2019 (Para 27.17)
Settlement of creditors includes settlement via 'account adjustment' — payment is not limited to bank transfers alone.
When to Use: Reversal of ITC on ground of unpaid suppliers — cite to show "payment" includes credit notes, return supplies, and account adjustments
Section 155 — Burden of Proof (Para 27.12, 27.19)
Appellant is responsible only to prove eligibility for claiming input tax credit. The burden of proof with respect to allegations made in the Impugned Order rests on the Revenue and not on the Appellant. The allegation is not one as to 'eligibility' to credit but on 'treatment' to be applied on eligible credits.
When to Use: When Revenue demands reversal without specific evidence — invoke Sec 155 to shift burden back to Revenue
Section 75(12) + Rule 88B — Interest Only Recovery (Para 27.22)
Where output tax payable has already been paid before initiating adjudication under Sec 73 or 74, further proceedings are to be continued under Sec 75(12). Recovery of interest must be initiated under Sec 79 r/w Rules 88B and 142B — NOT under Sec 73. A more specific provision must prevail over a less specific provision.
When to Use: When Sec 73 notice is issued solely for interest — challenge jurisdiction at the threshold
Section 47 vs Section 73 — Late Fee Jurisdiction (Para 27.21)
Levy of late fee is applicable under Section 47 but there is no statutory provision in Section 73 of the Central GST Act to demand late fee. When Legislature enacts a specific provision, that provision cannot be invoked for an alternate or additional purpose.
When to Use: Late fee demanded through SCN/OIO under Sec 73 — raise this ground as a preliminary jurisdictional objection
Circular 26/26/2017-GST dated 29 Dec 2017 — Net Values (Para 27.14)
Reporting of 'net values' and offline adjustment of 'negative values' is permitted vide Circular 26/26/2017-GST. These must be excluded from any third-party data comparison exercise.
When to Use: Third-party data mismatch demands — insist on exclusion of all adjustments covered by this Circular
Rule 37(4) — Temporary Nature of Reversal (Para 27.17)
Even credit reversed under Rule 37(1) is eligible to be restored under Rule 37(4). The Impugned Order converts a temporary reversal under law into a permanent one — which is contrary to the scheme of the statute.
When to Use: When OIO confirms reversal of ITC for 180-day delay — argue reversal is temporary and restorable
Notification 52/2023 — Not Applicable to Past Period (Para 27.16)
Notification No. 52/2023 dated 26 October 2023 comes into force from the date of its publication in the Official Gazette — not retrospectively from the Disputed Tax Period. Where the valuation mechanism itself was not in force, the transaction of corporate guarantee was never taxable in the Disputed Tax Period.
When to Use: Corporate guarantee GST demand for pre-Oct 2023 tax periods — attack the very foundation of valuation
Lex non cogit ad impossibilia
The law does not compel the doing of impossible things.
Use when: Revenue demands tax based on data that was not in existence, or computation mechanism not prescribed for the disputed period.
Delegatus non potest delegare
A delegate cannot further delegate.
Use when: Power exercised by wrong officer — e.g., Sec 130 confiscation by officer authorized only under Sec 129; or Sec 73 demand where Sec 75(12) was the specific remedy.
Generalia specialibus non derogant
General provisions do not derogate from specific ones.
Use when: Sec 73 invoked for interest-only or late-fee demands where more specific provisions (Sec 75(12), Sec 47) already exist.
Audi alteram partem
Hear the other side — no person should be condemned unheard.
Use when: Demand confirmed without giving Appellant meaningful opportunity to rebut specific allegation, or when new material introduced post-notice without fresh opportunity.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 27 — Pleadings in Appeal Memo (Full)
CHAPTER 27

Pleadings in Appeal Memo

Form · Belated Service · Rectification · Recovery Stay · Order Beyond Limitation · Save-the-Day Grounds · Sec 75(2) · Provisional Attachment · Audit-Led Demands
Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index — Click to Navigate

27.1 — Form for Communication Before Appellate Fora 27.2 — Belated Service of Impugned Order 27.3 — Rectification: Appeal Dismissed Due to Limitation Lapse 27.4 — Recovery After Statutory Appeal: Non-Issuance of APL-2 27.5 — Impugned Order Passed Beyond Limitation 27.6 — Grounds That Could 'Save the Day' 27.7 — Relief Under Section 75(2) 27.8 — Objections to Provisional Attachment 27.9 — Dismissal of Appeal Due to Payment via DRC-3 27.10 — Audit-Led Demand: Output Tax on Forward Charge 27.11 — Audit-Led Demand: Output Tax on Reverse Charge 27.12 — Audit-Led Demand: Input Tax Credit on Various Grounds ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

27.1 — Form for Communication Before Appellate Fora

§ 27.1 — Submission Before Quasi-Judicial Authority is NOT a Letter — "Pleadings Not Urged Will Prejudice Relief Prayed"
Core Principle of Pleadings
GOLDEN RULE "Pleadings not urged, will prejudice relief prayed." Omission to include pleadings, even if implicit or obvious to taxpayer, will be 'with prejudice'.
APPROACH Submission before quasi-judicial authority is NOT a letter. Approach to drafting varies based on: nature of proceedings · context · purpose · relief sought · addressee. Maintain consistency of formats and develop a consistent style of presenting written correspondence. Baseline authority: Sec 151 of CPC.
Standard Caption for Appellate Submissions
Before the Hon'ble Appellate Authority / Revisionary Authority / Appellate Tribunal / Judicial Magistrate I Class, ....CITY....
In the matter of ………………………
(under section 79(1)(g) of Central GST Act)

1. ………………………
                                                  …………… Petitioner / Appellant
                     Versus
2. ………………………
                                                  ……………… Respondent
Application under Section 151 of Code of Civil Procedure, 1908
Objections to Petition for recovery of demand considered undischarged arrears

THIS RES[PONDENT] MOST RESPECTFULLY SHEWETH THAT:
1. ………………… 2. …………………

PRAYER: In view of the foregoing, this Petitioner-Appellant/Applicant most respectfully prays that……………

Signature of Petitioner-Appellant/Applicant

27.2 — Belated Service of Impugned Order Passed Within Limitation

§ 27.2 — Service Beyond Limitation but Within Extended Time (Notification 53/2023-CT): Choice of Mode Must Be Fair; Sec 75(10) Vested Right Cannot Be Glossed Over
Core Principle — Mode of Service Must Be Fair
MISCHIEVOUS Choice of mode of service must also be fair and NOT mischievous to bring the taxpayer to forfeit right to appeal. Legislature prescribed fourteen (14) modes of service — the only explanation is not that the task gets done but that PO avails these alternatives to ensure fairness right from service of notice to service of order.
SEC 75(10) VESTED RIGHT Where there is belated service of order → vested rights created in taxpayer under Sec 75(10) cannot be glossed over by Appellate Authority by entering into merits in appeal WITHOUT first reaching a finding on the fatal delay in service of order.
Two Questions of Law Opened
SUBSTANTIAL QUESTIONS (i) Whether the use of the least expeditious mode of service by Proper Officer satisfies the ends of justice that 'service' of impugned order deserves;

(ii) Whether there is a loss of remedy due to non-prosecution amounting to neglect by taxpayer in omitting to be constantly clued into what new things may be posted online.
KEY OUTCOME Findings on these questions will open up new questions of law and fact maintainable under Sec 117 (HC). Omission to plead can result in forfeiture of relief that would have been available for taxpayer.
Illustrative Pleading (APL5)
Pleading — Belated Service (Para 27.2)
Service of Impugned Order involves a mixed question of law and facts. Impugned Order came to be served only on the date stated in DRC6 [or APL1/APL5] — it is trite law that 'date of notice or order' and 'date of communication of notice or order' are NOT the same.
Section 169(1)(d) permits online service by uploading Impugned Order on the GST portal — but while Sec 169 affords more than fourteen (14) different modes of service, the choice of any of the modes must itself show that it was just and proper and suited to 'put at notice' the Appellant to avail appellate remedies by adopting the most expeditious of modes possible.
In the instant case, given that the taxpayer is engaged in business that does not entail one to be user of online services every day, choosing the most inexpeditious to communicate is ex facie unjust and improper.
This appeal is liable to be admitted based on the pleading of actual date of service as stated in APL5 above, without adopting a pedantic view that affirms choice of least expeditious mode. Doing so would effectively rob the taxpayer of the remedy available in law — itself unjust on the part of Proper Officer if not mischievous, and perhaps a fraud perpetrated on this Adjudicating Authority.
PRAYER: Appeal be admitted on the date of actual communication of Impugned Order and a finding be reached as to the appropriateness of mode of service.

27.3 — Rectification of Appeal Dismissed Due to Lapse of Limitation

§ 27.3 — Rectification Under Sec 161 CGST Act: Appeal Was Within Sec 107(4)/112(6) Limitation; Dismissal Citing Pedantic View of Service = Fraud on Authority
When This Arises
SCENARIO Appeal dismissed by equating "date of order" with "date of communication of order" in Sec 112(1) and determining whether appeal is within limitation or beyond — without independently examining date of actual communication to taxpayer.
APPROACH Illustrative pleadings that are clear yet respectful while demanding justice — important task of putting at notice one who could potentially be respondent in a writ petition. Such person would just as easily redress this grievance and be safe from possible counter-allegation of misapplication.
Rectification Application (Sec 161 CGST Act)
THIS RESPONDENT MOST RESPECTFULLY SHEWETH THAT: (App for Rectification)
This has reference to order of dismissal of above-mentioned appeal filed on ……… [INCLUDE AS RELEVANT]. In this regard, we beg to submit before you that there are mistakes apparent on record that deserve your urgent attention by this application for rectification under section 161 of Central GST Act — dismissal of appeal citing delay beyond limitation under section 107(4)/112(6) of Central GST Act where a prima facie fit case on merits was made out but not received consideration.
Appeal against Impugned Order dated ………… which came to be served on ………… was filed on ………[INCLUDE DATE(S) AS RELEVANT] which is well within the due date prescribed under section 107(4)/112(6) of Central GST Act. As such the dismissal is ex facie illegal.
Further, dismissal of appeal at the threshold based on a pedantic view that accepts the choice of the least expeditious mode to serve the order, effectively robbing the Appellant the remedy of appeal, is unjust on the part of Proper Officer if not mischievous, is also a fraud perpetrated on the Appellate Authority.
Although Impugned Order may be 'served' by making that available on the GST portal under section 169(1)(d) of Central GST Act, but the same did not amount to 'communication' of Impugned Order only had Appellant come to know about same.
At this stage, without the merits of the appeal having received consideration, the acrimonious interpretation of law and erroneous application of law to facts become final and binding. Ends of justice demand that this application be considered and a finding reached as to the appropriateness of mode of service.
PRAYER: Opportunity to appear in-person and better present the irreparable prejudice that will result if this application were not considered based on questions of law that are imminent and essential to prevent miscarriage of justice.

27.4 — Recovery After Statutory Appeal: Non-Issuance of APL-2

§ 27.4 — Disputed Demand is 'Deemed to Be Stayed' Once Statutory Appeal Filed — Recovery Action Without APL-2 is Bad in Law
Legal Position
ILLEGAL RECOVERY Proper Officer proceeding to initiate recovery action under Sec 78 (read with CBIC instructions 1/2024-GST dated 30 May 2024) unaware that appeal has been filed — is bad in law, at least until limitation under Sec 79 has not passed and there is no risk of flight or other concern.
TAXPAYER'S ACTION Promptly inform Proper Officer that the disputed demand is 'deemed to be stayed' once statutory appeal is duly and properly filed. Recovery action will attract remedy before a Court of law if not withdrawn forthwith.
Two Scenarios — Letter to Proper Officer
Scenario A — Letter to Proper Officer (Taxpayer)
THIS TAXPAYER MOST RESPECTFULLY SHEWETH THAT:
Statutory appeal under section 107(1)/112(1) has been filed on ………… against order ………… dated ………… passed by Ld. …………, ………… Commissionerate.
Proper Officer has been duly informed vide letter dated ………… that statutory appeal has been filed before this office.
However, citing non-issuance of APL2, Proper Officer has issued / initiated recovery action vide letter dated ………… / DRC13 No. ………… dated ………… which has robbed Appellant of the statutory relief under section 107(7)/112(9) of Central GST Act.
