PRAYER: Proposed recovery action be withdrawn and prevent miscarriage of justice.
Scenario B — Application to Appellate Authority (where PO declines)
THIS RESPONDENT MOST RESPECTFULLY SHEWETH THAT:
Same three bullets as above (statutory appeal filed, PO informed, recovery action taken citing non-issuance of APL2).
PRAYER: Said APL2 be issued OR Proper Officer, being Respondent in this appeal, be directed to desist from such recovery action and prevent miscarriage of justice.

27.5 — Impugned Order Passed Beyond Limitation

§ 27.5 — Order Passed Beyond Statutory Limitation is Non Est in Law — No Requirement to Examine Date of Service — Entire Demand Given Quietus In Toto
Governing Principle
NON EST IN LAW Where 'date of communication' of order (check date of DRC7 or APL4) is beyond statutory limitation to pass orders → such orders are non est in law. There is no requirement to enter into examination of the date of their service.
SAFEGUARDS IN LAW Sec 75(10) applies to orders of adjudication. Sec 108(2) applies to orders of Revisionary Authority. These outer time limits do NOT apply to proceedings under Sec 107 or 112, notwithstanding (non-binding) guidance in Sec 107(13) or 113(4).
Illustrative Pleading
Ground — Impugned Order Passed Beyond Limitation
Impugned Order is liable to be dismissed for being time-barred in view of the date of service affirmed in APL1/APL5 above. When Impugned Order has been served after the period of limitation, it creates a vested right in the Appellant in view of the express provision in section 75(10) of Central GST Act based on the prevailing jurisprudence laid down by a catena of decisions of Apex Court going as far back as 1962.
This limitation is after considering the extension granted under section 168A of Central GST Act read with notification 9/2023-Central Tax dated 31 Mar. 2023 as amended by notification 56/2023-Central Tax dated 28 Dec. 2023.
In view of the above, the entire demand in Impugned Order is liable to be given quietus and demand set aside in toto.
Forum-Wise Applicability
WHERE TO RAISE Adjudication order: Before Appellate Authority (FAA)

First Appellate Order: Before Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT)

Revisionary Authority order: Before Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) under Sec 108(2)

27.6 — Grounds That Could 'Save the Day'

§ 27.6 — Include These Grounds in APL1/APL5 to Avoid Amendment Application — "Grounds Urged in APL Need Not Be Pressed Forward During Presentation"
Why These Grounds Must Be Included
CRITICAL RULE New grounds can be raised at any stage before any forum — but NOT without: (i) application for amendment of grounds presented, and (ii) ruling by authority for good and sufficient reasons to permit (or not). Bar in Rule 112 CGST Rules is limited to introduction of new evidence.
SAFE PRACTICE Grounds urged in APL1/APL5 need not be pressed forward during presentation of the appeal. Include them to avoid moving an amendment application and being subject to a ruling by the authority on this application.
Ready Grounds — Always Include in Appeal Memo
INCLUDE ALL APPLICABLE
Impugned Order is erroneous insofar as it demands interest when output tax (on RCM and on forward charge) are OR underlying demand for reversal of ITC itself is not payable and disputed ITC are admissible → demand for interest liable to be set aside.
Impugned Order is erroneous insofar as it demands penalty when the demand itself is erroneous, inapplicable and denied → demand for penalty liable to be set aside.
Impugned Order is illegal insofar as it demands penalty when the required animus is not shown to exist. Without the animus → demand for penalty is misapplication of law.
Impugned Order is illegal insofar as it demands penalty bypassing the general discipline in section 126 of CGST Act. Without considering the mitigating factors → demand for penalty mechanically is gross misapplication of law.
WITHOUT PREJUDICE, Impugned Order is contrary to law insofar as it does not allow cum-tax computation, even if it were to be assumed that the alleged rate of output tax were somehow correct and payable → entire demand liable to be modified. [CHECK AND RETAIN IF APPLICABLE]
Impugned Order is illegal insofar as authority exercised in underlying proceedings is without jurisdiction of law → entire demand liable to be set aside. [CHECK AND RETAIN IF APPLICABLE]
Impugned Order confirms demand pursuant to Impugned SCN issued under Sec 73 and NOT under Sec 74 → entire proceedings affirm absence of any mala fides on part of Appellant. Free of mala fides, self-assessment cannot be lightly dismissed or unseated unless interpretation adopted in self-assessment is patently wrong.
Impugned Order is illegal insofar as neither any allegation has been made nor any finding reached on facts that 'tax is collected but not deposited' which is also evident in election made to issue Impugned SCN under section 73/74 [DELETE ONE NOT APPLICABLE] and NOT issued under section 76, imputation of mala fides is incongruous.
Impugned Order is contrary to judicial authorities and administrative instructions that are binding in law → demand liable to be set aside.

27.7 — Relief Under Section 75(2)

§ 27.7 — Power to 'Downgrade' Notice from Sec 74 to Sec 73 is Vested ONLY with Appellate Authority and Appellate Tribunal Under Sec 75(2) — Relief Not Prayed = Cannot Be Granted
Why This Relief Must Be Pleaded
CRITICAL Proper Officer does NOT have authority to 'downgrade' a notice of demand from Sec 74 to Sec 73. This power is vested with Appellate Authority and Appellate Tribunal under Sec 75(2).
RELIEF NOT PRAYED = CANNOT BE GRANTED Relief not prayed cannot be granted, not even in the interests of justice. And if relief under Sec 75(2) cannot be granted in appellate proceedings, urging this relief is not undesirable.
TURN OVER EVENTS By the time appeal comes up for presentation, if any higher forum were to share the view entertained by Appellant (even if overturned later), such turn over events (even if short-lived) afford grounds to plead relief under Sec 75(2) at the time of disposal of appeal.
Ready Grounds for Sec 75(2) Relief (Include in Appeal)
GROUNDS TO INCLUDE
Impugned Order is bad in law insofar as it confirms demand under section 74 of Central GST Act without establishing the 'special circumstances' needed to support demand under extended period of limitation. Impugned SCN under section 74 is a mischievous and mala fide attempt at procuring unlawful extension of limitation for a demand that cannot but be made under section 73 of Central GST Act. For this reason, the demand, notwithstanding that the entire demand is disputed and allegations in support of demand rejected, within normal limitation would stand reduced to ₹ …… NIL…… [EDIT AS APPLICABLE]. As such and without prejudice, the demand to the extent of extended period of limitation is liable to be set aside.
Impugned Order is contrary to law insofar as it has upheld extension of limitation invoked to issue Impugned SCN without reaching any finding on which of the three (3) special circumstances are alleged and does not demonstrate the existence of ingredients necessary to support such a demand → demand to extent of extended period liable to be set aside.
Impugned Order is erroneous insofar as it further fails to sustain demand under extended period without demonstrating the superadded requirement of 'evasion of tax' — 4-ingredient test: (i) non-payment of tax / refund / credit, (ii) knowledge of liability / inadmissibility, (iii) active concealment to impair detection, (iv) gains from misadventure. Empty allegation parroting statutory language defeats Revenue's case.
Impugned Order is illegal insofar as the relief admissible under section 75(2) of Central GST Act has not been established. And for this reason, the said relief read with Circular 185/17/2022-GST dated 27 Dec. 2022 is liable to be allowed → demand to extent of extended period set aside.
Impugned Order is contrary to facts insofar as it admits that taxable turnover is 'inclusive of tax' but omits to compute demand on 'inclusive of tax' basis → demand to extent of extended period set aside. [ONLY WHERE DEMAND IS FOR OUTPUT TAX ON FCM OR RCM BASIS]

27.8 — Objections to Provisional Attachment Even After Initiation of Due Process

§ 27.8 — Provisional Attachment Under Sec 83: Pre-Emptive Power of Commissioner — Authorized Only Upon Initiation of Listed Proceedings — Balance of Convenience Heavily in Favour of Registered Person
Legal Framework — Sec 83
PRE-EMPTIVE POWER Pre-emptive power of provisional attachment left in hands of Commissioner (held in Matajog Dobey v. HC Behari AIR 1956 SC 44) — to be exercised immediately upon initiation of proceedings listed in Sec 83(1). Available to be exercised with discretion, even if it is not explicit.
PROCEEDINGS TABLE
Chapter of CGST ActSectionsProof of Initiation
Chapter XII — AssessmentSections 59 to 64ASMT10 / ASMT13 / ASMT16
Chapter XIV — Inspection, Search and ArrestSections 67 to 72INS1
Chapter XV — Demands and RecoverySections 73 to 84DRC1A
When Altered Circumstances Allow Withdrawal
IMPERMISSIBLE IF Circumstances which necessitated have changed or notice of demand for which provisional attachment was exercised, cannot be lightly dismissed — continuation of provisional attachment even after an application is made is evident in manner of presentation of relief.
Illustrative Letter of Objection (DRC22 Receipt)
Objection — Provisional Attachment (Form DRC22)
Undersigned is in receipt of provisional attachment notice in Form DRC22 under section 83 of Central GST Act read with rule 159 in respect of bank account No. ………… at ………… Bank, ………… Branch, which undersigned vehemently objects to for being premature and excessive use of authority, particularly when due process of law proceedings is underway and Registered Person is fully participating in the said proceedings as directed by Proper Officer.
Provisional attachment is authorized ONLY when one of the listed proceedings can be shown to be underway. Undersigned brings to kind attention that none of the above proceedings can be shown to be underway. As such the exceptional powers invoked are incongruous with the statutory provisions.
There is NO prejudice caused or likely to be caused to interests of Revenue when Registered Person: (a) is regular in filing returns; (b) has not been in default in discharging self-assessed taxes and there is no delinquency in discharging lawful dues from time to time; (c) has undergone Aadhaar verification on Common Portal; (d) there is no risk of flight — total amount for which provisional attachment made is ₹ ………… whereas total tax discharged per year is in excess of ₹ …………; (e) has business premises located within jurisdiction which comprises of valuable property sufficient to meet all current and any future liabilities.
Business has effectively come to a stand still due to provisional attachment of said bank account. With debits not being permitted, Registered Person is unable to pay salaries, supplier's bills, statutory payments, bank dues and other obligations. This has caused irreparable prejudice to the running of the business which is likely to shut down if the provisional attachment is not withdrawn.
In the absence of any undischarged arrear and full cooperation being extended in lawful proceedings, there is no justification to invoke exceptional powers in section 83 of CGST Act — which is more of misplaced apprehension. The balance of convenience is heavily in favour of Registered Person and amply justify withdrawal. Continuing to keep the bank account under provisional attachment is disproportionate and unjust.
RELIEF SOUGHT: Great and irreparable prejudice has been caused by provisional attachment as per above referred Form DRC22 and in the absence of any prejudice to Revenue, said provisional attachment be kindly withdrawn. Registered Person is agreeable for a charge to be created on any other assets in the business — copy of Balance Sheet is attached. Registered Person undertakes that undisputed arrears, if any, will be discharged within seven (7) days of its intimation and will participate in any enquiry initiated in accordance with law.

27.9 — Dismissal of Appeal Due to Payment of Pre-Deposit via DRC-3

§ 27.9 — APL5 is Both Appeal Memo AND Challan for Pre-Deposit — DRC3 Payment Under Extraneous Circumstances ≠ Admission of Liability — Articles 246A, 265 & 300A Violated
When This Situation Arises
SCENARIOS Taxpayers may have deposited disputed demand via DRC3 when:
(i) Voluntarily or otherwise to prevent recovery action pending constitution of Appellate Tribunal
(ii) Pursuant to recovery action via DRC13 under Sec 78 without awaiting constitution of Appellate Tribunal
(iii) Pursuant to Court directions in absence of constitution of Appellate Tribunal
(iv) Payment of disputed penalty under Sec 129 to secure release of consignment-conveyance when release under bond was not permitted
Illustrative Grounds (Two Scenarios)
Scenario A — Pre-Deposit Portal Not Operational
Impugned Order is bad in law insofar as it affirms payments made as if there was any admission of liability. This appeal is evidence that the payments made were so made under extraneous circumstances — payment was recovered before issuance of notice / payment made as a pre-condition for release of consignment / conveyance / persons detained / documents seized.
Selection of 'voluntary' as category for making such payment was without prejudice as there is no option to select 'involuntary' on Common Portal. Without any admitted liability or a finalized demand, not even a willing taxpayer can pay non-existent liability.
In so affirming payment made in the extraneous circumstances detailed in SoF, Impugned Order has travelled beyond Articles 246A, 265 and 300A of the Constitution of India. For this reason, the payment made is 'without prejudice' to the relief prayed in this appeal and any affirmation or appropriation of payment are without authority of law.
Scenario B — Online Functionalities for Pre-Deposit Not Available
Same structure as Scenario A — but reason for extraneous payment is: online functionalities for pre-deposit payment were not operational at that time.
Substantive remedies in appeal cannot be lightly denied when pre-deposit is made albeit by a mode that is not inappropriate even if it is not via APL1/APL5 itself, due to limitations of Common Portal.

27.10 — Audit-Led Demand for Output Tax on Forward Charge Basis

§ 27.10 — Grounds of Appeal: Demand for Output Tax (FCM) in Annexure A — Estimation / Guesswork / Conjecture Cannot Support Sec 74 Demand
G1 — Estimation / Guesswork / Conjecture Incompatible with Sec 74
ILLEGAL Demand confirmed based on estimation, guesswork and conjecture — incompatible with Sec 74 which requires demand based on cogent reasons and corroborative evidence. Adverse inferences based on data mismatch between underlying records insufficient to affirm demand for output tax on FCM basis → entire demand in Annexure A liable to be set aside.
G2 — Audit Concluded; Demand Cannot Be Based on Adverse Inference
APPROBATE & REPROBATE Demand confirmed based on thorough audit having been conducted and concluded under Sec 65 — yet demands are supported by adverse inference relying on data mismatch. Trite law: where audit has been conducted → demand cannot be based on adverse inference. Revenue cannot approbate and reprobate. If audit could not be completed → proceedings under Sec 65 ought to have been withdrawn and proceedings under Sec 67 initiated.
G3 — Five Taxing Ingredients of Output Tax Not Established
SEC 9(1) CGST / SEC 5(1) IGST Demand for output tax made without establishing taxing ingredients required to attract incidence of tax:
(i) Taxability of transactions
(ii) Categorization as supply of goods or services
(iii) HSN tariff classification
(iv) Time of supply
(v) Place of supply
Merely computing tax does not satisfy requirements of law. Without discharging burden of proof → merely computing output tax is illegal → demand liable to be set aside.
G4 — Linear Comparison Ignores Tax Liability of Earlier Period Discharged in Subsequent Period
CONTRARY TO FACTS Assumes tax has not been discharged based on linear comparison within specific tax period. When audit assignment issued for Jul 2017 to Mar 20XX → tax liability of earlier tax period discharged in subsequent tax period ought to have been considered. Routinely making comparison of liability is illegal → demand liable to be set aside.
Additional Grounds (G5 to G11)
G5 — GSTR-9 Filed; Earlier Returns Null Once Annual Returns in Form GSTR-9 filed under Sec 44 → taking up for comparison earlier returns for disputed tax period is a nullity in law and offends instructions of Government to report rectified data. Demand for output tax based on outdated data cannot be sustained.
G6 — Profit & Loss Account Entry ≠ Income Demand for output tax based on entries in "profit and loss account" as income received or accrued. Demand made merely based on reflection of entry is contrary to the law.
G7 — Sec 59 Self-Assessment Cannot Be Impeached Without Evidence (Sec 9 r/w Sec 7 & 12) Appellant's self-assessment of taxability under liberty of Sec 59 cannot be easily impeached without producing evidence to bring transaction within operation of Sec 9 r/w Sec 7 and Sec 12.
G8 — CGST (Second) Removal of Difficulties Order 2018 Requires 21 months' data for comparison of 9 months returns for 2017-18 and 18 months' data for comparison for 2018-19 and subsequent. Carrying out linear comparison of 9 months data or 6 months data is ex facie erroneous.
G9 — CBIC Instructions 26/26/2017-GST (29 Jan 2022): Netting Off Negative Values "Netting off negative values" do not ipsi dixit result in recoverable demands. Instruction of the Board cannot be disregarded by Proper Officer.
G10 — CBIC Instructions 1/2022-GST (7 Jan 2022): Bona Fide Errors Admits "bona fide errors" do not ipsi dixit result in recoverable demands. Instruction of the Board cannot be disregarded by Proper Officer.

27.11 — Audit-Led Demand for Output Tax on Reverse Charge Basis

§ 27.11 — Grounds of Appeal: Demand for Output Tax (RCM) in Annexure B — Several Important Dissimilarities with FCM Grounds; Sec 9(3)/(4) Cannot Sustain Without Sec 9(1) Incidence
G1 — Sec 59 Self-Assessment Cannot Be Impeached (Sec 9 r/w Sec 7, 12/13)
AUTHORITY IN LAW Demand attempts to challenge Appellant's self-assessment of taxability under authority conferred by Sec 59 — which cannot be easily impeached without providing evidence to bring alleged transaction within Sec 9 r/w Sec 7 and 12 or 13 of CGST Act or Sec 5 of IGST Act → entire demand in Annexure B liable to be set aside.
G2 — Sec 9(3)/(4) Cannot Sustain Without Sec 9(1) Incidence Being Established
FUNDAMENTAL RULE Impugned Order fails to make demand under Sec 9(3) or 9(4). Also fails to show that alleged transaction is exigible to tax in first instance under Sec 9(1). Liability to discharge tax under Sec 9(3) or 9(4) cannot sustain without incidence to tax under Sec 9(1) being established. AND/OR same analysis for Sec 5(3)/5(4) of IGST Act r/w Sec 5(1).
G3 — Books of Account vs Time of Supply: Expenditure ≠ Taxable Turnover
ACCOUNTING VS GST LAW Demand for output tax on RCM based on data in 'books of account'. Trite law: expenditure is reported in 'books of account' based on rules of accounting — whereas turnover in GST is reported based on 'time of supply'. Mere classification as 'certain expenditure accounts' does not affirm that expenditure in said ledger accounts satisfies ingredients of taxable event in law.
G4 to G7 — Additional Grounds
G4 — Nature and Form of Supply Not Classified Fails to classify the 'nature' and 'form' of supply on which tax demanded on RCM basis. Revenue assumed HSN code merely based on entries in books of account — attempting to unseat self-assessment. Demand based on mere assumption, guesswork and conjecture is illegal.
G5 — Total Output Tax Discharged Exceeds Demand; ECL Verification When total output tax discharged has been shown to exceed total demand of output tax on RCM basis → alleged demand, to extent applicable, has already been discharged and ITC thereof availed in same tax period. Verification of ECL reveals this position.
G6 — Time of Supply Not Established Failed to establish the 'time of supply'. Demand estimated based on reflection of entries in financial statements alone — has not considered actual time of supply. Not even a willing taxpayer can accept to discharge a non-existent tax liability. Tax already discharged cannot be demanded once again without there being yet another occasion to attract the incidence of tax.
G7 — Double Demand for Royalty / Specific Transactions Impugned Order makes a 'double demand' of GST on RCM basis in respect of royalty paid by Appellant when it is already discharged from time to time → entire demand in Annexure B liable to be set aside.

27.12 — Audit-Led Demand for Input Tax Credit on Various Grounds

§ 27.12 — Grounds of Appeal: Demand for Reversal / Repayment of ITC in Annexure C — GSTR-2A vs 3B Linear Comparison Unreliable; Six Reasons for Mismatch; Vested and Indefeasible Right
G1 — Linear Comparison GSTR-2A vs 3B: No Reliable Material; Adverse Inference Insufficient
UNRELIABLE DATA Demand based on linear comparison of data auto-populated in GSTR-2A with that availed in GSTR-3B. No reliable material in support. When underlying data set is doubtful → any conclusion derived based on such unreliable data set is also tainted with the same unreliability. No demand for reversal of ITC can be sustained by adverse inference based on estimation, guesswork and conjecture.
G2 — Six Reasons Why GSTR-2A vs GSTR-3B Linear Comparison is Imminently Erroneous
TABLE OF REASONS
Reason for DifferenceEffect on Mismatch
(a) Credit matching for each FY to be examined based on data reported up to due date extended under Sec 16(4) of CGST ActDecrease
(b) Credit appearing in GSTR-2A includes inadmissible credits under Sec 17(5) of CGST ActIncrease
(c) Credit appearing in GSTR-2A omits to include bona fide credits subject to 'certificate-based verification' in terms of Circular 183/15/2022-GST dated 27 Dec. 2022Decrease
(d) Credit not appearing in GSTR-2A but recovery action successfully carried out against Suppliers in terms of CBIC Press Release of 4 May 2018Decrease
(e) Credit not appearing in GSTR-2A but rectified by Supplier subsequently in terms of CBIC instruction 1/2022-GST dated 7 Jan. 2022Decrease
(f) Credit already appearing in GSTR-2A of FY but claimed in GSTR3B of FY+1 as permitted under section 16(4) of Central GST ActIncrease
Without establishing effect of these factors → demanding reversal/repayment of bona fide ITC based on data differences is illegal and contrary to law.
G3 — GSTR-2A Does Not Prove Timely Discharge by Suppliers; Sec 16(2)(c) Not Violated
CONJECTURE Non-reflection of data in GSTR-2A does NOT establish violation of condition prescribed in Sec 16(2)(c). Data in GSTR-2A only shows suppliers have admitted their liability by filing returns under Sec 37 — it does not prove timely discharge of taxes. Impugned Order makes adverse inference by conjecture → contrary to facts.
G4 — GSTR-2A is Procedure, Not Mandate (Circular 183/15/2022-GST) Data appearing in GSTR-2A is a matter of procedure and NOT a mandate in law. Matters of procedure are directory in nature and not mandatory. The fact that there is no bar on claiming admissible ITC even when there is mismatch as seen from GSTR-2A as proof of matching is procedural — Appellant free to otherwise satisfy conditions of Sec 16(2)(c) as evidenced by Circular 183/15/2022-GST dated 27 Dec. 2022.
G5 — Notification 49/2019: GSTR-2A Introduced Prospectively Impugned Order attempts to give retrospective effect to Notification No. 49/2019-Central Tax dated 9 Oct. 2019 which introduced the concept of GSTR-2A prospectively → demand on this issue liable to be set aside.
G6 — Vested and Indefeasible Right: Self-Assessment Cannot Be Lightly Disturbed Impugned Order is bad in law insofar as it attempts to recover vested and indefeasible ITC. Without unseating the compliance with vesting conditions, self-assessment carried out cannot be lightly disturbed. For demanding repayment of bona fide ITC on a mere assumption → material evidence must be brought on record to impeach this satisfaction. Nothing brought on record in Impugned Order.

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 27: Pleadings in Appeal Memo

These principles and maxims are for use when arguing maintainability of appeals, challenging service of order, opposing provisional attachment, and putting forward audit-led demand grounds before FAA, GSTAT and High Court. State them confidently, cite the section / case / notification, and link them precisely to your client's facts.

⚖️ Key Legal Principles — Chapter 27

Principle 1 — "Pleadings Not Urged Will Prejudice Relief Prayed" — The Golden Rule of Appellate Drafting
Para 27.1 | Section 111(2) CGST Act r/w Section 151 CPC — Inherent Powers of Appellate Tribunal
"Omission to include pleadings, even if implicit or obvious to taxpayer, will be 'with prejudice'. Grounds urged in APL1/APL5 need not be pressed forward during presentation of the appeal. Certain grounds are illustrated that could be 'save the day' if they were included in the appeal memo so as not to require moving an amendment application and being subject to a ruling to be pronounced by the authority."
🎯 Use When: Filing any appeal — always include all possible grounds even if not all will be argued. The art of appellate drafting is that grounds not argued are still on record as a safety net.
Principle 2 — Mode of Service: Choice of Least Expeditious Mode is Mischievous; Section 75(10) Creates Vested Right on Belated Service
Para 27.2 | Section 75(10), 107(1), 112(1), 169 CGST Act | Notification 53/2023-Central Tax dated 2 Nov. 2023
"When for carrying out one task — sending the notice, order or communication to taxpayer — if Legislature has prescribed fourteen (14) modes, the only explanation is not that this task gets done but Proper Officer avail of these alternatives to ensure fairness right from service of notice all the way to service of order. And where there is a belated service of order, vested rights created in taxpayer under section 75(10) cannot be glossed over by Appellate Authority by entering into merits in appeal without first reaching a finding on the fatal delay in service of order."
🎯 Use When: Appeal appears time-barred because Revenue served the order by uploading on GST portal (Sec 169(1)(d)) without email/physical service — argue that choice of least expeditious mode is mischievous and date of actual communication governs limitation.
Principle 3 — Dismissed Appeal: Pedantic View of Service = Fraud Perpetrated on Appellate Authority
Para 27.3 | Section 161, 107(4), 112(6) CGST Act — Rectification of Order
"Dismissal of appeal at the threshold of these proceedings based on a pedantic view that accepts the choice of the least expeditious mode to serve the order, effectively robbing the Appellant the remedy of appeal is unjust on the part of Proper Officer if not mischievous, is also a fraud perpetrated on the Appellate Authority. Although Impugned Order may be 'served' by making that available on the GST portal under section 169(1)(d) of Central GST Act, but the same did not amount to 'communication' of Impugned Order only had Appellant come to know about same."
🎯 Use When: Filing rectification application under Sec 161 against dismissal of appeal for limitation — argue that date of portal upload ≠ date of communication; dismissal on this basis is a mistake apparent on the record.
Principle 4 — Disputed Demand 'Deemed to Be Stayed' Once Statutory Appeal Filed; Recovery Without APL-2 is Bad in Law
Para 27.4 | Section 107(7), 112(9) CGST Act | CBIC Instructions 1/2024-GST dated 30 May 2024
"Unaware that appeal has been filed, Proper Officer proceeding to initiate recovery action under section 78 (read with CBIC instructions 1/2024-GST dated 30 May 2024) is bad in law, at least until limitation under section 79 has not passed and there is no risk of flight or other concern that taxpayer may avoid paying lawful dues. Taxpayer-Appellant will do well to promptly inform Proper Officer that the disputed demand is 'deemed to be stayed' once statutory appeal is duly and properly filed."
🎯 Use When: Proper Officer initiates recovery action after statutory appeal has been filed — immediately write to PO citing statutory stay under Sec 107(7)/112(9) and demand withdrawal of recovery action; if PO persists, file application before Appellate Authority / seek writ relief.
Principle 5 — Order Passed Beyond Statutory Limitation is Non Est in Law — No Examination of Service Date Required
Para 27.5 | Section 75(10), 108(2) CGST Act | Notification 9/2023-CT dated 31 Mar. 2023 as amended by 56/2023-CT dated 28 Dec. 2023 | Section 168A CGST Act
"Where the 'date of communication' of the order (check date of DRC7 or APL4) is beyond the statutory limitation to pass orders, such orders are non est in law. There is no requirement to enter into examination of the date of their service. When Impugned Order has been served after the period of limitation, it creates a vested right in the Appellant in view of the express provision in section 75(10) of Central GST Act based on the prevailing jurisprudence laid down by a catena of decisions of Apex Court going as far back as 1962."
🎯 Use When: OIO or FAA order is passed beyond the statutory time limit — raise this as the very first ground; if the order is non est, the Appellate Authority need not go into the merits at all. Entire demand given quietus in toto.
Principle 6 — Section 75(2) Relief: Power to Downgrade Sec 74 to Sec 73 Vested ONLY with Appellate Authority / GSTAT — Relief Not Prayed Cannot Be Granted
Para 27.7 | Section 75(2) CGST Act | Circular No. 185/17/2022-GST dated 27 December 2022
"Although Proper Officer does not have authority to 'downgrade' a notice of demand from section 74 to section 73, this power is vested with Appellate Authority and Appellate Tribunal under section 75(2). Relief not prayed cannot be granted, not even in the interests of justice. And if relief under section 75(2) cannot be granted in appellate proceedings, urging this relief is not undesirable in appellate proceedings."
🎯 Use When: Filing any appeal against Sec 74 demand — ALWAYS include the Sec 75(2) ground requesting the Authority to downgrade the demand to Sec 73 and treat the extended period demand as non-existent. Never omit this ground.
Principle 7 — Provisional Attachment: Exceptional Power Must Be Exercised with Discretion — Balance of Convenience Heavily in Favour of Registered Person
Para 27.8 | Section 83 CGST Act r/w Rule 159 CGST Rules | Matajog Dobey v. HC Behari AIR 1956 SC 44
"Pre-emptive power of provisional attachment is left in the hands of Commissioner (held in Matajog Dobey v. HC Behari AIR 1956 SC 44) to be exercised immediately upon initiation of proceedings listed in section 83(1). Available to be exercised with discretion, even if it is not explicit. In the absence of any undischarged arrear and full cooperation being extended in lawful proceedings initiated in accordance with law, there is no justification to invoke exceptional powers in section 83 of Central GST Act — which is more of misplaced apprehension."
🎯 Use When: Bank account or assets of a regular taxpayer are provisionally attached — argue (i) no qualifying proceedings underway, (ii) no risk of flight, (iii) regular filer, (iv) assets sufficient to meet all demands, (v) business brought to standstill — balance of convenience heavily in favour of withdrawal.
Principle 8 — DRC-3 Payment Under Extraneous Circumstances ≠ Admission of Liability — Articles 246A, 265 & 300A of Constitution Violated
Para 27.9 | Articles 246A, 265, 300A of Constitution of India | Section 107, 112 CGST Act
"This appeal is evidence that the payments made were so made under extraneous circumstances. And that the selection of 'voluntary' as category for making such payment was without prejudice as there is no option to select 'involuntary' on Common Portal. Without any admitted liability or a finalized demand, not even a willing taxpayer can pay non-existent liability. In so affirming payment made in the extraneous circumstances, Impugned Order has travelled beyond Articles 246A, 265 and 300A of the Constitution of India."
🎯 Use When: Appellate Authority dismisses appeal treating DRC-3 payment as pre-deposit and thereby admission of liability — argue that the payment was made under compulsion; constitution bars taxation without authority of law regardless of mode of payment.
Principle 9 — Audit Concluded with ADT2; Demand Cannot Be Based on Adverse Inference (Revenue Cannot Approbate and Reprobate)
Para 27.10 | Section 65, 67 CGST Act — Audit-Led Output Tax Demands
"Demand confirmed based on a thorough audit having been conducted and concluded under section 65 of Central GST Act — yet demands are supported by adverse inference relying on data mismatch. It is trite law that where audit has been conducted, then demand cannot be based on adverse inference. Revenue cannot approbate and reprobate. If audit could not be completed, then proceedings under section 65 ought to have been withdrawn and proceedings under section 67 of Central GST Act initiated."
🎯 Use When: Sec 65 audit has concluded with ADT2 and yet the SCN / OIO relies on data mismatch and adverse inference — the Revenue is approbating the audit (using it as jurisdictional basis) and reprobating it (ignoring its conclusion). Both positions are impermissible.
Principle 10 — GSTR-2A is Procedure, Not Mandate; Circular 183/15/2022-GST (27 Dec 2022): Certificate-Based Verification
Para 27.12 | Section 16(2)(c), (d) CGST Act | Circular 183/15/2022-GST dated 27 Dec. 2022 | Notification 49/2019-CT dated 9 Oct. 2019
"Data appearing in GSTR-2A is a matter of procedure and not a mandate in law. Matters of procedure are directory in nature and not mandatory. The fact that there is no bar on claiming admissible input tax credit even when there is mismatch of data as seen from GSTR-2A as proof of matching is procedural and Appellant is free to otherwise satisfy the conditions of section 16(2)(c) of Central GST Act as evidenced by circular 183/15/2022-GST dated 27 Dec. 2022."
🎯 Use When: Revenue demands reversal of ITC solely on the basis of GSTR-2A vs GSTR-3B mismatch without verifying actual compliance with Sec 16 conditions — argue that GSTR-2A is directory and taxpayer can satisfy Sec 16(2)(c) through certificate-based verification under Circular 183.

🔤 Latin Maxims — Chapter 27

Ex Facie
Meaning: "On the face of it" or "from the face" — apparent without further inquiry or examination. Used throughout Chapter 27 to describe dismissal of appeals on pedantic grounds as ex facie illegal, and errors on the face of the record which form the basis of a rectification application.
Use in Hearing: "The dismissal of this appeal citing delay beyond limitation is ex facie illegal — on the very face of the APL5 filed, the date of actual service of the Impugned Order is stated to be ………… which brings the filing well within the due date prescribed under Section 107(4)/112(6) of the Central GST Act. No further inquiry is required to establish this position."
Prima Facie
Meaning: "At first sight" or "on first appearance" — on the evidence initially available, which may be rebutted by further evidence. A prima facie fit case on merits must have received consideration before being dismissed — dismissal in limine without consideration is impermissible.
Use in Hearing: "Dismissal of appeal citing delay where a prima facie fit case on merits was made out but not received consideration on account of dismissal at the threshold of the substantive remedy available in law — is contrary to the purpose of appellate proceedings. This Tribunal is requested to admit this appeal and consider it on merits."
Approbate and Reprobate
Meaning: One cannot both approve and disapprove the same thing — cannot take advantage of something while simultaneously denying its consequences. Used where Revenue treats audit as valid for jurisdiction but ignores its conclusion (ADT2) when making demands based on adverse inference.
Use in Hearing: "Revenue cannot approbate and reprobate. Having conducted a thorough audit under Section 65 and concluded it with ADT2, Revenue cannot then make demands supported only by adverse inference relying on data mismatch — ignoring the very findings of the concluded audit. It is trite law that where audit has been conducted and concluded, demand cannot be based on adverse inference."
Impasse
Meaning: A situation in which no progress is possible — a deadlock. Provisional attachment of bank account creates an impasse for the business — salaries cannot be paid, suppliers cannot be paid, statutory payments cannot be made, ultimately causing the business to shut down.
Use in Hearing: "This letter of objection-cum-request for withdrawal of provisional attachment is made to remedy this impasse that the provisional attachment has caused. With debits not being permitted from the provisionally attached bank account, the Registered Person is unable to meet any of its obligations — this constitutes irreparable prejudice to the running of the business and amply justifies immediate withdrawal."
Ipsi Dixit (in ITC Reversal Context)
Meaning: "He himself said it" — an unproven assertion. In the ITC reversal context: not every claim of inadmissible credit ipsi dixit establishes that vesting conditions have been violated. ITC is a vested and indefeasible right — the burden of proving non-compliance with vesting conditions lies on Revenue.
Use in Hearing: "Demand for reversal of input tax credit cannot be sustained by adverse inference based on estimation, guesswork and conjecture. Input tax credit is a cause-of-action vested and indefeasible right. Not every non-reflection of data in GSTR-2A ipsi dixit establishes violation of section 16(2)(c) — it only shows the supplier has admitted liability by filing returns; it does not prove timely discharge of taxes by the supplier."
Catena of Decisions
Meaning: A chain or series of judicial decisions — a line of precedents consistently supporting a legal principle. A catena of decisions of the Apex Court going as far back as 1962 supports the proposition that an order passed beyond statutory limitation creates a vested right in the taxpayer under Section 75(10).
Use in Hearing: "It is respectfully submitted that the Impugned Order, having been communicated after the period of limitation, is non est in law. A vested right is created in the Appellant in view of the express provision in Section 75(10) of the Central GST Act, supported by a catena of decisions of the Apex Court going as far back as 1962 — the entire demand is liable to be given quietus and set aside in toto."
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 27 — Pleadings in Appeal Memo
⚖️ Chapter 29 — Appellate Tribunal GSTAT (Para 29.1–29.7)

Chapter 29 — Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT)

Para 29.1–29.7 | Pages 699–718 | Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Table of Contents

29.1 Notes on APL-5 (Memorandum of Appeal) 29.2 Notes on APL-6 (Cross Objections) 29.3 CoD Application & Affidavit 29.4 Preliminary Grounds 1 — Section 61 29.5 Preliminary Grounds 2 — Section 65 29.6 Preliminary Grounds 3 — Section 67 29.7 Preliminary Grounds 4 — Limitation + Burden of Proof ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

29.1 · Notes on APL-5 — Memorandum of Appeal (GSTAT)

Form GST APL-05 — Appeal to GSTAT under Section 112
Critical Filing Rules
CRITICAL
Separate APL-5 for every OIO
even if disposed in single OIA
Use LEGAL NAME — not trade name
(state trade name in SOF)
Do NOT fill AR's name in Field 12
— POA submitted separately
after APL-2A issued
DO NOT sign on Left side box
— Appellant signs on Right
Field 9 — Date of Communication:
Do NOT fill without Affidavit
and Proof of Service
Respondents — Field 4
4(a)
Commissioner of...
[Central Appeals]
OR Revisionary Authority
[if against RVN1 Order]
NEVER
4(b) Supervisor/PO
in original proceedings
FAA is NEVER a
Respondent before GSTAT
4(c)
Original Adjudicating Authority
(whose records are
called for in this appeal)
Field 10 — Order Type (Annexure A)
TypeForm
Demand OrderDRC7 ✓
Refund OrderRFD6 ✓
Registration OrderREG5 ✓
Enforcement OrderMOV9/11 ✓
Recovery OrderDRC13 — NO
Other OrderANY — NO
Field 14(b) — Category of Case
Standard response: "Various questions of fact and law are involved in this appeal, and it is not feasible to accurately (i) arrange within options in Annexure B (ii) allocate disputed demand and (iii) link statutory provisions. Details in SoF read with GoA attached separately in respect of each issue raised in this appeal."
DOES NOT INVOLVE
(i) Registration cancellation S.25
(ii) Refund S.54
(iii) Anti-profiteering S.171
(iv) PoS under IGST Act
[Check and delete not relevant]
3 Mandatory Tables in Every APL-5
Table X
Disputed tax period, SCN date, service date, OIO date, service date, Total demand (CGST/SGST/IGST/Cess), Underlying proceedings, SCN issued under
Table Y
All documents on record: ADT2, Authorization, Reply, MOV1-6, INS1-5, Proper Officer, DRC3, SCN+DRC1, RUDs, Adjudication docs, First appeal docs, Revisionary proceedings docs
Table Z
Issues list (A), (B)...
vs ADT2 / SCN / OIO / OIA columns (YES/NO)
+ Demand (Rs. Lakhs) per issue
SOF Structure (Paras 10(b), 15, 16, 17)
Para 1: M/s... GSTIN... Disputed Tax Period
→
Para 2: Original proceedings — SCN date → OIO date (ex parte/after reply)
→
Para 3: Appellant appealed to FAA → OIA date (aggrieved) → prefers THIS appeal u/s 112(1)
→
OR Revenue appealed → OIA adverse → prefers THIS appeal u/s 112(1)
→
OR Revenue agitated in Revisionary proceedings → prefers THIS appeal
→
Para 4: Details in Annexure A (order type), Annexure D (about appellant), Annexure E (Tables X, Y, Z)
Grounds of Appeal Structure
Preliminary Grounds
1. .......
2. .......
Issue (A): Output Tax
3. .......
4. .......
Issue (B): ITC Reversal
5. .......
6. .......
Common Grounds
7. .......
8. .......
PRAYER
(a) Set aside entire disputed demand
(b) Pass orders fit and proper in facts
Craves leave to add/modify/withdraw grounds
without prejudice to each other
Pre-Deposit Calculation
10% pre-deposit of disputed tax (or 20% in ewb/penalty cases) OR 100% in penalty-only cases
DO NOT
Leave pre-deposit column auto-populated — it will be EDITABLE field
Pre-deposit must include pre-deposit of admitted demand and any other amount payable (interest, penalty, late fee, other charges)

29.2 · Notes on APL-6 — Memorandum of Cross Objections

Form GST APL-06 — Cross Objections under Section 112(5)
When Filed
Against departmental appeal (APL-7) filed by Revenue/Revisionary Authority before GSTAT
LIMITATION
Filed from date of ORDER,
NOT date of communication
of order — check carefully
Respondent Field 4(a)
Commissioner of... [ALWAYS in case of Central MoCO]
OR
Revisionary Authority [if APL-6 is against RVN-1 order]
Field 4(b): Jurisdiction = Original proceedings S.61/63/65 or 67
Field 4(c): Order passed by: same as 4(a)
Field 8 — Summary of Issues
Standard response (same as APL-5 Field 14(b)): "Various questions of fact and law are involved — Details in SoF read with GoCO attached separately in respect of each issue raised in this appeal."
Field 9 — Date of Receipt of Notice
KEY DISTINCTION
= Date of Communication of APL-7
NOT the same as Col. 5
(date of communication of OIA/RA order)
Fields 13 & 14 — Grounds + Para-wise Reply
Grounds of cross-objections: attached separately
Para-wise reply: attached separately
APL-2A → POA Sequence
DO NOT
Fill AR's name in Field 6
— POA may be submitted
separately after APL-2A issued

29.3 · CoD Application & Affidavit at GSTAT

Application under Section 151 CPC for Condonation of Delay at GSTAT
Application Format
Before Hon'ble GSTAT Bench
In the matter of Appeal against OIA/RA No....dated....
Arising out of OIO No....dated....
For reasons stated in accompanying Affidavit, Appellant prays that CoD be allowed under inherent powers and appeal admitted for disposal on grounds urged in APL-5/APL-6 filed on.... which are relied upon but not reiterated for brevity.
Section 112(6) — Maximum Condonable Delay
KEY RULE
Delay must be within MAXIMUM
condonable delay under
Section 112(6) counted from
date of communication of Order
as stated in APL-5/APL-6
THAT Clauses in Affidavit
THAT registered under S.22
holding GSTIN... based on
authorization of Principal Commissioner...
THAT original proceedings under
S.61/63/65/67 culminated in order
bearing No.... under S.107(11)/108
THAT being aggrieved, statutory appeal
under S.112(1)/cross objections under
S.112(5) has been preferred before
Hon'ble Bench of GSTAT
THAT statutory limitation of
3 months ended on... but filed on...
resulting in delay of ... days
THAT Row 1 typical delay reason:
Appellant was given to understand
remedy was to prefer rectification
under S.161, but realized appropriate
remedy is statutory appeal under S.112
4 Mandatory Closing THAT Clauses
THAT no other reason for this delay — NOT deliberate or neglect
THAT no prejudice will be caused to Revenue if delay condoned
THAT a good case is made out on merits — ends of justice better served by condoning delay
THAT application is submitted before this Hon'ble Bench

29.4 · Preliminary Ground 1 — Section 61 (Scrutiny of Returns)

Section 61 — Chapter XII — Verification of Correctness Only
Section 61 Jurisdiction Limit
Section 61 resides in Chapter XII — "Verification of Correctness"
Proper Officer acting under S.61 CANNOT enter into matters involving "plurality of interpretation of" applicable statutory provisions, where SCN involves alternate interpretation to data already contained in contemporaneous records and returns available on Common Portal.
RULE
Such exercise would entail entering into jurisdiction of Proper Officer authorized under Section 65 (Chapter XIII) — beyond S.61 jurisdiction
Ground 1 — Verification of Correctness
Impugned Order is bad in law — no finding reached on question of jurisdiction raised at adjudication, but proceeded to enter into merits when underlying proceedings are entirely void ab initio for want of jurisdiction. PO acting under S.61 (Chapter XII) could NOT enter into matters involving "verification of correctness" of self-assessment where issues involve records-based findings requiring verification of contemporaneous documents — that is jurisdiction of S.65 (Chapter XIII). Demand liable to be set aside in toto.
Ground 2 — Plurality of Interpretation
Impugned Order is bad in law — underlying proceedings are entirely void ab initio for want of jurisdiction. PO acting under S.61 could NOT enter into matters involving "plurality of interpretation of" applicable statutory provisions, where SCN involves alternate interpretation to data already on Common Portal. Such exercise falls under S.65 (Chapter XIII) jurisdiction.
Implied Finding in Favour of Appellant
STRATEGIC ARGUMENT
By failing to reach affirmative finding on jurisdiction and skirting the issue, Impugned Order has impliedly found in favour of Appellant that the underlying proceedings are without jurisdiction. Once jurisdiction is absent, demand could not have been confirmed without rendering adjudication proceedings unfair and illegal and appellate proceedings an empty public relations exercise.

29.5 · Preliminary Ground 2 — Section 65 (Audit)

Section 65 — Chapter XIII — Audit Jurisdiction — Cannot Outsource to Appellant
Section 65 Jurisdiction Limit
Section 65 resides in Chapter XIII
Proper Officer acting under S.65 CANNOT take recourse to "unavailability of documents for verification" when the audit has been concluded by issuance of report under S.65(6) in Form ADT2. Issuance of ADT2 is CONFIRMATION of participation in audit by Appellant who facilitated access to all material.
Ground 1 — Documents Unavailability Cannot Be Excuse
Impugned Order is bad in law — underlying proceedings are entirely void ab initio. PO acting under S.65 (Chapter XIII) CANNOT enter into matters taking recourse to "unavailability of documents for verification" when audit has already been concluded by ADT2. Revenue cannot approbate and reprobate. Failure to carry out audit effectively does NOT shift burden of proof on Appellant and does not support adverse inference by Revenue on matters left open and poorly verified by PO. To raise demands on adverse inference would be tantamount to outsourcing the audit exercise to Appellant — not the mandate of law.
Ground 2 — Plurality of Interpretation
Same as S.61 Ground 2 — where alternate views are sought to be canvassed, burden of proof rests entirely on Revenue. Issuance of ADT2 is confirmation of participation — to support views in SCN requires adducing material evidence. Without new material brought on record, merely raising additional demand would be tantamount to estimation, guesswork and conjecture. Such is not the mandate of law.
Implied Finding — Jurisdiction Absent
SAME AS S.61
By skirting jurisdiction issue, Impugned Order has impliedly found in favour of Appellant that underlying proceedings are without jurisdiction. Once jurisdiction absent, demand could not have been confirmed without rendering adjudication unfair and illegal.

29.6 · Preliminary Ground 3 — Section 67 (Inspection/Search/Seizure)

Section 67 — Chapter XIV — Enforcement Jurisdiction — Cannot Bleed into S.65
Section 67 Jurisdiction Limit
Section 67 resides in Chapter XIV
Proper Officer acting under S.67 CANNOT enter into matters involving "verification of correctness" of self-assessment where issues involve records-based findings requiring verification of contemporaneous documents — that is S.65 (Chapter XIII) jurisdiction.
ALSO
PO under S.67 cannot enter into matters involving "plurality of interpretation" — same as S.61/S.65 argument
Ground 1 — Verification of Correctness
Impugned Order is bad in law — underlying proceedings entirely void ab initio for want of jurisdiction. PO acting under S.67 (Chapter XIV) could NOT enter into matters involving "verification of correctness" of self-assessment — that entails entering into jurisdiction of S.65 (Chapter XIII). Impugned Order has skirted jurisdiction issue — implying finding in Appellant's favour. Demand liable to be set aside in toto.
Ground 2 — Plurality of Interpretation
PO acting under S.67 (Chapter XIV) could NOT enter into matters involving "plurality of interpretation of" applicable statutory provisions where SCN involves alternate interpretation to data on Common Portal — that falls under S.65 (Chapter XIII). Burden of proof entirely on Revenue. Without new material, demand = estimation, guesswork, conjecture.
Ground 3 — Extended Limitation (For All S.74 + S.67 Cases)
Impugned Order is illegal — demand confirmed while failing to establish extended period of limitation without demonstrating superadded requirement of 'evasion of tax', even if any special circumstances somehow admitted. Not every non-payment of tax or claim of inadmissible credit ipsi dixit admits evasion. Evasion requires all 4 elements: (i) non-payment (ii) knowledge (iii) active concealment (iv) gains from misadventure. Empty allegation parroting statutory language defeats Revenue's case.
Ground 4 — Burden of Proof (For All Cases)
Impugned Order is erroneous — burden of proof rests on Revenue to bring home allegations in SCN — not discharged. Impugned Order has glossed over this mandate and proceeded to support SCN's view without a shred of evidence except estimation, guesswork and conjecture. Failure to discharge burden of proof vacates allegations cast on Appellant. Trite law: burden of proof is not only applicable when notice is under extended limitation — applicable in all cases involving assertion of new or different state of affairs to self-assessed position.

29.7 · Preliminary Grounds 4 — Multi-year SCN + Limitation + Burden of Proof

Preliminary Grounds 4 — Structural Defects in SCN/OIO
Ground 1 — Multi-Period SCN
At the outset, Impugned Order is contrary to law — upholds demand based on SCN covering multiple period(s) within the Disputed Tax Period, when multiple judicial authorities concurring have proscribed issuance of multi-year notice of demand. Adjudication is rendered sub silentio and Impugned Order is tainted with illegality and liable to be set aside.
Ground 2 — Section 168A Extension at Naught
Impugned Order is illegal — the extension of limitation under Section 168A has itself been set at naught by jurisdictional High Court. Adjudication is rendered sub silentio and Impugned Order tainted with illegality.
Ground 3 — Section 74 Special Circumstances
Impugned Order is bad in law — confirms demand under Section 74 without establishing 'special circumstances' needed for extended period of limitation. SCN under S.74 is a mischievous and mala fide attempt at procuring unlawful extension of limitation for a demand that cannot but be made under S.73.
Ground 4 — Burden of Proof (Universal)
KEY PRINCIPLE
Trite law: burden of proof is not only applicable when notice is issued under extended period of limitation, but in ALL cases where assertion is made as to existence of new or different state of things
When liability is determined on self-assessment basis, any view contrary to self-assessment requires this burden to be discharged by Revenue by placing unimpeachable material on record, uncovered in original proceedings duly authorized. This burden remains on Revenue and does NOT shift to Appellant. There is no presumption in favour of Revenue under Section 155 when Appellant has claimed credit in self-assessment and credit has reached ECrL. Onus on Appellant arises only AFTER Revenue makes out a prima facie case based on reliable material and puts Appellant at notice to answer third-party data relied upon.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 29 (GSTAT)
These are ready-to-state preliminary grounds and jurisdiction-based arguments for use before GSTAT. State them as the FIRST grounds before entering into merits — they are potentially fatal to the demand ab initio.
Section 61 — Void Ab Initio for Want of Jurisdiction (Verification of Correctness)
Preliminary Ground 1 | Para 29.4 | Section 61 CGST Act (Chapter XII)
"Proper Officer acting under Section 61, which resides in Chapter XII of Central GST Act, could not have entered into matters involving 'verification of correctness' of self-assessment carried out by Appellant, where the issues at large in Impugned SCN involve records-based findings that could be readily redressed by verifying contemporaneous documents — such an exercise would entail entering into the jurisdiction of Proper Officer authorized under Section 65, which resides in Chapter XIII. The underlying proceedings are entirely void ab initio for want of jurisdiction. Once jurisdiction is absent, demand could not have been confirmed without rendering adjudication proceedings unfair and illegal and appellate proceedings an empty public relations exercise. For this reason, the entire demand is liable to be set aside in toto."
When to Use: In all GSTAT appeals where SCN was issued by Scrutiny Officer (ASMT-10/11 proceedings) but the substance of the SCN requires verification of books of account — invoke this ground as a preliminary ground to argue that the very initiation of proceedings was without jurisdiction.
Section 61 — Void Ab Initio for Want of Jurisdiction (Plurality of Interpretation)
Preliminary Ground 1 | Para 29.4 | Section 61 CGST Act (Chapter XII)
"Proper Officer acting under Section 61, which resides in Chapter XII of Central GST Act, could not have entered into matters involving 'plurality of interpretation of' applicable statutory provisions, where the issues at large in Impugned SCN involve alternate interpretation to data already contained in contemporaneous records and returns of Appellant available on Common Portal. Such an exercise would entail entering into the jurisdiction of the Proper Officer authorized under Section 65, which resides in Chapter XIII. The underlying proceedings are entirely void ab initio for want of jurisdiction."
When to Use: When SCN issued by Scrutiny Officer goes beyond data discrepancies and attempts to impose an alternate legal interpretation of transactions — this jurisdictional objection should be the first preliminary ground, before any merits argument.
Section 65 — Audit Cannot Be Outsourced to Appellant
Preliminary Ground 2 | Para 29.5 | Section 65 CGST Act (Chapter XIII)
"Issuance of audit report in Form ADT-2 is confirmation of participation in audit proceedings by Appellant by facilitating access of all material considered relevant by Proper Officer. Failure to carry out audit effectively does not shift burden of proof on Appellant and support adverse inference by Revenue on matters left open and poorly verified by Proper Officer. To raise demands based on adverse inference would be tantamount to outsourcing the audit exercise by Proper Officer to Appellant. Such is not the mandate in law."
When to Use: In all audit-led demands where Revenue issues SCN with observations based on 'documents not provided' or 'further verification pending' even after ADT-2 was issued — use this to establish that ADT-2 marks the completion of the audit and all unsettled matters are the PO's own failure.
Section 67 — Enforcement Cannot Substitute for Audit Jurisdiction
Preliminary Ground 3 | Para 29.6 | Section 67 CGST Act (Chapter XIV)
"Proper Officer acting under Section 67, which resides in Chapter XIV of Central GST Act, could not have entered into matters involving 'verification of correctness' of self-assessment carried out by Appellant, where issues involve records-based findings that could be readily redressed by verifying contemporaneous documents or those procured from counter-Parties. Such an exercise would entail entering into the jurisdiction of the Proper Officer authorized under Section 65, which resides in Chapter XIII. The underlying proceedings are entirely void ab initio for want of jurisdiction."
When to Use: In DGGI/enforcement-led demands where SCN is issued after search/seizure proceedings under Section 67 but the actual demand is based on audit-type verification of records — challenge the jurisdiction at the threshold before entering into merits.
Burden of Proof — Universal Applicability (Not Limited to Extended Limitation)
Preliminary Ground 4 | Para 29.7 | Section 155 CGST Act
"Trite law that burden of proof is not only applicable when notice of demand is issued under extended period of limitation, but in all cases where assertion is made as to the existence of new or different state of things, or relation of things. When liability to tax is determined on self-assessment basis, any view canvassed to the contrary requires this burden be discharged by Revenue. Except by placing unimpeachable material on record, uncovered in original proceedings duly authorized, this burden remains on Revenue and does not shift to Appellant. There is no presumption in favour of Revenue under Section 155 of Central GST Act when Appellant has claimed credit in self-assessment and credit has reached ECrL."
When to Use: As a universal preliminary ground in ALL GSTAT appeals — firmly establish that the burden is on Revenue in every case involving self-assessment, and that Section 155 creates no presumption for Revenue when credit has already flowed into ECrL.
Multi-Year SCN — Sub Silentio Adjudication
Preliminary Ground 4 | Para 29.7
"The Impugned Order upholds demand based on Impugned SCN covering multiple periods within the Disputed Tax Period, when multiple judicial authorities concurring with each other have proscribed issuance of multi-year notice of demand. For this reason, adjudication is rendered sub silentio and the Impugned Order is tainted with illegality and liable to be set aside."
When to Use: In all matters where a single SCN covers multiple financial years — raise this as a preliminary ground citing the judicial authorities proscribing multi-year SCNs, and argue that the entire adjudication stands vitiated sub silentio.
APL-5 Filing — Separate APL-5 for Every OIO
Para 29.1 | GSTAT Procedure | Critical Filing Rule
A separate APL-5 must be filed for every OIO even if multiple OIOs were disposed in a single OIA. The GSTIN/Temporary ID/UIN must be that of the appellant — and separate APL-5 for every GSTIN if same legal name has multiple GSTINs. Respondent(s) must include Commissioner (for Central appeals) — FAA is NEVER a respondent before GSTAT. AR's name must NOT be filled in Field 12 at the time of filing — POA is submitted separately after APL-2A is issued.
When to Use: As a checklist before filing APL-5 at GSTAT — this is a critical procedural safeguard that prevents defects from being raised at Registry stage and avoids rejection of appeal on technical grounds.
Section 112(6) — Maximum Condonable Delay Must Be Pleaded Upfront
Section 112(6) CGST Act | Para 29.3
"The delay must be within the maximum condonable delay under Section 112(6) of Central GST Act counted from the date of communication of said Order as stated in APL5/APL6. The affidavit must first STATE the delay (so there is no dispute as to the extent of delay and such delay being without the power of condonation) and then proceed to offer the explanation — not of 'reasons' but 'explanation' of every day of delay."
When to Use: In every CoD application at GSTAT — ensure the affidavit first states the exact delay in days, confirms it is within the maximum condonable period under Section 112(6), and then provides day-by-day explanation in a timeline table format.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 29 — Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) | Para 29.1–29.7 | Pages 699–718
⚖️ Chapter 29 — GSTAT Grounds of Appeal (Para 29.7–29.21)
PARAS 29.7 — 29.21

GSTAT Grounds of Appeal

Special Grounds · Common Grounds · Mismatch · EWB Penalty · Blocked Credit
Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index — Click to Navigate

29.7 — Sec 74 Limitation (Mala Fide) 29.8 — Special Grounds 1 (Refund SCN) 29.9 — Special Grounds 2 (Sec 61 Scrutiny) 29.10 — Special Grounds 3 (Sec 65 Audit) 29.11 — Special Grounds 4 (Sec 67 Enforcement) 29.12 — Common Grounds 1 29.13 — Common Grounds 2 29.14 — Mismatch: 2A vs 3B 29.15 — Mismatch: R1 vs 3B 29.16 — Mismatch: R7 vs 3B 29.17 — Output Tax on RCB 29.18 — EWB Penalty 1 (129(1)(a)) 29.19 — EWB Penalty 2 (129(1)(b)) 29.20 — EWB Penalty 3 / Sec 130 29.21 — Reversal of Blocked Credit ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

29.7 — Limitation: Section 74 Used as Mala Fide Device

§ 29.7 — Sec 74 SCN = Mala Fide Attempt to Extend Limitation Beyond Sec 73
G1 — Sec 74 = Mischievous Device
FATAL Mala Fide Extension of Limitation SCN under Sec 74 is a mala fide attempt to procure unlawful extension of limitation for a demand that cannot but be made under Sec 73 of the CGST Act.
RELIEF Demand within normal limitation → reduced to that extent. Demand to extent of extended period → liable to be set aside.
G2 — Three Special Circumstances Not Alleged
NOT ESTABLISHED Impugned SCN does not establish which of the three special circumstances is alleged and does not demonstrate existence of necessary ingredients to support demand under extended period.
Demand to extent of extended period → liable to be set aside.
G3 — Evasion of Tax Not Demonstrated
SUPERADDED REQUIREMENT Extended period requires proof of "evasion of tax" even if special circumstances were admitted. Four Ingredients of Evasion:
(i) Non-payment of tax / refund / credit
(ii) Knowledge of liability / inadmissibility
(iii) Active concealment to impair detection
(iv) Gains from this misadventure
PRINCIPLE Empty allegation parroting statutory language defeats Revenue's case. Not every non-payment ipsi dixit admits evasion.
G4 — Sec 75(2) Relief Not Established
RELIEF AVAILABLE Relief under Sec 75(2) read with Circular No. 185/17/2022-GST (27 Dec 2022) has not been established — said relief is liable to be allowed and demand to extent of extended period is liable to be set aside.
G5 — Wrong Provision for Interest Recovery
WRONG PROVISION Recovery of interest on delayed payment of outward tax must be initiated under Sec 79 r/w Rule 142B — NOT under Sec 74.
Sec 75(12) r/w Rule 88B and Rule 142B: where output tax paid before adjudication under Sec 73/74 → further proceedings must be under Sec 75(12). Impugned Order placed reliance on wrong provisions → entire proceedings non est.
G6 — Multiple Periods in Single SCN (Sec 74(4))
SEC 74(4) Demand on same grounds for subsequent tax periods → subject to relief under Sec 74(4). Demand for periods beyond Apr 2020 from First Month of Demand → to be recomputed under Sec 73.

29.8 — Special Grounds 1: Refund SCN — Second Adjudication

§ 29.8 — Refund Application Scrutinized & Sanctioned (RFD6) → Impugned SCN = Impermissible Second Adjudication
G1 — Natural Justice Violated
NJ VIOLATED Adjudication concluded in violation of principles of natural justice → entire demand liable to be set aside.
G2 — Without Jurisdiction
NO JURISDICTION Authority exercised in underlying proceedings is without jurisdiction of law → entire demand liable to be set aside.
G3 — Second Adjudication Impermissible (Core Ground)
POWER OF REVIEW ABSENT Refund application already scrutinized (RFD8) → sanctioned (RFD6). No new information or material available. Issuing SCN = maliciously exercising power of review absent in law.
Proper Officer Empowered Only Under:
Sec 54 — Refund scrutiny
Sec 61 — Return scrutiny
Sec 65 — Audit
Sec 67 — Inquiry
CORRECT ROUTE Departmental appeal u/s 107(3) against RFD6 — but limitation under Sec 107(2) and Sec 107(4) had lapsed. Hence SCN is mischievous.
G4 — PO Not Under Sec 65 / 67
TRESPASS OF JURISDICTION Jurisdictional PO is NOT empowered under Sec 65 or 67 to re-examine correctness of underlying documents submitted at time of RFD6 sanction. Passion to protect Revenue does not permit exercise of non-existent powers.
G5 — Composite Notice Mandatory (Circular 125/44/2019)
PARA 20(b) CIRCULAR 125 Grievance about ITC balance pursuant to refund application → mandatory to address via composite notice of demand under Sec 73/74 as per Para 20(b) of Circular No. 125/44/2019-GST (18 Nov 2019).
VESTED RIGHT Electing NOT to issue composite notice → confers vested right on Appellant: no grievance as to entire ECL balance at time of refund. Reviewing those grounds = amending RFD8 ex post facto after RFD6 → even more impermissible.
G6 — Revenue's Admission: No Mala Fides
ADMISSION Revenue admitted: Appellant engaged in supply of [nature] and inward supplies are admissible ITC not blocked under Sec 17(5)(c). SCN issued under Sec 73 and NOT Sec 74 → affirms absence of mala fides → entire demand liable to be set aside.

29.9 — Special Grounds 2: Section 61 Scrutiny Proceedings

§ 29.9 — Sec 61 Scrutiny: Limited Authority → Cannot Arrive at Demand on Taxability / Valuation / ITC
G1 & G2 — NJ / Jurisdiction
ALWAYS RAISED G1: Adjudication concluded in violation of natural justice → entire demand set aside.

G2: Authority exercised in underlying proceedings without jurisdiction → entire demand set aside.
G3 — Sec 61 PO: Limited Authority Only (Core Ground)
LIMITED POWER PO under Sec 61 r/w Rule 99 has limited authority to examine discrepancies arising from returns only.
CANNOT DETERMINE Taxability · Classification · Exemption · Valuation · Admissibility of ITC — these require verification of documents and records, not available in return scrutiny.
In absence of "discrepancy arising from returns" → PO left to rely on estimation, guesswork and conjecture. Confirming demand on "plausibility" = methodology under Sec 62/63 only — NOT Sec 73/74.
G4 — Non-Availability of Explanation ≠ Suppression
PRINCIPLE Non-availability of extensive documented explanation to ASMT-10 may remotely support suppression — but WITHOUT showing "evasion of tax" → SCN cannot be issued under Sec 74.
MANDATORY SEQUENCE Correct path: PO must set law in motion under Sec 65, 66 or 67 → which may then lead to demand under Sec 73/74 as mandated in Sec 61(3). PO for each proceeding is different — separation of inquiry power from adjudication power is legislative mandate.

29.10 — Special Grounds 3: Section 65 Audit Proceedings

§ 29.10 — Sec 65 Audit Concluded (ADT2 Issued) → Jurisdiction Extinguished by Exercise → SCN Impermissible
G1 & G2 — NJ / Jurisdiction
ALWAYS RAISED G1: Adjudication in violation of natural justice.

G2: Authority in underlying proceedings without jurisdiction.
G3 — Non-Participation Cannot be Basis of Demand
FATAL REASONING Once ADT2 issued → Revenue cannot shelter behind non-participation without a written demand for documentation showing dereliction.
KEY PRINCIPLE Jurisdiction under Sec 65 once invoked → power to audit is extinguished by exercise. ADT2 = evidence of satisfactory conclusion. Assertion of non-participation in underlying proceedings is 'with prejudice' to interests of Revenue.
ALTERNATIVE ROUTE PO was at liberty to withdraw from audit and refer files for enforcement under Sec 67 where non-participation could form "reasons to believe". Evasion still needs to be demonstrated.
G4 — Records-Based Demand; No Evasion Uncovered
CONTRADICTION Entire demand is records-based and no evasion uncovered despite exercising authority under Sec 65 (which concluded with ADT2). SCN under Sec 74 canvasses alternate interpretation to extant facts — no uncovered turnover not already forthcoming in returns, financial statements, contemporaneous records.
G5 — Demand Based on Undisclosed Confidential Inspection Report
CHECK Demand confirmed based on confidential inspection report from Enforcement (Sec 67) to PO (Sec 65) — without disclosure of said report or addressing findings in ADT2.
Proceeding with SCN = operating on "borrowed satisfaction". Impugned Order abdicated responsibility by "acting under dictation" → illegality confirmed. [CHECK IF SCN REFERS TO INSPECTION REPORT FROM ENFORCEMENT]
G6 — Triple Interpretation (Financial Statement)
TRIPLE INTERPRETATION Financial statements → interpretation of underlying commercial transaction.
Appellant's understanding → interpretation of accountant's interpretation.
SCN allegation → interpretation of that interpretation.
Principle: Reliance on triple interpretation to impeach self-assessment is arbitrary and illegal. Demand → liable to be set aside.
G7 — Penalty Against Legal Entity Without Human Agency
LEGALLY IMPOSSIBLE Offences under Sec 122(1) or (2) without the involvement of some human agency is impossible. Enquiry ought to have located the natural persons who conceptualized the misadventure.
CONTRA-INDICATOR Omission to impose penalty under Sec 122(3) = contra-indicator of any mala fides by the legal entity → raises presumption against Revenue's allegations under Sec 74. Even if demand sustainable → relief under Sec 75(2) to be readily allowed.

29.11 — Special Grounds 4: Section 67 Enforcement / Investigation

§ 29.11 — Sec 67 Exceptional Powers: Evasion of Tax as Pre-Condition Not Shown; Cross-Examination Denied
G1 & G2 — NJ / Jurisdiction
ALWAYS RAISED G1: Violation of natural justice.

G2: Underlying proceedings without jurisdiction.
G3 — Evasion Pre-Condition Not Shown (Core)
PRE-CONDITION ABSENT Sec 67 exceptional powers require pre-condition: "evasion of tax" shown to exist based on material on record. Not every claim of ITC about which some doubts are raised ipsi dixit admits evasion.
Four Ingredients of Evasion (same test as §29.7):
(i) Claim of inadmissible credit
(ii) Full knowledge of inadmissibility
(iii) Active concealment to impair detection
(iv) Gains from this misadventure
G4 — Cross-Examination Under Sec 67(10) Denied
DUE PROCESS DENIED Application under Sec 67(10) to Commissioner demanding: (i) disclosure of investigative material, (ii) cross-examination of IO and witnesses — denied up to stage of adjudication.
Impugned Order has parroted allegations in SCN → adjudication = empty public relations exercise. Several defects in SCN merged into Order → demand merits quietus immediately.
G5 — No Mastermind Identified Under Sec 122(1A) / 122(3)
NO EVASION PROVEN No notice issued under Sec 122(1A) or 122(3) → implies investigation has NOT revealed any "mastermind" of the alleged offence. Without any person being identified → investigation only reaches conclusion about evasion by adverse inference, estimation and conjecture.
G6 — Records-Based; Alternate Interpretation; Wrong Jurisdiction
JURISDICTIONAL OVERREACH SCN under Sec 74 canvasses alternate interpretation = undertaking enquiry akin to audit under Sec 65 r/w Sec 2(13). Appellant registered under [State / Central] → no jurisdiction for such audit by other department's officers.
Matters involving interpretation of law (where turnover forthcoming from contemporaneous records) → beyond reach of Sec 67 powers; reserved for audit under Sec 65.

29.12 — Common Grounds 1: Universal Grounds for All Demand Types

§ 29.12 — Common Grounds 1: Applicable Across All Demand Scenarios
G1 — Grounds in Memorandum Not Addressed
NJ + DUE PROCESS No findings reached on grounds raised in memorandum — grounds skirted while confirming demand. Respondents had duty to address all grounds. Impugned Order to be set aside after reaching finding on those grounds (integral part hereof by reference).
G2 — Interest: ITC / Output Tax Disputed → Not Payable
NOT PAYABLE Interest demanded when output tax (on RCM and forward charge) OR underlying ITC reversal demand itself is not payable and disputed ITC is admissible → demand for interest liable to be set aside.
G3, G4, G5 — Penalty Grounds
G3 — DEMAND ERRONEOUS Penalty demanded when demand itself is erroneous, inapplicable and denied → penalty falls.
G4 — NO ANIMUS Required animus not shown to exist. Without animus → penalty = misapplication of law.
G5 — SEC 126 NOT FOLLOWED Penalty without complying with Sec 126 discipline — mandatory examination of mitigating circumstances omitted → demand for penalty liable to be set aside.
G7 — Sec 73 SCN Affirms No Mala Fides; Self-Assessment Must Prevail
KEY ARGUMENT SCN issued under Sec 73 (NOT Sec 74) → affirms absence of any mala fides. Self-assessment carried out free of mala fides cannot be lightly dismissed or unseated just because another interpretation is possible. Unless shown that interpretation in self-assessment is patently wrong → liability determined in self-assessment is liable to be admitted.
G8 — Interest: Wrong Provision (Sec 79 / Rule 142B)
WRONG PROVISION Recovery of interest on delayed payment of outward tax → Sec 79 r/w Rule 142B. Sec 75(12) r/w Rule 88B and Rule 142B: output tax paid before adjudication → further proceedings only under Sec 75(12). Relying on wrong provisions → proceedings non est.
G10 — Tax Collected Not Deposited: No Allegation [Conditional]
CHECK IF APPLICABLE Neither allegation made nor finding reached that "tax is collected but not deposited" — evident in election to issue SCN under Sec 73/74 [DELETE ONE NOT APPLICABLE] and NOT under Sec 76. Imputation of mala fides is incongruous → liable to be set aside.

29.13 — Common Grounds 2: Output Tax / ITC Reversal / Erroneous Refund

§ 29.13 — Issue-Specific Grounds: Three Scenarios
Issue (A) — Demand for Output Tax on ………
A-G1 — TAXING INGREDIENTS ABSENT Demand for OUTPUT TAX confirmed when ingredients to attract incidence of output tax are consistently absent. Without incidence of tax → inconsistency in documentation or procedural compliance cannot attract liability.
A-G2 — GROUNDS SKIRTED No findings on grounds raised in memorandum → violates natural justice → set aside after finding.
Revenue was enjoined with duty to demonstrate existence of taxing ingredients. Omission to reach a finding renders Impugned Order illegal for failure of purpose of appellate proceedings.
Issue (B) — Demand for Reversal of ITC on ………
B-G1 — VESTING CONDITIONS SATISFIED Demand for ITC reversal confirmed when none of the vesting conditions in Sec 16, 17, 18 have been shown to be violated. ITC is a cause-of-action vested and indefeasible right — except to show cause-of-action in Sec 16/17/18 → no occasion to superimpose extra-legislative considerations.
B-G2 — GROUNDS SKIRTED No findings on grounds raised in memorandum → liable to be set aside after finding.
Issue (B Alt) — Demand for Repayment of Erroneous Refund on ………
BR-G1 — 'ERROR' NOT DEMONSTRATED Demand for ERRONEOUS REFUND confirmed when ingredients to demonstrate the 'error' in refund sanctioned are not shown. When refund sanctioned → mandatory course: departmental appeal u/s 107(3) OR revision u/s 108(1).
SCN = perverse back-door attempt to unseat affirmative finding in favour of Appellant. No new material uncovered. Plurality of interpretation cannot offer yet another opportunity to unsettle issues given quietus with passing of limitation.

29.14 — Mismatch: GSTR-2A vs GSTR-3B

§ 29.14 — Mismatch GSTR-2A with GSTR-3B: Linear Comparison Without Reference to Deferred Time / Extended Period = Illegal
G1 — Deferred Time (Sec 37(3)) & Extended Time (Sec 16(4)) Ignored
CONTRARY TO LAW Demand based on data mismatch between GSTR-2A and GSTR-3B by linear comparison WITHOUT reference to:
Deferred time under Sec 37(3) for suppliers to file GSTR-1
Extended time under Sec 16(4) to claim credit in GSTR-3B
G2 — All Vesting Conditions Satisfied
FACTS Appellant availed ITC after satisfying all vesting conditions in Sec 16, 17, 18. Without demonstrating non-compliance with any vesting condition → adverse inference based only on data mismatch between GSTR-2A and GSTR-3B is inadmissible.
G3 — Sec 17(2) / 17(5) Inadmissible Credits Improperly Included
DEFECTIVE EXERCISE Impugned Order allowed credits inadmissible under Sec 17(2) or Sec 17(5) by treating them as admissible in the linear comparison exercise. Erroneous comparison cannot authorize inadmissible credit. Entire exercise of linear comparison → defective, illegal and erroneous.
G4 — Burden of Proof Not Discharged (Sec 155)
SEC 155 CGST ACT Demand confirmed without satisfactory discharge of burden of proof by Revenue beyond that mandated for Appellant under Sec 155. Asserting data mismatch from Common Portal = demand based on adverse inference → contrary to law and facts.

29.15 — Mismatch: GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B

§ 29.15 — Mismatch GSTR-R1 with GSTR-3B: Output Tax Discharged in Self-Assessment; Selective Use of Returns Illegal
G1 — Sec 37(3) Deferred Time + Extant Circular Disaffirms Liability
CONTRARY TO LAW Demand based on mismatch between GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B WITHOUT reference to deferred time in Sec 37(3) to file GSTR-1. Read with extant circular that disaffirms liability to output tax based on such data mismatch.
G2 — Output Tax Discharged; No Arrears
FACTS Appellant discharged output tax on self-assessment → no liability fallen in arrears. Without demonstrating taxing ingredients → demand cannot be confirmed on adverse inference. No presumption can be raised based on data reported in impugned GSTR-1 when entire difference is disputed by Appellant as unreliable and erroneous.
G3 — Selective Reliance on GSTR-1 While Rejecting GSTR-3B
SELECTIVE & DEFECTIVE Implied reliance on data in GSTR-1 (Sec 37) but selective rejection of data in GSTR-3B (Sec 39). Erroneous comparison cannot authorize non-existent liability. Entire exercise → defective, illegal and erroneous.
G4 — Burden of Proof Not Discharged (Sec 155)
SEC 155 CGST ACT Demand confirmed without satisfactory discharge of burden of proof by Revenue. Asserting data mismatch = demand based on adverse inference → contrary to law and facts.
