G5 (WP) — Sec 77 CGST / Sec 19 IGST: Admittedly Erroneous Data
WITHOUT PREJUDICE Impugned Order contrary to Sec 77 CGST Act and Sec 19 IGST Act — relying on assumption in admittedly erroneous data in GSTR-1, while disaffirming liability by limiting to GSTR-3B under Sec 39.

29.16 — Mismatch: GSTR-7 vs GSTR-3B (TDS)

§ 29.16 — Mismatch GSTR-7 with GSTR-3B: Third-Party TDS Data Cannot Override Appellant's Self-Assessment
G1 — Third-Party Delay in GSTR-7 Ignored
CONTRARY TO LAW Demand based on mismatch between GSTR-7 and GSTR-3B WITHOUT reference to delay or omission to file returns in respect of tax deduction under Sec 51 and file GSTR-7 by third-parties. Extant circular disaffirms liability to output tax based on such mismatch.
G2 — No Tax Arrears; Taxing Ingredients Absent
FACTS Appellant discharged output tax on self-assessment → no liability fallen in arrears. No presumption can be raised based on data reported by third-parties in GSTR-7 when entire tax deducted is disputed by Appellant as unreliable and erroneous.
G3 — Selective Reliance on GSTR-7 While Rejecting GSTR-3B
SELECTIVE & DEFECTIVE Implied reliance on GSTR-7 (Sec 51) but selective rejection of GSTR-3B (Sec 39) → erroneous comparison cannot authorize non-existent liability. Entire exercise → defective, illegal and erroneous.
G4 — Burden of Proof Not Discharged (Sec 155)
ADVERSE INFERENCE INSUFFICIENT Demand confirmed without satisfactory discharge of burden of proof by Revenue → demand based on adverse inference → contrary to law and facts.

29.17 — Output Tax on Reverse Charge Basis (RCM)

§ 29.17 — RCM Demand: Linear Comparison of Expenditure in Contemporaneous Records vs GSTR-3B Without Reference to RCM Already Paid = Illegal
G1 — Reference to RCM Already Paid and ITC Thereon Ignored
CONTRARY TO LAW Demand based on linear comparison of expenditure in contemporaneous records WITHOUT reference to output tax on RCB already discharged AND ITC thereon in same tax period — permitted in law read with extant instructions.
G2 — RCM Discharged; ECL Debits Exceed Demand
EVIDENCE ON RECORD Appellant discharged output tax on RCB on self-assessment → no liability fallen in arrears. Debits in Electronic Cash Ledger during Disputed Tax Period exceed demand in Impugned SCN. Impugned Order failed to record a finding to the contrary to impeach self-assessment.
G3 — Selective Reliance on Contemporaneous Records; Rejecting GSTR-3B
SELECTIVE & DEFECTIVE Implied reliance on contemporaneous records in SCN but selective rejection of GSTR-3B (Sec 39) → erroneous comparison cannot authorize non-existent liability. Entire exercise → defective, illegal and erroneous.
G4 — Burden of Proof Not Discharged (Sec 155)
ADVERSE INFERENCE INSUFFICIENT Demand confirmed without satisfactory discharge of burden by Revenue → demand based on adverse inference → contrary to law and facts.
G5 (WP) — Sec 77 CGST / Sec 19 IGST
WITHOUT PREJUDICE Impugned Order contrary to Sec 77 CGST Act and Sec 19 IGST Act — relying on admittedly erroneous contemporaneous records and disaffirming by limiting liability to GSTR-3B.

29.18 — EWB Penalty 1: Section 129(1)(a) — Owner Elects to Pay

§ 29.18 — EWB Penalty 1: Bona Fide Movement Shown → Maximum Penalty Under Sec 129(1)(a) Not Justified; Sec 126 Not Followed
G1 — Bona Fide Movement; Rule 138A Satisfied
BONA FIDE MOVEMENT Extant facts affirm bona fide movement of consignment and conveyance referred to in MOV6. Where requirements of Rule 138A are satisfied → imposition of maximum penalty is not justified.
Appellant relies upon extant circular proscribing maximum penalty in absence of unassailable mala fides affirmed at point of interception. Entire demand → contrary to law.
G2 — No Unnatural Exercise; No Resistance by Carrier
NOT MALA FIDE No unnatural exercise during interception of conveyance by PO. No resistance by independent carrier in ensuing inquiry. Without demonstrating mala fides → confirming maximum penalty contrary to extant Board circulars. Routinely confirming maximum penalty = erroneous.
G3 — Sec 126 Discipline Not Followed
SEC 126 VIOLATED Maximum penalty confirmed for bona fide discrepancies without examining contemporaneous records evidencing mitigating circumstances. Nominal penalty warranted → runs counter to discipline under Sec 126 → entire demand contrary to law and facts.

29.19 — EWB Penalty 2: Section 129(1)(b) — Third Party / No Tax Outstanding

§ 29.19 — EWB Penalty 2: Bona Fide Movement; Law Disaffirms Ingredients for Maximum Penalty Under Sec 129(1)(b)
G1 — Bona Fide Movement; Rule 138A Satisfied; Law Disaffirms
BONA FIDE MOVEMENT Bona fide movement affirmed by facts. Where requirements of Rule 138A satisfied → maximum penalty not justified. Law disaffirms ingredients needed to attract maximum penalty at point of interception.
G2 — No Unnatural Exercise; No Resistance
NOT MALA FIDE No unnatural exercise during interception; no resistance by independent carrier. Without mala fides → maximum penalty contrary to Board circulars. Routinely confirming maximum penalty = erroneous.
G3 — Sec 126 Discipline Not Followed
SEC 126 VIOLATED Maximum penalty for bona fide discrepancy without examining mitigating circumstances → nominal penalty runs counter to Sec 126 discipline → liable to be set aside.

29.20 — EWB Penalty 3: Confiscation Under Section 130

§ 29.20 — Sec 130 Confiscation: No Lawful Seizure; Sec 129 Abandoned; Prior Authorization Absent; Sec 122 Penalty Forfeited
G1 — Confiscation Without Lawful Seizure; MOV7 Omitted
TRITE LAW Confiscation without lawful seizure = impermissible. Demand for redemption fine without express election by taxpayer = barred in law.
Seizure in MOV6 under Sec 129 stands vacated when MOV7 was omitted to be issued by PO. Redemption fine cannot be foisted without being requested by taxpayer.
G2 — Migration from Sec 129 to Sec 130 = Impermissible
ABANDONMENT WITH PREJUDICE Migration to Sec 130 after interception under Sec 129 — abandonment of proceedings under Sec 129 is 'with prejudice' to proceedings under Sec 130 → entire demand contrary to law.
G3 — Sec 130(1) Ingredients Not Shown; Prior Authorization Absent
NO JURISDICTION None of the ingredients of Sec 130(1) shown to exist. Jurisdiction under Sec 67(1) or 67(2) involving "evasion of tax" → circumscribed by prior authorization from Officer not below rank of Joint Commissioner.
No such authorization placed on record → entire proceedings under Sec 130 tainted with illegality. Without jurisdiction → imputing mala fides falls short of exacting standards → confirming maximum fine is erroneous, arbitrary and illegal.
G4 — Redemption Fine: Revaluation of Consignment Beyond Authority
BEYOND AUTHORITY Redemption fine under Sec 130(2) by undertaking revaluation of consignment → beyond authority vested in Sec 68 r/w Sec 129 (the only provision under which original authority is designated as PO) → entire demand contrary to law.
G5 — Penalty Under Sec 122: Forfeited; Sub-Section Not Specified
PENALTY FORFEITED When proceedings under Sec 68 r/w Sec 129 stood abandoned → power to impose penalty under Sec 122 also forfeited. When ingredients of greater offence alleged → ingredients of lesser offence stand subsumed.
AMBIGUITY = ILLEGAL Penalty confirmed under Sec 122 without specifying sub-section and clause (whose ingredients vary greatly) → ambiguity is the anti-thesis of fairness. Maximum penalty for bona fide discrepancy without examining mitigating circumstances → counter to Sec 126 → liable to be set aside.

29.21 — Reversal of Blocked Credit: Section 17(2) — Exempt Supplies

§ 29.21 — Sec 17(2) Reversal Demand Based on Linear Comparison of 'Exempt Supplies' Without Reference to ITC Already Discharged = Illegal
G1 — ITC Already Discharged / Availed Net Ignored
CONTRARY TO LAW Demand for reversal of ITC under Sec 17(2) based on linear comparison of 'exempt supplies' in contemporaneous records WITHOUT reference to ITC to the extent already discharged and availed net of inadmissible credit for the rest — observed from Common Portal and claimed in GSTR-3B.
G2 — All Vesting Conditions Satisfied; No Non-Compliance Shown
FACTS Appellant availed ITC after satisfying all vesting conditions in Sec 16, 17, 18. Without demonstrating non-compliance with any vesting condition → adverse inference reached about inadmissibility based only on 'exempt supplies' in contemporaneous records → inadmissible.
G3 — ITC Claimed on Self-Assessment; No Reversal Liability in Arrears
FACTS Appellant claimed ITC on self-assessment → no liability for reversal of inadmissible credits fallen in arrears for Disputed Tax Period. Without demonstrating taxing ingredients → demand cannot be confirmed on adverse inference.

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Paras 29.7 to 29.21

These ratios, principles and Latin maxims are distilled from Paras 29.7–29.21. State them confidently at the personal hearing, cite the section and principle, and link them to the precise facts of your client's case.

⚖️ Key Legal Principles & Ready-to-State Ratios

Principle 1 — Section 74 as Mala Fide Device to Extend Limitation
Para 29.7 | Section 73, 74 CGST Act, 2017
"Impugned SCN under Section 74 is a mala fide attempt at procuring unlawful extension of limitation for a demand that cannot but be made under Section 73 of the Central GST Act. As such and without prejudice, the demand to the extent of extended period of limitation is liable to be set aside."
🎯 Use When: Revenue uses Sec 74 solely to get 5-year limitation without pleading or proving the three special circumstances — argues evasion by mere assertion.
Principle 2 — Four-Ingredient Test for Evasion of Tax (Universal)
Para 29.7, 29.10, 29.11 | Section 74 / Section 67 CGST Act — All Extended Period Demands
"Evasion of tax requires: (i) non-payment of tax or refund or credit; (ii) knowledge of such liability or inadmissibility; (iii) active concealment of information or record to impair prompt detection; and (iv) gains from this misadventure. Without all of these ingredients, empty allegation parroting statutory language defeats Revenue's case."
🎯 Use When: Countering any extended period demand or Sec 67 SCN where Revenue has merely alleged suppression without proving all four ingredients of evasion of tax.
Principle 3 — Section 75(2) Relief + Circular 185/17/2022-GST
Para 29.7 | Section 75(2) CGST Act | Circular No. 185/17/2022-GST, dated 27 December 2022
"The relief admissible under Section 75(2) of the Central GST Act read with Circular No. 185/17/2022-GST, dated 27 December 2022 has not been established and for this reason, the said relief is liable to be allowed and the demand to the extent of extended period of limitation is liable to be set aside."
🎯 Use When: In any Section 74 demand where Appellant can argue there was no suppression or intent — seek mandatory relief of treating demand as Section 73 demand even at appellate stage.
Principle 4 — Power of Review Absent in Law; Departmental Appeal Was Correct Remedy (Section 107)
Para 29.8 | Section 107(2), (3), (4) CGST Act — Limitation on Departmental Appeal
"Having realized the lapse of limitation under Section 107(2) and even passed the condonable period of limitation under Section 107(4) of the Central GST Act, the Proper Officer has maliciously issued the Impugned SCN and then proceeded to confirm the demand — which tantamounts to exercising power of review that is absent in law."
🎯 Use When: Revenue issued SCN to effectively reverse a refund sanction instead of filing a departmental appeal in time. Power of review is absent in law for the original PO.
Principle 5 — Composite Notice Mandatory; Election Not to Issue = Vested Right (Circular 125/44/2019, Para 20(b))
Para 29.8 | Para 20(b), Circular No. 125/44/2019-GST, dated 18 November 2019
"When jurisdictional Proper Officer elected NOT to issue composite notice of demand, it confers a vested right on Appellant that there is no grievance to Revenue as to the entire balance of Electronic Credit Ledger at the time of claiming refund. To review those grounds tantamount to amending the said RFD8 ex post facto, after passing orders in RFD6. This is even more impermissible in law."
🎯 Use When: Revenue disputes ITC balance in a refund application through a fresh SCN instead of a composite notice at time of refund processing.
Principle 6 — Section 61 PO: Limited Authority Only; Cannot Arrive at Taxability / ITC Demand
Para 29.9 | Section 61 r/w Rule 99, CGST Act and CGST Rules
"Jurisdictional Proper Officer vested with limited authority to undertake scrutiny of returns under Section 61 of the Central GST Act read with Rule 99 of the Central GST Rules could not have arrived at the demand in Impugned SCN on taxability, classification and exemption, valuation and admissibility of input tax credit — which requires verification of documents and records to be satisfied with self-assessment carried out by Appellant. In the absence of any 'discrepancy arising from returns', jurisdiction Proper Officer is left to only rely on estimation, guesswork and conjecture."
🎯 Use When: Adjudication arose out of Sec 61 scrutiny notice (ASMT-10) and resulted in demand on complex issues beyond return discrepancy — challenge jurisdiction at its root.
Principle 7 — ADT2 Issued = Audit Power Extinguished by Exercise; Non-Participation Cannot Support Demand
Para 29.10 | Section 65 CGST Act — Audit Jurisdiction
"Once jurisdiction under Section 65 is invoked, power to audit is extinguished by exercise. And ADT2 issued is evidence of satisfactory conclusion of proceedings initiated. For this reason, assertion of non-participation in underlying proceedings is 'with prejudice' to interests of Revenue. The Impugned Order on the said premise renders the demand unsustainable in law."
🎯 Use When: Revenue's SCN is based on Sec 65 audit that had already concluded with ADT2 — argue that issuance of fresh SCN = impermissible second adjudication.
Principle 8 — Triple Interpretation Doctrine: Demand Based on Financial Statements = Arbitrary
Para 29.10, 29.11 | Self-Assessment under Section 59; Financial Statements
"Information contained in financial statements are themselves an interpretation of the financial effect of underlying commercial transactions. Appellant's understanding of the disclosure in financial statements is itself an interpretation of the accountant's interpretation of the underlying commercial transaction. Allegation in Impugned SCN about impermissibility of tax treatment in self-assessment is a further conjecture — yet another interpretation. Reliance on such triple interpretation is arbitrary and illegal."
🎯 Use When: SCN or OIO is based solely on accounting entries in financial statements without any independent evidence of tax liability — triple interpretation argument shuts down the case.
Principle 9 — No Mastermind Identified in Sec 67 Investigation = No Evasion; Demand = Guesswork
Para 29.11 | Section 67, 122(1A), 122(3) CGST Act
"No notice is issued under Section 122(1A) or 122(3) of the Central GST Act implying that the investigation has not revealed any 'mastermind' of the alleged offence(s) involving evasion of tax. Without any person being identified to have orchestrated this misadventure, the investigation only reaches a conclusion about evasion of tax by adverse inference based on estimation, guesswork and conjecture. For this reason, the entire demand is baseless and unsubstantiated and liable to be set aside in toto."
🎯 Use When: Sec 67 investigation resulted in SCN under Sec 74 without identifying any natural person as mastermind — proves evasion allegation is legally hollow.
Principle 10 — Section 73 SCN Affirms No Mala Fides; Self-Assessment Must Prevail
Para 29.12 | Section 73, 59 CGST Act — Self-Assessment
"The entire proceedings affirm the absence of any mala fides on the part of Appellant. Free of mala fides, self-assessment carried out cannot be lightly dismissed or unseated just because yet another interpretation is possible to the extant facts. Unless it is shown that the interpretation adopted in self-assessment is patently wrong, liability determined in self-assessment is liable to be admitted and this demand is liable to be set aside."
🎯 Use When: In any Section 73 demand — the very use of Sec 73 (not Sec 74) is Revenue's own admission that there is no evasion, and self-assessment must therefore prevail.
Principle 11 — Mismatch Demands: Deferred Time (Sec 37(3)) and Extended Time (Sec 16(4)) Must Be Reference Point
Para 29.14, 29.15 | Section 16(4), 37(3) CGST Act
"Impugned Order is contrary to law in so far as it has confirmed demand based on data mismatch between GSTR-2A and GSTR-3B based on linear comparison of records obtained from Common Portal during Disputed Tax Period without reference to deferred time allowed in Section 37(3) to file GSTR-1 by respective suppliers and extended time allowed in Section 16(4) of the Central GST Act to claim credit in GSTR-3B."
🎯 Use When: ITC mismatch-based demands where Revenue has done a linear comparison without accounting for permissible filing delays under law — entire exercise is legally defective.
Principle 12 — Burden of Proof Under Section 155: Adverse Inference is Insufficient (All Mismatch Demands)
Para 29.14, 29.15, 29.16, 29.17 | Section 155 CGST Act
"Impugned Order is erroneous in so far as it has confirmed demand without examining satisfactory discharge of burden of proof by Revenue, beyond that mandated for Appellant to discharge under Section 155 of the Central GST Act, by asserting that data mismatch based on linear comparison of records obtained from Common Portal during Disputed Tax Period is sufficient to support this demand which is based on adverse inference."
🎯 Use When: Any mismatch-based demand (2A vs 3B, R1 vs 3B, R7 vs 3B, RCM) where Revenue has merely pointed to a number difference without proving the actual tax liability — invoke Sec 155 burden of proof.
Principle 13 — Maximum EWB Penalty Not Justified Where Bona Fide Movement Shown (Rule 138A + Sec 126)
Para 29.18, 29.19 | Section 126, 129(1)(a)/(b) CGST Act | Rule 138A CGST Rules
"It is trite law that where requirements of Rule 138A of Central GST Rules are satisfied, imposition of maximum penalty is not justified. Without demonstrating mala fides, confirming maximum penalty is contrary to extant Board circulars. Omission to examine extant circumstances runs counter to the mandate in extant Board circulars. Routinely confirming maximum penalty imposed is erroneous."
🎯 Use When: E-Way Bill interception matters where goods, documents and vehicle were regular — challenge maximum penalty citing Sec 126 discipline and Board circulars requiring bona fide assessment.
Principle 14 — Confiscation Without Lawful Seizure + MOV7 Omission = Vacated Seizure (Section 130)
Para 29.20 | Section 129, 130, 68 CGST Act — E-Way Bill Enforcement
"It is trite law that confiscation without lawful seizure is impermissible and demand for redemption fine without express election to discharge fine in-lieu by taxpayer is barred in law. Seizure in MOV6 under Section 129 of Central GST Act stands vacated when MOV7 was omitted to be issued by Proper Officer. Redemption fine cannot be foisted without the same being requested by taxpayer."
🎯 Use When: Revenue jumped from Sec 129 interception directly to Sec 130 confiscation without issuing MOV7 — seizure itself has lapsed in law; confiscation and redemption fine are impermissible.
Principle 15 — Penalty Against Legal Entity Requires Human Agency; Omission of Sec 122(3) is Contra-Indicator
Para 29.10, 29.11 | Section 122(1), (2), (3) CGST Act
"Offences as specific and purposeful as described in Section 122(1) or (2) of Central GST Act without the involvement of some human agency is impossible. As such, enquiry undertaken ought to have located specifically one or more natural persons who could have provided the human agency. Omission to impose penalty under Section 122(3) of Central GST Act is a contra-indicator of any mala fides by the legal entity and raises presumption against Revenue's allegations to support the demand under Section 74."
🎯 Use When: Penalty is demanded on a firm/company without identifying any individual responsible — demand for penalty under Sec 74 is legally untenable. Even if demand sustainable, Sec 75(2) relief is mandatory.

🔤 Latin Maxims — Paras 29.7 to 29.21

Mala Fides
Meaning: "Bad faith" or wrongful intent. Used extensively in Sec 74 demands — the entire invocation of Sec 74 over Sec 73 must be backed by proven mala fides of the taxpayer. Empty assertion does not suffice.
Use in Hearing: "The issuance of Impugned SCN under Section 74 is a mala fide attempt at procuring unlawful extension of limitation. It is trite law that mala fides must be demonstrated with material on record — not merely asserted by parroting statutory language. In the absence of such demonstration, the demand to the extent of extended period is liable to be set aside."
Ipsi Dixit
Meaning: "He himself said it" — an unproven assertion asserted by the speaker alone, without supporting evidence. Not every non-payment of tax or claim of doubtful credit ipsi dixit admits evasion of tax.
Use in Hearing: "It is the consistent position that not every claim of input tax credit about which some doubts are raised ipsi dixit admits evasion of tax. The four ingredients of evasion must be independently satisfied before extended period can be invoked. Bare assertion ipsi dixit is insufficient."
Non Est
Meaning: "It does not exist" — has no legal existence or validity. Used when entire proceedings are void due to wrong provision or absence of jurisdiction. The Order is declared non est — as if it never happened.
Use in Hearing: "The Impugned Order has confirmed reliance on wrong provisions of law for recovery of interest — relying on Section 74 when the correct provision is Section 79 read with Rule 142B. Placing reliance on wrong provisions renders the entire proceedings non est and liable to be set aside in the interest of justice."
Ex Post Facto
Meaning: "After the fact" — an action or rule that retroactively changes the consequences of a prior act. Reviewing grounds of a refund rejection that were not raised in the composite notice amounts to amending the original proceeding ex post facto.
Use in Hearing: "To review those grounds tantamounts to amending the said RFD8 ex post facto, after passing orders in RFD6 under Section 54. This is even more impermissible in law — no officer has the power to rewrite a concluded proceeding ex post facto by the device of a fresh SCN."
Borrowed Satisfaction
Meaning: Acting on the conclusion or satisfaction of another officer without forming independent satisfaction. Impugned Order based on confidential inspection report not disclosed to Appellant = operating on borrowed satisfaction of Enforcement, not the adjudicating officer's own finding.
Use in Hearing: "The Impugned Order has operated on 'borrowed satisfaction' from the inspection report of Enforcement without any finding on the validity and sufficiency of jurisdiction exercised — thereby abdicating its responsibility in law by 'acting under dictation'. Demand confirmed on borrowed satisfaction is illegal and non est."
Quietus
Meaning: "A final discharge or settlement" — the case receives quietus when it is finally and completely disposed of. Demand merits quietus when all defects in SCN are merged into the Order and no further life can be given to the proceeding.
Use in Hearing: "The several defects in Impugned SCN have merged into the Impugned Order bearing tainting the entire demand — which merits to be given quietus immediately. This Tribunal is requested to put a final end to these proceedings which are vitiated from their inception."
In Toto
Meaning: "As a whole" or "entirely" — the demand is liable to be set aside in toto where no part of the proceedings can be severed as valid. Used in Sec 67 demands where no evasion has been uncovered.
Use in Hearing: "Since the entire demand is baseless and unsubstantiated — with no mastermind identified, no evasion demonstrated, and no valid jurisdiction exercised under Section 67 — the demand is liable to be set aside in toto. No part of this demand can survive severance as the root jurisdiction itself is absent."
Mutatis Mutandis
Meaning: "With the necessary changes having been made" — the same principle applies to a different but analogous situation with appropriate modifications. Principles applicable to 2A vs 3B mismatch apply mutatis mutandis to R1 vs 3B and R7 vs 3B mismatches.
Use in Hearing: "The principles applicable to the illegality of GSTR-2A vs GSTR-3B linear comparison apply mutatis mutandis to the GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B and GSTR-7 vs GSTR-3B mismatch demands pressed in the Impugned SCN. In all cases, burden of proof under Section 155 remains undischarged by Revenue."
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Paras 29.7–29.21 — GSTAT Grounds of Appeal
⚖️ Chapter 29 — GSTAT Procedure Rules (Para 29.22–29.25 & Ch 24)
PARAS 29.22 — 29.25 & CHAPTER 24

GSTAT Grounds & Procedure Rules

Blocked Credit · Non-Genuine Suppliers · Abetment Penalty · General Penalty · GSTAT Procedure Rules 2025
Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Index — Click to Navigate

29.22 — Reversal of Blocked Credit 2 (Sec 17(5)) 29.23 — Reversal: Non-Genuine Suppliers 29.24 — Penalty for Abetment (Sec 122(3)) 29.25 — General Penalty (Sec 125/127) 24.1 — GSTAT Procedure Rules: Introduction 24.2 — Powers of Appellate Tribunal 24.3 — Instituting Appeal (Form APL5) 24.4 — Registry 24.5 — Hearing Appeals 24.6 — Case Management 24.7 — Forms & Registers 24.8 — Records & Inspection 24.9 — Authorized Representative 24.10 — Code of Civil Procedure 24.11 — Decisions & Orders 24.12 — E-Filing 24.13 — Miscellaneous & Fees ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

29.22 — Reversal of Blocked Credit 2: Section 17(5) — Expenditure-Based Demand

§ 29.22 — Sec 17(5) ITC Reversal: Linear Comparison of Expenditure in Contemporaneous Records vs GSTR-3B = Illegal; Rule 36(4)(b) Notified Only from 1 Oct 2022
G1 — Reference to ITC Already Discharged and Availed Net Ignored
CONTRARY TO LAW Demand for reversal of ITC blocked under Sec 17(5) based on linear comparison of EXPENDITURE/[REPLACE AS APPROPRIATE] in contemporaneous records WITHOUT reference to ITC to the extent already discharged and availed net of inadmissible credit for the rest — observed from Common Portal during Disputed Tax Period and claimed in GSTR-3B.
G2 — All Vesting Conditions Satisfied; No Non-Compliance Demonstrated
FACTS Appellant availed ITC after satisfying all vesting conditions in Sec 16, 17, 18 of CGST Act. Without demonstrating non-compliance with any vesting condition → adverse inference reached about inadmissibility merely based on EXPENDITURE/[REPLACE AS APPROPRIATE] in contemporaneous records during Disputed Tax Period.
No presumption can be raised based on data albeit reported as EXPENDITURE/[REPLACE AS APPROPRIATE] when entire demand is disputed by Appellant as unreliable and erroneous → entire demand contrary to facts.
G3 — No Reversal Liability; ITC Claimed on Self-Assessment
FACTS Appellant claimed ITC on self-assessment → no liability for reversal of inadmissible credits / blocked credits that has fallen in arrears for Disputed Tax Period. Without demonstrating cause-of-action directly from clause (a) to clause (i) of Sec 17(5) or impliedly under tariff conditions → demand cannot be confirmed on adverse inference.
G4 — Burden of Proof Not Discharged (Sec 155)
SEC 155 CGST ACT Demand confirmed without examining satisfactory discharge of burden of proof by Appellant to the extent mandated in Sec 155 by asserting that comparison of EXPENDITURE/[REPLACE AS APPROPRIATE] in contemporaneous records is sufficient to support this demand — which is based on adverse inference → entire demand contrary to law and facts.
G5 — Rule 36(4)(b) Notified Only from 1 Oct 2022
IMPORTANT DATE Mandate to claim entire ITC and reverse inadmissible portion thereof came to be mandated only when Rule 36(4)(b) of CGST Rules was notified from 1 Oct 2022. Without any evidence to support the premise that inadmissible credit has, in fact, been availed by Appellant during Disputed Tax Period → demand for reversal under Sec 17(2) would be presumptuous.

29.23 — Reversal of Credit from Non-Genuine Suppliers

§ 29.23 — Reversal of ITC from Non-Genuine Suppliers: Confidential Intelligence Without Notice; Double Recovery; Output Tax Admitted = Demand Unsustainable
G1 — Confidential Intelligence Relied Upon Without Notice to Appellant
NJ VIOLATED Demand for reversal of ITC based on confidential intelligence relied upon without putting Appellant at notice that certain supplies are in default with/without discharge of output tax ('alleged suppliers-in-default') on supplies made to Appellant during Disputed Tax Period.
BONA FIDE SUPPLIES Notwithstanding — impugned transactions are/are not reflected in GSTR2A during Disputed Tax Period. Appellant's position: all inward supplies are bona fide with underlying supplies delivered to Appellant as permitted in law along with contemporaneous records in support.
G2 — All Vesting Conditions Satisfied; Adverse Inference Violates NJ
VESTING CONDITIONS MET Appellant availed ITC after satisfying all vesting conditions in Sec 16, 17, 18. Without demonstrating non-compliance with any vesting condition → adverse inference based on inadmissibility of said credit based on confidential intelligence is violative of principles of natural justice.
G3 — Confidential Intelligence: No Finding on Quality and Reliability
EMPTY DEMAND Impugned Order failed to reach a finding on the quality and reliability of said confidential intelligence said to have been acted upon in issuing Impugned SCN. Material withheld from Appellant becomes inadmissible in law. Without said material → Impugned Order is rendered an empty demand.
Entire exercise is illegal and devoid of merit → defective, illegal and erroneous → liable to be set aside for being contrary to law and facts.
G4 — Double Recovery Contrary to Law: Sec 93 Exceptional Powers
DOUBLE RECOVERY IMPERMISSIBLE Without exhausting remedies available in law → demand for reversal of ITC on Appellant is contrary to avowed scheme of this law. With exceptional powers under Sec 93 of CGST Act, successful recovery from alleged suppliers-in-default as well as from Appellant will result in double recovery which is contrary to law.
G5 — Output Tax Admitted as Valid and Proper; Appropriation of Output Tax Renders Demand Unsustainable
INTERNAL INCONSISTENCY Impugned Order affirmed OUTPUT TAX DISCHARGED by Appellant during Disputed Tax Period is valid and proper, while disaffirming the bona fides of inward supplies therefor.
UNSUSTAINABLE Without any finding to the contrary → appropriation of output tax effected out of impugned inward supplies renders the demand unsustainable on law and facts.
G6 — Burden of Proof Under Sec 155: Mere Assertion of Default Not Sufficient
SEC 155 CGST ACT Demand confirmed without satisfactory discharge of burden of proof by Revenue, beyond that mandated for Appellant under Sec 155 CGST Act. Asserting supplies to be in default based on impeached intelligence falls short to support this demand → demand based on conjecture → liable to be set aside for being contrary to law and facts.

29.24 — Penalty for Abetment: Section 122(3)

§ 29.24 — Penalty for Abetment Under Sec 122(3): Intelligence of Doubtful Probative Value; Extensive Cooperation = Incompatible with Abetment; Animus Absent
G1 — Demand Confirmed on Intelligence of Doubtful Probative Value; No Cross-Examination
INADMISSIBLE Demand for output tax confirmed against Firm/Company acting on intelligence which has been assailed for lack of probative value and statements of various persons procured under threat and inducement.
DUE PROCESS DENIED Without a valid notice of demand addressed to Appellant AND without opportunity of cross-examination of third-party witnesses and Investigating Officer → averments relied upon are inadmissible. Without said averments → conclusions in Impugned SCN are merely suspicion and adverse inference. Notice of demand under Sec 74 cannot be issued based on borrowed satisfaction.
G2 — Burden on Revenue: Extensive Cooperation = Incompatible with Abetment Ingredients
EXTENSIVE COOPERATION Burden rests on Revenue to prove that Appellant either did not participate in investigations or evaded participation deliberately. On the contrary: Appellant's statement recorded on .............. and on subsequent occasions forms the pith of the allegations in Impugned SCN.
INCOMPATIBLE Such extensive cooperation is incompatible with the ingredients needed to support the allegations in Sec 122(3)(a)/..../(e) of CGST Act → entire demand liable to be set aside.
G3 — Specific Clause of Sec 122(3) Not Established
CLAUSE NOT SPECIFIED Demand for penalty confirmed without establishing ingredients of the specific clause in Sec 122(3) of CGST Act of ...AS PER SPECIFIC CLAUSE...... which is unsustainable in law on Appellant. Demand for penalty → liable to be set aside.
G4 — Animus Not Shown to Exist
ANIMUS ABSENT Required animus is not shown to exist. Without the animus → demand for penalty is misapplication of law → demand for penalty liable to be set aside.

29.25 — General Penalty: Section 125 / 127

§ 29.25 — General Penalty Under Sec 125/127: Ingredients Not Established; State Actions Denied; Burden of Proof on Revenue; Sec 126 Not Followed
G1 — Ingredients to Attract Penalty Under Sec 125/127 Not Established
INGREDIENTS ABSENT Demand for penalty confirmed without establishing ingredients needed to attract imposition of such penalty under Section 125/......./127 of CGST Act. Without the ingredients → imposition of penalty is illegal → entire demand liable to be set aside for being contrary to law.
G2 — State Actions in SCN Not Performed; Exculpatory Actions Affirm Appellant's Position
FACTS Impugned Order contrary to facts in so far as Appellant has not.......... STATE ACTIONS ALLEGED IN SUPPORT OF PENALTY IN SCN.......... It is evident in .......... STATE EXCULPATORY ACTIONS OF APPELLANT TO TAKE SUCH POSITION ON FACTS.........
NO PRESUMPTION Without demonstrating ingredients to support imposition of said penalty → no presumption can be raised based on supposition and adverse inference → entire demand liable to be set aside for being contrary to facts.
G3 — Burden of Proof Not Discharged by Revenue
ADVERSE INFERENCE INSUFFICIENT Demand confirmed without examining satisfactory discharge of burden of proof by Revenue, which is based on adverse inference → entire demand liable to be set aside for being contrary to law and facts.
G4 (WP) — Sec 126 Discipline Not Followed for Bona Fide Discrepancy
WITHOUT PREJUDICE WITHOUT PREJUDICE: Impugned Order erroneous in so far as demand confirmed for penalty for bona fide discrepancies without examining contemporaneous records that evidence mitigating circumstances to warrant nominal penalty. Runs counter to discipline specified in Section 126 of CGST Act → entire demand contrary to law and facts.
CHAPTER 24

GSTAT Procedure Rules, 2025

Online Filing · Hybrid Hearing · Registry · Hearing · Evidence · Orders · E-Filing

24.1 — Introduction: GSTAT Procedure Rules, 2025

§ 24.1 — GSTAT Procedure Rules Notified 24 Apr 2025 — Operationalizing Statutory Appeal Before Last Fact-Finding Authority
Why These Rules Matter Now
OPERATIONAL High Courts have been burdened with writ petitions against impugned (pro: imp-yoond) orders. With Appellate Tribunal set to be operational, mass withdrawal of petitions with liberty to file statutory appeal — all contentions kept open for fresh consideration by Appellate Tribunal.
MODUS OPERANDI "Online filing — hybrid hearing" model. Civil Procedure Code and Rules of Evidence do not apply with the same rigour, but equitable principles still have application. Specific references to CPC in these Rules are instructive.
Limitation — Current Position
DEFERRED Section 112(1) CGST Act: Limitation presently stands deferred within "the date as may be notified" in first instance when Appellate Tribunal is set up, and "three months" subsequently.
CIRCULAR 224/18/2024-GST — 11 Jul 2024 All litigants directed to: (i) deposit 10% of disputed tax (in addition to already deposited for first appeal), (ii) undertake statutory appeal before GSTAT will be filed in due course, (iii) Proper Officer to desist from recovery action under Sec 78 or 79 in respect of demand residing in APL4 under Sec 107(11) or 108(1).
Filing Urgency — Both Sides
TAXPAYER File statutory appeal under Sec 112(1) or cross-objections under Sec 112(5) as soon as Appellate Tribunal commences hearing appeals.
REVENUE Also needs to file application under Sec 112(4) within similar limitation. Belated filing allowed additional time of three months (appeal/application) or forty-five days (cross-objections). Both sides primed and ready to file as soon as online filing opens.
Most Striking Rules Needing Revision
REVIEW REQUIRED
RuleBrief DescriptionReason to Revise
21Appeal against adjudication orderAppeal only against Sec 107(11) / 108(1) OIA
23Correctness of translationNew step — no procedure in Act; causes undue delay
27Calling for records by RegistrarOutdated; Appellant should submit paper-book
29Interlocutory applicationsNo provision in Act; should be Misc. Application (rule 64)
34Copies to be servedOnline filing makes physical copies otiose (rule 115)
50Reference to Larger BenchSec 109(9) only provides for Third Member — not 3-member Bench
56Compilation before hearingPaper-book procedure omitted — may be restored

24.2 — Powers of Appellate Tribunal

§ 24.2 — 31 Benches Notified (MoF SO No. 3048(E), 31 Jul 2024) — Inherent Powers + Key Judicial Principles on Natural Justice
Tribunal Structure
BENCHES Principal Bench — New Delhi. 31 Benches appointed at specified locations (circuit + permanent). President or Member discharges duties under CGST Act. Registrar (incl. Joint/Deputy/Asst. Registrar) signs notices/orders in name of President or Member of respective Bench (rule 4). Official seal with emblem (rule 5).
Inherent Powers — Sec 151 CPC (Rule 10)
INHERENT POWERS Sec 151 of CPC lays out broad powers of a Court — referred as "inherent powers". Powers of Appellate Tribunal to avail all such powers deemed necessary to ensure equitable disposal of all matters within its purview.
Rule 10 (traceable to Sec 111(2) CGST Act): "to make such orders or to give such directions as may be necessary for meeting the ends of justice or to prevent abuse of the process of the Appellate Tribunal."
Key Judicial Authorities on Natural Justice at GSTAT
APEX COURT Chuharmal Mohanani v. CIT AIR 1988 SC 1384 (citing Bombay HC in J.S. Parkar v. V.B. Palekar, 94 ITR 616): Evidence Act does not apply with same rigour — but "the Evidence Act embodies a salutary principle of common law jurisprudence which could be attracted to a set of circumstances that satisfy its condition."

Rajesh Kumar v. Dy. CIT (2007) 2 SCC 181: When civil consequences ensue by reason of action of statutory authority → "principles of natural justice are required to be followed." In case of denial of NJ in a statute → may be held ultra vires Article 14.

AK Kraipak v. UoI AIR 1970 SC 150: "It is against all canons of justice to make a man judge in his own cause."

Ranjit Kumar Thakur v. UOI (1987) 4 SCC 25: "The minimum requirement of natural justice is that the authority must be composed of impartial persons acting fairly, without prejudice and bias." A decision which is a result of bias is a nullity and the trial is "coram non judice".

Laxmidhar v. State of Orissa AIR 1974 Ori 127: Defect of NJ in trial body cannot be cured by presence of NJ in appellate body — that would deprive the member of "his right to appeal to another body from the effective decision."

Menaka Gandhi v. UoI AIR 1978 SC 597: Executive authorities must take care to see that "justice is not only done but manifestly appears to be done" — duty to proceed free from even appearance of arbitrariness or unfairness.

24.3 — Instituting Appeal: Form GST APL5

§ 24.3 — Online Filing on www.gstat.gov.in in Form GST APL5 — Filed Qua Person & Qua OIO (Not Qua SCN)
Prescribed Format — Form GST APL5
ONLINE In the Goods and Services Tax Appellate Tribunal
………… Bench at …………, State
In the matter of appeal under Sec 112(1) / 112(4) / 112(5) of Central GST Act
Arising out of Order-in-Appeal / Revisionary Order No. ………… dated …………
M/s ……………… Appellant
versus
M/s ……………… Respondent
CRITICAL RULE APL5 to be filed qua person and qua OIO (Adjudication Order / Order-in-Original) — NOT qua SCN. Where impugned order is OIA (First Appellate Order / Order-in-Appeal) whether based on one or more OIOs (rule 18(2) or 18(3)(a)).
SCN → OIO → OIA → APL5 Matrix
FILING MAP
Notice (SCN)Adjudication Order (OIO)First Appellate Order (OIA)Second Appeal (APL5)
SCN 1OIO 1OIA 1APL5 1
SCN 2OIO 2APL5 2
SCN 3--APL5 2
SCN 4OIO 3OIA 2APL5 3
Where common or separate notice issued to different persons → appeals must still be filed qua person and qua OIO (rule 18(3)(b)).
Appeal Format Requirements (Rule 20)
MANDATORY
RequirementSpecification
Paper sizeA4 (30.5 × 21.5 cms)
Left margin5 cms
Right margin2.5 cms
Top & bottom margin"sufficient"
Entire documentIndex with page numbers
Every pagePaginated prominently
Verificationby Appellant
Every page ("true copy")by Appellant / Authorized Representative
Impugned order ("certified copy")by Appellant / Authorized Representative
Online fee paymentas per Rule 110(5)
Note: "Certified copy" = original copy received by party, or copy duly authenticated by concerned department, or by authorized representative of appellant or respondent.
Scrutiny & Admission Flow
FLOW
Online Filing (www.gstat.gov.in)
↓
Scrutiny — 7 working days for compliance
↓
If Defective → Registrar may extend up to 30 days
↓
Unattended defects → personal hearing → rejection (rule 24(3))
↓
Admission — uneventful if no defects (rule 25)
ENDORSEMENT MANDATORY Authorized representative's endorsement mandatory on every appeal or pleading (rule 22). Documents without endorsement will NOT be entertained by the Bench. English is the medium for all documents. Non-English documents to be submitted with certified translated copy (rule 23).

24.4 — Registry

§ 24.4 — Registry Functions: Cause-List · Hearing Notices · Service · Substituted Service
Cause-List Publication
DAILY Daily cause-list published online and on notice board of Appellate Tribunal (rule 38). Hearing notice sent to litigant and authorized representative. Changes promptly published and circulated (rule 39).
Service of Notices
SEC 169 CGST ACT Due process in Sec 169 of CGST Act applies mutatis mutandis to "service" of notices, orders and communication by Registry. "Substituted service" and "accord or waiver" in respect of service are recognized (rule 40).
Registrar's Functions (Rule 15)
ADMINISTRATIVE (a) Day-to-day administration of Appellate Tribunal
(b) Notify procedure of filing appeal
(c) Registration of appeals, petitions and applications and scrutiny thereof
(d) Receive applications for amendment of appeal/petition/application
(e) Receive applications for fresh summons or notices
(f) Receive applications for substituted service
(g) Maintain records and manage registry
(h) Such other incidental matters as President may direct
New Concepts in GST Procedure (Rules 12–14)
FAMILIARIZE
DescriptionRule 12 (Listing)Rule 13 (Exemption from Compliance)Rule 14 (Extension of Time)
PurposeUrgent mattersDeficient complianceBelated compliance
Mention to Bench<12 noon but <3 pmShow sufficient cause to exempt by applicationPrayer to extend time by application
DisposalBy orderBy orderBy order

24.5 — Hearing Appeals

§ 24.5 — Both Sides Entitled to Be Heard — Non-Prosecution → Dismissal; Death Abates; Evidence by Affidavit; Open Court
Right to Be Heard (Rule 41)
EXPRESS RIGHT Appellant and Respondent both entitled to "be heard" by the Bench — expressly provided in rule 41. Absence or non-prosecution of appeal → dismissal of appeal or disposal on ex parte basis (rule 42).
RESTORATION Appeal dismissed due to non-prosecution may be restored on application with affidavit explaining reasons unseating the reasons cited in order of dismissal.
Death and Impleading Successors (Rule 44)
ABATEMENT Death abates appeals pending unless successors-in-interest move application to be impleaded and continue appeal from the stage at which they were pending. Recovery of finalized demand permitted under Sec 93 from successors-in-interest. Impleading application within sixty (60) days — extendable by Bench.
Evidence — Affidavit + Cross-Examination (Rule 46)
RULE 112 CGST RULES Rule 112 of CGST Rules prohibits introduction of additional evidence subject to exceptions. Evidence may be actual or by deposition in affidavit. Deponent in affidavit is exposed to cross-examination by video conference or as directed by Bench on application (rule 46).
NOTE Further procedures requiring submission of questions in advance or addition of new questions in cross-examination impromptu are not addressed in these Rules.
Open Court + Larger Bench Issue (Rules 48, 50)
OPEN TO PUBLIC Proceedings before Appellate Tribunal are "open to public" — inherent safeguard when proceedings are not private and behind closed doors. Removal of person is permitted by Bench only in interests of the appeal (rule 48).
DRAFTING ISSUE Sec 109(9) of CGST Act only provides for reference to Third Member in case of difference of opinion of two Members on a Bench. These Rules appear to make reference to a "Larger Bench" — but there are no statutory provisions to this effect (rule 50). Sea of difference between these two concepts.

24.6 — Case Management

§ 24.6 — Two-Step Process: Order Sheet + Court Diary → Compilation Before Hearing → Open Court Case Presentation
Two-Step Process Per Appeal (Rules 53–55)
MANDATORY Every appeal/application/IA listed for disposal involves: (i) Bench records proceedings in Order Sheet (GSTAT Form 2) (rule 53); (ii) Court Officer records relevant information in Court Diary (GSTAT CDR2) (rule 54). Court Diary available on GSTAT portal with brief case particulars (rule 55).
CESTAT vs GSTAT Filing Structure
COMPARISON
Table A: CESTAT Procedure Rules
Appeal memo
→
Paper-book
→
Compilation
→
Synopsis
→
Case presentation
Table B: GSTAT Procedure Rules
Appeal memo
→
Additional documents
→
Call-for-records
→
Compilation
→
Case presentation
Gap: No reference to "Paper Book" submission in these Rules. CESTAT's effective structure has not been imported into GSTAT Procedure Rules — Paper-book procedure may need to be restored.
Compilation — Before Commencement of Proceedings (Rule 56)
TIMING Compilation to be submitted "before commencement of proceedings" — implying same may be submitted on the date of personal hearing or a little earlier. Open court proceedings involve Court Officer 'calling out' cases in cause-list order for case presentation (rule 57).

24.7 — Forms and Registers

§ 24.7 — Registers, Record of Pending Matters — Documents Maintained 5 Years; Record 15 Years
Registers Maintained (Rules 59–64)
GSTAT REGISTERS (a) Register of un-numbered petitions/appeals — GSTAT-CDR-03
(b) Register of petitions/appeals — GSTAT-CDR-04
(c) Register of interlocutory applications — GSTAT-CDR-05
Record of Pending Matters — Four Files
FOUR FILES Main File: Index · Order Sheet · Final Order/Judgment · Form of appeal/petition · Counter/reply · Oral evidence/affidavit · Documentary evidence · Written arguments

Process File: Index · Power of Attorney/Vakalatnama · Summons · Applications for summoning witness · Letters calling records

Miscellaneous Application File

Execution File: Index · Order sheet · Execution application · Processes · Transmission of order to civil court · Result of execution
Document Retention Timeline
RETENTION Documents of proceedings → maintained for 5 years after disposal of appeals and applications (rule 65). Record of documents → maintained for 15 years after disposal. After maximum period → destroyed expeditiously (rule 66).
SCRUTINY Records received → scrutinized for compliance within 3 days of receiving them in records room.

24.8 — Records and Inspection

§ 24.8 — Inspection of Case Records on Payment of Fee (GSTAT Form 3) — 2 Days Advance Notice Required
Application for Inspection (Rule 67–71)
PROCEDURE Application in GSTAT Form 3 to Registrar on payment of fee. Application must be made 2 days in advance between 10:30 am to 1:30 pm on all working days (rule 68).
Inspection permitted in presence of any Officer between 10:30 am to 12:30 pm and 2:30 pm to 4:30 pm (rule 69). Mode of inspection facilitates without permitting mutilation or prejudice to counter-Party (rule 70). Register of inspection maintained in GSTAT CDR6 (rule 71).

24.9 — Authorized Representative

§ 24.9 — Conduct of Authorized Representative: Memo of Appearance · Conflict of Interest · Dress Code · Expert Panel
Memo of Appearance (Rule 72–75)
MANDATORY Submit memo of appearance in GSTAT Form 4 with specified particulars or vakalatnama or authorization duly executed by Appellant and accepted by authorized representative (rule 72).
NO CHANGE WITHOUT NOC Change of authorized representative not permitted without 'no objection' from authorized representative on-record (rule 73). Conflict of interest and impairment of independence of authorized representative is barred (rule 74). Appearance of Party-in-person restricted where authorized representative is already appointed (rule 75).
Expert Panel (Rule 76) + Dress Code (Rule 77)
INTERESTING Appellate Tribunal may draw up and maintain a panel of "authorized representatives or valuers or such other experts" to assist in proceedings where required (rule 76). Without a statutory provision and without this measure falling within inherent powers — such empanelment is fraught with additional matters (selection criteria, remuneration, administration) left open-ended.
Dress code of authorized representative: as prescribed by their respective professional governing body (rule 77).
Delegatus Non Potest Delagare — President's Delegation
LATIN MAXIM Functions of President may be delegated to Vice-President of State Benches (rule 17(1)). This is NOT violative of delegatus non potest delagare (one cannot delegate delegated authority) due to the exceptional nature of the office of President. President may further delegate powers of Registrar to Joint/Deputy/Asst. Registrar (rule 17(2)).

24.10 — Code of Civil Procedure: Affidavits, Discovery, Evidence

§ 24.10 — Affidavits (Or.19 CPC) · Discovery (Sec 67 CGST / Or.11 CPC) · Marking of Documents · Examination of Witnesses (Or.16 & 26 CPC)
Affidavits (Rules 78–83)
CPC OR.19 RULE 3 Form of affidavit required to conform to Or.19 Rule 3 of CPC (rule 79). Affidavits to be notarized (rule 80). Affidavits by visually impaired / illiterate persons must be accompanied by verification in GSTAT Form 5 (rule 81). Annexures must be numbered and duly endorsed with reference in affidavit (rule 83).
Discovery and Production of Documents (Rule 84–85)
NOTE Rules make reference to "discovery or production and return of documents" — defers to CPC for applicable procedure (rule 84). Discovery also addressed in Sec 67(10) of CGST Act drawing from Sec 185(5) of Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Adhiniyam. However, without reference to Or.11 of CPC → discovery here limited to discovery in summons. Bench may issue summons suo motu (rule 85).
Marking of Documents (Rule 86) & Witnesses (Rules 88–98)
PROCEDURE Documents placed on record to be "marked" (rule 86). Or.16 and Or.26 of CPC — entire procedure of examination of witnesses and issue of commissions made mutatis mutandis applicable to proceedings before Appellate Tribunal (rule 88). Proceedings of examination may be in-camera (rule 89). Witness to be examined under oath (rule 90).
Deposition recorded in GSTAT Form 7. Each page of deposition must be initialled by a Member of Bench (rule 93). Witnesses numbered as PW (for applicant/petitioner) and RW (for respondent) (rule 94). Discharged witness issued GSTAT Form 8 by Registrar (rule 95).

24.11 — Decisions and Orders

§ 24.11 — Maintainability Check → Admission → Speaking Order → Operative Portion Separately Stated → Order Within 30 Days
Admission and Disposal (Rule 99)
PROCESS Appeals and applications examined for maintainability. Unless liable to be dismissed in limine → admitted and taken up for disposal (i) on grounds raised and (ii) allow relief as prayed (rule 99).
Speaking Order Requirements (Rules 100–104)
MANDATORY After detailed speaking order is passed → operative portion separately stated at end of decision (rule 100). Every Member to affix signature (rule 101). Bench empowered to impose Costs (rule 102 — but statutory basis in Sec 111 or 113 not forthcoming). Orders must be passed within 30 days after conclusion of hearings (rule 103 — to be harmonized with Sec 113(4) CGST Act).
ORDER FORMAT (RULE 112)
RequirementSpecification
Paper sizeA4 (30.5 × 21.5 cms)
Left margin5 cms
Right margin2.5 cms
Top & bottom margin"sufficient"
Signature by seniorityFrom right to left of page
Rectification and Amendment (Rules 108–109)
RECTIFICATION Rectification by filing application in GSTAT Form 1 with affidavit. Bench, if satisfied, allows: (i) withdraw earlier order and pass fresh order, OR (ii) issue corrigendum (rule 108). General power to amend specified with 30-day time restriction and power to impose costs (rule 109).
Conflict of Interest — Recusal (Rule 105)
RULE 105 Conflict of interest for a Member in respect of a particular case or a particular litigant → specified to be good grounds for Members seeking recusal. (Traceable to the AK Kraipak principle: one cannot be judge in one's own cause.)

24.12 — E-Filing

§ 24.12 — "Online Filing — Hybrid Disposal" Approach: All Filing Electronic; Hearings Physical / Virtual (with Prior Permission of President)
Rule 115 — The Core Rule
HYBRID MODEL "Online filing — hybrid disposal" is the approach. All filing — whether appeal, application, additional submissions or rectification memo — to be uploaded electronically.
Hearings to be conducted by physical mode and in-person. Virtual mode permitted only with prior permission of President (not any Member).
PRACTICAL NOTE With all appeal memos and applications to be filed online (rule 115) → rule on serving physical copies to Respondents (rule 34) becomes otiose. Similarly rule on serving physical copies to Registrar becomes redundant.

24.13 — Miscellaneous: Fee Schedule + Costs + Supreme Court / High Court Orders

§ 24.13 — Fee Schedule · Registers for SC / HC Orders · Registrar Compliance with Court Directions
Fee Schedule (Rule 119)
FEES
S.No.Relevant Rule / SectionNature of Application / PetitionFees
1Rule 67 of Procedure RulesApplication for inspection of recordsRs. 5,000
2Rule 118(2) of Procedure RulesInterlocutory applicationRs. 5,000
3Rule 110(3) of CGST RulesAppeals to GSTATAs per rule
4Any other provision not specifically mentionedAny other applicationRs. 5,000
5Rule — certified true copyCertified true copy of final order (for parties other than concerned parties)Rs. 5 per page
Costs, SC / HC Orders and Registers (Rules 116–124)
DISCIPLINE Rule 120: In imposing costs → Appellate Tribunal must endeavour to discourage abuse of process of Court.

Rules 116–117: GSTAT CDR7 and CDR8 are registers for all orders carried by litigants to Supreme Court (Sec 118) and High Court (Sec 117). Orders/directions of SC or HC to be placed before President and the same Bench whose order was carried in revision petition, for information and to act on directions.

Rule 118: Responsibility of Registrar to comply with directions of Courts.

Rule 123: Difficulties in implementing these Rules may be redressed by President by issuing suitable orders.

Rule 124: President or any nominated Judicial or Technical Member may conduct inspection of State Benches to review their functioning and extend support.

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Paras 29.22–29.25 & Chapter 24

These ratios, principles and Latin maxims are distilled from Paras 29.22–29.25 (Blocked Credit 2, Non-Genuine Suppliers, Abetment Penalty, General Penalty) and Chapter 24 (GSTAT Procedure Rules). State them confidently at personal hearings, written submissions and GSTAT proceedings. Cite the case name / section / circular and link to facts of your client's case.

⚖️ Key Principles — Paras 29.22 to 29.25

Principle 1 — Rule 36(4)(b) CGST Rules Notified Only from 1 Oct 2022: Pre-Oct 2022 Sec 17(2) Reversal Demands Are Presumptuous
Para 29.22 | Rule 36(4)(b) CGST Rules | Section 17(2), (5) CGST Act
"The mandate to claim entire input tax credit and reverse the inadmissible portion thereof came to be mandated only when Rule 36(4)(b) of Central GST Rules were notified from 1 October 2022. Without any evidence to support the premise that inadmissible credit has, in fact, been availed by Appellant during Disputed Tax Period, demand for reversal under Section 17(2) of Central GST Act would be presumptuous."
🎯 Use When: Revenue seeks reversal of blocked credit under Sec 17(2) for periods prior to Oct 2022 — argue that the mechanism to track and reverse inadmissible credit was not in place before that date; demand is presumptuous.
Principle 2 — Confidential Intelligence Without Disclosure: Material Inadmissible; Order Rendered an Empty Demand
Para 29.23 | Section 122(3), 74 CGST Act — Non-Genuine Supplier ITC Reversal
"Impugned Order has failed to reach a finding on the quality and reliability of said confidential intelligence said to have been acted upon in issuing Impugned SCN. As such, material relied upon in support of demand that is withheld from Appellant becomes inadmissible in law. And without said material, Impugned Order is rendered an empty demand. For this reason, the entire exercise is illegal and devoid of merit, is liable to be rejected as defective, illegal and erroneous."
🎯 Use When: Revenue relies on confidential intelligence (enforcement reports, third-party statements) without disclosing the same to the taxpayer — challenge the quality, reliability and admissibility of that intelligence at the very threshold.
Principle 3 — Double Recovery Impermissible: Section 93 Exceptional Powers
Para 29.23 | Section 93 CGST Act — Recovery from Non-Genuine Supplier
"Without exhausting remedies available in law, demand for reversal of input tax credit on Appellant is contrary to the avowed scheme of this law. With the exceptional powers vested in Section 93 of Central GST Act, successful recovery from alleged suppliers-in-default as well as from Appellant will result in double recovery which is contrary to law."
🎯 Use When: Revenue demands ITC reversal from the recipient without first exhausting recovery from the defaulting supplier — argue double recovery principle and invoke Sec 93 to show that Revenue has a remedy directly against the defaulting supplier.
Principle 4 — Output Tax Admitted as Valid by Revenue; Cannot Simultaneously Disaffirm Bona Fides of Inward Supplies
Para 29.23 | Section 15, 16, 74 CGST Act — Internal Inconsistency
"Impugned Order is erroneous in so far as it has affirmed OUTPUT TAX DISCHARGED by Appellant during Disputed Tax Period is valid and proper, while disaffirming the bona fides of inward supplies therefor. Without any finding to the contrary, appropriation of output tax effected out of impugned inward supplies renders the demand unsustainable on law and facts."
🎯 Use When: Revenue has accepted Appellant's outward supplies (output tax discharged) while simultaneously challenging ITC on corresponding inward supplies — expose the internal contradiction in Revenue's case at GSTAT.
Principle 5 — Extensive Cooperation Incompatible with Abetment Ingredients Under Section 122(3)
Para 29.24 | Section 122(3)(a)/(e) CGST Act — Penalty for Abetment
"Burden rests on Revenue to prove that Appellant either did not participate in investigations or evaded participation deliberately. On the contrary, Appellant's statement recorded on ………… and on subsequent occasions forms the pith of the allegations in Impugned SCN. Such extensive cooperation is incompatible with the ingredients needed to support the allegations in Section 122(3)(a)/..../(e) of Central GST Act."
🎯 Use When: Revenue seeks to impose penalty for abetment under Sec 122(3) but the taxpayer has voluntarily cooperated with the investigation — cooperative conduct is a bar to abetment penalty. State the dates and occasions of cooperation on record.
Principle 6 — Borrowed Satisfaction: Notice of Demand Under Section 74 Cannot Be Issued on Borrowed Satisfaction
Para 29.24 | Section 74 CGST Act — Adjudication on Borrowed Satisfaction
"Without a valid notice of demand addressed to Appellant and without opportunity of cross-examination of third-party witnesses and Investigating Officer, averments relied upon are inadmissible. Without said averments, conclusions in Impugned SCN are merely suspicion and adverse inference. Notice of demand under Section 74 of Central GST Act cannot be issued based on borrowed satisfaction."
🎯 Use When: SCN is based entirely on intelligence gathered by enforcement, statements procured under duress or inducement, or third-party affidavits without cross-examination — the entire jurisdictional basis falls if borrowed satisfaction is established.
Principle 7 — General Penalty: Specific Clause of Sec 125/127 Must Be Established; Sec 126 Discipline Mandatory
Para 29.25 | Section 125, 126, 127 CGST Act — General Penalty
"Demand for penalty confirmed without establishing ingredients needed to attract the imposition of such penalty under Section 125/127 of Central GST Act. Without the ingredients, imposition of penalty is illegal. WITHOUT PREJUDICE: demand confirmed for penalty for bona fide discrepancies without examining contemporaneous records that evidence mitigating circumstances to warrant nominal penalty runs counter to the discipline specified in Section 126 of Central GST Act."
🎯 Use When: Penalty is imposed under Sec 125 or 127 without specifying which sub-clause is attracted and without examining mitigating circumstances — challenge both (i) absence of ingredients and (ii) failure of Sec 126 discipline in one compound argument.

⚖️ Key Principles — Chapter 24 (GSTAT Procedure Rules, 2025)

Chuharmal Mohanani v. CIT AIR 1988 SC 1384 (Apex Court; Bombay HC in J.S. Parkar v. V.B. Palekar, 94 ITR 616)
Para 24.1 | Evidence Act Principles Applicable in Tax Proceedings
"The Evidence Act embodies a salutary principle of common law jurisprudence which could be attracted to a set of circumstances that satisfy its condition. What was meant by saying that the Evidence Act did not apply was that the rigour of the rules of evidence contained in the Evidence Act was not applicable — but that did not mean that the taxing authorities desirous in invoking the principles of the Act were prevented from doing so."
🎯 Use When: Revenue attempts to introduce evidence of doubtful probative value at GSTAT — argue that while the strict rigour of Evidence Act does not apply, the salutary principles of evidence law (reliability, probative value, cross-examination) must still be satisfied.
Rajesh Kumar v. Dy. CIT (2007) 2 SCC 181
Para 24.2 | Supreme Court — Civil Consequences; Natural Justice Implied
"Effect of civil consequences arising out of determination of lis under a statute — when by reason of action on the part of a statutory authority, civil or evil consequences ensue, principles of natural justice are required to be followed. In such an event, although no express provision is laid down in this behalf, compliance of principles of natural justice would be implicit. In case of denial of principles of natural justice in a statute, the same may also be held ultra vires Article 14 of the Constitution."
🎯 Use When: At GSTAT where natural justice was violated at the original adjudication stage — argue that regardless of express provisions, compliance with NJ is implied in all proceedings carrying civil consequences, and denial of NJ vitiates the entire proceeding.
Ranjit Kumar Thakur v. UOI (1987) 4 SCC 25
Para 24.2 | Supreme Court — Minimum Requirement of Natural Justice; Bias = Nullity
"The minimum requirement of natural justice is that the authority must be composed of impartial persons acting fairly, without prejudice and bias. In this manner impartiality, objectivity and public confidence provide the foundation on which the superstructure of rule against bias is built. A decision which is a result of bias is a nullity and the trial is 'coram non judice'."
🎯 Use When: Raising bias of the adjudicating authority before GSTAT — argue that a decision reached by a biased authority is a nullity and is not mere irregularity that can be cured; the proceeding is coram non judice (not before a properly constituted authority).
Menaka Gandhi v. UoI AIR 1978 SC 597
Para 24.2 | Supreme Court — Justice Must Manifestly Appear to Be Done
"Even executive authorities when taking administrative action which involves any deprivations of or restrictions on inherent fundamental rights of citizens must take care to see that justice is not only done but manifestly appears to be done. They have a duty to proceed in a way which is free from even the appearance of arbitrariness or unreasonableness or unfairness."
🎯 Use When: Raising issues of high-handedness, recovery action during appeal pendency, or denial of stay at GSTAT — argue that the executive's actions must be free from even the appearance of arbitrariness, not just actual arbitrariness.

🔤 Latin Maxims — Paras 29.22–29.25 & Chapter 24

Delegatus Non Potest Delagare
Meaning: "A delegate cannot further delegate" — one who holds authority by delegation cannot further delegate that authority unless expressly permitted. In GSTAT context: the President delegating to Vice-Presidents and Registrars is expressly carved out as an exception due to the exceptional nature of the office of President.
Use in Hearing: "The principle of delegatus non potest delagare applies generally, but Rule 17 of GSTAT Procedure Rules expressly permits the President to delegate functions to the Vice-President of State Benches and further to Joint/Deputy/Asst. Registrars — due to the exceptional nature of the office of President. Any exercise of delegated functions by Officers within this framework is intra vires the Rules."
Coram Non Judice
Meaning: "Not before a judge" or "before a person not a judge" — proceedings conducted before an authority that lacks jurisdiction or is constitutionally incompetent are void ab initio and have no legal effect. A decision resulting from bias is a nullity and the trial is coram non judice.
Use in Hearing: "The Impugned Order is a nullity in so far as the adjudication was conducted by an authority that was not impartial. A decision which is a result of bias is a nullity and the trial is coram non judice — the Appellate Tribunal is requested to declare the Impugned Order as non est and void ab initio, and not merely to set it aside."
In Limine
Meaning: "At the threshold" or "at the outset" — disposed of at the preliminary stage without full hearing on merits. GSTAT Rules provide that appeals not liable to be dismissed in limine are admitted and taken up for disposal.
Use in Hearing: "This Tribunal is requested to not dismiss this appeal in limine and to admit the same for full hearing on the grounds urged in the appeal memo — the grounds are substantial and the Impugned Order is perverse on its face, warranting a full hearing on merits."
Suo Motu
Meaning: "On its own motion" — the court or authority acts on its own initiative, without being prompted by a party. The Bench may issue summons suo motu under Rule 85 to require public officers to produce documents or information.
Use in Hearing: "This Tribunal is requested to exercise its power under Rule 85 to issue summons suo motu to the Investigating Officer requiring production of the confidential intelligence relied upon in the Impugned SCN — the non-disclosure of which has vitiated the entire proceeding for denial of natural justice."
Mutatis Mutandis
Meaning: "With the necessary changes having been made" — procedures applicable in one context apply to another context with appropriate modifications. Or.16 and Or.26 of CPC apply mutatis mutandis to examination of witnesses before GSTAT; Sec 169 CGST Act applies mutatis mutandis to service of notices by Registry.
Use in Hearing: "The provisions of Or.16 and Or.26 of the Code of Civil Procedure apply mutatis mutandis to examination of witnesses and issue of commissions before this Tribunal under Rule 88 of the GSTAT Procedure Rules. The Appellant requests that the deponent of the affidavit filed by Revenue be summoned for cross-examination before this Tribunal."
Pro Imp-Yoond (Pro: Impugned)
Meaning: The term "impugned" is the legal word for "challenged" or "attacked" — an impugned order is the order under challenge before the appellate authority. The book uses the phonetic guide "pro: imp-yoond" to help practitioners pronounce the word correctly when appearing before GSTAT.
Use in Hearing: "The Impugned Order — the order that is the subject of challenge before this Tribunal — was passed in excess of jurisdiction, in violation of natural justice, and contrary to the provisions of the Central GST Act on multiple independent grounds, each of which is sufficient to warrant setting aside the demand in its entirety."
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Paras 29.22–29.25 & Chapter 24 — GSTAT Grounds & Procedure Rules
